# Container & Beans

> The 21 designs cover the Container & Beans core comprehensively and with strong mutual awareness — the engine/façade split, the definition metamodel + merged-def + freeze boundary, the three-store registry, the construct→expose-early→populate→init→publish sequence, the fail-fast layered resolution + primary/fallback + collection aggregation, scopes/lazy/deferral/circular handling, factory-bean indirection, background bootstrap, and programmatic registration. The dominant cross-cutting pattern is that nearly every feature correctly defers the SAME small set of keystones (scope/ownership handle type, async-across-dyn boxing, compile-vs-runtime/AOT split, registry substrate, the proxy mechanism) to Phase 2, and they are impressively consistent about it. The genuine GAPS are a handful of Spring mechanics that are NAMED by multiple neighbors but OWNED by none: @DependsOn forced-init-and-destroy ordering (only ever a metadata field), lookup-method/@Lookup injection (cited as a scope-mismatch fix and an abstract-scanning-gate exception but with no owner), self-injection (its own Spring section, absent here), the resolvableDependencies REGISTRATION mechanism + its ignore-Aware-setter counterpart (consumers named, registration unowned), the NullBean/present-but-None representation, and mid-bootstrap dynamic registration (deferred as a question by three features rather than decided). The most important COHERENCE risks are: (1) the @Order/@Priority comparator is triplicated across collection-injection, autowiring-resolution, and deferral-primitives with no designated owner; (2) circular-references' setter-cycle tolerance and injection-styles' construction model are jointly constrained but presented as independent (if injection-styles picks single-phase construction, circular-references CANNOT offer early-reference tolerance); (3) @Lazy-edge and deferral-primitives' cached handle are near-duplicate handle types each described authoritatively; and (4) the 'everything is a Provider' instinct appears as R-variants in three features that are really one bet on the registry/creation meta-architecture. None of these are contradictions today — they are unforced-error risks if Phase 2/3 resolves the shared seams in different features inconsistently."

_27 feature designs. Implementation-agnostic — candidate realizations for Phase 2/3, not decisions._

## IoC Container Core (BeanFactory / ApplicationContext)  `[container-core]`

Spring's container is split into two interface layers. The lower layer — BeanFactory, realized by the single workhorse engine DefaultListableBeanFactory — is the bare DI engine: a registry of bean definitions keyed by name, the singleton cache, candidate resolution, autowiring, and the full per-bean lifecycle (Aware → BPP-before → init → BPP-after). It is deliberately "agnostic about special beans": it does NOT auto-detect post-processors, so on a raw factory the declarative features (autowiring, @PostConstruct, AOP, @Configuration parsing, ${...} resolution — all themselves implemented as BPPs/BFPPs) silently do nothing. The upper layer — ApplicationContext — is NOT a subclass; it HAS-A exactly one engine and is a façade/orchestrator that (1) auto-detects and registers the framework's own post-processors by type, and (2) layers context services on top (events, i18n MessageSource, resource loading, Environment, hierarchy). The two behavioral consequences that matter: (a) auto-detection of infrastructure post-processors is exactly what turns declarative config into working behavior with zero wiring — this is THE point of the feature; (b) a bare factory is lazy (creates on first getBean) while a context's refresh() eagerly pre-instantiates all non-lazy singletons, a deliberate fail-fast that surfaces config errors at boot. leaf must replicate this INTENT: a minimal, embeddable, fully-controllable engine; a convenience façade composing it that auto-discovers and orders the framework's own infrastructure and exposes context services; the engine stays origin-agnostic (a provider of dyn Trait must not assume native construction) so dynamic sources (e.g. a future WASM-module layer) can register the same way native code does. Per-engine policy toggles (override-allowed, circular-refs-allowed, strict-locking) live on the engine and are applied by the façade during bring-up. The design must NOT pick the meta-architecture (codegen boundary, registry substrate, scope/ownership model) — those are Phase 2.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Two-trait split with an owned engine struct (the literal HAS-A façade)
Mirror Spring's interface layering directly in idiomatic Rust. A `BeanFactory` trait is the bare client view: `async fn get<T>() -> Result<Arc<T>, ResolveError>`, `get_by_name`, `contains`, plus a `ConfigurableEngine` SPI super-trait for registering definitions, post-processors, and reading policy toggles. The single concrete engine `Engine` (the DefaultListableBeanFactory analogue) implements it: it owns the definition map (`HashMap<BeanName, BeanDefinition>` + a parallel `TypeId → Vec<BeanName>` index for type queries), the singleton cache (`HashMap<TypeId, Arc<dyn Any + Send + Sync>>`, beans stored type-erased and recovered via 1.86 trait-upcasting / `Any` downcast), ordered post-processor lists, and the policy struct (allow_override, allow_circular, strict_locking). `Engine` is fully usable standalone — register definitions, hand-register post-processors, call `get` — and is the embeddable/minimal-footprint path (Spring's raw DefaultListableBeanFactory use case). `ApplicationContext` is a separate struct that *owns* one `Engine` (HAS-A, never `Deref`-subclassing) plus the context-service handles (`EventPublisher`, `MessageSource`, `ResourceLoader`, `Environment`, optional `parent: Option<Arc<ApplicationContext>>`). It re-exposes the engine surface by delegation and adds `refresh()`. The defining difference from the bare engine: `ApplicationContext::refresh()` *auto-detects* infrastructure post-processors from the collected descriptor set (by a `role == Infrastructure` marker on the descriptor) and registers them into the engine before any application bean is built — whereas a hand-built `Engine` has none unless you add them. The macro layer emits, per `#[bean]`/`#[component]`, ONE thin `const` descriptor carrying name, `TypeId`, role, a `const`-constructible async factory fn-pointer `fn(&dyn BeanFactory) -> BoxFuture<Result<Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync>, _>>`, declared dependencies, and a conditions/priority blob — exactly the hand-writable artifact the cross-crate doc prescribes. Whether descriptors reach the context via a link-time slice, build-time codegen, or an explicit `with(...)` list is left open (see realizations differ only in *registration substrate*, this one fixes the *type layering*).

**Pros:** Closest intent parity: the BeanFactory-vs-ApplicationContext split and the HAS-A composition are expressed structurally, so the 'features silently fail on a bare factory' semantics fall out naturally and are teachable.; Clean escape hatch (charter §2.10): the bare `Engine` is a first-class public API — embedders get Spring's raw-DefaultListableBeanFactory control without the façade, satisfying the minimal-footprint use case.; Origin-agnostic by construction: the engine only ever sees `Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync>` produced by a factory fn-pointer; native vs WASM-module vs test-double providers are indistinguishable to it.; Trait upcasting (stable 1.86) + `Arc<dyn Any>` give a zero-extra-alloc 'inject as any declared supertype' story, matching Spring's 'a bean is injectable as any of its supertypes'.; Auto-detection becomes a single, inspectable runtime pass over descriptors filtered by role — trivially diagnosable (list what got auto-registered).

**Cons:** Delegation boilerplate: ApplicationContext must forward the whole BeanFactory surface to its owned Engine (no inheritance); risk of surface drift between the two layers.; `async fn get<T>` on a public dyn-dispatched trait hits the RPITIT/AFIT dyn-incompatibility wall — the trait object path must box the future (`Pin<Box<dyn Future>>`) at the dyn boundary, an alloc per resolution on the dynamic path (acceptable for cold wiring, costs on hot per-request scoped lookups).; Two named types (`Engine` + `ApplicationContext`) is more API surface than a single container; users must learn which to reach for.; Generic `get<T>` over a type-erased store needs a `TypeId`-keyed downcast; ambiguity/collection queries still go through the name index, so the type layering alone doesn't settle candidate resolution (delegated to autowiring-resolution).

**Mechanisms considered:** proc-macro emits one const Descriptor per bean (thin macro; heavy logic in leaf-core); trait upcasting + Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync> as the erasure substrate (stable 1.86/1.91); runtime registry: HashMap<TypeId,…> singleton cache + HashMap<BeanName,Definition>; dyn dispatch for the BeanFactory client trait; generics for the typed get<T> sugar layer; boxed futures at the dyn boundary (async-trait/trait_variant shim) since AFIT traits aren't object-safe

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The two-trait/HAS-A layering is substrate-neutral — it works identically whether descriptors arrive by linkme distributed-slice, build-time codegen, or explicit `with()`. What it DOES fix cross-crate: contributing crates depend only on `leaf-core` (the trait + Descriptor + Engine live there), never the umbrella, avoiding cycles. The macro emits absolute-path const descriptors (`::leaf_core::Descriptor`) — no proc-macro state, hand-writable. Layer-0 hazard remains: a registration-only crate the binary never references contributes nothing; this realization does NOT solve force-linking (that is R2/R3's job) — it only guarantees that *whatever* descriptors are collected flow through one origin-agnostic registration API on the Engine. Auto-detection of infrastructure is a runtime filter over the collected set, so it inherits whatever completeness the substrate provides; the façade must run a self-check (expected-vs-found count) and fail loudly if an expected infrastructure descriptor is missing (DCE silent-drop defense).

**T2 — execution context:** Engine construction and definition registration are synchronous, cold-path, single-threaded (typically in `main` before the runtime spins up; descriptors are const data, no pre-main code). `refresh()` is `async` and runs on the app's chosen runtime: it (1) runs BFPP-equivalents to rewrite definitions, (2) registers BPP-equivalents, (3) eagerly awaits construction of all non-lazy singletons — each factory fn-pointer returns a boxed future awaited by the refresh driver. Construction may `.await` (DB pools, etc.) but MUST NOT block the executor; blocking factories must be documented to use `spawn_blocking` (execution-context discipline — Phase 2 owns the propagation model). The singleton cache publishes fully-built `Arc`s; a per-bean creation guard (NOT a global lock — charter §2.5) serializes only concurrent first-creation of the same bean. Errors propagate as a rich `ResolveError` carrying the assembly chain (what was being built, what it needed, candidates considered) per §2.9; refresh is all-or-nothing (fail-fast at boot). Cancellation of `refresh()` must drop partially-constructed singletons in reverse dependency order (ties to context-close); no async Drop on stable, so teardown is an explicit driven `shutdown().await`.

#### R2 — Single typed container, façade-vs-engine as a capability/layer toggle (collapse the split)
Question whether Rust needs two named types at all. Intent parity says replicate the *behavioral* split (auto-detect-or-not, eager-or-not, context-services-or-not) — not necessarily the two-interface taxonomy, which exists partly because Java interfaces were the only composition tool. Here there is ONE `Container` type built through a typestate builder. `Container::engine_only()` yields the bare engine (no infrastructure auto-detected, lazy, no context services) — the embeddable path. `Container::application()` (or `leaf::run`) yields the same struct but the builder runs an `auto_detect_infrastructure` step and attaches the context-service layers. The 'two layers' become two *configurations of one engine* plus a set of optional, individually-attachable service capabilities (`with_events`, `with_messages`, `with_resources`, `with_environment`) — each context service is a trait the Container may or may not expose, queried via `container.events()? -> Option<&dyn EventPublisher>`. This makes the façade's 'superset' relationship literal: the application container is the engine with capabilities switched on, and you can build any intermediate point (engine + just events, say) — strictly more flexible than Spring's fixed two tiers, while still defaulting to 'all on' for DX. Infrastructure post-processors are themselves just beans with `role = Infrastructure`; `auto_detect_infrastructure` is a builder phase that scans the descriptor set for that role and registers them — the single switch that flips declarative features from inert to live. The eager-vs-lazy difference is a builder flag (`eager_singletons(true)` defaulted on for `application()`, off for `engine_only()`).

**Pros:** Fewer concepts for users: one `Container` type, capabilities are additive (mirrors the charter's 'additive features' and Spring's 'superset' framing without the inheritance baggage).; More flexible than Spring: any subset of context services can be attached, so an embedder can opt into exactly events without i18n, etc. — the 'opt into exactly the post-processors you want' intent generalized.; Capability queries (`events() -> Option<&dyn EventPublisher>`) make 'is this service available here?' a first-class, diagnosable runtime question — good DX and good error messages ('events requested but this container has no event layer').; Typestate builder can encode at compile time that, e.g., `with_events` was attached, giving zero-cost typed access on the static path where the concrete builder type is known.; One struct means no façade/engine surface-drift maintenance problem.

**Cons:** Drifts from Spring's named taxonomy — practitioners porting mental models lose the literal BeanFactory/ApplicationContext landmarks (intent-parity argument says that's fine, but it's a teaching cost).; A single struct carrying optional service handles tends toward `Option<Arc<...>>` fields (heap/Arc churn the charter §2.5 warns against) unless the typestate is pushed hard — and full typestate explodes builder type combinatorics.; Capability-as-Option erodes the hard guarantee Spring gives ('an ApplicationContext ALWAYS has events'); call sites must handle the None case or the framework must assert presence, reintroducing a runtime check the two-type split made structural.; Harder to express 'this is the minimal embeddable engine with provably nothing extra' — the bare config is a runtime state of a struct that still has the fields for the full feature set (footprint concern).

**Mechanisms considered:** typestate builder (compile-time capability tracking) vs runtime Option<capability> fields; role=Infrastructure descriptor filter as the single auto-detect switch; context services as queryable dyn trait capabilities (Option<&dyn Trait>) on one struct; builder flags for eager-vs-lazy and override/circular policy; proc-macro still emits the same thin const descriptor; only assembly differs

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Same descriptor/registration substrate-neutrality as R1. The capability model interacts cleanly with Cargo features: a context service (events, i18n) can live in a separate crate gated by an additive feature, and `with_events()` is only callable when that crate is linked — but this re-creates the Layer-0 trap (the service crate must be path-referenced or its registrations vanish). The builder's explicit `with_*` calls are themselves force-link references (calling `leaf_events::install(&mut builder)` forces `leaf_events` onto the link graph), which is a *pro*: explicit capability attachment doubles as the anti-DCE force-link mechanism the cross-crate doc wants, blending toward Architecture D (explicit ergonomic helpers). Infrastructure auto-detect over the descriptor slice still needs the self-check. The typestate variant complicates cross-crate APIs (the concrete builder type leaks into signatures), pushing toward `dyn`-typed boundaries at crate edges.

**T2 — execution context:** Builder steps are synchronous cold-path. The single async seam is the same eager-singleton instantiation as R1 (gated by the `eager_singletons` flag). Because context services are attachable capabilities, each service that owns background work (event multicaster's async dispatch, scheduling) registers its execution-context needs at attach time — making 'what runs where' explicit per capability rather than bundled, which the charter §2.3 favors. Capability queries are cheap synchronous lookups (an `Option` check or a typestate field access). Cancellation/shutdown must walk the attached capabilities in reverse attach order plus reverse-dependency singleton teardown; the single-struct shape makes the teardown ordering one list to drive. No global lock; per-bean creation guard as in R1.

#### R3 — Engine as a small object-safe kernel + façade composed from pluggable 'context extensions' (SPI-first)
Push unification (charter §2.11) hardest: the engine is a deliberately tiny, object-safe kernel and EVERYTHING above it — auto-detection, eager instantiation, context services, hierarchy — is expressed as ordered 'context extensions' that the façade drives in a fixed-order refresh template. The kernel `Engine` exposes only the irreducible primitives: `register_definition`, `merged_definition(name)`, `get_singleton(TypeId/name)`, `put_singleton`, `definitions_by_type(TypeId)`, and a creation-guard. No async in the kernel itself beyond a factory-invocation hook; the kernel is a passive store + index. A `ContextExtension` SPI (`async fn post_process_definitions`, `async fn register_processors`, `async fn on_refresh`, ordered by a priority field) is the single mechanism through which BOTH the framework's own infrastructure (the BFPP/BPP analogues, eager-instantiation driver, message-source init) AND user/integration features attach. The 'ApplicationContext' is then just an `Engine` + an ordered `Vec<Box<dyn ContextExtension>>` + the refresh template that runs them in Spring's documented order (load defs → run definition-rewriters → register instance-processors → init context services → eager-instantiate → smart-init). Auto-detection becomes: the refresh template scans the descriptor set for `role=Infrastructure` extensions and folds them into the extension list before running — so 'bare engine' is literally 'Engine with an empty extension list,' and 'application context' is 'Engine with the auto-detected infrastructure extensions plus user-requested ones.' This makes the BeanFactory/ApplicationContext difference reduce to *which extensions are installed*, and dogfoods the framework's own features as ordered infrastructure exactly as Spring does (post-processor-spi, refresh-lifecycle).

**Pros:** Maximal unification: one extension SPI carries auto-detection, the post-processor tiers, context-service init, and the refresh ordering — neighbours (post-processor-spi, refresh-lifecycle, events, messages) compose into the SAME machinery instead of bespoke hooks.; Tiny object-safe kernel is genuinely minimal-footprint and easy to keep stable/semver-frozen (the charter's 'small stable kernel' topology principle).; Origin-agnostic to the extreme: a WASM-module layer is just another ContextExtension that registers descriptors whose factory fn-pointers happen to call into the module — the kernel can't tell.; The fixed-order refresh template is the natural home for refresh-lifecycle's state machine and gives one place to instrument startup (startup-instrumentation) and one place where errors are caught with full context.; Extension ordering is data (priority field) resolved at runtime via topo-sort — directly matches the cross-crate doc's 'do all ordering at runtime, never link order.'

**Cons:** Most indirection: every behavior is behind a `dyn ContextExtension`, so the simple 'just get a bean' path now threads through an extension-driven refresh — heavier to reason about and to keep zero-overhead on the static path.; `Box<dyn ContextExtension>` with async methods forces boxed futures and Arc/Box churn (charter §2.5) for the extension list; the kernel staying sync helps but the façade is alloc-heavy at bring-up.; Risk of over-engineering Phase 1: a fully general extension SPI pre-judges machinery that Phase 2 is supposed to unify — borders on declaring meta-architecture if taken too literally (must stay a candidate, not a decision).; Ordering correctness across user + infrastructure + integration extensions is subtle (Spring's three-tier PriorityOrdered/Ordered/rest partition plus the BDRPP-rescan); getting the template wrong silently mis-wires features.; Harder for users to find the 'bare engine' affordance — it's an empty-extension-list special case rather than a named minimal type.

**Mechanisms considered:** object-safe sync kernel trait + Box<dyn ContextExtension> SPI for everything above; fixed-order async refresh template driving ordered extensions (priority field, runtime topo-sort); role=Infrastructure descriptors auto-folded into the extension list (auto-detection as a template phase); dyn dispatch + boxed futures on the extension SPI; kernel stays generic/zero-cost where monomorphizable; descriptor fn-pointer factories so the kernel never assumes native construction (WASM-module-ready)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for the cross-crate 'layered strategy': infrastructure extensions ship in leaf-core, integration extensions ship in their own crates and register via the same SPI, and the WASM-module 'classpath' is literally another extension installed at refresh — the two-source duality (static link-time registry PLUS optional dynamic directory-scanned source) becomes two extensions feeding the same descriptor set, NOT two code paths. Force-linking: an integration crate's extension must be referenced to be installed, so the explicit `install_extension(leaf_redis::extension())` doubles as the anti-DCE reference (Architecture D-leaning), while a link-time slice of `&'static dyn ContextExtensionFactory` could auto-collect them (Architecture A/B) — both expressible, decision deferred. The kernel/extension split keeps the proc-macro thin (emits descriptors only) and keeps the ABI surface (`Descriptor` + `ContextExtension` trait) in leaf-core, minimal and stable. Self-check over expected infrastructure extensions guards the silent-DCE failure.

**T2 — execution context:** The kernel is fully synchronous (passive store/index) — no execution-context ambiguity there. ALL async lives in the refresh template and the `ContextExtension::on_refresh`/`post_process_definitions` methods, each of which declares its execution context explicitly (the template documents: definition-rewrite extensions run sequentially on the cold startup path; eager-instantiation may fan out construction futures but awaits them before refresh completes — ties to background-bootstrap for opt-in concurrent init). Extension futures are boxed (dyn SPI). Cancellation of refresh tears down in reverse extension order then reverse-dependency singletons (no async Drop → explicit `shutdown().await` driven by the template, the symmetric context-close path). Errors from any extension abort refresh with the full causal chain attributed to the named extension+phase (§2.9) — the fixed template gives a precise 'failed in phase X of extension Y' diagnostic. No global lock; the kernel's creation-guard is the only synchronization and is per-bean, not container-wide.

### Rust clashes
- GC'd shared singletons vs Rust ownership: Spring beans are GC-rooted and freely aliased; Rust must pick a sharing model. `Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync>` in the singleton cache is the obvious candidate but commits to reference counting (charter §2.5 wants minimal Arc) and to `Send+Sync` bounds that not every bean satisfies. The scope/ownership model is an explicit Phase 2 keystone — this design must store beans behind SOME erased handle without prejudging whether it's Arc, an arena index, or a borrow.
- No runtime reflection / no classpath: Spring auto-detects post-processors by walking loaded types for an interface. Rust has no such scan — auto-detection must be a runtime filter over a *pre-collected* descriptor set whose completeness depends on a fragile link-time/codegen substrate (the entire cross-crate doc). 'Auto-detect by type' becomes 'mark role=Infrastructure on the descriptor and filter,' and the marking happens at the declaration site, not by introspection.
- Async fn in traits is not dyn-compatible: the BeanFactory client surface wants `async fn get<T>()` AND wants to be a trait object (so the engine is replaceable / the WASM layer slots in). RPITIT/AFIT traits can't be dyn — so the dynamic path must box futures (alloc per resolution). Zero-cost async + dyn dispatch can't both hold on the same surface on stable; the design must split a zero-cost monomorphized typed path from a boxed dyn path.
- No async Drop on stable: Spring's DisposableBean/@PreDestroy destruction is mirror-image of init and the container drives it. Rust has no async Drop, so context-close/teardown of async-cleanup beans cannot ride `Drop` — it must be an explicit container-driven `shutdown().await`. This makes 'the container owns the lifecycle' a hard, non-syntactic discipline (charter calls this a first-class design constraint).
- Eager-instantiation fail-fast vs Rust's lazy/typed nature: Spring eagerly builds all non-lazy singletons at refresh to fail fast. In Rust, eager construction of type-erased beans means awaiting a pile of boxed futures and downcast-checking — the fail-fast benefit (rich boot-time errors) fights the cost of constructing everything up front; some of leaf's validation could move to compile/codegen time (charter §2.9 prefers the richest-diagnostic timing), partially dissolving the need for runtime eager-build as the only error-surfacing mechanism.
- Generics can't be registered / monomorphization erases identity: Spring registers any class by name including generic ones. A generic `Component<T>` has no single runtime symbol for link-time collection and no `TypeId` until instantiated — so the engine's type index and any auto-discovery must register *concrete instantiations only*, with a per-instantiation shim, unlike Spring's by-name generics.
- Name-keyed vs type-keyed identity: Spring's BeanFactory is fundamentally `String`-name-keyed with a type index layered on. Rust strongly prefers `TypeId`-keyed lookup (typed, zero-string-overhead) but Spring semantics (aliases, name collisions as loud errors, name-keyed Map injection, & dereference) need the name layer too — the engine must carry BOTH a name map and a TypeId index and keep them coherent, which Spring gets implicitly from the JVM.

### Interactions
- **bean-registry** — IS the engine layer of this feature. container-core defines the BeanFactory/engine boundary; bean-registry fills in the definition store, singleton-cache, and candidate-resolution internals the engine exposes. The HAS-A composition (façade owns one registry) is the load-bearing contract between them.
- **bean-definition** — The engine stores and merges these. container-core consumes the BeanDefinition metamodel (incl. parent→merged-definition) as the input the engine turns into instances; defines where 'merged definition' is computed (engine.merged_definition).
- **post-processor-spi** — The reason the two-layer split exists. Auto-detection of BFPP/BPP-equivalents by role is THE behavior that distinguishes façade from bare engine; container-core provides the registration slots and the auto-detect-by-role pass, post-processor-spi defines the processor contracts that fill them.
- **refresh-lifecycle** — Owns the fixed-order template that container-core's façade runs in refresh() (load defs → BFPPs → BPPs → init services → eager-instantiate → smart-init). container-core provides the engine+façade these phases operate over; R3 makes the template the central organizing structure.
- **bean-instantiation** — The engine's get/eager-instantiate drives instantiation; container-core defines the singleton cache and per-bean creation guard (no global lock) that bean-instantiation publishes into, including the three-level/early-reference cache hooks for cycles.
- **container-hierarchy** — The façade carries the optional parent link; container-core defines that lookups delegate upward (child sees parent, local shadows, containsLocalBean ignores ancestors) while listing stays local — the engine is hierarchy-aware on resolution, hierarchy-blind on enumeration.
- **autowiring-resolution** — The engine exposes definitions_by_type / candidate sets; autowiring-resolution decides the winner. container-core supplies the type index and name map it queries but delegates the fail-fast layered policy itself.
- **lazy-initialization** — The eager-vs-lazy fail-fast distinction (bare engine lazy, context eager at refresh) is set here; lazy-initialization toggles individual beans out of the eager pass — a per-definition flag the eager-instantiate phase honors.
- **context-close** — Symmetric teardown of the façade: because no async Drop, container-core must expose an explicit shutdown().await that context-close drives (stop Lifecycle beans, then destroy singletons in reverse-dependency order).
- **events** — One of the context services the façade layers on (ApplicationEventPublisher analogue). container-core decides whether events are an always-present façade service (R1) or an attachable capability (R2/R3).
- **messages-i18n** — MessageSource is an ApplicationContext-only, hierarchy-aware context service the façade exposes — same attach question as events; the magic-named-bean resolution lives on the façade, not the bare engine.
- **resource-loading** — ResourceLoader/ResourcePatternResolver is another façade-exposed context service; container-core defines it as a context-layer capability the bare engine lacks.
- **environment** — EnvironmentCapable is part of the façade superset; container-core wires the Environment handle into the context so conditions/value-injection/profiles can read it during refresh.
- **programmatic-registration** — The BeanRegistrar-equivalent imperative/conditional registration path writes into the engine's register_definition SPI; container-core must expose that SPI as a stable public API (the escape hatch per charter §2.10).
- **smart-initializing** — The single lock-free callback after all eager singletons exist is fired by container-core's refresh at the end of the eager-instantiate phase, explicitly outside any creation guard.
- **concurrency-contract** — container-core's singleton cache is where safe-publication of fully-built singletons is guaranteed; the per-bean creation guard + Arc publication is the realization of the 'visibility of fully-built singletons' contract.
- **factory-bean** — The engine must distinguish a factory bean from its product (the & dereference escape hatch) in its name/type resolution — container-core's get path branches on factory-bean identity.
- **auto-proxy-creator** — AOP attaches as a BPP-equivalent auto-detected by the façade; the proxy-creator is exactly the kind of infrastructure bean whose role=Infrastructure auto-detection is the whole point of the two-layer split.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Registry substrate (deferred §3): is the singleton cache `Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync>` keyed by TypeId, an arena-indexed handle, or something else? This drives whether the engine's stored-bean type is Send+Sync-bounded and whether the WASM-module origin can produce the same handle. The whole engine API shape depends on this choice.
- Scope/lifetime/ownership (deferred §3, the keystone): do singletons live as Arc (commits to refcount, fights §2.5), as owned-by-the-container values handed out as borrows, or per-scope arenas? container-core's get<T> return type (Arc<T> vs &T vs a handle) cannot be settled until this is.
- Does the BeanFactory/ApplicationContext SPLIT survive as two named types (R1) or collapse into one capability-configured Container (R2) or kernel+extensions (R3)? This is a unification call that needs the whole Phase 1 catalog to judge — many neighbours assume one shape.
- Codegen/metadata boundary (deferred §3): are descriptors emitted by proc-macro into a link-time slice, by build.rs codegen, or assembled at runtime — and is auto-detection of infrastructure therefore a link-time-collected set, a generated table, or a runtime scan? Auto-detection completeness (the silent-DCE risk) depends entirely on this.
- Default cross-crate substrate for collecting descriptors on server targets — linkme distributed-slice (A/B) vs build-time codegen (C) vs explicit with() (D) — and how 'which crates participate' is expressed. container-core is substrate-neutral by design, but the façade's auto-detect/force-link/self-check behavior must bind to whichever is chosen.
- Async surface across the dyn boundary: does leaf accept boxed futures on the dyn BeanFactory path (alloc per resolution) as the standard, or push a monomorphized zero-cost typed path as primary with dyn as the escape hatch? This is the async-execution-model decision (deferred §3) and shapes whether hot-path scoped lookups can avoid allocation.
- Is there exactly ONE engine implementation (Spring's single DefaultListableBeanFactory) or is the engine itself a trait with multiple impls? Making it a trait helps the WASM/test-double origin-agnosticism but costs the zero-cost monomorphized path and complicates the HAS-A composition.
- How is per-bean lifecycle (Aware → init → destroy) modeled given no async Drop — an explicit shutdown().await driven by the container is forced, but the exact destruction-ordering data structure and its interaction with reverse-dependency teardown is cross-cutting with context-close, lifecycle-callbacks, and runtime-lifecycle.
- Eager-instantiation as fail-fast vs moving validation to compile/startup-validation time: §2.9 prefers the richest-diagnostic timing — how much of 'eagerly build everything to surface errors' can be replaced by a build-time or dedicated startup validation pass over descriptors, reducing the runtime eager-build cost?
- Name-vs-type identity: is the engine primarily TypeId-keyed with a name layer, or name-keyed with a type index (Spring's model)? Aliases, name collisions, name-keyed Map injection, and & dereference all need the name layer; the coherence contract between the two keying schemes is unresolved and touches bean-naming, collection-injection, and factory-bean.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent (Spring section is explicit and I read it fully); the three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters for THIS feature — the shape of the engine/façade boundary (two types / one capability-configured type / kernel+extension-SPI) — while deliberately staying substrate-neutral on the cross-crate registration mechanism (that is a separate, cross-cutting Phase 2 decision surfaced in phase2Questions, not re-litigated per realization). I resisted picking the meta-architecture: every realization is compatible with any of cross-crate Architectures A/B/C/D and with any registry/scope substrate, by keeping descriptors as thin const fn-pointer factories and keeping the stored-bean handle abstract.

Three things I want the reader to carry forward: (1) The single most important behavior to preserve is auto-detection of infrastructure post-processors — it is literally why declarative features work and why a bare engine is inert; in Rust this MUST become a role-marked descriptor + runtime filter, and it inherits the silent-DCE risk, so a façade self-check (expected-vs-found infrastructure count, failing loudly) is non-negotiable regardless of realization. (2) The origin-agnostic factory fn-pointer (`fn(&dyn BeanFactory) -> BoxFuture<Result<ErasedHandle,_>>`) is the seam that keeps a future WASM-module 'classpath' slot-in-able — I treated it as a fixed constraint across all three. (3) Two unresolved Rust clashes dominate everything downstream and belong to Phase 2: the scope/ownership/handle type (what get<T> returns) and the async-across-dyn boxing tradeoff — I refused to settle either and they appear as phase2Questions.

Naming note: I used 'Engine' for the DefaultListableBeanFactory analogue and 'ApplicationContext'/'Container' for the façade purely as Phase-1 placeholders; final names are a later concern. The duality the brief asked me to flag (static link-time registry as the default 'classpath' PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module 'classpath') is captured as two descriptor sources feeding ONE registration API — most explicit in R3 where each is just a ContextExtension — and left as candidates, not a decision.

I did not write any files; this is the complete Phase-1 design for container-core. Relevant source paths I grounded against (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (lines 104-232 the two-layer section, 330-369 refresh template, 2434-2555 the HAS-A engine detail), /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred §3), /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/A/B DCE, Architectures A-D), /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (stable envelope: trait upcasting 1.86, RPITIT/AFIT 1.75 dyn-incompat, Arc<dyn Any> registry, no async Drop).

## BeanDefinition Metamodel  `[bean-definition]`

Spring represents every bean as a `BeanDefinition` — a mutable metamodel object recording how to create and configure ONE bean (class, scope, lazy, depends-on, autowire-candidate flag, primary/fallback, factory-bean/factory-method, constructor-arg values, property values, init/destroy methods, role hint, description, parentName + abstract flag, qualifiers) — strictly BEFORE any instance exists. This decouples *description* from *instantiation* and yields the load-bearing two-phase model: (1) parse/register/REWRITE all definitions, then (2) post-process and instantiate. That decoupling is the structural reason the framework is extensible (BFPPs rewrite blueprints before any object is built) and format-agnostic (annotations/Java-config/programmatic all collapse to the same metamodel). Three hidden mechanics matter for parity: (a) the MERGED definition — a child inherits from a parent template via parentName; at creation time the engine computes a recomputed, parent-flattened, read-only `RootBeanDefinition` copy (cached in a map, invalidated by setting `stale=true` not by removal; frozen the first time the bean is touched via markBeanAsCreated, which is the implicit 'no more BFPP edits' boundary) — and it is the MERGED def, never the raw registered one, that drives creation; the merge rule is asymmetric (child takes depends-on/autowire/scope-from-child/lazy from itself; inherits constructor args, property values, method overrides from the parent; abstract=true parents are pure templates never instantiated). (b) role hints (APPLICATION/SUPPORT/INFRASTRUCTURE) — mostly metadata for tooling/diagnostics and certain selection logic, the marker that lets the framework distinguish user beans from its own plumbing. (c) primary/fallback flags carried on the definition as autowire tie-breakers (fallback is the inverse of primary: chosen only when no non-fallback matches, letting a library default be silently superseded). leaf must replicate this INTENT — an editable, instance-decoupled, format-agnostic per-bean metadata record that supports parent→merged flattening, role/primary/fallback/lazy/scope/depends-on/qualifier metadata, and a rewrite window that closes deterministically before instantiation — WITHOUT importing Spring's reflective string-typed property-bag mechanism, which Rust's lack of class-by-name and field reflection makes both impossible and unnecessary. The construction recipe is the key impedance point: Spring's reflective `beanClassName`+`constructorArgumentValues` becomes, in Rust, a typed factory (fn-pointer/closure) the macro emits — so 'rewrite the args' must be reframed as 'rewrite the metadata the engine and the factory read', not 'reflectively poke setters'.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Mutable owned struct with explicit overlay-merge + a freeze gate (literal Spring metamodel, rust-owned)
Mirror Spring's metamodel directly as one owned, mutable Rust struct, but replace reflective fields with typed handles. `BeanDefinition { name: BeanName, type_id: TypeId, produced_type: TypeId (getObjectType analogue, may differ from the bean's own type for factory beans), scope: Scope, lazy: Option<bool>, depends_on: Vec<BeanName>, autowire_candidate: bool, primary: bool, fallback: bool, role: Role, description: Option<&'static str>, qualifiers: Vec<Qualifier>, init: Option<LifecycleHook>, destroy: Option<LifecycleHook>, parent: Option<BeanName>, is_abstract: bool, factory: Factory }` where `Factory` is an ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC erased async constructor (`fn(&dyn ResolveCtx) -> BoxFuture<Result<ErasedBean, ResolveError>>` — never assumes native construction, so a WASM-module factory or a test double satisfies the same shape). The macro emits a `const`/near-const seed (a `RawBeanDefinition` descriptor) that is the only hand-writable artifact; at registration the engine lifts it into the owned, mutable `BeanDefinition`. Rewriting (the BFPP analogue, owned by post-processor-spi) is `&mut BeanDefinition` mutation during the cold definition phase. Parent→merged is an explicit `overlay(parent, child) -> MergedBeanDefinition` function: child-wins for the asymmetric fields, parent-supplies for the inheritable ones, exactly Spring's rule, producing a SEPARATE `MergedBeanDefinition` type (a distinct, read-only struct — the type system encodes 'merged is not the registered one'). A `FreezeToken`/state flag closes the rewrite window: `freeze()` consumes mutable access and yields an immutable `Arc<MergedBeanDefinition>` (or owned, per Phase-2 ownership decision) the engine instantiates from. Re-registration under a name is a loud error by default (allow_override toggle) — bean-naming's collision contract lives partly here.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: the BeanDefinition-vs-merged distinction and the rewrite-then-freeze window are structural, so the two-phase model and 'merged drives creation, not the raw def' fall out directly and are teachable to Spring users.; Mutability during the cold phase is honest and ergonomic for the BFPP/BDRPP rewrite story (post-processor-spi gets a plain `&mut BeanDefinition`), matching Spring's 'rewrite blueprints before any object is built' with zero ceremony.; Distinct `MergedBeanDefinition` type makes the 'recomputed read-only copy' a compile-time guarantee, not a runtime convention — a MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor (post-processor-spi) receives `&MergedBeanDefinition`, can never accidentally mutate the registered one.; Origin-agnostic Factory handle keeps the recipe abstract: the metamodel never assumes native construction, so a future WASM-module 'classpath' contributes definitions whose factory dispatches into the module, indistinguishable to the engine.; Freeze gate gives a precise, diagnosable boundary ('definition X was edited after freeze') — directly serves the rich-diagnostics invariant and mirrors markBeanAsCreated.

**Cons:** Mutable owned struct + Vec fields lean on heap (Vec<Qualifier>, Vec<BeanName>) and, if merged defs are shared, Arc — exactly the heap/Arc churn the charter §2.5 warns against; needs care (SmallVec / arena / interned names) to stay disciplined.; An explicit `overlay()` merge function must be hand-maintained field-by-field; adding a metamodel field means updating the merge rule — drift risk (a forgotten field silently doesn't inherit), needing a test or a derive to keep merge total.; Mutation-based rewriting is inherently a runtime, non-AOT-analyzable edit; it preserves Spring's dynamism but fights the AOT/build-time-determinism lineage the Spring doc itself now leans toward (definitions knowable at build time).; Freeze enforced at runtime (a flag/state) not at the type level for the mutable phase — a BFPP could in principle hold a `&mut` across freeze unless the API is shaped to consume access; getting the lifetime discipline right is fiddly.; Two named types (raw `BeanDefinition` + `MergedBeanDefinition`) plus a `RawBeanDefinition` seed = three representations of 'the metadata' to keep coherent.

**Mechanisms considered:** proc-macro emits one thin const `RawBeanDefinition` seed (name/TypeId/role/flags + factory fn-pointer); engine lifts to owned mutable BeanDefinition; origin-agnostic erased async Factory fn-pointer (`fn(&dyn ResolveCtx)->BoxFuture<Result<ErasedBean,_>>`) as the construction recipe — never reflective; explicit `overlay(parent,child)` merge fn producing a distinct read-only `MergedBeanDefinition` type; runtime freeze gate / state flag (markBeanAsCreated analogue) closing the BFPP rewrite window; interned BeanName + SmallVec to bound heap churn from Vec metadata fields; allow_override toggle on registration; loud collision error by default (Boot semantics)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Substrate-neutral on collection: the same owned `BeanDefinition`/`RawBeanDefinition` shape works whether seeds arrive via linkme distributed-slice (Arch A/B), build.rs codegen (Arch C), or explicit `with()` (Arch D). What it FIXES cross-crate: contributing crates depend only on leaf-core (where `RawBeanDefinition`, `MergedBeanDefinition`, `Role`, `Factory`, `Qualifier` live) and the macro emits absolute-path `::leaf_core::RawBeanDefinition { .. }` seeds — thin, stateless, hand-writable (charter §2.10). Generics cannot be registered (no symbol/TypeId until instantiated) → the macro must hard-error on generic beans and offer a per-instantiation `register_bean!(ConcreteType)`; the metamodel stores only concrete TypeIds. The merge/overlay computation is pure runtime data manipulation in leaf-core, immune to DCE. Definitions added by a programmatic registrar (programmatic-registration) or a BDRPP write into the same owned-struct API — one registration surface for static + dynamic + WASM-module sources (the static-link-time registry PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned source are just two producers of the same `RawBeanDefinition`; CANDIDATES, not a decision). Layer-0/silent-DCE risk is inherited from whatever collection substrate Phase 2 picks; a self-check counting expected-vs-found definitions per contributing crate is the required defense.

**T2 — execution context:** Definition construction, registration, mutation/rewrite, and merge/overlay are ALL synchronous, cold-path, single-threaded — they run in the definition phase before the bean graph is built (typically in/around `main` before heavy runtime work; const seeds need no pre-main code). NO async and NO blocking in the metamodel itself — the only async is the `Factory` it CARRIES, which is invoked later by the instantiation engine (bean-instantiation), not by the metamodel. The freeze gate is a synchronous state transition; after freeze, merged defs are read-only and safely shareable (publication of immutable data — no lock needed once frozen; concurrent readers see a stable value, matching Spring's stale-flag lock-light strategy without a global lock per §2.5). Errors during the definition phase (bad parent reference, abstract-parent-instantiation attempt, post-freeze edit, name collision) surface as a rich definition-phase error (`DefinitionError` carrying name + parent chain + what was expected) BEFORE any instance exists — the earliest-richest-diagnostic timing (§2.9), the metamodel's analogue of BeanDefinitionStoreException. No cancellation concern (sync cold path); teardown is N/A (definitions hold no resources, just metadata + a fn-pointer).

#### R2 — Immutable layered definition (persistent overlay stack); 'rewrite' = push a new layer, 'merge' = collapse the stack
Question whether the metamodel needs to be MUTABLE at all. Spring's mutability exists because reflective property-bag editing was the only tool; the INTENT is 'the effective definition can be revised before instantiation,' which an immutable layered model serves better in Rust. A `BeanDefinition` is an immutable base layer (emitted by the macro as a `const` seed). Every revision — a BFPP rewrite, a parent-template contribution, a profile/condition adjustment — is a separate immutable `DefinitionLayer` (a sparse patch: `Option`-per-field overrides + qualifier add/remove ops) pushed onto an ordered `DefinitionStack` keyed by bean name. There is NO in-place mutation; rewriting is `stack.push(layer)`. The 'merged definition' is then literally `stack.collapse() -> MergedBeanDefinition`: fold layers in order (base → parent-template-as-a-layer → rewrite layers), child/last-wins per the asymmetric Spring rule, producing the read-only effective def the engine instantiates from. Parent inheritance is just 'the parent's collapsed definition is inserted as a low-priority layer under the child's.' Freeze = the stack is sealed (no more pushes) and collapsed once; the collapsed result is cached and the layers can be dropped. Provenance is free: each layer carries WHO contributed it (which BFPP, which parent, which profile), so a diagnostic can show 'scope=prototype came from layer 3 (LazyInitProcessor)' — origin-tracking-style provenance for the blueprint, not just for config values.

**Pros:** No mutable shared state and no freeze-race: layers are immutable, the stack is append-only on the cold path, collapse is a pure fold — squarely inside the no-global-locks/memory-discipline invariant (§2.5) and trivially safe to read concurrently once sealed.; Rewriting-as-layers makes BFPP edits AUDITABLE: every change is an attributable layer, so 'why does this bean look like this?' is answerable by listing the stack — a strictly better diagnostic story (§2.9) than Spring's destructive in-place mutation where the original is lost.; Parent→merged unifies with rewriting: inheritance is 'parent collapsed = a layer', so ONE collapse algorithm handles both parent-flattening AND post-processor rewriting (charter §2.11 unification) — no separate overlay() vs merge() code.; Closer to the AOT lineage: an immutable base + a known set of layers is analyzable; collapse could be done at build time to emit a frozen effective definition (the Spring v7 'definitions knowable at build time' direction), while still allowing runtime layers where needed.; Sparse-patch layers can be cheap (mostly `None` fields, a few overrides) and the collapsed result can be interned/arena-allocated, avoiding per-bean Arc.

**Cons:** More machinery than Spring users expect: 'definition' is now a base + a stack, not one object — a conceptual translation cost for those porting the literal `BeanDefinition.setScope()` mental model.; A sparse `Option`-per-field patch layer is verbose to define and, for a wide metamodel, the layer struct is large (many `Option`s); qualifier add/remove ops need their own little patch algebra (add vs remove vs replace) to be expressive.; Collapse cost is paid per bean at freeze; for many beans with deep stacks this is more work than a single mutable struct (though still cold-path and cheap relative to instantiation).; Layer ordering must be deterministic and well-specified (which layer wins) — if a BFPP's layer and a parent layer disagree, the priority rule must be unambiguous, recreating some of Spring's ordered-post-processor subtlety inside the collapse.; Holding layers + provenance until freeze costs transient memory vs in-place edit; must be dropped after collapse to reclaim.

**Mechanisms considered:** immutable const base `BeanDefinition` from a thin macro; revisions as separate immutable `DefinitionLayer` patches; append-only ordered `DefinitionStack` per bean name (no in-place mutation, no lock on the cold path); pure `collapse()` fold producing a read-only `MergedBeanDefinition`; parent inheritance modeled as a low-priority layer (unifies merge + rewrite); per-layer provenance handle (contributing processor/parent/profile) for blueprint-level diagnostics (origin-tracking-style); sparse Option-per-field patch + a qualifier add/remove patch algebra; build-time collapse option (emit frozen effective definition) aligning with the AOT lineage

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Same substrate-neutrality as R1 for collection (linkme/codegen/explicit all emit the immutable base seed). The layered model interacts especially well with multiple definition SOURCES: a static link-time registry, a programmatic registrar, AND an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source each contribute LAYERS (or base defs) to the same per-name stack — the duality the brief asks me to flag becomes 'N producers pushing onto one stack', not N code paths (CANDIDATES, not a decision; most natural fit of the three realizations for the two-source duality). Macro stays maximally thin (emits an immutable const base only; no merge logic ever in the macro). Generics still cannot be registered (hard-error in macro). Provenance per layer pairs perfectly with the mandatory anti-DCE self-check: the self-check can report not just counts but WHICH source contributed each layer, turning a silently-dropped contribution into a legible 'expected a layer from leaf_redis, found none'. Immutable layers are also friendlier to a future `#[global_registration]` opaque-iterator world (order-independent base seeds; ordering encoded as data on layers and resolved at collapse, per the cross-crate doc's 'never rely on registration order').

**T2 — execution context:** All layer construction, pushing, and collapse are synchronous cold-path operations in the definition phase — no async, no blocking, identical to R1 in that the metamodel carries but never invokes the async Factory. Append-only stack means writers don't contend (each push is to a per-name structure on the single-threaded cold path; if parallel definition loading is ever wanted, per-name append is naturally shardable without a global lock). Collapse is a pure function → referentially transparent, freely parallelizable across beans if desired (cold-path fan-out is acceptable per §2.4; not required). Once sealed+collapsed, the `MergedBeanDefinition` is immutable and shared without synchronization (safe publication of read-only data). Errors (a layer referencing a missing parent, a collapse producing an invalid def such as abstract-but-requested-for-instantiation, contradictory required fields) surface at collapse time with FULL layered provenance — the richest possible blueprint diagnostic (§2.9): not just 'scope invalid' but 'scope set to X by layer from processor Y, conflicting with base'. No cancellation/teardown concern (pure data, cold path).

#### R3 — Thin core record + open typed attribute-map ('extras') for extensibility; merge is per-attribute-policy-driven
Split the metamodel into a SMALL closed core of universally-needed fields and an OPEN, typed, heterogeneous attribute map for everything else — mirroring Spring's `AbstractBeanDefinition.setAttribute(name, value)` / `getAttribute()` extensibility surface (qualifiers, OBJECT_TYPE_ATTRIBUTE, AOT targetType hints, arbitrary BFPP-stashed metadata all ride this in Spring) but typed instead of `Object`-keyed-by-String. Core `BeanDefinition { name, type_id, produced_type, scope, lazy, role, primary, fallback, autowire_candidate, parent, is_abstract, factory }` — the irreducible fields the engine itself reads. Everything else — qualifiers, depends-on, init/destroy hooks, description, condition metadata, AOP role hints, scheduling/validation/tx markers from cross-cutting features — lives in `Attributes`, a `TypeMap` (`HashMap<TypeId, Box<dyn DefinitionAttribute>>` or a typed slot vector) where each attribute is a typed value implementing a `DefinitionAttribute` trait that DECLARES ITS OWN MERGE POLICY (`fn merge(parent: &Self, child: &Self) -> Self` — child-wins, union, parent-wins, or replace). This makes the metamodel OPEN for extension by downstream/integration features without touching leaf-core: a tx or cache feature attaches its own typed attribute and defines how it inherits across parent/child, exactly the extensibility Spring buys from the string attribute bag — but type-safe and with a principled, per-attribute merge rule instead of one monolithic overlay() that must know every field. Core fields merge by the fixed Spring rule; attributes merge by their own declared policy. Rewriting can be in-place (R1-style) or layered (R2-style) — orthogonal to this axis; R3 is about the SHAPE/EXTENSIBILITY of the record, deliberately divergent from R1/R2 which fix a closed schema.

**Pros:** Open for extension (charter §2.11 unification + §6 'optional features as separate crates'): cross-cutting features (tx/cache/validation/scheduling/AOP) and integration crates attach typed definition metadata WITHOUT editing leaf-core's BeanDefinition — the metamodel is a stable kernel, features layer on, exactly the topology principle.; Per-attribute declared merge policy dissolves R1's biggest weakness (one giant overlay() that must hand-handle every field and silently drifts) — each attribute owns its inheritance semantics, so adding metadata can't break the merge of unrelated metadata; the merge is total by construction.; Closest analogue to Spring's actual `setAttribute`/qualifier-bag extensibility AND to OBJECT_TYPE_ATTRIBUTE / AOT setTargetType hints — those map naturally onto typed attributes rather than special-casing them into the core.; Keeps the core record tiny and semver-stable (the irreducible engine-read fields), which is good for the cross-crate ABI the macro hard-codes paths to (cross-crate doc wants leaf-core's registration ABI minimal and ultra-stable).; A `DefinitionAttribute` trait is a single unification primitive that ALSO gives diagnostics a uniform way to enumerate/describe all metadata on a bean (each attribute can `describe()` itself for error reports).

**Cons:** `HashMap<TypeId, Box<dyn DefinitionAttribute>>` is heap + dyn + per-attribute boxing on EVERY definition — the most allocation-heavy of the three, directly in tension with §2.5 (stack-over-heap, minimal boxing); mitigations (small-slot inline vector, only-when-present) add complexity.; Type-erased attributes need downcast on every read (`attributes.get::<Qualifiers>()`), reintroducing a runtime lookup + TypeId dance where R1/R2 have a plain typed field — a small DX and overhead cost on a hot-ish definition-read path.; Over-openness risk: 'anyone can stash anything' can fragment what should be first-class (e.g. qualifiers and depends-on are arguably core to autowiring-resolution/candidate-resolver and belong in the closed core, not the bag) — the core-vs-attribute boundary is a judgment call that, if wrong, either bloats the core or hides important fields.; A `dyn DefinitionAttribute` with a `merge` method is a trait-object self-merging pattern; getting the trait object-safe AND able to merge two of itself (needs `&dyn` + downcast of the other side, or an associated-type dance) is fiddly and may force `Any`-bound gymnastics.; Harder to make const/AOT-friendly: a heterogeneous boxed map is not a `const` seed, so the macro emits builder calls rather than a flat const struct — slightly less thin, less analyzable than R1/R2's flat records.

**Mechanisms considered:** small closed core record + open typed `Attributes` map (TypeMap: HashMap<TypeId, Box<dyn DefinitionAttribute>> or inline typed-slot vector); `DefinitionAttribute` trait carrying a per-attribute `merge(parent,child)` policy (child-wins / union / parent-wins / replace) — total merge by construction; typed attributes for qualifiers, depends-on, init/destroy, description, produced-type/AOT-target-type hints, and downstream cross-cutting markers (tx/cache/validation); Any + downcast for typed attribute access; trait upcasting where attributes share a supertrait; macro emits builder-style attribute attachment (less const than R1/R2) — the cost of openness; orthogonal to mutable-vs-layered rewrite (composes with R1 or R2 for the edit model)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** BEST extensibility story across crates: an integration/cross-cutting crate (leaf-tx, leaf-cache, a future leaf-wasm) defines its OWN `DefinitionAttribute` impl in its own crate and attaches it to definitions it cares about — no edit to leaf-core, no widening of the core ABI, dependencies still point inward (charter §6, no cycles). The core record + `DefinitionAttribute` trait are the only ABI in leaf-core the macro hard-codes paths to, kept minimal. Same generics-cannot-be-registered constraint (macro hard-errors). The trade is that the metamodel is harder to emit as a flat `const` (heterogeneous boxed map), so on the linkme/const-seed path (Arch A/B) the macro emits a const CORE seed plus registration-time attribute attachment, while on the codegen path (Arch C) the generated code can attach attributes explicitly — both work, substrate decision deferred. The open map also means the anti-DCE self-check must verify not just that definitions exist but that EXPECTED attributes attached (a silently-dropped attribute-contributing crate yields a bean missing its tx/cache metadata — a new silent-failure surface this realization introduces, requiring the self-check to be attribute-aware).

**T2 — execution context:** Core fields and attribute attachment/merge are synchronous cold-path, as in R1/R2; the metamodel still only CARRIES the async Factory, never runs it. The added execution-context consideration is the typed-attribute downcast/merge: these are synchronous pure computations on the definition phase, but the per-attribute `merge` runs user/feature-supplied code (a downstream crate's merge impl) on the cold definition path — that code MUST be pure, non-blocking, non-async (it manipulates metadata only), which the `DefinitionAttribute` trait should document/constrain (no async in merge — it's a blueprint fold, mirroring the rule that life-before-main and definition-phase code never runs async/IO). Once merged + frozen, the effective definition (core + collapsed attributes) is immutable and shared without locks. Errors from a faulty attribute merge (e.g. two incompatible tx attributes) surface at merge time, attributed to the specific attribute type via its `describe()` — rich, localized diagnostics (§2.9). No cancellation/teardown (pure data); the boxed attributes are dropped synchronously when the definition is dropped (plain Drop, no async-Drop concern since attributes hold metadata, not live resources).

### Rust clashes
- Reflective property-bag vs typed factory: Spring's `BeanDefinition` records `beanClassName: String` + `constructorArgumentValues` and the engine reflectively instantiates and pokes setters. Rust has no class-by-name and no runtime field reflection — the construction recipe MUST be a typed factory (fn-pointer/closure) the macro emits, not editable arg metadata. So 'rewrite the constructor args' (a routine BFPP operation in Spring) does NOT translate: you cannot reflectively change what a typed factory does. leaf must reframe rewriting as 'edit the METADATA the engine + factory read' (scope, lazy, qualifiers, which collaborator name resolves an injection point) — the factory itself is opaque and fixed at the declaration site. This narrows what a BFPP-analogue can rewrite compared to Spring; some Spring rewrites (placeholder-resolved constructor args) move to resolve-time inputs the factory reads, not definition edits.
- Mutability vs ownership/immutability: Spring's metamodel is freely mutable and the merged copy is recomputed with a stale-flag, lock-light cache. Rust strongly prefers immutability + clear ownership; a mutable shared `BeanDefinition` either needs interior mutability (locks/cells the charter §2.5 discourages) or a clear single-owner-during-cold-phase discipline. The 'who may mutate and when does the window close' (Spring's markBeanAsCreated freeze) must become an explicit ownership/state transition (a freeze gate or an append-only layered model), not an implicit side effect — and it must be a per-bean concern, never a global lock.
- getObjectType / produced-type ambiguity: Spring's `getObjectType()` lets a definition declare what type it produces WITHOUT instantiating (returning null = invisible to autowiring; OBJECT_TYPE_ATTRIBUTE / setTargetType declare it statically for AOT/factory beans). In Rust the bean's own type is a TypeId, but for a factory-bean the PRODUCED type differs and must be known for type-based resolution before construction — so the metamodel needs BOTH the defining TypeId and a declared produced TypeId, and the macro must capture the produced type at the declaration site (no runtime predictBeanType reflection available). Unknown-produced-type (Spring's null) becomes a typed 'opaque product' state that excludes the bean from type resolution gracefully.
- Generics cannot be a registered definition: Spring registers any class by name including generic ones and resolves type variables at runtime via ResolvableType. A generic `Component<T>` has no `'static` symbol and no TypeId until instantiated, so it cannot have a single registered BeanDefinition — the metamodel can only hold CONCRETE instantiations, with the macro hard-erroring on generic beans and a per-instantiation `register_bean!(ConcreteType)` escape hatch. Spring's generic-type-as-implicit-qualifier (candidate-resolver) must therefore key off concrete TypeIds, not open generic definitions.
- Parent template inheritance semantics don't map to typed factories cleanly: Spring's child inherits constructor args / property values / method overrides from the parent and the engine reflectively applies them. With typed factories, 'inherit constructor args from parent' has no reflective meaning — a child's factory is its own typed closure. So parent→merged in leaf can only meaningfully inherit the METADATA fields (scope, qualifiers, lazy, role, init/destroy hooks, autowire-candidate, primary/fallback) per the asymmetric rule, NOT the construction recipe itself. The 'abstract parent template never instantiated' concept survives (a parent with no factory = pure metadata template); a parent that DOES carry a factory and isn't abstract is instantiable, preserving Spring's abstract=true footgun in typed form.
- String-keyed attribute extensibility vs typed slots: Spring's `setAttribute(String, Object)` open bag (carrying qualifiers, OBJECT_TYPE_ATTRIBUTE, AOT hints, arbitrary BFPP state) relies on untyped Object + String keys — anathema to Rust's typing. Replicating the EXTENSIBILITY (downstream features attaching definition metadata) without the untyped-ness forces either a closed schema (loses open extension) or a typed heterogeneous map (TypeId-keyed, boxed, downcast — heap/dyn cost). This is a genuine open-vs-closed tension with no free answer (R1/R2 closed, R3 open).
- No async Drop / definitions-as-pure-data is actually a relief here: unlike most container features, a BeanDefinition holds only metadata + an opaque factory fn-pointer — NO live resources — so it sidesteps the no-async-Drop clash that plagues instances. But the Factory it carries must be an erased async fn-pointer (RPITIT/AFIT traits aren't dyn-compatible), so the recipe handle on the dyn/heterogeneous path is a boxed-future-returning fn-pointer (alloc per construction), not a zero-cost monomorphized async fn — the metamodel must store the boxed-future shape to stay origin-agnostic and dyn-storable, accepting that alloc on the construction path (cold for singletons, a Phase-2 hot-path concern for prototypes/scoped).

### Interactions
- **bean-registry** — The registry STORES these definitions (name-keyed map + TypeId index) and OWNS the merged-definition cache (the stale/freeze map) and the registration/override API. The metamodel defines the record shape and the merge function; bean-registry is where instances of that shape live and where getMergedDefinition is computed and cached. Load-bearing producer/consumer pair.
- **container-core** — The engine CONSUMES this metamodel — it is the input the engine turns into instances. container-core defines WHERE the merged definition is computed (engine.merged_definition) and that the engine instantiates from the merged, not the raw, def. This feature defines WHAT that metadata is.
- **post-processor-spi** — BFPP/BDRPP-analogues are the EDITORS of this metamodel during the rewrite window (in-place &mut in R1, push-a-layer in R2, attach-attribute in R3); MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor reads the merged def to discover/cache injection metadata. The freeze gate IS the 'no more BFPP edits' boundary. The metamodel must expose the exact mutation/extension surface these processors need.
- **programmatic-registration** — The BeanRegistrar-analogue MINTS definitions imperatively (loops/conditionals) via the same registration API, with an instance-supplier (= the Factory handle). This feature must offer a programmatic builder (BeanDefinitionBuilder analogue) as the hand-writable escape hatch (§2.10) that produces the same record the macro emits.
- **bean-naming** — Name + aliases live on the definition; the loud collision/override-vs-shadow contract (Boot's allow-override=false default) is enforced at registration into the name-keyed store — partly a metamodel field (name/aliases) and partly a registry policy (override toggle).
- **scopes** — `scope` is a metamodel field; the asymmetric merge rule takes scope-from-child. The Scope value (singleton/prototype/request/custom) the engine reads to decide instance multiplicity is carried here; custom scopes via the Scope SPI are referenced by the definition.
- **lazy-initialization** — `lazy` is a per-definition flag (Option<bool>, child-wins on merge) that the eager-instantiate phase honors; a global lazy default with exclude-filters is a BFPP-analogue rewriting the lazy field on definitions (post-processor-spi).
- **primary-fallback** — `primary` and `fallback` flags are carried on the definition as autowire tie-breakers; this feature defines where they live and that they survive merge, while primary-fallback defines the SELECTION semantics (fallback chosen only when no non-fallback matches) read off these flags.
- **candidate-resolver** — `autowire_candidate` flag and the qualifiers list (Vec<Qualifier> in R1/R2, a typed attribute in R3) are carried here and consumed by the resolver chain to decide which beans QUALIFY for an injection point; generics-as-implicit-qualifier keys off the produced TypeId on the definition.
- **autowiring-resolution** — Provides the metadata (primary/fallback/qualifiers/autowire-candidate/produced-type) over which the fail-fast layered selection policy runs; this feature is the data source, autowiring-resolution the policy.
- **factory-bean** — The metamodel must distinguish a factory bean from its product: the defining TypeId vs the produced TypeId (getObjectType analogue), and a flag/marker so the engine's get-path knows the & dereference escape hatch applies. Produced-type-unknown = the graceful 'invisible to autowiring' state.
- **lifecycle-callbacks** — `init`/`destroy` hook handles are metamodel fields (the init-method/destroy-method analogues, child-wins or merged per rule); lifecycle-callbacks reads them to run ordered init/destruction. They are typed hook references, not reflective method-name strings.
- **conditions** — Two-phase parse-vs-register: a condition decides whether a definition is registered AT ALL (a false condition = no definition, surfacing later as no-candidate). Condition metadata may ride the definition (R3 attribute) or gate registration; the metamodel must record enough for condition-report to explain a backed-off bean.
- **container-hierarchy** — Parent CONTEXT lookups (child sees parent's beans) are distinct from parent DEFINITION templates (parentName inheritance) — both called 'parent' in Spring but orthogonal. This feature owns definition-parent (merge/overlay); container-hierarchy owns context-parent (lookup delegation). The design must keep the two 'parents' clearly separate to avoid the classic confusion.
- **component-stereotypes** — Stereotype annotations (@Component/@Service/etc analogues) are the declaration sites the macro reads to emit the definition seed, setting defaults (role, name). The stereotype is metadata-about-the-source that becomes definition fields.
- **value-injection** — Spring resolves ${placeholder} constructor/property args by REWRITING definition arg metadata (a BFPP). With typed factories leaf cannot rewrite args reflectively, so resolved values become inputs the factory reads at resolve-time (via ResolveCtx) rather than edits to the definition — a key consequence of the reflective-vs-typed clash for THIS feature.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Mutable-in-place vs immutable-layered vs open-attribute-map for the metamodel record shape (R1 vs R2 vs R3) — this is a unification call (does rewriting share machinery with parent-merge? is the record open for downstream extension?) that needs the whole Phase 1 catalog, especially how heavily cross-cutting features (tx/cache/validation/AOP) want to attach per-bean metadata, to judge. R3's openness vs R1/R2's closed schema trades heap/dyn cost against extensibility.
- What can a BFPP-analogue actually REWRITE, given typed factories make Spring's reflective arg-rewriting impossible? The boundary between 'editable metadata' (scope/lazy/qualifiers/which-name-resolves-an-injection-point) and 'fixed recipe' (the typed factory closure) must be drawn — and it determines how much of Spring's BFPP power (placeholder resolution into args, @Configuration parsing minting defs) survives as definition edits vs moves to resolve-time factory inputs. Cross-cuts value-injection, configuration-classes, post-processor-spi.
- Ownership/lifetime of the stored definition and the merged def (THE deferred keystone §3): is a `BeanDefinition`/`MergedBeanDefinition` owned by the registry and handed out as `&`, shared as `Arc`, or arena-indexed? This drives whether merge produces an owned value, an Arc, or an index, and whether the metamodel's Vec/heap fields can be interned/arena'd to honor §2.5. Cannot be settled here; shapes the whole record API.
- Metadata/codegen boundary for the definition SEED (deferred §3): does the macro emit a flat `const RawBeanDefinition` (clean for R1/R2, requires the record be const-constructible) or builder-style attachment (forced by R3's heterogeneous map)? This couples the metamodel's const-friendliness to the codegen-vs-runtime decision and to whether definitions can be collapsed/frozen at BUILD time (the AOT lineage — Spring's 'definitions knowable at build time').
- How much of definition VALIDATION and even MERGE/COLLAPSE moves to build/compile time vs startup vs runtime (§2.9 richest-diagnostic timing)? An immutable-layered or const-seed model could collapse parent→merged at build time and emit a frozen effective definition (AOT-style), surfacing bad-parent/abstract-instantiation/post-freeze errors earlier — but only where the compile/build error stays legible. The split is unresolved and interacts with the conditional strategy.
- The freeze/seal boundary (markBeanAsCreated analogue): how is 'rewrite window closed' enforced — a runtime state flag, a type-state consuming mutable access, or implicit by the append-only-then-collapse model? This must be a per-bean, no-global-lock mechanism and must give a precise post-freeze-edit diagnostic; the exact mechanism depends on the ownership model and the R1/R2/R3 choice.
- Where is the qualifiers/depends-on/autowire-candidate boundary between the closed core and any open extension surface? Spring carries qualifiers on AbstractBeanDefinition; leaf must decide if these are first-class core fields (R1/R2) or attributes (R3). The decision ripples into candidate-resolver, autowiring-resolution, and collection-injection, which all read this metadata on a hot-ish path.
- Origin-agnostic Factory handle shape across the dyn boundary: the recipe must be storable in a heterogeneous registry AND be invocable for WASM-module/test-double origins (RPITIT/AFIT not dyn-safe → boxed-future fn-pointer). Is the standard a boxed-future Factory (alloc per construction, fine for singletons, a cost for prototypes/scoped) or a monomorphized typed fast-path with boxing only at the dyn edge? This is the async-execution-model decision (§3) viewed through the metamodel's construction recipe.
- Provenance/diagnostics depth on the blueprint: how much per-field (R2's per-layer, R3's per-attribute) provenance does the metamodel retain to answer 'why does this bean look like this?' — and is that retained at runtime (memory cost) or only in a diagnostic/validation mode? Cross-cuts the unified diagnostics model (§2.9) and condition-report/startup-instrumentation.
- Coherence of the definition-parent (template merge) vs context-parent (hierarchy lookup) 'two parents': both exist, are orthogonal, and are a known confusion source — Phase 2 should fix a clear naming/API separation so container-hierarchy's lookup-parent and this feature's template-parent never blur.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent — I read the BeanDefinition section (lines 145-177) AND the deep-dive (2434-2473) in full, including the three hidden mechanics (merged-as-recomputed-copy with stale/freeze, role hints, fallback), the asymmetric merge rule, and the abstract-template footgun. The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters for THIS feature — the SHAPE and edit-model of the metadata record: R1 = mutable owned struct + explicit overlay + freeze gate (literal Spring, rust-owned); R2 = immutable layered overlay-stack where rewrite and parent-merge UNIFY into one collapse fold (the most rust-native and most diagnosable, best fit for multiple definition sources); R3 = thin closed core + open typed attribute map with per-attribute merge policies (the extensibility play, mirroring Spring's setAttribute bag type-safely). R1 and R2 differ on mutable-vs-immutable + whether merge unifies with rewrite; R3 differs on closed-vs-open schema and is orthogonal to the edit-model (it composes with R1 or R2). I deliberately stayed substrate-neutral on the cross-crate collection mechanism (linkme/codegen/explicit) and on the ownership/Arc-vs-arena question — both are cross-cutting Phase 2 decisions surfaced in phase2Questions, not re-litigated per realization.\n\nThree things to carry forward: (1) The single deepest clash for THIS feature is reflective-property-bag vs typed-factory. Spring's BFPPs routinely rewrite constructor args reflectively; in Rust the factory is an opaque typed closure, so 'rewriting' shrinks to editing METADATA (scope/lazy/qualifiers/which-name-resolves-an-injection-point) and resolved values become resolve-time factory INPUTS, not definition edits. This narrows the BFPP-analogue's power and is the most consequential intent-vs-mechanism divergence — flagged as a phase2Question because it touches value-injection, configuration-classes, and post-processor-spi.
