(2) The merged-definition / freeze mechanic (recomputed read-only copy, stale-not-removed, frozen at first touch) is the load-bearing hidden concept and MUST be preserved as a distinct read-only `MergedBeanDefinition` with a per-bean (never global-lock) freeze boundary; R2's collapse-fold expresses it most cleanly and unifies it with parent-inheritance. (3) Definitions are pure data + an opaque async Factory fn-pointer — they hold NO live resources, so they sidestep the no-async-Drop clash entirely; the only async concern is that the carried Factory is a boxed-future fn-pointer (dyn-storage forces this), an alloc-per-construction the metamodel accepts to stay origin-agnostic (WASM-module-ready).\n\nThe two-source duality the brief asked me to flag (static link-time registry as the default 'classpath' PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module 'classpath') is captured as multiple PRODUCERS feeding ONE registration API — most natural in R2 where each source pushes layers/base-defs onto a per-name stack — and left as CANDIDATES, not a decision. I refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization is compatible with cross-crate Arch A/B/C/D and with any registry/scope/ownership substrate, by keeping the definition seed thin and the stored handle abstract.\n\nNaming note: 'BeanDefinition'/'MergedBeanDefinition'/'RawBeanDefinition'/'DefinitionLayer'/'DefinitionAttribute' are Phase-1 placeholders. I wrote no files; this is the complete Phase-1 design for bean-definition. Relevant grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (lines 145-177 the metamodel section incl. merged/role/fallback hidden concepts; 2440-2473 the three-strata data model, merged-recomputed-copy stale/freeze, abstract-template footgun, preInstantiateSingletons); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/DCE silent-failure, generics-cannot-register, Architectures A-D, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI, self-check mandate); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (stable envelope: const TypeId 1.91, Any+downcast, trait upcasting 1.86, RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe, no async Drop as a first-class constraint).

## Bean Definition Registry & Engine (DefaultListableBeanFactory)  `[bean-registry]`

Spring's `DefaultListableBeanFactory` is THE real IoC engine — the single workhorse implementation of `ListableBeanFactory` + `BeanDefinitionRegistry` + `SingletonBeanRegistry` + `AutowireCapableBeanFactory` that every context owns (HAS-A, never subclasses). container-core fixed the engine/façade BOUNDARY and the auto-detect-by-role split; bean-definition fixed the per-bean metadata RECORD and its parent→merged flatten. THIS feature is the engine's INTERNALS: the three coherent stores and the algorithms over them. Precisely: (1) a NAME-keyed definition map (`beanDefinitionMap`, registration order preserved in `beanDefinitionNames`) plus the parallel type index used to answer by-type queries — registration is name-keyed with override policy (`allowBeanDefinitionOverriding`; Boot defaults FALSE → loud `BeanDefinitionOverrideException`, raw Spring defaults TRUE/silent-last-wins). (2) The MERGED-definition cache (`mergedBeanDefinitions` ConcurrentHashMap): the engine instantiates from the recomputed read-only `RootBeanDefinition`, not the raw one; invalidation sets `stale=true` rather than removing (lock-light); `markBeanAsCreated` freezes it on first touch = the implicit 'no more BFPP edits' boundary. (3) The SINGLETON cache as a strict-promotion three-level structure — `singletonObjects` (full, lock-free fast read) → `earlySingletonObjects` (half-built) → `singletonFactories` (ObjectFactory callbacks deferring early-reference/early-proxy computation), backing circular-reference resolution; plus a separate `factoryBeanObjectCache` (one canonical name → factory in `singletonObjects` + product here). (4) CANDIDATE RESOLUTION: `getBeanNamesForType`/`findAutowireCandidates` produce the qualifying set (filtered through the pluggable `AutowireCandidateResolver` chain — bare engine uses `SimpleAutowireCandidateResolver`, no qualifiers/generics; the rich resolver is INSTALLED by the context, which is *why* a bare factory silently loses qualifier/generic matching), and `determineAutowireCandidate` runs the fixed fail-fast tie-break (primary → name-match → qualifier-name → @Priority → unique default-candidate → resolvableDependency) refusing to guess on ambiguity (`NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException`). The locking model (v6.2+, carried to 7.0) is the load-bearing concurrency design: NOT a single global mutex but a per-registry `ReentrantLock` + separate lenient-creation lock, with a per-thread strict-vs-lenient policy (`isCurrentThreadAllowedToHoldSingletonLock`) so background-init threads build leniently outside the lock rather than deadlocking on user code held under it. leaf must replicate this INTENT — a self-contained, embeddable, format-agnostic engine that stores definitions, computes/caches merged defs with a freeze boundary, manages a multi-level singleton cache enabling cycle-tolerance and safe publication WITHOUT a global lock, and produces candidate sets for a fail-fast resolver — WITHOUT importing the reflective string-keyed property-bag, the JVM's GC'd-alias singleton model, or the assumption of native-only construction (the singleton cache must hold an origin-agnostic erased handle so a WASM-module/test-double provider publishes the same way). It must NOT pick the registry substrate, the scope/ownership model, or the async-across-dyn boxing decision — those are Phase 2.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Three-store engine with a per-bean creation guard and a stale-flag merged cache (literal DefaultListableBeanFactory, rust-owned)
Mirror Spring's three coherent stores directly as one `Engine` struct, replacing reflection with typed handles and the global mutex with per-bean guards. `Engine { defs: IndexMap<BeanName, Arc<BeanDefinition>>, type_index: HashMap<TypeId, SmallVec<[BeanName; 2]>> (defining + produced TypeIds both indexed, so factory-bean products are findable pre-construction), aliases: HashMap<BeanName, BeanName>, merged: DashMap<BeanName, MergedEntry> where MergedEntry { def: Arc<MergedBeanDefinition>, stale: AtomicBool, frozen: AtomicBool }, singletons: SingletonCache, resolver: Box<dyn AutowireCandidateResolver>, policy: EnginePolicy { allow_override, allow_circular, strict_locking } }`. `SingletonCache` is the three-level structure made explicit: `ready: DashMap<BeanName, ErasedBean>` (lock-free read fast path — `ready.get(name)` with NO lock), `early: HashMap<BeanName, ErasedBean>` (half-built, guarded), `factories: HashMap<BeanName, EarlyRefFactory>` (the deferred-early-reference/early-proxy callbacks), `in_creation: HashSet<BeanName>`, plus `factory_bean_products: DashMap<BeanName, ErasedBean>` (the separate FactoryBean product cache). `ErasedBean` is an ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC erased handle (placeholder `Arc<dyn Any + Send + Sync>` — the concrete handle type is Phase-2's scope/ownership keystone) so native, WASM-module, and test-double providers publish identically. NO global lock: a per-bean `CreationGuard` (a keyed lock — e.g. a sharded map of `Mutex<()>` or a `tokio` keyed primitive, Phase-2 picks) serializes only concurrent FIRST-creation of the SAME bean; distinct beans build in parallel. The strict-vs-lenient policy is a per-creation flag (`try_acquire` on the guard; on contention a lenient-flagged builder constructs outside the guard and reconciles, a strict builder awaits). Merged-def computation is `merged.entry(name).or_insert_with(|| flatten(parent_chain))`; invalidation flips `stale=true` (entry kept, recomputed on next read — Spring's lock-light strategy); `freeze()` on first `get` sets `frozen=true` and any later definition edit errors loudly. `register_definition` keys by name, checks `aliases`+`defs` for collision, applies `allow_override` policy, and updates `type_index`. Candidate resolution: `candidates_for_type(TypeId)` reads `type_index` then filters each through `resolver.is_candidate(def, descriptor)`; the fixed tie-break lives in `determine_winner` on the engine, not the resolver. The resolver is swappable so the façade can upgrade `Simple` → context-aware (the exact 'bare engine is dumb' parity).

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: the three stores, the stale-flag merged cache, the lock-free `ready` read path, the separate FactoryBean product cache, and the resolver-swap-as-policy all map 1:1 to Spring — maximally teachable and traceable to the source.; Per-bean creation guard is the direct, honest realization of charter §2.5 (no global lock): distinct beans build concurrently, only same-bean first-creation serializes — which is exactly what enables background-bootstrap and parallel eager init.; Lock-free `ready.get(name)` fast read path is the hot-path-friendly resolution shape (§2.4 reactive/no-busy-wait): a fully-built singleton is fetched with zero locking, matching Spring's `singletonObjects.get` with no lock.; Origin-agnostic `ErasedBean` in the cache means the singleton store cannot tell native from WASM-module from test-double — the future dynamic 'classpath' slots in by publishing the same handle.; Swappable `Box<dyn AutowireCandidateResolver>` reproduces the load-bearing 'bare factory silently loses qualifier/generic matching' behavior structurally — the façade installs the rich resolver, the bare engine keeps `Simple`.; Stale-flag (not remove) merged invalidation lets concurrent readers keep getting a soon-recomputed value without holding a lock across the recompute — directly honors the lock-light invariant.

**Cons:** Three name-keyed maps + a TypeId index that must stay COHERENT under registration/override/alias is real bookkeeping; an inconsistency (type_index pointing at a removed def) is a latent bug class Spring gets implicitly from the JVM.; `DashMap`/sharded maps for the caches lean on a concurrency crate and on `Arc`-heavy erased handles — exactly the Arc/heap churn §2.5 warns against; needs the Phase-2 ownership decision to discipline (arena/interned names) or it bloats.; The per-bean creation guard's strict-vs-lenient reconciliation (lenient builder constructed outside the guard, then deduped against a racing strict build) is genuinely subtle — Spring's own `BeanCurrentlyInCreationException`-tolerate path took multiple point releases to stabilize; re-deriving it is high-risk.; Two keying schemes (name map + TypeId index) plus aliases mean every mutation path has to update several structures transactionally on the cold path — more surface for the override/collision diagnostics to get wrong.; The three-level cache's middle `factories` level only earns its keep for circular-reference + early-proxy (circular-references, auto-proxy-creator); for the common acyclic case it is pure overhead — Spring accepts this, but in Rust the early-proxy story is itself unsettled, so the level may be speculative.

**Mechanisms considered:** runtime registry: IndexMap<BeanName,Arc<BeanDefinition>> + HashMap<TypeId,SmallVec<BeanName>> type index + alias map; three-level singleton cache (ready DashMap lock-free read / early guarded / factories deferred-early-ref) + separate factory-bean product cache; per-bean keyed CreationGuard (sharded Mutex map or async keyed lock) instead of a global mutex; strict-vs-lenient per-creation flag; stale-flag (AtomicBool, entry-retained) merged-def invalidation + frozen AtomicBool freeze gate (markBeanAsCreated analogue); origin-agnostic ErasedBean handle (placeholder Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync>) so the cache is construction-source-blind; swappable Box<dyn AutowireCandidateResolver> chain; fixed determine_winner tie-break on the engine

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Substrate-neutral on collection — the Engine consumes whatever descriptor set arrives (linkme distributed-slice / build.rs codegen / explicit `with()`), since registration is a runtime call into `register_definition`. What it FIXES cross-crate: the Engine, `BeanDefinition`/`MergedBeanDefinition`, `AutowireCandidateResolver`, and `ErasedBean` all live in leaf-core; contributing crates depend only on leaf-core; the thin macro emits absolute-path descriptor seeds the Engine lifts. The type_index can ONLY hold concrete TypeIds (generics have no `'static` symbol/TypeId until instantiated → macro hard-errors on generic beans; a `register_bean!(Concrete)` escape hatch per instantiation). The Engine inherits the silent-DCE risk from whatever collection substrate Phase 2 picks: a registration-only crate the binary never references contributes zero definitions and the Engine simply never sees them — so the façade's auto-detect self-check (expected-vs-found infrastructure count, fail loud) is the required defense, and the Engine should expose a `definition_count`/`names()` introspection surface to make that self-check writable. The candidate resolver swap is the cross-crate seam for the 'bare engine vs context' behavior: the rich resolver ships in leaf-core (or a context crate) and is installed at façade bring-up; the WASM-module 'classpath' is just another source calling `register_definition` with descriptors whose factory dispatches into the module — CANDIDATES (static link-time registry PLUS optional dynamic directory-scanned source = two producers of the same registration API), not a decision.

**T2 — execution context:** Registration, override-check, alias-add, merged-flatten, and type-index update are SYNCHRONOUS cold-path (definition phase, single-threaded, around main; descriptors are const data, no pre-main code). The async seam is bean CONSTRUCTION: each definition's origin-agnostic factory returns a boxed future (RPITIT/AFIT not dyn-safe → boxed at the dyn boundary) awaited by the get/eager-instantiate driver. Construction may `.await` (DB pools) but MUST NOT block the executor — blocking factories documented to use spawn_blocking (execution-context discipline; Phase 2 owns propagation). The lock-free `ready` read path is the hot path: a built singleton is fetched with no await and no lock. First-creation of a bean takes the per-bean guard (NOT global); background-init threads (background-bootstrap) acquire leniently — build outside the guard, reconcile via a tolerated in-creation race exactly as Spring's lenient mode. Safe publication: a fully-built bean is moved into `ready` (the Arc/handle publish is the §concurrency-contract realization) — visibility of the finished singleton is guaranteed, runtime method thread-safety is the developer's. Errors propagate as a rich ResolveError carrying the assembly chain (what was being built, what it needed, candidates considered, why ambiguous) per §2.9; eager-instantiate is all-or-nothing fail-fast at boot. No async Drop → teardown of cached singletons is an explicit container-driven `shutdown().await` in reverse dependency order (context-close); cancellation of an in-flight eager build drops partially-constructed beans and unwinds the in_creation set.

#### R2 — Generation/epoch-immutable registry: definition phase produces a frozen snapshot; runtime reads a lock-free immutable index, singletons in a separate concurrent store
Question whether the registry needs to be a mutable concurrent map at runtime at all. Spring's maps are mutable+concurrent because definitions can be registered mid-bootstrap and the JVM has no notion of a 'frozen' phase; leaf's INTENT (store defs, compute merged, resolve candidates, cache singletons, no global lock) is served better by separating an APPEND-ONLY definition phase from a READ-ONLY resolution phase. Two epochs: (Epoch 1, cold/single-threaded) definitions and BFPP-rewrites accumulate into a mutable builder; at `freeze()` the builder COMPUTES ALL merged definitions up front and emits an immutable `RegistrySnapshot { defs: Box<[MergedBeanDefinition]>, by_name: phf-style/FrozenMap<BeanName, DefIdx>, by_type: FrozenMap<TypeId, Box<[DefIdx]>>, aliases: FrozenMap<BeanName, BeanName> }` — a single arena of definitions referenced everywhere by a small `DefIdx` (a `u32` index, NOT an Arc/name string), eliminating per-def Arc and the name/type coherence problem (the snapshot is built coherent once, then never mutated). (Epoch 2, runtime/concurrent) the singleton cache is the ONLY mutable structure: `singletons: Box<[OnceCell<ErasedBean>]>` (one slot per DefIdx, so a singleton's storage slot is its definition index — array-indexed, no map, no hashing on the hot read path; the `OnceCell`/`OnceLock` per slot IS the per-bean creation guard, giving lock-free reads of ready beans and at-most-once init with zero global lock by construction). Circular-reference early refs live in a tiny side `early: SmallMap<DefIdx, ErasedBean>` consulted only while a creation is in flight. Merged definitions are precomputed so there is no stale-flag/recompute machinery — the freeze IS the markBeanAsCreated boundary, made a hard phase transition (typestate: `Engine<Open>` has `register`/`rewrite`; `Engine<Frozen>` has only `get`/candidates — a post-freeze edit is a COMPILE error on the static path, the strongest possible 'no more BFPP edits' diagnostic). Candidate resolution reads the immutable `by_type` (returns `&[DefIdx]`); the resolver filter + fixed tie-break run over indices. This is the AOT-lineage shape Spring's GenericApplicationContext gestures at ('definitions frozen and contributed at build time') taken to its Rust conclusion.

**Pros:** No global lock AND no concurrency-map crate on the runtime path: per-slot `OnceCell` gives lock-free reads of ready singletons and at-most-once init intrinsically — the §2.5 invariant satisfied by data shape, not by careful locking discipline.; DefIdx (u32) instead of Arc<BeanName>/Arc<BeanDefinition> everywhere kills the per-definition Arc/heap churn and the name↔type coherence bug class — the snapshot is built coherent once and is immutable, so type_index can never dangle.; Array-indexed singleton storage (`singletons[def_idx]`) is the cheapest possible hot read — no hash, no lock, just a bounds-checked slot — the most reactive resolution path (§2.4) of the three.; Typestate freeze (`Engine<Open>`→`Engine<Frozen>`) makes 'rewrite window closed' a COMPILE-time guarantee on the static path, not a runtime flag — the richest-diagnostic timing for the post-freeze-edit error (§2.9), strictly stronger than Spring's runtime markBeanAsCreated.; Precomputing all merged definitions at freeze surfaces bad-parent/abstract-instantiation/merge-conflict errors at the phase boundary, BEFORE any instance — earliest rich diagnostic, and folds the merged-cache + freeze into one step (unification, §2.11).; Snapshot is trivially shareable read-only across threads/tasks with zero synchronization — the immutable index is safe-publication-for-free, and pairs naturally with the AOT/build-time-determinism lineage.

**Cons:** Sacrifices Spring's mid-bootstrap dynamism: an init-method that registers a NEW definition (Spring's defensive-copy-of-beanDefinitionNames case) cannot mutate a frozen snapshot — leaf would need a documented escape (a second deferred epoch, or registrar-only-before-freeze) that diverges from Spring's 'register during preInstantiate' freedom.; DefIdx-as-slot ties a singleton's storage to its definition index — fine for singletons, but prototypes/scoped beans (multiplicity > 1) don't fit one slot, so scoped storage needs a SEPARATE structure, partially undoing the elegant array model and pre-judging scope (which is a Phase-2 keystone).; Typestate `Engine<Open>`/`Engine<Frozen>` leaks the type parameter into every signature that holds an engine (the façade, registrars, post-processors) — viral generics that complicate dyn boundaries and cross-crate APIs (must offer a `dyn`-erased frozen view at edges).; Precompute-everything at freeze does MORE cold-path work than lazy merge (Spring computes merged defs on demand) and retains all merged defs in the arena even for beans never resolved — memory the lazy stale-cache avoids; acceptable cold-path cost but real.; FrozenMap/phf for `by_name`/`by_type` either needs build-time perfect-hashing (couples to the codegen substrate, a Phase-2 call) or a sorted-slice binary search (fine, but not literally O(1)); the 'frozen' framing tempts a build-time-only optimization that the runtime-assembly substrate can't provide.; Less faithful to practitioners' DefaultListableBeanFactory mental model — 'the registry is a mutable map you can poke' is gone; the two-epoch shape is a translation cost.

**Mechanisms considered:** two-epoch model: mutable Open builder → immutable RegistrySnapshot at freeze (arena of MergedBeanDefinition + FrozenMaps), DefIdx (u32) handles; per-slot OnceCell/OnceLock singleton array indexed by DefIdx — the creation guard AND the cache in one, lock-free reads, no global lock; typestate Engine<Open>/Engine<Frozen> making post-freeze edit a compile error; dyn-erased frozen view for crate edges; precompute-all-merged-defs at freeze (no stale-flag/recompute machinery); freeze = the hard markBeanAsCreated phase boundary; candidate resolution over &[DefIdx] from an immutable by_type index; resolver filter + fixed tie-break unchanged; origin-agnostic ErasedBean in the OnceCell slots; separate side structure for early refs (cycles) and (deferred) for scoped multiplicity

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Strongest fit for the AOT/build-time lineage and for a codegen substrate: the immutable snapshot is exactly what a build.rs/codegen path could EMIT (a `const`/generated `RegistrySnapshot`), and the runtime-assembly path builds the same snapshot at startup — substrate-neutral but biased toward 'freeze early'. The two-epoch boundary is the natural place to run the anti-DCE self-check: at freeze, assert expected-vs-found definition/infrastructure counts BEFORE emitting the snapshot, turning a silently-dropped contributing crate into a loud freeze-time error (and the snapshot can record per-source provenance so the self-check names the missing crate). Generics still cannot register (macro hard-errors; concrete-instantiation escape hatch). FrozenMap perfect-hashing tempts coupling to build-time codegen — must stay a runtime-buildable sorted-slice if the substrate is runtime-assembly, so the snapshot shape is kept substrate-agnostic (CANDIDATE: build-time phf vs runtime sorted-slice, not decided). The WASM-module 'classpath' must contribute its definitions BEFORE freeze (it is a pre-freeze registration source) OR force a second epoch — flagged as the duality (static link-time registry + optional dynamic directory-scanned source feeding the Open builder) and as a real tension with the freeze-early shape (a Phase-2 question, not resolved). Typestate generics push toward dyn-erased engine views at crate boundaries to avoid leaking `Engine<Frozen>` into every integration crate's signatures.

**T2 — execution context:** Epoch 1 (registration + rewrite + merge-all + freeze + self-check) is fully synchronous cold-path, single-threaded, around main — no async, no IO, descriptors are const. The freeze transition is a sync phase change producing an immutable snapshot safely shared across all later tasks with ZERO synchronization (immutable data = safe publication for free). Epoch 2 runtime: bean construction is async (boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary, awaited by get/eager driver); the per-slot `OnceCell` provides at-most-once async init — concurrent `get`s of the same not-yet-built bean race to the slot, the loser awaits the winner's published value (the per-bean guard, lock-free for the ready case, no global lock). Hot read of a ready singleton is `singletons[idx].get()` — no await, no lock, no hash. Background-init/concurrent eager (background-bootstrap) fans out construction futures keyed by DefIdx; the OnceCell-per-slot makes lenient vs strict moot for storage (the slot is the arbiter) — the only ordering concern is depends_on, read from the immutable snapshot. Errors propagate as rich ResolveError with the assembly chain; merge/parent/abstract errors surface at freeze (earliest), wiring/ambiguity errors at eager-instantiate. No async Drop → explicit `shutdown().await` walks slots in reverse dependency order (context-close); cancellation drops un-published OnceCell inits cleanly (an un-set OnceCell holds nothing to leak). Scoped/prototype execution context is deferred (their storage isn't the singleton array).

#### R3 — Provider-store kernel: the registry is a thin coherent index over origin-agnostic `Provider` handles; merge/cache/resolution are pluggable strategies on a minimal core (SPI-first, maximal unification)
Push unification (§2.11) hardest and make ORIGIN-AGNOSTICISM the organizing principle. The engine is a deliberately tiny kernel whose ONLY job is to be a coherent, queryable index of `Provider`s; merged-def computation, the singleton-cache POLICY, the creation guard, and candidate filtering are all pluggable strategies the kernel composes. A `Provider` is the irreducible origin-agnostic unit: `trait Provider { fn descriptor(&self) -> &Descriptor; fn provide<'a>(&'a self, ctx: &'a dyn ResolveCtx) -> BoxFuture<'a, Result<ErasedBean, ResolveError>>; }` — a native factory, a WASM-module bridge, a test double, an already-realized singleton, or a FactoryBean product are ALL just `Provider` impls; the kernel cannot tell them apart (the deepest realization of 'a provider of dyn Trait must not assume native construction'). Kernel `Engine { providers: Vec<Arc<dyn Provider>>, by_name: HashMap<BeanName, ProviderId>, by_type: HashMap<TypeId, SmallVec<[ProviderId; 2]>>, aliases: HashMap<BeanName, BeanName>, merge: Box<dyn MergeStrategy>, cache: Box<dyn SingletonStore>, guard: Box<dyn CreationGuard>, resolver: Box<dyn AutowireCandidateResolver> }`. The kernel exposes only primitives: `register(Arc<dyn Provider>)`, `merged(id) -> &MergedBeanDefinition` (delegates to `merge`), `candidates(TypeId/descriptor) -> Vec<ProviderId>` (reads by_type, filters via resolver), `get(id) -> BoxFuture<ErasedBean>` (consults `cache`; on miss takes `guard`, calls `provider.provide`, publishes into `cache`). `SingletonStore` is an SPI — the three-level cache (R1), the OnceCell-array (R2), an arena, or a no-op (prototype-only) are interchangeable IMPLEMENTATIONS chosen at composition; same for `MergeStrategy` (eager-precompute vs stale-flag-lazy) and `CreationGuard` (sharded-mutex vs onceflag vs lenient/strict). The FactoryBean indirection (& dereference) is a Provider that, when resolved, registers/returns its product Provider — no special-casing in the kernel. This makes the bare-engine-vs-context difference reduce to WHICH strategies + WHICH resolver are installed, and lets neighbours (circular-references via the SingletonStore's early-ref hook, factory-bean via a Provider, candidate-resolver via the resolver SPI) compose into the SAME kernel rather than bespoke branches.

**Pros:** Maximal origin-agnosticism: native, WASM-module, test-double, FactoryBean-product, and pre-realized providers are ONE trait the kernel can't distinguish — the future dynamic 'classpath' is not a special path, it's more `Provider`s, exactly the brief's slot-in requirement.; Maximal unification (§2.11): merged-def, singleton-cache, creation-guard, and candidate-filter are each ONE swappable SPI, so Phase 2's deferred keystones (scope/ownership = `SingletonStore` choice; registry substrate = how Providers are collected; async-across-dyn = the `provide` boxing) become STRATEGY choices the kernel already accommodates rather than rewrites.; Tiny kernel is genuinely minimal-footprint and semver-stable (the §6 'small stable kernel' topology) — the SPIs are the extension surface, the kernel barely changes; great for the leaf-core ABI the macro hard-codes paths to.; FactoryBean (& dereference), circular-ref early exposure, and the resolver chain all compose as kernel SPI implementations instead of engine special-cases — fewer bespoke code paths, the neighbours plug in uniformly.; The `SingletonStore` SPI lets R1's three-level cache and R2's OnceCell-array BOTH exist as implementations — so Phase 2 can A/B them without touching the kernel, and a context can even pick per-scope stores.; Provider-as-handle keeps the construction recipe fully opaque and async-boxed at one place (`provide`), so the alloc-per-construction cost is localized and the kernel stays sync where it can.

**Cons:** Most indirection by far: every `get` threads through `Box<dyn SingletonStore>` + `Box<dyn CreationGuard>` + `Box<dyn Provider>` — boxed-future and dyn-dispatch churn on what should sometimes be a cheap path, in direct tension with §2.5 and with hot-path reactivity (§2.4); the zero-cost monomorphized path is hard to preserve through four dyn SPIs.; Borderline over-engineering for Phase 1: a fully general strategy-SPI engine PRE-JUDGES the very meta-architecture Phase 2 is meant to unify — it risks declaring (not just proposing) that 'everything is a swappable strategy', which is itself a meta-architecture decision; must be held as a CANDIDATE shape, not a decision.; An SPI for the SingletonStore means the three-level-cache coherence (early/ready/factories promotion, circular-ref hooks) becomes a CONTRACT the kernel must specify precisely for every implementer — a subtle interface that's easy to implement wrongly (an impl that mis-orders promotion silently breaks cycle resolution).; `Vec<Arc<dyn Provider>>` is Arc-per-provider plus a dyn-dispatch on every resolve — heavier than R2's DefIdx-into-arena and even R1's direct cache; the abstraction tax is paid on the hot path unless aggressively monomorphized away.; Harder to give a great default-DX 'just works' story: a kernel that needs a MergeStrategy + SingletonStore + CreationGuard + resolver injected is ceremony unless leaf ships an opinionated default bundle — which then looks a lot like R1/R2 anyway, questioning whether the SPI generality earns its weight in Phase 1.; 'Find the bare engine affordance' is muddier: the minimal engine is 'kernel + default strategies', a composition rather than a named type.

**Mechanisms considered:** origin-agnostic `Provider` trait (descriptor + async provide → ErasedBean) as the single registered unit; native/WASM/test-double/FactoryBean-product all impls; tiny kernel holding by_name/by_type/alias index over ProviderIds; merge/cache/guard/resolver as Box<dyn _> strategy SPIs; SingletonStore SPI (three-level cache OR OnceCell-array OR arena OR no-op) — scope/ownership keystone expressed as a strategy choice; MergeStrategy SPI (eager-precompute vs lazy stale-flag) and CreationGuard SPI (sharded-mutex vs once-flag vs lenient/strict); FactoryBean & circular-ref & resolver-chain as kernel SPI implementations, not engine special-cases (unification); boxed-future provide() at the one dyn seam; kernel sync elsewhere; monomorphized fast-path only where strategies are statically known

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for the cross-crate layered strategy AND the two-source duality: a static link-time registry contributes native `Provider`s; an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source contributes WASM-bridge `Provider`s — BOTH register through the same `register(Arc<dyn Provider>)` API into the same index, so the duality is two producers of one Provider stream, NOT two code paths (CANDIDATES, not a decision — most natural of the three for the WASM-module slot-in). leaf-core hosts the kernel + `Provider`/`Descriptor`/`SingletonStore`/`MergeStrategy`/`CreationGuard`/`AutowireCandidateResolver` traits — a minimal, ultra-stable ABI the thin macro targets (emits a `Descriptor` + a native-Provider seed via absolute paths). Generics: a generic component has no Provider until instantiated (no TypeId/symbol) → macro hard-errors, concrete-instantiation escape hatch registers a concrete Provider. Silent-DCE: same inherited risk — Providers from an unreferenced crate never register; the kernel exposes `provider_count`/introspection so the façade self-check (expected-vs-found, per-source provenance) can fail loud. The strategy SPIs are themselves a cross-crate extension surface (an integration crate could ship a custom SingletonStore for a scope) — powerful but widens the stable-ABI commitment, a real cost to weigh. Substrate-neutral on HOW Providers are collected (linkme/codegen/explicit), by design.

**T2 — execution context:** Kernel registration/index-building/merge are synchronous cold-path (definition phase, around main). The single async seam is `Provider::provide` (boxed future at the dyn boundary — RPITIT/AFIT not dyn-safe, and origin-agnosticism REQUIRES the dyn boundary so WASM/test-double impls slot in, so boxing is accepted here as a structural cost, cold for singletons / a Phase-2 hot-path concern for prototypes/scoped). `get` consults the `SingletonStore` (lock-free ready read if the chosen store provides it — e.g. OnceCell/DashMap impls do), takes the `CreationGuard` on miss (per-bean, never global — the guard SPI's contract forbids a container-wide lock), awaits `provide`, publishes into the store (safe publication = §concurrency-contract). Construction may await but must not block (spawn_blocking for blocking providers — execution-context discipline; Phase 2 owns propagation). The strategy boundaries are the natural place to DECLARE execution context per concern: a background-init CreationGuard (background-bootstrap) implements lenient-outside-the-guard construction; the SingletonStore declares its read/write concurrency contract. Errors are rich ResolveError with the assembly chain attributed to the Provider/strategy that failed (§2.9) — the SPI boundary gives precise 'failed in store/guard/provider X' diagnostics. No async Drop → explicit `shutdown().await`: the SingletonStore SPI carries a `drain_reverse_dependency() -> impl Stream/Vec` so context-close drives ordered teardown; cancellation unwinds in-flight `provide` futures and the guard releases. The kernel itself never blocks or awaits beyond delegating to `provide`.

### Rust clashes
- GC'd aliased singletons vs Rust ownership — the keystone clash for THIS feature. Spring's singleton cache holds GC-rooted objects freely aliased by every injector; the cache just maps name→Object. In Rust the singleton store must commit to a SHARING model for `ErasedBean`: `Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync>` is the obvious candidate but commits to refcounting (fights §2.5 minimal-Arc) and to `Send+Sync` bounds not every bean satisfies; an arena+index (R2's DefIdx slot) avoids per-bean Arc but ties storage to a fixed slot model that prototypes/scoped beans break. The singleton-cache handle type is the scope/ownership keystone (Phase 2 §3) — this feature must store beans behind SOME erased, origin-agnostic handle WITHOUT prejudging Arc-vs-arena-vs-borrow, which is why all three realizations keep `ErasedBean` abstract.
- No global mutex but safe publication still required — Spring moved OFF a single global `synchronized` to a per-registry ReentrantLock + lenient-creation lock + per-thread strict/lenient policy precisely because the global lock held during user init code (@PostConstruct) deadlocked re-entrant/cross-checking threads. The charter §2.5 forbids global locks outright. So leaf must reproduce the INTENT (serialize only same-bean first-creation; let distinct beans and background threads build in parallel; guarantee visibility of the finished singleton) with a PER-BEAN guard (sharded mutex / OnceCell-per-slot / async keyed lock) — and the lenient-vs-strict 'build outside the lock and reconcile a tolerated in-creation race' is genuinely hard to get right (Spring took several point releases), a high-risk re-derivation.
- Three-level cache exists for early-proxy lazy computation — Spring registers an `ObjectFactory` (not the raw instance) at the middle level SO an AOP early-proxy can be computed lazily, exactly once, and only if a cycle materializes. In Rust the early-proxy story (auto-proxy-creator) is itself unsettled and there is no CGLIB; so the middle `factories` level may be partly speculative — it earns its keep only for circular-references + early-proxy, and whether leaf needs the full three levels or just two (ready + early) depends on Phase-2 decisions about proxying that don't exist yet. The cache MUST keep the early-reference hook origin-agnostic so a future proxy mechanism can slot in.
- No runtime reflection / no classpath for the type index — Spring builds its type index by walking loaded classes and can answer `getBeanNamesForType` by reflecting over registered class names including supertypes. Rust has no such walk: the by-type index must be built from PRE-DECLARED TypeIds in the descriptor (the bean's own type AND, for factory beans, the produced type captured at the declaration site — no runtime predictBeanType). 'Injectable as any supertype' (Spring matches a bean to all its assignable types) has no automatic Rust analogue — each injectable `dyn Trait` view must be explicitly registered (a per-trait index entry the macro emits), because there is no runtime `isAssignableFrom` over arbitrary types; trait-upcasting (stable 1.86) helps recover a supertrait object from a stored one but does not enumerate which traits a type implements.
- Generics cannot be a registered definition / monomorphization erases identity — Spring registers generic classes by name and resolves type variables at runtime via ResolvableType; generics-as-implicit-qualifier (candidate-resolver) keys off that. A generic `Component<T>` has no `'static` symbol and no TypeId until instantiated, so the registry's name map and type index can hold only CONCRETE instantiations; the macro hard-errors on generic beans with a `register_bean!(Concrete)` escape hatch. The candidate resolver's generic narrowing therefore compares concrete TypeIds, not open generic types — `Store<Integer>` vs `Store<String>` distinction works only because both concrete instantiations are separately registered.
- Async fn in traits is not dyn-compatible, but the engine MUST be dyn at two seams — the construction factory (`provide`) must be dyn-storable AND origin-agnostic (so WASM/test-double slot in), and (in R3) the engine itself is composed of dyn strategy SPIs. RPITIT/AFIT can't be trait objects → the factory and the SPI async methods must return boxed futures (`Pin<Box<dyn Future>>`), an alloc per construction/resolution on the dynamic path. This is cold for singletons but a real cost for prototypes/scoped hot-path lookups — leaf must split a zero-cost monomorphized typed path from the boxed dyn path; cannot have both on the same surface on stable (Phase 2's async-execution-model decision).
- Name-keyed vs TypeId-keyed identity coherence — Spring's registry is fundamentally `String`-name-keyed with a type index layered on; Rust prefers TypeId-keyed (typed, zero-string-overhead) but Spring semantics REQUIRE the name layer: aliases, name-collision-as-loud-error (allow-override), name-keyed `Map<String,T>` injection (collection-injection), the `&` FactoryBean dereference (factory-bean), and bean-name-as-implicit-qualifier (autowiring-resolution step 5). So the engine must carry BOTH a name map and a TypeId index and keep them COHERENT across registration/override/alias — coherence the JVM gives Spring implicitly but Rust must maintain by hand (R2's immutable snapshot dissolves this by building coherence once; R1/R3 must maintain it transactionally on every mutation).
- No async Drop on stable — Spring's DisposableBean/@PreDestroy teardown is the mirror of init and the container drives it under the same lock infrastructure. Rust has no async Drop, so destruction of cached singletons holding async-cleanup resources (DB pools) cannot ride `Drop` — it MUST be an explicit container-driven `shutdown().await` in reverse dependency order (context-close). This makes 'the registry owns the singleton lifecycle' a hard non-syntactic discipline: the singleton cache must retain enough dependency/creation-order data to drive reverse teardown, and cancellation of an in-flight eager build must unwind partially-constructed beans out of the in-creation set.

### Interactions
- **container-core** — bean-registry IS the engine internals that container-core's BeanFactory/ConfigurableEngine boundary exposes. container-core fixed the engine-vs-façade split, HAS-A composition, and auto-detect-by-role; bean-registry fills in the definition store, merged cache, three-level singleton cache, and candidate-set production those traits surface. The swappable AutowireCandidateResolver here is exactly the 'bare engine is dumb / context installs the rich resolver' mechanism container-core relies on.
- **bean-definition** — The registry STORES `BeanDefinition`s and OWNS the merged-definition cache + freeze boundary that bean-definition's metamodel feeds. bean-definition defines the record shape, the asymmetric parent→merged flatten rule, and the freeze semantics; bean-registry is where instances of that record live (name map + type index), where `merged(name)` is computed/cached (stale-flag in R1, precomputed at freeze in R2), and where override/collision policy is enforced on registration.
- **bean-instantiation** — The registry's `get`/eager-instantiate DRIVES instantiation and provides the publish target. bean-registry owns the three-level singleton cache, the per-bean creation guard (no global lock), the in-creation set, and the early-reference hook; bean-instantiation runs the actual construct→populate→init sequence and publishes the finished Arc/handle into `ready`. The deferred early-ref ObjectFactory at the middle cache level is the seam between them.
- **autowiring-resolution** — The registry PRODUCES the candidate set (`candidates_for_type` reads the type index, filters through the resolver) and OWNS the fixed fail-fast tie-break (`determine_winner`: primary → name-match → qualifier-name → @Priority → unique default-candidate → resolvableDependency). autowiring-resolution defines the layered policy and the no-guess-on-ambiguity contract; bean-registry supplies the type index, name map, and resolvableDependencies the policy queries and executes the selection.
- **candidate-resolver** — The registry holds the swappable `Box<dyn AutowireCandidateResolver>` and calls `is_candidate(def, descriptor)` per type-index hit to compute WHICH beans qualify (the three-valued match/veto/abstain), distinct from winner selection. candidate-resolver defines the chain (autowire-flag → generics → qualifiers → @Lazy/SpEL) and the abstain semantics; bean-registry is where the resolver is installed (Simple on bare engine, rich on context) and invoked during candidate finding.
- **primary-fallback** — The registry reads the primary/fallback flags (carried on the definition) during `determine_winner`: the two-pass selection (unique @Primary, else unique non-@Fallback) lives in the tie-break the registry executes. primary-fallback defines the promote-one / demote-defaults semantics and the cross-module-default-supersession use case; bean-registry applies it as the first tie-break stage over the candidate set.
- **collection-injection** — The registry answers the multi-valued candidate query (all matching beans for List/Set/array/Map) over the type index and name map — and crucially does NOT apply primary/fallback collapse to collections. collection-injection defines ordering as a consumer concern (@Order/@Priority, name-keyed Map) strictly separate from startup order; bean-registry supplies the full unfiltered candidate set and the name keys, the ordering comparator is applied above (the orderedStream-vs-stream distinction).
- **factory-bean** — The registry must distinguish a factory bean from its product: one canonical name → factory in the singleton cache + product in the SEPARATE `factory_bean_products` cache, with the `&` dereference branch in the get path (throws if dereferencing a non-factory). The type index records both the defining TypeId and the produced TypeId so the product is findable pre-construction. In R3 the FactoryBean is just another Provider, removing the special-case.
- **circular-references** — The three-level singleton cache (ready → early → factories) IS the circular-reference mechanism: the middle deferred-ObjectFactory level lets a half-built bean expose an early reference (and an early proxy) exactly once. bean-registry owns the cache and the in-creation set that detect cycles; circular-references defines tolerate-setter/field-cycles-via-early-ref and fail-fast-on-constructor-cycles, plus the raw-vs-wrapped-reference consistency check.
- **concurrency-contract** — The registry's singleton cache is WHERE safe-publication of fully-built singletons is realized: the per-bean creation guard + the publish-into-ready (Arc/handle move) guarantees visibility of the finished singleton across threads, NOT runtime method thread-safety. bean-registry is the concrete realization of the 'visibility of fully-built singletons' contract; concurrency-contract frames scope choice as the primary concurrency lever and delegates mutable-state safety to the developer.
- **container-hierarchy** — Resolution is hierarchy-aware (a local miss delegates upward to the parent; local definitions shadow same-named parent defs), but LISTING (the registry's `names()`/by-type enumeration) is deliberately LOCAL-ONLY. bean-registry implements the local stores and the upward-delegation-on-miss for `get`/candidate-finding; container-hierarchy defines the one-directional visibility and the listing-local/injection-hierarchy-aware asymmetry the registry must honor.
- **bean-naming** — The registry's name map + alias map + override policy enforce bean-naming's contract: deterministic names, multiple aliases resolving to one canonical name, and name collisions as LOUD errors (allow-override FALSE by default → BeanDefinitionOverrideException) rather than silent shadowing. bean-naming derives the names; bean-registry stores them and enforces collision/override at registration.
- **lazy-initialization** — The eager-instantiate pass (the registry's preInstantiateSingletons analogue) honors the per-definition lazy flag — a lazy singleton is registered/merged but NOT built at refresh, deferring its potential failure to first `get`. bean-registry's eager driver reads the lazy flag to gate construction; lazy-initialization defines the per-bean @Lazy and global-flag-with-exclude-filters semantics.
- **smart-initializing** — The single lock-free callback after every eager singleton exists is fired by the registry's eager-instantiate pass at the END (phase 2 of preInstantiateSingletons), explicitly OUTSIDE any creation guard — the safe place for cross-bean aggregation that would deadlock under the per-bean lock. bean-registry provides the 'all eager singletons now exist' boundary; smart-initializing defines the callback contract.
- **post-processor-spi** — The registry exposes the registration slots BPP/BFPP-analogues write into and the merged-definition the MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor-analogue reads (to discover/cache injection metadata before population). The freeze boundary IS the 'no more BFPP edits' line. bean-registry provides the stores and freeze; post-processor-spi defines the processor contracts and ordering that mutate defs / decorate instances.
- **context-close** — Because there is no async Drop, the registry must retain creation-order/dependency data and expose an explicit `shutdown().await` that destroys cached singletons in reverse dependency order. bean-registry owns the singleton cache and the data needed to drive ordered teardown; context-close defines the stop-Lifecycle-then-destroy-in-reverse sequence that drives it.
- **background-bootstrap** — The strict-vs-lenient per-thread creation policy on the per-bean guard is exactly what lets background-init threads build a slow singleton OUTSIDE the guard (lenient) while the main thread stays strict — enabling concurrent eager init without deadlocking on user code held under the guard. bean-registry owns the guard and the lenient/strict flag; background-bootstrap marks which beans init concurrently and supplies the bootstrap executor.
- **programmatic-registration** — The BeanRegistrar-analogue writes definitions imperatively into the registry's `register_definition` SPI (the stable public escape hatch per §2.10). In R2 this must happen before freeze (a tension with Spring's register-during-preInstantiate freedom); bean-registry exposes the registration API, programmatic-registration defines the functional/conditional/looping instance-supplier contract over it.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Singleton-cache handle type (THE scope/ownership keystone, §3): is a cached singleton stored as `Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync>` (commits to refcount, fights §2.5; forces Send+Sync on every bean), an arena+DefIdx slot (R2 — no per-bean Arc but ties storage to one slot, breaks for prototypes/scoped), or owned-by-container handed out as borrows? The whole get<T> return type and the cache's internal type depend on this, and it must stay origin-agnostic so a WASM-module provider publishes the same handle.
- Do we need all THREE singleton-cache levels (ready/early/factories) or just two (ready/early)? The middle deferred-ObjectFactory level exists in Spring purely to compute an AOP early-proxy lazily-and-once; leaf's proxy mechanism (auto-proxy-creator) doesn't exist yet and there's no CGLIB, so whether the third level earns its keep is undecidable until the proxying strategy is chosen. Cross-cuts circular-references and auto-proxy-creator.
- Per-bean creation guard substrate: sharded `Mutex<()>` map, `OnceCell`/`OnceLock` per slot (R2), or an async keyed lock — and how is the strict-vs-lenient 'build outside the guard and reconcile a tolerated in-creation race' (Spring's hardest-won concurrency code) realized without a global lock? This is where §2.5 meets background-bootstrap and is genuinely subtle.
- Mutable-concurrent-map registry (R1/R3) vs immutable-snapshot-at-freeze registry (R2): does leaf preserve Spring's mid-bootstrap dynamism (init-methods/registrars registering new defs during preInstantiate, the defensive-copy case) or take the AOT-lineage freeze-early shape that forbids it? This is a unification + AOT-lineage call (§3 compile-time-vs-runtime) needing the whole catalog, especially programmatic-registration and conditions, to judge.
- Async-across-dyn boxing standard: the origin-agnostic factory/Provider MUST be dyn (so WASM/test-double slot in) and async-fn-in-trait isn't dyn-safe → boxed futures (alloc per construction). Is the standard boxed-future-on-the-dyn-path (fine for singletons, a cost for prototypes/scoped hot lookups) with a monomorphized zero-cost typed fast-path as the escape, or vice versa? The async-execution-model decision (§3) viewed through the registry's get path.
- Name-keyed vs TypeId-keyed primary identity and how coherence is maintained across registration/override/alias: R2's immutable snapshot builds coherence once; R1/R3 maintain it transactionally on every mutation. Aliases, name-collision errors, name-keyed Map injection, `&` dereference, and bean-name-as-qualifier all need the name layer — the coherence contract touches bean-naming, collection-injection, factory-bean, autowiring-resolution.
- 'Injectable as any supertype' has no automatic Rust analogue (no runtime isAssignableFrom over arbitrary types): must every injectable `dyn Trait` view be EXPLICITLY registered as a type-index entry the macro emits (per declared trait), and how does that interact with trait-upcasting (stable 1.86)? This shapes the type index's contents and the candidate-resolver's matching surface.
- Is the engine ONE concrete type (Spring's single DefaultListableBeanFactory — R1/R2) or a kernel with swappable strategy SPIs (R3 — SingletonStore/MergeStrategy/CreationGuard pluggable)? Making the stores pluggable helps origin-agnosticism and lets Phase 2 A/B the cache shapes, but costs the zero-cost monomorphized path, widens the stable ABI, and borders on pre-deciding the meta-architecture Phase 2 owns.
- How much of merged-def computation and wiring validation moves to BUILD/freeze time vs runtime eager-instantiate (§2.9 richest-diagnostic timing)? R2 precomputes all merged defs and runs the self-check at freeze (earliest rich diagnostic); R1's lazy stale-cache defers. The split interacts with the conditional strategy and the codegen boundary (§3) and with whether eager-build-everything is still the primary error-surfacing mechanism or partly replaced by a startup-validation pass.
- The two-source duality concretely: the static link-time registry (default 'classpath') AND an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source both feed ONE registration API — but R2's freeze-early shape needs the WASM source to register pre-freeze (or a second epoch), while R1/R3's mutable registry accommodates late registration naturally. How the dynamic source's timing reconciles with the freeze boundary is unresolved (and is a candidate, not a decision).

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent — I read the engine deep-dive (lines 2434-2557: the HAS-A engine, three strata, merged-as-recomputed-copy with stale/freeze, the v6.2+ ReentrantLock + lenient-creation lock + isCurrentThreadAllowedToHoldSingletonLock per-thread policy, the three-level singleton cache and WHY it registers a factory not the raw instance, the FactoryBean separate product cache + & dereference), the candidate-resolution six-step pipeline + determineAutowireCandidate tie-break (685-722, 2595-2618), the bare-engine-uses-SimpleAutowireCandidateResolver fact (2610) which is the load-bearing 'bare factory silently loses qualifier/generic matching', and the hierarchy listing-local/injection-aware asymmetry (2692-2700). I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc's Layer-0/A/B DCE and Architectures and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimal-Arc, §2.9 rich-early-diagnostics) and the stable envelope (RPITIT/AFIT not dyn-safe, trait-upcasting 1.86, Arc<dyn Any> registry, no async Drop).

The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters for THIS feature — the SHAPE of the registry's stores and their synchronization: R1 = three mutable concurrent stores with a stale-flag merged cache and a per-bean creation guard (the literal DefaultListableBeanFactory, rust-owned, highest fidelity); R2 = a two-epoch generation model where the definition phase freezes into an IMMUTABLE snapshot (DefIdx arena, no per-def Arc, no name/type coherence bug class) and the ONLY runtime-mutable structure is a per-slot OnceCell singleton array that IS the creation guard (most rust-native, AOT-lineage, lock-free-by-shape, but sacrifices mid-bootstrap dynamism and pre-judges scope via the slot model); R3 = a tiny origin-agnostic Provider-store kernel where merged/cache/guard/resolver are swappable strategy SPIs (maximal unification + origin-agnosticism, best WASM-module slot-in, but most indirection and borders on pre-deciding the meta-architecture). R1 and R2 differ on mutable-concurrent vs immutable-snapshot; R3 differs on monolithic-vs-pluggable-kernel and composes the cache choices of R1/R2 as SPI implementations.

Three things to carry forward: (1) The single most load-bearing behavior to preserve is the NO-GLOBAL-LOCK concurrency model — Spring's own evolution off a global mutex to a per-bean lock + lenient/strict per-thread policy is precisely what §2.5 demands and what enables background-bootstrap; the lenient-build-outside-the-guard-and-reconcile path is Spring's hardest-won code and the highest re-derivation risk, flagged as a phase2Question. (2) The three-level singleton cache's MIDDLE level exists only to compute an AOP early-proxy lazily-and-once; since leaf's proxy mechanism doesn't exist yet, whether leaf needs three levels or two is genuinely open — I refused to assume it. (3) The bare-engine-uses-SimpleAutowireCandidateResolver fact is the structural realization of container-core's 'bare engine is dumb' split AT THE REGISTRY LEVEL — the swappable resolver is why a hand-built engine silently loses qualifier/generic matching, and I made the resolver swap a first-class store in all three realizations.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization keeps `ErasedBean` abstract (the scope/ownership keystone), keeps the construction factory an origin-agnostic boxed-future (WASM-module-ready), and is compatible with cross-crate Arch A/B/C/D. The two-source duality (static link-time registry + optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source) is captured as multiple producers of ONE registration API — most natural in R3 (each is a Provider stream) — and flagged as a tension with R2's freeze-early shape, left as a CANDIDATE not a decision. Naming ('Engine', 'SingletonCache', 'Provider', 'RegistrySnapshot', 'CreationGuard', 'ErasedBean', 'DefIdx') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Relevant grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (124-138 engine-behind-façade; 167-177 merged/role/fallback; 685-722 candidate six-step + tie-break + primary/fallback; 2434-2557 the engine deep-dive incl. locking 2506-2516, three-level cache 2518-2539, FactoryBean 2541-2557; 2595-2618 resolver chain + Simple-is-bare-default + three-valued checkQualifiers; 2692-2700 hierarchy listing-local asymmetry); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3, topology §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/A/B DCE silent-failure + self-check mandate, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI, generics-cannot-register, linkme-default); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (stable envelope: trait upcasting 1.86, const TypeId 1.91, RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe, Arc<dyn Any>, no async Drop).

## Parent/Child Container Hierarchies  `[container-hierarchy]`

Spring lets a context hold a parent (`HierarchicalBeanFactory.getParentBeanFactory()`; `setParentBeanFactory` on the writable SPI) so lookups delegate UPWARD with strictly ONE-DIRECTIONAL visibility: a child sees and injects its own beans plus all ancestor beans; a parent NEVER sees descendants; no sibling coupling. The load-bearing details to replicate as INTENT (not mechanism): (1) `getBean(name)`/`containsBean` delegate to ancestors on a LOCAL MISS, but `containsLocalBean(name)` deliberately ignores ancestors — the asymmetry IS the isolation lever. (2) A local definition with the same name SHADOWS the parent's ("lowest factory wins" — even for by-type lookups, the lowest-level match is injected). (3) THE central asymmetry and the most common practitioner surprise: INJECTION/single-resolution (`resolveDependency`) is hierarchy-aware (walks ancestors automatically, lowest match wins, fail-fast on ambiguity at each level), but LISTING (`getBeanNamesForType`/`getBeansOfType`) is deliberately LOCAL-ONLY and never traverses ancestors — spanning the hierarchy is an explicit OPT-IN (`...IncludingAncestors`, which also collapses an overridden name to ONE entry). The rationale: by-type listing is a per-container introspection op that must be deterministic and cheap, and silently merging ancestors would make collection injection non-obvious and risk double-counting; so flattening is something you ASK FOR. (4) `MessageSource` and other "walk the chain on miss" lookups also delegate upward (messages-i18n). (5) A direct corollary of no-downward-visibility: post-processors are scoped PER-CONTAINER — a parent's BPP physically cannot process child beans, and each container has its OWN private namespace. Design intent mirrors classloader-parent delegation: stable shared infrastructure lives "up," specialized/volatile components "down," so many children layer on one parent without leaking into each other or the parent (Spring MVC root + DispatcherServlet). leaf must replicate this INTENT — upward delegation on miss, local shadowing including for by-type single-resolution, the injection-hierarchy-aware/listing-local-only asymmetry, opt-in ancestor-spanning listing that de-dups shadowed names, per-container isolation of namespace and post-processors — built ON TOP of the bean-registry engine (which is per-container; the hierarchy is a relationship BETWEEN engines, NOT an engine internal), WITHOUT importing the JVM's classloader-rooted lifetime model, and keeping every cross-level handle origin-agnostic (a parent-provided `dyn Trait` must not assume native construction so a WASM-module layer slots in). It must NOT pick the registry substrate, the scope/ownership model, the async-across-dyn boxing, or whether the parent link is a runtime field vs a typed compile-time relation — those are Phase 2.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Parent link as a runtime delegation field on the engine (literal HierarchicalBeanFactory, rust-owned)
Add a single optional parent handle to the per-container engine and route resolution through it, mirroring Spring's `getParentBeanFactory()`/`setParentBeanFactory()` directly. `Engine { /* bean-registry stores: defs, type_index, singletons, resolver, … */ parent: Option<Arc<dyn ParentView>> }`. `ParentView` is a deliberately NARROW, origin-agnostic read-only trait — the only surface a child may call upward: `trait ParentView: Send + Sync { fn get_by_name<'a>(&'a self, name: &BeanName) -> BoxFuture<'a, Result<Option<ErasedBean>, ResolveError>>; fn contains(&self, name: &BeanName) -> bool; fn candidates_for_type(&self, ty: TypeId, desc: &Descriptor) -> Vec<CandidateRef>; fn resolve_one(&self, ty: TypeId, desc: &Descriptor) -> BoxFuture<Result<Option<ErasedBean>, ResolveError>>; fn message(&self, key: &MessageKey, args: &Args) -> BoxFuture<Option<Resolved>>; }`. Crucially `ParentView` exposes NO listing-spanning method (no `names()`/`beans_of_type`) and NO registration — the trait's shape STRUCTURALLY enforces one-directional, read-only, listing-is-not-spanned visibility (the parent can't be enumerated upward, and the child can't mutate the parent). The engine's own `Engine` impls `ParentView` so any engine can BE a parent; `set_parent(p)` just stores `Arc<dyn ParentView>`. Resolution routing (Spring's lowest-factory-wins): `get_by_name` checks local `defs`/`singletons` FIRST; on miss, `self.parent.as_ref()?.get_by_name(name).await`. `contains_local(name)` = local check only, never touches parent; `contains(name)` = local OR parent. Single-resolution (`resolve_one`, the autowiring-resolution entry): run the LOCAL candidate pipeline + fail-fast tie-break; if it yields a unique winner, done (local shadows); if the LOCAL set is empty, delegate `parent.resolve_one(..)` (lowest level that has a match wins — exactly Spring's behavior); if the local set is ambiguous, fail LOCALLY without consulting the parent (ambiguity at a level is a level-local error). LISTING is local-only by construction — `names()`/`beans_for_type()` read ONLY local stores and NEVER call `parent`. Spanning is a SEPARATE, explicit method: `names_including_ancestors()`/`beans_for_type_including_ancestors()` that walks `parent` and collapses a name already defined locally to the LOCAL entry (shadow-de-dup) — the opt-in. Per-container isolation falls out: each engine has its own `defs`, post-processors, resolver, singleton cache; a parent's post-processors run only at the parent's refresh, never see child beans.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: `set_parent`/`get_by_name`-delegates-on-miss/`contains_local` map 1:1 to `setParentBeanFactory`/`getBean`/`containsLocalBean`; the listing-local-vs-injection-hierarchy-aware asymmetry is reproduced exactly, with `...IncludingAncestors` as the named opt-in that de-dups shadows.; The `ParentView` trait's SHAPE enforces the invariants structurally, not by discipline: no upward enumeration method exists (listing can't accidentally span), no mutation method exists (a child cannot poke the parent), and the parent never holds a child handle (no-downward-visibility is unrepresentable) — the strongest possible encoding of one-directional read-only visibility (§2.9 fail-by-construction).; Origin-agnostic by design: `ParentView` traffics only in `ErasedBean`/`CandidateRef`, so a parent could be a native engine, a test-double parent, or a future WASM-module-backed context — the child cannot tell, and the same handle publishes across the boundary.; Minimal footprint: one `Option<Arc<dyn ParentView>>` field and a thin dyn call only ON LOCAL MISS — the common (local-hit) path pays ZERO hierarchy cost, honoring §2.5 (the hot path of a locally-resolved bean is untouched).; Composes cleanly with bean-registry without reopening it: the hierarchy is a relationship BETWEEN engines layered on the existing `resolve_one`/`candidates_for_type`/`names` surface, not a change to the three-store internals — clean separation of concerns.; Naturally supports many-children-one-parent (Spring MVC root + N child contexts): N child engines each hold an `Arc<dyn ParentView>` to the SAME parent; siblings share nothing and cannot see each other (the Arc is read-only).

**Cons:** A `BoxFuture` on every upward `get_by_name`/`resolve_one` (dyn `ParentView` + async-not-dyn-safe) means a parent-resolved bean pays an alloc on the (cold-ish) miss path; fine for singletons/startup but a cost for request-scoped or prototype lookups that fall through to the parent on a hot path — needs the Phase-2 async-across-dyn decision to bound.; Lowest-factory-wins for SINGLE by-type resolution is subtle: 'local set empty → delegate; local set ambiguous → fail locally' must be specified precisely, and the question of whether a local NON-unique-but-present set should still beat a unique parent match (Spring resolves at the lowest level that has ANY candidate) is a semantic edge that's easy to get wrong.; `Arc<dyn ParentView>` re-introduces an Arc on the parent link and `Send + Sync` bounds on the parent engine — modest, but it does commit the parent handle to refcounted sharing (the scope/ownership keystone is deferred, so the concrete handle stays abstract, but the Arc-on-parent is a real choice this realization leans toward).; Cycle prevention (a child accidentally set as its own ancestor) is a runtime invariant to check at `set_parent` (walk the chain), not a compile-time guarantee — a latent footgun Spring also has, but Rust could in principle do better.; The narrow `ParentView` must anticipate EVERY upward-walking lookup (beans, single-resolve, messages-i18n, and any future 'walk the chain on miss' facet); each new hierarchy-aware facet widens this trait — a small but growing stable-ABI surface.

**Mechanisms considered:** runtime delegation field `Option<Arc<dyn ParentView>>` on the per-container engine; `set_parent`/`get_parent` mirroring the writable SPI; narrow origin-agnostic read-only `ParentView` trait (get_by_name/contains/candidates/resolve_one/message) with NO enumerate-upward and NO mutate methods — invariants by trait shape; local-first resolution with on-miss `parent.await` delegation; lowest-factory-wins via 'local empty → delegate, local ambiguous → fail local'; listing methods read local stores ONLY; separate explicit `*_including_ancestors` walkers that collapse shadowed names to the local entry; per-container post-processor/resolver/singleton-cache isolation falls out of each engine owning its own stores; parent BPPs never see child beans; runtime cycle check on `set_parent` (walk ancestor chain); `Arc`-shared parent so many children reference one parent

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The hierarchy relationship is established at RUNTIME (`set_parent` is a runtime call during context bring-up), so it is substrate-neutral on the collection question — definitions are still discovered per-container via whatever Phase-2 picks (linkme distributed-slice / build.rs codegen / explicit `with()`), and the parent/child wiring is a separate, explicit composition step in the binary/aggregator. What it FIXES cross-crate: `ParentView`, `Engine`, and the `*_including_ancestors` API live in leaf-core; integration crates depend only on leaf-core and never need to know they're in a child context. The hierarchy does NOT interact with the silent-DCE hazard directly (no new registry slice), but it MULTIPLIES the self-check surface: the façade self-check (expected-vs-found infrastructure count) must run PER CONTAINER, because a parent and child have independent post-processor sets — a dropped registration could leave a child missing an infrastructure bean the parent has, and the parent can't supply post-processors downward. The WASM-module 'classpath' duality fits naturally: a dynamic source contributes definitions into a SPECIFIC engine (parent or child) which then participates in the hierarchy unchanged — origin-agnostic `ParentView` means a WASM-backed parent is indistinguishable (CANDIDATES: a static link-time registry feeds each engine, an optional dynamic directory-scanned source feeds a designated engine; not a decision). Whether the parent link must be expressed in a way a build-time codegen path can pre-wire (vs purely runtime) is left open.

**T2 — execution context:** `set_parent`/`contains_local`/`names()`/the local-store reads are SYNCHRONOUS cold-path (context composition + introspection; no IO). The async seam is upward DELEGATION: `parent.get_by_name`/`resolve_one`/`message` return boxed futures awaited only ON LOCAL MISS — the local-hit hot path neither awaits nor allocates. Upward delegation may `.await` (the parent bean might itself need async construction) but must not block the executor; the SAME execution-context discipline as a local resolve applies, just one level up. Cancellation: an in-flight upward `resolve_one` future is cancel-safe — dropping it abandons the parent-side construction attempt exactly as a local one (the parent's per-bean creation guard from bean-registry unwinds its own in_creation set). Errors propagate as a rich ResolveError whose assembly chain RECORDS THE LEVEL ('not found locally in child `web`; delegated to parent `root`; parent reported ambiguous between X, Y') — the cross-level chain is the §2.9 diagnostic payoff, and is precisely what disambiguates the 'why is the parent's bean injected here?' surprise. No global lock is introduced: delegation is a read-only call through `Arc<dyn ParentView>`; the parent's own per-bean guards still govern its singleton creation. No async Drop implication beyond bean-registry's: each engine drives its OWN reverse-order `shutdown().await`; children are torn down before parents (child depends on parent), which the composer must order (context-close).

#### R2 — Scoped resolution view: no parent field; a per-resolution layered ResolutionScope walks an explicit ordered chain of engines
Invert the relationship: an engine knows NOTHING about a parent. Instead, hierarchy is a property of the RESOLUTION REQUEST — a `ResolutionScope<'a>` is constructed for a lookup and carries an ordered, borrowed slice of engine layers (lowest/child first … highest/root last). `struct ResolutionScope<'a> { layers: &'a [&'a Engine] }` (or a small cons-list `Layer<'a> { engine: &'a Engine, parent: Option<&'a Layer<'a>> }` to avoid even a slice alloc). The engine exposes ONLY local primitives (`local_get`, `local_candidates`, `local_resolve_one`, `local_names`, `local_message`); ALL hierarchy logic lives in `ResolutionScope`, which is the single place that encodes the asymmetry: `resolve_one(ty, desc)` iterates layers low→high, returns the FIRST layer whose `local_resolve_one` yields a unique winner (lowest-factory-wins by iteration order; a layer's local ambiguity fails the whole resolve at that layer); `get_by_name(name)` returns the first layer that contains it locally (shadow-by-position); `contains_local` = `layers[0].local_contains`; `message` walks low→high on miss. LISTING is NOT on `ResolutionScope` at all by default — `names()`/`beans_for_type()` are local methods on the bottom engine; the SPANNING variant is an EXPLICIT, separately-named `ResolutionScope::names_across_layers()` that walks all layers and de-dups by name keeping the lowest — so 'listing is local' is the default you get from the engine, and 'spanning' is something you reach for on the scope. This makes the hierarchy a borrowed, allocation-light, per-request construct: a request-scoped resolve builds a `ResolutionScope` over [request-engine, session-engine, app-engine] cheaply on the stack. The chain is assembled by the composer (the bootstrap/context layer) when it builds the layered contexts; the engine itself is hierarchy-agnostic, so a bare engine is trivially 'a one-layer scope'.

**Pros:** Zero hierarchy cost and zero Arc baked into the engine: an engine is hierarchy-AGNOSTIC, so the common bare/single-context case has literally no parent machinery; the chain is a borrowed `&[&Engine]` (or stack cons-list) built per-resolution — strongest §2.5 (stack over heap, no gratuitous Arc) of the three.; The asymmetry is centralized in ONE type (`ResolutionScope`): listing-is-local because the spanning method lives only on the scope and is separately named; injection-is-hierarchy-aware because `resolve_one` on the scope iterates layers. The 'most common practitioner surprise' becomes an explicit, single-site, reviewable policy rather than scattered delegation.; Naturally generalizes to N-level / non-tree layering and to SCOPE chains (request→session→application), which Spring's strict parent FIELD does NOT model well — the same `ResolutionScope` mechanism serves both parent/child CONTEXT hierarchies and scope hierarchies (a unification lever, §2.11), letting scopes and parent-contexts share one resolution-walk primitive.; Borrowed layers mean lifetimes enforce that a child's resolution cannot outlive its parent engine — the borrow checker encodes 'parent must outlive child' that R1 leaves to Arc refcounting; a genuinely rust-native safety win.; Bare engine = one-layer scope: the 'engine knows nothing about hierarchy' keeps the bean-registry engine's API smaller and its semver surface narrower; hierarchy is purely additive policy in the context layer.; Cycle-by-construction-impossible: a `ResolutionScope` is an ordered list built fresh per resolve; there is no persistent parent pointer to accidentally make cyclic.

**Cons:** Biggest divergence from Spring's mental model and API: there is no `getParentBeanFactory()`/`setParentBeanFactory()` field — code that expects to ASK an engine for its parent (Aware-style `BeanFactoryAware` analogues, programmatic walks) has nowhere to look; the parent relationship is ambient in the resolution call, not a queryable property, which complicates any feature that wants to introspect the hierarchy.; Lifetimes leak: `ResolutionScope<'a>` and borrowed `&'a Engine` layers thread a lifetime through every resolution-driving API (autowiring, ObjectProvider/deferral-primitives, scoped-proxies that resolve lazily LATER) — a deferred/lazy resolver that outlives the stack frame can't hold a borrowed scope, forcing an owned `Arc`-layer variant anyway (partially undoing the no-Arc win) for any deferred handle.; Who OWNS the layer list and ensures it's the SAME chain on every resolve becomes the composer's responsibility — if two code paths build different layer orders for 'the same' child context, resolution silently diverges; R1's single stored parent field can't drift like this.; Async + borrowed scope is awkward: a `BoxFuture` that borrows `ResolutionScope<'a>` across `.await` forces the scope (and all borrowed engines) to outlive the await, constraining how upward delegation interleaves with async construction — the borrow can fight the async-first requirement on deferred paths.; Per-container post-processor isolation is LESS obvious: since the engine doesn't own a parent, the 'a parent BPP can't see child beans' corollary must be re-established by the composer never running a parent's post-processors over a child's engine — true here but not structurally enforced the way R1's separate engines make it.; Singleton identity across the chain needs care: a parent singleton resolved through a child's scope must be the parent's ONE cached instance (not re-created per scope); the scope must delegate to `layers[i].local_resolve_one` which hits the parent's OWN singleton cache — correct, but the indirection makes 'where is this singleton cached' less obvious than R1's direct parent engine.

**Mechanisms considered:** per-resolution `ResolutionScope<'a>` holding an ordered borrowed `&[&Engine]` (or stack cons-list) low→high; engine is hierarchy-agnostic; all hierarchy logic centralized on the scope: resolve_one iterates layers (lowest-wins), get_by_name first-local-hit, contains_local = bottom layer only; listing-local by default (engine method); spanning is a separately-named `names_across_layers` on the scope that de-dups shadowed names keeping lowest; borrow-checker enforces parent-outlives-child (no Arc on the engine link); cons-list avoids slice allocation; one resolution-walk primitive shared by parent/child CONTEXT hierarchies AND scope chains (request→session→application) — unification; owned `Arc`-layer variant as the escape hatch for deferred/lazy resolvers (ObjectProvider, scoped-proxy) that outlive the stack frame

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Most decoupled from the discovery substrate: the engine has no hierarchy state, so each engine is independently populated by whatever Phase-2 substrate picks, and the CHAIN is assembled purely in the composer (the bootstrap/context layer in the binary) — a build-time codegen path could even EMIT the layer order as data, and a runtime path builds it at startup, substrate-neutral. leaf-core hosts `Engine` (hierarchy-free) and `ResolutionScope`; the context/boot layer owns chain assembly. The self-check stays per-engine (each layer independently verifies its expected infrastructure), and because the composer explicitly constructs the chain, it is the natural place to assert 'every expected layer is present and ordered' — turning a mis-assembled hierarchy into a loud composer-time error. The WASM-module duality slots in as an additional LAYER in the chain (a WASM-backed engine inserted at a chosen position), or as a dynamic source feeding one engine — origin-agnostic because the scope only calls `local_*` on `&Engine` trait objects (CANDIDATES, not a decision). Tension: the borrowed-`&Engine` chain pushes toward all engines being co-located/owned by one composer; a dynamically-loaded WASM engine with an independent lifetime would need the owned-`Arc`-layer variant — flagged.

**T2 — execution context:** Building a `ResolutionScope` is synchronous, stack-allocated, zero-IO cold work done at the START of a resolution. The async seam is the same as R1 — `local_resolve_one`/`local_get`/`local_message` on a higher layer return boxed futures awaited on miss — but the SCOPE must remain borrowed-alive across those awaits, so the resolution driver holds the scope on its stack frame for the duration (fine for an inline resolve; the constraint bites for DEFERRED resolves where the owned-Arc-layer variant is needed so the handle can outlive the frame). Local-hit on the bottom layer is the hot path: no await, no alloc, no chain walk past index 0. Lowest-wins iteration is a cheap synchronous loop over `&[&Engine]`; only the layer that actually constructs awaits.
