Cancellation: dropping the resolution future drops the borrowed scope and abandons any in-flight higher-layer construction (each layer's own creation guard unwinds). Errors carry the layer index/name in the assembly chain (same §2.9 cross-level diagnostic as R1, but the layer list is RIGHT THERE in the scope, making 'which layers were searched, in order' trivially reportable). No global lock; each layer's singleton cache governs its own creation. Teardown is per-engine `shutdown().await` ordered by the composer (children/lower layers first), since the scope is transient and owns nothing.

#### R3 — Hierarchy as a composable ResolverSource SPI: the parent is just another origin-agnostic source plugged into a unified resolution chain
Push unification (§2.11) and origin-agnosticism hardest: treat 'delegate to a parent container' as ONE instance of a general pattern — a resolution may consult an ordered list of `ResolverSource`s, of which 'a parent container' is merely one impl alongside 'local engine', and (later) 'a WASM-module source' or 'a remote source'. `trait ResolverSource: Send + Sync { fn provenance(&self) -> SourceId; fn get_by_name<'a>(&'a self, name: &BeanName) -> BoxFuture<'a, Result<Option<ErasedBean>, ResolveError>>; fn local_candidates(&self, ty: TypeId, desc: &Descriptor) -> Vec<CandidateRef>; fn resolve_one<'a>(&'a self, ty: TypeId, desc: &Descriptor) -> BoxFuture<'a, Result<Option<ErasedBean>, ResolveError>>; fn message<'a>(&'a self, k: &MessageKey, a: &Args) -> BoxFuture<'a, Option<Resolved>>; /* NO enumerate/listing, NO mutate — visibility shape preserved */ }`. The local engine impls `ResolverSource`; a `ParentSource(Arc<dyn ResolverSource>)` wraps another container as a source. The engine holds `sources: SmallVec<[Arc<dyn ResolverSource>; 1]>` ORDERED by precedence (self at index 0, parent next, grandparent next …), and a `HierarchyPolicy` strategy that ENCODES the asymmetry as data: `resolve_one` walks sources in order applying lowest-wins; `get_by_name` first-hit; listing methods consult ONLY the source flagged `is_local`/index 0; spanning is `resolve_across(sources)` gated by an explicit `IncludeAncestors` flag. The KEY move: the precedence-and-visibility rules are a swappable `HierarchyPolicy` (default = Spring's lowest-wins + listing-local), so a context could install a stricter (e.g. fully-isolated, no upward delegation for a sandbox) or different (e.g. parent-wins for forced shared infra) policy WITHOUT changing the engine — and the same chain mechanism serves parent/child contexts, scope layering, AND the future dynamic-classpath as ADDITIONAL sources rather than bespoke branches. A bare engine is 'a chain with one local source and the default policy'.

**Pros:** Maximal origin-agnosticism and unification: 'parent container', 'local engine', 'WASM-module source', 'test-double source' are ONE trait the resolver can't distinguish — the future dynamic 'classpath' is not a special path, it's another `ResolverSource` in the chain (exactly the brief's slot-in requirement), and parent/child + scope layering + dynamic sources all reduce to ordering sources.; The visibility invariants are preserved by the trait shape (no enumerate-upward, no mutate on `ResolverSource`) AND made into an explicit, swappable `HierarchyPolicy` — so 'lowest-wins + listing-local' is the inspectable default, and a sandbox/strict-isolation context can install a different policy declaratively (the richest extensibility of the three).; Phase-2's deferred keystones become STRATEGY choices the chain already accommodates: whether the parent link is Arc-shared vs borrowed becomes 'which `ResolverSource` impl/wrapper', and the async-across-dyn boxing is localized to the one `ResolverSource` dyn seam — the engine stays sync where it can.; A single chain-walk mechanism subsumes parent/child CONTEXT hierarchy, scope chains, AND dynamic sources — fewer bespoke code paths than R1 (which special-cases 'parent') or R2 (which special-cases 'layers'); strongest §2.11 unification.; Provenance-per-source (`SourceId`) makes the §2.9 cross-level diagnostic first-class: the assembly chain reports exactly which source answered or vetoed ('local: miss; parent[root]: ambiguous X,Y; wasm[plugins]: not consulted, listing-local'), the deepest debuggability for the 'why was the parent's bean injected' surprise.; Keeps the stable ABI small and extension-surface explicit (the §6 small-stable-kernel topology): `ResolverSource` + `HierarchyPolicy` are the seams, the engine barely changes — and an integration crate could even contribute a custom source.

**Cons:** Most indirection and the heaviest abstraction tax: every upward resolve threads through `Box<dyn ResolverSource>` + a `HierarchyPolicy` dispatch + a `BoxFuture` — dyn + alloc churn on a path that R1 keeps a thin single-field delegation and R2 keeps a stack walk; in direct tension with §2.5/§2.4 unless the local-source-index-0 fast path is aggressively monomorphized.; Borderline over-engineering for Phase 1 and risks pre-deciding meta-architecture: making the WHOLE resolution a swappable-source chain with a swappable policy is itself a meta-architecture stance ('everything is a source + strategy') that Phase 2 is meant to own — must be held as a CANDIDATE shape, not a declaration.; A swappable `HierarchyPolicy` is a powerful footgun: a policy that, say, makes listing span ancestors or makes the parent win silently would VIOLATE the most load-bearing Spring intent (deterministic local listing, lowest-wins) — the very surprise the feature exists to make principled becomes user-reconfigurable, weakening the guarantee unless the default is strongly privileged and deviations are loud.; `SmallVec<[Arc<dyn ResolverSource>; 1]>` is Arc-per-source plus dyn dispatch per resolve — heavier than R1's single `Option<Arc>` and far heavier than R2's borrowed slice; the abstraction is paid on every resolve unless the index-0 local case short-circuits before touching the chain machinery.; Harder default-DX story: a chain + policy that must be assembled looks like ceremony unless leaf ships an opinionated default bundle (one local source + default policy) — at which point it resembles R1, questioning whether the SPI generality earns its weight in Phase 1.; 'Find the parent' introspection (for Aware-style features or programmatic hierarchy walks) is muddier — the parent is one source among several, identified only by `provenance()`, not a distinguished `parent()` accessor; features that genuinely need 'the parent context' must filter the chain by source kind.

**Mechanisms considered:** origin-agnostic `ResolverSource` trait (get_by_name/local_candidates/resolve_one/message, NO enumerate, NO mutate) as the single consulted unit; local engine / parent-wrapper / WASM / test-double all impls; engine holds an ORDERED `SmallVec<[Arc<dyn ResolverSource>; 1]>` (self at 0, ancestors after) + a swappable `HierarchyPolicy` encoding lowest-wins + listing-local as DATA; listing reads only the `is_local` source (index 0); spanning is an explicit `IncludeAncestors`-gated `resolve_across` that de-dups shadowed names; one chain-walk subsumes parent/child contexts, scope chains, AND the dynamic-classpath source (added as more sources) — maximal unification; provenance-per-source (`SourceId`) feeding the cross-level assembly-chain diagnostic; privileged default `HierarchyPolicy` (Spring lowest-wins/listing-local) with loud opt-out; index-0 monomorphized fast path to dodge the dyn tax on local hits

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for the cross-crate layered-source AND the two-source duality: a static link-time registry produces the local engine's definitions; a parent container is a `ParentSource`; an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source is ANOTHER `ResolverSource` — ALL plug into the same ordered chain via one API, so parent/child + dynamic discovery are producers of one source stream, NOT separate code paths (most natural of the three for the WASM-module slot-in; CANDIDATES — static link-time registry PLUS optional dynamic source as additional sources — not a decision). leaf-core hosts `ResolverSource`/`HierarchyPolicy`/`SourceId` — a minimal ultra-stable ABI; the thin macro never touches it (hierarchy is composed at runtime/bootstrap, not emitted per-bean). The self-check generalizes: per-source provenance lets the composer assert expected-vs-found PER SOURCE and name a source that contributed zero (the anti-DCE defense, now hierarchy-aware). The `HierarchyPolicy`/`ResolverSource` SPIs are themselves a cross-crate extension surface (an integration crate could ship a custom source/policy) — powerful but widens the stable-ABI commitment, a real cost. Substrate-neutral on HOW each source's contents are collected, by design; whether a build-time codegen path can pre-bake the source ORDER vs assembling it at runtime is left open.

**T2 — execution context:** Chain assembly (ordering sources, installing the policy) is synchronous cold-path bootstrap work. The single async seam is `ResolverSource::resolve_one`/`get_by_name`/`message` (boxed future at the dyn boundary — origin-agnosticism REQUIRES the dyn boundary so WASM/test-double/parent sources slot in, so boxing is an accepted structural cost: cold for singletons, a Phase-2 hot-path concern for prototype/scoped resolves that fall through the chain). The local source (index 0) SHOULD be consulted via a monomorphized fast path that neither boxes nor dyn-dispatches on a local hit — the chain machinery engages only on local miss. `HierarchyPolicy` decisions (which source next, fail-on-local-ambiguity) are synchronous between awaits. Construction may await but must not block (spawn_blocking for blocking sources — execution-context discipline; Phase 2 owns propagation). Cancellation unwinds the in-flight source's `resolve_one` future; each source's own creation guard (the parent engine's, the WASM bridge's) governs its construction, so NO new global lock is introduced — the chain only reads through `Arc<dyn ResolverSource>`. Errors are a rich ResolveError attributing the failing/vetoing source by `provenance()` (§2.9), giving precise cross-level/cross-source diagnostics. No async Drop: each source-backed container drives its own `shutdown().await`; the composer orders teardown (lower/child sources before higher/parent) — context-close. The policy boundary is also where per-resolution execution context could be declared per source kind (e.g. a remote source is awaitable-but-cancel-on-deadline).

### Rust clashes
- Classloader-rooted lifetime vs Rust ownership — THE keystone clash for this feature. Spring's parent/child mirrors classloader-parent delegation, and a child context simply holds a reference to a GC-rooted parent context that the GC keeps alive as long as anyone references it; cross-level injection hands the child a freely-aliased reference to a parent's singleton. In Rust the parent→child relationship must commit to a SHARING/lifetime model for the parent link: `Arc<dyn ParentView/ResolverSource>` (R1/R3 — refcounted, commits to Arc and Send+Sync on the parent, fights §2.5-minimal-Arc) vs a borrowed `&'a Engine` chain (R2 — borrow checker enforces parent-outlives-child with zero Arc, but lifetimes leak into every resolution-driving API and break for deferred/lazy/scoped-proxy handles that outlive the stack frame). And a parent singleton injected into a child must be the parent's ONE cached instance shared into the child — which re-raises the scope/ownership keystone (Phase 2 §3): the cross-level handle must stay origin-agnostic (so a WASM-module parent publishes the same handle) WITHOUT prejudging Arc-vs-borrow.
- The injection-hierarchy-aware / listing-local-only ASYMMETRY has no automatic Rust analogue and must be MANUFACTURED. On the JVM it's a behavioral convention enforced in code (`resolveDependency` walks ancestors; `getBeanNamesForType` doesn't; `BeanFactoryUtils.*IncludingAncestors` is the opt-in). Rust gives no help here — leaf must DELIBERATELY split the API so the upward-walking resolution path and the local-only listing path are DIFFERENT methods/types, with spanning a separately-named opt-in that de-dups shadowed names. Getting this wrong (a listing method that silently delegates upward, or a resolve that doesn't) reproduces the exact 'most common practitioner surprise' the feature is supposed to make principled — and Rust offers no runtime reflection to paper over a mistake.
- Lowest-factory-wins for by-TYPE single resolution requires cross-level candidate reasoning the type index can't do alone. Spring resolves a by-type injection at the lowest level that has ANY candidate, and a local same-named/same-type bean shadows the parent's — but each engine's TypeId index is LOCAL (no runtime isAssignableFrom, no cross-container type walk). So 'lowest-wins' must be encoded as an explicit walk policy (local candidate set first; delegate only if local is EMPTY; fail locally if local is AMBIGUOUS) rather than a single merged index — and the subtle edge (does a local non-unique set still beat a unique parent match? Spring resolves at the lowest level with candidates) must be specified by hand, because there is no global type graph spanning containers to consult.
- No queryable parent without a stored link — the `getParentBeanFactory()` introspection (used by Aware-style features and programmatic hierarchy walks) presumes the container CAN be asked for its parent. R1/R3 store a link so this works; R2 makes the parent ambient in the resolution call, so 'ask the engine for its parent' has no answer — any feature that introspects the hierarchy (aware-callbacks injecting the parent context, programmatic-registration that pulls a parent collaborator) needs the link to be a queryable property, which constrains the realization choice.
- Async-fn-in-traits is not dyn-compatible, but the upward-delegation seam MUST be dyn AND origin-agnostic — a `ParentView`/`ResolverSource` must be dyn-storable (so native, test-double, and future WASM-module parents slot in) and its resolve/get/message methods are async → boxed futures (`Pin<Box<dyn Future>>`), an alloc on every CROSS-LEVEL resolution. Cold for parent singletons resolved once at child startup, but a real cost for request/prototype-scoped lookups that fall through to the parent on a hot path — leaf must keep the LOCAL-hit path monomorphized/zero-alloc and accept boxing only on the upward miss path (the async-execution-model decision is Phase 2's).
- No async Drop, and teardown ORDER is hierarchy-constrained. A child depends on its parent (it injects parent beans), so the child must be torn down BEFORE the parent (reverse dependency order across the boundary), and each container drives its own explicit `shutdown().await` (no Drop can do async cleanup of DB-pool singletons). Spring's GC + classloader teardown handles cross-context lifetime implicitly; leaf must make the composer ORDER cross-level teardown explicitly (children/lower-layers first), and a parent cannot be dropped while a child still references it — which the Arc-link (R1/R3) enforces by refcount and the borrow-chain (R2) enforces by lifetime, two different non-syntactic disciplines for the same constraint.
- Per-container post-processor isolation must be structural, not implicit. Spring's no-downward-visibility makes 'a parent's BeanPostProcessor physically cannot process child beans' a free corollary (each context has its own BPP list). In Rust this is only free if each container OWNS its own post-processor set and the composer never runs a parent's processors over a child's engine — R1/R3 (separate engines, each with its own stores) get this structurally; R2 (hierarchy-agnostic engines + composer-built chains) must re-establish it by composer discipline. Either way leaf must ensure the per-container isolation isn't accidentally broken by a shared/global post-processor registry (which would also be a §2.5 global-state violation).

### Interactions
- **bean-registry** — container-hierarchy is layered ON TOP of bean-registry, NOT inside it: the registry's `resolve_one`/`candidates_for_type`/`get_by_name`/`names`/`contains_local` are PER-CONTAINER local primitives; the hierarchy adds upward delegation between engines (R1's parent field, R2's layer chain, R3's source chain). bean-registry explicitly noted this interaction — resolution is hierarchy-aware (local miss delegates upward, local shadows parent) but LISTING (`names()`/by-type enumeration) is deliberately LOCAL-ONLY. The hierarchy honors that asymmetry without reopening the three-store internals; each engine keeps its own singleton cache, so a parent singleton resolved through a child is the parent's ONE cached instance.
- **autowiring-resolution** — This is WHERE the injection-hierarchy-aware half of the asymmetry lives: single-valued injection runs the LOCAL fail-fast tie-break first; on a local miss it delegates upward (lowest-factory-wins — the lowest level with a candidate provides it). The crucial contract: AMBIGUITY at a level fails LOCALLY at that level (no-guess-on-ambiguity per autowiring-resolution) and does NOT silently fall through to the parent. The hierarchy must specify the lowest-wins edge precisely (local-empty→delegate; local-ambiguous→fail-local) on top of autowiring-resolution's layered policy.
- **collection-injection** — The listing-local-only half of the asymmetry directly shapes collection injection: by-type List/Set/Map injection is LOCAL-ONLY by default and does NOT silently merge ancestors (Spring's rationale: avoid non-obvious results and double-counting). Spanning the hierarchy for a collection is an EXPLICIT opt-in (`*IncludingAncestors`) that collapses a shadowed name to ONE entry. The hierarchy must expose the spanning variant as a distinct, named API and apply shadow-de-dup so an overridden bean isn't double-counted across levels.
- **bean-naming** — Local shadowing IS name-based across levels: a child bean with the same NAME as a parent's shadows it ('lowest factory wins'). This extends bean-naming's collision/override model across the hierarchy boundary — but cross-level shadowing is SILENT-and-intended (the isolation lever), in deliberate contrast to bean-naming's WITHIN-container loud collision error. The hierarchy must distinguish 'same name in child shadows parent (intended, silent)' from 'same name twice in one container (loud BeanDefinitionOverrideException)'. Spanning-listing de-dup also keys on name to collapse shadows.
- **container-core** — The parent/child relationship lives at the CONTEXT/façade altitude container-core defines, not the bare engine: container-core fixed the engine-vs-façade split and HAS-A composition; the hierarchy is a relationship between façades/contexts (Spring's `HierarchicalBeanFactory` is part of the BeanFactory interface family, `setParentBeanFactory` on the writable SPI). The 'plain engine is dumb' split also means a bare engine has no parent machinery (R2/R3 make a bare engine trivially a one-layer/one-source chain); the context layer is where parent wiring and per-container post-processor isolation are established.
- **messages-i18n** — MessageSource is the canonical OTHER hierarchy-aware lookup: message resolution walks the parent chain on a local miss, exactly like bean lookup (Spring's `MessageSource` is hierarchy-aware). The hierarchy's upward-delegation surface (R1's `ParentView::message`, R3's `ResolverSource::message`) must carry message resolution as a first-class facet, not just bean resolution — confirming the upward-walk primitive generalizes beyond beans and is a reason the delegation trait anticipates multiple 'walk-on-miss' facets.
- **post-processor-spi** — Direct corollary of no-downward-visibility: post-processors are scoped PER-CONTAINER — a parent's BFPP/BPP physically cannot process child beans (each container has its own processor list). The hierarchy must ensure each engine owns its own post-processor set and a parent's processors never run over a child's beans (structural in R1/R3 via separate engines; composer-enforced in R2). This bounds what 'shared infrastructure up' can do: shared BEANS yes, shared bean-PROCESSING of child beans no.
- **scopes** — Strong unification candidate (esp. R2/R3): the same upward-walk-on-miss mechanism that serves parent/child CONTEXT hierarchies could serve SCOPE chains (request→session→application→singleton), where a shorter-lived scope delegates to a longer-lived one. Whether parent/child and scope layering share ONE resolution-walk primitive or stay separate is a unification lever (§2.11). The hierarchy must keep its delegation mechanism general enough that scopes can reuse it, without prejudging the scope/ownership model (Phase 2 §3).
- **scoped-proxies** — A scoped-proxy resolves its live target LATER, potentially after the resolving stack frame is gone — which stresses R2's borrowed `ResolutionScope<'a>` (the borrow can't outlive the frame) and pushes toward an owned/Arc-held delegation handle for deferred resolution. The hierarchy's choice of parent-link lifetime model (borrowed vs Arc) must accommodate a proxy that holds a cross-level resolution capability beyond the immediate call — a concrete tension feeding the Phase-2 ownership decision.
- **deferral-primitives** — ObjectProvider/ObjectFactory analogues that defer or repeat resolution must capture the HIERARCHY-AWARE resolution path (so a deferred 0..1/0..N lookup still walks ancestors for single-resolve and stays local for listing). Like scoped-proxies, a deferred provider can outlive the stack frame, so the hierarchy's delegation handle must be holdable beyond the immediate resolve (Arc-link works; borrowed-chain needs the owned variant) — the deferral primitives are a primary consumer that constrains the parent-link lifetime model.
- **context-close** — No async Drop means cross-level teardown ORDER is explicit and hierarchy-constrained: a child (which injects parent beans) must be shut down BEFORE its parent (reverse dependency order across the boundary), each via its own `shutdown().await`. context-close defines the stop-then-destroy-in-reverse sequence; the hierarchy adds the cross-container ordering constraint (children/lower-layers first) the composer must honor, and the parent-link must keep the parent alive until all children are torn down (Arc refcount in R1/R3, lifetime in R2).
- **aware-callbacks** — Aware-style injection of container infrastructure (the `BeanFactoryAware`/`ApplicationContextAware` analogues) presumes a bean can receive ITS context and, transitively, query the parent — which requires the parent to be a queryable property (favoring R1/R3's stored link; R2's ambient-in-resolution parent has nothing to hand to an Aware bean). The hierarchy must decide whether 'the parent context' is introspectable infrastructure an Aware bean can be handed; this interaction argues against a purely ambient (R2) parent unless an owned-link variant backs it.
- **events** — Spring delivers an event published in a child context ALSO to listeners in ancestor contexts (context-hierarchy double-delivery), the event-bus analogue of upward delegation. While event-multicaster owns dispatch, the hierarchy defines the visibility along which events propagate upward — and the same no-downward-visibility rule means a parent's event does NOT auto-deliver to child listeners. leaf must reconcile event propagation direction with the bean-visibility direction (both upward), and surface the multi-context double-fire as a known, disambiguable behavior (compare the publishing context).
- **environment** — Spring's hierarchical contexts also merge Environments with child-wins semantics (`AbstractEnvironment.merge(parent)` keeps the child's identically-named PropertySource and dedups profiles). This is the environment-layer mirror of bean shadowing (lowest/child wins). The hierarchy's one-directional, child-shadows-parent rule recurs in the property model; leaf should keep the bean-visibility and environment-merge rules consistent (both child-wins, both one-directional) even though property-sources owns the merge mechanics.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Parent-link lifetime/ownership model (the keystone clash for THIS feature, feeding §3 scope/ownership): is the parent held as `Arc<dyn ParentView/ResolverSource>` (R1/R3 — refcounted, commits to Arc + Send+Sync on the parent, fights §2.5) or as a borrowed `&'a Engine` chain (R2 — zero Arc, borrow-checker enforces parent-outlives-child, but lifetimes leak into every resolution API and break for deferred/scoped-proxy handles that outlive the stack frame)? This depends on the cross-level shared-singleton handle type, which IS the scope/ownership keystone — and it must stay origin-agnostic so a WASM-module parent publishes the same handle. Cross-cuts scopes, scoped-proxies, deferral-primitives, aware-callbacks.
- Is the parent a QUERYABLE property of the container (stored link, R1/R3) or AMBIENT in the resolution request (R2's `ResolutionScope`)? aware-callbacks and programmatic hierarchy walks want to ask 'what is my parent?'; R2 has no answer without an owned-link variant. This decides whether hierarchy is engine state or a per-resolution construct, and interacts with the metadata/codegen-vs-runtime split (can a build-time path pre-bake the hierarchy, or is it purely runtime composition?).
- Should the SAME upward-walk-on-miss primitive serve BOTH parent/child CONTEXT hierarchies AND scope chains (request→session→application→singleton), per the §2.11 unification goal — or are they separate mechanisms? R2/R3 naturally unify them; R1's strict single-parent field does not. Resolving this needs the scope/ownership model (Phase 2 §3) and touches scopes, scoped-proxies, deferral-primitives.
- Is hierarchy a fixed engine relationship (R1's single parent field) or a swappable resolution-source/policy chain (R3's `ResolverSource` + `HierarchyPolicy`)? The chain unifies parent/child + scopes + the future dynamic-classpath as ordered sources and lets a sandbox install a stricter policy, but costs the monomorphized fast path, widens the stable ABI, makes the load-bearing 'lowest-wins + listing-local' guarantee user-reconfigurable (a footgun), and borders on pre-deciding the meta-architecture Phase 2 owns. How much pluggability is warranted?
- How is the listing-local / injection-hierarchy-aware ASYMMETRY surfaced in the API so it is impossible to get wrong AND not a practitioner surprise (§2.9 — the feature exists to make this principled)? Concretely: are listing and spanning DIFFERENT named methods (so 'span ancestors' is an explicit opt-in that de-dups shadowed names), and is the upward-resolve path structurally distinct from the local-listing path? This interacts with the unified diagnostics model (the cross-level assembly chain must explain WHICH levels were searched and WHY a parent bean was injected).
- Lowest-factory-wins for by-TYPE single resolution: precise semantics of the cross-level walk when the local candidate set is non-empty-but-ambiguous vs empty. Spring resolves at the lowest level that has ANY candidate and fails on local ambiguity rather than delegating — leaf must pin this edge by hand (no global cross-container type graph exists). Interacts with autowiring-resolution's no-guess-on-ambiguity contract and candidate-resolver matching across levels.
- Async-across-dyn boxing on the CROSS-LEVEL resolution path: the parent-delegation seam must be dyn (so native/test-double/WASM parents slot in) and async-not-dyn-safe → boxed future per upward resolve. Standard = boxed-future-on-upward-miss with a monomorphized zero-alloc LOCAL-hit fast path? Acceptable for parent singletons resolved once at startup, but a cost for request/prototype lookups that fall through to the parent on a hot path — the §3 async-execution-model viewed through the cross-level get path.
- Cross-container teardown ordering and the no-async-Drop constraint: how does the composer guarantee children/lower-layers are `shutdown().await`-ed BEFORE parents (reverse cross-boundary dependency order), and keep a parent alive until all children are torn down? Arc-refcount (R1/R3) vs lifetime (R2) are two disciplines for the same constraint. Interacts with context-close and the broader lifecycle ordering across a multi-context application.
- The two-source duality across the hierarchy: a static link-time registry feeds each engine, and an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source feeds a designated engine OR slots in as an additional resolution source (R3). Does the dynamic source attach to a specific level, and how does its (independent) lifetime reconcile with a borrowed-chain hierarchy (R2) that wants co-located engines? CANDIDATE, not a decision — interacts with the cross-crate composition model and the registry substrate.
- Per-container isolation as structure vs discipline: post-processor sets, namespaces, and resolvers are per-container (a parent BPP can't process child beans). Is this enforced structurally (separate engines each owning their stores — R1/R3) or by composer discipline (R2's hierarchy-agnostic engines)? And does any shared/global infrastructure registry risk both breaking isolation AND violating §2.5 (no global state)? Interacts with post-processor-spi and the cross-crate self-check (which must run per-container).

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent — I read the dedicated Spring sections (lines 179-188: upward delegation, one-directional visibility, child-shadows-parent, MessageSource-walks-chain, the `containsLocalBean` isolation lever and the 'why is the parent's bean injected here?' surprise; lines 2692-2702: `HierarchicalBeanFactory.getParentBeanFactory()`/`containsLocalBean`/`setParentBeanFactory` on the writable SPI, `getBean` delegates-upward-on-local-miss, lowest-factory-wins even for by-type, and THE central asymmetry — listing-local-only vs injection-hierarchy-aware, `*IncludingAncestors` as the de-dup opt-in, and the post-processors-are-per-container corollary; line 53: the layered-isolation design intent; line 1315: the parallel child-wins Environment merge; line 1903: context-hierarchy event double-delivery; line 232: hierarchy semantics UNCHANGED in v4/v7). I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc (Layer-0/A/B DCE, the per-container self-check, leaf-core ABI / thin-macro absolute-path discipline, Architectures A-D as composable substrates) and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimal-Arc/stack-over-heap, §2.9 rich-early-diagnostics, §2.11 unification) and the stable envelope (RPITIT/AFIT not dyn-safe → boxed futures at the dyn seam, trait-upcasting 1.86, `Arc<dyn Any>`/`TypeId`-as-key, NO async Drop, no runtime reflection / no cross-container type graph).

KEY framing: container-hierarchy is a relationship BETWEEN bean-registry engines, NOT an engine internal — so all three realizations build ON the upstream bean-registry surface (`resolve_one`/`candidates_for_type`/`names`/`contains_local`) rather than reopening its three-store internals. The single most load-bearing behavior to preserve is THE ASYMMETRY: injection/single-resolution is hierarchy-aware (walks ancestors, lowest-wins, fails locally on ambiguity), listing is deliberately LOCAL-ONLY with an explicit shadow-de-dup spanning opt-in. Getting that split wrong reproduces the exact practitioner surprise the feature exists to make principled, and Rust gives no runtime-reflection safety net — so I made the listing/spanning split structural (different named methods/types) in every realization.

The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters HERE — WHERE the parent relationship lives and HOW upward delegation is expressed: R1 = a runtime parent FIELD on the engine (`Option<Arc<dyn ParentView>>`) with a narrow read-only origin-agnostic trait whose SHAPE enforces one-directional/no-mutate/no-enumerate visibility (literal HierarchicalBeanFactory, highest fidelity, queryable parent, Arc-on-link); R2 = NO parent field — hierarchy is a per-resolution borrowed `ResolutionScope<'a>` over an ordered `&[&Engine]` chain, centralizing the asymmetry in one type, zero-Arc/stack-friendly and borrow-checker-enforces parent-outlives-child, generalizing to scope chains, but lifetimes leak and break for deferred/scoped-proxy handles and there's no queryable parent; R3 = hierarchy as a composable `ResolverSource` SPI + swappable `HierarchyPolicy`, where 'parent' is one origin-agnostic source among local/WASM/test-double, maximal unification + WASM-slot-in, but most indirection, makes the load-bearing guarantee user-reconfigurable (footgun), and borders on pre-deciding meta-architecture. R1↔R2 differ on stored-link-vs-ambient (and Arc-vs-borrow); R3 differs on fixed-relationship-vs-pluggable-source-chain and composes R1/R2's notions as source impls.

Three things to carry forward: (1) the parent-link LIFETIME model (Arc vs borrow) is the local face of the §3 scope/ownership keystone — every realization keeps the cross-level handle (`ErasedBean`/`CandidateRef`) abstract and origin-agnostic so a WASM-module parent publishes identically; I refused to pick. (2) deferral-primitives and scoped-proxies are the concrete consumers that STRESS the borrowed-chain (R2) — a deferred handle outliving the stack frame forces an owned/Arc variant, a real tension flagged as a phase2Question, not resolved. (3) the unification question — does ONE upward-walk-on-miss primitive serve BOTH parent/child contexts AND scope chains (request→session→application)? — is the biggest §2.11 lever this feature exposes; R2/R3 naturally unify, R1 doesn't, and I left it open for Phase 2.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: no realization decides the registry substrate, the scope/ownership model, the async-across-dyn boxing standard, whether the hierarchy is runtime-composed vs build-time-pre-baked, or whether the parent is stored vs ambient. The two-source duality (static link-time registry per engine + optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source feeding a designated engine or slotting in as another source) is captured as CANDIDATES (most natural in R3) and flagged as a tension with R2's co-located borrowed chain — not a decision. Naming ('ParentView', 'ResolutionScope', 'ResolverSource', 'HierarchyPolicy', 'ErasedBean', 'CandidateRef', '*_including_ancestors') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Relevant grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (53 layered-isolation intent; 179-188 upward-delegation + one-directional + containsLocalBean + the surprise; 1315 child-wins Environment merge; 1903 event double-delivery; 2692-2702 HierarchicalBeanFactory surface + listing-local-vs-injection-aware asymmetry + per-container post-processors); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3, topology §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/A/B DCE silent-failure + self-check mandate, thin-macro/absolute-path leaf-core ABI, Architectures A-D composable substrates, WASM weak-spot); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (trait upcasting 1.86, const TypeId 1.91, Arc<dyn Any>, RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe → boxed futures, no async Drop, no runtime reflection).

## Container Extension SPIs (BeanFactoryPostProcessor / BeanPostProcessor)  `[post-processor-spi]`

Spring's container is built on its own two public extension tiers, and dogfoods nearly every core feature (@Configuration parsing, @Autowired/@Value, @PostConstruct, AOP, ${...} placeholders) as ordinary infrastructure beans on them. The load-bearing distinction is BLUEPRINT vs PRODUCT: a BeanFactoryPostProcessor (BFPP) edits DEFINITIONS — rewrites/sets values on already-loaded BeanDefinitions (placeholder resolution, scope changes) — before ANY non-processor bean is instantiated and must NOT instantiate beans (doing so triggers premature instantiation that bypasses other BFPPs); a BeanDefinitionRegistryPostProcessor (BDRPP, a BFPP sub-tier) may additionally ADD/REMOVE definitions via postProcessBeanDefinitionRegistry, which runs strictly BEFORE all postProcessBeanFactory callbacks (so e.g. ConfigurationClassPostProcessor — BDRPP at HIGHEST_PRECEDENCE — registers @Bean defs before a placeholder configurer resolves ${...} inside them). A BeanPostProcessor (BPP) decorates INSTANCES: postProcessBeforeInitialization (before @PostConstruct/init) and postProcessAfterInitialization (after), either of which may RETURN A DIFFERENT OBJECT — exactly how AOP swaps in a proxy. Two specialized BPP sub-tiers carry hidden machinery: MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor reads the read-only merged definition to discover/cache injection metadata (and re-registers LAST into an internal list so metadata processing lands at a well-defined late point), and SmartInstantiationAwareBeanPostProcessor.getEarlyBeanReference lets the auto-proxy creator publish a PROXY as the early reference into a cycle. Ordering is the structural enforcement: refresh runs invokeBeanFactoryPostProcessors then registerBeanPostProcessors, each partitioning processors into three sorted phases — PriorityOrdered (the bootstrap layer, e.g. config parsing/autowiring that must win before anything is even sorted) > Ordered > unordered — so framework-critical processors guarantee precedence a user cannot accidentally reorder via plain @Order. The definition-freeze boundary IS the 'no more BFPP edits' line (markBeanAsCreated). Two accepted costs are explicitly documented, not hidden: processors are eagerly instantiated front (a processor cannot process a bean built before it exists), and consequently BPPs AND the beans they directly reference are NOT eligible for auto-proxying. leaf must replicate this INTENT — two ordered, role-detected extension tiers (edit-definitions vs decorate-instances, with the merged-def and early-reference sub-tiers), auto-discovered as infrastructure contributions, the framework's own features built on the SAME public hooks, with a hard 'definitions frozen / no more edits' boundary and deterministic priority ordering — WITHOUT importing the JVM mechanisms it sheds: the 'auto-detect by Java type via instanceof' role discovery (Rust has no runtime type-walk over registered beans), the proxy-as-returned-Object identity swap (no CGLIB; leaf's proxy story is unsettled), life-before-main side effects, or the assumption that a processor is always a natively-constructed bean. The 'returns a different object' instance-decoration must stay ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC so a WASM-module-provided processor (or its target) decorates identically; and the discovery of processors must be SCOPED (the static link-time 'classpath' PLUS an optional dynamic source), not implicitly global. It must NOT pick the registry substrate, the proxy/early-reference mechanism, the async-across-dyn boxing decision, or whether tiers unify into one hook — those are Phase 2.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Two literal ordered trait tiers (DefinitionPostProcessor / InstancePostProcessor), role-detected as infrastructure descriptors
Mirror Spring's two tiers as two distinct Rust traits the engine drives at two fixed points in refresh-lifecycle, with role discovered from the DESCRIPTOR (a declared role flag + which trait the type implements), never from a runtime type-walk. Definition tier: `trait DefinitionPostProcessor { fn order(&self) -> Order; async fn post_process_definitions(&self, reg: &mut dyn DefinitionRegistry, env: &Environment) -> Result<(), PpError>; }` (the BFPP analogue — it gets &mut access to the definition store but the store's API offers ONLY edit/read of existing defs); and a sub-trait `trait DefinitionRegistryPostProcessor: DefinitionPostProcessor { async fn post_process_registry(&self, reg: &mut dyn DefinitionRegistry) -> Result<(), PpError>; }` (the BDRPP analogue — its registry handle additionally exposes add/remove). Instance tier: `trait InstancePostProcessor { fn order(&self) -> Order; async fn before_init(&self, bean: ErasedBean, ctx: &BeanCtx<'_>) -> Result<ErasedBean, PpError>; async fn after_init(&self, bean: ErasedBean, ctx: &BeanCtx<'_>) -> Result<ErasedBean, PpError>; }` returning a possibly-DIFFERENT ErasedBean (origin-agnostic handle, the bean-registry keystone) — the seam through which a future proxy mechanism swaps identity. Two specialized sub-traits: `trait MergedDefinitionPostProcessor: InstancePostProcessor { fn post_process_merged(&self, merged: &MergedBeanDefinition, ty: TypeId, name: &BeanName); }` (sync, reads the read-only merged def to cache injection metadata) and `trait EarlyRefPostProcessor: InstancePostProcessor { fn early_reference(&self, bean: &ErasedBean, ctx: &BeanCtx<'_>) -> Option<ErasedBean>; }` (the getEarlyBeanReference analogue for cycles). Discovery: each tier has its own role-tagged descriptor (`PostProcessorDescriptor { role: Bfpp|Bdrpp|Bpp, order: Order, tier_flags, make: fn(&Container)->BoxFuture<Result<ErasedPostProcessor>> }`) collected via the SAME catalog substrate beans use (Phase-2 picks linkme/codegen/explicit) — the engine partitions by role at refresh, never by inspecting instances. Ordering: an explicit three-band `Order { Priority(i32) | Ordered(i32) | Unordered }` enum, sorted Priority>Ordered>Unordered, so framework processors declare Priority and a user @Order cannot preempt them. The engine wires the SPI into refresh exactly as Spring: (1) run ALL post_process_registry (re-scanning to catch newly-added BDRPPs) before any post_process_definitions; (2) run all post_process_definitions; (3) FREEZE definitions; (4) instantiate+register all InstancePostProcessors (MergedDefinitionPostProcessors pulled into an internal list re-appended LAST); (5) only then build application singletons, running before_init/merged/after_init per bean. leaf's own features (placeholder resolution, injection-metadata, conversion) ship as Priority-band processors — the dogfooding parity.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: the BFPP/BDRPP/BPP/MergedBDPP/SmartInstantiationAware split maps 1:1 to five Rust traits, the three-band ordering and the 'registry callbacks before factory callbacks' and 'merged-def processors last' hidden rules are reproduced literally — maximally teachable and traceable to Spring.; Role-from-descriptor discovery is the honest rust-native replacement for Spring's instanceof auto-detect: there is no runtime type-walk over beans (T1/rustClash), so the macro/descriptor DECLARES the role and which sub-tier, and the engine partitions by data — no reflection needed, and it stays correct cross-crate.; The two-trait separation structurally enforces the core invariant (definitions edited before any instance exists): the DefinitionRegistry handle simply does not exist after freeze, and DefinitionPostProcessor has no way to get a built instance — the 'must not instantiate beans' rule becomes an API shape, not a runtime warning.; ErasedBean-returning before_init/after_init keeps instance decoration origin-agnostic and proxy-mechanism-neutral: a WASM-module processor, a test double, or a future native-proxy all swap the handle the same way, so the auto-proxy-creator/aop-proxying features slot onto this seam without the SPI prejudging how proxies are built.; Sync MergedDefinitionPostProcessor + sync EarlyRefPostProcessor avoid the boxed-future tax on the two hottest sub-hooks (injection-metadata caching runs per-bean; early-ref runs inside cycle resolution) — async is reserved for the genuinely IO-bearing before/after_init.; Each tier is independently swappable and the bare-engine-vs-context split (container-core) falls out naturally: a bare engine runs zero auto-detected processors; the context installs the standard Priority-band ones — the exact 'features silently do nothing in a plain BeanFactory' parity.

**Cons:** Five traits + a role enum + two registry-handle capability levels is the most surface area of the three; it commits early to 'two tiers stay two tiers' and forecloses the unification (§2.11) that R3 explores — Phase 2 might want one hook.; The MergedDefinitionPostProcessor re-register-LAST rule and the BDRPP-rescan-loop are subtle ordering machinery that must be specified precisely; getting the re-scan termination wrong (a BDRPP that registers another BDRPP) reintroduces Spring's own historical fragility.; `&mut dyn DefinitionRegistry` handed to an async definition processor fights Rust's aliasing/Send story: an async fn holding &mut across .await over a shared registry needs either single-threaded cold-path execution or a redesign to a message/command API — a real ergonomics cost the brief's async-first invariant surfaces.; Two registry capability levels (read/edit vs add/remove) means two handle types or a runtime-checked capability flag — either adds boilerplate (T1: the macro must emit the right one) or pushes a 'you called add() on a non-registry processor' failure to runtime.; EarlyRefPostProcessor presupposes the three-level early-reference cache exists in bean-registry; if Phase 2 decides leaf needs only two cache levels (no early-proxy), this sub-trait is speculative — it's coupled to an undecided proxy story.; Boxed-future before_init/after_init on the dyn path is unavoidable (AFIT not dyn-safe) — an alloc per processor per bean on the instance hot path unless monomorphized, which a heterogeneous processor chain cannot be.

**Mechanisms considered:** runtime registry of role-tagged PostProcessorDescriptors partitioned by the engine at refresh (no instanceof auto-detect); five distinct traits: DefinitionPostProcessor / DefinitionRegistryPostProcessor (sub) / InstancePostProcessor / MergedDefinitionPostProcessor (sub) / EarlyRefPostProcessor (sub); three-band Order enum (Priority>Ordered>Unordered) sorted at refresh; framework processors declare Priority so user @Order can't preempt; two-capability DefinitionRegistry handle (edit-only vs add/remove) gating the BFPP-vs-BDRPP distinction by API shape; sync merged-def + early-ref sub-hooks to dodge boxed-future tax; async before/after_init at the dyn boundary boxed; origin-agnostic ErasedBean in/out of before_init/after_init as the proxy/identity-swap seam

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Substrate-neutral on HOW processor descriptors are collected — leaf-core defines the five traits, the Order enum, PostProcessorDescriptor, and the DefinitionRegistry handle traits; contributing crates depend only on leaf-core and emit a role-tagged descriptor via the same catalog mechanism beans use (linkme distributed-slice / build.rs codegen / explicit with(), Phase 2 decides). The role is DATA on the descriptor (the macro emits role=Bfpp/Bdrpp/Bpp from the attribute/which trait is impl'd), so the engine partitions by reading the slice — never by a runtime instanceof, which Rust lacks (rustClash). leaf's own infrastructure processors (placeholder resolution, injection-metadata, conversion) ship in leaf-core/context crates as Priority-band descriptors installed at façade bring-up — and are absent in a bare engine, reproducing the 'silently does nothing in a plain BeanFactory' behavior across the engine/context boundary. Inherits the silent-DCE hazard: a contributing crate the binary never path-references contributes zero processors, so a feature silently does nothing exactly as Spring's plain BeanFactory — the façade self-check (expected-vs-found infrastructure-processor count by role, fail loud) is the required defense, and the engine must expose a processors-by-role introspection surface so the check is writable. Generic processors cannot register (no 'static symbol/TypeId until instantiated) → macro hard-errors with a register-concrete escape hatch. The two-source duality applies: the static link-time registry contributes native-processor descriptors; an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source contributes descriptors whose make() bridges into the module — both feed ONE role-tagged descriptor stream, CANDIDATES not a decision. The EarlyRefPostProcessor + ErasedBean seam is the cross-crate hook the aop-proxying feature plugs a proxy mechanism into without leaf-core depending on it (origin-agnostic).

**T2 — execution context:** Two execution phases, both DRIVEN by refresh-lifecycle under its startup discipline. Definition phase (post_process_registry then post_process_definitions then freeze) is COLD-PATH, single-threaded, around main: it may .await (a processor reading remote config to rewrite a def) but runs before any application bean exists, so concurrency is bounded — the &mut registry handle is held only on this single-threaded cold path, sidestepping the aliasing-across-await problem by execution-context (NOT by a global lock). The 'must not instantiate beans' rule is enforced because the definition handle exposes no get(). Instance phase: before_init/after_init are async and run during bean construction (bean-instantiation drives them per bean), inside whatever per-bean creation guard bean-registry provides — NOT under a global lock; a processor may .await (an async proxy/decoration acquiring a resource) but MUST NOT block the executor (blocking work → spawn_blocking, execution-context discipline; Phase 2 owns propagation). merged-def and early-ref sub-hooks are SYNC and run on the construction path with no .await — early-ref specifically runs inside cycle resolution where awaiting would risk re-entrancy. Eager-instantiate of the processors themselves is front-loaded (a processor can't process a bean built before it exists — Spring's documented constraint), with the accepted, documented cost that processors and their direct deps are not themselves decorated by all processors. Errors are rich PpError carrying which processor (by name+role+order) failed on which definition/bean and why, folded into the assembly chain (§2.9); a definition-phase failure aborts before any instance is built (earliest), an instance-phase failure aborts that bean's eager build all-or-nothing. No async Drop → processors needing teardown participate in the container's explicit shutdown().await (context-close), not Drop; cancellation of an in-flight before/after_init unwinds the partially-decorated bean out of the creation set.

#### R2 — Build-time pipeline of typed transform stages: the framework's own features are compiled into the assembly plan, with an escape-hatch runtime stage for user processors
Question whether leaf needs a general runtime processor SPI at all for ITS OWN features. Spring uses BFPP/BPP at runtime because the JVM has no build step and discovers everything reflectively; leaf has a codegen seam (charter §3 metadata/codegen boundary). So model the post-processor INTENT as a two-stage PIPELINE where the framework's own 'processors' are TYPED COMPILE-TIME TRANSFORMS over the assembly plan, and a thin runtime-stage SPI exists only as the escape hatch for genuinely-dynamic user processors. Stage 1 (definition transforms): the metadata/codegen layer produces an editable `AssemblyPlan` (the set of merged BeanDefinitions) and runs an ordered list of `DefinitionTransform` over it — but each framework transform (placeholder-resolution, scope-rewrite, config-class-expansion) is a concrete typed pass the codegen/startup-validation pass invokes directly, NOT an erased dyn bean. Placeholder resolution that needs runtime env reads is split: the STRUCTURE (which keys, where) is resolved at plan time; the VALUES at startup. User-supplied definition edits enter via a typed `register_transform(impl DefinitionTransform)` builder call (programmatic-registration's lane) with a declared Order, OR — for the dynamic add-defs case — via the BeanRegistrar-style functional contract rather than a free-form BDRPP. Stage 2 (instance decoration): a single ordered `DecorationChain` of `InstanceDecorator { order; async fn decorate(&self, phase: InitPhase, bean: ErasedBean, ctx) -> Result<ErasedBean> }` where InitPhase ∈ {BeforeInit, AfterInit} collapses before/after into one parameterized hook (still an identity-swap seam returning ErasedBean). Framework decorators (injection-metadata, the proxy-weaver when it exists) are concrete decorators the context installs; the merged-def-read step becomes a plan-time `InjectionMetadata` artifact attached to each definition at freeze (computed once, no per-bean runtime MergedBDPP loop). Ordering is resolved at plan time by topological sort over declared before/after + priority (the cross-crate ordering doc's 'do all ordering at runtime via topo-sort over descriptor data' applied at the plan/freeze boundary). The dogfooding intent is preserved but INVERTED: the framework still expresses its features 'as processors', but they are first-class typed stages the build/freeze pass knows, while the runtime erased SPI is the documented escape hatch — fewer dyn hops on the framework's own hot path.

**Pros:** Best fit for charter §3's metadata/codegen lineage and §2.9 richest-early-diagnostic: definition transforms and ordering run at plan/freeze time, so a bad placeholder, an unsatisfiable ordering cycle, or a missing infrastructure transform fails at a dedicated startup-validation pass BEFORE any instance — the earliest legible failure.; Drastically fewer dyn/boxed-future hops on the framework's OWN features (the common case): injection-metadata is a plan-time artifact, not a per-bean runtime MergedBDPP loop; placeholder/scope rewrites are typed passes — directly serves §2.5 (avoid gratuitous boxing/Arc) and §2.4 (the instance hot path carries only user decorators).; Collapsing before/after into one InitPhase-parameterized decorate() and folding merged-def-read into a freeze-time artifact is genuine unification (§2.11) — fewer trait identities than R1, one ordered chain instead of partitioned bands.; Topo-sort ordering over declared before/after at the freeze boundary is strictly more expressive than Spring's three coarse bands AND matches the cross-crate doc's explicit guidance (encode ordering as descriptor data, resolve by topo-sort), with cycle detection giving a rich diagnostic Spring's band model cannot.; The escape-hatch-only runtime SPI keeps the dynamic surface small and AOT/native-analyzable (the BeanRegistrar lineage the Spring doc flags as the modern, AOT-friendly replacement for hand-rolled BDRPPs), aligning leaf with where Spring 7 itself is heading.; Plan-time framework transforms are trivially introspectable/testable as ordinary code (thin-macro invariant §2.10): the assembly plan is data a test can assert over, and 'what the framework did to my definitions' is inspectable, not hidden in dyn beans.

**Cons:** Diverges most from Spring's mental model: 'add a BeanFactoryPostProcessor bean and the context auto-detects it' becomes 'register a transform / it's a compiled stage' — the dogfooding-via-the-SAME-public-hook symmetry is weakened (framework features use a privileged typed path, users use the escape hatch), which is exactly the asymmetry Spring's design explicitly rejected.; Splitting placeholder resolution (and any env-dependent definition edit) into plan-time-structure + startup-time-values is a real complication and may not cleanly handle a definition edit that genuinely needs a fully-built collaborator — a case Spring forbids anyway, but the boundary is subtle and easy to get wrong.; Pushing framework processors to compile/plan time pre-judges the metadata/codegen boundary and the compile-vs-runtime split — both explicitly Phase-2 keystones (§3); presenting this as a realization risks biasing that signal, so it must be held as a CANDIDATE shape only.; A runtime-discovered user processor that needs to run at PRIORITY ahead of a framework transform has no clean slot if framework transforms are privileged compile-time stages — the 'a user can replace a framework feature via the same mechanism' property degrades.; The dynamic add-definitions path via a BeanRegistrar-style contract is less general than a free-form BDRPP (single concrete registration per call, the very constraint Spring's BeanRegistrar imposes) — some legitimate looping/conditional bulk-registration becomes more ceremonious.; Two authoring models (typed plan transforms for the framework, erased runtime SPI for users) is more total concepts to learn than one uniform tier pair, cutting against DX (#1) for the power user who wants Spring-identical extensibility.

**Mechanisms considered:** build/freeze-time AssemblyPlan + ordered typed DefinitionTransform passes (framework features as compiled stages, not erased dyn beans); plan-time topological sort over declared before/after + priority for ordering (cross-crate doc's 'ordering as data' applied at freeze) with cycle-detection diagnostics; merged-def-read folded into a freeze-time InjectionMetadata artifact per definition (no per-bean runtime MergedBDPP loop); single InitPhase-parameterized InstanceDecorator chain (before/after collapsed) returning ErasedBean as the identity-swap seam; thin runtime escape-hatch SPI (register_transform + BeanRegistrar-style add-defs) for genuinely-dynamic user processors; env-dependent definition edits split into plan-time structure + startup-time value resolution

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Strongest fit for the build-time-codegen substrate (cross-crate Architecture C, the sqlx model): framework definition-transforms are emitted as explicit typed passes by leaf-codegen walking the resolved graph, sidestepping the silent-DCE layers entirely for the framework's OWN features (no link-time slice to drop). User/integration-crate processors STILL need cross-crate collection (the escape-hatch runtime SPI), so they ride whatever catalog substrate Phase 2 picks and inherit the DCE hazard — meaning the self-check still applies, but only to the smaller dynamic-processor set. leaf-core defines DefinitionTransform, InstanceDecorator, InitPhase, Order, and the AssemblyPlan/registry-edit handle traits; leaf-codegen owns plan-time transform emission; the umbrella/app-root force-links integration crates contributing runtime decorators. The plan/freeze boundary is the natural place to run the anti-DCE self-check AND the ordering cycle-detection: assert expected framework transforms are present and topo-sort succeeds before emitting/finalizing the plan, turning a dropped contribution or an ordering cycle into a loud freeze-time error with per-source provenance. Generics still can't register a runtime decorator (no symbol until instantiated) → escape-hatch macro hard-errors with a concrete-instantiation shim. The two-source duality is cleaner here for the framework half (compile-time codegen = the static 'classpath') but the dynamic WASM-module source can ONLY contribute through the runtime escape-hatch SPI (it has no build-time presence) — flagged as a tension with the freeze-early shape, a Phase-2 question, not resolved.

**T2 — execution context:** Plan/freeze stage (definition transforms + topo-sort + self-check) is SYNCHRONOUS cold-path at startup-validation time, single-threaded, around main — framework transforms are pure-ish passes over the AssemblyPlan; the only async is the startup-time VALUE resolution of placeholders (an env/remote read), executed after structural plan freeze and before instance build. Because framework definition edits are typed passes, there is no &mut-across-.await-over-shared-registry hazard for them (they own the plan single-threaded); the runtime escape-hatch add-defs path runs before freeze on the same cold single-threaded path. Instance stage: the DecorationChain runs during bean-instantiation per bean; decorate() is async at the dyn boundary (boxed future — AFIT not dyn-safe) and may .await but MUST NOT block (spawn_blocking otherwise), executing inside bean-registry's per-bean creation guard, never a global lock. Crucially the framework's own per-bean work (injection-metadata) is NOT in this async chain — it's a freeze-time artifact read synchronously during construction — so the async/boxed cost on the instance hot path is borne only by genuinely-dynamic user decorators (§2.4/§2.5 win). Ordering is fixed at freeze (topo-sorted), so the instance phase does no per-bean sorting. Errors: a plan-stage transform or ordering-cycle failure aborts at the startup-validation pass with the richest context (earliest); a decorate() failure aborts that bean's eager build all-or-nothing with a PpError naming the decorator+phase+bean. No async Drop → decorators with teardown participate in explicit shutdown().await (context-close); cancellation of an in-flight decorate() unwinds the partially-decorated bean.

#### R3 — Unified ordered Contributor pipeline over a single Stage enum: one extension primitive, tiers are stage tags
Push unification (§2.11) hardest: collapse BOTH tiers into ONE extension primitive — a `Contributor` — that participates at one or more typed STAGES of the container lifecycle, where 'BFPP', 'BDRPP', 'BPP', 'MergedBDPP', 'SmartInstantiationAware' become STAGE TAGS rather than separate traits. `trait Contributor { fn id(&self) -> ContributorId; fn stages(&self) -> StageSet; fn order(&self) -> Order; async fn run(&self, stage: Stage, cx: &mut StageCtx<'_>) -> Result<(), PpError>; }` where `enum Stage { RegistryEdit, DefinitionEdit, BeforeInit, AfterInit }` and two SYNC fast-path hooks for the per-bean hot stages stay separate by capability (`fn merged_def(&self, _:&MergedBeanDefinition,_:TypeId,_:&BeanName){}` default-noop; `fn early_ref(&self, _:&ErasedBean,_:&BeanCtx)->Option<ErasedBean>{None}` default-none). `StageCtx` is a stage-typed capability object: at RegistryEdit it exposes add/remove+edit of defs; at DefinitionEdit only edit; at Before/AfterInit it carries the ErasedBean (in via field, out via a set_bean/replace returning the possibly-different handle — the origin-agnostic identity-swap seam) plus &BeanCtx. The engine holds ONE ordered Vec<Arc<dyn Contributor>> and, at each lifecycle point, runs the subset whose StageSet contains the current Stage, in the unified global order — with the two fixed cross-stage rules preserved as engine policy: ALL RegistryEdit runs (re-scanned for newly-added contributors) before any DefinitionEdit, and contributors tagged with the merged_def capability are ordered LAST within the instance-registration set. The dogfooding parity is exact and SYMMETRIC: leaf's own placeholder-resolver, injection-metadata, and proxy-weaver are each ONE Contributor declaring the stages it participates in (placeholder→DefinitionEdit; injection-metadata→merged_def+BeforeInit; proxy-weaver→AfterInit+early_ref) — and a user replaces or augments any of them by contributing another Contributor at the same stage with a chosen Order, through the IDENTICAL mechanism. This is the maximal-unification bet: one trait, one ordered list, one discovery path, tiers as data.

**Pros:** Maximal unification (§2.11): ONE trait, ONE ordered contributor list, ONE discovery/catalog path, ONE ordering model — the fewest concepts to learn (DX #1) and the smallest stable kernel ABI (§6) the macro targets; tiers become a StageSet flag, not a trait taxonomy.; Perfectly symmetric dogfooding: leaf's own features and user extensions are the SAME Contributor type contributing at the SAME stages with the SAME ordering — the exact 'extend the framework using the mechanism the framework uses on itself' property Spring prizes, with no privileged path (the asymmetry R2 introduces is gone).; A single global Order over all contributors across all stages makes cross-stage precedence (a Priority RegistryEdit contributor vs a Priority BeforeInit contributor) reason about one number line — and the stage-gating means each contributor only runs where it opted in, so one list cleanly serves all lifecycle points.; Stage-typed StageCtx capability object enforces 'what you may do here' by the stage value, not by which trait you implemented — RegistryEdit-only-can-add-defs becomes a property of the ctx handed in, eliminating R1's two-registry-handle-types boilerplate while keeping the 'must not instantiate during definition stages' guarantee (no get() on those ctx variants).; Easiest to evolve under Phase 2: if a new lifecycle extension point emerges (e.g. a post-merge stage, or a scope-entry hook), it's a new Stage variant + StageCtx case, not a new trait and a new discovery path — the SPI is open to new stages without churning the contributor surface.; Keeps the genuinely-hot per-bean hooks (merged_def, early_ref) SYNC and capability-default-noop, so the boxed-future cost lands only on the async run() stages a contributor actually opts into — a heterogeneous chain pays the dyn tax per participating stage, not per trait method.

**Cons:** A single `run(stage, cx)` with a match on Stage is LESS type-safe than R1's distinct method signatures: a contributor that mishandles a stage it tagged (or reads the wrong StageCtx capability) fails at runtime, not compile time — trading R1's API-shape guarantees for uniformity, a real §2.9 cost (later, less-legible failure).; StageCtx as a stage-tagged capability enum means every run() impl pattern-matches the ctx and handles 'wrong stage' arms — ergonomics friction and a class of 'unreachable but must-handle' arms that a typed-method design avoids; cuts against DX for the simple single-stage contributor.; The unification risks PRE-DECIDING that the two tiers SHOULD merge — which is itself a meta-architecture stance (does leaf keep Spring's deliberate blueprint/product separation as two surfaces, or fuse them?) that Phase 2 owns; presenting one fused primitive as THE shape biases that call, so it must stay a CANDIDATE.; One ordered Vec<Arc<dyn Contributor>> with a global Order can make the blueprint-vs-product PHASE separation (the load-bearing intent: definitions fully done before any instance) less visually obvious in the API — the engine still enforces it by stage-gating, but a reader sees one list, not two phases, weakening the teachability Spring's split provides.; Arc<dyn Contributor> per contributor + boxed-future run() is dyn-dispatch + alloc on every participating stage; while the sync fast-paths help, the async stages still box, and a single trait object held across all stages may force wider Send/Sync bounds than a per-tier design would.; Mapping the two fixed cross-stage rules (RegistryEdit-before-DefinitionEdit with re-scan; merged_def-capability-last) onto a single ordered list requires engine-side stage-phase sequencing that partly re-introduces the partitioning R1 makes explicit — the unification is partly notional at the engine layer.

**Mechanisms considered:** one Contributor trait with a StageSet + a single async run(stage, &mut StageCtx) dispatched per lifecycle point; Stage enum (RegistryEdit/DefinitionEdit/BeforeInit/AfterInit) as tier tags; stage-typed StageCtx capability object gating what each stage may do; two sync capability hooks (merged_def, early_ref) kept off the async run() path to dodge the boxed-future tax on per-bean hot stages; single global Order over all contributors across stages; engine enforces RegistryEdit-before-DefinitionEdit re-scan + merged_def-capability-last as policy; one ordered Vec<Arc<dyn Contributor>> + stage-gating, replacing partitioned per-tier lists; tiers become data not types; origin-agnostic ErasedBean carried in/out via StageCtx at the init stages as the identity-swap seam

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for a small, ultra-stable kernel ABI (§6): leaf-core exports ONE Contributor trait + the Stage/StageCtx/Order/ContributorId types + the ErasedBean handle — the smallest surface the thin macro must target (it emits one descriptor with a StageSet and an Order). Contributing crates depend only on leaf-core and contribute Contributor descriptors via the Phase-2 catalog substrate (linkme/codegen/explicit) — one discovery path for ALL extension, framework and user alike, so there is a single self-check surface (expected-vs-found contributors, by stage, with per-source provenance) rather than per-tier checks. Inherits the silent-DCE hazard identically (an unreferenced contributing crate yields an empty stage subset → a feature silently does nothing exactly as Spring's plain BeanFactory) — the façade self-check, mandated by the cross-crate doc, must assert the expected framework contributors are present per stage and fail loud; the engine exposes a contributors-by-stage introspection surface so the check is writable. Generic contributors can't register (no 'static symbol until instantiated) → macro hard-errors with a register-concrete shim. The two-source duality is cleanest of the three here: the static link-time registry and an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source are simply two producers of the ONE Contributor descriptor stream — a WASM-module contributor's run() bridges into the module, and the kernel cannot tell it from a native one (origin-agnostic), so the dynamic 'classpath' slots in with no new path (CANDIDATES, not a decision). The single-trait kernel is also the easiest to keep semver-stable as features grow (new Stage variants extend the enum without changing the trait), at the cost of the enum being a wider stable commitment.

**T2 — execution context:** The engine drives stages at refresh-lifecycle's fixed points, all under its startup discipline. Definition stages (RegistryEdit then DefinitionEdit) run COLD-PATH single-threaded around main: run() may .await but the StageCtx capability for these stages exposes no get(), enforcing 'must not instantiate beans' by API; the &mut def-store access lives inside StageCtx held only on this single-threaded cold path, sidestepping aliasing-across-await by execution context, not a global lock. The RegistryEdit-before-DefinitionEdit re-scan loop runs to fixpoint on the cold path before freeze. Instance stages (BeforeInit/AfterInit) run during bean-instantiation per bean inside bean-registry's per-bean creation guard — never a global lock; run() at these stages is async/boxed and may .await but MUST NOT block (spawn_blocking otherwise; Phase 2 owns context propagation). The sync capability hooks merged_def and early_ref run on the construction path with NO .await — early_ref inside cycle resolution where awaiting risks re-entrancy, merged_def to cache injection metadata cheaply per bean. Contributors are eager-instantiated front (a contributor can't run on a bean built before it exists — Spring's documented constraint), with the accepted documented cost that contributors and their direct deps are not fully decorated. Ordering is resolved once (global Order, plus engine stage-phase sequencing) — no per-bean re-sort on the hot path. Errors are rich PpError naming contributor id + stage + target (def or bean), folded into the assembly chain (§2.9); a definition-stage failure aborts before any instance (earliest), an instance-stage failure aborts that bean all-or-nothing. No async Drop → contributors needing teardown participate in explicit shutdown().await (context-close); cancellation of an in-flight run() at an init stage unwinds the partially-decorated bean out of the creation set.

### Rust clashes
- No runtime instanceof / type-walk for role auto-detection — Spring auto-detects processors by checking each registered bean against `instanceof BeanFactoryPostProcessor`/`BeanPostProcessor` (and the sub-interfaces), iterating the live bean population. Rust has no runtime walk over registered beans and no isinstance over arbitrary types. So 'which tier is this' must be DECLARED as data on the descriptor (a role/StageSet flag the macro emits from the attribute or from which trait is impl'd) and partitioned by the engine reading that data — never discovered by inspecting instances. This is why all three realizations make role/stage a descriptor field, not a runtime type test.
- The 'returns a different object' identity swap has no CGLIB and an unsettled proxy story — Spring's BPP.postProcessAfterInitialization returning a proxy in place of the target is THE mechanism for AOP, and it relies on the JVM letting a subclass/interface-proxy stand in for the original reference freely (GC'd, aliased). In Rust there is no CGLIB, the bean is an origin-agnostic erased handle, and the auto-proxy-creator/aop-proxying realization doesn't exist yet. So the instance-decoration hook must return an ErasedBean (handle in, possibly-different handle out) and stay PROXY-MECHANISM-NEUTRAL — the SPI defines the seam (a processor may replace the handle) without prejudging how a replacement is built; this also keeps it origin-agnostic so a WASM-module/test-double decoration swaps the handle identically.
- Async fn in traits is not dyn-compatible, but the processor SPI MUST be dyn (a heterogeneous, cross-crate-contributed, origin-agnostic chain) — RPITIT/AFIT desugar to opaque types that aren't object-safe, so the async before_init/after_init/run() hooks at the dyn boundary must return boxed futures (Pin<Box<dyn Future + Send>>), an alloc per processor per bean on the instance path. This is acceptable for cold definition-phase processors but a real cost on the per-bean instance hot path — mitigated in all three by keeping the genuinely-hot per-bean sub-hooks (merged-def-read, early-reference) SYNC and reserving async for the IO-bearing init hooks; the zero-cost monomorphized path can't coexist with the dyn heterogeneous chain on the same surface on stable (Phase-2 async-execution-model call).
- `&mut` registry access across `.await` over a shared definition store — Spring's BFPP freely mutates the definition map; an async leaf DefinitionPostProcessor holding `&mut dyn DefinitionRegistry` across an .await collides with Rust's aliasing rules and Send-across-threads. The realizations resolve this by EXECUTION CONTEXT, not a lock: definition-phase processors run single-threaded on the cold path (around main, before any instance), so the &mut handle is never aliased concurrently — but this constrains how parallel the definition phase can be, and any genuinely-concurrent definition editing would need a command/message API instead of a shared &mut handle (a divergence from Spring's direct-mutation model).
- No async Drop on stable for processor teardown — a processor that holds resources (a remote-config client a BFPP used, a connection a decorator opened) cannot clean up via async Drop. Mirroring the bean teardown story, processors needing async cleanup must participate in the container's explicit shutdown().await (context-close) in reverse order rather than riding Drop — so 'the container owns processor lifecycle' is a non-syntactic discipline, and cancellation of an in-flight async hook must unwind partially-applied work explicitly.
- Eager processor instantiation collides with async-first construction — Spring instantiates all processors synchronously and early (a processor can't process a bean built before it exists). In leaf, processor construction is itself async (a processor may be built from an async factory like any bean), so the 'front-load all processors before any application bean' rule becomes 'await the eager construction of the entire processor population first', a fixed async cold-path step in refresh — and the documented Spring cost (processors and their direct deps aren't eligible to be processed by all processors) carries over and must be surfaced with a rich diagnostic rather than Spring's log line.
- Three-band ordering (PriorityOrdered>Ordered>unordered) vs link-time unspecified order — Spring sorts processors into three bands at runtime; the cross-crate doc warns that link-time/constructor registration order is UNSPECIFIED (and may be deliberately randomized), so leaf cannot rely on contribution order for any tier. Ordering MUST be encoded as data (an Order/priority field on each descriptor) and resolved at runtime/freeze by an explicit sort (or topo-sort over declared before/after, R2), never inferred from the registry's iteration order — a stricter discipline than Spring needs because Spring's bean-definition order is deterministic where leaf's link order is not.
- The merged-definition-read sub-tier needs the merged def to already exist and be read-only — Spring's MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor reads the recomputed read-only RootBeanDefinition to cache injection metadata, and re-registers LAST. This couples the SPI to bean-registry's merged-def cache + freeze boundary: the hook can only fire after the merged def is frozen, and the 'read-only' guarantee must be a real Rust borrow (&MergedBeanDefinition), not a convention — and whether the early-reference sub-tier (getEarlyBeanReference) is even needed depends on the still-undecided proxy/cycle story, making that sub-hook speculative.

### Interactions
- **refresh-lifecycle** — refresh-lifecycle is THE driver that invokes this SPI at fixed points: it runs all definition-tier processors (registry-edit callbacks before definition-edit callbacks), then freezes definitions, then instantiates+registers all instance-tier processors, then builds application singletons running the instance hooks per bean. This feature defines the processor contracts and ordering; refresh-lifecycle defines WHERE in the fixed-order template they fire (the invokeBeanFactoryPostProcessors → freeze → registerBeanPostProcessors → preInstantiateSingletons sequence) and the all-or-nothing failure semantics.
- **bean-registry** — bean-registry provides the stores this SPI acts on: definition-tier processors edit the name-keyed definition map (BFPP) and add/remove via the registry handle (BDRPP); the merged-def sub-tier reads bean-registry's read-only merged definition; the freeze boundary bean-registry owns IS the 'no more definition edits' line. bean-registry also provides the per-bean creation guard inside which instance-tier hooks run (never a global lock) and the early-reference cache hook the SmartInstantiationAware sub-tier targets.
- **bean-definition** — bean-definition is the editable metamodel definition-tier processors mutate (the blueprint) and the merged/flattened read-only view the merged-def sub-tier reads. This feature's BFPP/BDRPP contract is meaningful only because bean-definition decouples metadata from instances (parse-then-instantiate); the 'edit definitions before any instance' invariant is the two-phase model bean-definition establishes.
- **bean-instantiation** — bean-instantiation drives the instance-tier hooks at the right points in the construct→populate→init sequence: before_init runs before init callbacks, after_init after them, the merged-def hook before population (to cache injection metadata), and early_ref when a cycle demands an early reference. This feature defines the hooks; bean-instantiation calls them and applies the possibly-different returned handle as the bean's published identity.
- **container-core** — container-core's engine-vs-façade split and 'auto-detect post-processors by role' is realized BY this SPI: the bare engine runs zero auto-detected processors (so features silently do nothing), while the context façade installs the standard Priority-band infrastructure processors — the exact 'why @Configuration/@Autowired/AOP need an ApplicationContext' parity. container-core fixes the boundary and the auto-detect convention; this feature provides the role-tagged processor descriptors and the tiers that convention discovers.
- **auto-proxy-creator** — The auto-proxy creator IS an instance-tier processor (an InstancePostProcessor/Contributor at AfterInit) that returns a proxy in place of the target, plus an EarlyRefPostProcessor for early-proxy exposure into cycles.
