This feature provides the identity-swap seam (handle in, possibly-different handle out, origin-agnostic) and the early-reference hook; auto-proxy-creator plugs the actual proxy-weaving into that seam — and the documented 'BPPs and their direct deps are not auto-proxied' constraint is enforced because processors are instantiated before the proxy-creator processor exists.
- **aop-proxying** — aop-proxying is the substrate that turns a bean into a proxy AT the DI seam this SPI exposes — it is the canonical 'a BPP returns a different object' use case. This feature keeps the instance-decoration hook proxy-mechanism-neutral (just 'replace the handle') so aop-proxying's eventual proxy mechanism slots onto it without leaf-core depending on AOP.
- **autowiring-resolution** — @Autowired/@Value injection is implemented AS an instance-tier processor (the AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor analogue) that is also a merged-def processor: it caches injection metadata from the merged def, then populates the bean during the instance phase. This feature provides the merged-def sub-tier and the before-init hook; autowiring-resolution provides the actual field/constructor/setter resolution logic that rides them, and is one of leaf's dogfooded infrastructure processors.
- **value-injection** — ${...} placeholder/value injection is dogfooded across BOTH tiers: a definition-tier processor (PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer analogue) resolves ${...} in definitions, and an instance-tier processor injects @Value at injection points using a ConversionService. This feature provides both tiers; value-injection is a concrete infrastructure processor on them.
- **lifecycle-callbacks** — @PostConstruct/@PreDestroy/init/destroy handling is dogfooded as an instance-tier processor (the CommonAnnotation analogue) — it is a Priority-band processor that wires init/destroy callbacks. The before_init/after_init ordering relative to @PostConstruct (before_init runs BEFORE @PostConstruct, after_init AFTER) is the contract this feature defines and lifecycle-callbacks depends on.
- **aware-callbacks** — Aware injection (bean name, factory, environment, context) is dogfooded as a single instance-tier processor (the ApplicationContextAwareProcessor analogue) firing the Aware group as a unit in before_init, before @PostConstruct — which is why Aware injection only works for container-created beans. This feature provides the before_init hook; aware-callbacks is the concrete processor that uses it as a group.
- **configuration-classes** — @Configuration/@Bean/@Import/@ComponentScan parsing is dogfooded as the highest-Priority definition-registry processor (the ConfigurationClassPostProcessor analogue, a BDRPP) that registers derived definitions BEFORE any other definition-tier processor runs — so e.g. placeholder resolution can act on @Bean-defined definitions. This feature provides the BDRPP tier and the strict 'registry-edit before definition-edit' ordering; configuration-classes is the marquee dogfooded processor on it.
- **programmatic-registration** — The BeanRegistrar functional contract is the AOT-friendly, sanctioned replacement for hand-rolling a BDRPP — it writes definitions imperatively/conditionally into the registry-edit handle this SPI exposes. This feature provides the low-level definition-registry tier (the escape hatch per §2.10); programmatic-registration provides the higher-level functional/looping/conditional registration API layered over it (and in R2 is the primary dynamic-add-defs lane).
- **conditions** — @Conditional gating is evaluated over the descriptors this SPI discovers — a processor's contribution (like a bean's) is filtered by conditions before it joins the ordered chain. The cross-crate doc's guidance applies: link-time collection answers 'what could contribute', runtime conditions answer 'what does'. This feature supplies the role-tagged descriptors; conditions decides which actually wire up.
- **exception-translation** — @Repository exception translation is dogfooded as an instance-tier processor (the PersistenceExceptionTranslationPostProcessor analogue) that weaves a translating advisor onto matching beans — a concrete BPP that returns a possibly-different (proxied) handle. This feature provides the after_init identity-swap seam; exception-translation is one of the dogfooded processors riding it.
- **circular-references** — The early-reference sub-tier (getEarlyBeanReference analogue) lets a processor publish an early/proxy reference into a cycle; circular-references defines tolerate-setter/field-cycles-via-early-ref and the raw-vs-wrapped consistency check that this hook participates in. Whether leaf needs this sub-tier at all depends on the (undecided) proxy/cycle story — making it the most speculative part of this SPI.
- **context-close** — Because there is no async Drop, processors needing teardown participate in context-close's explicit shutdown().await in reverse order rather than Drop. This feature's processors that hold resources register into the same disposal path the container drives; context-close defines the stop-then-destroy-in-reverse sequence that tears down processors alongside beans.
- **smart-initializing** — smart-initializing's single lock-free 'all eager singletons exist' callback fires AFTER the instance-tier processors have run on every eager bean (at the end of preInstantiateSingletons), explicitly outside any creation guard — the safe place for cross-bean work that would deadlock if done in a per-bean instance hook. This feature's instance phase establishes the 'all beans built and decorated' boundary smart-initializing's callback follows.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Do the two tiers stay TWO distinct trait surfaces (R1's blueprint-vs-product separation, faithful to Spring's deliberate split) or UNIFY into one Contributor primitive with stage tags (R3), or does the framework's own features take a privileged compile-time path while users get a thin runtime escape hatch (R2)? This is a meta-architecture stance on whether Spring's edit-definitions/decorate-instances separation is a load-bearing user-facing boundary or an implementation detail to fuse — and it cross-cuts the metadata/codegen boundary and the unification goal (§2.11).
- How much of the post-processor pipeline runs at BUILD/freeze time vs runtime (§3 compile-vs-runtime split)? R2 compiles the framework's own definition transforms and folds injection-metadata into a freeze-time artifact (earliest diagnostic, fewest dyn hops); R1/R3 run everything at runtime as dyn processors. The richest-early-diagnostic timing (§2.9), the dyn/boxing cost on the instance hot path (§2.5), and whether placeholder/env-dependent edits can be split structure-vs-value all hinge on this — and it directly pre-judges the metadata/codegen boundary if decided here.
- Is the instance-decoration identity-swap (handle in, possibly-different handle out) the right seam, and what is the proxy/early-reference mechanism it feeds? leaf has no CGLIB and the auto-proxy-creator/aop-proxying realization doesn't exist yet, so whether the SmartInstantiationAware/early_ref sub-tier earns its keep (and whether the bean-registry singleton cache even has a three-level early-proxy slot) is undecidable until the proxy strategy is chosen. Cross-cuts circular-references, auto-proxy-creator, aop-proxying, bean-registry.
- The async-across-dyn boxing standard for the processor SPI: the chain MUST be dyn (heterogeneous, cross-crate, origin-agnostic incl. WASM-module sources) and async-fn-in-trait isn't dyn-safe → boxed futures (alloc per processor per bean). Is the standard boxed-future-on-the-dyn-path with sync fast-paths for the per-bean hot sub-hooks (merged-def, early-ref) as all three propose, and is that enough, or does the instance hot path need a monomorphized zero-cost escape? The async-execution-model decision (§3) viewed through the processor pipeline.
- How is processor/contributor ORDERING expressed and resolved — Spring's three coarse bands (Priority>Ordered>unordered) as R1/R3 keep, or a topological sort over declared before/after + priority as R2 proposes (more expressive, cycle-detectable, matches the cross-crate doc's 'ordering as data' guidance)? Link/constructor order is unspecified, so ordering must be data resolved by an explicit sort — but the band-vs-topo-sort choice interacts with auto-config-ordering and the conditional strategy, and whether ordering resolves at freeze or runtime.
- How is the &mut definition-store access reconciled with async-first definition-phase processors (the &mut-across-.await aliasing clash)? All three resolve it by running the definition phase single-threaded on the cold path (sidestepping aliasing by execution context, not a lock) — but does that adequately constrain concurrency, or does leaf need a command/message-based definition-edit API instead of Spring's direct shared-&mut mutation, and does that diverge from Spring's mid-bootstrap dynamism? Cross-cuts bean-registry (mutable-concurrent vs immutable-snapshot registry) and programmatic-registration.
- The two-source duality for processor discovery: the static link-time registry (default 'classpath') AND an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source both feed ONE role/stage-tagged descriptor stream — but a freeze-early/build-time-codegen shape (R2) can only accommodate the dynamic WASM source through the runtime escape hatch (it has no build-time presence), while runtime-dyn shapes (R1/R3) accommodate it naturally. How the dynamic source's timing reconciles with the definition-freeze boundary is unresolved (a candidate, not a decision).
- What is the anti-DCE self-check contract for processors specifically? A dropped contributing crate yields zero processors of a tier → a framework feature silently does nothing, exactly the worst-case Spring 'plain BeanFactory' symptom but for an unintended reason. The cross-crate doc mandates an expected-vs-found self-check; what is the canonical 'expected infrastructure processors by role/stage' manifest, where does it live (façade? generated app-root?), and how does it produce a rich diagnostic naming the missing source — and does it differ across the catalog substrates (linkme vs codegen vs explicit)?

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the dedicated post-processor section twice (lines 190-198 the two-tier framing; 912-956 the dogfooding philosophy with all the hidden concepts: BDRPP-registry-before-factory 917/940, the three-phase Priority>Ordered>unordered ordering 938-942, MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor-re-registers-last 944-946, BPPs-not-auto-proxied 948-950, getEarlyBeanReference 952-954, eager-processor-instantiation cost 956), plus the refresh-order template that DRIVES this SPI (331-363, esp. invokeBeanFactoryPostProcessors/registerBeanPostProcessors/freeze/preInstantiateSingletons 357-362), the static-@Bean-methods-for-processors hidden rule (1017, 950), the registrars-cannot-register-a-BDRPP lifecycle constraint (1040), and the dogfooding table mapping each Spring feature to its tier (922-928). I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc's Layer-0/A/B silent-DCE (135-152, 341-362), the self-check mandate (152, 269, 345), the linkme-default + thin-const-descriptor + ordering-as-data guidance (165-171), the four architectures A/B/C/D (277-337), and the no-instanceof reality; T2 against the charter invariants (§2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimal-Arc/boxing, §2.9 rich-early diagnostics, §2.10 escape-hatch, §2.11 unification) and the stable envelope (RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe 162-182, trait-upcasting 1.86 118-126, no async Drop 273-281, dyn+Send needs trait_variant/boxing 260).

The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters for THIS feature — the SHAPE of the extension surface and WHEN/HOW it runs: R1 = two literal ordered trait tiers (five traits) role-detected from descriptors, highest Spring fidelity, keeps the blueprint/product split as two surfaces; R2 = a build-time pipeline where the framework's OWN features are typed compile-time transforms over the assembly plan (fewest dyn hops, earliest diagnostics, AOT/BeanRegistrar lineage) with a thin runtime escape-hatch SPI for users — but introduces a privileged-path asymmetry Spring deliberately rejected; R3 = maximal unification into ONE Contributor primitive where tiers are Stage tags on a single ordered list (smallest kernel ABI, perfectly symmetric dogfooding) — but trades compile-time type-safety for a runtime stage match and risks pre-deciding that the two tiers should merge. R1 vs R3 differ on two-traits-vs-one-fused-primitive; R2 differs on compile-time-typed-vs-runtime-dyn for the framework's own features.

Things I deliberately did NOT decide (held as Phase-2 questions): whether the two tiers unify; how much moves to build/freeze time; the proxy/early-reference mechanism the instance-swap seam feeds (and whether the SmartInstantiationAware sub-tier is even needed — flagged as the most speculative part, coupled to the undecided proxy + three-level-cache story); the async-across-dyn boxing standard; band-vs-topo-sort ordering; how &mut definition access reconciles with async; and the two-source (link-time + WASM-module) discovery duality vs a freeze-early shape. Every realization keeps the instance-decoration seam origin-agnostic (ErasedBean in/out — a WASM-module/test-double/native-proxy swap identically) and keeps role/stage as DATA on the descriptor (the rust-native replacement for Spring's instanceof auto-detect, since Rust has no runtime type-walk), and all three are compatible with cross-crate Architectures A/B/C/D and inherit the silent-DCE self-check mandate. Naming ('DefinitionPostProcessor', 'InstancePostProcessor', 'Contributor', 'Stage', 'StageCtx', 'ErasedBean', 'Order', 'AssemblyPlan', 'DefinitionTransform') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Relevant grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (190-198 two-tier framing; 331-363 refresh-order driver; 912-956 dogfooding + hidden concepts; 1017 & 1040 static-@Bean & registrar-cannot-register-BDRPP rules; 922-928 dogfood table; 1693-1768 proxy-as-BPP + getEarlyBeanReference; 2438-2443 bare-engine-is-dumb + BFPP/MergedBDPP-see-what); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3, topology §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (135-171 DCE layers + linkme-default + thin-descriptor + ordering-as-data; 277-362 architectures A/B/C/D + self-check + hard constraints); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (118-126 trait-upcasting 1.86; 160-182 RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe; 260-281 dyn+Send boxing + no async Drop).

## Bean Instantiation & Singleton Caching  `[bean-instantiation]`

Spring's `preInstantiateSingletons` refresh loop + `doCreateBean` together are the engine's CREATION DRIVER: the algorithm that turns a merged definition into a live, published instance. THIS feature is that driver and its publication discipline — distinct from bean-registry (which fixed the STORES: the three-level singleton cache shape, the merged-def cache, candidate-set production) and from scopes (which fixed per-scope multiplicity/teardown POLICY). bean-instantiation is the SEQUENCE and the CONCURRENCY around it. Precisely, the intent to replicate: (1) The EAGER pre-instantiation pass — two passes over a defensive copy of the definition names: pass 1 resolves each merged def and, if `!abstract && singleton && !lazy`, drives creation (the lazy flag gates eager build; FactoryBeans create the factory always but the product only if SmartFactoryBean.isEagerInit); pass 2 fires the single SmartInitializingSingleton callback AFTER every eager singleton exists. The design intent is FAIL-FAST AT BOOT: front-load wiring/validation/proxy so a misconfigured app refuses to start rather than failing on first request ("a context that started is a context that works"). (2) The per-bean CREATION SEQUENCE inside doCreateBean: instantiate → (if earlySingletonExposure: singleton && allowCircular && currentlyInCreation, register an ObjectFactory — NOT the raw instance — into the middle cache level so an AOP early-proxy can be computed lazily-and-once and recorded to avoid double-wrap) → populate → initialize (Aware → before-init BPP → init methods/@PostConstruct → after-init BPP where proxies are created) → publish into the full cache. (3) The SINGLETON CREATION LOCK discipline — Spring's v6.2/7.0 move OFF a global mutex to a per-registry ReentrantLock + lenientCreationLock with a per-thread strict-vs-lenient policy (isCurrentThreadAllowedToHoldSingletonLock): the main bootstrap thread is strict, background-init threads lenient (build outside the lock and reconcile a TOLERATED, now-benign BeanCurrentlyInCreationException), so independent beans build concurrently and the lock never deadlocks on user init code. Init methods run UNDER the lock; a bean is published only after init returns (safe publication of fully-built fields); heavy cross-bean work belongs in the post-all-singletons lock-free SmartInitializing callback. (4) PROTOTYPE: build-init fully then HAND OFF AND FORGET — init runs, destruction NEVER does, nothing cached, the container tracks nothing (tracking would be the leak the hand-off avoids). (5) Two distinct BeanCurrentlyInCreation origins: genuinely-unresolvable constructor cycle (fatal) vs resolved-but-wrapped-inconsistency vs benign lenient-locking skip. leaf must replicate this INTENT — an all-or-nothing eager pass that fail-fast validates the graph at boot, a per-bean construct→expose-early→populate→init→publish sequence with safe publication of the finished instance, a NO-GLOBAL-LOCK creation guard that serializes only same-bean first-creation while letting distinct/background beans build concurrently, prototype hand-off-and-forget, and lazy/FactoryBean eager-gating — WITHOUT Spring's global-mutex lineage, its under-lock @PostConstruct (which in async Rust means blocking the executor), its GC-published aliasing, or its assumption of native-only construction (the construction recipe and the published handle must be origin-agnostic so a WASM-module/test-double provider is created and published identically). It must NOT pick the singleton-handle type, the creation-guard substrate, the async-across-dyn boxing standard, whether two or three cache levels are needed, or how much validation moves to build-time — those are Phase 2.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Literal doCreateBean driver: a per-bean async creation pipeline + a two-pass eager loop, guarded by a per-bean creation lock with strict/lenient policy (Spring-faithful, rust-retyped)
Mirror Spring's creation driver directly as an async pipeline retyped to eliminate the global lock and the under-lock blocking. The driver is a free function over the registry's stores (bean-registry owns the three-level cache + in_creation set; this feature DRIVES them): `async fn create_singleton(engine, name, merged) -> Result<ErasedBean, ResolveError>`. The per-bean sequence is the literal doCreateBean steps as `.await` points: (a) mark in_creation (the cycle sentinel); (b) `instantiate` — call the definition's ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC erased factory (a boxed-future constructor reused from bean-definition, so native/WASM/test-double construct identically), awaiting it; (c) if `early_singleton_exposure = singleton && allow_circular && in_creation(name)`, register an `EarlyRefFactory` (a closure, NOT the raw instance) into the middle cache level so a future early-proxy is computed lazily-and-once and recorded to avoid double-wrap (circular-references owns the proxy hook; this feature only places the factory at the right moment); (d) `populate` — resolve and inject collaborators (delegates to autowiring-resolution, which may recursively re-enter `get` for dependencies); (e) `initialize` — run the aware/before-init/init/after-init chain (lifecycle-callbacks owns the ordering; this feature invokes it as the init phase) where the after-init step is where proxies attach; (f) consistency check (raw-vs-wrapped early-ref mismatch → rich error); (g) PUBLISH — move the finished handle into the full `ready` cache, clear in_creation. The CREATION GUARD is per-bean (NOT global, §2.5): a keyed async primitive (sharded `Mutex` map or a keyed lock — substrate is Phase 2) serializes only concurrent FIRST-creation of the SAME name; distinct names run concurrently. The strict-vs-lenient policy is a per-creation flag carried in the call context: a strict (main) builder awaits the guard; a lenient (background) builder `try_acquire`s and, on contention, builds OUTSIDE the guard and reconciles — a losing build's value is dropped when the winner's published value is found in `ready` (Spring's benign BeanCurrentlyInCreation skip). The EAGER PASS is the two-loop preInstantiateSingletons: pass 1 iterates a SNAPSHOT of definition names (defensive copy, so a creation that registers a new def doesn't invalidate the iteration), drives `create_singleton` for each `!abstract && singleton && !lazy` (FactoryBean: create factory always, product only if eager-init), all-or-nothing fail-fast; pass 2 fires the single SmartInitializing callback OUTSIDE any guard after all exist. Prototype path is a SEPARATE entry (`create_prototype`): same construct→populate→init sequence but NO in_creation-for-publish, NO cache write, NO destruction registration — hand-off-and-forget by structural absence.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: the doCreateBean step order, the register-a-factory-not-the-instance early-exposure timing, the two-pass eager loop with the trailing SmartInitializing callback, and the strict/lenient creation policy all map 1:1 to Spring — maximally teachable and traceable, and it dovetails exactly with the cache shape bean-registry already fixed.; The per-bean creation guard with a strict/lenient per-creation flag is the direct, honest realization of charter §2.5 (no global lock) AND the literal enabler of background-bootstrap: distinct beans build concurrently, background threads build leniently outside the guard, only same-bean first-creation serializes.; Async-first by construction (§2.3): the construct/populate/init steps are explicit `.await` points, so a factory that hits a DB pool awaits instead of Spring's under-lock blocking — the @PostConstruct-deadlock-trap is dissolved rather than inherited, because nothing blocks the executor under a held guard.; Origin-agnostic at the only seam that matters: step (b) calls an erased boxed-future factory, so the driver cannot tell native from WASM-module from test-double — the future dynamic 'classpath' publishes through the identical sequence into the identical `ready` cache.; All-or-nothing eager pass gives the fail-fast-at-boot diagnostic (§2.9): wiring/ambiguity/cycle errors surface during pass 1 with the full assembly chain, before any request — preserving 'a context that started is a context that works'.; Prototype hand-off-and-forget falls out as ABSENCE (no cache write, no destruction registration, no in_creation-for-publish), so the never-destroyed contract can't be accidentally violated.

**Cons:** The strict-vs-lenient 'build outside the guard and reconcile a tolerated in-creation race' is genuinely the hardest-won concurrency code in Spring (took multiple point releases, issues #34349/#30887); re-deriving it correctly in async Rust — where a losing build's partially-constructed value plus its in-flight dependency awaits must unwind cleanly — is high-risk and the single subtlest part of this realization.; Recursive `populate` re-entering `get` for dependencies means the creation driver is mutually recursive with autowiring-resolution across `.await` points; an async recursive call graph holding a per-bean guard at each frame is exactly where a naive lock-ordering deadlock could reappear if the guard granularity or the lenient fallback is wrong.; The middle cache level (EarlyRefFactory) only earns its keep for circular-references + early-proxy; for the common acyclic singleton it is pure overhead, and since leaf's proxy mechanism (auto-proxy-creator) doesn't exist yet, placing the early-ref factory at step (c) is partly speculative scaffolding.; Two near-identical sequences (singleton vs prototype) that must stay semantically aligned (same construct/populate/init, divergent publish/teardown) is duplicated logic that can drift — a coherence burden the driver must police.; Carrying the strict/lenient flag through the whole recursive creation context (so a background build stays lenient across its transitive dependency builds) threads execution-context state through every frame — easy to lose across a spawn or an executor hop.

**Mechanisms considered:** async creation pipeline as a free function over bean-registry's stores: instantiate → early-expose → populate → initialize → publish, each an `.await` point; per-bean keyed creation guard (sharded Mutex map or async keyed lock) + per-creation strict/lenient flag carried in the call context; lenient builds outside the guard and reconciles via the `ready` cache; two-pass eager loop over a defensive snapshot of definition names; pass 1 fail-fast all-or-nothing creation, pass 2 trailing SmartInitializing callback outside any guard; origin-agnostic erased boxed-future factory (reused from bean-definition) as the sole construction seam — native/WASM/test-double identical; EarlyRefFactory (a closure, not the raw instance) placed into the middle cache level only when early_singleton_exposure holds; early-proxy computed lazily-and-once; separate prototype entry: same sequence, no cache write / no in_creation-publish / no destruction registration (hand-off-and-forget by absence)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Substrate-neutral on collection — the driver consumes whatever merged defs the registry holds, regardless of how descriptors were collected (linkme distributed-slice / build.rs codegen / explicit). What this feature FIXES cross-crate: the creation driver, the EAGER-pass orchestration, the creation guard, and the publish step all live in leaf-core alongside the registry; contributing crates supply only descriptors + the origin-agnostic factory (a thin const the macro emits via absolute paths). The eager pass is the NATURAL HOME for the mandatory anti-DCE self-check (cross-crate doc §152): before pass 1, the façade asserts expected-vs-found singleton/infrastructure counts so a silently-DCE'd contributing crate (whose definitions never registered) becomes a LOUD boot-time error rather than a quietly-incomplete graph — and the driver should surface enough introspection (names being instantiated, count) to make that self-check writable. Generics cannot be eagerly instantiated (no `'static` symbol/TypeId until concretely instantiated → the descriptor that feeds creation can only name concrete types; macro hard-errors on generic beans with a per-instantiation escape hatch). The WASM-module 'classpath' slots in as descriptors whose factory dispatches into the module — the eager pass creates and publishes them through the identical sequence (CANDIDATES: a static link-time registry default PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned source, both feeding the SAME definition set the eager pass walks — not a decision). The creation guard substrate (sharded mutex vs keyed async lock) is a Phase-2 call that lives entirely in leaf-core; no cross-crate ABI surface.

**T2 — execution context:** The eager pass runs at REFRESH on the bootstrap task (cold path) — it `.await`s every singleton's construction; it may await (DB pools) but a blocking factory must use spawn_blocking (execution-context discipline, §2.3). The per-bean creation SEQUENCE is the cold construction path: construct/populate/init are `.await` points; init code that does cross-bean async work must NOT do it under a held guard (the rust-native dissolution of Spring's @PostConstruct-under-lock deadlock — heavy cross-bean work moves to the post-all-singletons SmartInitializing callback, which runs OUTSIDE any guard after every singleton exists, §smart-initializing). The creation guard is per-bean and never global (§2.5): a strict (main bootstrap) builder awaits it; a lenient (background-bootstrap) builder builds outside it and reconciles a benign in-creation race. Safe publication: the finished handle is MOVED into `ready` only after init returns — visibility of the fully-built singleton is guaranteed (§concurrency-contract), runtime method thread-safety is the developer's. CANCELLATION: cancelling an in-flight eager build (or a background build) must drop the partially-constructed instance, clear it from in_creation, and unwind any early-ref factory it registered — and because there is no async Drop, a partially-built bean holding async-cleanup resources cannot rely on Drop; the driver must run any registered async cleanup explicitly on the unwind path. Prototype creation runs on whatever task calls `get` (hot path for stateful beans): build-init-handoff inline, register no destruction → cancellation drops a half-built value with nothing to clean. Errors propagate as a rich ResolveError carrying the assembly chain (what was being built, what it needed, candidates considered, why it failed) per §2.9; eager-pass failure aborts refresh all-or-nothing. Teardown of published singletons is a separate explicit `shutdown().await` in reverse dependency order (context-close), not part of creation.

#### R2 — Layered-graph plan-then-execute: a build-time/refresh-time topological creation PLAN drives a wave-parallel executor; cycles are a planning diagnostic, not a runtime trick
Question whether creation must be a recursive, lock-guarded re-entrant walk at all. Spring re-enters `getBean` recursively because it discovers the dependency graph LAZILY during creation and the JVM gives it no phase boundary. leaf's intent (fail-fast at boot, all-or-nothing, no global lock) is served better by separating PLANNING from EXECUTION. PLAN phase (cold, after the registry freezes): walk the merged defs and their declared dependency edges to compute a topological CREATION PLAN — a DAG of `CreationNode { def_idx, deps: SmallVec<DefIdx>, kind: Singleton|FactoryBeanFactory|... }` partitioned into dependency LAYERS (wave 0 = no deps, wave N = deps satisfied by waves < N). Cycle detection is a PLANNING result: a constructor cycle (mandatory edges) is a hard plan error with the full cycle path (the richest possible diagnostic, before any instance exists); a setter/field cycle (breakable edges) is either marked as needing early-exposure or — leaf's stricter default option — also a plan error pointing at the deferral-handle/lazy fix (matching Spring Boot's allow-circular-references=false default). EXECUTE phase: walk waves in order; within a wave, all nodes are INDEPENDENT by construction, so fan them out as concurrent construction futures (`join_all`/a bounded concurrency scope) — this IS the no-global-lock model, achieved by DATA SHAPE: there is no creation guard at all on the common path because the plan guarantees a bean's dependencies are already in `ready` before its own construction future starts, so no two futures ever contend on the same not-yet-built bean. The publish per node is a single move into `ready` after init. Background-bootstrap beans are just nodes the executor is free to run on a bootstrap executor (the plan already says they're independent). Lazy singletons are excluded from the plan (created on first `get` via a small fallback driver, R1-style, but rare). FactoryBean factories are plan nodes; their products are lazy unless eager-init (a dependent node). Prototypes are never in the plan (built on demand, hand-off-and-forget). The SmartInitializing callback fires after the last wave completes. Early-exposure (if leaf permits breakable cycles at all) becomes a special intra-wave construct: the planner emits a node pair (instantiate-only, then populate-and-init) so the half-built reference is in `ready`/`early` between them — making the three-level cache a PLAN artifact rather than a runtime-discovered race.

**Pros:** No creation guard on the common path AT ALL (the strongest §2.5 answer): the topological plan guarantees a bean's deps are in `ready` before its construction future starts, so same-bean contention and lock-ordering deadlocks are eliminated by data shape — Spring's hardest-won strict/lenient code simply isn't needed for planned beans.; Wave-parallel execution is the cleanest, most abuse-resistant parallel startup (§2.3): each wave's nodes are provably independent, so fan-out is safe and bounded — and background-bootstrap is not a special lenient-locking mode but just 'the executor runs these independent nodes wherever' (the planner already proved independence).; Cycles become a PLANNING diagnostic with the full cycle path BEFORE any instance is built — the earliest, richest diagnostic (§2.9), strictly better than Spring's mid-creation BeanCurrentlyInCreationException, and it cleanly separates fatal constructor cycles from breakable ones at analysis time.; Strongest AOT-lineage fit: the creation plan is exactly what a build-time pass could EMIT (a const wave schedule), and the runtime-assembly path computes the same plan at refresh — so the most expensive startup analysis can move to build time later without changing the executor (aligns with the §3 compile-vs-runtime keystone).; Eager-pass fail-fast is sharper: the plan validates the WHOLE graph's resolvability (every edge has a candidate, no fatal cycle) up front, so refresh either has a valid plan or aborts before constructing anything — true all-or-nothing with zero partial side effects on a plan failure.; The three-level cache's middle level, if needed at all, becomes an explicit planned node-pair rather than a runtime-discovered race — far easier to reason about and to make origin-agnostic for a future proxy mechanism.

**Cons:** Requires the dependency EDGES to be knowable BEFORE construction — but Spring discovers many dependencies dynamically (by-type/qualifier resolution that depends on what's registered, ObjectProvider lookups, FactoryBean products whose type is only known after creating the factory); a static plan can't capture edges that only materialize at resolution time, so the planner needs a conservative over-approximation plus a runtime fallback, partially reintroducing the recursive driver it sought to avoid.; Sacrifices Spring's mid-bootstrap dynamism: an init-method/registrar that registers a NEW definition mid-creation (Spring's defensive-copy case) has no node in an already-computed plan — leaf would need to re-plan or forbid it (a divergence from Spring, entangled with the bean-registry mutable-vs-frozen question and programmatic-registration).; Breakable (setter/field) cycles are awkward in a pure layered plan — they have no valid topological order — so either leaf forbids them (stricter than Spring's runtime default, a real DX/parity decision) or the planner emits special instantiate-then-populate node pairs that complicate the clean wave model and re-add early-exposure machinery.; More cold-path work and memory than lazy recursive creation: building and holding a full DAG + wave partitioning for every singleton (even ones with trivial deps) is overhead Spring's on-demand walk avoids — acceptable at boot but real, and wasted for the lazy/on-demand beans excluded from the plan.; Two execution paths (planned wave executor for eager singletons + a recursive fallback driver for lazy/dynamically-discovered/on-demand beans) must produce identical creation semantics (same init order, same publication, same error shape) — a coherence burden, and the fallback is essentially R1 anyway, so the planner's value is only as large as the fraction of beans it can plan statically.; Conservative edge over-approximation can serialize beans that are actually independent (false dependencies), reducing the parallelism that is the whole point — and getting the approximation tight enough to be worthwhile is non-trivial.

**Mechanisms considered:** plan phase: topological sort of merged-def dependency edges into dependency WAVES (a DAG of CreationNode partitioned by depth), computed after registry freeze; cycle detection as a planning result: constructor (mandatory-edge) cycle = hard plan error with full path; setter/field (breakable) cycle = plan error (strict default) OR planned instantiate-then-populate node pair; execute phase: wave-ordered, intra-wave concurrent fan-out (bounded join_all / structured-concurrency scope) — NO creation guard on the planned path (independence proven by the plan); conservative dependency over-approximation + a recursive R1-style fallback driver for lazy / dynamically-discovered / on-demand (prototype) beans; background-bootstrap as 'planner-proven-independent nodes the executor schedules freely on a bootstrap executor' — not a lenient-locking mode; origin-agnostic erased factory per node; SmartInitializing callback after the final wave; FactoryBean factory as a node, product lazy/eager as a dependent node

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for the AOT/build-time lineage: the creation plan (wave schedule + cycle verdict) is exactly what a build.rs/codegen pass could EMIT as a const artifact, and the runtime-assembly path computes the identical plan at refresh — substrate-neutral but biased toward 'analyze early'. The plan computation is the IDEAL place to run the anti-DCE self-check AND graph validation together (cross-crate doc §152): a silently-DCE'd contributing crate shows up as a missing node / an unsatisfiable edge → a loud plan-time error naming the missing dependency, strictly better than R1's count-only check because it can name WHAT is unsatisfiable. The dynamic-edge problem is the real T1 tension: by-type/qualifier/ObjectProvider/FactoryBean-product edges may only be knowable at runtime, so a build-time plan must conservatively include all candidate edges (coupling to candidate-resolver's output) — and a WASM-module 'classpath' contributing late definitions either must register before planning (a pre-freeze source, tension with build-time planning) or force a re-plan (the dynamic source feeding the runtime executor's fallback, not the static plan). Both the static link-time registry and the optional dynamic directory-scanned source are CANDIDATES feeding the planner's input set — not a decision. Generics-cannot-register inherited (the plan holds only concrete nodes; macro hard-errors).

**T2 — execution context:** PLAN phase is fully synchronous cold-path (after freeze, around refresh, single-threaded) — no async, no IO; it reads merged defs and edges and produces a wave schedule. EXECUTE phase runs on the bootstrap task: each wave is a bounded concurrent fan-out of construction futures (structured-concurrency scope so a failure/cancel in one node cancels the wave and unwinds cleanly — §2.3 abuse-resistant threading); construction may `.await` (DB pools) but blocking factories use spawn_blocking. Because the plan proves intra-wave independence, there is NO creation guard and NO lock-ordering hazard on the planned path — the strongest no-global-lock posture (§2.5). Background-bootstrap nodes run on a bootstrap executor concurrently with the main waves where the plan permits. Safe publication: each node moves its finished handle into `ready` after init; a later wave reads `ready` lock-free (the deps are already published). CANCELLATION: cancelling refresh cancels the structured scope → in-flight construction futures are dropped; partially-built beans with async cleanup must have it run explicitly (no async Drop) on the unwind path; un-published nodes hold nothing in `ready` to leak. The recursive FALLBACK driver (for lazy/dynamic/prototype beans) runs on the calling task with R1's per-bean guard semantics for the rare contended case — its execution context is the get-caller's, and it `.await`s construction the same way. The SmartInitializing callback fires after the final wave, OUTSIDE any guard, on the bootstrap task. Errors are rich ResolveError: plan-phase errors carry the full graph context (cycle path, unsatisfiable edge) at the earliest point; execute-phase errors carry the node's assembly chain. Prototype/teardown contexts unchanged from R1 (hand-off-and-forget; explicit shutdown().await for singletons).

#### R3 — Instantiation as a pluggable creation STRATEGY over an origin-agnostic Provider: the driver is a thin policy that composes provider.provide + a publication strategy + a guard strategy
Push unification (§2.11) and origin-agnosticism hardest: make CREATION itself a swappable strategy rather than a fixed sequence, so native construction, FactoryBean indirection, background-init, prototype hand-off, and a future WASM-module source are all the SAME composed pipeline differing only in injected strategies. The irreducible unit is the origin-agnostic `Provider` (shared with bean-registry's R3): `trait Provider { fn descriptor(&self) -> &Descriptor; fn provide<'a>(&'a self, cx: &'a dyn CreationCtx) -> BoxFuture<'a, Result<RawInstance, ResolveError>>; }` — a native factory, a FactoryBean bridge, a WASM bridge, a test double, or a pre-realized instance are ALL Provider impls; `provide` returns the RAW (pre-init) instance. The CREATION DRIVER is a thin orchestrator parameterized by three strategies: `struct Creator { populate: Box<dyn PopulateStrategy>, initialize: Box<dyn InitStrategy>, guard: Box<dyn CreationGuard>, publish: Box<dyn PublicationStrategy> }`. `create(provider, cx)` = `guard.scope(provider.descriptor(), || async { let raw = provider.provide(cx).await?; let early = publish.expose_early(raw)?; /* optional middle-level early-ref hook */ populate.populate(&raw, cx).await?; let ready = initialize.run(raw, cx).await?; publish.commit(ready) })`. Each strategy is the composition seam for a deferred keystone: `PublicationStrategy` IS the singleton-handle + cache-level decision (a singleton publish moves into `ready` and registers reverse-teardown; a PROTOTYPE publish is a NO-OP commit that hands the owned value back and registers nothing — hand-off-and-forget as a strategy value, impossible to get wrong; a two-level vs three-level cache is two PublicationStrategy impls); `CreationGuard` IS the no-global-lock decision (a per-bean keyed-lock impl, a OnceCell-per-slot impl, a lenient/strict background impl — swap without touching the driver); `PopulateStrategy`/`InitStrategy` delegate to autowiring-resolution and lifecycle-callbacks but are pluggable so a 'lite/AOT' path can skip dynamic resolution. The EAGER pass is itself a strategy consumer: `eager_instantiate(engine)` iterates the eager singleton providers and calls `create` with the singleton strategy bundle, all-or-nothing; the SmartInitializing callback is a post-pass hook. FactoryBean is NOT special-cased — it is a Provider whose `provide` returns the product (and registers the factory's own lifecycle), with the `&` dereference resolved one layer up. This makes the bare-engine-vs-context and the native-vs-dynamic-source differences reduce to WHICH strategy bundle is installed.

**Pros:** Maximal origin-agnosticism (the brief's core mandate): native, FactoryBean-product, WASM-module, test-double, and pre-realized instances are ONE Provider the driver can't distinguish — the future dynamic 'classpath' is not a special creation path, it's more Providers created and published by the identical strategy bundle.; Maximal unification (§2.11): prototype hand-off-and-forget (NO-OP commit), two-vs-three cache levels, singleton-vs-scoped publication, and per-bean-guard-vs-OnceCell are all PublicationStrategy/CreationGuard CHOICES the driver already accommodates — so Phase 2's deferred keystones (handle type, cache levels, guard substrate, async-across-dyn boxing) become strategy selections rather than rewrites of the driver.; Prototype's never-destroyed and singleton's destroy-at-close become DECLARATIVE strategy behavior (a no-op vs a teardown-registering commit), provably correct by construction and trivially testable as a policy table — the contract can't be violated by a forgotten branch.; FactoryBean (& dereference), background-init, and early-exposure all compose as strategy implementations instead of bespoke branches in a monolithic doCreateBean — fewer code paths, neighbours plug in uniformly, and the eager pass is strategy-agnostic.; The CreationGuard SPI lets R1's per-bean keyed lock and R2's plan-implied no-guard BOTH exist as implementations — so Phase 2 can A/B the concurrency model (even per-scope) without rewriting the creation sequence.; Cleanly separates the always-async seam (`provide` returns a boxed future, REQUIRED to be dyn so WASM/test-double slot in) from the strategy-composable rest, localizing the alloc-per-construction cost to one well-understood boundary.

**Cons:** Most indirection by far: every creation threads through `Box<dyn CreationGuard>` + `Box<dyn Provider>` + `Box<dyn PopulateStrategy>` + `Box<dyn InitStrategy>` + `Box<dyn PublicationStrategy>` — boxed-future + dyn-dispatch churn on the construction path, in tension with §2.5 (minimal boxing) even though construction is cold for singletons (the prototype hot path pays it per resolution, a real cost flagged for Phase 2).; Borderline over-engineering for Phase 1: a fully general strategy-SPI creation driver risks DECLARING (not just proposing) the 'everything is a swappable strategy' meta-architecture that Phase 2 owns — must be held strictly as a CANDIDATE shape, and its generality only earns its weight if leaf ships an opinionated default bundle (which then resembles R1, questioning the SPI tax).; The PublicationStrategy must precisely specify the three-level promotion + early-ref + reverse-teardown contract for every implementer — a subtle interface where a mis-ordered promotion silently breaks cycle resolution or a forgotten teardown-registration silently leaks; the coherence Spring gets implicitly from one concrete doCreateBean is now a contract spread across implementers.; Harder default-DX story (§2.1): a driver needing four strategies + a guard injected is ceremony unless the default bundle is invisible — and 'find the creation logic' is muddier (it's a composition, not a readable sequence), hurting the cargo-expand-legibility/teachability that R1 maximizes.; The strict-vs-lenient background-creation subtlety doesn't disappear — it just moves INTO a CreationGuard impl, where it's still the hardest-won Spring concurrency code; the SPI boundary makes it pluggable but no easier to get right, and a wrong guard impl is now a silent correctness bug behind an interface.; Recursive populate still re-enters the driver for dependencies, so the strategy composition is mutually recursive across `.await` — the same deadlock-surface as R1, now obscured behind strategy indirection that makes the lock-ordering reasoning harder, not easier.

**Mechanisms considered:** origin-agnostic `Provider` trait (descriptor + async provide → RAW instance) as the single created unit; native/FactoryBean-product/WASM/test-double all impls (shared with bean-registry R3); thin `Creator` orchestrator parameterized by PopulateStrategy + InitStrategy + CreationGuard + PublicationStrategy (Box<dyn _> SPIs); PublicationStrategy SPI = the singleton-handle + cache-level + teardown-registration decision; prototype = NO-OP commit (hand-off-and-forget as a strategy value); CreationGuard SPI = the no-global-lock decision (per-bean keyed lock OR OnceCell-per-slot OR lenient/strict background) — swappable without touching the driver; eager pass as a strategy consumer (iterate eager providers, create with the singleton bundle, all-or-nothing); SmartInitializing as a post-pass hook; FactoryBean as a Provider (provide returns the product, registers factory lifecycle); & dereference resolved one layer up — no special-casing in the driver

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for the cross-crate layered-strategy model AND the two-source duality: a static link-time registry contributes native `Provider`s; an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source contributes WASM-bridge `Provider`s — BOTH created and published through the identical strategy bundle, so the duality is two producers of one Provider stream feeding one creation driver, NOT two code paths (CANDIDATES, most natural of the three for the WASM slot-in — not a decision). leaf-core hosts the `Creator`, the four strategy traits, `Provider`/`Descriptor`/`RawInstance` — a minimal, ultra-stable ABI the thin macro targets (emits a `Descriptor` + a native-Provider seed via absolute paths; the macro holds zero creation logic, §2.10). The eager-pass strategy consumer is where the anti-DCE self-check lives (cross-crate doc §152): expected-vs-found eager-provider counts (with per-source provenance so the check names the missing crate) before the all-or-nothing pass. Generics: no Provider until concretely instantiated → macro hard-errors, concrete-instantiation escape hatch registers a concrete Provider. The strategy SPIs are themselves a cross-crate extension surface (an integration crate could ship a custom PublicationStrategy for a scope, or a CreationGuard) — powerful but WIDENS the stable-ABI commitment the whole workspace hard-codes paths to, a real cost to weigh against the generality. Substrate-neutral on HOW Providers are collected (linkme/codegen/explicit), by design.

**T2 — execution context:** Strategy composition/wiring is synchronous cold-path (around refresh). The single mandatory async seam is `Provider::provide` (boxed future at the dyn boundary — AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe, and origin-agnosticism REQUIRES the dyn boundary so WASM/test-double slot in, so boxing is accepted as a structural cost: cold for singletons, a Phase-2 hot-path concern for prototypes/scoped); `populate`/`initialize` are also async strategy methods (boxed at the dyn boundary). The eager pass runs on the bootstrap task, awaiting each `create`; blocking providers use spawn_blocking (§2.3). The CreationGuard strategy DECLARES the concurrency contract: a per-bean guard scopes only same-bean first-creation (never global, §2.5); a background/lenient guard impl builds outside and reconciles; a OnceCell-per-slot impl makes the slot the arbiter. Safe publication is the PublicationStrategy's `commit`: a singleton commit moves the handle into `ready` and registers reverse-dependency teardown (§concurrency-contract); a prototype commit is a no-op returning the owned value (nothing registered → cancellation drops a half-built value cleanly). CANCELLATION: cancelling an in-flight `create` drops the future; the guard strategy releases; a partially-built instance with async cleanup must have it run explicitly (no async Drop) on the unwind path — a contract the PublicationStrategy/Creator must specify since the raw instance may already hold resources. Errors are rich ResolveError attributed to the failing strategy/Provider (the SPI boundary gives precise 'failed in provide/populate/init/commit for bean X' diagnostics, §2.9). The SmartInitializing post-pass hook runs outside any guard after the eager pass. Teardown is driven by the PublicationStrategy's registered reverse-dependency ledger via an explicit shutdown().await (context-close).

### Rust clashes
- @PostConstruct/init under the singleton creation lock is a deadlock trap that becomes an EXECUTOR-blocking hazard in async Rust. Spring runs init methods INSIDE the creation lock and publishes only after they return — guaranteeing field visibility but inviting deadlock when init code accesses other beans or spawns re-entrant threads. In async Rust the analogue is worse: an init future that `.await`s under a held guard ties up an executor worker and can deadlock the whole pool if the awaited work needs the same guard. leaf must NOT hold any guard across a user init `.await`; the rust-native realization is to keep the per-bean guard scope minimal (publication, not user code) and to move heavy cross-bean work to the post-all-singletons lock-free SmartInitializing callback (§smart-initializing) — preserving Spring's escape-hatch intent while dissolving the trap rather than inheriting it.
- Global mutex → per-bean guard with strict/lenient policy is the load-bearing concurrency re-derivation, and it is genuinely hard. Spring moved OFF a global singleton mutex (which deadlocked on user init code) to a per-registry ReentrantLock + lenientCreationLock + a per-thread strict-vs-lenient policy precisely so background/independent beans build concurrently. Charter §2.5 forbids global locks outright. leaf must reproduce the INTENT — serialize only same-bean first-creation, let distinct/background beans build in parallel, guarantee visibility of the finished singleton — with a per-bean guard. The lenient path ('build outside the guard and reconcile a TOLERATED, now-benign BeanCurrentlyInCreation race') is Spring's hardest-won code (multiple point releases, issues #34349/#30887); re-deriving it in async Rust, where a losing build's partially-constructed value AND its in-flight dependency awaits must unwind cleanly across cancellation, is the single highest-risk part of this feature.
- No async Drop means a partially-constructed bean cannot self-clean on the unwind path. Spring relies on GC + the container's lock infrastructure; a failed/cancelled creation just becomes garbage. In Rust, a bean whose factory already acquired an async-cleanup resource (a DB connection, a spawned task) and then fails populate/init — or whose eager build is cancelled — cannot run async cleanup via Drop. The creation driver must therefore retain enough state to run any registered async cleanup EXPLICITLY when a creation fails or is cancelled, and must clear the in_creation/early-ref state deterministically — the all-or-nothing eager pass must leave NO partially-constructed beans with dangling resources on abort.
- Register-a-factory-not-the-instance early exposure exists for an AOP early-PROXY that leaf doesn't have yet. Spring places an ObjectFactory (not the raw bean) into the middle cache level SO a SmartInstantiationAwareBeanPostProcessor can compute the eventual proxy lazily-and-once, recorded to avoid double-wrap. leaf has no CGLIB and its proxy mechanism (auto-proxy-creator) is unsettled, so WHEN to expose the early reference and WHAT it should be (raw vs early-proxy) is partly undecidable now. This feature must place the early-exposure HOOK at the structurally-correct moment (after instantiate, before populate, only under cycle conditions) while keeping it origin-agnostic and proxy-mechanism-agnostic — and the raw-vs-wrapped consistency check (Spring's allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping) has no meaning until the proxy story exists.
- Prototype hand-off-and-forget maps cleanly to Rust ownership (an OWNED MOVE to the caller, no container retention) — a rare case where Rust is BETTER than Spring. Spring runs init but never destroy and forgets the instance (relying on GC); the danger Spring avoids (tracking prototypes = a leak) is exactly what Rust's ownership prevents by construction: handing the caller an owned value means the container retains nothing and the never-destroyed contract is structural. The clash is only that the SAME construct→populate→init sequence must produce an owned move for prototypes and a shared/published handle for singletons — so the publication step (not the construction step) is where the ownership model diverges, and the singleton-handle type itself (Arc<dyn> vs arena slot vs borrow) is the deferred §3 keystone this feature must not pre-decide.
- Recursive getBean during populate is mutually-recursive async creation across `.await` — a re-entrancy and lock-ordering hazard. Spring populates a bean by recursively resolving its collaborators, re-entering getBean (and the creation lock) at each dependency. In async Rust this is a recursive async call graph that may hold a per-bean guard at each frame; without care this re-creates the lock-ordering deadlock the per-bean guard was meant to avoid. The async stack depth of recursive construction is also a real concern (boxed futures, potential stack growth). leaf must ensure the guard is per-bean (so distinct deps don't contend) and that the lenient fallback breaks any same-bean re-entrant wait — and consider whether plan-then-execute (R2) sidesteps the recursion entirely for the common case.
- Two-pass eager loop over a 'defensive copy' assumes mid-bootstrap definition mutation that leaf may forbid. Spring iterates a defensive copy of beanDefinitionNames so an init-method can register a NEW definition without ConcurrentModificationException. Whether leaf preserves this dynamism depends on the bean-registry mutable-vs-frozen-snapshot decision (R2 there freezes; R1/R3 stay mutable) — if the registry is frozen at refresh, the defensive-copy concern disappears but so does the ability to register beans during creation (a divergence from Spring, entangled with programmatic-registration and conditions). This feature's eager pass must be written to whichever registry shape Phase 2 picks — it cannot assume mutability.
- FactoryBean product is created lazily and its lifecycle is ORPHANED — a poor fit for Rust's explicit-teardown discipline. Spring creates the FactoryBean factory eagerly but the product lazily (unless SmartFactoryBean.isEagerInit), caches the product separately, and does NOT call the product's close() automatically (the factory must implement DisposableBean and delegate). In Rust with no async Drop and a container-driven shutdown, an orphaned product holding async-cleanup resources is a leak waiting to happen — leaf must decide whether the product's teardown rides the factory's registered cleanup (more disciplined than Spring) or stays orphaned (Spring-faithful but leak-prone); either way the creation driver's eager-gating (factory-always, product-only-if-eager) must be reproduced while the teardown ownership is reconciled with context-close.

### Interactions
- **bean-registry** — PRIMARY UPSTREAM. bean-registry OWNS the stores this feature drives: the three-level singleton cache (ready/early/factories), the in_creation set, the merged-def cache, and the candidate sets. bean-instantiation is the CREATION DRIVER and the EAGER-pass orchestration over those stores — it reads the merged def, runs construct→expose-early→populate→init, and PUBLISHES the finished handle into the registry's `ready` cache. The boundary: registry = data structures + their concurrency-safe access; bean-instantiation = the algorithm and the creation guard around it. The deferred-early-ref ObjectFactory at the registry's middle cache level is placed BY this feature at the correct moment in the sequence.
- **bean-definition** — UPSTREAM. The driver instantiates from the MERGED RootBeanDefinition (not the raw def), reading scope, lazy, abstract, and the origin-agnostic construction FACTORY/recipe from it. bean-definition fixes the metadata record and the freeze boundary; bean-instantiation consumes the merged def's factory to construct and honors its lazy/abstract/eager-init flags to gate the eager pass. The factory is the origin-agnostic boxed-future seam every realization constructs through.
- **scopes** — TIGHTLY COUPLED SIBLING. scopes fixes per-scope multiplicity/lifetime POLICY (singleton = once+cached+published+destroy-at-close; prototype = build-init-handoff-forget, never destroyed; web/custom = per-context-key store); bean-instantiation is the SEQUENCE that realizes those policies — it runs the same construct→populate→init for all, but the PUBLISH step diverges per scope (singleton → publish to ready + register teardown; prototype → owned move, register nothing; scoped → store in the scope's per-key store). The ownership boundary (does the Singleton scope own the cache or does the registry?) is a shared phase2Question.
- **refresh-lifecycle** — UPSTREAM DRIVER. refresh-lifecycle's finishBeanFactoryInitialization step CALLS this feature's eager pre-instantiation pass at the fixed point in bring-up (after all BFPPs/BPPs registered, message source/multicaster initialized, config frozen). refresh-lifecycle owns the all-or-nothing bring-up state machine and the ordering; bean-instantiation owns the eager pass (two loops + SmartInitializing) that refresh invokes as its penultimate step. Eager-pass failure aborts refresh.
- **circular-references** — TIGHTEST COUPLING. The early-exposure step in the per-bean sequence (register an EarlyRefFactory into the middle cache level when singleton && allow_circular && in_creation) IS circular-references' mechanism, placed by this feature at the structurally-correct moment (after instantiate, before populate). circular-references defines tolerate-setter/field-cycles-via-early-ref, fail-fast-on-constructor-cycles, and the raw-vs-wrapped consistency check; bean-instantiation places the hook and runs the in_creation cycle detection. In R2, cycles become a planning diagnostic instead of a runtime trick.
- **lifecycle-callbacks** — The INITIALIZE phase of the per-bean sequence IS lifecycle-callbacks' ordered init chain (Aware → before-init BPP → @PostConstruct/afterPropertiesSet/init-method → after-init BPP where proxies attach). lifecycle-callbacks owns the callback ordering and dedup rules; bean-instantiation invokes it as step (e) of creation and publishes only AFTER it returns (safe publication). Prototypes run init but the mirrored destruction is never registered.
- **smart-initializing** — The eager pass's SECOND loop fires smart-initializing's single lock-free callback AFTER every eager singleton exists, explicitly OUTSIDE any creation guard — the sanctioned place for cross-bean aggregation that would deadlock under the per-bean guard (the rust-native dissolution of Spring's @PostConstruct-under-lock trap). bean-instantiation provides the 'all eager singletons now exist' boundary; smart-initializing defines the callback contract. Fired only for eager singletons, never prototypes/lazy/scoped.
- **lazy-initialization** — The eager pass reads the lazy flag to GATE construction: a lazy singleton is registered/merged but NOT built during the eager pass — its potential failure is relocated to first get(). bean-instantiation's eager driver skips lazy beans (and in R2 excludes them from the plan, building them via the recursive fallback on first access); lazy-initialization defines the per-bean @Lazy and global-flag-with-excludes semantics that gate the driver.
- **autowiring-resolution** — The POPULATE phase delegates to autowiring-resolution to resolve each injection point, which may recursively re-enter get() to create dependencies — making creation mutually recursive with resolution. autowiring-resolution defines the layered fail-fast selection policy; bean-instantiation invokes it during populate and the recursive dependency creation it triggers re-enters this driver. The recursion-across-await is a documented hazard (rustClashes).
- **factory-bean** — The eager pass reproduces FactoryBean eager-gating: create the factory ALWAYS (it's a singleton bean) but the PRODUCT only if it's eager-init, else the product is created lazily on first get(). bean-instantiation's driver branches on is-factory-bean to construct the factory vs dereference to the product (& prefix), publishing the product into the separate product cache. In R3, FactoryBean is just another Provider, removing the branch. The orphaned-product-teardown clash (rustClashes) is reconciled here.
- **background-bootstrap** — The strict-vs-lenient per-creation policy on the per-bean guard is exactly what lets background-init beans build OUTSIDE the guard (lenient) concurrently with the strict main bootstrap thread — without deadlocking on user init code held under the guard. bean-instantiation owns the guard and the lenient/strict flag carried through the creation context; background-bootstrap marks which beans init concurrently and supplies the bootstrap executor the eager pass fans them out onto. In R2 these are just planner-proven-independent nodes.
- **concurrency-contract** — The publish step IS the realization of safe-publication-of-fully-built-singletons: the per-bean guard + the move-into-ready-after-init guarantees visibility of the finished instance across threads, NOT runtime method thread-safety. bean-instantiation is the concrete site where the concurrency contract is honored; concurrency-contract frames the doctrine (scope choice as the concurrency lever) and delegates mutable-state safety to the developer.
- **context-close** — Because there is no async Drop, the publish step for singletons must register reverse-dependency teardown data that context-close's explicit shutdown().await drains in reverse creation/dependency order. bean-instantiation records the creation/dependency order during the eager pass (and on lazy first-creation); context-close defines the stop-Lifecycle-then-destroy-in-reverse sequence that consumes it. Prototypes register nothing (never destroyed).
- **post-processor-spi** — The before-init and after-init BPP-analogue invocations in the initialize phase are where post-processors decorate the instance (after-init is where AOP proxies attach); MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor-analogues run earlier to discover injection metadata. bean-instantiation invokes these processor hooks at the fixed points in the sequence; post-processor-spi defines the processor contracts and ordering. The eager pass runs only after all BPPs are registered (refresh ordering).
- **aware-callbacks** — The initialize phase fires the aware-callbacks group (bean name, factory, environment, context, event publisher) BEFORE the init methods, as a sub-step of before-init. bean-instantiation invokes the aware group at the correct point in the sequence; aware-callbacks defines which infrastructure is injected and the group-before-init timing.
- **deferral-primitives** — When populate encounters a deferral handle (ObjectProvider/lazy-resolution proxy) injection point instead of a direct dependency, the driver injects the handle WITHOUT eagerly creating the target — relocating creation to the handle's first call. bean-instantiation's populate respects the deferral type (not triggering recursive creation); deferral-primitives defines the handle types and their re-resolution semantics, and is a sanctioned cycle-break that changes the dependency edges the eager driver/plan sees.
- **auto-proxy-creator** — The after-init step is where the auto-proxy-creator wraps the instance (returning a different object as the published handle), and the early-exposure EarlyRefFactory exists specifically so an early-proxy can be computed lazily-and-once during a cycle. bean-instantiation places the early-ref hook and runs the after-init proxy step; auto-proxy-creator defines the proxying mechanism. Since leaf's proxy story is unsettled, the raw-vs-wrapped consistency check and whether the middle cache level is needed are open (rustClashes/phase2Questions).

### Open questions for Phase 2
- The singleton-handle type and ownership model (THE scope/ownership keystone, charter §3) viewed through PUBLICATION: when the creation driver finishes a singleton, does it publish an Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync> (commits to refcount, fights §2.5, forces Send+Sync on every bean), an arena+DefIdx slot (no per-bean Arc but ties storage to a fixed slot, breaks for prototype/scoped), or a container-owned value handed out as borrows? Prototype publishes an OWNED MOVE cleanly; singleton/scoped need a shared/stored handle. The publish step's signature and the whole get<T> return type depend on this, and it must stay origin-agnostic so a WASM-module provider publishes identically. Shared with bean-registry and scopes.
- The per-bean creation-guard substrate AND the strict-vs-lenient reconciliation: sharded Mutex<()> map, OnceCell/OnceLock per slot, or an async keyed lock — and how is Spring's hardest-won 'build outside the guard and reconcile a tolerated benign BeanCurrentlyInCreation race' (the background-bootstrap enabler) realized in async Rust without a global lock, where a losing build's partially-constructed value and its in-flight dependency awaits must unwind cleanly across cancellation? This is where §2.5 meets background-bootstrap and is the subtlest concurrency call. Shared with bean-registry.
- Recursive-lazy creation (R1/R3) vs plan-then-execute wave parallelism (R2): is creation a recursive, guard-protected re-entrant walk (Spring-faithful, handles dynamic edges, but re-creates the lock-ordering hazard and async recursion depth), or a topological plan executed in independence-proven waves (no guard on the common path, earliest cycle diagnostic, AOT-friendly — but can't capture dynamically-discovered edges and forbids mid-bootstrap definition registration)? This is a unification + AOT-lineage call (§3 compile-vs-runtime) needing the whole catalog (esp. autowiring-resolution's dynamic edges, programmatic-registration, conditions, deferral-primitives) to judge.
- Two cache levels (ready/early) vs three (ready/early/factories): the middle deferred-ObjectFactory level exists in Spring ONLY to compute an AOP early-proxy lazily-and-once; leaf's proxy mechanism (auto-proxy-creator) doesn't exist yet and there's no CGLIB, so whether the third level — and thus where/what the early-exposure hook places — earns its keep is undecidable until the proxying strategy is chosen. Cross-cuts circular-references and auto-proxy-creator. Shared with bean-registry.
- Async-across-dyn boxing standard for the construction seam: the origin-agnostic factory/Provider MUST be dyn (so WASM/test-double slot in) and async-fn-in-trait isn't dyn-safe → boxed futures (alloc per construction). Is the standard a boxed-future-on-the-dyn-path (fine for singletons, a cost for prototype/scoped hot creation) with a monomorphized zero-cost typed fast-path as the escape, or vice versa? The async-execution-model decision (§3) viewed through the creation path. Shared with bean-registry and scopes.
- How much wiring/graph VALIDATION moves to build/freeze/plan time vs runtime eager-instantiate (§2.9 richest-diagnostic timing): R2 validates the whole graph (every edge resolvable, no fatal cycle) at plan time before constructing anything (earliest, richest); R1/R3 surface errors during the all-or-nothing eager pass as beans are built. Is the eager-build-everything pass still the PRIMARY error-surfacing mechanism, or is it partly replaced by a dedicated startup-validation/plan pass? Interacts with the conditional strategy and the codegen boundary (§3).
- Mutable mid-bootstrap registration vs frozen-at-refresh: Spring's eager loop iterates a defensive copy so init-methods/registrars can register NEW definitions mid-creation. Does leaf preserve this (forcing the eager pass and any creation plan to tolerate late additions / re-planning) or freeze the registry at refresh (simpler, AOT-aligned, but a divergence)? Entangled with the bean-registry mutable-vs-snapshot decision, programmatic-registration, and conditions.
- Cancellation + partial-construction cleanup contract given no async Drop: when an eager build (or a background/lenient build) is cancelled or fails mid-sequence, what mechanism guarantees a partially-constructed bean's already-acquired async-cleanup resources are released and the in_creation/early-ref state is unwound — a structured-concurrency scope-guard, an explicit registered-cleanup ledger run on the unwind path, or a drop-bomb scheduling async teardown? Given the all-or-nothing eager pass, leaving this open risks partial-construction resource leaks on the common boot-failure path. Shared with context-close and the async-execution model.
- Is the creation driver ONE concrete sequence (Spring's single doCreateBean — R1/R2) or a composition of swappable strategies (R3 — PopulateStrategy/InitStrategy/CreationGuard/PublicationStrategy pluggable)? Making creation strategy-pluggable maximizes origin-agnosticism and lets Phase 2 A/B the concurrency/publication models, but costs the zero-cost monomorphized path, widens the stable ABI the whole workspace hard-codes paths to, and borders on pre-deciding the meta-architecture Phase 2 owns. Shared with bean-registry and scopes (the 'one engine vs pluggable-strategy-kernel' question recurs).
- FactoryBean product teardown ownership: Spring orphans the product (the factory must implement DisposableBean and delegate; close() is NOT called automatically). With no async Drop and a container-driven reverse-order shutdown (context-close), does leaf keep the product orphaned (Spring-faithful, leak-prone) or ride its teardown on the factory's registered cleanup (more disciplined, divergent)? The eager-gating (factory-always, product-only-if-eager) is settled; the teardown ownership is not. Shared with factory-bean and context-close.
- The two-source duality concretely for CONSTRUCTION: the static link-time registry (default 'classpath') AND an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source both feed the SAME set of definitions/Providers the eager pass creates and publishes — but R2's plan-then-execute needs the dynamic source registered before planning (or a re-plan), while R1/R3's recursive driver accommodates late/dynamic Providers naturally. How the dynamic source's timing reconciles with the eager pass / creation plan is unresolved (a CANDIDATE, not a decision). Shared with the whole discovery/SPI family.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the precise feature section — `The preInstantiateSingletons refresh loop` (2466-2477: the two passes over a defensive copy, the !abstract&&singleton&&!lazy gate, FactoryBean eager-gating, the trailing SmartInitializing loop, the fail-fast-at-boot design intent) — plus the surrounding creation mechanics that are this feature's body: the full lifecycle/init ordering and init-under-the-singleton-lock (2479-2504), the strict-vs-lenient singleton creation locking with isCurrentThreadAllowedToHoldSingletonLock and the benign-BeanCurrentlyInCreation tolerance (2506-2516), the three-level singleton cache and WHY it registers a factory not the raw instance for lazy-once early-proxy (2518-2539), the two distinct BeanCurrentlyInCreation origins (2535-2537), the circular-reference doCreateBean early-exposure (773-785), and FactoryBean creation/orphaned-product/eager-gating (2541-2557). I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc (Layer-0/A/B DCE silent-failure + the mandatory self-check §152, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI §99-102, generics-cannot-register §153, linkme-default §166, the donsz.nl scope-discovery-not-implicitly-global warning §36) and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimal-Arc, §2.9 rich-early-diagnostics) and the stable envelope (RPITIT/AFIT not dyn-safe forcing boxed-future factories §160-176, trait upcasting 1.86 §80, const TypeId 1.91 §81, Arc<dyn Any> registry §17, no async Drop as a first-class constraint §86).

CRITICAL SCOPING NOTE for Phase 2/3: this feature massively overlaps bean-registry and scopes, and I deliberately drew the boundary so they compose rather than collide. bean-registry FIXED the STORES (the three-level cache shape, the merged cache, candidate sets, the per-bean-guard-not-global-lock requirement) and scopes FIXED the per-scope POLICY (singleton-cached-published-destroyed / prototype-handoff-forget / scoped-per-key-store). bean-instantiation is the DRIVER and its CONCURRENCY: the construct→expose-early→populate→init→PUBLISH sequence, the all-or-nothing two-pass eager loop, the creation-guard mechanics and strict/lenient policy, prototype's owned-move publish, lazy/FactoryBean eager-gating, and cancellation/partial-construction unwind. My three realizations therefore diverge on the SHAPE OF THE CREATION ALGORITHM (not the cache shape, which is bean-registry's, nor the scope policy, which is scopes'): R1 = literal recursive doCreateBean async pipeline + two-pass eager loop guarded by a per-bean strict/lenient creation lock (highest fidelity, dovetails with the existing cache shape, but inherits Spring's hardest-won lenient-reconciliation code and the recursion-across-await hazard); R2 = plan-then-execute — a topological creation PLAN partitioned into independence-proven dependency WAVES executed with bounded fan-out and NO creation guard on the common path, cycles as a planning diagnostic (most rust-native no-global-lock-by-data-shape, earliest+richest cycle diagnostic, best AOT fit, but can't capture dynamically-discovered edges and forbids mid-bootstrap registration); R3 = creation as a composition of swappable strategies (PopulateStrategy/InitStrategy/CreationGuard/PublicationStrategy) over an origin-agnostic Provider (maximal unification + origin-agnosticism + best WASM slot-in, prototype-handoff-as-a-strategy-value, but most indirection and borders on pre-deciding the meta-architecture). R1↔R2 differ on recursive-lazy-walk vs plan-wave-parallel; R3 differs on monolithic-sequence vs pluggable-strategy. R3's strategies can host R1's guard and R2's no-guard as implementations, and R3's PublicationStrategy can host the two-vs-three-level cache choice — so the three are genuinely divergent yet Phase-2-composable.

Three things to carry forward: (1) The single most load-bearing behavior is the NO-GLOBAL-LOCK creation discipline AND the init-not-under-a-held-guard rule — Spring's @PostConstruct-under-the-singleton-lock is a deadlock trap that in async Rust becomes an EXECUTOR-blocking hazard (an init `.await` under a held guard can deadlock the pool), so leaf must keep the guard scope to publication only and route heavy cross-bean work to the lock-free SmartInitializing callback; the strict/lenient lenient-reconciliation that enables background-bootstrap is the highest re-derivation risk, flagged as a phase2Question. (2) NO ASYNC DROP makes partial-construction cleanup a non-syntactic discipline: a failed/cancelled eager build whose factory already grabbed an async-cleanup resource cannot self-clean via Drop, so the all-or-nothing eager pass must leave NO partially-constructed beans with dangling resources on abort — surfaced as a phase2Question shared with context-close. (3) Prototype hand-off-and-forget is a place Rust is BETTER than Spring: an OWNED MOVE to the caller means the container retains nothing (the leak Spring avoids by forgetting is structurally impossible), so the divergence point between scopes is the PUBLISH step (owned move vs shared/stored handle), not construction — which keeps the construction sequence unified and isolates the §3 ownership keystone to publication.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization keeps the published handle abstract (the scope/ownership keystone), keeps the construction recipe an origin-agnostic boxed-future factory (WASM-module/test-double ready), keeps two-vs-three cache levels open (proxy story unsettled), and is compatible with cross-crate Arch A/B/C/D. The two-source duality (static link-time registry + optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source) is captured as multiple producers of the SAME definition/Provider set the eager pass creates and publishes — most natural in R3, a real timing tension with R2's plan-then-execute — and flagged as a CANDIDATE not a decision. Naming ('create_singleton', 'EarlyRefFactory', 'CreationGuard', 'Provider', 'PublicationStrategy', 'CreationNode', 'wave', 'ErasedBean', 'RawInstance') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files.
