Relevant grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (773-785 circular-reference early exposure; 2466-2477 the preInstantiateSingletons two-pass loop + fail-fast intent; 2479-2504 init ordering + init-under-the-lock; 2506-2516 strict-vs-lenient creation locking; 2518-2539 three-level cache + register-a-factory-not-the-instance; 2535-2537 two BeanCurrentlyInCreation origins; 2541-2557 FactoryBean creation/orphaned-product/eager-gating); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2 esp. 2.3/2.4/2.5/2.9/2.11, deferred keystones §3, topology §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/A/B DCE silent-failure + self-check mandate §139-152, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI §89-102, generics-cannot-register §153, linkme-default §166, donsz.nl scope-not-global §36, Architectures §219-232); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe forcing boxed-future factories §160-176, trait upcasting 1.86 §80, const TypeId 1.91 §81, Arc<dyn Any> §17, no async Drop first-class constraint §86).

## Autowiring Resolution Algorithm  `[autowiring-resolution]`

Spring splits dependency resolution into FILTERING ("which beans qualify" — candidate-resolver) and SELECTION ("which single qualifier wins") plus the surrounding "is there exactly one / zero / many" arbitration. THIS feature owns the SELECTION + ARBITRATION for a SINGLE-VALUED injection point: it consumes the already-filtered qualifying set (from candidate-resolver, which applied autowire-candidate flag, generics-as-implicit-qualifier, @Qualifier/custom-qualifier-attributes) and runs a FIXED, FAIL-FAST, LAYERED tie-break that refuses to guess. The load-bearing intent to replicate (intent, NOT JVM mechanism): (a) the layered policy, each layer more explicit than the last — match by type -> narrow by generic type -> apply @Qualifier -> apply @Primary(promote one)/@Fallback(demote the rest) -> fall back to bean-name-vs-field/param-name match -> if still >1, FAIL with NoUniqueBean naming the exact injection point AND the candidate set. (b) The precise internal pipeline of Spring's doResolveDependency (v7.0.5): (1) a pre-resolved @Autowired shortcut, (2) a @Value/expression suggested-value short-circuit (delegated to value-injection — NOT this feature when present), (3) a fast declared-name/qualifier-name shortcut tried EARLY before the expensive candidate find, guarded by usesStandardBeanLookup AND all of {type-match, isAutowireCandidate, NOT a fallback, NO primary conflict (hasPrimaryConflict), NOT a self-reference}, (4) multiple-bean/collection handling + candidate finding, (5) determineAutowireCandidate when >1, (6) validation of the single result. (c) The determineAutowireCandidate tie-break ORDER is a FIXED container contract (NOT pluggable — filtering is the pluggable part): @Primary (and unique non-@Fallback) -> bean-name-vs-dependency-name match -> qualifier-suggested-name match -> highest @Priority -> unique default-candidate -> directly-registered resolvableDependency. determinePrimaryCandidate is itself a two-pass: unique @Primary (two local primaries = NoUniqueBean 'more than one primary'; local primary beats parent-context primary), else unique non-@Fallback. (d) Zero candidates -> NoSuchBean UNLESS the point is optional (required=false / Optional<T> / @Nullable). (e) Two hidden, non-obvious facts: the bean NAME is an implicit qualifier used BOTH as the early step-3 shortcut AND the late step-5 tie-break (so renaming a field can silently change which bean injects — a real coupling, not cosmetic); and resolvableDependencies are infrastructure objects (the container handle, event publisher, etc.) injectable by-type at the LOWEST precedence even though they are not normal bean definitions. (f) The fail-fast philosophy is the soul: any guess (first/newest/alphabetical) is a silent correctness landmine, so ambiguity is reported as a developer decision to be made explicit via @Primary/@Fallback/@Qualifier/generics/naming. Deepest Rust impedance: Spring reads @Value/@Qualifier/@Priority/required-ness reflectively off a live BeanFactory and matches generics via runtime ResolvableType; Rust has none of that, so every input the ladder reads (qualifier request, generic-arg TypeIds, @Priority/@Order, required-ness, the declared field/param name for the name tie-break) must be captured at the @Autowired declaration site by the thin macro into the injection-point descriptor, and generics-as-qualifier compares CONCRETE monomorphized TypeIds (generics cannot be registered).

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Fixed ordered tie-break ladder as a hand-written total function over a borrowed qualifying set (Spring-faithful determineAutowireCandidate, rust-owned)
Mirror Spring's doResolveDependency + determineAutowireCandidate directly as one hand-written, non-pluggable function on the bean-registry/context — the fixed contract realized as a literal sequence of guard clauses, NOT a strategy chain (filtering is the pluggable part, owned by candidate-resolver; selection is fixed). Inputs: `InjectionPoint` (statically captured by the macro at the @Autowired site: required produced-TypeId, generic-arg TypeIds, requested @Qualifier/custom-qualifier metadata, declared field/param NAME for the name tie-break, an `arity` = Single|Optional (the required=false/Option<T>/Nullable demotion) and a `uses_standard_lookup` flag) and the QUALIFYING SET handed over from candidate-resolver as a borrowed `&[Candidate<'_>]` (each `Candidate` = borrow over the frozen MergedBeanDefinition exposing primary/fallback/default_candidate/priority flags + name+aliases + the bean handle obtainer). `enum Resolved<'a> { One(&'a Candidate<'a>), None, Ambiguous(SmallVec<&'a Candidate>) }`. The algorithm is a literal pipeline: STEP 3 EARLY-SHORTCUT — if `uses_standard_lookup` and exactly one candidate matches name-or-qualifier-name AND passes all guards (is_autowire_candidate, NOT fallback, no `has_primary_conflict` over the set, not self-ref) return it without running the full ladder (Spring's pre-findAutowireCandidates fast path, an optimization that MUST preserve identical outcomes to the slow path); else STEP 4/5 — if set len 0 => `None` (caller maps to NoSuchBean unless arity==Optional); if len 1 => that one; if len>1 => run `determine_winner` = the FIXED ladder: (5a) `determine_primary` two-pass (unique @Primary across the set — two => Ambiguous-by-primary; local beats parent via the hierarchy depth carried on Candidate — else unique non-@Fallback); (5b) bean-name == injection-point-name; (5c) qualifier-suggested-name == bean-name/alias; (5d) highest @Priority/@Order (lower value wins, Ordered-interface-beats-annotation parity); (5e) unique default_candidate==true among the rest; (5f) resolvableDependency by-type (the infrastructure-handle map consulted last); still >1 => `Ambiguous`. The ladder is short-circuiting: first layer that yields a UNIQUE survivor wins. resolvableDependencies live in a small registry-owned `HashMap<TypeId, ErasedHandle>` consulted at 5f and also folded into the by-type candidate set so the count is correct. Optional arity (Option<T>/required=false) only changes the None->error mapping, never the tie-break. The whole function is sync, allocation-light (SmallVec for the rare >1 case), and produces a rich `ResolveError` carrying the full candidate set + per-candidate why-not when it returns Ambiguous/None.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: the layered policy and the determineAutowireCandidate tie-break ORDER are a literal, line-by-line, totally-ordered guard sequence — maximally teachable, every Spring step traceable to a code line, and the fixed-contract-not-pluggable nature is structural (it is just a function, there is no strategy seam to abuse).; The fail-fast contract is the function's return type: `Resolved::Ambiguous`/`None` cannot be silently coerced into a winner — refusing to guess is enforced by the type system, not convention, exactly honoring the soul of the feature and charter invariant #9.; Operates over a BORROWED `&[Candidate]` qualifying set with zero ownership transfer and a SmallVec only on the cold >1 path — minimal Arc/heap churn (charter #5); the common len-0/len-1 cases allocate nothing and run as a couple of comparisons.; Clean producer/consumer seam with candidate-resolver: this function NEVER filters (no qualifier/generic matching here — that already happened upstream) and candidate-resolver NEVER selects; the two phases the catalog deliberately separated stay separated because selection is a concrete function consuming a set, not a resolver method.; The early step-3 shortcut and the late step-5 name tie-break read the SAME captured declaration-site name, so 'name is an implicit qualifier in two places' is realized once and consistently; the shortcut's guard set (no primary conflict, not fallback, not self-ref) is explicit, auditable code rather than emergent behavior.; resolvableDependencies as a small TypeId->handle map consulted at the lowest precedence is a faithful, origin-agnostic realization (the infrastructure handle is just another ErasedHandle), and folding it into the candidate count keeps the ambiguity arithmetic correct.

**Cons:** A long hand-written ladder with many short-circuit branches is exactly the kind of code Spring took several point releases to get right (the early-shortcut-must-match-slow-path invariant is a latent divergence bug class) — high correctness-test burden, and an outcome mismatch between the fast shortcut and the full ladder would be a silent wrong-injection.; The tie-break reads MANY heterogeneous flags off each Candidate (primary, fallback, default_candidate, priority, name, aliases, hierarchy-depth) — the Candidate borrow surface is wide, coupling this feature tightly to the exact shape bean-definition/bean-registry expose, and any new flag (e.g. a future qualifier-priority) ripples into the ladder.; Step 2 (@Value/expression suggested value) is a short-circuit that belongs to value-injection, not here — the boundary 'if the point carries a @Value, this feature never runs' must be drawn precisely or the two features fight over the same injection point; getting that hand-off wrong re-tangles them.; The @Priority layer (5d) needs the ordering infrastructure (Ordered-beats-annotation, lower-value-wins, @Priority-is-class-level-only) which is a shared mechanism with collection-injection — duplicating or mis-importing it here risks single-vs-collection ordering divergence.; Being a concrete function on the registry, it is the LEAST extensible of the three for the (rare, by-design-rejected) case of a user wanting a different tie-break — which is actually faithful to Spring (the order is fixed) but offers no escape hatch if leaf later wants one.

**Mechanisms considered:** hand-written total function `determine_winner(&InjectionPoint, &[Candidate]) -> Resolved` — the fixed contract as a literal short-circuiting guard ladder, not a strategy object; `enum Resolved { One/None/Ambiguous }` making fail-fast a return type (no silent winner-coercion); borrowed `&[Candidate<'_>]` qualifying set from candidate-resolver + SmallVec only on the >1 cold path (minimal heap); macro-captured `InjectionPoint` descriptor (required-TypeId, generic-arg TypeIds, qualifier request, declared NAME, Single|Optional arity, uses_standard_lookup) — no runtime reflection; early step-3 name/qualifier-name shortcut guarded by {standard-lookup, autowire-candidate, not-fallback, no-primary-conflict, not-self-ref}, contractually equal-outcome to the slow ladder; resolvableDependencies as a registry-owned HashMap<TypeId, ErasedHandle> consulted at lowest precedence and folded into the candidate count

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** This feature needs NO cross-crate discovery of its own — the tie-break ladder is a fixed function living entirely in leaf-core (or the context crate), so there is no DCE hazard for the algorithm itself. What it CONSUMES cross-crate is (a) the qualifying set produced by candidate-resolver over whatever discovery substrate Phase 2 picks (linkme/inventory/codegen/explicit) and (b) the per-candidate flags (primary/fallback/default/priority/name) frozen onto each MergedBeanDefinition by the thin macro at the declaration site using absolute ::leaf_core paths. The inherited cross-crate constraint is the @Priority/@Order VALUE and the qualifier metadata: both are const data the macro emits per bean, so a bean defined in a remote crate carries its own priority/primary/fallback flags into the set with no central table. Generics-as-qualifier at the type layer compares CONCRETE generic-arg TypeIds, so the type narrowing only distinguishes Store<RemoteType> from Store<i32> if both concrete instantiations were registered upstream (register_bean!(Store<RemoteType>) escape hatch) — inherited from bean-definition/candidate-resolver, this feature just reads the result. The silent-DCE risk is INDIRECT but sharp: if a contributing crate is dropped (Layer-0), its bean simply never enters the qualifying set, so this feature silently sees fewer candidates — which can flip an Ambiguous into a wrong One, or a One into a NoSuchBean. That makes the bean-registry/context anti-DCE self-check (expected-vs-found bean count, per-source provenance) a PRECONDITION for this feature's correctness, and argues the ResolveError diagnostic should be able to say 'N beans of this type were registered from crates [..]' so a missing-candidate symptom points back at a dropped crate. resolvableDependencies are registered by the context at bring-up (not cross-crate discovered), so they are DCE-immune. The two-source duality (static link-time registry + optional dynamic directory-scanned/WASM-module source) is invisible here: both just contribute Candidates to the set this function consumes (CANDIDATES, not a decision).

**T2 — execution context:** The tie-break itself is PURE, SYNCHRONOUS, CPU-only, non-blocking, allocation-light — it only compares TypeIds, reads const flags, and matches names; it never awaits, never does IO, never spawns. It runs at WIRING time: a cold startup pass for eager singletons (during the eager-instantiate phase of refresh), but WARMER for prototype/request/session-scoped beans re-resolved per acquisition — so although selection is cheap, the warm path matters and the function must stay branch-only with no per-call allocation on the common len<=1 case (charter #4 reactive-on-hot-paths: this is pure computation, never a poll/busy-wait). The qualifying set + InjectionPoint descriptors are immutable after refresh (frozen by the registry), so concurrent resolutions read them with NO lock (safe publication of immutable data — no global lock, charter #5). CRITICAL execution-context boundary: selection PICKS a winner but does not CONSTRUCT it — obtaining the actual bean handle from the chosen Candidate is the async step (a boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary, since AFIT/RPITIT are not dyn-safe) owned by bean-instantiation; this feature returns the winning Candidate (or its ErasedHandle obtainer), and the await happens in the caller. So selection is sync, construction-of-the-winner is async and may .await (DB pools) but MUST NOT block the executor (blocking factories documented to spawn_blocking — Phase 2 owns propagation). Cancellation: if the caller's construction future is cancelled after selection, nothing in this feature leaks (it held only borrows). Errors propagate as a rich ResolveError carrying the injection point + the full candidate set considered + per-layer elimination reasons (charter #9), surfaced fail-fast at eager-instantiate (boot) for singletons and at first-acquisition for lazy/scoped. No teardown concern (holds only borrows + a TypeId map).

#### R2 — Declarative ranked-layer pipeline: each tie-break layer is a data-described `SelectionLayer` producing a uniqueness verdict, executed by one generic driver (unification-first, diagnostics-native)
Question whether the tie-break should be a hand-written ladder at all. The fixed policy is a SEQUENCE of layers each answering the SAME shape of question — 'given the current surviving set, does this layer single out exactly one, narrow to a smaller set, or abstain?' — so model the contract as ORDERED DATA: a `const LAYERS: &[SelectionLayer]` where `enum LayerVerdict<'a> { Unique(&'a Candidate), Narrowed(SmallVec<&'a Candidate>), Abstain }` and each layer is a pure fn `fn(&InjectionPoint, &[&Candidate]) -> LayerVerdict`. The layers, in the FIXED order leaf commits to (mirroring determineAutowireCandidate): `[GenericNarrow, QualifierNarrow, PrimaryPromote, NameMatch, QualifierNameMatch, PriorityRank, DefaultCandidate, ResolvableDependency]` (generic/qualifier are narrowing layers that may have already run in candidate-resolver — kept here only as the single-valued safety net; primary/name/priority/default/resolvable are the selection layers). One generic DRIVER folds the set through the layers: start with the full qualifying set; for each layer, if it returns `Unique` STOP and return the winner; if `Narrowed` to exactly one STOP; if `Narrowed` to >1 continue with the smaller set; if `Abstain` continue unchanged; after all layers, if the surviving set is len 1 return it, if 0 return None, if >1 return Ambiguous. The ORDER is a fixed const array (not user-reorderable — the contract), but each layer is an isolated, independently-testable pure fn, and the driver is the single place the fail-fast 'still >1 after all layers = Ambiguous' rule lives. The early step-3 shortcut is itself just a `pre_layer` (a fast NameMatch+guards run first, contractually identical to running NameMatch inside the ladder). Crucially, the driver RECORDS a trace: a `SmallVec<(LayerName, before_count, after_count)>` so the resulting diagnostic can say 'type matched 3, generics narrowed to 2, no primary, name matched 0, priority tied 2 -> AMBIGUOUS' — the candidates-considered chain is a byproduct of the fold, not bolted on. determinePrimaryCandidate's two-pass (unique primary, else unique non-fallback) is one PrimaryPromote layer internally doing both passes.

**Pros:** Maximal unification (charter #11): every tie-break layer answers ONE verdict shape and runs through ONE driver, so primary/name/priority/default/resolvable are uniform data not bespoke branches — and the SAME driver could later serve a different fixed policy if leaf ever needed one, without rewriting the arbitration core.; Best diagnostics of the three (charter #9): the layer-by-layer trace (before/after counts per layer) IS the candidates-considered narrative for free — the NoUniqueBean error can explain EXACTLY which layer failed to disambiguate and what survived, far richer than R1's after-the-fact reconstruction.; Each layer is an isolated pure fn with a tiny contract (set in, verdict out) — independently unit-testable against Spring's documented behavior, so the high-risk correctness surface (the part R1 bundles into one long function) is decomposed into small verifiable pieces; the early-shortcut-equals-slow-path invariant becomes 'pre_layer NameMatch == in-ladder NameMatch', a checkable property.; The fixed ORDER is explicit, auditable const data (`LAYERS = [..]`) — the container contract is literally readable as a list, making 'selection order is fixed, filtering is pluggable' visually obvious and impossible to accidentally reorder at a call site.; Fail-fast lives in exactly ONE place (the driver's terminal 'len>1 => Ambiguous'), so the no-guess guarantee cannot be partially forgotten in one branch the way a hand-written ladder risks.; Layers are pure data-described fns with no dyn dispatch on the order (the array is const, fns are static) — keeps the warm-path cost near R1's while gaining the trace; the SmallVec working-set fold is the only allocation, on the >1 cold path.

**Cons:** More machinery than R1 for what IS a fixed sequence — a generic driver + verdict enum + per-layer fns is more code and indirection than a straight-line function, and risks looking like a pluggable strategy engine when the whole point is that the order is NOT pluggable (must be carefully documented as 'fixed contract, decomposed for testability/diagnostics', not an extension seam).; The Narrowed-vs-Abstain distinction per layer is subtle: a layer that finds zero matches must Abstain (fall through, narrow nothing) vs one that finds some-but-not-one must Narrow — getting this wrong silently changes fall-through semantics (the same abstain!=veto hazard candidate-resolver has, now in the selection layers), needing a precise per-layer spec.; Carrying a trace SmallVec on every resolution (even successful len-1 cases) is a small overhead the warm prototype/scoped path pays for diagnostics it usually won't need — must be gated (only record on the >1 path, or behind a diagnostic flag) to honor low-overhead #2, adding a branch.; The fold-through-narrowing-sets model can mask an ordering subtlety Spring has: some layers (primary) PROMOTE one regardless of count while others (priority) RANK — expressing 'promote exactly one or abstain' vs 'rank and keep ties' uniformly in LayerVerdict is possible but the uniformity is slightly forced, and primary's two-pass (primary then non-fallback) is a layer that internally does two things, denting the 'one layer one concern' cleanliness.; Slightly further from the practitioner mental model of determineAutowireCandidate as a single method — the decomposition is a translation cost for someone cross-referencing Spring source, though the explicit LAYERS array arguably reads BETTER than the nested if-ladder.

**Mechanisms considered:** `const LAYERS: &[SelectionLayer]` fixed-order array of pure-fn layers + a single generic fold DRIVER (order fixed = the contract, layers decomposed for test/diagnostics); `enum LayerVerdict { Unique | Narrowed(SmallVec) | Abstain }` with driver rule 'first Unique or narrow-to-one wins; after all layers len>1 => Ambiguous' (fail-fast in one place); per-resolution trace `SmallVec<(LayerName, before, after)>` feeding the candidates-considered diagnostic as a byproduct of the fold; early step-3 shortcut as a `pre_layer` NameMatch+guards, contractually identical to the in-ladder NameMatch; PrimaryPromote layer doing determinePrimaryCandidate's two-pass (unique primary, else unique non-fallback) internally; ResolvableDependency as the terminal lowest-precedence layer; trace gated to the >1 path / diagnostic flag to keep the warm-path allocation-free

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Same as R1 on the core point: the layer driver and the const LAYERS array live entirely in leaf-core, are const/static data + pure fns, and have NO cross-crate discovery surface or DCE hazard of their own. The layers READ the same macro-captured, declaration-site-frozen flags (primary/fallback/default/priority/qualifier/name) off Candidates contributed by candidate-resolver over Phase-2's chosen substrate. A subtle T1 ADVANTAGE: because the order is a single explicit const array in leaf-core, there is zero risk of a downstream crate contributing a 'selection layer' (the contract forbids it) — the only cross-crate extensibility is in FILTERING (candidate-resolver), so this feature's surface stays a closed, stable kernel contract, which is exactly the 'small stable kernel' topology (charter §6). The diagnostic trace can be made to carry per-candidate source provenance (which crate registered each surviving candidate), so a NoUniqueBean across module boundaries names the offending crates — directly useful for the cross-module @Fallback/auto-config-override story (a library ships a @Fallback default, the app's plain bean wins; if BOTH are non-fallback the trace shows both crates). Generic/qualifier safety-net layers compare CONCRETE TypeIds (generics-cannot-register inherited). resolvableDependencies registered by the context, DCE-immune. The two-source duality is invisible (both feed the same set). The silent-missing-candidate hazard (a dropped crate shrinks the set, flipping outcomes) is identical to R1 and again makes the upstream anti-DCE self-check a precondition — but R2's trace makes the SYMPTOM more legible ('expected 3 candidates of type T, set contained 2').

**T2 — execution context:** Identical execution-context profile to R1 with one addition: the fold + verdict computation is pure, synchronous, CPU-only, non-blocking, runs at wiring time (cold for eager singletons, warm for prototype/scoped re-resolution), over immutable-after-refresh data read lock-free (no global lock). The ONE extra cost vs R1 is the trace SmallVec — kept off the hot path by only allocating/recording it when entering the >1 ladder (the len<=1 fast path returns before any trace), so the common warm case stays allocation-free and branch-only (charter #4). The layers themselves MUST be documented as pure/sync/non-blocking (they only inspect metadata + compare TypeIds/names) — the driver contract forbids a layer that awaits or blocks, exactly like candidate-resolver's filter purity rule. Winner CONSTRUCTION remains the async step owned by bean-instantiation (boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary); selection returns the chosen Candidate and the caller awaits the build — selection sync, build async-may-await-never-block (spawn_blocking for blocking factories; Phase 2 owns propagation). Cancellation after selection leaks nothing (borrows + a const array). Errors are the rich ResolveError, now CARRYING the layer trace, surfaced fail-fast at eager-instantiate / first-acquisition (charter #9). No teardown concern.

#### R3 — Typed disambiguation at the injection-site type: lift @Primary/@Qualifier/name into the resolution KEY so single-valued ambiguity is largely resolved by the type system, with a thin runtime tie-break only for genuinely runtime-decided cases (compile-time fail-fast, DX-maximal)
Push intent-not-mechanism hardest. Spring needs a runtime tie-break ladder because the injected-into type (`DataSource ds`) is ambiguous and the disambiguator (@Qualifier value, @Primary status, field name) is only readable reflectively at runtime. In leaf, the COMMON disambiguation can be lifted into the lookup KEY the compiler resolves, turning fail-fast from a startup exception into (where legible — charter #9) a COMPILE error. A qualified single injection becomes a typed key: `Qualified<DataSource, Main>` (or `#[autowired(qualifier = Main)] ds: DataSource` where `Main` is a zero-sized marker) so 'which bean qualifies AND wins' is one typed registry probe keyed by (produced-TypeId, qualifier-marker-TypeId) — no candidate set, no tie-break, the winner is unique BY KEY or it is a missing-key error. Generics-as-qualifier falls out for free (Store<i32> and Store<String> are distinct TypeIds = distinct keys, so the 'narrow by generic' layer is the type system). @Primary becomes a declaration-site assertion: a bean marked `#[component(primary)]` registers under BOTH its plain-type key and a `Primary<T>` key, so an UNqualified `DataSource ds` probes `Primary<DataSource>` first, then plain `DataSource` only if unique — a unique-primary or unique-plain hit is a key probe, NOT a runtime scan. The bean-name implicit-qualifier tie-break and @Fallback and @Priority and resolvableDependencies — the genuinely RUNTIME-decided, count-dependent layers — fall back to a THIN runtime arbiter (essentially R1's ladder but reached only when the typed key probe is non-unique): so the typed path handles type+generic+explicit-qualifier+primary at compile/probe time (the majority), and the runtime ladder handles name-match/fallback/priority/default/resolvable for the residual ambiguous set. Fail-fast splits across timing (charter #9): a typo'd qualifier marker or an unregistered (type,qualifier) pair fails at COMPILE time with a legible error (richest timing); a genuinely ambiguous unqualified point with no primary fails at the startup validation pass / eager-instantiate with the NoUniqueBean + candidate set (the deferred-but-richer timing). This is the most DX-forward and overhead-minimal on the common path but most divergent from Spring's uniform-runtime-ladder model.

**Pros:** Best DX (charter #1) and refactor-safety on the common case: a qualifier is a real type, so a wrong/typo'd qualifier or an unsatisfiable qualified injection is a COMPILE error (the richest-and-earliest diagnostic timing, charter #9 ideal) — fail-fast moves from startup exception to compile error where the compile error is legible, and rename-refactoring follows the type.; Lowest overhead on the dominant path (charter #2): the type+generic+qualifier+primary disambiguation is a `HashMap<(TypeId,TypeId), Handle>` probe — no candidate set materialization, no ladder fold, no allocation — so the WARM prototype/request-scoped re-resolution path (the one that matters per charter #4) is a single keyed lookup for the majority of injection points.; Generics-as-implicit-qualifier needs ZERO special machinery — distinct concrete types are distinct TypeIds = distinct keys — which is precisely Spring's stated design intent ('the type already encodes the developer's intent; a redundant @Qualifier would duplicate it') realized natively, not emulated by a runtime ResolvableType comparison.; @Primary-as-a-secondary-key (`Primary<T>` probed before plain `T`) turns the most common single-valued disambiguation into a key probe rather than a two-pass scan — and is origin-agnostic (any source, native or WASM-module, registers under the Primary<T> key the same way).; Keeps a faithful runtime tie-break ladder (R1/R2) for the genuinely runtime/count-dependent layers (name-match, @Fallback cross-module override, @Priority, default-candidate, resolvableDependency), so nothing in the fixed contract is LOST — it is just reached only when typed keys cannot decide, preserving full Spring parity for the residual cases.

**Cons:** Biggest divergence from Spring's model and from R1/R2: the disambiguation is split across compile-time (typed keys) and runtime (residual ladder), so the SAME conceptual 'autowiring resolution' has two code paths with two failure timings — a real cognitive + implementation cost, and the boundary (what the key decides vs what the ladder decides) must be drawn with surgical precision or @Primary/@Fallback/name interact inconsistently between the two paths.; @Fallback is fundamentally count-and-cross-module-dependent ('chosen only if no non-fallback exists') and CANNOT be a static key (a library's @Fallback bean and the app's plain bean live in different crates compiled separately) — so the headline cross-module-override use case is forced onto the runtime ladder anyway, meaning the typed path does NOT cover @Fallback, the very feature whose intent is most about module composition.; String/dynamic qualifiers (@Qualifier value chosen from config, or a name-as-qualifier tie-break) do not fit a TypeId key — the bean NAME as implicit qualifier (used at BOTH step-3 and step-5) is a string, so the name layers MUST live in the runtime ladder, splitting the 'name is an implicit qualifier' intent awkwardly across a typed primary path and a stringly runtime path.; Marker-type qualifiers force the orphan-rule/shared-crate constraint: a `Main` marker used by a bean in crate A and an injection point in crate B must live in a crate both depend on (leaf-core or a shared crate) — less flexible than Spring's open string qualifiers, and a topology constraint (dependencies-point-inward) the other two realizations avoid entirely.; Two-pass key probing (`Primary<T>` then `T`) plus a residual runtime ladder is arguably MORE total machinery than R1's single ladder for the full feature, not less — the overhead win is only on the common path; the tail (fallback/name/priority) carries both a key-miss AND a ladder run, and the dual-path correctness surface is larger.; Custom-qualifier ATTRIBUTE sets (match-ALL of genre=.., format=..) map awkwardly onto type keys (combinatorial markers / const generics), so attribute-rich qualifiers also spill to the runtime path — the typed path cleanly covers only single-marker qualifiers + primary + generics.

**Mechanisms considered:** zero-sized marker-type qualifiers + typed key (produced-TypeId, qualifier-marker-TypeId) probe — qualifier-and-winner in one compiler-resolved lookup; @Primary as a secondary `Primary<T>` registry key probed before plain `T` (unique-primary = key hit, not a scan); generics-as-qualifier free via distinct concrete TypeIds (the narrow-by-generic layer = the type system); residual thin runtime tie-break ladder (R1-shaped) reached ONLY on non-unique key probe — handles name-match, @Fallback, @Priority, default-candidate, resolvableDependency (the count/string/cross-module-dependent layers); compile-time fail for typo'd/unsatisfiable qualified keys (richest timing); startup-validation/eager-instantiate fail for residual runtime ambiguity (deferred-but-rich timing); string/name/attribute qualifiers explicitly routed to the runtime ladder (cannot be type keys)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Split cross-crate story. The TYPED-KEY path needs no per-candidate runtime discovery — a bean indexes itself under its (type, qualifier-marker) and (Primary<T>) keys wherever registered, and the qualified probe is compiler-resolved at the injection site, giving COMPILE-TIME qualifier-typo detection and NO DCE exposure for the keyed path (a missing key is a compile/probe error, the richest timing). The hard cross-crate constraint is the SHARED MARKER TYPE: a qualifier marker used by a bean in one crate and an injection point in another must live in a crate both depend on (leaf-core for built-ins, or a shared user crate) — a Rust coherence/topology constraint (dependencies-point-inward), not a discovery problem, but a real divergence from Spring's open string qualifiers. The RESIDUAL RUNTIME-LADDER path inherits R1's full cross-crate story: it consumes the qualifying set over Phase-2's substrate and is exposed to the silent-missing-candidate hazard (a dropped crate shrinks the set), so the anti-DCE self-check is a precondition for the runtime path specifically. CRITICAL @Fallback point: because @Fallback is the cross-MODULE-override mechanism (library default vs app bean in different crates) and cannot be a static key, the headline cross-crate use case lands ENTIRELY on the runtime ladder — so R3 does not simplify the most cross-crate-relevant part of the feature. The two-source duality (static link-time + dynamic WASM-module): a WASM-module bean could register under typed keys IF it can name the marker TypeIds (shared crate), else it falls to the runtime path — flagged as a tension, CANDIDATE not decision. Generics-cannot-register still bounds the typed path (open Store<T> has no key; register the concrete instantiation).

**T2 — execution context:** Two execution profiles. TYPED-KEY path: the qualified/primary probe is synchronous, allocation-free, a single (or double, for Primary<T> then T) HashMap lookup — the cheapest possible warm-path resolution (charter #2/#4), no candidate set, no fold, safe inline on any executor, over an immutable-after-refresh index read lock-free (no global lock). RESIDUAL-LADDER path: identical to R1's profile — pure synchronous CPU-only tie-break over a borrowed candidate set, reached only when the key probe is non-unique. In BOTH paths, selection is sync and winner CONSTRUCTION is the async step owned by bean-instantiation (boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary, AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe) — the typed probe returns a Handle obtainer, the ladder returns a Candidate, and the caller awaits the build (may .await for DB pools, must not block; spawn_blocking for blocking factories — Phase 2 owns propagation). Fail-fast TIMING differs by path and is the charter-#9 nuance: the typed path fails at COMPILE time (typo'd/unsatisfiable qualified key — richest, earliest, legible) while the runtime path fails at the startup-validation pass / eager-instantiate (NoUniqueBean + candidate set + per-layer reasons — deferred but richer for genuinely runtime ambiguity) — leaf must keep these consistent so a developer is not surprised that some ambiguities are compile errors and others are startup errors. Cancellation after selection leaks nothing in either path. No teardown concern. The dual-path means the execution-context discipline must be documented twice (keyed vs ladder), a real cost vs R1/R2's single profile.

### Rust clashes
- Runtime reflection of the disambiguators vs declaration-site capture: Spring's doResolveDependency reads @Qualifier values, @Primary/@Fallback status, @Priority/@Order, required-ness (required=false/Optional/@Nullable), and the field/param NAME reflectively off live beans and the InjectionPoint at resolution time. Rust has no runtime annotation reflection — EVERY input the fixed ladder reads must be captured at the @Autowired declaration site by the thin macro into an InjectionPoint descriptor (requested qualifier, generic-arg TypeIds, declared name for the name tie-break, Single|Optional arity) and frozen onto each candidate's MergedBeanDefinition (primary/fallback/default_candidate/priority). The dynamism of reading arbitrary metadata at runtime is gone; the disambiguation inputs are whatever the macro captured at compile time.
- The bean NAME as implicit qualifier is a string-keyed tie-break with no clean type analogue: Spring uses the field/parameter identifier (a string) matched against bean names/aliases at BOTH the early step-3 shortcut AND the late step-5 tie-break — and 'renaming a field silently changes which bean injects' is a real, intended coupling. In Rust the declared name must be captured by the macro as a string literal (param/field idents are available at the macro site), so the name tie-break is a string comparison against captured bean names — fine for R1/R2, but it does NOT lift into a TypeId key (R3), forcing the name layers onto a runtime/stringly path and splitting the implicit-qualifier intent. There is no way to make field-name-as-qualifier a compile-time-checked type relationship.
- Three-way resolution outcome (zero/one/many) and optional-demotion vs panics-or-nulls: Spring throws NoSuchBean (zero) or NoUniqueBean (many) unless required=false/Optional<T>/@Nullable demotes zero-to-absent. Rust's win is to make this an explicit enum (Resolved::None|One|Ambiguous) and map None->Err(NoSuchBean) only when arity==Single, None->Ok(absent) when arity==Optional — the fail-fast 'refuse to guess' becomes a type-enforced non-coercible Ambiguous variant rather than a thrown exception, which is strictly better, but it forces every call site to handle three outcomes explicitly and the Optional arity (Option<T>) must be captured at the injection point and threaded through, not inferred at runtime from a @Nullable.
- ResolvableType generic matching vs concrete monomorphized TypeIds: Spring's 'narrow by generic type' layer compares parameterized types via runtime ResolvableType (Store<Integer> isAssignableFrom check). Rust cannot register an open generic and has no runtime ResolvableType — generics-as-implicit-qualifier must compare CONCRETE generic-arg TypeId tuples captured per monomorphized instantiation, so the generic-narrowing layer only distinguishes Store<i32> from Store<String> because both concrete instantiations were separately registered (register_bean!(Concrete) escape hatch). The implicit-qualifier INTENT survives; the open-generic mechanism does not (inherited from bean-definition/candidate-resolver).
- The early step-3 fast-path-must-equal-slow-path invariant is a silent-divergence hazard with no JVM equivalent: Spring's pre-findAutowireCandidates name/qualifier-name shortcut is an OPTIMIZATION guarded by usesStandardBeanLookup + {type-match, isAutowireCandidate, not-fallback, no-primary-conflict, not-self-ref} that MUST return the identical winner the full ladder would. In Rust this is two code paths that must provably agree; a guard that is subtly too loose makes the shortcut pick a bean the ladder would have rejected — a silent wrong-injection. This is exactly the class of bug Spring took point releases to stabilize and is a high correctness-test burden leaf inherits (mitigated in R2 by making the shortcut a pre_layer contractually identical to the in-ladder NameMatch).
- No runtime isAssignableFrom / 'injectable as any supertype' has no automatic analogue: Spring matches a bean to a single-valued injection point by ALL its assignable types (a DataSourceImpl satisfies a DataSource ds). Rust has no runtime supertype walk — each injectable dyn-Trait view a bean satisfies must be explicitly registered as a type-index entry the macro emits per declared trait (trait-upcasting, stable 1.86, recovers a supertrait object from a stored one but does NOT enumerate which traits a type implements). So the candidate SET this feature receives is bounded by which trait views were registered upstream; selection cannot conjure a candidate for an unregistered supertrait view (inherited from bean-registry/candidate-resolver).
- @Priority/@Order ordering semantics must be re-derived as a shared, non-duplicated mechanism: Spring's tie-break layer 5d uses AnnotationAwareOrderComparator (lower value = higher precedence; Ordered/PriorityOrdered INTERFACE beats the @Order/@Priority ANNOTATION; @Priority is class-level-only so it cannot sit on factory-method beans, modeled with @Order+@Primary/@Fallback instead). leaf must capture priority/order as const data on the definition and apply the SAME comparator collection-injection uses for ordering — duplicating it risks single-valued vs collection ordering divergence. There is no Rust analogue of the interface-beats-annotation precedence unless an Ordered-equivalent trait-implemented signal is captured distinctly from a declared @Order value.
- resolvableDependencies (infrastructure handles injectable by-type but not real bean definitions) must be an origin-agnostic erased handle, not a JVM-aliased object: Spring keeps ApplicationContext/BeanFactory/EventPublisher in a resolvableDependencies map matched at lowest precedence. In Rust these are the container's own handles, which must be stored as the SAME origin-agnostic ErasedHandle the singleton cache uses (so they publish/compare identically) and folded into the candidate count so the ambiguity arithmetic is correct — but they raise the scope/ownership keystone (Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync> vs borrow vs arena handle) that this feature must NOT prejudge, keeping the handle abstract.
- No exception-chain wrapping; the rich error must be a constructed value carrying the assembly chain: Spring nests BeanCreationException -> UnsatisfiedDependencyException(InjectionPoint) -> NoSuchBean/NoUniqueBean as thrown/wrapped exceptions. Rust has no exceptions — the ResolveError must be an explicit value carrying the injection point, the full candidate set considered, and per-layer elimination reasons, propagated via Result and re-wrapped by the caller (bean-instantiation) into the dependent's assembly-chain error. Building that causal chain by hand (rather than getting stack-unwinding wrapping for free) is the cost of honoring charter #9 richly.

### Interactions
- **candidate-resolver** — THE primary upstream seam. candidate-resolver does phase-1 FILTERING (autowire-candidate flag, generics-as-implicit-qualifier, @Qualifier/custom-qualifier-attribute matching, the three-valued match/veto/abstain) and produces the QUALIFYING SET; THIS feature consumes that set and does phase-2 SELECTION (the fixed tie-break). Strict producer/consumer split: candidate-resolver answers 'what qualifies', autowiring-resolution answers 'which one wins'. This feature NEVER filters by qualifier/generic again except as a single-valued safety net (R2's GenericNarrow/QualifierNarrow layers); candidate-resolver NEVER selects. The set must arrive with per-candidate veto/why-not data so this feature's NoUniqueBean/NoSuchBean carries the candidates-considered chain.
- **primary-fallback** — primary-fallback is the FIRST tie-break layer this feature applies (determinePrimaryCandidate's two-pass: unique @Primary — two local primaries = NoUniqueBean 'more than one primary', local beats parent — else unique non-@Fallback). It is logically a sub-policy of THIS feature's fixed ladder: primary-fallback defines the promote-one/demote-defaults semantics and the cross-module-default-supersession use case; autowiring-resolution EXECUTES it as ladder step 5a over the qualifying set, for SINGLE-valued points only (never collections). The hierarchy-depth needed for 'local primary beats parent primary' is read off the Candidate (from container-hierarchy).
- **bean-registry** — The registry is WHERE this feature runs and what it reads from. bean-registry produces the candidate set (candidates_for_type over its type index, filtered through candidate-resolver), OWNS the resolvableDependencies map this feature consults at lowest precedence, and the determine_winner ladder is a method ON the engine/context (R1/R2) consuming the registry's frozen definitions and the borrowed candidate set. The registry's freeze-after-refresh boundary is what makes the candidate set + injection-point descriptors immutable and lock-free-readable during concurrent resolution.
- **bean-definition** — Pure consumer of the metamodel: every flag the tie-break ladder reads — primary, fallback, default_candidate, priority/order, name+aliases — is frozen on the MergedBeanDefinition by bean-definition. The Candidate borrow this feature operates over is a read-only view of that frozen definition. This feature adds no metadata of its own; it reads what bean-definition captured at the declaration site and merged through the parent chain.
- **injection-styles** — Defines the injection POINTS this feature resolves: constructor (preferred/mandatory), setter (optional), field. injection-styles fixes the InjectionPoint descriptor shape (required produced-TypeId, generic-arg TypeIds, declared param/field NAME for the name tie-break, Single|Optional arity) that the macro captures and hands to this feature. Constructor-arg points are mandatory (Single arity by default); setter/field points may be Optional — the arity that decides None->NoSuchBean vs None->absent comes from here.
- **deferral-primitives** — ObjectProvider/Provider/Optional<T> change the ARITY and timing this feature sees: Optional<T>/required=false demote a zero-candidate result from NoSuchBean to absent (Resolved::None mapped to Ok(absent)); getIfUnique() depends on this feature's interaction with the default_candidate layer (resolves cleanly when every other same-type bean is non-default — the default-candidate tie-break (5e) is what makes that uniqueness check succeed). deferral-primitives owns the 0..1/0..N/lazy access shapes; this feature owns the single-valued selection underneath getObject()/getIfUnique().
- **collection-injection** — The deliberate NON-interaction is load-bearing: @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate selection and the whole tie-break ladder apply ONLY to single-valued points — a List/Set/array/Map injection takes ALL qualifying candidates and BYPASSES this feature's selection entirely. The shared piece is the @Priority/@Order comparator: collection-injection uses it for ORDERING the full set, this feature uses it as tie-break layer 5d for picking ONE — the comparator must be a single shared mechanism so single-valued and collection ordering never diverge. collection-injection also owns the name-keyed Map (keys = bean names) which reuses the same name data this feature uses for the name tie-break.
- **bean-naming** — Supplies the names + aliases that drive the bean-name-as-implicit-qualifier behavior used in BOTH the early step-3 shortcut and the late step-5 tie-break. bean-naming derives deterministic names and the alias set; this feature matches the captured injection-point declared name against those names/aliases (covering aliases via the matchesName analogue) as the last-resort disambiguator before NoUniqueBean. The 'renaming a field silently changes injection' coupling is the consequence of this interaction.
- **container-hierarchy** — Resolution is hierarchy-aware: a local miss delegates the candidate find upward to the parent, local definitions shadow same-named parent definitions, and 'a local @Primary beats a parent-context primary' is a tie-break nuance this feature honors via the hierarchy depth carried on each Candidate. container-hierarchy defines the one-directional visibility and local-shadows-parent rule; this feature executes the depth-aware primary tie-break and consumes the hierarchy-merged candidate set.
- **value-injection** — Boundary/short-circuit: Spring's step-2 is a @Value/expression suggested-value that PRE-EMPTS bean candidate resolution. The clean split is: if an injection point carries a @Value/#{expression}, value-injection (with type-conversion) resolves it and THIS feature never runs for that point; this feature only handles by-type/by-qualifier BEAN injection points. Drawing this hand-off precisely keeps the two features from contending over the same point.
- **container-core** — The context-services facade is what makes this feature's RICH behavior available: a bare bean-registry with SimpleAutowireCandidateResolver loses qualifier/generic FILTERING (candidate-resolver), so the qualifying set this feature receives is impoverished — the tie-break ladder still runs but over a set that never narrowed by qualifier/generic. container-core decides the bare-vs-rich resolver install; this feature's selection contract is FIXED regardless (selection order is not pluggable), but its INPUT quality depends on what container-core installed upstream.
- **bean-instantiation** — Downstream consumer of the selection result and owner of the async boundary. This feature SELECTS the winning Candidate (sync, pure); bean-instantiation CONSTRUCTS it (async, boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary, may .await, must not block). The handoff: selection returns the chosen Candidate / ErasedHandle obtainer, bean-instantiation awaits the build and publishes into the singleton cache. bean-instantiation also re-wraps this feature's ResolveError into the dependent bean's assembly-chain error (the UnsatisfiedDependency analogue).
- **condition-family** — @ConditionalOnSingleCandidate means 'autowire would resolve unambiguously' — which is precisely a query against THIS feature's selection outcome (it matches when a unique winner exists by type, including via @Primary among several or default-candidate filtering — NOT merely 'exactly one bean'). So condition-family's OnSingleCandidate predicate must call into or mirror this feature's determine_winner to know whether resolution would succeed, making this feature's outcome a reusable predicate for conditional registration.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Fixed-ladder-as-function (R1) vs decomposed-data-layers-with-trace (R2) vs typed-key-disambiguation-with-residual-ladder (R3) — or a blend (R3 typed keys for type+generic+explicit-qualifier+primary as the fast/compile-time-checked default, R2's traced ladder for the genuinely runtime/count-dependent residual of name/@Fallback/@Priority/default/resolvable)? This is the keystone shape decision for selection and is entangled with the compile-vs-runtime split (§3) and the type-erasure/registry-substrate (how dyn beans are keyed) meta-concepts.
- Fail-fast TIMING for ambiguity (charter #9): does single-valued ambiguity surface at COMPILE time (R3's typed-key path, where the compile error is legible), at a dedicated STARTUP VALIDATION PASS (resolve-the-whole-graph-before-instantiation, richest runtime context), or at EAGER-INSTANTIATE / first-acquisition (Spring's actual timing)? And can the timing be CONSISTENT across single vs collection vs scoped points, or is a split (some ambiguities compile errors, others startup errors) acceptable? Shares the unified-diagnostics meta-concept and the compile-vs-runtime split.
- String/open qualifiers + bean-name-as-implicit-qualifier vs typed-marker qualifiers (the open-vs-typed tension, shared with candidate-resolver): the name tie-break (steps 3 AND 5) is fundamentally a STRING match and the field-name coupling is intended — does leaf keep open string qualifiers + name-fallback (Spring-faithful, supports the name tie-break and dynamic qualifiers, no compile-time check) or typed-marker qualifiers (compile-safe, free generic-qualification, but forces name/@Fallback onto a stringly runtime path)? Cross-cuts bean-naming, candidate-resolver, annotation-model, DX #1 vs overhead #2.
- The early step-3 fast-path-equals-slow-path invariant: is the name/qualifier-name shortcut worth keeping at all in leaf (it is a JVM-era optimization to avoid an expensive findAutowireCandidates), given leaf's candidate find may be cheap (a type-index probe)? If kept, how is the fast-path == slow-path equivalence GUARANTEED (a property test, or R2's pre_layer-identical-to-in-ladder-layer construction)? Dropping it simplifies correctness; keeping it matches Spring. Couples to whether selection runs over a materialized set or a typed probe.
- The @Priority/@Order comparator as a single SHARED mechanism vs duplicated: this feature's tie-break layer 5d and collection-injection's ordering both need lower-value-wins + Ordered-interface-beats-annotation + @Priority-class-level-only semantics — where does the one canonical comparator live, and how is the 'interface signal beats annotation value' precedence captured in Rust (a distinct Ordered-trait-implemented flag vs a declared @Order const)? Shares the unification meta-concept and couples to collection-injection.
- resolvableDependencies handle type + the scope/ownership keystone: infrastructure handles (the container, event publisher) are injectable by-type at lowest precedence — must they be stored as the SAME origin-agnostic ErasedHandle as cached singletons (Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync>? arena handle? borrow?), and how does that reconcile with the deferred scope/lifetime/ownership model (§3)? This feature must consult and return such a handle WITHOUT prejudging the handle type, and the get<T> return type for an injected infrastructure handle depends on it.
- The Resolved::None -> NoSuchBean-vs-absent mapping and where Optional arity is decided: is required-ness/Optional captured purely at the injection-point descriptor (macro-emitted Single|Optional) and threaded as data, or is there a runtime nullability signal? And does the optional demotion live in THIS feature (return None and let arity decide) or in deferral-primitives (which owns 0..1)? Couples to injection-styles, deferral-primitives, and the unified-error model.
- Per-resolution diagnostic trace cost vs richness (charter #9 vs #2): R2 records a layer-by-layer narrowing trace for free-on-the-cold-path diagnostics — is the candidates-considered chain always materialized (warm-path cost on prototype/scoped re-resolution) or only on the ambiguous/error path / behind a diagnostic flag? And how much per-candidate source provenance (which crate registered it) does the trace carry to make cross-module NoUniqueBean legible? Shares the unified-diagnostics meta-concept and the cross-crate composition model.
- The qualifying-set ownership/shape handed across the candidate-resolver -> autowiring-resolution seam (couples to the type-erasure/registry-substrate and scope/ownership keystones): is it a borrowed &[Candidate], an Arc'd slice, a bitset over the registry's bean-index space, or an arena-backed SmallVec — and does selection allocate per resolution (a warm-path cost for prototype/request-scoped points)? This cannot be settled in isolation from how candidate-resolver materializes the set and how the registry indexes beans.
- Cross-crate @Fallback timing vs the freeze/compile-vs-runtime boundary: @Fallback is the cross-MODULE override mechanism (a library default in one crate transparently superseded by an app bean in another) and is inherently count-and-cross-module-dependent, so it CANNOT be a compile-time typed-key decision (R3) and must run on the runtime ladder over the fully-collected set. How does this reconcile with any freeze-early / AOT-lineage shape (a frozen snapshot must already contain all candidates from all crates, including a dynamic WASM-module source, before @Fallback can be correctly evaluated)? Couples to bean-registry's freeze model, auto-configuration's back-off, and the cross-crate two-source duality.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the dedicated autowiring-resolution-algorithm section (lines 680-705: the six-step layered policy, the doResolveDependency v7.0.5 pipeline, the determineAutowireCandidate tie-break ORDER, the fail-fast-on-ambiguity soul, the bean-name-as-implicit-qualifier hidden concept used at BOTH the early step-3 shortcut and the late step-5 tie-break with its full guard set, and resolvableDependencies at lowest precedence), the @Primary/@Fallback section (707-722: determinePrimaryCandidate two-pass, local-beats-parent, two-primaries=NoUnique, the @Fallback cross-module-override killer use case, and the crucial caveat that primary/fallback NEVER collapse collections), the qualifiers/generics/implicit-qualifier section (724-734), and the candidate-resolution-engine section (2595-2628: the explicit two-PHASE filtering-vs-selection split where SELECTION ORDER IS A FIXED CONTRACT and only filtering is pluggable, the bare-SimpleAutowireCandidateResolver fact, the autowire_candidate-vs-default_candidate distinction, and that @Primary/@Fallback affect only single points). I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc's Layer-0/A/B DCE silent-failure + the self-check mandate + generics-cannot-register + thin-macro/absolute-path ABI, and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimal-Arc, §2.9 rich-early-diagnostics) and the stable envelope (RPITIT/AFIT NOT dyn-safe -> boxed futures at the dyn construction boundary, trait-upcasting 1.86, const TypeId 1.91, Arc<dyn Any>, no async Drop).

THE most important framing for THIS feature, and the one I held throughout: it is the SELECTION + ARBITRATION phase (which single qualifier WINS, and the zero/one/many fail-fast arbitration), explicitly DISTINCT from candidate-resolver (FILTERING — what qualifies) and from primary-fallback (which is the FIRST tie-break LAYER this feature executes, not a separate engine). The selection ORDER is a FIXED container contract — NOT pluggable — which is the single sharpest design constraint: unlike candidate-resolver (whose whole point is a pluggable filter chain), this feature must NOT offer a strategy seam to reorder the tie-break. All three realizations honor that (R1 a fixed function, R2 a fixed const LAYERS array decomposed only for testability/diagnostics, R3 typed keys + a fixed residual ladder), and I made the fail-fast 'refuse to guess' a type-enforced non-coercible Ambiguous outcome in all three rather than a thrown exception.

The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters HERE — the SHAPE of the fixed tie-break and WHEN/WHERE it executes: R1 = a hand-written total function literally mirroring determineAutowireCandidate (highest fidelity, most teachable, but the early-shortcut-equals-slow-path invariant is a high correctness-test burden and the function couples tightly to many candidate flags); R2 = a declarative const-ordered pipeline of pure-fn layers run by one generic fold driver that records a narrowing trace (best diagnostics — the candidates-considered chain is a byproduct of the fold — and best unification/testability, at the cost of more machinery and a trace-overhead branch to keep off the warm path); R3 = lift type+generic+explicit-qualifier+@Primary into the lookup KEY so the common case is a compiler-resolved/keyed probe that fail-fasts at COMPILE time (best DX + lowest warm-path overhead + free generics-as-qualifier), with a thin RESIDUAL runtime ladder for the genuinely runtime/count/string/cross-module-dependent layers (name-match, @Fallback, @Priority, default-candidate, resolvableDependency) — most divergent from Spring's uniform-runtime model, splits fail-fast timing across compile-and-runtime, and notably does NOT simplify @Fallback (the most cross-crate-relevant layer, which cannot be a static key). R1 and R2 differ on monolithic-function vs decomposed-data-layers; R3 differs on runtime-ladder vs compile-time-key and is partially orthogonal (a likely Phase-2 blend is R3-keyed-default + R2-traced-residual-ladder).

Three things to carry forward: (1) SELECTION ORDER IS FIXED, filtering is pluggable — this is the load-bearing contract that separates this feature from candidate-resolver, and I refused to make the tie-break a strategy chain in any realization. (2) The bean NAME is an implicit qualifier used in TWO places (the early step-3 shortcut AND the late step-5 tie-break) and is fundamentally a STRING with no clean TypeId-key analogue — it is the part that resists R3's typed-key approach and is flagged as the open-vs-typed tension shared with candidate-resolver. (3) @Fallback is the cross-MODULE override mechanism (library default in crate A transparently superseded by app bean in crate B), inherently count-and-cross-module-dependent, so it cannot be a compile-time decision and forces the cross-crate-relevant part of the feature onto the runtime ladder over the fully-collected set — a real tension with any freeze-early/AOT-lineage shape, flagged as a phase2Question.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization keeps the bean handle / resolvableDependency handle an abstract origin-agnostic ErasedHandle (the scope/ownership keystone), keeps winner CONSTRUCTION an async boxed-future step owned by bean-instantiation (this feature does only sync selection), keeps the qualifying-set ownership/shape abstract (borrow vs Arc-slice vs bitset — a phase2Question), and stays substrate-neutral on how candidates were discovered (the two-source static-link + dynamic-WASM-module duality is invisible here — both just feed the set). Naming ('InjectionPoint', 'Candidate', 'Resolved', 'determine_winner', 'SelectionLayer', 'LayerVerdict', 'Qualified<T,Q>', 'Primary<T>', 'ErasedHandle') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (680-705 the layered algorithm + doResolveDependency pipeline + determineAutowireCandidate tie-break + name-as-implicit-qualifier + step-3 shortcut guards + resolvableDependencies; 707-722 @Primary/@Fallback two-pass + cross-module override + never-collapse-collections caveat; 724-734 generics-as-implicit-qualifier; 2595-2628 the two-phase filtering-vs-selection split with fixed selection order + bare-SimpleAutowireCandidateResolver + autowire-vs-default-candidate + single-point-only primary/fallback; 65/693 the fail-fast-on-ambiguity philosophy; 2178 the BeanCreation/UnsatisfiedDependency/NoSuchBean/NoUnique exception nesting); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3, topology §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/A/B DCE silent-failure + self-check mandate, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI, generics-cannot-register, scope-discovery-not-global guardrail, architectures A/B/C/D); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (stable envelope: RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe -> boxed futures at the dyn boundary, trait upcasting 1.86, const TypeId 1.91, Any+downcast, no async Drop, specialization avoided).

## Autowire Candidate-Resolution & Qualifiers  `[candidate-resolver]`

Spring splits dependency resolution into two phases: (1) candidate FILTERING — deciding which beans even *qualify* for an injection point — and (2) winner SELECTION among the qualifiers (tie-breaking). This feature owns ONLY phase 1; the fixed tie-break policy (primary -> name-match -> qualifier-name -> @Priority -> unique default-candidate) belongs to autowiring-resolution/primary-fallback. The filtering engine is a pluggable, composable `AutowireCandidateResolver` chain (a decorator-by-inheritance stack: bare autowire-candidate-flag-only -> generics-aware -> qualifier-annotation-aware -> context/@Lazy-aware), each level a superset. The load-bearing intent to replicate (intent, NOT mechanism): (a) a resolver answers per (injection-point, candidate) pair with THREE-VALUED logic — match (TRUE), active-veto (FALSE), or ABSTAIN (null); the null != false distinction is essential because qualifiers NARROW WITHIN type matches rather than being the sole gate, so 'no qualifier present' must fall through to type/primary/name while 'qualifier present but mismatched' is a hard veto. (b) Filtering dimensions: the `autowire_candidate` flag (false = removed from by-type autowiring entirely, still reachable by name); the weaker `defaultCandidate` flag (excluded from plain-type autowiring but still injectable when EXPLICITLY qualified — the lever that lets getIfUnique resolve when all other same-type beans are non-default); generics-as-implicit-qualifier (Store<Integer> will not match a Store<String> bean — the type parameter encodes intent, so no redundant @Qualifier needed, and it is refactor-safe); @Qualifier / custom qualifier annotations carrying attributes where a candidate must match ALL attribute values; and qualifier-value-falls-back-to-bean-name/alias as a deliberate LAST-RESORT convenience (Spring refuses to make @Qualifier('x') a synonym for getBean('x'), preserving type-driven-first DI). (c) The bare-vs-rich layering: a raw factory gets only the autowire-candidate-flag resolver; the rich qualifier/generic behaviour is INSTALLED, not innate — extensibility is a first-class design goal (CustomAutowireConfigurer registers annotations not meta-annotated with @Qualifier into the live resolver). (d) Filtering is the EXTENSIBLE/pluggable part; selection order is a FIXED container contract — a custom resolver changes what qualifies without re-encoding tie-break order. The deepest Rust impedance: Spring's resolver reads runtime-reflective annotation metadata and ResolvableType generics off a live BeanFactory; Rust has neither runtime annotation reflection nor open generic types, so qualifier metadata must be captured at the declaration site into the bean-definition metamodel (already designed) and generic-as-qualifier must key off CONCRETE TypeIds (generics cannot be registered).

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Trait-object filter chain of dyn CandidateFilter, three-valued Match enum, composed at registry build (Spring-faithful, rust-owned)
Mirror Spring's pluggable chain directly but rust-native. Define `enum Match { Yes, No, Abstain }` (the three-valued checkQualifiers, with Abstain != No load-bearing) and `trait CandidateFilter: Send + Sync { fn evaluate(&self, ip: &InjectionPoint, cand: &CandidateView, ctx: &ResolveCtx) -> Match; }`. `InjectionPoint` carries the statically-captured demand: required produced-TypeId, generic-argument TypeIds (the concrete monomorphized type args the macro captured at the @Autowired site — e.g. Store<Integer> captured as (Store_typeid, [i32_typeid])), the requested qualifier set (qualifier name + custom-qualifier attribute key/values), and a flag for whether the point uses 'standard bean lookup'. `CandidateView` is a read-only borrow over a bean's frozen MergedBeanDefinition (from bean-definition): its produced-TypeId, its declared generic-arg TypeIds, its qualifiers (Vec<Qualifier>), its name+aliases, and its autowire_candidate / default_candidate flags. leaf ships built-in filters mirroring the inheritance stack as composable list entries (not subclassing): `AutowireCandidateFilter` (flag only), `GenericTypeFilter` (concrete generic-arg TypeId match), `QualifierFilter` (@Qualifier + custom-attribute-all-match, with name/alias last-resort fallback), `DefaultCandidateFilter` (default_candidate gating). The registry (bean-registry) holds an ordered `Vec<Box<dyn CandidateFilter>>` built at refresh; `filter_candidates(ip)` runs every type-matching candidate through the chain and combines per-candidate: any No => excluded (veto wins), all-Abstain-or-Yes with at least one non-Abstain dimension => included, all-Abstain => included-by-type (qualifiers narrow within type, never the sole gate). The result is the QUALIFYING SET handed to autowiring-resolution for selection — this feature NEVER picks a winner. Extension: a downstream crate adds its own `Box<dyn CandidateFilter>` (the CustomAutowireConfigurer analogue) into the chain via a registration hook on the cold path. Bare-vs-rich: a minimal registry installs only AutowireCandidateFilter; the context-services facade (container-core) upgrades to the full stack — preserving Spring's exact bare-default footgun-avoidance, made explicit.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: the three-valued Match enum makes the null!=false abstain semantics a compile-checked type, not a convention — directly teachable to Spring users and impossible to collapse 'no qualifier' into 'mismatch'.; Pluggable/composable filtering as an ordered Vec<Box<dyn CandidateFilter>> is the literal rust-native form of Spring's pluggable resolver chain; downstream custom-qualifier filters slot in without touching leaf-core (honors the extensibility intent + charter open-base).; Clean separation enforced structurally: a CandidateFilter returns Match, it CANNOT pick a winner — the filtering-vs-selection split (the whole point of this feature vs autowiring-resolution) is encoded in the trait's return type.; Origin-agnostic: CandidateView borrows the frozen MergedBeanDefinition + reads erased TypeId/qualifier metadata, never assumes native construction — a WASM-module-contributed bean's definition presents the same CandidateView, so its qualifiers/generics filter identically.; Bare-vs-rich layering is explicit (which filters are installed), removing Spring's silent 'raw factory loses qualifiers' trap while preserving the same composition model.

**Cons:** dyn dispatch per (candidate, filter) on the resolution path: for N type-matching candidates and K filters that is N*K virtual calls + the Box indirection — resolution is hot-ish (every injection point at wiring time), brushing the low-overhead invariant #2; mitigable because the candidate set is usually tiny (1-3) and wiring is a cold-ish startup pass, but prototype/request-scope re-resolution makes it warmer.; An ordered Vec<Box<dyn _>> chain is shared read-only across concurrent bean init; must be frozen-after-refresh to avoid a lock (manageable, but it IS shared state during the registration window).; Combining per-candidate Match across K filters needs a precise, well-specified fold (veto-wins, abstain-falls-through) — if the fold rule is subtly wrong the narrow-within-type semantics break; needs a single canonical combinator + tests, not ad-hoc per-call logic.; Heap: Box<dyn> per filter + the qualifying-set output (a Vec) per resolution — Arc/Vec churn the memory-discipline invariant warns about; needs SmallVec/arena for the candidate set.; Custom-qualifier attribute matching (match ALL key/value attrs) needs a typed attribute representation that survives from the macro-captured declaration site through the definition into CandidateView — a non-trivial metadata pipeline.

**Mechanisms considered:** enum Match { Yes, No, Abstain } as the three-valued primitive (the null!=false abstain encoded in the type system); trait CandidateFilter + ordered Vec<Box<dyn CandidateFilter>> built at refresh (Spring chain as composition not inheritance); macro captures concrete generic-arg TypeIds + qualifier metadata at the @Autowired/@Component site into the bean-definition metamodel (no runtime ResolvableType reflection); downstream Box<dyn CandidateFilter> registration hook (CustomAutowireConfigurer analogue) on the cold path; per-candidate Match-fold combinator (veto-wins / abstain-fall-through) as a single canonical function; frozen-after-refresh chain for lock-free concurrent reads; SmallVec for the qualifying set

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The filter chain itself needs NO cross-crate discovery for the built-in filters — they live in leaf-core and are installed explicitly at refresh (no DCE hazard). What IS cross-crate: (a) downstream-contributed custom CandidateFilters (the CustomAutowireConfigurer analogue) and (b) downstream-defined custom qualifier annotations/types. Both ride whatever discovery substrate Phase 2 picks for components — the dual-classpath candidate applies: a static link-time registry of filter descriptors (default 'classpath') PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned/WASM-module source could each contribute filters into one ordered chain (CANDIDATES, not a decision). The Layer-0/DCE silent-empty hazard applies to a filter-only contributing crate: a silently-dropped custom-qualifier filter degrades to 'qualifier always abstains' = wrong matches with NO error — so the mandatory anti-DCE self-check must count expected-vs-found filters AND verify custom-qualifier types resolved. Custom qualifier ATTRIBUTE values (the key/value bag) must be representable as const-friendly data the macro emits with absolute ::leaf_core paths. Generics-as-qualifier is the sharp cross-crate constraint: leaf cannot register an open Store<T>; the macro captures concrete generic-arg TypeIds per monomorphized instantiation, so a Store<RemoteCrateType> only filters correctly if that concrete instantiation was registered (register_bean!(Store<RemoteCrateType>) escape hatch) — generic-as-implicit-qualifier keys off concrete TypeIds exactly as bean-definition's clash note requires.

**T2 — execution context:** All filtering is synchronous, CPU-only, non-blocking — no async, no I/O. It runs at resolution time: a cold startup pass for singletons (during the eager-instantiate phase), but WARMER for prototype/request-scope beans re-resolved per use, so the N*K dyn-call cost matters more on those paths (a Phase-2 hot-path concern flagged, not resolved here). The chain is built once at refresh (cold, single-threaded) and FROZEN; thereafter concurrent bean init reads it without a lock (safe publication of immutable data — no global lock, honoring memory discipline). evaluate() must never block or go async (it only reads metadata + compares TypeIds/qualifiers); the CandidateFilter trait documents this constraint, and because a downstream crate supplies filter code, the trait must forbid async/blocking in evaluate (a pure predicate, like the definition-phase rule). Errors: filtering itself does not error — it produces a (possibly empty) qualifying set. An EMPTY set or an over-ambiguous set is reported by autowiring-resolution downstream, but THIS feature must hand it the full causal data (which candidates were vetoed by which filter and why — 'bean X vetoed: qualifier role=primary requested, candidate carries role=replica') so the eventual NoSuchBean/NoUniqueBean diagnostic carries the candidates-considered chain (invariant #9). No cancellation concern (bounded sync work); no teardown (filters hold metadata refs only).

#### R2 — Predicate-fusion: compile a per-injection-point QualifyMask once, then candidate matching is bitwise/index lookups (data-oriented, low-overhead)
Question whether a dyn chain must run per (candidate, filter) at all. The INTENT is 'decide which beans qualify'; the filtering dimensions are FINITE and mostly reducible to fast equality/subset tests over pre-indexed metadata. Reframe: at refresh, the registry builds INDICES over candidate metadata — a by-produced-TypeId index, a (TypeId, generic-arg-TypeId-tuple) index, a qualifier-name -> bean-set multimap, a custom-qualifier-attribute index, and bitsets for autowire_candidate / default_candidate. For each injection point, COMPILE a `QualifyPlan` once: a small struct of (required produced-TypeId, required generic-arg tuple, requested qualifier predicate, default-candidate policy) plus a captured three-valued evaluation ORDER. Resolving the qualifying set is then index intersection + bitset ops: start from the by-type candidate set (a bitset), intersect with the generic-arg index if generics present (else abstain = no narrowing), intersect/annotate with the qualifier multimap (Yes/No/Abstain computed per candidate but via index hits not dyn calls), apply the autowire_candidate bitset mask, and the default_candidate mask CONDITIONALLY (only excludes when the point is NOT explicitly qualified — the defaultCandidate weak-exclusion rule encoded as 'apply mask unless QualifyPlan.has_explicit_qualifier'). The three-valued logic is preserved as data: a candidate is Yes if a qualifier index hit, No if it carries a conflicting qualifier, Abstain if absent — combined by the same veto-wins/abstain-fall-through fold but over index results, not virtual calls. Extension: a custom qualifier dimension registers its own index + a fusion rule (how it contributes to the mask) at refresh, not a per-call dyn predicate — extensibility without per-candidate dispatch.

**Pros:** Lowest overhead (invariant #2): per-injection-point resolution becomes bitset intersections + index lookups (near-O(matches)), eliminating the N*K dyn-call cost of R1 on the warm prototype/request-scope re-resolution paths the charter cares about — reactive/fast on hot-ish paths.; Data-oriented + immutable indices built once at refresh, read lock-free thereafter — squarely inside the no-global-locks/memory-discipline invariant; bitsets are stack/arena-friendly, far less Box/Arc churn than a dyn chain.; The QualifyPlan is computable ONCE per injection point and cacheable (or even build-time-emittable) — aligns with the AOT lineage (qualification knowable at build time where the graph is static), and a cached plan makes prototype re-resolution cheap.; Three-valued semantics survive as data (index-hit=Yes / conflicting-qualifier=No / absent=Abstain) with the same fold — fidelity kept without virtual dispatch; the abstain-fall-through is just 'no narrowing applied'.; Rich diagnostics are actually BETTER: the indices let the resolver report not just 'no match' but 'these 3 beans matched by type, candidate A excluded by generic-arg mismatch (wanted i32, has String), candidate B vetoed by qualifier role=replica' — the full candidates-considered chain (invariant #9) from index state.
