**Cons:** Less obviously 'pluggable' than R1's chain: a custom filter is now 'register an index + a fusion rule' rather than 'impl a trait + push to a Vec' — a more complex extension contract that may not cover arbitrary cross-candidate predicates a dyn filter could express (some exotic custom resolvers don't reduce to index intersection).; More machinery up front: building and maintaining several typed indices + bitsets is more code than one trait + Vec; the qualifier-attribute index (match-ALL-attributes) is a multi-key subset query that is fiddly to index efficiently.; The defaultCandidate conditional-mask rule (exclude unless explicitly qualified) and the name/alias last-resort fallback are special-cases that don't fold as cleanly into pure bitset ops — they need branch logic in the plan, eroding some of the uniformity.; Bitset-by-bean-index assumes a stable dense bean-index space (assigned at refresh) — couples this feature to the registry's internal indexing (bean-registry), and dynamic/late registration (programmatic-registration, WASM-module source) must extend the index space carefully.; Harder to express a genuinely dynamic/runtime custom predicate that depends on live container state (Spring's ContextAnnotationAutowireCandidateResolver does SpEL/@Lazy) — those don't reduce to a static index and need an escape hatch back toward R1's per-call evaluation.

**Mechanisms considered:** refresh-time typed indices: by-produced-TypeId, (TypeId, generic-arg-tuple) index, qualifier-name multimap, custom-attribute index, autowire/default-candidate bitsets; per-injection-point compiled QualifyPlan (cacheable; potentially build-time-emittable for the AOT lineage); candidate qualifying-set via bitset intersection + masks; three-valued logic as index-hit/conflict/absent data; custom dimension = register an index + a fusion rule at refresh (extension without per-candidate dyn dispatch); conditional default-candidate mask (apply unless plan.has_explicit_qualifier) for the weak-exclusion rule; escape hatch: a residual per-candidate predicate slot for genuinely dynamic custom resolvers (bridges toward R1)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The indices are built at refresh from whatever definitions were collected — substrate-neutral to how beans arrive (linkme/inventory/codegen/explicit), and the dual-classpath candidate is natural: a static link-time bean set AND an optional dynamic directory-scanned/WASM-module bean set both feed the SAME index-building pass (CANDIDATES, not a decision). The stable dense bean-index space is the cross-crate subtlety: it must be assigned by the registry over ALL collected beans (static + dynamic) before plan compilation, and a WASM-module source adding beans later must extend the index (and invalidate/extend bitsets) — a re-index event, fine on the cold path. Custom-qualifier dimensions contributed by downstream crates register their index+fusion-rule via the same scoped discovery substrate; the anti-DCE self-check must verify each expected custom-dimension index registered (a silently-dropped one means that qualifier dimension simply never narrows — a silent wrong-match, the worst failure mode, so the self-check is mandatory). Generics-as-qualifier again keys off CONCRETE generic-arg TypeId tuples captured by the macro (no open generics in the index); register_bean!(Concrete<T>) is the escape hatch. Build-time plan compilation (where the graph is static) is the most AOT-aligned of the three realizations and would push qualification errors to the richest-legible timing.

**T2 — execution context:** Index building and QualifyPlan compilation are synchronous cold-path work at refresh (single-threaded, no async, no blocking). Resolution-time matching is synchronous bitset/index ops — extremely cheap, safe to run inline on any async executor during bean construction without spawning/blocking, and fast enough to stay non-busy-wait on the warmer prototype/request-scope re-resolution paths (satisfies reactive-on-hot-paths since it is pure computation, not polling). Indices + plans are immutable after refresh => concurrent readers need no lock (safe publication; no global lock). A dynamic re-index (WASM-module beans arriving) is a cold-path mutation that must be synchronized only at that event (a justified, scoped lock or a copy-on-write index swap — never a steady-state lock), with the new immutable index published atomically. Errors: matching produces a qualifying set (possibly empty) + rich index-derived diagnostic data; the empty/ambiguous outcome is handed to autowiring-resolution. The escape-hatch dynamic predicate (if used) is the only place user code runs during matching and MUST be sync/non-blocking. No cancellation (bounded sync); no teardown (indices are metadata).

#### R3 — Qualifier-as-type: lift qualifiers and generic-args into the resolution KEY so the type system filters at the lookup site (compile-time, monomorphized)
Push hardest on intent-not-mechanism: Spring needs a runtime resolver chain because annotations + generics are only readable reflectively at runtime. In leaf, the injection point's type AND its qualifier are usually known at the call site, so make qualification part of the lookup KEY resolved by the compiler. A qualifier becomes a zero-sized marker type: `struct Main; struct Replica;` and an injection point is `Qualified<DataSource, Main>` (or `#[autowired(qualifier = Main)] ds: DataSource`). The macro emits a lookup keyed by (produced-TypeId, qualifier-marker-TypeId) — so 'which beans qualify' for a qualified point is a typed registry query, no per-candidate predicate. Generics-as-implicit-qualifier falls out for free: `Store<Integer>` and `Store<String>` are DISTINCT concrete types with distinct TypeIds, so the type system already separates them — the qualifier IS the type. Custom qualifier annotations with attributes become marker types or const generics (`Movie<Genre::SciFi>`). A bean DECLARES the qualifier markers it carries (`#[component(qualifies = [Main])]`) which the macro records as (bean-TypeId, [qualifier-marker-TypeIds]) in the definition; registration indexes the bean under each (type, qualifier) key. The three-valued abstain semantics map to: an UNqualified injection point queries by type only (abstain = no narrowing, all type matches qualify); a QUALIFIED point queries the (type, qualifier) key — a Yes is a key hit, a No is 'type matches but not under this key', and the name/alias last-resort fallback is a secondary lookup if the qualifier key misses. autowire_candidate/default_candidate stay as flags filtered after the keyed lookup. This deliberately diverges from R1/R2 by moving filtering from a runtime predicate to a typed lookup key — fewer dimensions are 'evaluated', more are 'keyed'.

**Pros:** Best DX + refactor-safety for the common case (invariant #1): a qualifier is a real type, so a typo is a COMPILE error, rename-refactoring follows the type, and generics-as-qualifier needs zero special machinery (distinct TypeIds already separate Store<i32> from Store<String>) — exactly Spring's stated generic-as-qualifier design-intent (don't duplicate what the type system encodes), realized natively.; Zero runtime filtering cost on the keyed path: qualification is a HashMap<(TypeId,TypeId), _> lookup, no chain, no per-candidate predicate, no dyn dispatch — the strongest low-overhead story (invariant #2), monomorphized at the call site.; Marker-type qualifiers are origin-agnostic and need no annotation reflection: a bean from any source declares its qualifier-marker TypeIds, indexed identically — and a WASM-module bean could be keyed the same way if it declares marker TypeIds.; Three-valued semantics still expressible (unqualified=by-type=abstain/no-narrowing; qualified=keyed Yes; type-match-but-key-miss=No; name fallback=secondary lookup), so the narrow-within-type philosophy survives even though filtering moved into the key.; Strongly aligns with the AOT lineage and rich compile-time diagnostics: a missing qualifier marker or an unregistered (type,qualifier) pair is catchable very early.

**Cons:** Biggest divergence from Spring's mental model: 'qualifier' is now a type, not a string/annotation value — a real translation cost for Spring migrants, and dynamic/string-valued qualifiers (@Qualifier('main') read from config, or programmatically-chosen qualifiers) do NOT fit a type key and need a fallback to R1/R2-style runtime matching (so this realization likely cannot stand alone).; Custom qualifier annotations with arbitrary attribute SETS (match-ALL of genre=.., format=..) map awkwardly onto types: combinatorial marker types or const generics (Movie<{SciFi},{Imax}>) get unwieldy, and runtime-valued attributes can't be types at all.; The orphan-rule / coherence friction: keying by marker TypeId means the marker type must be nameable at both the bean-declaration site and the injection site (shared in a common crate) — cross-crate qualifier sharing forces the marker into leaf-core or a shared crate, less flexible than Spring's open string qualifiers.; Pluggability shrinks: there is no 'resolver chain' for a downstream crate to extend with novel cross-candidate logic — extension is 'define a marker type', which can't express predicate-style custom resolvers (e.g. ContextAnnotationAutowireCandidateResolver's @Lazy/SpEL behaviour).; The name-as-implicit-qualifier and qualifier-falls-back-to-name rules sit uneasily with a TypeId key (names are strings, not types) — needs a parallel string-keyed secondary path, partially reintroducing R1/R2 machinery and undercutting the 'one keyed lookup' simplicity.

**Mechanisms considered:** zero-sized marker types as qualifiers (struct Main;) + Qualified<T, Q> wrapper or #[autowired(qualifier = Main)]; registry keyed by (produced-TypeId, qualifier-marker-TypeId); generics separated for free by distinct concrete TypeIds; const-generic / marker-combination encoding for custom-qualifier attribute sets (Movie<{SciFi}>); bean declares carried qualifier-marker TypeIds via #[component(qualifies=[..])] captured by the macro; secondary string-keyed lookup for name/alias fallback + dynamic string qualifiers (bridges to R1/R2); autowire_candidate/default_candidate applied as post-lookup flag filters

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Keyed lookup needs no per-candidate cross-crate discovery — beans index themselves under their (type, qualifier-marker) keys wherever registered, and the typed query is resolved by the compiler at the injection site (compile-time qualifier-typo detection, no DCE exposure for the key shape). The cross-crate constraint is the SHARED MARKER TYPE: a qualifier marker used by a bean in crate A and an injection point in crate B must live in a crate both depend on (leaf-core for built-ins, or a shared user crate) — Rust coherence/naming, not a discovery problem, but a real topology constraint (dependencies point inward). Generics-as-qualifier is the cleanest of all three realizations here: distinct concrete instantiations are distinct TypeIds with no special handling, though open generics still cannot be registered (register the concrete Store<RemoteType>). The dynamic/string-qualifier fallback path (and any predicate-style custom resolver) DOES need the R1/R2 discovery substrate, so this realization's cross-crate story is split: keyed path = no discovery, fallback path = inherits whichever substrate Phase 2 picks (dual static-link/WASM-module classpath candidates apply only to that fallback). The anti-DCE self-check matters only for the fallback registry; the keyed path fails at compile time instead — the richest-legible timing.

**T2 — execution context:** The keyed lookup is synchronous, allocation-light (a HashMap probe + flag check), non-blocking — safe inline on any executor and cheap on warm prototype/request-scope paths. The (type,qualifier) index is built at refresh (cold, single-threaded) and frozen => lock-free concurrent reads (no global lock). The string-keyed fallback path is likewise sync. No user code runs during the keyed match (qualification is a compiler/registry concern), so there is no async/blocking-in-user-predicate hazard on the primary path — only the fallback predicate path (if adopted from R1) carries that constraint and must be documented sync/non-blocking. Errors: a qualified point with no keyed match -> empty qualifying set handed to autowiring-resolution with the keyed candidates-considered data ('no bean carries qualifier Main for type DataSource; beans of type DataSource present: [replica, archive]'); a compile-time-detectable missing marker fails at build with a legible error (invariant #9 best-timing). No cancellation/teardown concern (pure data + typed lookup).

### Rust clashes
- Runtime annotation reflection vs declaration-site capture: Spring's resolver reads @Qualifier / custom-qualifier annotations and their attribute values REFLECTIVELY off live beans at resolution time. Rust has no runtime annotation reflection — qualifier metadata (qualifier name, custom-attribute key/values, which markers a bean carries) MUST be captured at the declaration site by the macro and frozen into the bean-definition metamodel. So 'a resolver inspects the candidate's annotations' becomes 'a filter reads pre-captured qualifier metadata off the MergedBeanDefinition' — the dynamism of reading arbitrary annotations at runtime is gone; the set of qualifier dimensions is whatever was captured at compile time.
- ResolvableType generics vs concrete TypeIds: Spring resolves generics-as-implicit-qualifier via ResolvableType at runtime (Store<Integer> != Store<String> by reflecting type arguments). Rust erases nothing at the type level but ALSO cannot register an open generic Store<T> (no symbol/TypeId until monomorphized). So generic-as-qualifier must key off CONCRETE generic-argument TypeIds captured per instantiation; List<Store<Integer>> collection-filtering must compare concrete arg-TypeId tuples, and a generic bean needs a per-instantiation register_bean!(Concrete) escape hatch (inherited from bean-definition's clash). The implicit-qualifier intent survives; the open-generic mechanism does not.
- Three-valued null-Boolean vs an explicit enum: Spring leans on Java's nullable Boolean (TRUE/FALSE/null) for match/veto/abstain. Rust has no implicit null — the abstain-vs-veto distinction (which is load-bearing: 'no qualifier present' must fall through, 'qualifier mismatched' must veto) must be an explicit enum Match { Yes, No, Abstain }. This is actually a Rust WIN (the distinction is type-enforced, can't collapse), but it forces every filter and the combinator to handle three arms explicitly rather than relying on truthiness.
- Pluggable runtime resolver chain (mutable, BeanFactoryAware, cloned-per-factory) vs immutable frozen composition: Spring's resolver is a stateful object cloned per factory and mutated at runtime (CustomAutowireConfigurer.addQualifierType reaches into the live resolver). Rust prefers an immutable, frozen-after-refresh chain/index read lock-free; the 'add a qualifier type to the live resolver' mutation must become a cold-path registration BEFORE freeze, not a runtime poke of a shared mutable resolver — preserving extensibility without the shared-mutable-state / global-lock the memory-discipline invariant forbids.
- String qualifiers vs typed markers (the open-vs-typed tension): Spring qualifiers are open strings (@Qualifier('main')), enabling dynamic/config-driven and last-resort name-fallback matching. A type-keyed approach (R3) gains compile-time safety and free generic-qualification but cannot represent runtime-valued or dynamically-chosen qualifiers, and the qualifier-falls-back-to-bean-name rule (names are strings) doesn't fit a TypeId key. There is no free answer: string-keyed keeps Spring fidelity + name-fallback but loses compile-time checking; type-keyed gains safety but needs a string-keyed fallback for dynamic/name cases — a genuine open tension.
- Filtering-vs-selection boundary must be drawn in code: Spring co-locates the resolver chain (filtering) and determineAutowireCandidate (selection) in the same DefaultListableBeanFactory; the split is conceptual. In leaf the boundary between THIS feature (produces the qualifying set, answers what-qualifies) and autowiring-resolution/primary-fallback (picks the winner from that set, the fixed tie-break order) must be an explicit API seam — the candidate-resolver outputs a qualifying set + per-candidate veto reasons and NEVER selects; getting that seam wrong re-tangles the two phases the catalog deliberately separated.
- No-op-on-empty silent-wrong-match hazard: a dropped/DCE'd custom-qualifier filter or a missing qualifier index degrades to 'that dimension always abstains' — producing a WRONG (over-broad) qualifying set with NO error, the worst cross-crate failure mode (Layer-0). Unlike a missing bean (which fails loudly downstream as NoSuchBean), a missing FILTER silently widens matches. This makes the anti-DCE self-check (expected-vs-found filters/dimensions) mandatory specifically for this feature, beyond the generic component self-check.

### Interactions
- **autowiring-resolution** — THE load-bearing seam. This feature produces the QUALIFYING SET (which beans pass filtering, with per-candidate veto reasons); autowiring-resolution applies the FIXED tie-break policy (primary -> name-match -> qualifier-name -> @Priority -> unique default-candidate) to pick the single winner and enforces fail-fast on ambiguity. Strict producer/consumer split: candidate-resolver answers 'what qualifies', autowiring-resolution answers 'which one wins'. The API must hand over enough candidates-considered data for the eventual NoSuchBean/NoUniqueBean diagnostic.
- **bean-definition** — The metamodel SUPPLIES the inputs this feature filters on: the produced-TypeId (getObjectType analogue), the declared generic-arg TypeIds, the qualifiers list (Vec<Qualifier> in R1/R2 / carried-marker TypeIds in R3), and the autowire_candidate / default_candidate flags — all frozen on the MergedBeanDefinition. CandidateView is a read-only borrow over that frozen def. This feature is a pure CONSUMER of bean-definition's metadata; it adds no metadata of its own.
- **bean-registry** — The registry STORES candidates and is where filtering RUNS: it holds the filter chain (R1) / the indices+bitsets (R2) / the (type,qualifier) keyed map (R3), builds them at refresh, and exposes filter_candidates(injection_point) -> qualifying set. The dense bean-index space (R2's bitsets) is the registry's internal indexing. The registry owns the freeze-after-refresh boundary that makes the chain/indices lock-free.
- **primary-fallback** — Reads the primary/fallback flags that this feature does NOT filter on — @Primary/@Fallback are SELECTION tie-breakers applied AFTER filtering, on the qualifying set, for SINGLE injection points only. Clean boundary: candidate-resolver filters (qualifiers/generics/flags), primary-fallback breaks ties among survivors. (Fallback's 'chosen only when no non-fallback matches' is selection, not filtering.)
- **collection-injection** — List/Set/array/Map injection takes ALL filtered candidates (this feature's qualifying set), bypassing primary/fallback/selection entirely. Crucially: generics-as-implicit-qualifier still applies (List<Store<Integer>> filters to only Store<Integer> beans), and @Qualifier still narrows a collection — so collection-injection consumes the SAME qualifying set this feature produces, then applies its OWN ordering (consumer concern). Note Spring's Map-no-sort asymmetry lives in collection-injection, not here.
- **deferral-primitives** — ObjectProvider streams take all qualifying candidates (like collections); getIfUnique() specifically depends on the default_candidate filtering rule — when every other same-type bean is marked default_candidate=false, getIfUnique resolves cleanly to the one default. So this feature's default-candidate filtering is what makes getIfUnique's uniqueness check work.
- **bean-naming** — Provides the bean name + aliases that this feature uses for the LAST-RESORT qualifier-value-falls-back-to-name/alias rule (@Qualifier('main') matches a bean literally named/aliased 'main' when no qualifier metadata matches). The name-as-implicit-qualifier path reads bean-naming's name+alias set; the deliberate last-resort positioning (not a getBean synonym) is this feature's policy.
- **post-processor-spi** — The CustomAutowireConfigurer analogue is a cold-path post-processor that registers custom qualifier types / custom CandidateFilters into the resolver chain BEFORE freeze — the extension hook. Also, a MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor may discover/cache the injection-point qualifier metadata that feeds this feature. The freeze boundary is the 'no more filter registration' line, mirroring bean-definition's freeze gate.
- **annotation-model** — Custom qualifier annotations are meta-annotated composable vocabulary (the @Qualifier-meta-annotation pattern); annotation-model defines how a user-minted @MovieQualifier(genre,format) is recognized as a qualifier and how its attributes are read. This feature consumes the RESULTING captured qualifier metadata; annotation-model owns the capture/composition, this feature owns the match-ALL-attributes filtering semantics.
- **container-core** — The context-services facade is what UPGRADES the bare resolver (autowire-candidate-flag-only) to the full generic+qualifier+context stack — replicating Spring's 'rich behaviour is installed, not innate'. container-core decides which filters are installed; a raw bean-registry gets only the bare filter (the explicit bare-vs-rich layering this feature surfaces).
- **value-injection** — Spring's QualifierAnnotationAutowireCandidateResolver also handles @Value at the injection point (the step-2 suggested-value shortcut). In leaf, a @Value injection point is resolved by value-injection/type-conversion, NOT by candidate filtering — so the boundary is: if the injection point carries a @Value, it short-circuits BEFORE candidate filtering; this feature only filters by-type/by-qualifier bean injection points.
- **lazy-initialization** — Spring's ContextAnnotationAutowireCandidateResolver (top of the chain) builds @Lazy resolution proxies. In leaf, lazy/deferred resolution is owned by deferral-primitives/lazy-initialization; whether the candidate-resolver chain participates (R1's chain could host a @Lazy-aware filter) or it lives entirely outside filtering is a boundary to draw — the @Lazy/SpEL-style dynamic resolver is the part R3's keyed approach cannot express and needs a runtime fallback for.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Filter-chain (R1 dyn predicates) vs predicate-fusion indices (R2 data-oriented) vs type-keyed lookup (R3 compile-time) — or a blend (e.g. R3-keyed for static type/marker qualifiers as the fast default, R2 indices for collections/attribute-sets, an R1 dyn-filter escape hatch for dynamic/string/@Lazy/SpEL-style custom resolvers)? This is the keystone shape decision and is entangled with the deferred type-erasure & registry-substrate meta-concept (how dyn beans are keyed/stored) and the compile-vs-runtime split (how much qualification is frozen at build time).
- String qualifiers vs typed-marker qualifiers (the open-vs-typed tension): does leaf expose qualifiers as open strings (Spring-faithful, supports dynamic/config-driven values + name-fallback, but no compile-time typo checking) or as typed markers (compile-safe, free generic-qualification, but can't represent runtime-valued qualifiers and forces a string-keyed fallback for the name rule)? Cross-cuts annotation-model, bean-naming, and DX invariant #1 vs low-overhead #2.
- Where does qualification VALIDATION/MATCHING sit in the fail-fast timing ladder (invariant #9): compile-time (R3 catches missing markers / unregistered (type,qualifier) pairs legibly), a build-time plan-compilation pass (R2 where the graph is static), a dedicated startup validation pass, or runtime resolution? And is it consistent for single vs collection injection points? Shares the unified error/diagnostics meta-concept.
- How is the abstain-vs-veto three-valued result combined and surfaced in diagnostics across an arbitrary set of filters/dimensions (built-in + downstream custom)? The veto-wins/abstain-fall-through fold must be canonical and total, and the per-candidate veto REASON must be retained for the candidates-considered chain — couples to the unified diagnostics model and to how much per-candidate provenance the resolver keeps at runtime (memory cost).
- Do custom qualifier filters / custom qualifier types contributed by downstream crates ride the SAME scoped cross-crate discovery substrate leaf picks for components/auto-config (linkme vs inventory vs codegen vs explicit)? And how is the silent-wrong-match hazard handled — a dropped filter degrades to 'always abstain' = over-broad matches with NO error, a worse failure than a missing bean — making a filter-aware anti-DCE self-check mandatory. Couples to the cross-crate composition meta-concept and the 'scope discovery, don't make it global' guardrail.
- Custom-qualifier ATTRIBUTE matching (match-ALL of multiple key/value attributes, possibly runtime-valued): how are attribute sets represented and matched — typed (markers/const-generics, combinatorially awkward, no runtime values) or a captured key/value bag matched at runtime (heap, but flexible)? This interacts with bean-definition's closed-core-vs-open-attribute-map question (R3 of bean-definition) since qualifier attributes are exactly the kind of extensible per-bean metadata that may live in its attribute map.
- The dense bean-index space (needed for R2's bitsets) — is it a stable registry-assigned index over ALL collected beans (static + dynamic/WASM-module), and how does late/dynamic registration (programmatic-registration, a directory-scanned source) extend it without a steady-state lock (copy-on-write index swap on the cold path)? Couples to bean-registry's internal indexing and the scope/lifetime/ownership model.
- The @Lazy-proxy / SpEL-dynamic-resolver tier (Spring's ContextAnnotationAutowireCandidateResolver, top of the chain): does it participate in candidate FILTERING at all, or is it entirely owned by deferral-primitives/lazy-initialization and expression-language outside this feature? R3's keyed approach cannot express it; drawing this boundary decides whether the filtering layer must retain a dynamic per-candidate predicate escape hatch.
- The qualifying-set output ownership/shape (couples to the deferred scope/ownership keystone): is the qualifying set a Vec of borrows, an Arc'd slice, a bitset over the registry index space, or an arena-backed SmallVec? This drives whether filtering allocates per resolution (a warm-path cost for prototype/request-scope) and how the set is handed across the seam to autowiring-resolution — cannot be settled here.

### Notes
Confidence high on intent: I read the dedicated 'autowire candidate-resolution engine' section (lines 2595-2628) AND the qualifiers/generics/implicit-qualifier section (724-734) AND the resolution-pipeline overview (685-734) in full, including the three load-bearing hidden concepts — three-valued checkQualifiers (null!=false abstain), qualifier-value-falls-back-to-name/alias as a deliberate last-resort, and the bare-SimpleAutowireCandidateResolver-vs-rich-stack split — plus the autowire_candidate (hard, by-type removal) vs defaultCandidate (weak, still-injectable-when-qualified) distinction and the @Primary/@Fallback-only-affect-single-injection-points rule.

The single most important framing for THIS feature: it is FILTERING ('what qualifies'), explicitly NOT selection ('which wins') — that belongs to autowiring-resolution/primary-fallback. Every realization is built around producing a qualifying set + per-candidate veto reasons and NEVER picking a winner; the filtering-vs-selection API seam is the load-bearing boundary and is flagged in interactions + rustClashes. I treated bean-definition (qualifiers/produced-TypeId/flags on the frozen MergedBeanDefinition) and bean-registry (storage + the freeze-after-refresh boundary) as upstream dependencies and consumed them concretely rather than reinventing them.

The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters HERE — WHERE/HOW filtering happens: R1 = runtime dyn filter-chain with an explicit three-valued Match enum (Spring-faithful, most pluggable, dyn-dispatch cost on warm paths); R2 = data-oriented predicate-fusion over refresh-built immutable indices+bitsets (lowest overhead, most AOT-aligned, extension is index+rule not a trait); R3 = qualifier-as-type, lifting qualifiers and generic-args into the typed lookup KEY resolved by the compiler (best DX + free generic-qualification + compile-time typo checking, but cannot express dynamic/string/@Lazy qualifiers and needs a fallback). R1 and R2 differ on dyn-predicate vs index/bitset; R3 differs on runtime-predicate vs compile-time-key and is partially orthogonal (a likely Phase-2 blend uses R3-keyed as the fast default with R2 indices for collections/attributes and an R1 dyn escape hatch for dynamic resolvers). I deliberately stayed substrate-neutral on the cross-crate collection mechanism (linkme/inventory/codegen/explicit) and on the qualifying-set ownership/Arc-vs-bitset question — both surfaced as phase2Questions, not re-litigated per realization.

Three things to carry forward: (1) The three-valued abstain!=veto distinction is non-negotiable intent and is a Rust WIN as an explicit enum Match { Yes, No, Abstain } — 'no qualifier present' (abstain, narrow-within-type fall-through) must never collapse into 'qualifier mismatched' (active veto). (2) The worst cross-crate failure mode here is UNIQUE to filtering: a dropped/DCE'd custom-qualifier filter degrades silently to 'always abstain' = an OVER-BROAD qualifying set with NO error (unlike a missing bean, which fails loudly downstream) — so a filter/dimension-aware anti-DCE self-check is mandatory, beyond the generic component self-check. (3) Generics-as-implicit-qualifier must key off CONCRETE generic-argument TypeIds captured at the declaration site (no open-generic registration, no runtime ResolvableType) — inherited directly from bean-definition's clash; R3 gets this for free (distinct concrete types = distinct TypeIds), R1/R2 capture and compare arg-TypeId tuples.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: all three realizations are compatible with whatever cross-crate substrate and registry/scope/ownership model Phase 2 chooses, by keeping qualifier metadata sourced from the frozen definition and the qualifying-set output shape abstract. Naming ('CandidateFilter'/'Match'/'QualifyPlan'/'CandidateView'/'Qualified<T,Q>') are Phase-1 placeholders. I wrote no files. Grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (2595-2628 the resolver-chain/three-valued-checkQualifiers/name-fallback/bare-vs-rich/autowire-vs-default-candidate; 724-734 generics-as-implicit-qualifier + custom qualifier attributes; 685-701 the resolution pipeline + name-as-implicit-qualifier); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/DCE silent-empty/silent-wrong-match, linkme-vs-inventory, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI, self-check mandate, scope-discovery guardrail); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (stable envelope: const TypeId 1.91, Any+downcast, trait upcasting 1.86, RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe).

## @Primary / @Fallback Tie-Breaking  `[primary-fallback]`

This feature is the @Primary/@Fallback SUB-POLICY of single-valued autowiring — NOT the whole tie-break ladder (that is autowiring-resolution, which EXECUTES this as its first selection layer "5a"). Its job is narrow and load-bearing: given a qualifying set of same-type candidates (already filtered by candidate-resolver), resolve single-valued ambiguity by PROMOTING one (@Primary) or DEMOTING library defaults (@Fallback), so a user bean transparently supersedes a shipped default ACROSS MODULE BOUNDARIES with zero ceremony on the user bean. The exact Spring mechanics to replicate (intent, not JVM mechanism), read at docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md lines 707-722 + 150-177 + 2618-2624: (a) determinePrimaryCandidate is a TWO-PASS selection — PASS 1: find a UNIQUE @Primary among the qualifying set (two local primaries => NoUniqueBean 'more than one primary bean found'; a LOCAL-context primary BEATS a parent-context primary — hierarchy-depth-aware); PASS 2: only if NO primary exists, find the unique NON-@Fallback candidate (exclude every @Fallback, and if exactly ONE regular bean remains it 'effectively becomes primary by type' with ZERO annotations on it). (b) @Fallback is the INVERSE of @Primary: it demotes a bean so it wins only when no non-fallback candidate exists — the killer use case being cross-module composition (a starter/library ships `@Fallback DataSource defaultDataSource()`, the app's plain `DataSource appDataSource()` wins automatically, no @Primary needed). This is the auto-configuration-override story made declarative (ties to auto-configuration's @ConditionalOnMissingBean backoff). (c) CRUCIAL caveat: @Primary/@Fallback NEVER collapse collections — they affect SINGLE-VALUED injection ONLY; a List<T>/Map<String,T>/array still receives EVERY matching candidate regardless of primary/fallback status (lines 722, 2622). (d) This sub-policy runs AFTER type+generic+qualifier narrowing and BEFORE the bean-name/@Priority/default-candidate/resolvableDependency tie-breaks; it is part of the FIXED, non-pluggable selection contract (filtering is the pluggable part, owned by candidate-resolver). (e) @Fallback (and the defaultCandidate flag) are Framework 6.2 features carried into 7.x, not v7-new — semantically stable. Deepest Rust impedance: Spring reads primary/fallback status reflectively off live BeanDefinitions at resolution time; Rust has no runtime annotation reflection, so primary/fallback must be captured at the declaration site by the thin macro as CONST flags frozen onto each candidate's merged definition, AND — the hard part — the @Fallback cross-module-override is fundamentally COUNT-and-CROSS-CRATE-dependent ('demote unless I am the only non-fallback'), which cannot be decided locally at any single declaration site and cannot be a compile-time typed-key decision, so it MUST be evaluated at runtime/assembly over the FULLY-collected candidate set from all linked crates (including any dynamic source) — making this the single most cross-crate-coupled layer of the whole selection algorithm.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Two-pass partition function over a borrowed candidate set (Spring-faithful determinePrimaryCandidate, rust-owned)
Realize the sub-policy as exactly what Spring's determinePrimaryCandidate is: one small, hand-written, non-pluggable PURE FUNCTION that autowiring-resolution calls as its first selection layer. Signature (Phase-1 placeholder names): `fn primary_fallback_select<'a>(set: &[Candidate<'a>]) -> PfVerdict<'a>` where `enum PfVerdict<'a> { Promoted(&'a Candidate<'a>), Narrowed(SmallVec<&'a Candidate<'a>>), Ambiguous(PrimaryConflict<'a>) }`. Each `Candidate` exposes (read off the frozen merged definition) two const flags `is_primary: bool`, `is_fallback: bool`, plus a `hierarchy_depth: u16` (0 = local context, increasing toward root parent — from container-hierarchy) and name/handle for the caller's later layers. The function is the literal two-pass: PASS 1 (primary promotion) — scan for `is_primary` candidates; if exactly one => `Promoted`; if >1, apply the LOCAL-BEATS-PARENT rule: keep only those at the MINIMUM hierarchy_depth among primaries — if that min-depth set is a singleton => `Promoted` (a local primary beats a deeper parent primary), if still >1 (two primaries at the same min depth) => `Ambiguous(PrimaryConflict{ primaries, depth })` carrying the 'more than one primary bean found' diagnostic and the offending candidate set; PASS 2 (fallback demotion, ONLY if zero primaries) — PARTITION the set into regulars (`!is_fallback`) and fallbacks; if regulars.len()==1 => `Promoted(the_regular)` (the zero-annotation auto-win — the cross-module override); if regulars.len()>1 => `Narrowed(regulars)` (hand the non-fallback subset back to autowiring-resolution's NEXT layer — name/@Priority/etc. — fallbacks excluded); if regulars.len()==0 (every candidate is a fallback) => `Narrowed(all)` (fall through with the full set so a fallback can still be chosen by a later layer — fallback means 'only if no regular', not 'never'). The function NEVER touches collections (collection-injection bypasses it entirely — see interactions) and NEVER constructs a bean (pure selection). It is sync, allocation-light (SmallVec only on the rare Narrowed/>1 path), and the Ambiguous/Narrowed return makes 'refuse to guess' a type-level guarantee, not a convention.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: a literal, line-by-line transcription of determinePrimaryCandidate's two-pass (primary-promote then non-fallback-narrow) — maximally teachable and every Spring nuance (two-primaries-error, local-beats-parent, zero-annotation-regular-wins, fallback-means-only-if-no-regular) maps to one code branch.; The Promoted/Narrowed/Ambiguous return type encodes the THREE possible sub-policy outcomes precisely: a unique winner this layer can decide, a narrowed-but-still-ambiguous subset handed to the next layer, or a hard primary-conflict the caller turns into NoUniqueBean — 'refuse to guess' is structural (the type system) not convention, honoring charter #9.; Clean producer/consumer seam with autowiring-resolution: this function is JUST the 5a layer — it consumes the qualifying set and returns a verdict; it does not run the bean-name/@Priority/default/resolvable tie-breaks (those are the caller's later layers), so the FIXED-but-decomposed selection contract stays clean and this feature owns exactly its slice.; Operates over a BORROWED &[Candidate] with a SmallVec only on the cold Narrowed path — minimal heap/Arc churn (charter #5); the common cases (one primary, or one regular after excluding fallbacks) allocate nothing and run as a couple of comparisons.; Local-beats-parent is realized faithfully and cheaply by a min-hierarchy_depth scan over only the primary subset (carried as a const u16 on each Candidate from container-hierarchy) — no separate parent-context walk, no JVM-style recursive getParentBeanFactory traversal.; @Fallback partitioning is origin-agnostic: a fallback bean from a remote starter crate and a regular bean from the app crate are just two Candidates with different is_fallback flags in the same set — the cross-module-override semantics fall out of set partitioning with no special cross-crate code path here.

**Cons:** The fallback-exclusion arithmetic is exactly count-AND-cross-crate-dependent: 'regulars.len()==1 => auto-win' is correct ONLY if the set already contains EVERY candidate from EVERY linked crate (and any dynamic source). If a contributing crate was silently dropped (Layer-0 DCE), a fallback that should have lost can win, or a should-have-won regular vanishes — a silent wrong-injection. This makes the upstream anti-DCE self-check a hard PRECONDITION for this feature's correctness, more sharply than for any other selection layer.; The PASS-2 'all-fallback => Narrowed(all)' branch is subtle: fallback means 'chosen only if no regular', NOT 'never chosen' — getting this wrong (e.g. returning Narrowed(empty) when every candidate is a fallback) would make an all-library-defaults graph fail to wire. The branch must be specified precisely and tested.; Reads two flags + hierarchy_depth off each Candidate, coupling the function to the exact Candidate borrow shape bean-definition/bean-registry expose; any future flag (a hypothetical priority-among-primaries) ripples into PASS 1.; The 'local primary beats parent primary' rule needs a correct hierarchy_depth on every Candidate, which only exists if container-hierarchy threads depth into the merged candidate set — a dependency on how the parent-delegated find labels its results; mislabeling depth silently flips which primary wins.; Least extensible of the three for the (by-design-rejected) case of a user wanting different promote/demote semantics — faithful to Spring (the order is fixed) but no escape hatch; and being a bare function it offers no built-in trace for diagnostics (the caller must reconstruct why).

**Mechanisms considered:** hand-written two-pass pure function `primary_fallback_select(&[Candidate]) -> PfVerdict` — determinePrimaryCandidate transcribed, not a strategy object; `enum PfVerdict { Promoted | Narrowed(SmallVec) | Ambiguous(PrimaryConflict) }` making the three sub-policy outcomes a non-coercible return type; const `is_primary`/`is_fallback` flags + `hierarchy_depth: u16` captured by the thin macro and frozen on the merged definition (no runtime reflection); local-beats-parent via min-hierarchy_depth scan over the primary subset (carried as const u16 from container-hierarchy); set-partition for fallback demotion (regulars vs fallbacks) with the three-way count branch (1 / >1 / 0); borrowed &[Candidate] + SmallVec only on the cold Narrowed path (minimal heap)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** This feature has NO discovery surface of its own — the two-pass function lives entirely in leaf-core and has zero DCE hazard for the algorithm. What it is acutely EXPOSED to is the cross-crate completeness of the candidate SET it partitions, because @Fallback's whole purpose is cross-MODULE override (library default in crate A, app regular in crate B). The is_primary/is_fallback flags are const data the thin macro emits per bean (via absolute ::leaf_core paths, per the cross-crate doc's thin-macro/absolute-path ABI), so a remote crate's bean carries its own flags into the set with no central table — good. But the killer hazard is Layer-0/A/B silent DCE (cross-crate doc lines 135-152): if a contributing crate is dropped because the binary never path-references it, its bean never enters the set, and THIS feature's count-dependent arithmetic silently mis-fires — a dropped REGULAR crate lets a @Fallback win (wrong default ships); a dropped FALLBACK crate is harmless here but flips other layers. So the bean-registry/context expected-count self-check (cross-crate doc line 152's mandate) is a correctness PRECONDITION specifically for the fallback partition, and the PrimaryConflict/no-winner diagnostic should be able to say 'N candidates of type T were registered from crates [..]; M were @Fallback' so a missing-override symptom points at a dropped crate. The two-source duality (static linkme/inventory registry as the default 'classpath' PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned/WASM-module source) is INVISIBLE to this function — both just contribute Candidates with their own primary/fallback flags to the set; the ONLY new T1 requirement the dynamic source imposes is that the full set (static + dynamic) must be assembled BEFORE this layer runs, since fallback-exclusion cannot be re-evaluated incrementally once a winner is published (CANDIDATES, not a decision). Generics-cannot-register (cross-crate doc line 153) bounds the set upstream; this layer only reads it. Local-beats-parent depends on container-hierarchy labeling candidates with the right depth across the parent/child context boundary (a single-process concern, not cross-crate).

**T2 — execution context:** The two-pass selection is PURE, SYNCHRONOUS, CPU-only, non-blocking, allocation-light — it only reads two const flags + a u16 depth, partitions a borrowed slice, and compares counts; it never awaits, does IO, spawns, or locks. It runs at WIRING time: a cold pass during eager-singleton instantiation (refresh) for most beans, but WARMER for prototype/request/session-scoped beans re-resolved per acquisition — so although the partition is cheap, the warm path matters and the function must stay branch-only with no per-call allocation on the common (one-primary / one-regular) cases (charter #4 reactive-on-hot-paths: this is pure computation, never a poll/busy-wait; charter #2 low overhead). The candidate set + flags are immutable after refresh (frozen by the registry), so concurrent resolutions read them with NO lock (safe publication of immutable data — no global lock, charter #5). CRITICAL execution-context boundary: this layer PICKS or NARROWS but never CONSTRUCTS — obtaining the actual bean handle from the chosen Candidate is the async step (a boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary, since AFIT/RPITIT are not dyn-safe — confirmed stable-envelope) owned by bean-instantiation; this feature returns a Candidate (or the narrowed subset) and the await happens in the caller (autowiring-resolution -> bean-instantiation). So selection is sync; construction-of-the-winner is async, may .await (DB pools) but MUST NOT block the executor (blocking factories documented to spawn_blocking — Phase 2 owns propagation). Cancellation after selection leaks nothing (this function held only borrows). Errors propagate as a rich PrimaryConflict value (the 'more than one primary' case) folded into the caller's NoUniqueBean / ResolveError carrying the injection point + the offending primaries + depth, surfaced fail-fast at eager-instantiate (boot) for singletons and at first-acquisition for lazy/scoped. No teardown concern (holds only borrows).

#### R2 — @Fallback as a definition-rewriting pre-pass (BeanDefinitionRegistryPostProcessor-style 'effective primary'), leaving the live selection a single primary check
Question whether @Fallback must be evaluated DURING selection at all. Spring's own framing (line 711) is that an unambiguous non-fallback regular 'effectively becomes primary by type'. So lift the COUNT-dependent fallback arithmetic OUT of the hot resolution path and into a one-time POST-PROCESSING pass over the frozen-but-not-yet-instantiated definition set (dogfooding the post-processor-spi: a built-in MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor / BFPP-like step that runs once during refresh AFTER all definitions from all crates are collected, BEFORE eager instantiation). The pre-pass groups all definitions by produced-TypeId; within each type group it computes an EFFECTIVE PRIMARY: if exactly one regular (non-fallback) candidate exists and there is no explicit @Primary, mark that regular with a derived `effective_primary` flag (the 'zero-annotation regular wins' rule materialized as a definition rewrite — exactly Spring's 'effectively becomes primary by type'); explicit @Primary always takes precedence over derived; two explicit local primaries in a group are recorded as a deferred PrimaryConflict diagnostic to be raised at first resolution of that type (or eagerly at a validation pass). After this pass, the runtime selection layer collapses to ONE check: 'is there a unique (explicit-or-effective) primary?' — the live tie-break never partitions fallbacks again because fallback-exclusion already happened once at assembly. The Candidate then carries a single `effective_primary: bool` (computed) instead of the raw is_fallback flag being re-interpreted per resolution. Hierarchy: the pre-pass runs per-context, and a child context's pre-pass sees the merged (local+parent) type group, so local-beats-parent is computed once into the effective flag. The pre-pass is itself a definition-level transform (editing the blueprint), squarely in post-processor-spi's 'edit the blueprint vs decorate the product' split.

**Pros:** Moves the count-AND-cross-crate-dependent arithmetic to a SINGLE one-time assembly pass (after all crates' definitions are collected, before instantiation) — the warm prototype/scoped resolution path becomes a single 'unique effective primary?' check with zero partitioning, the lowest-overhead hot path of the three (charter #2/#4).; Materializes Spring's own words ('an unambiguous regular effectively becomes primary by type') LITERALLY as a definition rewrite — high conceptual fidelity, and the rewrite is inspectable: a developer (or condition-report-style diagnostic) can ask the registry 'why is bean X the effective primary for type T?' and get 'derived: sole non-fallback among [X, libDefault@Fallback]'.; Dogfoods post-processor-spi (the BDRPP/MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor pattern) — leaf's own @Fallback resolution becomes an ordered infrastructure post-processor, exactly Spring's 'the framework dogfoods its own features as ordered infrastructure beans' philosophy, maximizing unification (charter #11) rather than a bespoke selection branch.; The cross-crate completeness REQUIREMENT becomes explicit and structural: the pre-pass runs at a well-defined point in refresh (after definition load, before instantiation), so 'the full candidate set must exist before @Fallback is decided' is enforced by the lifecycle ordering — and the pre-pass is the natural place to run the anti-DCE expected-count self-check, co-locating the hazard and its guard.; Fail-fast for two-primaries can be raised EARLY at the validation pass (richest runtime context — the whole definition set is in hand), not lazily at first injection, improving diagnostic timing (charter #9) without forcing it to compile-time where it would be cryptic.; Once computed, the effective_primary flag is immutable-after-refresh like every other definition flag, so concurrent resolution reads it lock-free (charter #5) and the selection layer is even simpler than R1's two-pass.

**Cons:** Biggest semantic risk: @Fallback's count is per-TYPE-GROUP but the same bean can satisfy MULTIPLE injection types (a DataSourceImpl injectable as DataSource AND as Closeable, each a different type-index view — trait-upcasting recovers supertype objects but the candidate set is per registered view). 'Effective primary' must be computed PER type-key, not per bean, or a bean that is the sole regular for DataSource but one-of-many for Closeable gets a wrong global flag — so effective_primary cannot be a single bool on the definition; it must be a per-type-key derived map, complicating the 'simple flag' promise and partially re-introducing the per-query arithmetic R1 has.; Pre-computing 'effective primary' assumes the candidate set per type-key is FIXED after refresh — but a dynamic WASM-module source that registers a bean AFTER the pre-pass would invalidate every effective_primary it touches (a late regular could demote a previously-effective fallback). Either the dynamic source must register before the pre-pass (constraining the two-source duality), or the effective flag must be recomputable — denting the 'compute once' win and coupling tightly to bean-registry's freeze model.; Definition rewriting is more machinery and more lifecycle coupling than R1's pure function — it must slot into refresh-lifecycle at the correct ordered position (after all BFPPs that might add/remove definitions, before instantiation), and an ordering bug (running before a late definition-registering post-processor) silently computes effective primaries over an incomplete set.; Conflates two concerns the catalog separates: this feature (primary-fallback) now partly lives in post-processor-spi / refresh-lifecycle as a pre-pass, so its logic is split between a definition transform and a thin runtime check — harder to point at 'where @Fallback is decided' than R1's single function.; Explicit @Primary still needs the live two-primaries-conflict + local-beats-parent handling, so the runtime layer is not purely 'one bool check' — it still must detect the two-explicit-primaries case (unless that too is fully pre-validated), meaning some of R1's logic survives anyway; the pre-pass mainly absorbs the FALLBACK half, not the PRIMARY half.; If the effective-primary derivation is per-type-key and a type group is large (many candidates), the pre-pass is O(types x candidates) at refresh — fine for cold startup, but a cost that R1 pays lazily and only for types actually injected, so R2 can do work for type groups that are never resolved single-valued.

**Mechanisms considered:** a one-time definition-rewriting pre-pass (BDRPP / MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor-style) computing a derived `effective_primary` per type-key during refresh, before instantiation — dogfooding post-processor-spi; Spring's 'sole regular effectively becomes primary by type' materialized as a blueprint edit, not a per-resolution partition; runtime selection collapsed to a single 'unique (explicit-or-effective) primary?' check over the frozen definitions; per-type-key derived primary map (NOT a single bool on the bean) to handle multi-type-view beans; two-primaries conflict + local-beats-parent computed once at the validation pass (early, rich diagnostic) rather than lazily per resolution; co-locating the anti-DCE expected-count self-check inside the pre-pass, where the full cross-crate definition set is in hand

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The pre-pass itself lives in leaf-core (post-processor infrastructure) with no discovery surface, but it is the realization MOST tightly bound to cross-crate completeness because it makes a DURABLE decision (the effective_primary flag) from the count of regulars-vs-fallbacks ACROSS ALL CRATES. The hard requirement: the pre-pass must run AFTER every contributing crate's definitions are collected into the registry (static linkme/inventory registry + any dynamic directory-scanned/WASM-module source) and BEFORE eager instantiation — so the two-source duality directly constrains pre-pass TIMING (a dynamic source registering after the pre-pass would invalidate effective_primary; CANDIDATE not decision: either gate the dynamic source to pre-pass time, or make the flag recomputable on dynamic registration). Layer-0/A/B silent DCE (cross-crate doc 135-152) is even MORE dangerous here than in R1: a dropped regular-bean crate doesn't just shrink a live set, it bakes a WRONG effective_primary into the frozen definitions (a @Fallback library default permanently marked effective because its app-side overrider's crate was DCE'd) — so the pre-pass is the natural and mandatory site for the expected-count anti-DCE self-check (cross-crate doc line 152's 'must add a self-check or users ship binaries silently missing components'), turning a silent wrong-default into a loud assembly-time failure. The is_primary/is_fallback flags arrive the same way as R1 (thin macro, absolute paths, const). Cross-context (parent/child) effective-primary is computed per-context over the hierarchy-merged type group, a single-process concern. Generics-cannot-register bounds each type group's membership upstream.

**T2 — execution context:** Two distinct execution profiles. The PRE-PASS runs ONCE at refresh time on the bootstrap thread (cold path) — it is synchronous, CPU-only, may iterate all definitions (O(types x candidates)), never awaits/blocks/spawns, and writes the derived effective_primary into definitions that are then FROZEN (safe publication; after this point everything is immutable, charter #5). It is a cold-path computation so polling/iteration is acceptable (charter #4 permits cold-path work). The LIVE SELECTION LAYER (the 'unique effective primary?' check at resolution) is then even cheaper than R1: pure, synchronous, allocation-free, lock-free read of immutable frozen flags — ideal for the warm prototype/scoped re-resolution path. As in R1, selection PICKS but never CONSTRUCTS; the winner's async boxed-future build is owned by bean-instantiation downstream (may .await, must not block; spawn_blocking for blocking factories — Phase 2 owns propagation). Cancellation after selection leaks nothing. The two-primaries / local-beats-parent conflict, if detected at the pre-pass validation step, surfaces as a rich error at refresh (the earliest rich timing, charter #9) rather than at first injection; if a dynamic late registration is permitted and triggers recompute, that recompute MUST run on a cold/setup path and re-freeze before serving resolutions (a real execution-context discipline point — no recompute concurrent with live lock-free reads). No async-Drop / teardown concern for the flags themselves.

#### R3 — Layered verdict primitives shared with autowiring-resolution: @Primary and @Fallback as two composable narrowing operators in one selection-trace pipeline (unification-first, diagnostics-native)
Stop treating primary and fallback as one fused two-pass and instead express them as TWO small, independently-testable narrowing OPERATORS that plug into autowiring-resolution's selection pipeline (mirroring R2-of-the-autowiring-resolution-design's `SelectionLayer`/`LayerVerdict` shape, so the two features share ONE driver and ONE verdict vocabulary). Define `enum LayerVerdict<'a> { Unique(&'a Candidate), Narrowed(SmallVec<&'a Candidate>), Conflict(LayerConflict<'a>), Abstain }`. THIS feature contributes two pure-fn layers to the fixed const LAYERS array autowiring-resolution owns: (1) `FallbackDemote` (runs FIRST among the selection layers, before primary, because excluding fallbacks shrinks the set primary then operates on): partition into regulars/fallbacks; if some regulars exist => `Narrowed(regulars)` (drop fallbacks); if NO regulars exist => `Abstain` (every candidate is a fallback — fall through unchanged so a later layer can still pick one); never returns Unique on its own (it only narrows). (2) `PrimaryPromote` (runs next): among the (already fallback-filtered) set, find unique @Primary with local-beats-parent min-depth tie-break; exactly one => `Unique`; two at same min depth => `Conflict(LayerConflict::MultiplePrimary{..})`; zero primary but the set is already a singleton (the sole regular after fallback-demote) => `Unique` (the zero-annotation auto-win emerges from FallbackDemote having narrowed to one, then PrimaryPromote passing it through); zero primary and >1 => `Abstain` (hand the narrowed set to the next layer — name/@Priority/etc.). The shared DRIVER folds the set through the const-ordered layers and RECORDS a trace `SmallVec<(LayerName, before_count, after_count, note)>` so a NoUniqueBean diagnostic reads 'type matched 3; FallbackDemote dropped 1 library default -> 2; PrimaryPromote found 0 primaries -> abstain; NameMatch 0; ... AMBIGUOUS among [appA@crateX, appB@crateY]'. The trace is gated to the >1 / error path so the warm len<=1 path stays allocation-free. The ORDER (FallbackDemote then PrimaryPromote) is FIXED const data, not user-reorderable — the contract — but each operator is an isolated pure fn this feature owns and unit-tests against Spring's documented behavior.

**Pros:** Maximal unification with autowiring-resolution (charter #11): @Primary and @Fallback become two layers in the SAME pipeline / SAME verdict vocabulary as the name/@Priority/default/resolvable layers, so there is ONE selection driver and ONE LayerVerdict enum across both features — no bespoke fused two-pass; the catalog's deliberate 'this feature is autowiring-resolution's layer 5a' relationship is realized structurally as literal shared layers.; Best diagnostics of the three (charter #9): the layer-by-layer trace IS the candidates-considered narrative for free — the cross-MODULE @Fallback story becomes legible ('FallbackDemote dropped libDefault@starterCrate; appBean@appCrate wins') and the two-primaries conflict names both offenders with their crates — directly serving the killer cross-module-override use case's debuggability.; Decomposing into FallbackDemote + PrimaryPromote separates the two genuinely-distinct policies (demote-the-defaults vs promote-one) into independently testable units with tiny contracts (set in, verdict out) — the highest-risk parts (the all-fallback Abstain branch, the local-beats-parent min-depth, the zero-annotation-emergent-win) each become a checkable property rather than a branch buried in one function.; The FIXED order is explicit auditable const data (LAYERS = [FallbackDemote, PrimaryPromote, NameMatch, ...]) shared with autowiring-resolution — 'selection order is fixed, filtering is pluggable' is visually obvious, and there is ZERO cross-crate extensibility into selection (a downstream crate cannot add a selection layer; the contract forbids it), keeping this a closed stable kernel (charter §6 small-stable-kernel).; The zero-annotation-regular-wins behavior emerges COMPOSITIONALLY (FallbackDemote narrows to one, PrimaryPromote passes the singleton through) rather than as a special-cased branch — a cleaner expression of 'the sole regular effectively becomes primary' than R1's explicit len==1 check, and it means fallback-demotion is correct even when combined with later layers.; Abstain != Conflict != Narrowed is a precise, shared distinction: a layer that finds no primaries ABSTAINS (fall through), one that finds two primaries CONFLICTS (hard fail), one that drops fallbacks NARROWS — encoding the fail-fast-only-when-truly-ambiguous philosophy in one place (the driver's terminal rule), reused by every layer.

**Cons:** More machinery and indirection than R1's single function for what IS a fixed two-pass — a shared verdict enum + two layer fns + a driver is more surface, and it risks LOOKING like a pluggable strategy engine when the whole point is the order is NOT pluggable (must be documented loudly as 'fixed contract, decomposed for testability/diagnostics/unification', not an extension seam).; Tightly couples this feature's realization to autowiring-resolution's realization CHOICE: R3 only makes sense if autowiring-resolution also chose its layered-pipeline realization (its R2). If autowiring-resolution ends up a hand-written ladder (its R1), this feature's two-layer decomposition has no shared driver to plug into and the unification win evaporates — so R3 is a JOINT decision with the neighbour, not independently selectable (a Phase-2/3 entanglement, not a standalone pick).; The FallbackDemote-then-PrimaryPromote ORDER differs subtly from Spring's literal determinePrimaryCandidate (which checks primary FIRST, then non-fallback as pass 2). Decomposing into demote-first-then-promote produces the SAME outcomes (a fallback can never be primary in practice, and primary-among-regulars == primary-overall when no primary is itself a fallback) BUT requires a proof/property-test that the reordered composition is outcome-equivalent to Spring's two-pass — a divergence-bug class that the fused R1 avoids by transcribing Spring's exact order.; Carrying a trace on the pipeline is a small overhead even gated to the >1 path (a branch on every resolution to decide whether to record) — must be carefully kept off the warm len<=1 path or it dents low-overhead (charter #2); and the trace's per-candidate crate-provenance (for the legible cross-module diagnostic) means each Candidate must carry source-crate metadata, widening the Candidate surface.; An edge case the demote-first ordering must handle: what if there are explicit @Primary beans that are THEMSELVES @Fallback? Spring's pass-1-primary-first would consider them; demote-first drops them before primary runs. This (admittedly pathological) combination needs an explicit decision (Spring's order implies a fallback-primary could still be promoted; demote-first would exclude it) — a semantic corner R1's faithful order resolves for free.; Slightly further from the practitioner mental model of determinePrimaryCandidate as one method — two layers + a driver is a translation cost for someone cross-referencing Spring source, though the explicit LAYERS array arguably reads better than a nested two-pass.

**Mechanisms considered:** @Primary and @Fallback as TWO composable pure-fn narrowing layers (FallbackDemote, PrimaryPromote) in autowiring-resolution's shared const LAYERS pipeline; shared `enum LayerVerdict { Unique | Narrowed | Conflict | Abstain }` vocabulary across both features (one driver, one verdict enum); FallbackDemote-first-then-PrimaryPromote ordering with a proof of outcome-equivalence to Spring's primary-first two-pass; zero-annotation-regular-wins emerging compositionally (demote narrows to one -> promote passes the singleton through) rather than a special len==1 branch; per-layer narrowing trace (before/after counts + crate provenance) gated to the >1 / error path for the legible cross-module diagnostic; local-beats-parent via min-hierarchy_depth inside PrimaryPromote; Abstain vs Conflict vs Narrowed encoding fail-fast in the driver

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Same core as R1/R2: the layers and driver live entirely in leaf-core as const/static pure fns with no cross-crate discovery surface or DCE hazard of their own. A T1 ADVANTAGE over R1: because the trace can carry per-Candidate source-crate provenance, a NoUniqueBean across module boundaries NAMES the offending crates ('two non-fallback DataSource beans: appA@crate_x, appB@crate_y') and the cross-module @Fallback override is legible in the trace ('FallbackDemote dropped defaultDataSource@starter_crate') — directly serving the killer cross-module use case's diagnosability, the most cross-crate-relevant property of THIS feature. The is_primary/is_fallback flags + source-crate metadata are const data the thin macro emits per bean (absolute ::leaf_core paths). The SAME Layer-0/A/B silent-DCE hazard as R1 applies (a dropped regular-bean crate shrinks the set and lets a fallback win), so the upstream anti-DCE expected-count self-check is a precondition — but R3's trace makes the SYMPTOM most legible ('expected 3 candidates of type T from crates [..], set contained 2; the missing one would have been the non-fallback override'). The const LAYERS array being a single leaf-core kernel means NO downstream crate can contribute a selection layer (the contract forbids it) — selection stays a closed stable kernel while only FILTERING (candidate-resolver) is cross-crate-pluggable (charter §6 topology). The two-source duality (static linkme/inventory + dynamic WASM-module) is invisible to the layers (both feed Candidates to the set), with the same requirement as R1/R2 that the full set exist before FallbackDemote runs (CANDIDATES, not a decision). Generics-cannot-register bounds the set upstream.

**T2 — execution context:** Identical execution profile to R1 with the trace addition: the fold + both layer fns are pure, synchronous, CPU-only, non-blocking, run at wiring time (cold for eager singletons, warm for prototype/scoped re-resolution) over immutable-after-refresh data read lock-free (no global lock, charter #5). The ONE extra cost vs R1 is the trace SmallVec — kept off the hot path by recording it ONLY when the pipeline enters the >1 / conflict path (the len<=1 fast path returns Unique before any trace allocation), so the common warm case stays allocation-free and branch-only (charter #4 reactive-on-hot-paths: pure computation, never poll/busy-wait; charter #2). Each layer MUST be documented as pure/sync/non-blocking (it only inspects const flags + depth + compares counts) — the driver contract forbids a layer that awaits or blocks, exactly like candidate-resolver's filter-purity rule and autowiring-resolution's layer-purity rule. As in R1/R2 selection PICKS/NARROWS but never CONSTRUCTS; the winner's async boxed-future build (AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe -> boxed future at the dyn boundary) is owned by bean-instantiation, which awaits downstream (may .await for DB pools, must not block; spawn_blocking for blocking factories — Phase 2 owns propagation). Cancellation after selection leaks nothing (borrows + a const array). The MultiplePrimary Conflict surfaces as a rich error (with the layer trace + offending crates) fail-fast at eager-instantiate / first-acquisition (charter #9). No teardown concern.

### Rust clashes
- Runtime reflection of primary/fallback status vs declaration-site capture: Spring reads each bean's primary/fallback flag reflectively off its live BeanDefinition at resolution time (and re-reads it freely). Rust has no runtime annotation reflection — the is_primary/is_fallback flags MUST be captured at the @Component/@Bean declaration site by the thin macro as CONST bools and frozen onto each candidate's merged definition. The flags are whatever the macro emitted at compile time; there is no live re-inspection. This is straightforward for the flags themselves, but it means a bean's primary/fallback status is fixed at build time and cannot be toggled by runtime container state the way reflective code could in principle.
- @Fallback is fundamentally COUNT-AND-CROSS-CRATE-dependent and CANNOT be a compile-time/local decision — the single sharpest clash for THIS feature. 'A fallback wins only if there is no non-fallback candidate' depends on the COMPLETE set of same-type candidates assembled from EVERY linked crate (and any dynamic source), which no single declaration site, no proc-macro (sees only its own invocation), and no build.rs (sees only its own crate) can know. So unlike type/generic/explicit-qualifier disambiguation (which CAN be lifted into a typed key), fallback-exclusion MUST be evaluated at runtime/assembly over the fully-collected set. This makes primary-fallback the most cross-crate-coupled selection layer, structurally unable to fail-fast at compile time, and acutely exposed to silent Layer-0 DCE (a dropped regular-bean crate silently lets a library @Fallback win — a wrong default shipped with no error).
- The 'sole regular effectively becomes primary by type' rule is per-TYPE-KEY, not per-bean — a bean can be the unique non-fallback for one injection type yet one-of-many for another (a DataSourceImpl injectable as DataSource AND Closeable, each a separately-registered type-index view recovered via stable-1.86 trait-upcasting). So 'effective primary' cannot be a single bool on the bean (clashing with R2's simple-flag promise); it is a relation between a bean and a TYPE-KEY, and the fallback arithmetic must be evaluated per type-key. In Spring this is implicit (resolution is always type-scoped); in Rust the per-type-view candidate sets are explicit registrations, so the per-type-key scoping must be made explicit.
- Two-local-primaries => NoUniqueBean has no exception-throw analogue and must be a constructed value: Spring throws NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException('more than one primary bean found') with stack-unwinding. Rust has no exceptions — the PrimaryConflict must be an explicit value carrying the offending primary candidates + their hierarchy depths + the type-key, returned via the verdict enum and re-wrapped by the caller (autowiring-resolution -> bean-instantiation) into the dependent's assembly-chain error. The fail-fast 'refuse to guess' becomes a non-coercible Conflict/Ambiguous variant (strictly better than an exception) but forces explicit three-way (promoted/narrowed/conflict) handling at the call site rather than letting an exception propagate implicitly.
- Local-primary-beats-parent-primary has no automatic BeanFactory-hierarchy-walk analogue: Spring's determinePrimaryCandidate knows a candidate's owning context and a local primary beats a parent-context primary via the live HierarchicalBeanFactory chain. Rust has no implicit context hierarchy at the candidate — the hierarchy_depth (0=local, increasing toward root) must be captured/labeled onto each Candidate by container-hierarchy when it merges the parent-delegated find results, and 'local beats parent' becomes an explicit min-depth scan over the primary subset. Mislabeled depth silently flips which primary wins; the coupling between container-hierarchy's candidate-labeling and this feature's tie-break is a real, non-cosmetic dependency.
- @Primary/@Fallback NEVER collapsing collections must be a STRUCTURAL non-interaction, not a runtime guard: in Spring it is an emergent fact that single-valued selection runs determinePrimaryCandidate while collection injection takes all matches. In leaf this must be designed in — the collection-injection path must NEVER call this feature's selection function at all (a List<T>/Map<String,T>/array gets every type-matching candidate regardless of primary/fallback), so the boundary is 'arity decides whether this feature runs', captured at the injection point (Single vs Collection) by the macro. Getting this wrong (e.g. applying primary-promotion before building a collection) would silently drop candidates from a List — a correctness bug Spring's separation prevents by construction.
- @Priority cannot model factory-method ordering, so the @Order+@Primary/@Fallback substitution carries into Rust: Spring's @Priority is class-level-only and cannot annotate @Bean methods, so factory-method beans express the same intent with @Order PLUS @Primary/@Fallback (lines 743, 2624). leaf must ensure @Bean-method-equivalent (factory) beans can carry primary/fallback flags (they can — flags are per-definition) and that the @Priority-vs-@Order distinction (a neighbour concern in autowiring-resolution/collection-injection) does not bleed into this feature's primary/fallback flags — keeping them orthogonal const data on the definition.
- No freeze-early/AOT shape can pre-bake @Fallback the way it could pre-bake type/generic keys: any compile-time or build-time-frozen bean-graph snapshot (the deferred AOT lineage, charter §3) must already contain ALL candidates from ALL crates — including a dynamic directory-scanned/WASM-module source — BEFORE @Fallback exclusion can be correctly evaluated. A snapshot taken before a late dynamic registration would bake a wrong winner. This clashes with any aggressive freeze-early strategy and means the @Fallback decision point must sit AFTER full candidate assembly in whatever lifecycle/AOT model Phase 2 picks (a hard ordering constraint, not a free choice).

### Interactions
- **autowiring-resolution** — THE owning consumer. autowiring-resolution runs the full FIXED single-valued tie-break ladder; THIS feature IS its first selection layer ('5a' / determinePrimaryCandidate). The seam: autowiring-resolution hands this feature the qualifying set (already type/generic/qualifier-narrowed) and this feature returns a verdict — Promoted (a unique winner this layer decides), Narrowed (fallbacks excluded, hand the regular subset to the NEXT layer: name/@Priority/default/resolvable), or Conflict (two primaries => the caller's NoUniqueBean). This feature NEVER runs the later layers and NEVER filters by qualifier/generic (that is upstream). In R3 the relationship is literal shared layers in one pipeline; in R1/R2 it is a function the ladder calls. This feature's outcome is also what @ConditionalOnSingleCandidate queries (a @Primary among several makes resolution succeed).
- **candidate-resolver** — Upstream producer of the SET this feature partitions. candidate-resolver does phase-1 FILTERING (autowire-candidate flag, generics-as-implicit-qualifier, @Qualifier/custom-qualifier matching, the three-valued match/veto/abstain) and yields the QUALIFYING set; THIS feature does the @Primary/@Fallback slice of phase-2 SELECTION over that set. candidate-resolver answers 'what qualifies'; this feature answers (partially) 'which qualifier wins / which defaults to demote'. The set must arrive carrying per-candidate primary/fallback flags + hierarchy depth (+ source-crate provenance for diagnostics) so this feature can partition without re-filtering.
- **bean-definition** — Pure consumer of the metamodel. The is_primary and is_fallback flags this feature reads are frozen on the MergedBeanDefinition by bean-definition (lines 156, 175-177: 'primary' and 'fallback' are first-class definition fields, merged through the parent template chain). This feature adds no metadata of its own; the Candidate it operates over is a read-only view of those frozen flags. R2 additionally WRITES a derived effective_primary back into the definition set as a blueprint edit.
- **bean-registry** — WHERE this feature runs and reads from. bean-registry produces the candidate set (candidates_for_type over its type index, filtered through candidate-resolver), and its freeze-after-refresh boundary is what makes the candidate set + primary/fallback flags immutable and lock-free-readable during concurrent resolution. R2's effective-primary pre-pass is a transform OVER the registry's definitions slotted into refresh. The registry's anti-DCE expected-count self-check is a correctness PRECONDITION for this feature's count-dependent fallback arithmetic.
- **container-hierarchy** — Supplies the local-beats-parent precedence. container-hierarchy defines one-directional visibility (child sees parent, local shadows parent) and merges parent-delegated candidate finds; it must LABEL each Candidate with a hierarchy_depth (0=local, increasing toward root) so this feature's PASS-1 primary tie-break can apply 'a local @Primary beats a parent-context primary' via a min-depth scan over the primary subset. Two primaries at the SAME min depth (both local) is the NoUniqueBean conflict; a local primary at depth 0 beating a parent primary at depth 1 is the resolution this feature performs using that label.
- **collection-injection** — The load-bearing NON-interaction. @Primary/@Fallback affect SINGLE-VALUED injection ONLY (lines 722, 2622) — a List/Set/array/Map injection takes ALL qualifying candidates and MUST BYPASS this feature's selection entirely. The boundary is structural: arity (Single vs Collection, captured at the injection point) decides whether this feature runs at all. A collection point never partitions out fallbacks or promotes a primary — it gets every match. This must be designed in (not a runtime guard) or a List<T> would silently lose candidates.
- **auto-configuration** — The killer use case's downstream beneficiary. @Fallback is 'the auto-configuration override story made declarative' (lines 63, 2624, 718): a starter/auto-config ships a @Fallback default that the application's plain bean transparently supersedes with zero annotations — the same backoff @ConditionalOnMissingBean provides, but at SELECTION time rather than registration time. auto-configuration relies on this feature to make its shipped defaults yield to user beans; the cross-MODULE override (library crate's fallback demoted by app crate's regular) is exactly what this feature's fallback partition realizes over the cross-crate candidate set.
- **bean-instantiation** — Downstream consumer of the verdict and owner of the async boundary. This feature SELECTS/NARROWS (sync, pure) and returns a Candidate (or hands a narrowed subset back to autowiring-resolution's later layers); bean-instantiation CONSTRUCTS the eventual winner (async, boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary, may .await, must not block). bean-instantiation also re-wraps this feature's PrimaryConflict into the dependent bean's assembly-chain error (the UnsatisfiedDependency analogue). Selection is sync; construction-of-the-winner is async — this feature never crosses that boundary.
- **post-processor-spi** — R2's realization home. If @Fallback is resolved by a one-time definition-rewriting pre-pass (computing effective_primary before instantiation), that pre-pass IS a BeanDefinitionRegistryPostProcessor / MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor — squarely post-processor-spi's 'edit the blueprint vs decorate the product' split, dogfooding leaf's own feature as ordered infrastructure (Spring's philosophy). post-processor-spi defines the ordered-infrastructure-bean contract and the BDRPP/MBDPP hooks; this feature's R2 slots into it. (R1/R3 do NOT use this — they evaluate fallback live during selection.)
- **refresh-lifecycle** — Timing constraint for the cross-crate completeness of @Fallback.
