The @Fallback decision (live in R1/R3, pre-pass in R2) must occur AFTER all definitions from all crates (static + any dynamic source) are loaded and BEFORE the chosen winner is instantiated — so refresh-lifecycle's fixed-order template (load definitions -> BFPPs -> register BPPs -> eager-instantiate) is where this feature's correctness window lives. R2's pre-pass needs a specific ordered slot (after definition-adding post-processors, before instantiation); R1/R3 inherit the constraint that eager-instantiate must not begin until the set is complete.
- **bean-naming** — Adjacent tie-break (NOT this feature's). The bean-name-vs-injection-point-name match is the tie-break layer that runs AFTER this feature when it returns Narrowed (no unique primary, multiple regulars survive fallback-exclusion). bean-naming supplies the names/aliases that layer uses. This feature does NOT do name matching; it only hands the fallback-filtered subset to the name-matching layer. The interaction is sequential within autowiring-resolution's ladder: fallback/primary first, name match next on the survivors.
- **deferral-primitives** — Shares the single-valued selection underneath. ObjectProvider.getIfUnique() and jakarta Provider honor @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate (line 2576) — getIfUnique resolves cleanly when this feature can promote a unique primary OR when fallback-exclusion leaves exactly one regular. deferral-primitives owns the 0..1/0..N/lazy access shapes; this feature owns the primary/fallback slice of the single-valued selection beneath getObject()/getIfUnique(). The arity (Single vs Collection vs Optional) deferral-primitives captures also decides whether this feature runs (collections bypass it).

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Live-during-selection (R1/R3) vs pre-computed-effective-primary-at-assembly (R2) — or a blend (R2 pre-pass for the cross-crate fallback-exclusion that is genuinely once-and-global, R1/R3 live evaluation for explicit-@Primary + local-beats-parent which is hierarchy-and-injection-point-relative)? This keystone shape decision is entangled with the compile-vs-runtime split (§3) and the bean-registry freeze model: pre-computing fallback-exclusion only works if the full candidate set is frozen before any resolution, which constrains the AOT lineage and the dynamic-source timing.
- WHERE does the complete candidate set become available, and is @Fallback's evaluation point provably AFTER it? @Fallback's correctness REQUIRES the fully-collected set from all linked crates plus any dynamic (WASM-module) source before the winner is chosen. How does this reconcile with (a) any freeze-early/AOT snapshot (must include late dynamic registrations), (b) lazy/scoped beans resolved long after refresh (the set must still be complete), and (c) auto-configuration's deferred-import ordering? Couples to refresh-lifecycle, bean-registry's freeze model, the cross-crate two-source duality, and the metadata/codegen boundary.
- R3's unification (shared LAYERS pipeline + LayerVerdict with autowiring-resolution) is only selectable if autowiring-resolution ALSO chose its layered-pipeline realization — so the realization of THIS feature is JOINTLY decided with autowiring-resolution, not independently. Should Phase 3 commit to the shared-pipeline shape across both features (maximal unification, charter #11) or keep primary-fallback a fused two-pass even if autowiring-resolution is a ladder? Shares the unification meta-concept.
- How is the per-TYPE-KEY scoping of 'sole regular effectively becomes primary' realized given a bean satisfies multiple injection types (DataSource AND Closeable via trait-upcasting)? Is effective-primary a per-(bean,type-key) relation in a derived map (R2), or recomputed live per type-key at resolution (R1/R3)? This couples to the type-erasure/registry-substrate keystone (how dyn beans are keyed per type-view) and determines whether R2's compute-once win survives.
- The anti-DCE expected-count self-check is a hard PRECONDITION specifically for this feature's count-dependent fallback arithmetic (a dropped regular-bean crate silently lets a library @Fallback win). Where does that self-check live (build-time manifest cross-check vs runtime expected-count assertion at assembly), what granularity (per-type-key counts so the @Fallback miss is detectable), and does it run before this feature's decision point? Shares the cross-crate composition model and the unified-diagnostics model.
- hierarchy_depth labeling contract with container-hierarchy: how does the parent-delegated candidate find label each Candidate's depth so local-beats-parent is correct, and is depth a simple u16 or a richer context-identity? Couples to container-hierarchy's realization and the scope/ownership keystone (how parent-context candidates are referenced/borrowed across the hierarchy without forcing Arc).
- Diagnostic richness vs hot-path cost for the cross-MODULE @Fallback story (charter #9 vs #2): R3's per-layer trace with crate-provenance makes 'libDefault@starter dropped, appBean@app wins / two non-fallback beans across crates X,Y' legible — but carries source-crate metadata on every Candidate and a trace-gating branch on every resolution. Is the candidates-considered + crate-provenance chain always materialized, only on the >1/error path, or behind a diagnostic flag? Shares the unified-diagnostics meta-concept and cross-crate composition model.
- Should leaf offer ANY escape hatch from the fixed primary/fallback semantics (charter #2.10 'escape hatches at every level' vs Spring's deliberately-fixed contract)? R1/R3 are intentionally non-pluggable (the order is the contract); but charter §2.10 wants a programmatic fallback at every level. Is the escape hatch at the DEFINITION level (programmatically set primary/fallback flags via programmatic-registration) rather than at the SELECTION level (no reordering the tie-break)? Couples to programmatic-registration and the fixed-contract-vs-escape-hatch tension.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the dedicated section (lines 707-722: determinePrimaryCandidate two-pass — unique @Primary first with two-local-primaries=NoUniqueBean and local-beats-parent, then unique non-@Fallback; the @Fallback inverse-of-@Primary 'demote defaults' semantics and the 'sole regular effectively becomes primary by type with zero annotations' rule; the cross-MODULE-override killer use case with the @Bean @Fallback DataSource example; the carried-from-6.2 provenance; and the CRUCIAL caveat that @Primary/@Fallback NEVER collapse collections), the bean-definition mentions (lines 156, 175-177: primary/fallback as first-class merged-definition fields + the @Fallback-as-autowire-candidate hidden concept + provenance note), the autowiring-resolution context this sub-policy sits inside (lines 680-705: the six-step layered policy where @Primary/@Fallback is step 4, the fail-fast soul, the determineAutowireCandidate ordering placing primary first), and the candidate-resolution-engine summary (lines 2618-2624: the explicit selection order 'primary (and unique non-fallback) -> priority -> default-candidate -> name', the single-injection-point-only caveat, and @Fallback as the declarative auto-config-override story). I grounded the @Fallback=auto-config-override link at lines 63 + 718 + 2624, and the @Priority-class-level-only / model-with-@Order+@Primary/@Fallback fact at 743/2624.

THE most important framing I held throughout, and the one that distinguishes THIS feature from autowiring-resolution: primary-fallback is NOT the whole tie-break ladder — it is the @Primary-promote-one / @Fallback-demote-the-defaults SUB-POLICY that is autowiring-resolution's FIRST selection layer ('5a' / determinePrimaryCandidate). So all three realizations are about how @Primary/@Fallback are CAPTURED (const flags via thin macro), REPRESENTED (per-definition + hierarchy depth), and APPLIED (the two-pass: promote-unique-primary-with-local-beats-parent, else narrow-to-non-fallbacks-where-a-sole-regular-auto-wins) — NOT about reinventing the name/@Priority/default/resolvable tie-breaks (those belong to autowiring-resolution and run AFTER this feature returns Narrowed). I kept the feature's slice clean and deferred everything else to the neighbour.

The single sharpest design truth for THIS feature: @Fallback is COUNT-AND-CROSS-CRATE-dependent ('demote unless I am the only non-fallback'), so unlike type/generic/explicit-qualifier disambiguation it CANNOT be a local or compile-time decision — it MUST be evaluated at runtime/assembly over the FULLY-collected candidate set from every linked crate (plus any dynamic source), making primary-fallback the most cross-crate-coupled and most DCE-exposed selection layer of the whole algorithm. A dropped regular-bean crate silently lets a library @Fallback win (a wrong default shipped, no error) — so the upstream anti-DCE expected-count self-check is a hard correctness PRECONDITION specifically here, and I flagged it in every realization's T1 and as a phase2Question.

The three realizations are genuinely divergent on WHEN and HOW the fallback arithmetic runs: R1 = a hand-written two-pass pure function transcribing determinePrimaryCandidate, called live during selection (highest fidelity, simplest, but evaluates the count-dependent partition on every resolution and offers no built-in trace); R2 = lift the count-dependent fallback-exclusion OUT of the hot path into a one-time definition-rewriting pre-pass that computes a derived 'effective_primary' at assembly (dogfooding post-processor-spi, lowest warm-path cost, materializes Spring's own 'effectively becomes primary by type' words — but the effective flag must be per-type-key not per-bean because beans satisfy multiple type-views, and a dynamic late registration invalidates it, denting the compute-once win and tightly coupling to the freeze model); R3 = decompose @Primary and @Fallback into TWO composable narrowing layers (FallbackDemote + PrimaryPromote) sharing autowiring-resolution's LAYERS pipeline + LayerVerdict vocabulary (maximal unification + best cross-module diagnostics via a per-layer crate-provenance trace — but only selectable if autowiring-resolution ALSO chose its layered-pipeline realization, so it is a JOINT decision, and the demote-first-then-promote reordering needs an outcome-equivalence proof against Spring's primary-first two-pass). R1↔R2 differ on live-vs-precomputed; R3 differs on fused-vs-decomposed-and-shared and is partially entangled with the neighbour's realization — a likely Phase-3 blend is R2's pre-pass for the once-and-global fallback-exclusion + R1/R3 live evaluation for explicit-@Primary and local-beats-parent (which are hierarchy/injection-point-relative, not global).

Three things to carry forward: (1) @Fallback's cross-MODULE-override is the KILLER use case and the auto-configuration-override story made declarative — it cannot be compile-time, must run over the complete cross-crate set, and is acutely DCE-exposed (the dominant tension). (2) @Primary/@Fallback NEVER collapse collections must be a STRUCTURAL non-interaction (arity decides whether this feature runs at all), not a runtime guard, or a List<T> silently loses candidates. (3) local-primary-beats-parent-primary requires container-hierarchy to label each Candidate with a hierarchy_depth, and two-local-primaries is the NoUniqueBean('more than one primary') conflict — both realized via a min-depth scan over the primary subset.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization keeps the bean handle an abstract origin-agnostic obtainer (the scope/ownership keystone), keeps winner CONSTRUCTION an async boxed-future step owned by bean-instantiation (this feature does only sync selection/narrowing), keeps the candidate-set ownership/shape abstract (borrow vs Arc-slice — deferred to candidate-resolver/registry), stays substrate-neutral on how candidates were discovered (the static-linkme/inventory + dynamic-WASM-module two-source duality is invisible here — both feed the set), and does NOT decide compile-vs-runtime or the metadata/codegen boundary. Naming ('Candidate', 'PfVerdict', 'Promoted/Narrowed/Ambiguous', 'PrimaryConflict', 'effective_primary', 'FallbackDemote/PrimaryPromote', 'LayerVerdict', 'hierarchy_depth') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (707-722 the @Primary/@Fallback two-pass + cross-module override + never-collapse-collections caveat; 150-177 primary/fallback as merged-definition fields + @Fallback-autowire-candidate hidden concept + 6.2 provenance; 680-705 the layered single-valued policy this is step 4 of + fail-fast soul; 2618-2624 the fixed selection order + single-point-only + @Fallback-as-auto-config-override; 63/718 the overridable-defaults-yield-to-user-intent philosophy; 743 @Priority-class-only / model-with-@Order+@Primary/@Fallback); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3, topology §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/A/B silent-DCE 135-152 + the self-check mandate line 152, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI 96-102, generics-cannot-register 153, linkme-default 166-168, scope-discovery-not-global 36, two-source/static+dynamic topology); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (stable envelope: trait-upcasting 1.86 for multi-type-view candidates, AFIT/RPITIT-not-dyn-safe -> boxed futures at the construction boundary, const TypeId 1.91, Any+downcast, no async Drop).

## Collection / Array / Map Injection & Ordering  `[collection-injection]`

This feature owns MULTI-VALUED injection: when an injection point asks for a List/Set/array of T (or a Map<name,T>), it receives ALL beans whose type matches, NOT one selected winner. It is the deliberate STRUCTURAL COMPLEMENT to autowiring-resolution (which owns single-valued selection): the two are mutually exclusive by injection-point arity, and the load-bearing non-interaction is that @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate NEVER collapse a collection — a List<T>/Set<T>/Map<name,T> takes every type-matching candidate regardless of which is primary/fallback (spring doc 722, 2622). The intents to replicate (intent, NOT JVM mechanism):\n\n(a) AGGREGATION not selection. The qualifying set (produced by candidate-resolver via type-match + generics-as-implicit-qualifier + @Qualifier filtering) is collected WHOLE into the target container. Generic narrowing still applies elementwise: List<Store<Integer>> receives only Store<Integer> beans, excluding Store<String> (doc 728) — the generic param is an implicit per-element qualifier, identical to single-valued matching but applied as a FILTER over the whole set rather than a tie-break.\n\n(b) ORDERING is a CONSUMER concern, STRICTLY SEPARATE from startup/instantiation order. The injected List/Set/array is ordered by a SINGLE shared comparator (Spring: AnnotationAwareOrderComparator) with FIXED rules: lower value = higher precedence (HIGHEST_PRECEDENCE = i32::MIN, @Order defaults to LOWEST); a PriorityOrdered/Ordered-INTERFACE signal sorts ahead of and beats the @Order/@Priority ANNOTATION value (the runtime-computed/trait-implemented order is the more specific signal, doc 2634); absent any order signal, fall back to bean-definition REGISTRATION order (doc 734) — a stable tiebreak. This SAME comparator is what autowiring-resolution uses as its @Priority tie-break layer (doc 2632, 2640), so single-valued and collection ordering must NEVER diverge — one canonical mechanism.\n\n(c) The Map<String, T> (name-keyed): keys are bean NAMES, values are beans of T; only String keys supported (doc 734). THE most-missed gotcha: Spring's resolveMultipleBeanMap does NO sort — an injected Map follows REGISTRATION order and @Order/@Priority/Ordered have NO effect on it (doc 2638), whereas array/List/Set ARE sorted by the comparator. This array/List-vs-Map ordering asymmetry is a precise behaviour to replicate (or consciously reconcile in Rust where ordered maps are cheap).\n\n(d) @Order vs @Priority SCOPE asymmetry: @Order targets class AND factory-method (per-definition); @Priority (jakarta, type-level only) cannot annotate a @Bean/factory method (doc 743) — for factory beans you express ordering via @Order. And the orthogonality (doc 749, 2640): @Order sorts ALL (collection presentation), @Priority ALSO uniquely breaks single-valued ties (that part belongs to autowiring-resolution); @Order/Ordered do NOT break single-valued ties and NEITHER influences singleton startup order (governed only by real dependency edges + @DependsOn). Conflating the two would let a cosmetic hint silently reorder init and mask dependency bugs.\n\n(e) Optionality/arity: an empty qualifying set yields an EMPTY collection (not NoSuchBean) — multi-valued points are inherently 0..N and never fail on zero, distinct from single-valued required points.\n\nDeepest Rust impedance: Spring reads @Order/@Priority/Ordered reflectively off live beans + factory methods and matches generics via runtime ResolvableType; Rust has none of that, so the order key (an i32 + an interface-vs-annotation provenance flag) and the per-element generic-arg TypeIds must be captured at the declaration site by the thin macro and frozen onto each candidate's definition; the "Ordered interface beats annotation" precedence has no runtime analogue and must be a distinct captured trait-implemented signal, not just the numeric value; and the injection-point's container shape (Vec/HashSet/[T;N]/HashMap, element type, Map-or-not) must be captured statically since there is no reflective Collection/Map detection.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Aggregate-then-sort over a borrowed qualifying set: collection injection as a hand-written total function that COLLECTS all candidates and applies the one shared OrderComparator (Spring-faithful, array/List-vs-Map asymmetry preserved)
Mirror Spring's resolveMultipleBeans / resolveMultipleBeanMap directly: collection injection is a hand-written function on the bean-registry/context that, given a multi-valued InjectionPoint, takes the WHOLE qualifying set from candidate-resolver (no selection, no tie-break — that is autowiring-resolution's job) and materializes the requested container. Inputs: a multi-valued `InjectionPoint` statically captured by the thin macro at the @Autowired site — `element_type: TypeId`, `element_generic_args: &[TypeId]` (for List<Store<i32>> elementwise narrowing), `shape: CollectionShape { Vec | HashSet | Array(n) | Map }`, and (for Array) the const length n — plus the borrowed qualifying set `&[Candidate<'_>]` where each `Candidate` exposes the bean's order key. The order key is a SHARED `OrderKey` value-type frozen on every MergedBeanDefinition by the macro: `struct OrderKey { value: i32 /* default LOWEST */, source: OrderSource }` where `enum OrderSource { OrderedInterface | PriorityAnnotation | OrderAnnotation | None }` capturing the interface-beats-annotation precedence as DATA (the trait-implemented Ordered signal is a distinct captured flag, NOT just the i32). The function: (1) COLLECT — clone the candidate handles into the working set (every match, regardless of primary/fallback/default — those flags are simply not read here); (2) for List/Set/Array, SORT via the ONE canonical `order_cmp(a: &OrderKey, b: &OrderKey)` (the same comparator autowiring-resolution calls for its @Priority tie-break): interface-source sorts ahead of annotation-source before numerics compare, then lower i32 wins, then registration-index as the stable final tiebreak; (3) MATERIALIZE — Vec from the sorted slice, HashSet by inserting (Set drops order — document that Set is unordered-by-container even if sorted), Array by length-checking (n must equal candidate count or it is a rich error), Map by pairing bean-NAME -> handle. For Map, faithfully replicate Spring's NO-SORT (registration order) — but FLAG in T1/phase2 that Rust can cheaply offer an ordered map (IndexMap-style insertion order = registration order, or BTreeMap by name) so the gotcha can be RECONCILED rather than inherited; the Phase-1 default is registration-order to match intent. Construction of each element's bean handle is the async step owned by bean-instantiation; this function returns an ordered/keyed list of Candidate handle-obtainers and the caller awaits each build (or the whole set concurrently). Empty set -> empty container, never an error. The function is sync, allocation-light (one Vec the size of the match set, only on multi-valued points which are rarer than single-valued), and produces a rich error only for the Array-length-mismatch and bean-name-collision-in-Map cases.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity and most teachable: COLLECT-then-SORT-then-MATERIALIZE maps line-for-line onto resolveMultipleBeans, and the deliberate NON-reading of primary/fallback/default makes 'collections never collapse' a structural fact (the flags are simply absent from the function), exactly honoring doc 722/2622.; Reuses the ONE canonical order_cmp shared with autowiring-resolution's @Priority tie-break, so single-valued and collection ordering provably cannot diverge (charter #11 unification) — the comparator lives in one place and both features call it.; Captures the 'Ordered interface beats annotation' precedence as explicit OrderSource DATA rather than trying to emulate a runtime trait check — faithful to doc 2634 within Rust's no-reflection envelope, and auditable.; Clean producer/consumer seam: candidate-resolver FILTERS (incl. elementwise generic narrowing), this function only AGGREGATES + ORDERS; it never selects, so the deliberate complementarity with autowiring-resolution is preserved by construction (the two functions are reached by injection-point arity, never both).; Allocation-light and sync: one Vec sized to the match set on the (rarer) multi-valued path, no per-resolution allocation on the common single-valued path (which routes to autowiring-resolution instead); ordering is pure CPU branch-and-compare (charter #5/#4).; Preserves the array/List-vs-Map asymmetry as a conscious, documented choice — and surfaces the Rust opportunity to reconcile it (cheap ordered maps) as a phase2Question rather than silently inheriting a JVM gotcha.

**Cons:** A hand-written materializer per CollectionShape (Vec/Set/Array/Map) is several code paths; the Array-length-mismatch and Map-name-collision error cases are easy to get subtly wrong and need targeted tests.; Reading the OrderKey + registration-index off every Candidate widens the Candidate borrow surface (same coupling concern autowiring-resolution has) and ties this feature to the exact shape bean-definition freezes — any change to how order/registration-index is captured ripples into both features.; The Set-drops-order subtlety (a HashSet container cannot preserve the comparator order even though we sorted) is a real semantic wart that must be documented; users may expect a 'Set' to honor @Order and be surprised — Spring has the same issue but it is a latent confusion either way.; The Map no-sort fidelity vs Rust's cheap ordered maps is a genuine fork: matching Spring (registration order, HashMap) inherits the most-missed gotcha; choosing an ordered map diverges from documented Spring behaviour — R1 defers this to phase2 rather than resolving it, leaving a behavioural ambiguity.; Being a concrete function, it offers no seam for a user wanting a custom container type (e.g. a user newtype wrapper or a SmallVec) — faithful to Spring (only List/Set/array/Map) but inflexible if leaf later wants pluggable collection targets.

**Mechanisms considered:** hand-written total function `collect_multiple(&InjectionPoint, &[Candidate]) -> OrderedCandidates` mirroring resolveMultipleBeans (collect-all, no selection); shared `OrderKey { value: i32, source: OrderSource }` value-type + ONE canonical `order_cmp` reused by autowiring-resolution's @Priority tie-break (single source of ordering truth); `enum CollectionShape { Vec | HashSet | Array(usize) | Map }` captured statically by the macro (no reflective Collection/Map detection); registration-index frozen per definition as the stable final tiebreak (faithful to 'absent order signal -> registration order'); Map keyed by bean NAME -> handle; Phase-1 default registration-order (HashMap insertion) to match Spring's no-sort, with ordered-map reconciliation flagged; origin-agnostic Candidate handle-obtainer returned; element construction deferred to bean-instantiation's async boxed-future path

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** This feature owns NO cross-crate discovery of its own — the collect/sort/materialize function lives entirely in leaf-core and is a pure consumer of the qualifying set candidate-resolver produces over whatever discovery substrate Phase 2 picks (linkme distributed-slice / inventory / build.rs codegen / explicit). The CRITICAL cross-crate point is that 'all matching beans' is fundamentally a WHOLE-PROGRAM aggregation — exactly the classpath-scan analogue the cross-crate doc says Rust lacks — so a collection injection is the feature MOST exposed to the silent-DCE failure mode (doc lines 34/152): if a contributing crate is dropped (Layer 0: never path-referenced) or its registration is GC'd (Layer A rustc-reachability / Layer B linker --gc-sections), its bean silently never enters the set and the injected List/Set/Map is SILENTLY SHORT — no error, an under-populated collection, possibly differing debug-vs-release or by linker. This makes collection injection the canonical motivating case for the anti-DCE self-check (expected-count assertion / build-time manifest cross-check) the cross-crate doc mandates, and argues the empty/short-collection diagnostic should be able to say 'N beans of element-type T were registered from crates [..]; M reached this injection point'. The OrderKey (value + interface-vs-annotation source) and registration-index are const data the macro emits per bean using absolute ::leaf_core paths, so a remote-crate bean carries its own order metadata into the set with no central ordering table — but registration ORDER across crates is UNSPECIFIED for both linkme (link/section order) and inventory (constructor order) per doc 154, so the 'registration-order' fallback tiebreak is NOT stable cross-crate unless the descriptor carries an explicit deterministic ordering key (the doc's recommendation, line 377) — a real constraint this feature inherits: the registration-order fallback must be backed by a deterministic per-descriptor sequence number, not actual link order. Generics-as-elementwise-qualifier compares CONCRETE generic-arg TypeIds, so List<Store<RemoteType>> only collects beans whose concrete Store<RemoteType> was registered upstream (register_component!(Store<RemoteType>) escape hatch; generics-cannot-register, doc 153). The two-source duality (static link-time registry + optional dynamic directory-scanned/WASM-module source) is INVISIBLE to the materializer — both just contribute Candidates to the set being aggregated (CANDIDATES, not a decision); a WASM-module bean would appear in the List/Map identically if its descriptor reached the set.

**T2 — execution context:** SELECTION-equivalent here is AGGREGATION+ORDERING, which is PURE, SYNCHRONOUS, CPU-only, non-blocking, allocation-light: collect handle-obtainers, compare OrderKeys, materialize a Vec/Set/Map. It never awaits, does IO, or spawns. It runs at WIRING time: cold for eager singletons (eager-instantiate phase of refresh) but WARMER for prototype/request/session-scoped multi-valued points re-resolved per acquisition — so although a collection injection is rarer than single-valued, the warm path matters and the sort must stay branch-and-compare with one Vec allocation sized to the match set (charter #4: pure computation, never a poll/busy-wait). The qualifying set + InjectionPoint descriptors are immutable after refresh (frozen by the registry), read lock-free by concurrent resolutions (no global lock, charter #5). CRITICAL execution-context boundary: aggregation PICKS and ORDERS the candidates but does not CONSTRUCT them — obtaining each element's bean handle is the async step owned by bean-instantiation (boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary; RPITIT/AFIT are NOT dyn-safe so async bean methods route through a boxed shim per the nightly doc). For a List of N beans the caller may build them sequentially or CONCURRENTLY (join the N construction futures) — a meaningful async-execution choice this feature exposes but does not decide (Phase 2 owns the concurrency policy + context propagation); each build may .await (DB pools) but MUST NOT block the executor (blocking factories documented to spawn_blocking). Cancellation: if the caller's element-construction futures are cancelled mid-collection, partially-built elements must not leak — but this feature holds only borrows/handle-obtainers, so its own state is leak-free; the partial-construction cleanup is bean-instantiation's concern. Errors propagate as a rich CollectError (Array-length-mismatch, Map-name-collision, or — surfaced via the upstream self-check — a short-collection-from-dropped-crate hint), surfaced fail-fast at eager-instantiate for singletons and at first-acquisition for scoped points (charter #9). No teardown concern (holds only borrows + handle-obtainers).

#### R2 — Type-driven container target: the collection SHAPE and element type ARE the resolution key, with a const pre-sorted candidate index per element-type (compile-time-shaped, lowest-overhead, ordering precomputed)
Push intent-not-mechanism harder by letting the injection-point TYPE drive everything and PRECOMPUTING the order. Spring must detect 'this is a Collection/Map' and its element type reflectively at runtime; in leaf the macro KNOWS at the @Autowired site that the field is `Vec<Filter>` / `HashSet<Filter>` / `[Filter; 3]` / `HashMap<String, Filter>`, so it emits a typed request `Multi<Filter, Vec>` (the container shape is a type/const generic, the element type is a real type param). The registry maintains, per element-TypeId, a PRE-SORTED candidate index: a `&'static`/frozen-at-refresh `OrderedGroup<T>` = the list of all beans matching type T, sorted ONCE by the shared OrderComparator at registry-freeze time (not per resolution). A `Vec<Filter>` injection is then a single index probe returning the pre-sorted group, cloned into the container — NO per-resolution sort. Generics-as-elementwise-qualifier falls out for free: Store<i32> and Store<String> are distinct element TypeIds = distinct OrderedGroups, so List<Store<i32>> probes the Store<i32> group and never sees Store<String> (doc 728) with zero filtering work — the type system did the narrowing, mirroring R3 of autowiring-resolution's typed-key idea applied to the SET rather than the winner. @Order/@Priority/Ordered are folded into each group's sort at freeze time using the captured OrderKey; the interface-beats-annotation precedence is applied once during the freeze sort. For Map<String, T>, the group is additionally indexed by bean name into a name->handle map; the array/List-vs-Map asymmetry becomes a CHOICE made at freeze (the Map view can be built in registration order to match Spring, or as a BTreeMap/IndexMap if leaf reconciles — same group, two views). Optionality is the container's own emptiness (an empty group = empty Vec). The whole thing trades a small freeze-time sort cost for near-zero warm-path cost on every multi-valued resolution (a probe + clone of a pre-sorted slice).

**Pros:** Lowest warm-path overhead (charter #2): a multi-valued resolution is an index probe + clone of an already-sorted slice — no per-resolution sort, no per-resolution filter — which matters for prototype/request-scoped collection injections re-resolved per acquisition (charter #4 hot-path discipline).; Generics-as-elementwise-qualifier is FREE and type-safe: distinct concrete element TypeIds are distinct OrderedGroups, so List<Store<i32>> excluding Store<String> needs zero runtime narrowing (doc 728 realized natively, not emulated by a ResolvableType walk), and a typo'd element type is a compile error (charter #9 richest timing).; Ordering is computed ONCE at registry-freeze using the shared comparator, so it cannot drift between resolutions and the interface-beats-annotation precedence is applied in exactly one place — strong unification (charter #11) and the order is a stable property of the frozen graph, not a per-call computation.; The Map array/List asymmetry is cleanly expressed as two VIEWS over one frozen group (sorted slice view + name-keyed view), making the reconcile-or-inherit decision a single localized choice rather than scattered — and the name-keyed view reuses the same names autowiring-resolution and bean-naming use.; The typed `Multi<T, Shape>` request is a hand-writable public API (charter #10): a user can construct it without the macro, and the macro only emits what the user could write — the container shape as a type/const generic keeps the request fully typed and inspectable in cargo expand.; Aggregation-not-selection is structural: an OrderedGroup is the WHOLE matching set by construction, so primary/fallback/default are never consulted — collections-never-collapse is enforced by the index shape (doc 722).

**Cons:** Maintaining a pre-sorted OrderedGroup per element-TypeId is extra registry STATE and freeze-time work, and the groups must be invalidated/rebuilt if definitions can still change after the first probe — only sound under a strict freeze-after-refresh boundary (which the registry has, but this feature now DEPENDS on it more tightly than R1).; The cross-crate registration-order tiebreak problem is SHARPER: because the sort happens once at freeze, the 'absent order signal -> registration order' fallback must have a deterministic per-descriptor sequence number baked in (link/constructor order is unspecified, doc 154) — otherwise the pre-sorted group's tie order is nondeterministic across builds/linkers, a silent reproducibility hazard frozen into the index.; Generic element types still cannot form a group unless the concrete instantiation was registered (generics-cannot-register, doc 153), so OrderedGroup<Store<T>> for an un-instantiated T is simply empty — same escape-hatch (register_component!) burden as R1 but now the emptiness is a missing index entry that may be less obvious to diagnose.; More machinery than R1 for the same intent: an index keyed by element-TypeId with per-group pre-sorting and dual Map/slice views is more infrastructure than a straight collect-then-sort function — and it overlaps heavily with how the registry already indexes beans by type for single-valued lookup, risking duplication unless unified (a phase2 concern).; The `Multi<T, Shape>` typed request with a const-generic/marker container shape is more type-machinery at the injection point than R1's runtime CollectionShape enum, and supporting Array(n) as a const generic with a length-mismatch error is awkward to express purely in the type (the count is runtime-determined by how many beans matched).; Set still drops the precomputed order (a HashSet container cannot preserve slice order) — same wart as R1, and now the pre-sorting work is partly wasted for Set targets.

**Mechanisms considered:** typed `Multi<T, Shape>` injection request: element type as a type param, container shape as a type/const-generic marker (Vec/HashSet/Array<N>/Map) — macro emits what a user could write by hand; per-element-TypeId frozen `OrderedGroup<T>`: the whole matching set PRE-SORTED once at registry-freeze by the shared comparator (no per-resolution sort); generics-as-elementwise-qualifier free via distinct concrete element TypeIds = distinct groups (no runtime narrowing); interface-beats-annotation precedence applied once during the freeze-time sort using captured OrderKey; Map as a name-keyed VIEW over the same group (registration-order to match Spring, or ordered-map to reconcile — one localized choice); deterministic per-descriptor sequence number as the registration-order tiebreak baked into the group sort (defeating unspecified link/constructor order)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Same core fact as R1: the OrderedGroup index and the materializer live in leaf-core and consume whatever discovery substrate Phase 2 picks; collection injection remains the feature MOST exposed to silent-DCE (a dropped/GC'd contributing crate yields a SHORT group with no error — doc 34/152). R2 ADDS a sharper cross-crate constraint: because the group is PRE-SORTED ONCE at freeze, the registration-order fallback tiebreak (used when beans have equal/no OrderKey) MUST be a deterministic per-descriptor sequence number emitted by the macro — link-section order (linkme) and constructor order (inventory) are UNSPECIFIED across builds/linkers/platforms (doc 154), so relying on actual registration order would freeze a nondeterministic tie-order into the index, a reproducibility hazard worse than R1's per-resolution sort (which has the same input but at least could be re-derived). This makes the doc's 'carry an explicit ordering key in the descriptor and sort deterministically, never rely on registration order' (line 377) a HARD requirement for R2, not just a recommendation. The freeze-time group build is also the natural place to run the anti-DCE self-check: 'expected K beans of type T across declared crates, group contains J' — collection injection is precisely where an under-count is most diagnosable, so the OrderedGroup build emitting a provenance-annotated count (which crate each member came from) directly serves the cross-crate self-check mandate. Generics: an OrderedGroup<Store<RemoteType>> exists only if the concrete instantiation was registered upstream (generics-cannot-register, doc 153); an un-instantiated generic element type yields a missing/empty group. The two-source duality is invisible: a WASM-module bean's descriptor, if it reaches the freeze, joins the group like any other (CANDIDATES not a decision) — but the freeze TIMING now matters more, because the group must be built AFTER all sources (including any dynamic directory-scanned/WASM source) have contributed, or a late-arriving bean misses the pre-sort (a tension with any dynamic source flagged for phase2).

**T2 — execution context:** Two execution profiles. FREEZE-TIME (cold, once per refresh): building each OrderedGroup is a SYNCHRONOUS CPU sort over the captured OrderKeys — runs during the registry-freeze step after all candidates are registered, never awaits/blocks, and is the natural anti-DCE self-check site; this is a cold path so the sort cost is paid once and is acceptable (charter #4 permits cold-path work). WARM-PATH (per multi-valued resolution): an index probe + clone of the pre-sorted slice / name view — near-zero, synchronous, allocation = one container clone, no sort, no global lock (reads the frozen immutable group, charter #5). As in R1, aggregation PICKS+ORDERS but element CONSTRUCTION is the async step owned by bean-instantiation (boxed-future at the dyn boundary; AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe). For a Vec<T> of N beans the caller awaits N builds, sequentially or CONCURRENTLY (join) — the concurrency/context-propagation policy is Phase 2's, not decided here; each build may .await but MUST NOT block (spawn_blocking for blocking factories). The pre-sorted-group model makes the ORDER deterministic and computed off the hot path entirely, which is the strongest charter-#4 story of the three. Cancellation: partial element construction is bean-instantiation's cleanup concern; this feature holds only the frozen group + handle-obtainers, leak-free. Errors: short-group (via self-check), Array-length-mismatch, Map-name-collision — surfaced fail-fast at the freeze self-check (richest, earliest runtime context for under-count) and at first-acquisition for materialization errors (charter #9). The freeze-must-happen-after-all-sources timing is the one execution-context subtlety vs R1 (which sorts lazily per resolution and so tolerates late registration if the registry allowed it).

#### R3 — Order-agnostic aggregation + an EXPLICIT consumer-side ordering API: the collection arrives unordered (registration/sequence order) and ordering is a deliberate consumer operation, making 'ordering is a consumer concern' a first-class, opt-in surface
Take the feature's own thesis literally — 'ordering is a consumer concern STRICTLY SEPARATE from startup order' — and make that separation EXPLICIT in the API rather than implicit-by-default. Spring conflates two consumer behaviours: injecting List<T> auto-sorts, but ObjectProvider.stream() is UNORDERED and only orderedStream() sorts (doc 2572). R3 generalizes the ObjectProvider distinction to ALL multi-valued injection: aggregation ALWAYS produces an unordered-by-default group (in deterministic per-descriptor sequence order, the stable substrate), and ORDERING is a separate, explicit, opt-in operation the consumer asks for. Two surfaces: (a) the DEFAULT injected `Vec<T>` is documented as registration/sequence-ordered (NOT @Order-sorted) — honest about the fact that auto-sort is a convenience that hides whether order is load-bearing; (b) an EXPLICIT `Ordered<Vec<T>>` (or an `#[autowired(ordered)]` flag, or a `provider.ordered_stream()` on the deferral-primitives handle) opts INTO the shared OrderComparator sort. This diverges from Spring's 'List auto-sorts' default but FAITHFULLY realizes the deeper intent (ordering is a consumer concern) by refusing to silently apply a presentation order the consumer did not ask for — and it sidesteps the Set-drops-order wart (Set is explicitly unordered, asking for Ordered<HashSet> is a type error) and the Map-no-sort asymmetry (Map is always name-keyed, ordering a Map is asking for Ordered<Map> which yields an ordered-map view). The shared comparator is identical; what changes is WHEN it runs — only on explicit request, never implicitly. The aggregation core is R1's collect function minus the implicit sort; the sort becomes a public combinator the consumer (or the macro, when `ordered` is declared) applies. This is the most DX-divergent but most charter-aligned-to-the-feature's-own-thesis, and the most honest about the @Order-vs-startup-order separation the feature exists to enforce.

**Pros:** Most faithful to the feature's CORE THESIS (ordering is a consumer concern strictly separate from startup order): the separation becomes a first-class, opt-in API surface rather than an implicit auto-sort, so a consumer who needs order ASKS for it and a consumer who does not is never silently given a presentation order that hides whether order is load-bearing — the strongest expression of doc 749's intent.; Generalizes Spring's own ObjectProvider stream()-vs-orderedStream() distinction (doc 2572) to ALL multi-valued injection, UNIFYING the two ordering behaviours Spring inconsistently splits between List (auto-sort) and stream (no sort) — one consistent rule (explicit-to-order) instead of Spring's List-sorts-but-stream-and-Map-do-not asymmetry (charter #11).; Cleanly dissolves the array/List-vs-Map asymmetry AND the Set-drops-order wart: Set is honestly unordered (Ordered<HashSet> is a type error), Map is honestly name-keyed (Ordered<Map> is an explicit ordered-map view), Vec is sequence-ordered unless Ordered<Vec> is requested — no silent per-container surprises (the doc 2638 gotcha is designed out, not inherited).; Lowest DEFAULT overhead: the common aggregation does NOT sort (charter #2) — sorting is paid only when explicitly requested, so a consumer who does not care about order pays nothing, and the deterministic sequence order is free.; The explicit Ordered<...> combinator is a hand-writable public API (charter #10) reusing the one shared comparator; the macro emits the same combinator call a user could write when `ordered` is declared, keeping macros thin and the behaviour inspectable.; Aggregation-not-selection is structural (the group is the whole matching set); makes the ordering-is-separate-from-selection AND ordering-is-separate-from-startup boundaries both explicit and type-visible.

**Cons:** BIGGEST DX divergence from Spring: a Spring developer expects `@Autowired List<Filter>` to be @Order-sorted, and R3's default Vec<T> is NOT — this violates the principle of least surprise for the parity target and risks a silent CORRECTNESS change for code ported from Spring that relied on the auto-sort (a list iterated in @Order order that is now in sequence order). Charter #1 (DX/Spring-grade feel) is in direct tension with this realization's honesty.; Splitting ordered vs unordered into the type (Ordered<Vec<T>> vs Vec<T>) adds a type-level distinction at every multi-valued injection point that Spring does not require — more ceremony (charter #1 cost) and a new vocabulary item (Ordered<...>) users must learn and remember to apply.; The 'silent correctness change' risk is real and sharp: unlike a missing bean (loud-ish via self-check), a wrongly-ORDERED collection produces no error — it just behaves differently, exactly the kind of subtle reorder the feature warns about (doc 1772's 'misordering is invisible'), now potentially introduced BY the framework's default rather than prevented by it.; More surfaces to specify (default Vec, Ordered<Vec>, Ordered<HashSet> = type error, Map, Ordered<Map>) than R1/R2's single auto-sort rule — and the interaction with deferral-primitives' ObjectProvider stream()/orderedStream() must be reconciled so there are not two parallel ordered-vs-unordered vocabularies (the injection-point Ordered<T> AND the provider .ordered_stream()).; If leaf decides to match Spring's auto-sort default after all (likely, given charter #1), R3 collapses back toward R1 with an extra unordered-opt-OUT instead of opt-IN — so R3's distinctive value depends on a contestable bet that explicit-ordering is worth the divergence, which Phase 3 may reject.; Does not by itself address the cross-crate sequence-order determinism (it RELIES on a deterministic per-descriptor sequence as the default order, so it inherits R1/R2's requirement that the descriptor carry an explicit deterministic key — and now that order is USER-VISIBLE by default, making nondeterminism more damaging).

**Mechanisms considered:** order-agnostic aggregation core (R1's collect minus the implicit sort) producing a group in deterministic per-descriptor SEQUENCE order; explicit `Ordered<Vec<T>>` / `#[autowired(ordered)]` flag / `provider.ordered_stream()` opt-IN combinator applying the ONE shared comparator only on request; generalizing ObjectProvider's stream()-unordered vs orderedStream()-ordered distinction (doc 2572) to ALL multi-valued injection (one consistent explicit-to-order rule); Set as honestly-unordered (Ordered<HashSet> = compile error), Map as honestly name-keyed (Ordered<Map> = explicit ordered-map view) — designing out the array/List-vs-Map asymmetry and Set wart; macro emits the same Ordered<...> combinator a user could hand-write when `ordered` is declared (thin macro, charter #10); deterministic per-descriptor sequence number as the default (now user-visible) order — defeating unspecified link/constructor order

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Same core exposure as R1/R2: aggregation is a whole-program 'all matching beans' query, the feature MOST exposed to silent-DCE (dropped/GC'd crate -> silently short collection, doc 34/152), so the anti-DCE self-check (expected-vs-found count with per-crate provenance) is a precondition. R3 RAISES THE STAKES on the cross-crate sequence-order determinism: because the DEFAULT injected collection is in sequence order and that order is now USER-VISIBLE (not a hidden tiebreak as in R1/R2 where @Order usually dominates), a nondeterministic per-descriptor sequence (from unspecified linkme section / inventory constructor order, doc 154) would produce a collection whose default iteration order varies across builds/linkers/platforms — a more damaging reproducibility hazard than R1/R2 because the consumer sees it directly. So R3 makes the doc's 'carry an explicit deterministic ordering key in the descriptor, never rely on registration order' (line 377) an ABSOLUTE requirement: the default sequence order MUST be a deterministic, declared-per-descriptor key (e.g. a stable hash of crate+symbol, or an explicit declared sequence), computed by the macro using absolute ::leaf_core paths, not actual link order. The explicit Ordered<...> path uses the same OrderKey-driven shared comparator and is cross-crate-identical to R1's sort. Generics-as-elementwise-qualifier and generics-cannot-register are inherited identically (concrete element TypeIds; register_component! escape hatch). The two-source duality is invisible to aggregation (both sources contribute Candidates), and the explicit-ordering API is agnostic to candidate origin — a WASM-module bean orders identically by its declared OrderKey if its descriptor reached the set (origin-agnostic). The self-check provenance is especially valuable here: since the default is sequence order, a short collection is the symptom most likely to be noticed by a consumer, and pointing it at a dropped crate is high-value diagnostics (charter #9).

**T2 — execution context:** AGGREGATION is PURE, SYNCHRONOUS, CPU-only, allocation-light (collect handle-obtainers into a container in deterministic sequence order — NO sort by default), runs at wiring time (cold for eager singletons, warm for prototype/scoped re-resolution), over frozen immutable data read lock-free (no global lock, charter #5). The EXPLICIT Ordered<...> combinator adds a SYNCHRONOUS sort via the shared comparator ONLY when requested — so the default warm path is sort-free (the lowest-default-overhead profile, charter #2/#4), and ordering cost is paid exactly where the consumer opted in. As in R1/R2, aggregation PICKS the candidates but element CONSTRUCTION is the async step owned by bean-instantiation (boxed-future at the dyn boundary; AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe); for N elements the caller awaits N builds sequentially or CONCURRENTLY (join) — concurrency/context-propagation policy is Phase 2's, each build may .await but MUST NOT block (spawn_blocking for blocking factories). The deferral-primitives interaction is an explicit execution-context boundary: a provider.ordered_stream() is LAZY (each element built on demand, can be filtered before construction — doc 2573) whereas an injected Vec<T> is EAGER (all N built at injection) — R3 must keep these two consumer surfaces' execution semantics distinct and documented (lazy-per-element vs eager-all), and reconcile the ordered-vs-unordered vocabulary across them. Cancellation: partial element construction is bean-instantiation's cleanup; this feature holds only borrows/handle-obtainers, leak-free. Errors: short-collection (via self-check, the most consumer-visible symptom here), Map-name-collision, Array-length-mismatch — fail-fast at eager-instantiate / first-acquisition (charter #9). No teardown concern.

### Rust clashes
- Runtime reflective Collection/Map detection + element-type extraction vs static capture: Spring's resolveMultipleBeans inspects the injection point reflectively to learn it is a Collection/array/Map and what the element type is. Rust has no such reflection — the thin macro must capture at the @Autowired site the container SHAPE (Vec/HashSet/array-with-const-length/HashMap), the element TypeId, and the element's generic-arg TypeIds (for List<Store<i32>> elementwise narrowing), and freeze them into the multi-valued InjectionPoint descriptor. There is no 'detect that this is a List of T' at runtime; the shape and element type are compile-time facts the macro emits.
- 'Ordered INTERFACE beats @Order ANNOTATION' has no runtime analogue: Spring's AnnotationAwareOrderComparator gives a runtime-computed Ordered/PriorityOrdered interface value precedence over the @Order/@Priority annotation value BEFORE numerics compare (doc 2634) — because the interface order is computed live and is the more specific signal. Rust cannot compute an order live off a trait at sort time without it being captured; the interface-vs-annotation precedence must be modeled as a DISTINCT captured provenance flag (an OrderSource enum: OrderedInterface | PriorityAnnotation | OrderAnnotation | None) frozen on the definition, not merely the i32 value — and an Ordered-equivalent trait-implemented signal must be captured separately from a declared @Order const so the precedence rule can be applied.
- ResolvableType elementwise generic narrowing vs concrete monomorphized TypeIds: Spring's List<Store<Integer>> excludes Store<String> via runtime ResolvableType assignability (doc 728). Rust cannot register an open generic and has no runtime ResolvableType — the elementwise generic-as-implicit-qualifier must compare CONCRETE generic-arg TypeId tuples captured per monomorphized instantiation, so List<Store<RemoteType>> only collects beans whose concrete Store<RemoteType> was separately registered (register_component!(Concrete) escape hatch; generics-cannot-register, cross-crate doc 153). The elementwise-narrowing INTENT survives; the open-generic mechanism does not.
- Registration order as a tiebreak is UNSPECIFIED cross-crate in Rust: Spring's 'absent any order signal, by bean-definition registration order' (doc 734) is a stable, deterministic fallback on the JVM. In Rust, linkme link/section order and inventory constructor order are UNSPECIFIED across builds/linkers/platforms (cross-crate doc 154), and the pre-RFC even proposes deliberate randomization. So the 'registration-order' fallback CANNOT rely on actual link order — it must be backed by a deterministic per-descriptor sequence key emitted by the macro (the doc's 'carry an explicit ordering key, sort deterministically' recommendation, line 377), or the injected collection's tie-order becomes a silent build-varying reproducibility hazard.
- The array/List-vs-Map sort asymmetry (Spring sorts List/array, does NOT sort Map — doc 2638) is a JVM-incidental gotcha Rust can RECONCILE: Spring's Map-no-sort is an implementation artifact (resolveMultipleBeanMap calls no comparator). Rust has cheap ordered maps (IndexMap insertion-order = registration-order, BTreeMap by name), so leaf could offer an ordered Map view trivially — but doing so DIVERGES from documented Spring behaviour. This is intent-vs-mechanism tension: replicate the gotcha for fidelity, or fix it for DX (charter #1) — a real decision (flagged to phase2), not a mechanical port.
- 'All matching beans' is a whole-program aggregation = the classpath-scan analogue Rust structurally lacks: Spring queries a live BeanFactory for every bean assignable to T. Rust has no global classpath (cross-crate doc 17); the matching set is synthesized from link-time/build-time registration, making collection injection the feature MOST exposed to silent-DCE (a dropped/GC'd crate yields a SILENTLY SHORT collection, no error — doc 34/152). The anti-DCE self-check (expected-vs-found count) is therefore a CORRECTNESS PRECONDITION for this feature specifically, not a nicety — a wrong collection size is a silent landmine exactly like single-valued ambiguity is.
- No async element construction in parallel for free; concurrency is an explicit choice: Spring builds collection elements on a single thread implicitly. leaf's async-first model means a Vec<T> of N beans CAN be built concurrently (join N construction futures) — but RPITIT/AFIT are NOT dyn-safe (nightly doc), so async bean construction routes through a boxed-future shim at the dyn boundary, and the sequential-vs-concurrent element-build policy plus context propagation across those builds is an explicit execution-context decision (Phase 2's) this feature exposes but must not pre-decide.
- Set cannot preserve sort order (container-inherent), unlike a sorted List/array: a HashSet materialized from a sorted candidate slice loses the order — the comparator work is wasted for Set targets. Spring has the analogous issue (a LinkedHashSet would preserve insertion order but a plain Set need not); in Rust this is a sharper, type-visible wart — asking for an ordered Set is semantically incoherent unless leaf uses an insertion-ordered set type. Must be designed for (Set is honestly unordered) rather than silently surprising.
- No exception-chain wrapping for the short-collection/Array-mismatch/Map-collision diagnostics: Spring would throw/wrap. Rust has no exceptions — a rich CollectError must be a constructed value carrying the element-type, the count expected (from the self-check / declared sources) vs found, per-crate provenance of contributing beans, and the failing container shape, propagated via Result and re-wrapped by bean-instantiation into the dependent's assembly-chain error (charter #9). Building that causal chain by hand is the cost of honoring rich diagnostics.

### Interactions
- **autowiring-resolution** — THE deliberate structural COMPLEMENT and load-bearing NON-interaction. autowiring-resolution owns SINGLE-valued selection (pick one winner, fail-fast on ambiguity); THIS feature owns MULTI-valued aggregation (take ALL matching candidates). They are mutually exclusive by injection-point ARITY — a point is either single-valued (routes to autowiring-resolution) or multi-valued (routes here), never both. The critical shared invariant: @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate NEVER collapse a collection (doc 722/2622) — those tie-break flags are simply NOT consulted here, which is why the two features must stay separate functions. The ONE shared mechanism is the @Order/@Priority/Ordered COMPARATOR: autowiring-resolution uses it as its @Priority tie-break LAYER (pick one), this feature uses it to ORDER the whole set — it must be a single canonical comparator so single-valued and collection ordering never diverge (doc 2632/2640).
- **candidate-resolver** — THE primary upstream seam. candidate-resolver does phase-1 FILTERING (autowire-candidate flag, generics-as-implicit-qualifier, @Qualifier matching, the three-valued match/veto/abstain) and produces the QUALIFYING SET; THIS feature consumes that set WHOLE (no further selection). The elementwise generic narrowing (List<Store<i32>> excluding Store<String>, doc 728) is candidate-resolver's filtering applied per-element — this feature does not re-filter, it aggregates whatever qualified. Note (doc 2621): default-candidate=false EXCLUDES a bean from plain-type collection matching unless explicitly qualified — so the qualifying set this feature receives already reflects the default-candidate filter; collection injection respects it by consuming candidate-resolver's output, not by re-checking flags.
- **deferral-primitives** — The 0..N multiple-access shape (ObjectProvider stream()/orderedStream(), List eager vs stream lazy) overlaps directly with this feature's surface. deferral-primitives owns the LAZY/streamed multi access (stream() is unordered = registration order; only orderedStream() applies the comparator — doc 2572/2573; stream is lazy and filterable before construction); THIS feature owns the EAGER injected List/Set/array/Map. The shared rule: the comparator that orders an injected List is the SAME one orderedStream() applies. R3 explicitly generalizes the provider's stream-vs-orderedStream distinction to all multi-valued injection — so the ordered-vs-unordered vocabulary across the injection point and the provider handle must be reconciled (a phase2 concern). Empty-set-is-empty-collection (never NoSuchBean) is the multi-valued analogue of the provider's optional tolerance.
- **bean-naming** — Supplies the bean NAMES that become the Map<String, T> KEYS (doc 734 — keys are bean names, values are beans of T). bean-naming derives the deterministic default names and aliases; this feature pairs each candidate's name with its handle to build the name-keyed map. Name COLLISIONS within a Map injection are a fail-fast error this feature surfaces (two beans of T with the same name cannot both key the map) — reusing bean-naming's collision semantics. The same names also feed autowiring-resolution's name-as-implicit-qualifier tie-break, so names are shared infrastructure across both single and multi-valued resolution.
- **injection-styles** — Defines the multi-valued injection POINTS this feature resolves (constructor/setter/field of a Vec/Set/array/Map type) and fixes the InjectionPoint descriptor shape the macro captures: container SHAPE (Vec/HashSet/array-len/HashMap), element TypeId, element generic-arg TypeIds. Constructor-arg collection points are mandatory but their emptiness is an empty collection (not a failure) — the 0..N arity of multi-valued points is distinct from single-valued required/optional arity, and injection-styles fixes which points are collections vs scalars.
- **bean-definition** — Pure consumer of the metamodel for ORDERING data: the OrderKey (the @Order/@Priority i32 value + the OrderSource provenance flag distinguishing Ordered-interface-implemented vs @Order-annotation vs @Priority-annotation) and the deterministic registration-sequence number are frozen on each MergedBeanDefinition by bean-definition at the declaration site. This feature reads that order metadata; it adds none of its own. The @Order-vs-@Priority scope asymmetry (doc 743 — @Priority cannot annotate factory methods) is a capture-time constraint bean-definition enforces (a factory-method bean carries no @Priority OrderSource), which this feature simply reads.
- **bean-registry** — WHERE this feature runs and what it reads. bean-registry produces the candidate set (candidates_for_type over its type index, filtered through candidate-resolver), and the aggregation function is a method on the engine/context consuming the registry's frozen definitions + the borrowed candidate set. The freeze-after-refresh boundary makes the set + InjectionPoint descriptors immutable and lock-free-readable during concurrent resolution. R2's pre-sorted OrderedGroup index, if chosen, is registry STATE built at the freeze step and overlaps the registry's existing by-type index — a unification opportunity (avoid duplicating the type index).
- **container-hierarchy** — Collection aggregation is hierarchy-aware on INJECTION but listing is local-only (doc 2700): a child context's collection injection of T may aggregate matching beans from the parent (injection is hierarchy-aware), but by-type LISTING is per-container and does NOT silently merge ancestors (to keep collection injection deterministic and avoid double-counting — the doc explicitly warns merging ancestors 'would make collection injection non-obvious and risk double-counting'). So this feature must consult the hierarchy-merged candidate set for injection while respecting that local listing stays local — a precise boundary container-hierarchy defines and this feature honors. Local definitions shadow same-named parent definitions, affecting which bean keys a Map entry.
- **bean-instantiation** — Downstream consumer of the aggregated/ordered result and owner of the async element-construction boundary. This feature AGGREGATES + ORDERS the candidates (sync, pure); bean-instantiation CONSTRUCTS each element (async, boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary, may .await, must not block). The handoff: this feature returns an ordered/keyed list of Candidate handle-obtainers; bean-instantiation builds them — SEQUENTIALLY or CONCURRENTLY (join N futures), a concurrency/context-propagation policy decided in Phase 2, not here. bean-instantiation also re-wraps this feature's CollectError (short-collection/Array-mismatch/Map-collision) into the dependent's assembly-chain error, and owns cleanup of partially-built elements on cancellation.
- **refresh-lifecycle** — Timing dependency: 'all matching beans' can only be correctly aggregated AFTER all definitions (from all crates / all sources) are registered. For eager singleton collection injection this is the eager-instantiate phase of refresh; for R2's pre-sorted OrderedGroup the freeze/sort MUST happen after all candidates (including any dynamic directory-scanned/WASM-module source) have registered, or a late-arriving bean misses the set/sort. This couples to the refresh-lifecycle's fixed-order template (load definitions -> ... -> eager-instantiate) and to where the freeze boundary sits relative to dynamic registration sources (a phase2 tension).
- **condition-family** — @ConditionalOnBean / OnMissingBean and the auto-config back-off model determine WHICH beans are in the registry, hence which enter the collection — a conditional bean absent from the set yields a smaller collection. Distinct from autowiring-resolution's OnSingleCandidate (which queries single-valued selection), collection injection's set is simply 'all conditionally-present matching beans'. The interaction is indirect (conditions shape the candidate set upstream) but load-bearing for the auto-config-override story: a library's conditional default bean and an app bean both land in a List<T> together (collections do not collapse), whereas single-valued injection would arbitrate between them.
- **task-execution** — A canonical USE CASE and an execution-context interaction: aggregating all beans of a type into an ordered List is exactly how ordered infrastructure collections are wired (e.g. Spring's callRunners collecting all Runner beans as a single @Order-sorted stream — doc 309; ordered filter chains; ordered post-processors). When the collected elements are themselves executed in order, the @Order presentation order this feature produces becomes the EXECUTION order — reinforcing that collection ordering is a consumer concern (the consumer iterates in order) strictly separate from the startup order task-execution / the runtime-lifecycle govern. This feature provides the ordered set; it does not run it.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Aggregate-then-sort-per-resolution (R1) vs pre-sorted-OrderedGroup-per-element-TypeId-at-freeze (R2) vs explicit-consumer-side-ordering-API (R3) — or a blend (R2's pre-sorted frozen group as the substrate, with R3's explicit Ordered<...> opt-in as the ordering surface over it, and R1's per-resolution materializer for dynamic/late-registered scoped points)? This is the keystone shape decision for multi-valued resolution and is entangled with the type-erasure/registry-substrate (how dyn beans are indexed by element type) and the compile-vs-runtime / freeze-early meta-concepts.
- Should leaf MATCH Spring's auto-sort default (injected List<T> is @Order-sorted, charter #1 least-surprise / Spring-grade DX) or make ordering EXPLICIT/opt-in (R3, most faithful to the feature's own 'ordering is a consumer concern' thesis and avoids silently applying a presentation order)? This is a direct charter #1 (DX/parity) vs feature-thesis-fidelity tension with a silent-correctness-change risk for ported code, unresolved here.
- The array/List-vs-Map sort ASYMMETRY (Spring sorts List/array but NOT Map — doc 2638): replicate the gotcha for fidelity, or RECONCILE it using Rust's cheap ordered maps (IndexMap insertion-order / BTreeMap by name) for better DX? And if reconciled, is the Map ordered by name, by the @Order comparator, or by registration sequence? Cross-cuts charter #1 (fix the gotcha) vs intent-parity (replicate documented behaviour), shared with the unified-diagnostics/DX meta-concept.
- Where does the ONE canonical @Order/@Priority/Ordered COMPARATOR live, and how is the 'Ordered-interface-implemented signal beats the @Order/@Priority annotation value' precedence (doc 2634) captured in Rust — a distinct OrderSource provenance flag on the descriptor vs a separately-captured Ordered-trait-implemented marker vs just the i32? This comparator is SHARED with autowiring-resolution's @Priority tie-break (single-vs-collection ordering must not diverge) and with deferral-primitives' orderedStream() — shares the unification meta-concept and couples to autowiring-resolution and deferral-primitives.
- The cross-crate registration-ORDER determinism: the 'absent any order signal -> registration order' fallback (doc 734) is UNSPECIFIED across builds/linkers for linkme/inventory (doc 154). Must the descriptor carry a deterministic per-descriptor sequence key (stable crate+symbol hash? declared sequence? topo position?) so the collection's tie-order / default order is reproducible — and where is that key computed (macro / build.rs / link time)? Couples to the cross-crate composition substrate and is SHARPER for R2 (order frozen once) and R3 (default order user-visible).
- The anti-DCE self-check as a CORRECTNESS PRECONDITION for collection injection specifically: collection injection is the feature most exposed to silent-DCE (a dropped/GC'd crate -> silently short collection, no error). How does the expected-vs-found count + per-crate provenance self-check (cross-crate doc 152/345) integrate with this feature's diagnostics so a short collection points at the dropped crate — and is the 'expected count' even knowable without a build-time manifest? Shares the cross-crate composition model and the unified-diagnostics meta-concept.
- Sequential vs CONCURRENT construction of the N elements of an injected collection (and context propagation across those builds): an async-first framework can build a Vec<T>'s elements concurrently (join N futures), but this is an execution-context policy with cancellation/error-aggregation implications (one element's build fails — fail the whole collection? partial?). Belongs to the async-execution / context-propagation meta-concept (T2) and couples to bean-instantiation; unresolved here.
- The container SHAPE vocabulary: which target types does leaf support (Vec/HashSet/[T;N]/HashMap) and how is each captured — a runtime CollectionShape enum (R1) vs a typed Multi<T, Shape> with const-generic/marker container (R2/R3)? How are Set's order-dropping and Array's length-determined-at-runtime (vs a fixed const N) reconciled, and does leaf allow user-defined collection targets (SmallVec, newtype) — faithful-to-Spring (only the four) vs more-rust-native? Couples to injection-styles and the macro/codegen-boundary meta-concept.
- Freeze TIMING vs dynamic registration sources: 'all matching beans' requires the candidate set to be COMPLETE before aggregation (especially R2's pre-sort-once). How does this reconcile with an optional dynamic directory-scanned / WASM-module source that might register beans AFTER the static link-time set is frozen — does a dynamic bean miss already-built singleton collections, and is collection injection re-evaluated for scoped points after dynamic registration? Couples to the two-source static+dynamic duality, refresh-lifecycle's freeze boundary, and bean-registry's freeze model.
- Reconciling the ordered-vs-unordered VOCABULARY across the injection point and deferral-primitives' provider: injected List (eager) vs ObjectProvider.stream()/orderedStream() (lazy, doc 2572/2573) split ordered behaviour inconsistently in Spring (List sorts, stream does not). Does leaf unify to ONE rule (R3's explicit-to-order everywhere) or preserve the List-auto-sorts / stream-unordered split for parity — and how do the two surfaces share the comparator and the lazy-vs-eager element-construction semantics? Couples to deferral-primitives and the unification meta-concept.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the dedicated Collection/array/map injection section (lines 732-749: all-matching-beans aggregation, the @Order/@Priority/Ordered ordering with registration-order fallback, the Map<String,T> name-keyed semantics with String-keys-only, the @Order-vs-@Priority scope asymmetry — @Priority type-level-only so it cannot annotate factory methods — and the load-bearing 'injection ordering is NOT startup ordering' separation that is this feature's THESIS), the @Primary/@Fallback section's crucial caveat (722: primary/fallback NEVER collapse collections — a List/Map takes every candidate regardless), the generics-as-implicit-qualifier section (728: List<Store<Integer>> excludes Store<String>, elementwise narrowing), the candidate-resolution-engine section (2618-2622: default-candidate excludes from plain-type collection matching unless qualified; primary/fallback/streams affect only single points), the Ordering infrastructure section (2630-2642: the single shared OrderComparator/AnnotationAwareOrderComparator two-layer mechanism, lower-value-wins, Ordered-interface-beats-annotation, and THE most-missed gotcha — resolveMultipleBeanMap does NO sort so injected Maps follow registration order while List/array ARE sorted), and the ObjectProvider section (2572-2573: stream() unordered vs orderedStream() ordered, List eager vs stream lazy). I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc's whole-program-aggregation = classpath-scan-analogue Rust lacks, the three DCE elimination layers + silent-empty-iterable + self-check mandate (34/139-152/345), the unspecified registration-order for linkme/inventory + 'carry an explicit deterministic ordering key' recommendation (154/377), generics-cannot-register (153), and the four candidate architectures + two-source duality; and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.3 async-first/explicit-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimal-Arc, §2.9 rich-early-diagnostics) and the stable envelope (RPITIT/AFIT NOT dyn-safe -> boxed futures at the dyn construction boundary, trait-upcasting 1.86, const TypeId 1.91, no async Drop).\n\nTHE most important framing I held throughout: this feature is AGGREGATION, the deliberate structural COMPLEMENT to autowiring-resolution's SELECTION — they are mutually exclusive by injection-point arity, and the load-bearing non-interaction is that @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate NEVER collapse a collection (a List/Set/Map takes EVERY type-matching bean regardless of selection flags). That is why all three realizations make 'collections never collapse' STRUCTURAL: the tie-break flags are simply not consulted in the aggregation path. The feature's own THESIS — ordering is a consumer concern STRICTLY separate from startup/instantiation order — is the design axis that most distinguishes the realizations, and R3 takes it most literally by making ordering explicit/opt-in.\n\nThe three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters HERE — WHEN and HOW the ordering is applied and how the collection shape is determined: R1 = aggregate-then-sort per resolution, a hand-written collect/sort/materialize function mirroring resolveMultipleBeans (highest fidelity, preserves the array/List-vs-Map asymmetry as a conscious choice, per-resolution sort cost); R2 = pre-sorted OrderedGroup per element-TypeId computed ONCE at registry-freeze (lowest warm-path overhead, ordering precomputed off the hot path, generics-as-elementwise-qualifier free via distinct element TypeIds, but freeze-timing-and-determinism dependent and overlaps the registry's type index); R3 = order-AGNOSTIC aggregation + an EXPLICIT consumer-side Ordered<...> ordering API (most faithful to the feature's thesis, generalizes ObjectProvider's stream-vs-orderedStream distinction to all multi-valued injection, designs out the Set-drops-order wart and Map-no-sort asymmetry, but is the biggest DX divergence from Spring's List-auto-sorts default with a silent-correctness-change risk for ported code). R1 and R2 differ on per-resolution-vs-frozen-precomputed ordering; R3 differs on implicit-vs-explicit ordering and is partially orthogonal (a likely Phase-2 blend is R2's frozen substrate + R3's explicit opt-in surface + R1's lazy materializer for dynamic scoped points).\n\nFour things to carry forward: (1) AGGREGATION-not-selection is structural — collections never collapse because the selection flags are never read; the ONLY shared mechanism with autowiring-resolution is the @Order/@Priority comparator (used to ORDER here, to TIE-BREAK there), which must be ONE canonical mechanism or single-vs-collection ordering diverges. (2) The array/List-vs-Map sort ASYMMETRY (Spring sorts List/array, NOT Map) is a JVM-incidental gotcha Rust can cheaply RECONCILE (ordered maps) — replicate-for-fidelity vs fix-for-DX is a real unresolved decision, not a mechanical port. (3) Collection injection is the feature MOST exposed to silent-DCE (a dropped/GC'd crate yields a SILENTLY SHORT collection, no error) — the anti-DCE self-check is a CORRECTNESS PRECONDITION here, and the cross-crate registration-order fallback must be backed by a deterministic per-descriptor sequence key (link/constructor order is unspecified). (4) 'Ordering is a consumer concern strictly separate from startup order' is the feature's thesis — the @Order presentation order must NEVER influence singleton instantiation order (governed only by real dependency edges + @DependsOn), a separation all three realizations preserve.\n\nI refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization keeps the bean handle an abstract origin-agnostic obtainer (the scope/ownership keystone), keeps element CONSTRUCTION an async boxed-future step owned by bean-instantiation (this feature does only sync aggregation+ordering), keeps the qualifying-set ownership/shape abstract (borrow vs Arc-slice vs frozen group — a phase2Question), keeps the sequential-vs-concurrent element-build policy deferred (Phase 2's async-execution model), and stays substrate-neutral on how candidates were discovered (the two-source static-link + dynamic-WASM-module duality is invisible to aggregation — both just feed the set; CANDIDATES not a decision). Naming ('InjectionPoint', 'Candidate', 'CollectionShape', 'OrderKey', 'OrderSource', 'order_cmp', 'OrderedGroup', 'Multi<T,Shape>', 'Ordered<...>', 'CollectError') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (732-749 the collection/array/map section + ordering + @Order-vs-@Priority scope + injection-vs-startup-order thesis; 722 primary/fallback never collapse collections; 728 generics-as-elementwise-qualifier; 2618-2624 default-candidate + single-point-only flags; 2630-2642 the shared OrderComparator two-layer mechanism + lower-value-wins + interface-beats-annotation + the resolveMultipleBeanMap-no-sort gotcha; 2560-2576 ObjectProvider stream-vs-orderedStream + List-eager-vs-stream-lazy; 309 callRunners ordered-stream use case; 2700 hierarchy injection-aware-but-listing-local + double-counting warning); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3, topology §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (whole-program aggregation = classpath-scan analogue Rust lacks 17; Layer 0/A/B DCE silent-empty-iterable + self-check mandate 34/139-152/345; unspecified registration order + carry-deterministic-key 154/377; generics-cannot-register 153; architectures A/B/C/D + two-source duality 277-337); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (stable envelope: RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe -> boxed futures at the dyn boundary 162/174-181, trait upcasting 1.86, const TypeId 1.91, Any+downcast, no async Drop).

## Injection Styles (constructor / setter / field)  `[injection-styles]`

Spring offers three injection STYLES with a deliberate value ordering, and this feature owns the SHAPE of an injection point + the CONSTRUCTION/POPULATION recipe for one bean (distinct from autowiring-resolution, which selects which single bean wins for a given point, and from bean-instantiation, which owns the async build/cache/cycle machinery).
