The load-bearing intent to replicate (intent, NOT JVM mechanism): (a) CONSTRUCTOR injection is preferred and is the discipline that matters — dependencies are MANDATORY and IMMUTABLE, the object's invariant is encoded in the type system so it can never exist in an invalid/partially-wired state, is always handed to clients non-null & fully initialized, and — the deepest why — is decoupled from the container ENTIRELY so a unit test does `new OrderService(mock)` with NO container and NO reflection (the framework is optional infrastructure, not a runtime dependency; Service-Locator is explicitly rejected for hiding deps behind opaque get() calls). (b) SETTER/method injection is for OPTIONAL dependencies with sensible defaults ('constructors for mandatory, setters for optional'); the container calls setters AFTER no-arg construction, and a non-required method is NOT CALLED AT ALL if any arg is unsatisfiable (vs leaving a field at its default). (c) FIELD injection is supported but DISCOURAGED — its real cost is untestability (no constructor/setter through which a test supplies mocks, forcing slow integration tests), and a large constructor is left deliberately painful as a Single-Responsibility smell-signal rather than papered over with ten @Autowired fields. (d) Mechanics: single constructor needs NO @Autowired (implicit); only ONE constructor may be @Autowired(required=true); multiple @Autowired constructors must all be required=false and Spring picks the GREEDIEST satisfiable one; injection metadata is discovered+cached against the MERGED definition by a MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor BEFORE population; AOT must pick a DETERMINISTIC preferred constructor since there is no instance to probe. (e) The style chosen has lifecycle consequences elsewhere: constructor cycles are logically unsatisfiable (fail fast) while setter/field cycles are breakable via early-singleton exposure (circular-references), and each point's required-ness/arity (Single vs Optional/required=false) is what downstream maps to NoSuchBean-vs-absent. Deepest Rust impedance: Rust construction is NATIVELY all-at-once-via-constructor returning a fully-owned immutable value (constructor injection is the idiomatic default and `final`/immutability is free), but Rust has NO post-construction field reflection, NO container-driven setter calls on private fields, and NO partially-constructed mutable bean the container can populate — so 'setter' and 'field' injection have no native mechanism and must be re-expressed (a typed builder/with-methods, Default + an opt-in populate hook, or simply collapsed into constructor injection of Option<T>), and the discovery of 'which members need injection' that Spring does reflectively against the merged definition must instead be captured at the declaration site by the thin macro into a per-bean InjectionPlan of typed injection points.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Constructor-first canonical: every bean is built by ONE total async factory fn whose typed parameters ARE the injection points; setter/field collapse into constructor params of Option<T>/with-default (most Spring-faithful to the *discipline*, maximally POJO-testable)
Take Spring's own recommendation to its logical conclusion in Rust: the ONLY native, non-reflective way the container builds a bean is by calling a single async factory function whose parameters are the typed injection points — so make constructor injection THE mechanism and re-express setter/field injection on top of it rather than as separate runtime phases. A `#[component]` on a struct (or a `#[bean]` fn) emits a thin `BeanFactory`-shaped descriptor: `async fn build(ctx: &ResolveCtx) -> Result<Self, AssemblyError>` that resolves each declared dependency via the container and hands them to the user's plain `Self::new(..)` (the hand-writable artifact — exactly `new OrderService(gateway)`, no container inside it). MANDATORY deps are required constructor params (arity=Single, TypeId captured); OPTIONAL ('setter-style') deps become constructor params typed `Option<T>` (arity=Optional → resolves to None instead of failing) or carry a `#[inject(default)]` so the param is filled from `Default`/a user expression when unsatisfiable — directly realizing 'constructors for mandatory, setters for optional' as ONE construction step with no mutable post-phase. FIELD injection is offered only as sugar that still desugars to a constructor param (the macro reads `#[inject] field: T` on the struct and synthesizes the same `build` that constructs `Self { field, .. }`), so a 'field-injected' bean STILL has a constructor a test can call — neutralizing field injection's untestability cost by construction (the discouraged-style's real harm is designed out). 'Single ctor needs no @Autowired' = a struct with one obvious constructor / all-`#[inject]` fields needs no per-field annotation; 'multiple @Autowired ctors, pick greediest satisfiable' = the macro emits an ORDERED list of candidate factory arms (most params first) and `build` tries them in order, taking the first whose required deps all resolve (greediest-satisfiable as a try-in-order over arms). The InjectionPlan (ordered typed params + arity + captured param NAME for the name tie-break + qualifier metadata) is const data the macro emits; autowiring-resolution consumes each point to pick a winner; bean-instantiation owns the .await and caching. Immutability is free (fields are plain owned/`Arc` collaborators, never re-assigned). No setter phase, no mutable bean, no early-exposed half-object → constructor cycles are structurally unbreakable here (correct per Spring) and 'setter/field cycle tolerance' is explicitly NOT offered by this realization (delegated as a tension to circular-references).

**Pros:** Highest fidelity to the feature's SOUL (charter #2 intent-parity): the POJO/testability discipline is realized structurally — every bean, including 'field-injected' ones, has a plain constructor a unit test calls with mocks and NO container, exactly Spring's deepest 'why'; the framework stays optional infrastructure, Service-Locator stays rejected.; Most idiomatic & lowest-overhead Rust (charter #1/#2/#5): one owned, immutable, fully-initialized value per bean, built once; no mutable partially-constructed state, no post-construction setter pass, no `Cell`/`RefCell` to allow re-assignment — minimal heap churn, `final`-like immutability free, safe-publication trivial (the value is frozen the instant it exists).; Construction is a single async fn = one clean execution-context boundary (T2): the whole build is one awaitable, trivially cancellable as a unit, with no 'half-built then setter-failed' intermediate state to leak or roll back.; Collapses three Spring styles onto ONE primitive (charter #11 unification): mandatory=param, optional=`Option<T>`/default param, field=sugar-over-param — autowiring-resolution sees a uniform list of injection points regardless of the surface style the user wrote.; Thin macro (charter #10): it only emits the `build` glue that calls the user's hand-written `new`, plus the const InjectionPlan; `cargo expand` shows literally the constructor call a human would write — nothing hidden, escape hatch is 'just write `new` yourself and register a factory'.; Designs OUT field injection's documented harm (untestability) while still OFFERING the field-injection surface syntax for DX parity — users who reach for `#[inject] field` get the ergonomics without the testability tax.

**Cons:** Sacrifices setter/field SEMANTIC parity to gain the discipline: a genuine setter/field SINGLETON CYCLE (which Spring tolerates via early-singleton exposure) is structurally impossible here because there is no half-built instance to expose — so the cross-feature 'setter cycles are breakable' contract (circular-references) cannot be honored by this realization without an escape hatch (Option<ObjectProvider>/@Lazy), a real divergence the user may hit.; 'Greediest-satisfiable among multiple @Autowired constructors' becomes a try-arms-in-order at runtime, which must be deterministic and explainable — and 'first satisfiable arm' can mask a missing bean (an arm silently falls through to a smaller arm) unless the diagnostic clearly reports which arm was chosen and why the greedier ones were skipped; subtle DX hazard.; Re-expressing 'optional setter' as a constructor `Option<T>` param shifts WHEN optionality is decided to construction time only — Spring's 'setter not called at all if any arg unsatisfiable' (method-level all-or-nothing) maps awkwardly onto per-param `Option<T>`; a multi-arg optional method's all-or-nothing semantics needs a grouped optional (a single `Option<(A,B)>` param or a sub-builder) to preserve, adding a shape.; No place for genuine post-construction mutation some patterns want (a bean that must observe its own fully-wired self, or wiring that legitimately needs two phases) — those must be pushed to lifecycle-callbacks (afterPropertiesSet-analogue) rather than a setter phase, which is arguably cleaner but is a behavioral relocation users porting from Spring must learn.; Field-injection-as-sugar still requires the field types to be constructor-passable (owned/Arc/borrow per the deferred ownership keystone) — a `#[inject]` private field on a foreign struct the macro can't name in a constructor (orphan/visibility) can't be supported, narrowing where the sugar applies vs Spring's reflective set-any-field.

**Mechanisms considered:** proc-macro emits a thin `async fn build(&ResolveCtx)->Result<Self,_>` that calls the user's hand-written `Self::new(..)` (the hand-writable artifact) — constructor injection as THE mechanism; optional/'setter' deps as constructor params typed `Option<T>` (arity=Optional) or `#[inject(default)]` filled from `Default`/expr when unsatisfiable; field-injection surface as macro sugar that still desugars to a constructor param so the bean keeps a test-callable constructor; multiple-constructor 'greediest satisfiable' as an ORDERED list of factory arms tried in order (first all-required-resolve wins), reported in diagnostics; const InjectionPlan = ordered typed params + arity + captured param NAME + qualifier metadata, consumed by autowiring-resolution; grouped optional (`Option<(A,B)>` / sub-builder) to preserve Spring's method-level all-or-nothing setter semantics

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The bean's `build` factory + InjectionPlan are emitted at the declaration site by the thin macro using absolute `::leaf_core::` paths and registered as a thin `const`-constructible descriptor (TypeId key + canonical name + an erased async-factory fn pointer + per-param dependency TypeIds/arity/qualifier) — this is exactly the `&'static ComponentDescriptor { type_id, fn make(&Ctx)->BoxFuture<Box<dyn Any>>, dependencies, .. }` shape the cross-crate doc recommends, where the fn pointer is const-constructible and erases the bean's concrete type to a uniform signature. The factory fn pointer must be BOXED-future-returning at the dyn registry boundary (RPITIT/AFIT are NOT dyn-safe per the nightly doc), so a remote crate's bean contributes a `fn(&Ctx)->BoxFuture<'_, Result<Box<dyn Any+Send+Sync>,_>>` into whatever Phase-2 discovery substrate is chosen. CRITICAL cross-crate consequence: because the bean is built by a fn POINTER, construction is fully origin-agnostic — a native bean, a WASM-module-sourced bean, or a hand-registered factory all present the same erased async-factory descriptor, so the static link-time registry ('classpath') and an optional dynamic directory-scanned/WASM source both just contribute factories (CANDIDATES, not a decision). DCE hazard is inherited and SHARP: if a contributing crate is dropped (Layer-0, never path-referenced), its bean's factory descriptor never enters the table, so a constructor param that should have resolved silently becomes NoSuchBean — the anti-DCE self-check (expected-vs-found bean/factory count per source crate) is a precondition, and this feature's AssemblyError should be able to say 'no factory for type T was registered; T's defining crate may not be linked'. Generics: a generic `Component<T>` has no monomorphized factory the linker can point at, so generic beans need an explicit `register_component!(Concrete)` per instantiation (inherited from the registry substrate); the macro must HARD-ERROR on a bare generic `#[component]`. The captured param NAME and qualifier metadata are const data per param, traveling with the descriptor with no central table. AOT-lineage friendly: a single deterministic `build` fn per bean is precisely the 'write the boring wiring code you'd have written by hand' target.

**T2 — execution context:** Construction is the ONE async step (T2 boundary owned jointly with bean-instantiation): `build(&ResolveCtx).await` resolves each param (each param resolution is itself sync SELECTION via autowiring-resolution, then an async sub-build for any not-yet-built collaborator) and may `.await` (e.g. a param that is a pooled DB handle whose own factory awaits) but MUST NOT block the executor — a blocking constructor is documented to `spawn_blocking` (Phase-2 owns the propagation mechanism). The build runs at WIRING time: a cold startup pass for eager singletons (refresh eager-instantiate), warmer for prototype/scoped beans rebuilt per acquisition; on the warm path the InjectionPlan is immutable-after-freeze and read lock-free (no global lock, charter #5) and the per-param SELECTION is allocation-light. Cancellation: because the whole bean is one awaitable producing one owned value, cancelling the build mid-flight leaks NOTHING — there is no half-populated instance and no setter phase to unwind (a strict win over a mutable two-phase build); any already-built collaborators are owned by the container/cache, not by this in-flight future. Errors propagate as a rich AssemblyError carrying the bean being built + the failing param (its TypeId, declared name, arity, and which constructor arm was attempted) + the upstream ResolveError, re-wrapped by bean-instantiation into the dependent's assembly chain (charter #9). Because immutability is total, safe publication of the finished bean is trivial (the value is frozen at creation). No teardown concern in THIS feature (destruction is lifecycle-callbacks/context-close); construction never spawns its own tasks.

#### R2 — Two-phase construct-then-populate: a `Default`/no-arg-construct step then an explicit container-driven `inject(&mut self, &ResolveCtx)` populate hook (faithful to Spring's literal construct→populate ordering & setter/field cycle tolerance)
Preserve Spring's LITERAL two-phase mechanic — instantiate first, populate second — because it is exactly what makes setter/field singleton CYCLES breakable (the half-built A is exposed to B before A's deps are set). The macro emits, per bean: (1) a cheap first-phase construct (a no-arg `Default`-like / `#[component]`-derived empty shell for the 'no-arg construction' analogue, or a constructor taking only the MANDATORY ctor-injected deps), and (2) a generated `async fn populate(&mut self, ctx: &ResolveCtx) -> Result<(),AssemblyError>` that performs SETTER/FIELD injection by assigning each `#[inject]`-marked field/setter AFTER the shell exists. Mandatory deps still go through phase-1 constructor params (the immutable core stays immutable); optional setter/field deps are assigned in phase-2 `populate`, where Spring's 'a non-required method is not called at all if any arg unsatisfiable' is realized as: the macro groups a multi-arg setter into one populate step that is skipped wholesale if any of its required args is absent, and a non-required field is left at its `Default` if unsatisfiable. The 'field set via reflection' is replaced by the macro emitting direct `self.field = resolved;` assignments (no reflection — the macro saw the field at the declaration site), so field injection is hand-writable generated code. This is the realization that can honor circular-references: bean-instantiation can expose an early `&` to the half-constructed shell between phases so a setter/field cycle resolves, while a MANDATORY constructor arg (phase-1) cycle still fails fast (no shell yet) — preserving Spring's 'constructor cycles unbreakable, setter cycles breakable' DISTINCTION exactly. InjectionPlan splits into ctor-points (phase-1, Single arity) + member-points (phase-2, mostly Optional). To NOT lose testability, the macro ALSO emits an all-args constructor (or `with_*` builder) so a test can still build the fully-wired bean without the container — preserving the POJO-testability intent even though the container path is two-phase.

**Pros:** The ONLY realization that faithfully preserves Spring's setter/field singleton CYCLE tolerance (the half-built-shell early reference) AND the matching 'constructor cycle fails, setter cycle resolves' distinction — it directly satisfies the circular-references neighbour contract that R1 must decline.; Literal construct→populate ordering maps 1:1 onto Spring's MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor-discovers-then-populateBean flow, so practitioners porting from Spring find the mental model unchanged, and the two phases align cleanly with mandatory-vs-optional ('constructor for mandatory' = phase 1, 'setter for optional' = phase 2).; Method-level all-or-nothing setter semantics ('not called at all if any arg unsatisfiable') is natural here — a populate STEP is a unit that is run or skipped wholesale — so multi-arg optional setters keep exact Spring semantics without R1's grouped-`Option<tuple>` contortion.; Field injection is real generated `self.field = ..` assignment (the macro saw the field) — no reflection, hand-writable, charter #10 — and supports patterns that genuinely need post-construction wiring (a bean referencing its fully-wired self, two-phase init) that R1 must relocate to lifecycle-callbacks.; Still recovers POJO-testability by ALSO emitting an all-args constructor/builder, so the discouraged-field-injection testability cost is mitigated even though the container uses the two-phase path.

**Cons:** Re-introduces a MUTABLE, partially-constructed bean — the exact thing Rust's ownership model and the charter's memory discipline (#5) push against: phase-2 `&mut self` means fields can't be `final`/immutable-by-default during the window, and any early `&`-exposure for cycle-breaking collides head-on with Rust aliasing (a shared `&` to a shell that phase-2 then takes `&mut` to is the borrow-checker's nightmare, forcing `RefCell`/`UnsafeCell`/`Arc<Mutex>` interior mutability — heap + lock churn that violates the 'minimize Mutex/avoid gratuitous Arc' invariant).; Safe-publication & visibility get genuinely hard (concurrency-contract neighbour): a singleton observed mid-populate by a concurrent resolver could see a half-wired bean; Spring leans on the singleton creation lock + JMM final-field semantics, but leaf would need explicit synchronization on the populate window — a per-bean lock on a hot-ish path, exactly the global/coarse locking the charter resists.; The early-half-built-reference + AOP-early-proxy interaction is the most error-prone corner of Spring (`allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping`); replicating it in Rust (expose a raw `&` that may later need to become a proxy) is a deep, bug-prone surface with no borrow-checker-friendly analogue — high implementation risk for a DISCOURAGED style.; Two construction phases = a more complex execution-context story (T2): cancellation between phase-1 and phase-2 leaves a constructed-but-unpopulated shell that must be discarded or finalized, and error propagation must distinguish 'failed to construct' from 'failed to populate' — more states than R1's single awaitable.; Spring itself discourages field injection and disables cycle-breaking by default (Boot 2.6+); faithfully replicating the mechanism that exists mainly to TOLERATE a smell spends the most complexity/unsafe-surface on the least-recommended behavior — a questionable ROI against charter #1 (DX) and #5 (memory discipline).

**Mechanisms considered:** phase-1 cheap construct (no-arg `Default`-shell or mandatory-only constructor) + phase-2 generated `async fn populate(&mut self,&ResolveCtx)`; field injection as direct generated `self.field = resolved;` assignments (no reflection, macro saw the field); setter method-level all-or-nothing as a populate STEP run-or-skipped wholesale; early `&`-exposure of the half-built shell between phases to break setter/field cycles (requires interior mutability — `RefCell`/`UnsafeCell`/`Arc<Mutex>`); ALSO emit an all-args constructor/builder so the bean stays container-free testable despite two-phase wiring; split InjectionPlan into phase-1 ctor-points (Single) + phase-2 member-points (Optional)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Cross-crate descriptor shape is similar to R1 but RICHER: the const descriptor must carry TWO erased fn pointers — a phase-1 `fn construct(&Ctx)->BoxFuture<Box<dyn Any>>` and a phase-2 `fn populate(&mut dyn Any, &Ctx)->BoxFuture<Result<(),_>>` — both boxed-future at the dyn boundary (AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe). The phase-2 populate fn must downcast `&mut dyn Any` back to the concrete type (trait upcasting / `Any::downcast_mut`, stable) before assigning fields, so the descriptor and the registry substrate must agree on the erased-handle convention (this raises the deferred type-erasure/registry-substrate keystone more sharply than R1, because populate mutates THROUGH the erased handle). Origin-agnosticism holds (a WASM-module bean could expose construct+populate fn pointers the same way) but the two-pointer ABI is heavier and the `&mut dyn Any` populate is harder to reconcile with a remote/dynamic source that may not hand back a mutable native handle — flagged as a tension. DCE/Layer-0 hazard identical to R1 (a dropped crate = missing factory = silent NoSuchBean for a phase-1 param or a silently-skipped phase-2 step), with the same self-check precondition; but the phase-2 silent-skip is MORE insidious (an optional populate step skipped because its dep's crate was GC'd looks identical to an intentionally-absent optional dep), arguing the self-check + a 'this optional injection was skipped because no candidate' debug trace matters even more here. Generics-cannot-register and macro-emitted absolute-path const descriptors are inherited unchanged. The early-shell-exposure cycle-break is a RUNTIME registry concern (the half-built handle lives in the three-level cache), purely local to the final binary's container — no cross-crate surface of its own.

**T2 — execution context:** Two async steps with a hazardous middle (T2): phase-1 `construct().await` (mandatory deps, may .await, must not block — spawn_blocking for blocking ctors) produces a shell published into the early-singleton cache; phase-2 `populate(&mut self).await` then resolves+assigns optional members. The DANGEROUS window is between phases: if cycle-breaking exposes an early `&` to the shell, a concurrent resolver could observe a partially-wired bean, so visibility/safe-publication needs explicit synchronization (the populate window guarded — a per-bean lock or interior-mutability cell), which sits uneasily with charter #5 (no gratuitous locks) and #4 (this is wiring-time so cold-ish, but prototype/scoped re-population is warmer). Cancellation is MORE complex than R1: cancelling between phase-1 and phase-2 leaves a constructed-but-unpopulated shell in the cache that bean-instantiation must evict/finalize (a real teardown obligation absent in R1); cancelling during populate leaves a partially-populated `&mut self` that must be discarded. Errors distinguish construct-failure vs populate-failure and must name which (charter #9). The `&mut self` populate fundamentally conflicts with the immutable-shared-singleton ideal — once populated the bean should be frozen, so there is an explicit 'seal' moment after phase-2 (an unenforced-by-types invariant, weaker than R1's by-construction immutability). Setter/field cycle resolution is the one capability this T2 profile uniquely enables; it is also its costliest part.

#### R3 — Typed builder / `with_*` injection points: the macro derives a per-bean compile-checked builder whose required setters are type-state-enforced and optional setters default, unifying all three styles into a single fluent assembly the compiler validates
Push intent-not-mechanism hardest by lifting the mandatory-vs-optional distinction into the TYPE SYSTEM via a derived builder, so 'an object cannot exist without its mandatory collaborators' (Spring's constructor-injection invariant) becomes a COMPILE-CHECKED type-state rather than a runtime construct/populate sequence. `#[component]` derives a `OrderServiceBuilder` where each MANDATORY dependency is a required builder step encoded in a type-state (`OrderServiceBuilder<GatewaySet>` — `.build()` only exists once every required slot's type-state flips to Set), and each OPTIONAL ('setter-style') dependency is a `with_*` method that defaults if never called (`Default`/`#[inject(default=..)]`). FIELD injection is just 'the builder set this field'. The container builds a bean by: create builder → for each required step, resolve the dep (autowiring-resolution) and call the setter → for each optional step, resolve-if-available and call-or-skip → `.build()`. This UNIFIES the three styles onto one fluent mechanism: constructor injection = required type-state steps, setter injection = optional `with_*` steps with method-level all-or-nothing (a multi-arg optional setter is one builder method skipped wholesale), field injection = a builder-assigned field — all expressed as builder calls, all hand-writable (a test calls `OrderServiceBuilder::new().gateway(mock).build()` with NO container, recovering POJO-testability MORE explicitly than R1). The builder is the single InjectionPlan: required steps carry Single arity + TypeId + captured NAME + qualifier; optional steps carry Optional arity. Two flavours for the container-driven path: (a) the container drives the builder via an erased `apply_injection_point(builder, point, resolved)` reflective-style loop, or (b) the macro emits a flat `async fn build_via_builder(&Ctx)` that calls the builder steps in order — both end at `.build()` returning the immutable bean (no mutable bean ESCAPES; mutation is confined to the builder, which is consumed). Mandatory-immutability and 'never observable invalid' are preserved because `.build()` yields a fully-formed value and the builder enforces required slots at COMPILE time — a missing mandatory dep on a hand-written call is a COMPILE error (richest diagnostic timing, charter #9), while a missing mandatory BEAN at wiring time is a startup AssemblyError.

**Pros:** Best DX & strongest invariant encoding (charter #1, #9): 'an object cannot exist without its mandatory collaborators' becomes a COMPILE-time type-state — forgetting a required dep on a hand-written/test build is a compile error (the earliest, richest diagnostic where it is legible), elevating Spring's runtime construct-time invariant to compile time for the hand path while keeping a startup AssemblyError for the missing-bean-at-wiring case.; Cleanest unification of all three styles onto ONE primitive (charter #11): constructor=required type-state step, setter=optional `with_*` step (method-level all-or-nothing as one skippable builder call — solving R1's grouped-`Option<tuple>` awkwardness AND avoiding R2's mutable two-phase bean), field=builder-assigned field; autowiring-resolution sees a uniform ordered list of builder steps.; POJO-testability is the MOST explicit and ergonomic of the three: `Builder::new().dep(mock).build()` reads like idiomatic Rust, supports partial test wiring (set only what the test needs, defaults fill the rest), and the builder is a genuinely useful hand API independent of the container — the framework-is-optional ideal made fluent.; No mutable bean ESCAPES the builder (charter #5): all mutation is confined to the builder value which is consumed by `.build()`, so the resulting bean is immutable/frozen exactly like R1 — no interior mutability, no populate-window lock, none of R2's safe-publication hazard.; Optional-with-default and per-step qualifier/name capture sit naturally on builder methods, and the type-state cleanly distinguishes 'required' (must be set, compile-enforced) from 'optional' (defaults) — a structural, readable encoding of 'constructors for mandatory, setters for optional'.

**Cons:** Heaviest macro output of the three — a type-state builder (one marker type per required slot, combinatorial-ish builder type params) is a LOT of generated code, straining charter #10's 'thin macro / cargo expand stays legible': the expansion is hand-writable in principle but verbose, and the type-state generics can produce intimidating error messages that undercut the DX win they were meant to provide.; Container-driven assembly through a type-state builder is awkward: the container resolves deps DYNAMICALLY (at runtime, by TypeId) but the builder's type-state is a COMPILE-time progression — so the container path can't ride the type-state and must either use an erased/untyped builder twin (flavour a, losing the compile-checking on the container path and duplicating the builder) or a flat macro-emitted `build_via_builder` (flavour b, in which case the type-state builder is ONLY for hand/test use and the container ignores it) — meaning the headline compile-time-invariant benefit applies to the HAND path, not the container path, a real split.; Like R1, it cannot natively break setter/field singleton CYCLES (the bean only exists after `.build()`, there is no half-built shell to early-expose) — so the circular-references contract is declined here too; the builder makes constructor-cycle-impossibility even more structural but offers no setter-cycle escape beyond Option<ObjectProvider>/@Lazy.; Generic beans + builders multiply the generics problem: a `Component<T>` builder is itself generic, and 'generics cannot be registered' (T1) means the container path still needs a concrete per-instantiation factory — so the builder elegance doesn't extend to generic beans on the discovery path, and the type-state markers add monomorphization cost.; Most divergent from Spring's literal model (a builder is not how Spring injects), so while the INTENT is faithful, a practitioner cross-referencing Spring's construct→populate flow has the largest translation gap; and the optional-default-on-builder can silently mask a misconfiguration (an optional dep defaulting when the user expected it wired) unless surfaced in the condition-report/diagnostic.

**Mechanisms considered:** macro-derived type-state builder: required deps as compile-enforced type-state steps (`.build()` only exists when all Set), optional deps as defaulting `with_*` methods; method-level all-or-nothing optional setter as ONE skippable builder method (no grouped-tuple, no mutable bean); container path via either an erased untyped builder twin (flavour a) OR a flat macro-emitted `build_via_builder(&Ctx)` ending in `.build()` (flavour b); field injection as a builder-assigned field; the builder IS the single InjectionPlan carrying per-step arity/TypeId/NAME/qualifier; compile-time fail for missing mandatory dep on the HAND/test path; startup AssemblyError for missing mandatory BEAN on the container path; Option/`Default`/`#[inject(default=..)]` for optional builder steps

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Discovery descriptor is essentially R1's (a single erased async-factory fn pointer per bean — the flat `build_via_builder` form, since the type-state builder can't cross the dyn registry boundary), so the cross-crate ABI is the const `&'static ComponentDescriptor { type_id, fn make(&Ctx)->BoxFuture<Box<dyn Any>>, dependencies, name/qualifier per step }` shape the cross-crate doc recommends, boxed-future at the dyn boundary (AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe). The type-state builder itself is a HAND/test-facing API that lives entirely within the defining crate and does NOT cross crate boundaries — only the flat factory does — so the verbose builder generics impose no cross-crate ABI cost (a plus: the cross-crate surface stays as thin as R1's). Origin-agnostic identically to R1 (native/WASM/hand-registered all present the same flat factory; the builder is a local convenience). DCE/Layer-0 and generics-cannot-register hazards are inherited from R1 unchanged (dropped crate → missing flat factory → silent NoSuchBean; bare-generic `#[component]` must hard-error; concrete instantiations need `register_component!`). One extra T1 nuance: because optional builder steps DEFAULT silently, a cross-module misconfiguration (an optional dep whose providing crate was GC'd) defaults rather than errors AND looks intentional — so the self-check + condition-report-style 'this optional step defaulted because no candidate from any linked crate' diagnostic is important for cross-crate legibility, same concern as R2's silent-skip but via defaulting. The compile-time mandatory-slot check is purely WITHIN a crate (the builder type-state), giving no cross-crate guarantee — a bean in crate A whose mandatory dep lives only in an unlinked crate B still fails at wiring time, not compile time.

**T2 — execution context:** Construction is one async step like R1 (the flat `build_via_builder(&Ctx).await` on the container path): create builder, await each required dep's resolution+sub-build, call the step; resolve-or-skip each optional step; `.build()` yields the immutable bean. May `.await` (collaborator sub-builds, pooled handles), must not block (spawn_blocking for blocking work — Phase-2 owns propagation). Runs at wiring time (cold for eager singletons, warm for prototype/scoped). Cancellation is as CLEAN as R1: the in-flight builder is just a local value owned by the build future; cancelling drops it, leaking nothing (no escaped mutable bean, no early-exposed shell, no populate-window lock) — strictly better than R2's two-phase teardown obligation. Immutability/safe-publication is trivial (the bean is frozen at `.build()`), so concurrent resolvers never see a half-built bean (no per-bean lock needed beyond bean-instantiation's normal creation guard). Errors propagate as AssemblyError naming the bean + the failing builder step (its TypeId/name/arity) + upstream ResolveError (charter #9); a missing-mandatory-BEAN at wiring time is the startup failure, while a missing-mandatory-dep on the HAND path was already a compile error (the timing split). No setter/field cycle resolution (no half-built shell) — same decline as R1. No teardown concern beyond the dropped builder on cancellation.

### Rust clashes
- No post-construction field reflection / no container-driven setter calls: Spring sets `@Autowired` fields via reflection and calls setters on a constructed instance AFTER no-arg construction. Rust has NO runtime reflection to set a private field and NO mechanism for the container to call a setter on an arbitrary bean — so 'field injection' and 'setter injection' have no native runtime analogue. Every member that needs injection must be CAPTURED at the declaration site by the thin macro (it saw the `#[inject]` field/setter) and re-expressed as either a constructor param (R1), a generated `self.field = ..` assignment in a populate fn (R2), or a builder step (R3). The reflective 'discover injection metadata against the merged definition' (Spring's MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor) becomes compile-time macro capture into a const InjectionPlan.
- Construction is natively all-at-once and immutable, not construct-then-populate: Rust's idiomatic construction returns a fully-owned, immutable value from one constructor; there is no partially-constructed mutable object the container can hold and populate later. This makes CONSTRUCTOR injection the free, idiomatic default (final-like immutability and 'never observable invalid' come for free) but makes SETTER/FIELD injection's two-phase mechanic require a deliberate mutable window (`&mut self`/interior mutability) that fights ownership and the charter's memory discipline — the impedance is inverted from the JVM, where two-phase is easy and immutability is the effort.
- Setter/field singleton CYCLE tolerance has no borrow-checker-friendly form: Spring breaks setter/field cycles by exposing an early raw reference to a half-built singleton. In Rust, exposing a shared `&` to a value that a later phase mutates via `&mut` is an aliasing violation — it forces `RefCell`/`UnsafeCell`/`Arc<Mutex>` interior mutability (heap + lock churn against charter #5) and reopens the AOP-early-proxy `allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping` hazard. So the 'constructor cycles fail, setter cycles resolve' DISTINCTION is cheap to honor on the fail side and expensive/unsafe on the resolve side; realizations that keep construction single-phase (R1/R3) cannot offer setter-cycle resolution at all without an explicit deferral primitive.
- 'Single constructor needs no @Autowired' and 'multiple @Autowired ctors, pick greediest satisfiable' have no overload/reflection analogue: Rust has no constructor overloading and no reflective constructor enumeration. 'No annotation needed for the single obvious constructor' maps to the macro deriving the factory from the one constructor / the `#[inject]` fields; 'greediest satisfiable among multiple' must be re-expressed as an ORDERED list of candidate factory arms the macro emits and the container tries in order (first all-required-resolve wins) — a deterministic try-in-order, NOT JVM constructor selection, and the silent-fall-through-to-a-smaller-arm risk must be made loud in diagnostics.
- Method-level all-or-nothing optional setter ('not called at all if ANY arg unsatisfiable') vs per-value Option: Spring's optional setter is a method whose WHOLE invocation is skipped if any argument is unsatisfiable. Rust's natural optional is per-value `Option<T>`, which is per-PARAM, not per-method-group. Preserving the all-or-nothing group semantics needs either a grouped optional (`Option<(A,B)>` / sub-builder, R1) or a populate/builder STEP that is run-or-skipped wholesale (R2/R3) — the group is the unit, and a naive per-param `Option` would silently inject a partial group Spring would have skipped entirely.
- Required-ness/arity (Single vs Optional/@Nullable/required=false) is not runtime-inferable: Spring reads required-ness off `@Autowired(required=)`/`Optional<T>`/`@Nullable` reflectively. In Rust the arity of each injection point must be captured by the macro at the declaration site (a required param = Single, an `Option<T>` param/`#[inject(optional)]` = Optional) and frozen into the InjectionPlan as data — this feature DEFINES that arity that autowiring-resolution then uses to map zero-candidates to NoSuchBean (Single) vs absent (Optional). There is no `@Nullable`-as-runtime-signal; it is compile-time-captured data.
- The injection-point TYPE the bean accepts is bounded by ownership (the deferred keystone): a constructor param / builder step / populated field is typed `T` vs `Arc<dyn Trait>` vs `&T` per the scope/ownership model leaf has NOT yet decided. This feature must keep the injection-point type ABSTRACT (an ErasedHandle the container hands in, downcast/coerced to the declared collaborator type) and must NOT prejudge whether collaborators arrive as owned values, `Arc<dyn Trait>`, or borrows — the same origin-agnostic handle whether the collaborator was natively built, hand-registered, or sourced dynamically.
- Field-injection-on-a-foreign/private field can't be synthesized: Spring's reflection sets ANY field regardless of visibility or defining module. A Rust macro can only emit a constructor call / field assignment for fields it can NAME and ACCESS at the declaration site — a private field on a type from another crate, or a field the macro's emitted (unhygienic, absolute-path) code cannot reach, cannot be injected. So the set of field-injectable members is narrower than Spring's reflective set, bounded by Rust visibility/orphan rules.
- AOT 'pick a deterministic preferred constructor' is the NATIVE state, not a special mode: Spring's AOT must deterministically choose one constructor because there's no instance to probe; leaf is in that situation ALWAYS (no instance ever probed — the macro picks the factory/constructor at compile time deterministically). So Spring's AOT constructor-selection discipline is leaf's normal path, which is an advantage — but it means the choice of which constructor/arm is the factory is fixed at macro-expansion time and is part of the bean's stable descriptor ABI.
- No exceptions for the assembly chain: Spring nests BeanCreationException → UnsatisfiedDependencyException(InjectionPoint) as thrown/wrapped exceptions across the construct/populate flow. Rust must build the AssemblyError value by hand — carrying which bean was being built, which injection point (constructor arm/param or setter/field step) failed, its arity, and the upstream ResolveError — and re-wrap it via Result at each level (bean-instantiation owns the outer wrap). Two-phase realizations (R2) additionally must distinguish construct-failure from populate-failure in that value.

### Interactions
- **autowiring-resolution** — THE primary downstream seam. This feature DEFINES the injection points (their shape: produced TypeId, generic-arg TypeIds, captured declared param/field NAME for the name tie-break, qualifier request, and Single|Optional arity) and packages them as the per-bean InjectionPlan; autowiring-resolution CONSUMES each point and runs its fixed tie-break ladder to SELECT the single winning candidate (or None/Ambiguous). Strict split: injection-styles says 'here is a point needing exactly-one T with this name/qualifier/arity', autowiring-resolution says 'this candidate wins (or fails)'. The arity this feature captures (constructor param = Single by default, setter/field optional = Optional) is precisely what autowiring-resolution maps to NoSuchBean-vs-absent. The captured declared NAME is the implicit-qualifier string autowiring-resolution uses at its step-3 shortcut AND step-5 tie-break.
- **bean-instantiation** — THE primary downstream consumer & owner of BOTH the async/cache boundary AND the construct→populate ordering. This feature emits the per-bean construction RECIPE (the erased async-factory fn pointer(s) + InjectionPlan); bean-instantiation OWNS calling it: awaiting the build (the executor boundary, the boxed-future dyn factory call, spawn_blocking guidance for blocking constructors), publishing the finished bean into the singleton/three-level cache under the creation guard, executing the construct→populate ordering this feature defines (R2's doCreateBean-analogue), exposing the early-singleton reference between phases (R2), and the cancellation/teardown obligations (notably R2's between-phase shell eviction). bean-instantiation re-wraps this feature's AssemblyError into the dependent's assembly chain (the UnsatisfiedDependency analogue).
- **circular-references** — Style-dependent and load-bearing: this feature's CHOICE of construction model determines what cycles are possible. Constructor-injection cycles are LOGICALLY unsatisfiable and must fail fast (all three realizations honor this — no half-built instance exists). Setter/field singleton cycles are TOLERABLE only if construction is two-phase with an early half-built reference (ONLY R2 can offer this; R1/R3 structurally cannot and must decline, pushing cycle-breaking onto deferral-primitives @Lazy/ObjectProvider). So this feature defines the 'constructor cycle fails, setter cycle may resolve' distinction, and circular-references owns the early-singleton-exposure machinery that R2 would hook into. Whether leaf even SUPPORTS setter-cycle tolerance is entangled with which realization wins (a phase2Question).
- **deferral-primitives** — Provides the optional/lazy/cycle-breaking escape hatches this feature's points may use: an injection point typed ObjectProvider<T>/Option<T>/@Lazy changes the point's arity (Optional) and timing (deferred), and is the SANCTIONED way to break a cycle that constructor injection cannot (R1/R3 lean on this where R2 would use early-exposure). This feature captures 'this point is a deferral primitive' as part of the point's descriptor; deferral-primitives owns the 0..1/0..N/lazy access shapes underneath. 'Constructors for mandatory, setters for optional' interlocks with 'or ObjectProvider for explicit-optional/lazy'.
- **lifecycle-callbacks** — The post-construction work that this feature's single-phase model (R1/R3) RELOCATES setter-like behavior to: a bean that genuinely needs to act on its fully-wired self does so in an afterPropertiesSet/@PostConstruct-analogue AFTER construction, not in a setter phase. lifecycle-callbacks runs ordered init AFTER this feature's construction completes (and after any R2 populate phase). The boundary: injection-styles WIRES collaborators in; lifecycle-callbacks RUNS initialization logic once wiring is done. Prototypes get init callbacks but no destruction (relevant because this feature's construction recipe is re-run per prototype acquisition).
- **bean-definition** — Pure producer/consumer: this feature's per-bean InjectionPlan (ordered injection points + arity + captured names/qualifiers + the chosen-constructor/factory-arm identity) is metadata frozen onto the bean's (merged) definition — analogous to Spring discovering+caching injection metadata against the MergedBeanDefinition. bean-definition owns the metamodel (constructorArgumentValues/propertyValues analogues, the preferred-constructor attribute, parent→merged inheritance); this feature populates the injection-point portion of it at the declaration site. The 'greediest satisfiable' ordered-arms list is definition data.
- **concurrency-contract** — This feature's construction model determines safe-publication difficulty. R1/R3 (single-phase, immutable result) make safe publication TRIVIAL — the bean is frozen the instant it exists, exactly the 'fully-built singleton safely published' guarantee. R2 (two-phase mutable populate) STRAINS it — a concurrently-observed mid-populate bean needs explicit synchronization on the populate window. concurrency-contract owns the safe-publication/visibility guarantee for finished singletons and frames scope-choice as the concurrency lever; this feature must construct beans in a way that upholds that guarantee, which is free for R1/R3 and costly for R2.
- **value-injection** — Sibling injection-point KIND on the SAME plan: a constructor param / setter / field may be a @Value/${property}/#{expr} point rather than a bean point. This feature owns the injection-point STRUCTURE (which params/fields, their arity, mandatory-vs-optional); value-injection (with type-conversion) RESOLVES the value points while autowiring-resolution resolves the bean points. The InjectionPlan must tag each point's kind (bean vs value) so the right resolver handles it; a value point pre-empts bean resolution for that param (Spring's doResolveDependency step-2 suggested-value), and this feature's #[inject(default=..)] for optional setters overlaps value-injection's defaulting — boundary must be drawn so they don't both own a param's default.
- **component-stereotypes** — Provides the DECLARATION surface that triggers this feature: a #[component]/#[service]-marked struct (or #[bean] fn) is what the thin macro reads to derive the construction recipe + InjectionPlan. component-stereotypes owns 'this type is a managed bean'; this feature owns 'here is HOW it is constructed and what it needs injected'. The 'single constructor needs no @Autowired' convention lives at this junction — a stereotyped struct with one obvious constructor / all-#[inject] fields needs no per-member annotation.
- **container-hierarchy** — Injection through this feature's points is hierarchy-aware (a local miss for a constructor/setter/field dependency delegates upward to the parent context, injecting the lowest-level match) — but that traversal is owned by autowiring-resolution/bean-registry; this feature just emits points that are resolved against the hierarchy-merged candidate set. Mirrors Spring's 'injection is hierarchy-aware even though listing is local-only' — the points this feature defines participate in the hierarchy-aware injection path.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Constructor-first-collapse (R1) vs literal two-phase construct-then-populate (R2) vs derived type-state builder (R3) — or a blend (R1/R3 single-phase immutable construction as the default for the recommended constructor style + R2's two-phase ONLY as an opt-in escape hatch for the discouraged setter/field-cycle case)? This is the keystone shape decision for THIS feature and is entangled with the scope/lifetime/ownership keystone (do collaborators arrive owned, Arc<dyn Trait>, or borrowed — which dictates whether a bean can even be re-populated mutably) and with circular-references (only a two-phase model can break setter cycles).
- Does leaf SUPPORT setter/field singleton CYCLE tolerance at all? Spring tolerates it but discourages it and disables it by default since Boot 2.6, and AOT contexts fail on it entirely. Given Rust's aliasing rules make the early-half-built-reference mechanism require interior mutability/unsafe (against charter #5), is the right call to NOT support setter cycles (fail fast on ALL cycles, R1/R3, pushing cycle-breaking to @Lazy/ObjectProvider) — which is simpler, safer, AOT-aligned, and matches Spring Boot's own default — or to pay the cost (R2) for literal parity? Couples to circular-references, deferral-primitives, concurrency-contract, and the freeze/AOT-lineage shape.
- Where is mandatory-dependency fail-fast surfaced (charter #9 timing)? R3 can make a missing MANDATORY dep on the hand/test path a COMPILE error (richest timing) via type-state, but the container path (dep is a missing BEAN, not a missing arg) is inherently a wiring-time/startup failure regardless of realization. Is a split acceptable (some mandatory-dep errors compile-time, others startup-time), and can the startup AssemblyError be made rich enough (which bean, which constructor arm/param, which crate the missing factory should have come from) that pushing to compile-time isn't worth the type-state complexity? Shares the unified-diagnostics meta-concept and the compile-vs-runtime split.
- How are the THREE styles surfaced in the DX without re-importing field-injection's documented harm? Spring offers all three but discourages field injection for untestability. Does leaf offer field-injection SYNTAX (sugar that still desugars to a test-callable constructor/builder, R1/R3 — gets DX parity, designs out the harm) or omit field injection entirely (forcing constructor/builder, maximally opinionated)? And does it keep a setter-style surface at all, or fold 'optional' purely into Option<T>/with-default? Cross-cuts component-stereotypes, the macro surface, and DX #1 vs opinionatedness.
- 'Greediest satisfiable among multiple constructors' — keep it or drop it? It is a JVM constructor-overload-resolution feature; in Rust it becomes a deterministic try-ordered-factory-arms loop that risks silently falling through to a smaller arm. Is multiple-constructor support worth the silent-fall-through diagnostic burden, or should leaf require a SINGLE deterministic factory per bean (simpler, AOT-clean, matches leaf's always-AOT-like no-instance-to-probe situation) and force the 'multiple ways to build' case onto explicit factory-bean/@Bean-fn registration? Couples to bean-instantiation's determinism and factory-bean.
- The injection-point TYPE and the erased-handle convention (the deferred type-erasure/registry-substrate + scope/ownership keystones): a constructor param / builder step / populated field is typed T vs Arc<dyn Trait> vs &T per the undecided ownership model, and R2's populate mutates THROUGH an &mut dyn Any erased handle (raising the substrate question more sharply). How does the container hand a resolved collaborator into an injection point WITHOUT this feature prejudging owned-vs-Arc-vs-borrow, and is the convention uniform across native/hand-registered/dynamically-sourced (WASM) collaborators? Cannot be settled here.
- Method-level all-or-nothing optional setter semantics — preserve faithfully or simplify? Spring skips a whole optional setter method if ANY of its args is unsatisfiable. Realizing this needs a grouped optional (R1's Option<tuple>) or a run-or-skip-wholesale step (R2/R3). Is the multi-arg-optional-group worth preserving, or does leaf simplify to per-value Option<T> (per-param, losing the group semantics but simpler)? Minor but a genuine parity-vs-simplicity call coupled to value-injection's defaulting overlap.
- The two-source discovery duality for construction recipes: each bean contributes an erased async-factory descriptor to the static link-time registry (default 'classpath'), and an optional dynamic directory-scanned/WASM-module source could contribute factories the same way (origin-agnostic fn pointers). For R2 specifically, can a dynamically-sourced bean expose a two-pointer construct+populate ABI and a mutable native handle for populate, or does the two-phase model only work for natively-compiled beans (narrowing where setter/field injection applies by origin)? CANDIDATES, not a decision — couples to the cross-crate composition model and the DCE self-check precondition.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the dedicated injection-styles section (751-771: the three styles, constructor-preferred-for-required, the single-ctor-needs-no-@Autowired + only-one-@Autowired(required=true) + multiple-must-be-required=false-greediest-satisfiable mechanics, setter-for-optional with method-level all-or-nothing 'not called at all if any arg unsatisfiable', field-injection-discouraged-for-untestability, and the load-bearing design intent that dependencies are part of IDENTITY not bolted-on state — constructor injection encodes the class invariant in the type system, gives final/immutable/non-null/never-observable-invalid, and the deepest 'why' is total decoupling from the container so `new OrderService(mock)` tests with NO container/reflection), the POJO/Service-Locator-rejection framing (86-98), the construct→populate + setter/field-cycle-tolerance-via-early-singleton mechanics and the constructor-cycle-fails distinction (773-785, 2192, 2533), the MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor-discovers-injection-metadata hook (2454), and the AOT deterministic-preferred-constructor requirement (2686).\n\nTHE most important framing I held throughout: this feature is DISTINCT from its neighbours. It is NOT autowiring-resolution (which selects which single bean WINS for a point — a strict downstream seam consuming the points this feature DEFINES), and NOT bean-instantiation (which owns the async build/cache/cycle MACHINERY — the downstream owner of the execution boundary and the construct→populate ORDERING). injection-styles owns: (1) the SHAPE of an injection point (its typed dependency, captured declared NAME, qualifier, and Single|Optional ARITY that downstream maps to NoSuchBean-vs-absent), (2) the CONSTRUCTION/POPULATION recipe for one bean (which constructor/factory/builder, mandatory-vs-optional, and the ordering of construct-then-populate-if-any), and (3) the POJO-testability + mandatory-immutability DISCIPLINE that is the feature's actual soul.\n\nThe single sharpest Rust impedance, which drove the divergence of the three realizations: Rust's construction is NATIVELY all-at-once-via-constructor returning a fully-owned immutable value — so CONSTRUCTOR injection is the free, idiomatic default (immutability, never-observable-invalid, POJO-testability all come for free) while SETTER and FIELD injection have NO native mechanism (no post-construction field reflection, no container-driven setter on private fields, no partially-constructed mutable bean). This INVERTS the JVM situation (where two-phase is easy and immutability is the effort). So the three realizations diverge precisely on how they re-express the non-native setter/field styles: R1 collapses them into constructor params (Option<T>/default) keeping construction single-phase and immutable (most faithful to the discipline, declines setter-cycle tolerance); R2 literally replicates Spring's two-phase construct-then-populate (the ONLY one that can break setter/field cycles, at the cost of a mutable bean + interior-mutability/locking that fights charter #5); R3 lifts mandatory-vs-optional into a compile-checked type-state builder (best DX/invariant encoding + compile-time fail for the hand path, but heaviest macro output and the container path can't ride the type-state). A likely Phase-2 blend: R1/R3 single-phase immutable as the default for the RECOMMENDED constructor style, R2's two-phase as an opt-in escape hatch only for the discouraged setter/field-cycle case.\n\nThree things to carry forward: (1) Constructor injection is leaf's NATIVE/default and the others are re-expressions, not co-equal mechanisms — this is intent-faithful (Spring prefers constructor too) AND idiomatic-Rust-faithful. (2) Setter/field CYCLE tolerance is the one capability only the two-phase model offers, and it is expensive/unsafe in Rust (aliasing → interior mutability) — and Spring itself disables it by default since Boot 2.6 and AOT fails on it — so whether leaf supports it at all is a genuine open question I flagged, not resolved. (3) Field injection's documented HARM is untestability; R1/R3 design that harm OUT by keeping a test-callable constructor/builder even for the field-injection surface, which is the rust-native way to honor the intent while still offering the ergonomic surface.\n\nI refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization keeps the injection-point collaborator type ABSTRACT (an erased handle, owned-vs-Arc<dyn Trait>-vs-borrow deferred to the ownership keystone), keeps the construction recipe an origin-agnostic erased async-factory fn-pointer descriptor (so native/hand-registered/dynamic-WASM sources all slot in identically — the two-source static-link + dynamic-directory duality is a CANDIDATE I noted, not a decision), keeps the async build owned by bean-instantiation (this feature declares the plan; the .await happens downstream), and stays substrate-neutral on discovery (the InjectionPlan + factory are macro-emitted const data with absolute paths; how they're collected cross-crate is Phase-2's linkme/inventory/codegen call). Boxed futures at the dyn factory boundary are forced (RPITIT/AFIT not dyn-safe per the nightly doc). Naming ('InjectionPlan', 'ResolveCtx', 'AssemblyError', 'build', 'populate', 'with_*', 'ErasedHandle', '#[inject]', '#[component]') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (751-771 the three styles + design intent + mechanics; 86-98 POJO/Service-Locator-rejection; 773-785 + 2192 + 2533 setter/field-cycle-tolerance + constructor-cycle-fails distinction + early-singleton exposure; 2454 MergedBeanDefinitionPostProcessor injection-metadata discovery; 2686 AOT deterministic-preferred-constructor; 1928 final-like visibility / not-runtime-thread-safe; 2698 injection-is-hierarchy-aware); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3 — esp. scope/ownership + type-erasure/registry substrate, topology §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/A/B DCE silent-failure + self-check mandate, thin const-descriptor with erased async-factory fn pointer + absolute paths, generics-cannot-register + register_component! escape hatch, scope-discovery-not-global guardrail); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (RPITIT/AFIT NOT dyn-safe → boxed futures at the dyn factory boundary, trait upcasting 1.86 + Any/downcast for the erased handle, const TypeId 1.91 for registry keys, no async Drop → container-driven shutdown, specialization avoided/unsound).

## Deferral Primitives (ObjectProvider / Provider / @Lazy)  `[deferral-primitives]`

Spring's deferral primitives make deferred / optional(0..1) / multiple(0..N) / lazy / cycle-breaking access EXPLICIT IN THE TYPE SIGNATURE, keeping the eager-by-default model intact (docs 787-796, 2559-2578, 824, 2006, 2616, 2621-2622). The intent to replicate (intent, NOT JVM mechanism): (1) A FAMILY of consumer-side lookup handles of ascending richness — ObjectFactory<T> (just getObject()) -> ObjectProvider<T> (adds getIfAvailable/getIfUnique/stream/orderedStream; the recommended sophisticated tool) -> jakarta Provider<T> (JSR-330 get(), basic lazy only). (2) THE THREE-TIER STRICTNESS LADDER is the soul: getObject() is STRICT (never absent; errors NoSuchBean on zero, NoUniqueBean on ambiguity); getIfAvailable() tolerates ABSENCE ONLY (None on zero, but STILL errors on ambiguity — 'available' = optionality w.r.t. absence, the most-missed semantic); getIfUnique() tolerates BOTH (None on zero OR ambiguity). Optionality/not-unique tolerance lives in the METHOD CHOICE at the moment of use, NOT in a startup-resolved annotation attribute. (3) EACH PROVIDER IS A LIVE HANDLE — it holds NO instance, RE-RESOLVES against current container state on every call, deliberately does NOT extend Supplier (so it is never mistaken for a cached in-memory value: getObject can throw and re-resolve), giving the same lifecycle safety as a scoped proxy but with an honest, debuggable call SITE rather than hidden bytecode. (4) stream()/orderedStream() are LAZY (filterable, e.g. stream(Predicate), BEFORE any bean is created) and only orderedStream() applies the @Order/@Priority/Ordered comparator (stream() is registration-order); injecting List<T> is EAGER, stream() is lazy. (5) Uniqueness HONORS @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate (the getIfUnique() resolves cleanly when every other same-type bean is default-candidate=false — docs 2621), but @Primary/@Fallback affect ONLY single-valued points: streams/collections take ALL type-matching beans regardless (docs 2622). (6) @Lazy on an INJECTION POINT injects a lazy-resolution proxy resolving on first use (cycle-break/expensive-defer) — the docs themselves call it 'rather limited' (always injected even when no bean exists, so a missing target surfaces only as a first-call exception) and PREFER ObjectProvider.getIfAvailable() for honest optional+lazy. (7) v4/v7 ESCALATION: ObjectProvider/@Lazy are the documented REQUIRED remedy for breaking explicit injected cycles under AOT (which now fail fast) and the MANDATORY glue for @Bean(bootstrap=BACKGROUND) — a non-@Lazy/non-Provider direct dependent BLOCKS on the background bean, defeating parallelism (docs 2006, 2593). The deepest Rust impedance: Spring's provider is a runtime-reflective live handle re-querying a mutable BeanFactory, and @Lazy is a CGLIB/JDK transparent proxy that LOOKS like T — Rust has neither runtime reflection nor transparent runtime subclassing, so the provider must become a typed handle holding an origin-agnostic erased resolution closure + the captured injection-point descriptor (TypeId, generic-arg TypeIds, qualifier, arity), and @Lazy's transparent proxy must become either a deferral-VISIBLE-in-the-type handle (honest, the docs' own preference) or a generated trait-newtype (trait-typed-only, boxed-future shims at the async dyn boundary). And critically: in async leaf, getObject()/get() is an .await whose execution context, blocking risk, and cancellation must be explicit — Spring's synchronous live-handle lookup becomes an async resolution this feature must contextualize.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — The provider family as concrete generic handle TYPES over an origin-agnostic erased resolver closure; the strictness ladder is hand-written methods, deferral is visible in the type (Spring-faithful, type-honest)
Realize the family as a small set of concrete, generic, public handle types in leaf-core, each a thin wrapper over an origin-agnostic resolver and a statically-captured injection-point descriptor — laziness/optionality VISIBLE in the type, exactly the docs' stated intent ('encoding deferral in the type keeps the eager model intact while making it honest'). The macro, at an injection point typed `ObjectProvider<MyService>` (or `Provider<MyService>`, or `ObjectFactory<MyService>`), emits construction of that handle INSTEAD of eager-resolving `MyService`: `ObjectProvider::new(resolver_for::<MyService>(container), InjectionPoint { produced: TypeId, generic_args: &[TypeId], qualifier: Option<QualifierReq>, name: &'static str })`. The handle holds (a) a CLONE of an origin-agnostic resolution closure — `Resolve = Arc<dyn Fn(&InjectionPoint, Arity) -> BoxFuture<'_, Result<Resolved, ResolveError>> + Send + Sync>` that re-enters the container's autowiring-resolution+candidate-resolver each call (the LIVE-HANDLE property: holds no instance, re-resolves every call) — and (b) the frozen descriptor. THE THREE-TIER LADDER is hand-written async methods that differ ONLY in how they map the shared `Resolved{None|One|Ambiguous}` outcome (the same three-outcome enum autowiring-resolution returns): `get_object().await` = strict (None->Err(NoSuchBean), Ambiguous->Err(NoUniqueBean), One->Ok); `get_if_available().await` = tolerate-absence (None->Ok(None), Ambiguous->Err(NoUniqueBean) — the most-missed semantic, ambiguity STILL errors), `get_if_unique().await` = tolerate-both (None|Ambiguous->Ok(None)). `ObjectFactory::get_object` = just the strict arm; `Provider::get` (jakarta analogue) = strict-basic, no optional/unique/stream richness. The MULTIPLE arms — `stream()`/`ordered_stream()` — return a lazy async iterator over ALL type-matching candidates (bypassing the single-valued @Primary/@Fallback tie-break per docs 2622; ordered_stream applies the SHARED @Order/@Priority comparator collection-injection owns, stream is registration-order), and a `stream_filtered(pred)` filters candidate DESCRIPTORS before any construction (docs 2573 lazy filtering). For @Lazy on an injection point: lead with the SAME handle family — `@Lazy MyService` desugars to an internal `Lazy<MyService>` (essentially `ObjectProvider<MyService>` + cache-on-first-get for singleton targets via a `OnceCell`, no-cache re-resolve for shorter scopes per docs 2589) the consumer `.get().await`s; this is the docs'-preferred honest form. The resolver closure is ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC: it calls the container's resolve, so a native bean, a FactoryBean product, a WASM-module-backed bean, or a test double all resolve identically — the handle never assumes native construction. All construction-of-the-winner stays async and owned by bean-instantiation; this feature only packages the lookup.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity to the docs' EXPLICIT preference: deferral is VISIBLE in the type (`ObjectProvider<T>`/`Provider<T>`/`Lazy<T>`), the three-tier strictness ladder is three small hand-written async methods over one shared Resolved enum, and the live-handle 're-resolve every call, hold no instance' property is structural (the handle owns only a closure + descriptor, never a built value) — maximally teachable and 1:1 with getObject/getIfAvailable/getIfUnique.; The strictness ladder reuses autowiring-resolution's existing three-outcome Resolved{None|One|Ambiguous} verbatim — each tier is just a different match arm mapping that outcome, so optionality/uniqueness is decided at the CALL SITE (method choice) not at startup, exactly Spring's intent, and there is zero new selection logic (this feature consumes selection, never re-implements it).; Origin-agnostic by construction: the resolver is an erased closure over the container, so native / FactoryBean-product / WASM-module / test-double targets all resolve through the identical path — a dynamic WASM-module 'classpath' slots in with zero provider-side changes (the handle of dyn Trait never assumes native construction, honoring the guardrail).; Async-honest (charter §2.3): get_object()/get_if_available()/get() are `.await`, so the execution-context cost of a deferred lookup is EXPLICIT at the call site (the developer wrote `.await`) rather than hidden behind Spring's transparent synchronous proxy — and stream()/stream_filtered() are genuinely lazy (filter descriptors before constructing anything), matching docs 2573.; Unifies @Lazy-at-injection-point with the provider family (charter §2.11): `Lazy<T>` IS 'ObjectProvider<T> + cache-on-first-get', so leaf ships ONE handle family rather than a separate proxy mechanism — and lazy-initialization's injection-point case delegates here rather than inventing a parallel deferral type (the catalog's tightest-coupling interaction realized as code sharing).; Minimal allocation on the common path (charter §2.5): the handle is a cloned Arc-closure + a `&'static` descriptor (small), constructed once at the consumer's populate; getObject re-resolves but allocates only the BoxFuture at the dyn construction boundary (unavoidable since AFIT/RPITIT are not dyn-safe), and getIfAvailable's None/One arms allocate nothing beyond that.

**Cons:** Diverges from Spring's transparent-@Lazy DX: `@Lazy MyService` cannot be a drop-in `MyService` the consumer uses normally — it becomes a `Lazy<MyService>` the consumer must `.get().await`, more honest but more ceremony, and the docs DID ship a transparent proxy for the 'inject T, use T' feel; R1 deliberately trades that transparency for honesty (a DX-vs-fidelity call, §2.1).; The live-handle closure must hold an origin-agnostic container BACK-REFERENCE that outlives a single resolution (the provider is stored on the consuming bean and re-resolves for the bean's whole life), which pins a container handle — likely an `Arc`-ish reference in tension with charter §2.5 (minimize Arc); the lifetime/ownership of that back-reference is the deferred scope/ownership keystone this feature must NOT prejudge, so the handle keeps it abstract (`Resolve`), leaving a real open question.; A handle PER provider-typed injection point means each such field carries a cloned resolver closure — fine for a few, but a bean with many `ObjectProvider<_>` fields multiplies the stored closures; the descriptor is `&'static` (cheap) but the Arc-closure clone is per-field heap, a small but real cost vs Spring's shared BeanFactory reference.; The Resolved enum + the resolver closure couple this feature to autowiring-resolution's exact outcome shape AND to whatever container handle the scope/ownership model picks — if Phase 2 chooses borrows-with-lifetimes over Arc for the container handle, the `'static` provider stored on a bean may not be expressible without boxing, forcing a rework of the handle's lifetime story.; stream()/ordered_stream() returning a lazy async iterator over candidates needs a streaming surface over the candidate set (an async Stream or a hand-rolled poll-next) that bean-registry must expose lazily — more machinery than the single-valued arms, and the ordering comparator must be the SHARED one collection-injection uses or single-vs-stream ordering diverges (a duplication hazard, §2.11).; jakarta Provider<T> (only get()) vs ObjectFactory<T> (only get_object()) are near-duplicates differing by name/origin — shipping both for standards-compatibility is faithful but adds two thin types whose distinction (JSR-330 vs Spring-native) is cosmetic in Rust, slight surface bloat.

**Mechanisms considered:** concrete generic handle types `ObjectProvider<T>` / `ObjectFactory<T>` / `Provider<T>` / `Lazy<T>` in leaf-core, deferral visible in the type; origin-agnostic erased resolver closure `Arc<dyn Fn(&InjectionPoint, Arity) -> BoxFuture<Result<Resolved, ResolveError>>>` (live handle: holds no instance, re-resolves each call) — substrate/origin-neutral; the three-tier strictness ladder as three async methods over the SHARED Resolved{None|One|Ambiguous} enum (get_object=strict, get_if_available=tolerate-absence-only, get_if_unique=tolerate-both); macro-captured `InjectionPoint{produced TypeId, generic-arg TypeIds, qualifier, declared name, Arity}` emitted via absolute ::leaf_core paths, thin (no logic); lazy async `stream()`/`ordered_stream()`/`stream_filtered(pred)` over ALL candidates (bypassing single-valued primary/fallback per docs 2622), ordered via the SHARED @Order/@Priority comparator; `Lazy<T>` = ObjectProvider<T> + OnceCell cache-on-first-get for singleton targets / re-resolve for shorter scopes (docs 2589), as the honest @Lazy-injection-point form; BoxFuture at the dyn construction boundary (AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe) — the only forced heap on the resolve path

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The handle TYPES (ObjectProvider/ObjectFactory/Provider/Lazy) live entirely in leaf-core as stable public generics; the macro emits their construction at the consumer's injection point via absolute `::leaf_core` paths holding zero logic (cross-crate §90, §102), so a provider-typed field is fully LOCAL to each consuming crate's compilation — this feature needs NO cross-crate discovery of its own and has NO DCE hazard for the handle itself (a strong T1 property: deferral is a per-injection-point property the consumer's own macro emits, requiring zero link-time collection). What it CONSUMES cross-crate is the candidate set + the resolution outcome from autowiring-resolution/candidate-resolver over Phase-2's chosen discovery substrate (linkme-default §166 / inventory / build-time codegen / explicit) — so the provider inherits, but does not own, the silent-DCE hazard (cross-crate §149-152): if a contributing crate is dropped (Layer-0 unreferenced), its bean never enters the set, so getObject() sees NoSuchBean and stream() yields fewer items — a legitimately-empty provider is indistinguishable from a silently-DCE'd-source provider, which makes the upstream anti-DCE self-check (expected-vs-found, per-source provenance §152) a precondition and argues the provider's NoSuchBean diagnostic should be able to say 'N candidates registered from crates [..]'. The resolver closure is ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC: native / FactoryBean-product / WASM-module-backed candidates all resolve through the same erased closure, so the two-source duality (static link-time registry as the default 'classpath' PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source) is INVISIBLE to the provider — both just feed the candidate set the resolver re-queries (CANDIDATES, not a decision). Generics: `ObjectProvider<Store<RemoteType>>` resolves only if the concrete `Store<RemoteType>` instantiation was registered upstream (generics-cannot-register §153, inherited from candidate-resolver — the macro hard-errors on an open-generic TARGET). Qualifier metadata (`ObjectProvider<MyService>` plus a captured @Qualifier) is const data the macro emits per injection point — no central table.

**T2 — execution context:** The handle is CONSTRUCTED at the consumer's populate time (cold path, refresh, bootstrap task) holding only a cloned resolver closure + a `&'static` descriptor — NO target resolution yet (true deferral: nothing built, nothing to clean if the provider is never used). Each get_object()/get_if_available()/get_if_unique()/get() is an `.await` that runs the resolution + winner-construction on WHATEVER task calls it — the load-bearing execution-context shift (charter §2.3): a provider first-used from a request hot path runs the target's construct/populate/init .await THERE, so a blocking factory must spawn_blocking and the developer understands they deferred work onto a potentially-hot path (the rust-native sharp edge of Spring's hidden synchronous live-handle lookup, made explicit by the `.await`). Re-resolution is the LIVE-HANDLE property: a provider over a prototype/scoped target re-builds each call (hot-path cost, the documented behavior); over a singleton, getObject re-queries the cache (lock-free fast read, charter §2.4 reactive-not-polling), and `Lazy<T>`'s OnceCell caches-on-first-get so subsequent gets are lock-free. Concurrency: concurrent first-resolutions of the same singleton serialize on bean-instantiation's per-bean creation guard (never a global lock, §2.5) — one builds, others await the published value (safe publication, concurrency-contract). CANCELLATION: a getObject().await cancelled mid-construction (the caller's request was cancelled) drops the in-flight build; nothing in the provider leaks (it held only a closure + descriptor), and because the handle holds no instance the next get retries cleanly — no half-built state. stream()/stream_filtered() are LAZY: filtering candidate descriptors is pure-sync (no await, no construction), and only iterating-and-resolving each item awaits (so an unconsumed stream builds nothing). Errors propagate as the rich ResolveError (charter §2.9) attributed to the get() call site, carrying the injection point + candidates-considered; bean-instantiation re-wraps into the dependent's assembly chain when the provider is used during another bean's construction. TEARDOWN: the provider owns no teardown (it is a handle); a singleton it lazily materializes registers teardown like any singleton (context-close drains it), so the teardown ledger grows past end-of-refresh when providers first-build their targets — a divergence from a refresh-frozen ledger context-close must accommodate. The container back-reference the closure holds must not outlive the container (a §2.5 lifetime concern deferred to the scope/ownership keystone).

#### R2 — A unified single PROVIDER engine + a strictness-by-arity descriptor: one ProviderHandle parameterized by a captured Arity/Strictness, with the family types (ObjectProvider/Provider/ObjectFactory/Lazy) as thin facades; the ladder is data, not bespoke methods
Question whether the family needs distinct types with bespoke methods at all. Spring needs ObjectProvider/ObjectFactory/Provider as separate INTERFACES because Java has no way to parameterize strictness as data; but the three tiers differ ONLY in the {tolerate-absence, tolerate-ambiguity} bits, and the family members differ ONLY in {which tiers + stream are exposed, single-vs-multiple}. So model ONE engine: a `ProviderHandle<T>` holding the origin-agnostic resolver + the captured `InjectionPoint`, with the strictness expressed as DATA — `enum Strictness { Strict, AbsenceTolerant, FullyTolerant }` and `enum Cardinality { Single, Multiple }` — and a single resolution driver `resolve(strictness, cardinality).await -> ProviderResult<T>` that consumes autowiring-resolution's Resolved enum and maps it per the strictness bits (Strict: None->Err, Ambiguous->Err; AbsenceTolerant: None->Ok(absent), Ambiguous->Err; FullyTolerant: both->Ok(absent); Multiple: collect ALL, bypass single-valued tie-break). The PUBLIC family types are then THIN FACADES over the one engine that fix the strictness/cardinality at the type level for DX: `ObjectFactory<T>::get_object() = handle.resolve(Strict, Single)`; `Provider<T>::get() = handle.resolve(Strict, Single)` (jakarta facade, same engine call, different name for JSR-330 compat); `ObjectProvider<T>` exposes `.get_object()/.get_if_available()/.get_if_unique()` = `resolve(Strict|AbsenceTolerant|FullyTolerant, Single)` and `.stream()/.ordered_stream()/.stream_filtered()` = `resolve(_, Multiple)`; `Lazy<T>` = the facade that adds the OnceCell cache over `resolve(Strict, Single)`. The captured InjectionPoint carries a DEFAULT strictness/cardinality inferred from the declared type (so `ObjectProvider<T>` field starts at no-default, `Provider<T>` field is Strict-Single, an `Option<T>` field maps to AbsenceTolerant-Single — unifying with injection-styles' Optional arity).
