Crucially the three-tier ladder lives in ONE place (the driver's match on Strictness), so the most-missed semantic ('getIfAvailable throws on ambiguity, only getIfUnique swallows it') is encoded exactly once and cannot drift between family members. A `provider report`-style diagnostic can show, per resolution, the {strictness, cardinality, outcome, candidates-considered} as a byproduct of the one driver.

**Pros:** Maximal unification (charter §2.11): the entire family + @Lazy collapse to ONE engine parameterized by {Strictness, Cardinality} data, with the public types as thin facades — the three-tier ladder and the single-vs-multiple split are each expressed exactly ONCE in the driver, so the most-missed semantic (getIfAvailable errors on ambiguity, getIfUnique swallows it) physically cannot diverge between ObjectProvider/Provider/ObjectFactory.; Best diagnostics (charter §2.9): because every lookup flows through one driver that already has the strictness + cardinality + Resolved outcome in hand, the rich error / a `provider report` carries {requested strictness, candidates considered, why-absent-or-ambiguous} for free — far richer than reconstructing it per-bespoke-method, and the candidates-considered chain is a byproduct of the single fold.; Unifies optionality across features: an `Option<T>` injection point (injection-styles' Optional arity) and `ObjectProvider.getIfAvailable()` both reduce to `Strictness::AbsenceTolerant` over the one driver — so leaf has ONE notion of 'tolerate absence' rather than two (annotation-arity vs provider-method), and the same for ambiguity-tolerance, exactly the kind of shared primitive the charter wants.; Lowest type-surface duplication: ObjectFactory/Provider differ only by name (facades calling the same engine), and Lazy is a caching facade — no parallel bespoke implementations to keep in sync, and a new family member (e.g. a future `OrderedProvider`) is just a new facade fixing different data, not new logic.; Origin-agnostic and async-honest identically to R1: the engine holds the same erased resolver closure, so WASM-module / native / FactoryBean targets resolve uniformly, and every resolve is an explicit `.await` (§2.3); stream/Multiple bypasses single-valued tie-break (docs 2622) in the one place cardinality is handled.; Keeps the door open for the @Lazy transparent-proxy variant WITHOUT forking the engine: a generated trait-newtype proxy (for the rare trait-typed transparent case) can be a THIRD facade that internally calls `resolve(Strict, Single)` on first method dispatch — so transparency, if Phase 2 wants it, is a facade choice over the same engine, not a separate mechanism.

**Cons:** More indirection than R1 for what users SEE as distinct types: the facade-over-engine layering risks looking like over-abstraction when the common case is just `provider.get_if_available().await` — the engine + Strictness/Cardinality enums + facades are more moving parts than R1's three small methods, and must be documented as 'one engine, typed facades for DX' not an extension seam (the strictness ladder is a FIXED contract, not user-pluggable — same discipline autowiring-resolution needs).; Inferring a default Strictness/Cardinality from the declared injection-point TYPE (Option<T> -> AbsenceTolerant, Provider<T> -> Strict) is a macro-time mapping that must be precise and total — a wrong inference silently changes a lookup's failure mode (e.g. treating a field as AbsenceTolerant when it should be Strict turns a boot-time NoSuchBean into a silent None), a subtle correctness surface R1's explicit per-method calls avoid.; Carrying Strictness/Cardinality as runtime data on the handle (rather than encoding it purely in the type as R1 does) means a tiny per-handle data cost and a runtime match in the driver on the warm path — negligible, but it trades R1's zero-cost type-level encoding for data-level flexibility, a §2.2 low-overhead nick.; The facade types still need to EXIST as public API (DX requires `ObjectProvider<T>` to be a real type a user writes), so R2 does not actually reduce the public type COUNT vs R1 — it only unifies their IMPLEMENTATION; the user-facing surface is identical, so the unification benefit is internal-only and may not justify the extra engine layer if the bespoke methods (R1) are already small.; The single-driver-with-Multiple-cardinality must host BOTH the single-valued resolve AND the lazy candidate-stream in one function signature (returning either a single ProviderResult or a stream), which is awkward in Rust's type system (a sum return type or two driver entry points) — likely forcing the driver to split into resolve_single + resolve_multiple anyway, partially undoing the 'one driver' claim.; Same container-back-reference lifetime / scope-ownership-keystone tension as R1 (the handle pins a container reference), unchanged — the unification does not dissolve the deepest impedance, it just centralizes it.

**Mechanisms considered:** ONE `ProviderHandle<T>` engine holding the erased resolver + captured InjectionPoint, strictness/cardinality as DATA; `enum Strictness{Strict, AbsenceTolerant, FullyTolerant}` + `enum Cardinality{Single, Multiple}` driving a single resolution driver that maps autowiring-resolution's Resolved enum; public family as THIN FACADES fixing the data: ObjectFactory/Provider (Strict,Single, differing only by name for JSR-330 compat), ObjectProvider (all tiers + stream), Lazy (caching facade); macro-inferred default Strictness/Cardinality from the declared injection-point type, unifying Option<T> arity (injection-styles) with getIfAvailable's AbsenceTolerant; one place for the three-tier ladder (the driver's match on Strictness) so the most-missed semantic cannot drift between family members; optional @Lazy transparent-proxy as a THIRD facade (generated trait-newtype) over the same engine, NOT a forked mechanism; a `provider report` diagnostic (candidates-considered + strictness + outcome) as a byproduct of the single driver

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Identical core T1 to R1: the engine, the family facades, and the Strictness/Cardinality enums all live in leaf-core as a closed, stable kernel contract (the strictness ladder is a FIXED contract, not a downstream extension point — so the only cross-crate extensibility is in candidate FILTERING upstream, keeping this feature's surface a small stable kernel per charter §6). The macro emits facade construction + the inferred {strictness, cardinality} default at the consumer's injection point via absolute ::leaf_core paths (thin, §90), fully local per crate, NO discovery surface, NO DCE hazard for the handle. The driver consumes the candidate set + Resolved outcome over Phase-2's substrate, inheriting (not owning) the silent-DCE hazard exactly as R1 — but R2's single-driver trace makes the SYMPTOM more legible: a `provider report` can say 'getIfAvailable resolved absent: 0 candidates of type T (expected N from crates [..])', turning a dropped-crate symptom into an actionable diagnostic, which directly supports the §152 self-check mandate. Origin-agnostic resolver = the two-source duality (static link-time registry + optional dynamic WASM-module directory-scanned source) is invisible to the engine; both feed the set the driver re-queries (CANDIDATES not decision). Generics-cannot-register (§153) inherited: a `ProviderHandle<Store<RemoteType>>` resolves only if the concrete instantiation registered upstream; the macro hard-errors on an open-generic target. The macro-inferred-default-strictness mapping is purely compile-time per injection point (no central table), so it composes cleanly cross-crate — a `Provider<T>` field in a remote crate carries its own inferred Strict-Single default with no coordination.

**T2 — execution context:** Identical execution profile to R1: the facade/handle is constructed at populate time (cold, bootstrap task) holding only the resolver closure + descriptor + the inferred {strictness, cardinality} data — no resolution yet. Each resolve is an `.await` on the caller's task (the §2.3 execution-context shift: a deferred lookup first-used on a hot path runs construction there; blocking factories spawn_blocking). The ONE addition vs R1 is the runtime match on Strictness inside the driver — pure CPU, branch-only, allocation-free on the common path (the trace/report is gated to the error/ambiguous path or behind a diagnostic flag so the warm path stays allocation-free, §2.2/§2.4). Live-handle re-resolution, OnceCell caching for Lazy-singleton, per-bean-guard serialization of concurrent first-builds (never global, §2.5), lazy stream/Multiple (filter sync, resolve-on-iterate await), and rich ResolveError attributed to the call site — all identical to R1. CANCELLATION: a resolve().await cancelled mid-build leaks nothing (handle holds only closure + data + enum); next call retries cleanly. The Multiple/stream path resolving ALL candidates lazily means cancellation between items leaves earlier-built singletons cached (they registered teardown on publish) and simply stops — no partial-collection cleanup needed since each item is an independent published singleton (or an owned prototype the caller now owns and must clean). TEARDOWN: handle owns none; lazily-materialized singletons join the append-grown teardown ledger (same as R1). The container back-reference lifetime is the same deferred §2.5/scope-ownership concern.

#### R3 — Deferral as a COMPILE-TIME-resolved capability: provider/lazy as zero-or-thin-wrapper handles whose strictness AND target presence are checked at the injection-site type where legible, with a runtime arm only for the genuinely runtime/multiple/scoped cases (DX-maximal, fail-fast-earliest)
Push intent-not-mechanism hardest (charter §2.2/§2.9). Spring's provider is fully runtime because the BeanFactory is a mutable runtime registry and the target's presence/uniqueness is only knowable by querying it at use-time; but for the COMMON case in leaf — a singleton target, a single-valued lookup, a target that DOES exist in the graph — much of the strictness can be a COMPILE/STARTUP-time-checked typed capability rather than a runtime query. Model `ObjectProvider<T>` and friends as typed handles whose RESOLUTION is split by whether the answer is statically knowable: (a) PRESENCE — whether a candidate of type T exists at all — is knowable at the graph-freeze/startup-validation pass (autowiring-resolution can be asked 'would this resolve?' without building), so `get_object()` on a provider whose target is statically absent can FAIL at the startup validation pass (the richest deferred timing, §2.9) rather than at first .await; and a typo'd qualifier marker (if leaf uses typed-marker qualifiers, the open question shared with candidate-resolver) fails at COMPILE time. (b) STRICTNESS as a TYPE: `getIfAvailable` returning `Option<T>` and `getObject` returning `T` already encode tolerate-absence-vs-strict in the RETURN TYPE — so the family becomes a set of methods whose signatures make the failure mode legible (Option vs Result vs T), and the 'most-missed semantic' (getIfAvailable still errors on ambiguity) is encoded as `getIfAvailable() -> Result<Option<T>, AmbiguityError>` (the type itself says 'absence is None, ambiguity is still an error'). (c) The GENUINELY RUNTIME arms — re-resolution against a target whose presence/uniqueness depends on runtime scope (prototype/request/session re-resolved per call), the lazy cycle-break where the target is constructed on first use, stream() over a candidate set whose membership is only fully known after all crates link, and getIfUnique() whose uniqueness depends on runtime default-candidate flags — stay a thin runtime resolution (R1-shaped). So deferral splits: the COMMON single-valued-singleton-present case is a near-zero-cost typed handle whose strictness is in the return type and whose presence is startup-validated; the runtime/multiple/scoped/cycle cases fall to the runtime resolver. @Lazy similarly: a @Lazy edge to a present singleton is a `OnceCell`-cached handle resolved on first .await (cheap), while @Lazy as a cycle-breaker is the runtime deferral that must NOT be eagerly validated (validating it would build the cycle).

**Pros:** Earliest/richest fail-fast (charter §2.9): a provider whose target type is statically ABSENT from the graph can fail at the startup-validation pass (richest deferred context, naming the injection point + that zero candidates exist) instead of silently returning None or first-call-erroring — turning Spring's 'rather limited' @Lazy footgun (missing target surfaces only as a first-invocation exception, docs 2574/2586) into a boot-time diagnostic where the diagnostic is legible.; Strictness-in-the-return-type is maximally DX-honest (§2.1): `get_object() -> Result<T>`, `get_if_available() -> Result<Option<T>>` (Option=absence, Err=ambiguity — the most-missed semantic encoded in the SIGNATURE), `get_if_unique() -> Option<T>` (both tolerated) — a developer reads the failure mode off the type without docs, and the compiler enforces handling it (vs Spring's runtime-only contract).; Lowest overhead on the dominant path (§2.2): a present-singleton single-valued provider is a thin handle over a cache probe, no candidate-set materialization, no strictness branch — the warm re-resolution (the path that matters per §2.4) is a keyed lookup for the common case, with the full runtime resolver reached only for multiple/scoped/cycle cases.; Preserves the genuinely-runtime cases faithfully: stream() (membership only known after link), getIfUnique() (uniqueness depends on runtime default-candidate flags), prototype/scoped re-resolution, and @Lazy-as-cycle-breaker all stay runtime (R1-shaped) — so nothing in the family is LOST, the runtime arm is just reached only when the answer truly cannot be known earlier.; Aligns with the AOT/freeze lineage (charter §3 compile-vs-runtime keystone): if Phase 2 freezes the graph at build time, 'would this provider resolve?' becomes a build-time fact, so presence-validation and even some uniqueness moves to codegen — R3 is the realization that best exploits a frozen graph, while degrading gracefully to startup-validation when the graph is runtime-resolved.; @Lazy-cycle-break is correctly EXEMPTED from eager presence-validation (validating it would force the very construction the cycle-break defers) — so R3 distinguishes 'defer because expensive/optional' (validatable) from 'defer to break a cycle' (must NOT validate), a nuance Spring blurs, directly serving circular-references' AOT-cycle-break remedy intent (docs 2593).

**Cons:** Most divergent from Spring's uniform-runtime-provider model and the most split: the SAME `ObjectProvider<T>` has a compile/startup-validated arm AND a runtime arm with two failure timings, a real cognitive + implementation cost, and the boundary (what is statically knowable vs runtime) must be drawn with surgical precision or a provider's failure mode becomes unpredictable to the user (some absences are boot errors, others first-call None).; Presence-validation requires autowiring-resolution to answer 'would this resolve?' WITHOUT building — a non-trivial dry-run capability that must agree EXACTLY with the real resolution (a fast-path-equals-slow-path invariant of the kind that bit Spring repeatedly), and getIfUnique's uniqueness genuinely depends on runtime default-candidate flags so it CANNOT be statically validated, splitting the strictness tiers across timings unevenly.; The live-handle 're-resolve every call against CURRENT state' property (docs 2567) is fundamentally a RUNTIME contract — if R3 statically resolves the common case, it risks breaking the guarantee that a provider reflects current container state (e.g. a bean registered/removed at runtime), so R3 only safely applies the static arm to IMMUTABLE-after-freeze singletons, narrowing where the optimization holds and reintroducing a runtime query whenever the container is mutable.; stream()/orderedStream() membership is only fully known after ALL crates link (cross-crate §149) — so the lazy multiple case CANNOT be compile-time-resolved and must be runtime, meaning R3 does NOT simplify the multiple-cardinality arm at all (it carries the full runtime stream machinery PLUS the static single-valued path, more total surface than R1 for the complete family).; Strictness-in-the-return-type forces the family's method signatures to differ structurally (T vs Option<T> vs Result<Option<T>>), which is honest but means the family is NOT a uniform set of same-shaped methods (R2's unification is lost) — and mapping these onto a single underlying resolver is awkward when each returns a different type, likely needing per-method bespoke wrappers (closer to R1's bespoke methods than R2's unified engine).; Dual-timing fail-fast means the execution-context + diagnostics discipline must be documented twice (startup-validated vs runtime-first-call), and a developer may be surprised that a @Lazy-cycle-break provider is NOT presence-validated while an @Lazy-expensive provider IS — the exemption rule, while correct, adds a conceptual edge.

**Mechanisms considered:** split deferral: COMMON single-valued-present-singleton case = thin handle, presence statically/startup-validated; runtime/multiple/scoped/cycle cases = runtime resolver (R1-shaped); strictness encoded in the RETURN TYPE: get_object()->Result<T>, get_if_available()->Result<Option<T>> (Option=absence, Err=ambiguity — most-missed semantic in the signature), get_if_unique()->Option<T>; a startup-validation dry-run 'would this provider resolve?' (no construction) failing absent providers at the richest deferred timing; typo'd typed-marker qualifier failing at COMPILE time; @Lazy-as-cycle-breaker EXEMPTED from presence-validation (validating it would build the cycle) — distinguished from @Lazy-as-expensive-defer which IS validated; getIfUnique() + stream() + prototype/scoped re-resolution stay RUNTIME (uniqueness/membership not statically knowable, esp. cross-crate stream membership §149); best fit for a build-time-frozen graph (charter §3): presence/uniqueness become codegen facts when the graph is frozen, degrading to startup-validation when runtime-resolved; static arm restricted to IMMUTABLE-after-freeze singletons so the live-handle current-state contract is not violated

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Sharpest T1 tension of the three. The PRESENCE-VALIDATION arm wants to know 'does a candidate of type T exist?' at startup/freeze — but cross-crate candidate-set membership is only fully known AFTER all crates link (cross-crate §149, Layer-0), so static presence-validation can only run at the FINAL BINARY / startup-validation pass over the fully-collected set, NOT in a library crate's own compilation (a build.rs cannot see sibling crates §30/§77). This makes the 'compile-time' part of R3 mostly a STARTUP-validation pass anchored at the binary (the richest deferred timing, §2.9), with true compile-time failure only for a typo'd typed-marker qualifier (whose marker type is resolved at the injection site's own compilation). The stream()/Multiple arm is INHERENTLY cross-crate-runtime: membership depends on which crates linked + an optional dynamic WASM-module source, so the lazy stream MUST be a runtime query over the collected set — R3 does not and cannot move it earlier. The silent-DCE hazard (§149-152) cuts BOTH ways here: a dropped crate makes presence-validation see a target as absent (so getObject fails at the startup validation pass — actually a GOOD outcome, it surfaces the dropped crate loudly, which the §152 self-check wants), but it could also make a SHOULD-fail-absent provider spuriously pass if a different crate happens to register a same-type bean. The two-source duality is acute for R3: the static arm assumes the static link-time registry's membership is final, but a dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source could add a candidate AFTER the static validation pass — so R3's static arm is only sound if the dynamic source is either (a) also enumerated before the validation pass or (b) explicitly excluded from static-validatable presence (CANDIDATE: validate over the static 'classpath' but treat dynamic-WASM-source targets as always-runtime — a tension, NOT a decision). Generics-cannot-register (§153) inherited. So R3's T1 reality: the 'compile-time' framing is mostly startup-validation-anchored-at-the-binary, with the multiple/dynamic/scoped arms irreducibly runtime.

**T2 — execution context:** Two execution profiles. STATIC/VALIDATED arm: presence-validation runs once at the startup-validation pass (cold, bootstrap task, synchronous dry-run — no construction, no await, pure 'would-resolve' query over the frozen candidate set); a present-singleton get_object() is then a cache probe with a thin .await only for the (cold) first construction, after which it is lock-free (§2.4). RUNTIME arm (multiple/scoped/cycle/getIfUnique): identical to R1's profile — each resolve is an .await on the caller's task (the §2.3 execution-context shift; blocking factories spawn_blocking), live-handle re-resolution for prototype/scoped, lazy stream (filter sync, resolve-on-iterate await). The @Lazy-cycle-break case is the sharpest T2 nuance: it MUST NOT be presence-validated at startup (that would .await the construction it exists to defer, re-forming the cycle), so it is a pure runtime first-.await deferral whose construction runs on the caller's task on first use — and concurrent first-uses serialize on the per-bean guard (§2.5), with the cycle's two beans completing each other on first mutual access. CANCELLATION: a runtime-arm resolve().await cancelled mid-build leaks nothing (handle holds closure + descriptor); a cancelled cycle-break first-use must leave both beans' slots unfilled for a clean retry (no half-built cycle), which the per-bean guard release ensures. Fail-fast TIMING differs by arm and is the §2.9 nuance R3 must keep consistent: static-arm absence fails at the startup-validation pass (rich, names the injection point + zero candidates); runtime-arm absence/ambiguity fails at the get() .await call site (also rich, but later); a typo'd typed-marker qualifier fails at COMPILE time. Errors are the rich ResolveError throughout. TEARDOWN: handles own none; lazily/runtime-materialized singletons join the append-grown teardown ledger (same as R1/R2). The dual-arm means execution-context + fail-fast-timing discipline must be documented per-arm — a real cost vs R1/R2's single profile, mitigated by the arms being chosen by statically-knowable properties (present-singleton-single vs runtime/multiple/scoped/cycle), not by user whim.

### Rust clashes
- Spring's @Lazy lazy-resolution PROXY has no rust-native equivalent — no CGLIB/JDK runtime subclassing, no transparent runtime proxy. Spring's @Lazy at an injection point injects a proxy that LOOKS like MyService but resolves-and-delegates on first method call (and the docs ALREADY call this 'rather limited', docs 794/2574 — it is always injected even when no bean exists, so a missing target surfaces only as a first-invocation exception). Rust cannot do this transparently without either (a) a deferral-VISIBLE-in-the-type handle the consumer must `.get().await` (R1/R2/R3 — honest, matches the docs' own preference for ObjectProvider over @Lazy, but breaks 'inject T, use T'), or (b) generating a newtype that impls the target's TRAIT and defers each method (trait-typed injection ONLY, heavy codegen, and async trait methods force boxed-future shims at the dyn boundary since AFIT/RPITIT are not dyn-safe, nightly doc §160). leaf should LEAN INTO the docs' own preference: ObjectProvider/Provider/Lazy as visible handles over an invisible proxy — more honest, but a deliberate DX divergence from Spring's drop-in transparency (the workspace-wide 'how does leaf proxy without CGLIB' question shared with scoped-proxies, aop-proxying, lazy-initialization).
- getObject()/getIfAvailable()/get() is an `.await`, not a synchronous call — relocating a deferred lookup's construction onto a possibly-HOT request path with an EXECUTION-CONTEXT hazard Spring's synchronous live-handle never had. Spring's provider re-queries the BeanFactory synchronously (may block a thread, but on the JVM that is 'just' a blocked thread). In async leaf, each provider call runs the target's construct/populate/init .await on WHATEVER task calls it — a provider first-used on a latency-sensitive request path now stalls it (or must spawn_blocking) and concurrent first-uses serialize on bean-instantiation's per-bean creation guard, importing cold-path concurrency onto the hot path. leaf must make this shift EXPLICIT (charter §2.3) — the `.await` at the call site IS the explicitness, the rust-native sharp edge of Spring's hidden synchronous lookup — rather than silently inheriting 'a provider lookup is cheap'.
- The provider is a LIVE HANDLE that 're-resolves against CURRENT factory state, holds no instance' (docs 2567) — but in Rust this forces the handle to hold an origin-agnostic CONTAINER BACK-REFERENCE that outlives a single resolution and is stored on the consuming bean for its whole life, pinning a container reference (an Arc-ish handle in tension with charter §2.5 minimize-Arc). Spring's provider closes over the GC'd BeanFactory for free; Rust must decide the ownership/lifetime of that back-reference, which is the deferred scope/lifetime/ownership keystone (charter §3) — so the handle must keep the container reference ABSTRACT (a `Resolve` closure type) and NOT prejudge whether it is Arc<Container>, a borrow, or an arena handle. This also means a `'static` provider stored on a bean may be inexpressible if Phase 2 picks borrows-over-Arc, forcing boxing.
- Not extending Supplier / 'never a cached value' (docs 2561/2567) is a Rust modeling clash: Spring deliberately keeps ObjectProvider from being mistaken for an in-memory cached value (getObject can throw and re-resolve). In Rust the natural temptation is to impl `Fn`/give the handle a Deref to T — leaf must RESIST that (the handle is NOT a cached T; it is an async re-resolving lookup), keeping getObject()/get() as explicit async methods returning Result, NOT a Deref or a Supplier-like impl, so the call site stays honestly 'this is a lookup that can fail and re-resolves', preserving the docs' intent that failure modes are visible at the call site.
- The three-tier strictness ladder maps onto Rust's Result/Option but the 'most-missed semantic' must be ENCODED IN THE TYPE so it cannot be mis-handled: getObject() -> Result<T> (strict), getIfAvailable() -> Result<Option<T>> (absence -> Ok(None), ambiguity -> STILL Err — the subtle part), getIfUnique() -> Option<T> (both -> None). Spring expresses this via three method contracts on one interface read at runtime; Rust's win is that the RETURN TYPE encodes the failure mode (Result<Option<T>> literally says 'absence is None, ambiguity is an error'), but leaf must capture the {tolerate-absence, tolerate-ambiguity} bits at the injection point and map autowiring-resolution's three-outcome Resolved enum to the right type per method — the dynamism of choosing strictness at the call site survives (the method choice IS the strictness), but it is now type-checked rather than a runtime contract.
- stream()/orderedStream() laziness + 'membership only known after link' is a cross-crate clash with no JVM analogue. Spring's stream() is lazy and filterable before any bean is created (docs 2573), but its candidate membership comes from a runtime classpath query. In Rust the full candidate set is only known after ALL crates link (cross-crate §149, Layer-0) plus an optional dynamic WASM-module source — so stream() MUST be a runtime query over the collected set, cannot be moved earlier, and a dropped contributing crate silently shrinks the stream (a legitimately-short stream is indistinguishable from a DCE-victim stream). orderedStream() must apply the SAME @Order/@Priority comparator collection-injection uses (else single-vs-stream ordering diverges, §2.11), and that comparator's 'Ordered-interface-beats-annotation' precedence has no Rust analogue unless captured as a distinct const flag — inherited from collection-injection.
- jakarta.inject.Provider<T> is a STANDARD interface leaf must offer for JSR-330 ecosystem compatibility, but in Rust there is no 'implement the jakarta interface' — leaf's Provider<T> is its OWN type that merely mirrors the get() contract; the standards-compat value (a library written against jakarta Provider works) is LOST because Rust has no shared interface identity across the ecosystem the way the JVM does. So leaf's Provider<T> is faithful in SHAPE (basic lazy get(), no optional/unique/stream richness, docs 2576) but cannot deliver the cross-library standardization Spring's jakarta.inject.Provider does — it is effectively just a minimal alias of ObjectFactory, raising whether shipping a distinct Provider<T> earns its keep in Rust.
- No exception-chain wrapping; the provider's NoSuchBean/NoUniqueBean must be a constructed RICH VALUE, and @Lazy's 'rather limited' footgun (missing target surfaces only at first invocation) is WORSE in Rust without stack-unwinding context. Spring throws NoSuchBeanDefinitionException/NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException with the injection-point context. Rust's ResolveError must be an explicit value carrying the injection point + candidates-considered + per-source provenance (which crates registered candidates, for the cross-crate dropped-crate case), propagated via Result and re-wrapped by bean-instantiation into the dependent's assembly chain — and leaf can IMPROVE on Spring by making getIfAvailable() the honest optional+lazy choice (docs 2574) while emitting a rich diagnostic when a @Lazy handle's target is statically absent, turning Spring's silent first-invocation footgun into a startup-validatable one (R3) — but building that causal chain by hand is the cost of charter §2.9.
- @Lazy as a CYCLE-BREAKER must NOT be eagerly resolved/validated, but @Lazy as an EXPENSIVE-DEFER may be — a distinction Spring blurs and Rust's fail-fast pressure makes sharp. The v4/v7 escalation makes @Lazy/ObjectProvider the REQUIRED remedy for AOT-failing explicit cycles (docs 2593) — but presence-validating a cycle-break provider would .await the construction it exists to defer, re-forming the cycle. So leaf's deferral must distinguish 'defer because optional/expensive' (presence-validatable at startup, R3) from 'defer to break a cycle' (must stay a pure runtime first-use deferral) — a nuance with no clean Spring analogue, shared with circular-references.
- The MANDATORY-glue-for-background-bootstrap contract (docs 2006/2593) is a deferral-primitives responsibility with an async-execution twist: a non-@Lazy/non-Provider direct dependent BLOCKS on a @Bean(bootstrap=BACKGROUND) bean, defeating parallelism — so the deferral handle is what UNBLOCKS background init. In Rust this means the provider's first-.await is precisely the join point where the dependent awaits the background bean's readiness — so the handle must integrate with background-bootstrap's readiness signal (reactive, not polling, §2.4), and a provider over a background bean is the structured-concurrency seam where the dependent's task awaits the bootstrap task's completion. Spring's 'thread blocks' becomes 'task awaits a readiness notification' — a cleaner rust-native realization but one that must be designed, not inherited.

### Interactions
- **autowiring-resolution** — PRIMARY UPSTREAM / SHARED OUTCOME ENUM. Every provider method (getObject/getIfAvailable/getIfUnique) re-runs autowiring-resolution's SELECTION over a freshly-found candidate set and maps its three-outcome Resolved{None|One|Ambiguous} enum per the method's strictness bits (getObject: None->NoSuchBean, Ambiguous->NoUniqueBean; getIfAvailable: None->absent, Ambiguous->error; getIfUnique: both->absent). This feature OWNS the strictness mapping at the call site; autowiring-resolution OWNS the selection ladder underneath. The catalog's own note: deferral-primitives changes the ARITY autowiring-resolution sees (Optional<T>/getIfAvailable demote None->absent) and getIfUnique depends on the default-candidate tie-break (resolves cleanly when every other same-type bean is default-candidate=false, docs 2621). The live-handle re-resolves each call, so it re-enters autowiring-resolution every time (not cached selection).
- **lazy-initialization** — TIGHTEST COUPLING / SHARED MACHINERY. @Lazy at an injection point IS a deferral primitive — leaf's `Lazy<T>` is essentially 'ObjectProvider<T> + cache-on-first-get for singleton targets, re-resolve for shorter scopes' (docs 2589). lazy-initialization owns the NODE flag, the global-default, and the three structural exclusions; THIS feature owns the deferred-HANDLE types that make laziness actually HOLD (the docs: laziness only holds when the reference is deferred via a handle/proxy or there are no eager dependents — a direct injection defeats it). Phase 2/3 must decide whether @Lazy-edge is a distinct mechanism or fully SUBSUMED by this feature's handle family (a flagged phase2Question on both sides). This feature provides the visible-in-the-type deferral; lazy-initialization provides the eager-pass gate.
- **candidate-resolver** — UPSTREAM (via autowiring-resolution). candidate-resolver does the FILTERING that produces the qualifying set this feature's provider re-queries each call — including that @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate affect ONLY single-valued provider arms (getObject/getIfUnique), while stream()/orderedStream() take ALL type-matching candidates bypassing the single-valued tie-break (docs 2622). stream_filtered(pred) (docs 2573) filters candidate DESCRIPTORS before construction — a lazy filtering surface candidate-resolver/bean-registry must expose. The provider holds the captured InjectionPoint (qualifier, generic-arg TypeIds) candidate-resolver consumes for filtering on each re-resolution.
- **collection-injection** — OVERLAP + SHARED COMPARATOR. ObjectProvider.stream()/orderedStream() is the LAZY 0..N counterpart to collection-injection's EAGER List<T>/Set/Map (docs 2573: List resolves eagerly, stream() is lazy). The load-bearing shared piece: orderedStream() applies the SAME @Order/@Priority/Ordered comparator collection-injection uses for ordering (stream() is registration-order) — the comparator MUST be one shared mechanism so eager-collection ordering and lazy-stream ordering never diverge (§2.11). Both bypass the single-valued @Primary/@Fallback tie-break (take all matching). collection-injection owns the eager multiple-injection; this feature owns the lazy/streamed multiple-access.
- **circular-references** — CYCLE-BREAK REMEDY. The v4/v7 escalation makes ObjectProvider/@Lazy the REQUIRED remedy for breaking explicit injected cycles (which fail fast under AOT, docs 785/2539/2593) — a deferred handle at one edge of a cycle lets both beans construct because the deferred side is resolved on first .await, not during construction. This feature provides the deferral handle that breaks the cycle; circular-references owns the cycle-detection + the (regular-runtime) early-reference machinery and decides when a cycle is logically unsatisfiable (constructor cycles still fail). A @Lazy-as-cycle-breaker handle must NOT be eagerly presence-validated (validating it would re-form the cycle).
- **background-bootstrap** — MANDATORY GLUE. A non-@Lazy/non-Provider direct dependent BLOCKS on a @Bean(bootstrap=BACKGROUND) bean, defeating the concurrency (docs 2006/2593) — so a deferral handle on the dependent's injection point is what UNBLOCKS real parallelism. This feature's provider first-.await is the structured-concurrency join point where the dependent's task awaits the background bean's readiness (reactive readiness signal, §2.4, not polling). background-bootstrap owns the concurrent-init executor + readiness; this feature provides the deferred reference that enables the parallelism by NOT forcing eager construction.
- **scopes** — SCOPE-DEPENDENT CACHING + the prototype-in-singleton fix. The provider/Lazy handle CACHES on first access for SINGLETON targets but RE-RESOLVES every access for shorter scopes (docs 2589) — and ObjectProvider/Provider<T> is one of the canonical fixes for the prototype-in-singleton gotcha (injecting a provider re-resolves a fresh prototype per call instead of capturing one forever, docs 824). scopes fixes per-scope multiplicity; this feature's caching behavior (OnceCell-cache for singleton, re-resolve for prototype/request/session) is keyed on the resolved target's scope, and the live-handle re-resolution is precisely what defeats the scope-mismatch.
- **injection-styles** — DEFINES THE INJECTION POINTS + ARITY SOURCE. injection-styles fixes the InjectionPoint descriptor shape (produced TypeId, generic-arg TypeIds, declared name, qualifier, Single|Optional arity) the macro captures and hands to the provider handle. An `Option<T>` field (injection-styles' Optional arity) and getIfAvailable() both reduce to 'tolerate absence' — this feature unifies the annotation-arity notion of optional with the provider-method notion of optional (R2 makes them ONE Strictness::AbsenceTolerant). Constructor points are mandatory-Single; setter/field may hold provider handles.
- **bean-instantiation** — DOWNSTREAM ASYNC BOUNDARY. This feature SELECTS-and-PACKAGES the deferred lookup (sync handle construction at populate; the resolution is consumer's); bean-instantiation CONSTRUCTS the resolved winner (async, boxed-future factory at the dyn boundary, may .await, must not block — spawn_blocking for blocking factories). The provider's getObject().await drives bean-instantiation's normal create sequence on the CALLER's task; bean-instantiation re-wraps this feature's ResolveError into the dependent's assembly chain, and a lazily-materialized singleton registers teardown like any singleton (so the teardown ledger grows past end-of-refresh). bean-instantiation owns the creation sequence + per-bean guard; this feature owns when (deferred) it runs.
- **container-core** — ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC RESOLVER SOURCE. The provider's erased resolver closure re-enters the container/context — so the RICH provider behavior (qualifier/generic filtering, primary/fallback uniqueness) depends on container-core having installed the rich candidate-resolver upstream; a bare registry yields an impoverished provider (stream sees an unfiltered set). container-core decides bare-vs-rich install; this feature's handle is FIXED but its resolution quality depends on what container-core installed, and the resolver closure holds the (abstract, scope/ownership-deferred) container back-reference.
- **factory-bean** — ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC TARGET. A FactoryBean's PRODUCT is a normal resolvable target, so a provider of the product (ObjectProvider<Product>) resolves through the same erased closure that calls factory.getObject().await — the provider never assumes native construction (honoring the origin-agnostic guardrail). The product's separate caching/lifecycle (docs 2547) is bean-instantiation's concern; this feature just packages the deferred lookup. SmartFactoryBean's multi-type products (docs 2557) mean a provider may resolve different product types from one factory — handled upstream in resolution, transparent to the handle.
- **value-injection** — BOUNDARY / NON-INTERACTION. value-injection (@Value/#{expression}, doResolveDependency step 2, docs 800) PRE-EMPTS bean candidate resolution — a @Value injection point is resolved by value-injection, NOT wrapped in a provider. The clean split: providers handle by-type/by-qualifier BEAN lookups; @Value handles externalized-value lookups. The two never contend over the same point; a provider is never a @Value target.
- **primary-fallback** — UNIQUENESS SOURCE FOR SINGLE-VALUED ARMS. getObject()/getIfUnique() honor @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate for single-valued uniqueness (docs 2576) — getIfUnique() resolves cleanly precisely when @Primary promotes one OR every other same-type bean is default-candidate=false (docs 2621). But @Primary/@Fallback DO NOT apply to stream()/orderedStream() (which take all matching, docs 2622). primary-fallback defines the promote/demote semantics (executed as a tie-break LAYER in autowiring-resolution); this feature consumes that outcome only for its single-valued arms and deliberately bypasses it for the multiple arms.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Is @Lazy-at-an-injection-point a DISTINCT mechanism or fully SUBSUMED by this feature's handle family? `Lazy<T>` is essentially 'ObjectProvider<T> + cache-on-first-get'; if this feature provides a cached single-valued handle, lazy-initialization's injection-point case collapses into it, leaving lazy-initialization owning only the node-flag/global-default/exclusions. This is the same question flagged from lazy-initialization's side and must be resolved jointly before either commits to a handle type — and it entangles the proxy-vs-visible-handle decision below.
- Visible-in-the-type handle (the docs' own preference, the consumer `.get().await`s) vs a generated transparent PROXY (Spring's drop-in 'inject T, use T')? Rust has no CGLIB/JDK transparent proxy; a transparent realization needs a generated trait-newtype (trait-typed injection ONLY, boxed-future shims at the async dyn boundary, nightly doc §160) and is heavy. The visible handle is honest and unifies the family but breaks transparency. This is a DX(§2.1)-vs-fidelity call shared with lazy-initialization, scoped-proxies, and aop-proxying (the workspace-wide 'how does leaf proxy without CGLIB' meta-question) — and it determines whether @Lazy can ever be a drop-in T.
- Distinct family TYPES with bespoke methods (R1) vs one unified ENGINE + thin facades with strictness/cardinality as data (R2) vs a compile/startup-validated split with strictness-in-the-return-type + a runtime arm (R3) — or a blend (R2's unified engine for the runtime arms, R3's startup-presence-validation for the present-singleton-single case, visible-handle types as the public surface)? This is the keystone shape decision for the family and is entangled with the unified-diagnostics meta-concept and the compile-vs-runtime split (§3).
- What is the ownership/lifetime of the container BACK-REFERENCE every live handle holds (the docs' 're-resolve against current state' contract requires it)? Arc<Container>? a scoped borrow? an arena handle? A `'static` provider stored on a bean for its whole life may be inexpressible under a borrows-over-Arc model. This is the deferred scope/lifetime/ownership keystone (§3) surfacing directly in this feature — the handle must keep the reference abstract until Phase 2 decides, and the decision determines whether providers can be stored on beans at all without boxing.
- Fail-fast TIMING for a provider whose target is ABSENT or ambiguous (§2.9): does it surface at a STARTUP-VALIDATION pass (R3's 'would this resolve?' dry-run, richest deferred context, anchored at the binary since cross-crate membership is only known after link), at the get() .await CALL SITE (Spring's runtime timing), or at COMPILE time (only for a typo'd typed-marker qualifier)? And can timing be CONSISTENT across getObject vs getIfAvailable vs stream vs @Lazy-cycle-break (which must NOT be presence-validated)? Shares the unified-diagnostics meta-concept and the compile-vs-runtime split.
- Does leaf ship jakarta.inject.Provider<T> as a distinct type at all, given Rust has no shared cross-ecosystem interface identity (so the JSR-330 standardization value is lost — it becomes a minimal alias of ObjectFactory)? Keeping it is faithful-in-shape and aids migration familiarity; dropping it reduces surface bloat. Shared with the broader 'which jakarta annotations/types does leaf mirror by name vs reinterpret' question.
- How does stream()/orderedStream() materialize its lazy candidate set across crates, and where does the SHARED @Order/@Priority comparator live (it must be the same one collection-injection uses, else eager-vs-lazy ordering diverges)? stream membership is only fully known after all crates link (§149) plus an optional dynamic WASM-module source, so the lazy stream is irreducibly a runtime query over the collected set — couples to the type-erasure/registry substrate (how candidates are indexed/streamed lazily) and to collection-injection's comparator.
- Per-resolution diagnostic cost vs richness (§2.9 vs §2.2): should the provider's candidates-considered / per-source-provenance chain be materialized on every resolution (warm-path cost on prototype/scoped/live-handle re-resolution) or only on the absent/ambiguous/error path / behind a diagnostic flag? And how much crate-provenance does it carry so a cross-module NoSuchBean (a dropped-crate symptom, §152) is legible? Shares the unified-diagnostics meta-concept and the cross-crate composition model.
- How does the provider's first-.await integrate with background-bootstrap's readiness signal as the structured-concurrency join point (the dependent's task awaits the background bean's completion, reactively not polling §2.4)? This couples the deferral handle to the async execution & context-propagation model (T2 keystone) and to background-bootstrap/task-execution — Spring's 'thread blocks' must become 'task awaits a readiness notification', which must be designed, not inherited.
- How does the unresolved presence/membership for the static-validation arm (R3) reconcile with the two-source duality — a dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source could add a candidate AFTER the static link-time validation pass, so the static arm is only sound if the dynamic source is enumerated before validation OR dynamic-source targets are treated as always-runtime? Couples to the cross-crate composition model (static link-time registry as default 'classpath' + optional dynamic WASM-module source as CANDIDATES, not a decision) and to bean-registry's freeze model.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the dedicated feature sections: 'Deferral primitives: ObjectProvider, Provider, @Lazy' (787-796 — the family, the deferral-visible-in-the-type intent, and the eager-by-default-but-opt-in-laziness philosophy), the detailed 'ObjectProvider / ObjectFactory / jakarta.inject.Provider' engine section (2559-2578 — the THREE-TIER STRICTNESS LADDER getObject/getIfAvailable/getIfUnique, the deliberately-not-Supplier + live-handle 're-resolve every call, hold no instance' design intent, the most-missed semantics: getIfAvailable errors on ambiguity / only getIfUnique swallows it, stream() unordered vs orderedStream() comparator, List-eager-vs-stream-lazy, stream(Predicate) filters before construction, the @Lazy-proxy-vs-ObjectProvider 'rather limited' verdict, uniqueness honors @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate, jakarta Provider as the basic JSR-330 analogue, and the v4/v7 JSpecify/getBeanProvider(ParameterizedTypeReference)/javax-dropped notes), the @Lazy section (2580-2593 — relocate-not-remove, direct-injection-defeats-@Lazy, singleton-cached-vs-re-resolved proxy, and the v4/v7 escalation to required cycle-break remedy + mandatory background-bootstrap glue), the candidate-resolution-engine section (2595-2624 — the two-phase filtering-vs-selection split, the determineAutowireCandidate order, and crucially that @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate affect ONLY single points while collections/streams take ALL, plus default-candidate=false being the lever that lets getIfUnique resolve cleanly), the scope-mismatch fix (824 — Provider/ObjectProvider as the prototype-in-singleton remedy), and the eager-by-default fail-fast intent (548, 816). I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc (linkme-default thin-data-macro §166-167, absolute-path ABI §89-102, generics-cannot-register §153, the mandatory anti-DCE self-check + Layer-0/silent-empty-iterable §149-152, Architectures A/B §277-293) and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.1 DX-first, §2.2 low-overhead-#2, §2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimize-Arc, §2.9 rich-early-diagnostics, §2.10 thin-macros, §2.11 unification) and the stable envelope (RPITIT/AFIT NOT dyn-safe -> boxed futures at the dyn construction boundary §160, no async Drop as a first-class constraint §86, trait upcasting 1.86 §80, const TypeId 1.91 + Arc<dyn Any> registry §17/§81, OnceCell for the handle cache).

THE most important framing for THIS feature: it is the OWNER of the consumer-side deferred-lookup HANDLE FAMILY — the thing that makes deferred/optional(0..1)/multiple(0..N)/lazy/cycle-breaking access EXPLICIT IN THE TYPE — keeping the eager default intact. Its soul is two intertwined intents: (1) the three-tier STRICTNESS LADDER where optionality/uniqueness-tolerance is chosen at the CALL SITE by method choice (getObject strict / getIfAvailable absence-only / getIfUnique both), with the most-missed semantic being that getIfAvailable STILL errors on ambiguity; and (2) the LIVE-HANDLE property — holds no instance, re-resolves against current state every call, deliberately NOT a Supplier/cached value — giving scoped-proxy-grade lifecycle safety with an honest debuggable call site. It does NOT own selection (autowiring-resolution's), construction (bean-instantiation's), the eager-pass gate (lazy-initialization's), or filtering (candidate-resolver's) — it PACKAGES a deferred lookup over them.

The three realizations diverge on the SHAPE of the family and WHERE/WHEN strictness is decided: R1 = concrete generic handle TYPES (ObjectProvider/ObjectFactory/Provider/Lazy) over an origin-agnostic erased resolver closure, the ladder as bespoke async methods, deferral visible-in-the-type (highest fidelity to the docs' explicit preference, most teachable, but each handle pins a container back-reference and the type-surface has near-duplicate Provider/ObjectFactory); R2 = ONE unified ProviderHandle ENGINE parameterized by {Strictness, Cardinality} DATA with the public family as thin FACADES (maximal unification — the ladder + the most-missed semantic exist exactly once, best diagnostics as a byproduct of the one driver, unifies Option<T>-arity with getIfAvailable, but more indirection and the single-vs-multiple split awkwardly shares one driver); R3 = deferral split by what is statically knowable — present-singleton-single case is a near-zero-cost handle whose strictness is in the RETURN TYPE (Result<T> / Result<Option<T>> / Option<T>) and whose presence is startup-validated (earliest/richest fail-fast, lowest warm-path cost, best AOT-freeze fit, correctly EXEMPTS @Lazy-cycle-break from validation), with a runtime arm for the genuinely-runtime multiple/scoped/cycle/getIfUnique cases (most divergent, dual fail-fast timing, and the stream/multiple arm is irreducibly runtime so R3 does NOT shrink the full family). R1<->R2 differ on bespoke-types-vs-unified-engine; R3 is partially orthogonal (it could host R2's engine for its runtime arm and is a likely Phase-2 blend: visible-handle public types + R2 engine for runtime arms + R3 startup-presence-validation for the present-singleton case).

Three things to carry forward: (1) The sharpest rust clash is the @LAZY TRANSPARENT PROXY — Spring's CGLIB/JDK proxy that LOOKS like T has no rust-native equivalent, AND the docs themselves call it 'rather limited' and PREFER ObjectProvider, so leaf should lean into deferral-VISIBLE-in-the-type handles (the consumer `.get().await`s) over an invisible proxy — more honest, a deliberate DX divergence, and the workspace-wide 'how does leaf proxy without CGLIB' question shared with lazy-initialization/scoped-proxies/aop-proxying. (2) getObject()/get() is an `.await` ON THE CALLER'S TASK — the live-handle lookup relocates construction onto a possibly-hot path with an execution-context hazard Spring's synchronous lookup never had; the `.await` at the call site IS the explicitness (§2.3), and the handle must integrate with background-bootstrap's readiness as a reactive structured-concurrency join point, not a blocking wait. (3) The LIVE-HANDLE 're-resolve against current state, hold no instance' contract forces the handle to pin an origin-agnostic CONTAINER BACK-REFERENCE (Arc-ish, in tension with §2.5), whose ownership/lifetime is the deferred scope/ownership keystone — so all three realizations keep that reference ABSTRACT and refuse to prejudge it.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization keeps the resolver/container-reference an abstract origin-agnostic type (the scope/ownership keystone), keeps winner CONSTRUCTION an async boxed-future step owned by bean-instantiation (this feature only packages the lookup), keeps the @Lazy proxy-vs-visible-handle question open, keeps stream membership a runtime query over Phase-2's chosen substrate, and stays substrate-neutral on discovery (the two-source static-link + dynamic-WASM-module duality is invisible to the handle — both just feed the candidate set the resolver re-queries; CANDIDATE, not decision). The handle of dyn Trait never assumes native construction (origin-agnostic guardrail honored), so a dynamic WASM-module layer slots in unchanged. Naming ('ObjectProvider', 'ObjectFactory', 'Provider', 'Lazy', 'ProviderHandle', 'Resolve', 'InjectionPoint', 'Resolved', 'Strictness', 'Cardinality', 'ProviderResult', 'ResolveError') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (787-796 the deferral-primitives family + deferral-visible-in-the-type intent; 2559-2578 the three-tier strictness ladder + live-handle + not-Supplier + most-missed semantics + stream-vs-orderedStream + List-eager-vs-stream-lazy + the @Lazy-'rather limited' verdict + jakarta-Provider + v4/v7 notes; 2580-2593 @Lazy relocate-not-remove + direct-injection-defeats + singleton-cached-vs-re-resolved + the cycle-break/background-bootstrap escalation; 2595-2624 the filtering-vs-selection split + @Primary/@Fallback/default-candidate-single-points-only + default-candidate=false enabling getIfUnique + collections/streams-take-all; 824 the prototype-in-singleton provider fix; 548/816 eager-by-default fail-fast); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2 esp. 2.1/2.2/2.3/2.4/2.5/2.9/2.10/2.11, deferred keystones §3, topology/DX §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (anti-DCE self-check + Layer-0 silent-empty-iterable §149-152, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI §89-102, generics-cannot-register §153, linkme-default §166-167, Architectures A/B §277-293, two-source duality candidate); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe -> boxed-future shims at the dyn boundary §160-176, no async Drop first-class constraint §86, trait upcasting 1.86 §80, const TypeId 1.91 + Arc<dyn Any> registry §17/§81, OnceCell for the handle cache, specialization avoided).

## Lazy Initialization  `[lazy-initialization]`

Spring's `@Lazy` and `spring.main.lazy-initialization` are a GATE ON THE EAGER PASS, not a new construction path: a lazy singleton is registered/merged like any other, but bean-instantiation's eager pre-instantiation pass SKIPS it, RELOCATING (not removing) its potential failure from refresh to first `get()`. The precise intent to replicate (docs 2580-2593, 122, 548, 816, 2196): (1) PER-BEAN `@Lazy` — defaults to `true`, applies to a component, a `@Bean`-method analogue, a config-class (CASCADING to its beans), and (escalated in v4/v7) to INJECTION POINTS as the documented remedy for cycles and background-init. `@Lazy(false)` is an explicit OVERRIDE that opts a bean back into eager init under a lazy config/global flag. (2) GLOBAL FLAG — a single switch flips the DEFAULT for definitions that did not explicitly set their lazy flag, with three structural EXCLUSIONS that must stay eager because deferring them is semantically wrong, not just slow: infrastructure/role beans (the framework's own ordered machinery), the post-all-singletons SmartInitializingSingleton-analogue beans (laziness would mean their once-after-everything callback never fires), and anything matched by a user-supplied exclude-filter SPI (the seam for DSLs/registrars that need their beans eager). A lazy auto-startup runtime-lifecycle bean is STILL started (laziness barely affects it). (3) THE CENTRAL TRAP — DIRECT injection silently DEFEATS `@Lazy`: if any EAGER bean injects a lazy bean directly by type/value, the container must build it at refresh anyway. Laziness only HOLDS when the REFERENCE is deferred — via a lazy-resolution proxy at the injection point, a deferral handle (ObjectProvider-analogue, see deferral-primitives), or simply having no eager dependents. (4) The proxy is SINGLETON-CACHED (resolve-once-then-cache) but RE-RESOLVES every access for shorter scopes. (5) Honest framing: lazy reduces STARTUP WORK, not total heap — the process must still be sized for all beans; and a lazy bean carrying event-listener registration silently LOSES it (a deferred bean cannot have registered at refresh). leaf must replicate this INTENT — a flag that gates the eager driver, cascading config-class semantics, the three structural exclusions + the exclude-filter SPI, the global-default-vs-explicit-flag precedence, the deferred-reference requirement for laziness to actually hold, and the relocate-failure-to-first-access contract — WITHOUT Spring's JDK/CGLIB lazy-resolution proxy (Rust has no runtime subclassing; the "lazy proxy at the injection point" must become a rust-native deferral type or a generated wrapper), its `@EventListener`-silently-lost footgun inherited unmentioned, and its assumption that "build at first access" is a synchronous call (in async leaf, first access is an `.await` whose execution context, blocking risk, and cancellation must be explicit). It must NOT decide the deferred-handle/proxy realization (shared with deferral-primitives), the singleton-handle/ownership type the lazy slot eventually holds, the global-flag's compile-vs-runtime locus, or whether exclude-filters are a runtime SPI or a build-time predicate — those are Phase 2.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Lazy as a definition flag that gates the eager pass + a first-access creation slot (Spring-faithful, retyped): laziness lives in metadata, the eager driver skips it, first get() drives the normal creation sequence under the per-bean guard
Treat `@Lazy` exactly as Spring does — a BOOLEAN on the merged definition, NOT a distinct kind of bean. The macro emits `lazy: Option<bool>` on the Descriptor (None = unset, so the global default can flip it; Some(true/false) = explicit, honoring `@Lazy(false)` as an override). A config-class-analogue annotated lazy CASCADES the flag to the beans it declares at metadata-emit time (the macro, given the enclosing config's lazy state, stamps each emitted bean's `lazy: None`→inherits, so cascade is a metadata default, not runtime logic). The GLOBAL FLAG is a single container-config value resolved during refresh setup: a `resolve_lazy(def, global_flag, exclude) -> bool` policy that returns `def.lazy.unwrap_or(global_flag && !is_excluded(def))`, where `is_excluded` checks the three structural exclusions (role==Infrastructure, implements the SmartInitializing-analogue marker, started-at-refresh runtime-lifecycle) PLUS any registered exclude-filter. This is the literal `LazyInitializationBeanFactoryPostProcessor` intent: a refresh-time pass that finalizes each unset definition's effective lazy flag BEFORE the eager pass runs. bean-instantiation's eager pre-instantiation loop already reads `!abstract && singleton && !lazy` (its documented gate); this feature OWNS the right-hand `!lazy` term and its resolution policy. A lazy singleton is therefore registered, merged, candidate-resolvable, but NOT built in the eager pass. FIRST ACCESS is the ordinary path: `get<T>()` (or a resolved dependency during another bean's populate) finds no `ready` entry, sees the slot is lazy, and drives bean-instantiation's normal `create_singleton` sequence under the SAME per-bean creation guard — so the lazy bean is constructed/populated/initialized and published into `ready` exactly once (singleton-cached), and its failure surfaces HERE with the full assembly chain (relocate-not-remove). The DIRECT-INJECTION-DEFEATS-LAZY trap is preserved structurally: if an eager bean's populate resolves a lazy bean DIRECTLY (not via a deferral handle), the recursive resolution builds it at refresh — exactly Spring's behavior, and a rich diagnostic can WARN that an eager dependent forced a lazy bean eager (improving on Spring's silence). For laziness to HOLD, the dependent must inject a deferral handle (delegated to deferral-primitives — this feature does not invent the handle type). Prototypes are never eager anyway, so `@Lazy` on a prototype is a no-op (matching Spring); web/custom scopes never cache the first resolution.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity and lowest new surface: `@Lazy` is a metadata flag gating an existing driver term, so this feature adds a resolution POLICY and an eager-pass SKIP, not a new construction path — it composes cleanly with bean-instantiation (owns the `!lazy` gate) and bean-registry (the slot already exists, just unfilled until first access).; The relocate-not-remove contract falls out for free: first `get()` runs the identical create_singleton sequence under the identical per-bean guard, so a lazy bean's wiring/init failure surfaces with the SAME rich assembly-chain diagnostic (§2.9) it would have at boot — just later, at the documented cost of deferred predictability.; The three structural exclusions + exclude-filter SPI map 1:1 to Spring and are pure predicate logic over the definition (role/marker/lifecycle + a filter list) — no construction machinery, easy to make a rich, testable policy table, and the SmartInitializing/auto-startup exclusions correctly prevent the 'callback never fires'/'listener silently lost' footguns by keeping those beans eager.; Cascade-from-config and `@Lazy(false)`-override become metadata defaults the THIN macro stamps (Option<bool> with None=inherit), keeping all logic hand-writable (§2.10) — no hidden runtime cascade walk; cargo-expand shows the resolved flag.; Singleton-cached-once is automatic: first access publishes into `ready`, all subsequent accesses hit the cache lock-free — no separate caching layer, the registry's existing publication IS the cache (matching Spring's 'caches on first access for singletons').; Improves on Spring's silence: because the policy runs at refresh and direct-injection forcing is observable during populate, leaf can emit a diagnostic ('bean X marked @Lazy was built eagerly because eager bean Y injects it directly — use a deferral handle to defer') — turning two of Spring's documented silent traps into actionable warnings.

**Cons:** First-access creation runs on the CALLER's task, not the bootstrap task — a lazy bean whose factory blocks or hits a DB pool now `.await`s (or must spawn_blocking) on whatever execution context first touched it, which may be a latency-sensitive request hot path; the cost Spring hides behind a synchronous proxy call becomes an explicit async cost at an unpredictable site (§2.3 execution-context discipline must be surfaced to the user).; The direct-injection-defeats-lazy trap, while faithful, means `@Lazy` ALONE rarely defers anything in a richly-wired graph — laziness only truly holds with a deferral handle, so this feature is half-useless without deferral-primitives, and users will be surprised their @Lazy bean built at boot anyway (the diagnostic mitigates but does not remove the conceptual trap).; The global flag's resolution locus is ambiguous in leaf's compile-vs-runtime-undecided world: a refresh-time `resolve_lazy` pass is runtime, but if leaf freezes the graph at build time (AOT lineage) the flag might want to be a build-time `cfg`/codegen input — this feature can't pick, so it must define the POLICY abstractly and leave the locus to Phase 2.; First-access concurrency: two tasks racing to first-access the same lazy singleton must serialize on the per-bean guard (one builds, the other awaits the published result) — correct, but it imports bean-instantiation's hardest-won strict/lenient guard semantics onto the HOT path (request-time), where Spring only paid it at cold boot; a slow lazy init now blocks every concurrent first-accessor.; No async Drop means a lazy bean built at first-access and later failing still needs its partial-construction cleanup run explicitly — and because it was built off the bootstrap path, the context-close teardown ledger must capture lazily-created singletons too (the ledger can't be finalized at end-of-refresh), complicating reverse-order shutdown.

**Mechanisms considered:** `lazy: Option<bool>` on the Descriptor (None=unset→global-default-eligible, Some=explicit override) emitted by the thin macro; config-class cascade as a metadata default the macro stamps (enclosing-lazy → emitted-bean None inherits), not a runtime walk; refresh-time `resolve_lazy(def, global_flag, exclude)` policy = `def.lazy.unwrap_or(global && !excluded)`; this feature owns the `!lazy` term in bean-instantiation's eager gate; structural exclusion predicate (Infrastructure role / SmartInitializing-marker / started-at-refresh lifecycle) + a registered exclude-filter list (SPI seam); first-access lazy creation = bean-instantiation's normal create_singleton under the same per-bean guard, publishing into `ready` once (singleton-cached by the existing cache); direct-injection-defeats-lazy preserved structurally (eager populate resolving a lazy bean directly builds it), with a new diagnostic warning rather than Spring's silence

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Substrate-neutral on collection: the lazy flag is just another field on whatever Descriptor the static link-time registry (linkme default) or a build-time codegen path emits — the macro stamps `lazy: Option<bool>` via an absolute path to the leaf-core Descriptor type, holding zero logic (§2.10, cross-crate §99-102). The resolution POLICY (`resolve_lazy` + exclusions + exclude-filters) lives entirely in leaf-core alongside the eager driver — no cross-crate ABI beyond the flag field. The exclude-filter SPI is itself a cross-crate extension point: a contributing crate registers a filter (the same shape as any other contribution — a Descriptor-like entry in a distributed slice), which WIDENS the contribution surface but is exactly the DSL-registrar seam Spring's LazyInitializationExcludeFilter serves (CANDIDATE: a static link-time filter registry as the default 'classpath' PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned source for a WASM-module layer's filters — not a decision). The global flag is a config input bound from the environment family (config-data/property-resolution) at refresh — but it COULD instead be a build-time codegen/`cfg` input if the graph is frozen at build time (the compile-vs-runtime keystone, §3); this feature defines the policy so it works either way. ANTI-DCE INTERACTION (cross-crate §139-152): laziness must NOT make a contributing crate's bean look DCE'd — a legitimately-lazy bean is registered but uninstantiated, which is indistinguishable from a silently-DCE'd one to a naive count-check; the eager-pass self-check must count DEFINITIONS (registered descriptors), not instances, so a lazy bean still proves its crate linked. Generics-cannot-register inherited (the descriptor names concrete types; macro hard-errors on generic beans). The WASM-module 'classpath' slots in identically: a lazy WASM-backed descriptor is skipped by the eager pass and built via its origin-agnostic factory on first access — same path.

**T2 — execution context:** The `resolve_lazy` policy pass runs at REFRESH SETUP on the bootstrap task (cold path, synchronous, before the eager pass) — pure predicate evaluation, no IO, no await. The eager pass then SKIPS lazy singletons (it simply never calls create on them). FIRST-ACCESS creation runs on the CALLER's task — this is the load-bearing execution-context shift: if `get()` is first-called from a request hot path, the lazy bean's construct/populate/init `.await` points run THERE, so a blocking factory must spawn_blocking and the user must understand they relocated cold-path boot cost onto a hot path (§2.3 — explicit execution context; this is the rust-native sharp edge of Spring's hidden synchronous-proxy cost). Concurrency: concurrent first-accessors of the same lazy singleton serialize on bean-instantiation's per-bean creation guard (never global, §2.5) — one builds, others await the published `ready` value (safe publication of the finished instance, §concurrency-contract); the guard scope stays publication-only, init `.await`s are NOT under a held guard (the rust-native dissolution of Spring's @PostConstruct-under-lock trap, applied to the hot-path lazy build). CANCELLATION: a first-access build cancelled mid-sequence (the caller's request was cancelled) must drop the partially-constructed instance, clear in_creation, and run any registered async cleanup explicitly (no async Drop, §86) — and crucially must NOT leave the lazy slot in a half-built state for the next accessor; the guard release + slot-stays-unfilled means the next first-accessor retries cleanly. Errors propagate as the rich ResolveError with the full assembly chain (§2.9), now attributed to a first-access site rather than refresh. TEARDOWN: a lazily-created singleton must register reverse-dependency teardown data when it publishes (just like an eager one), so context-close's `shutdown().await` drains it — meaning the teardown ledger is APPEND-grown past end-of-refresh (lazy beans join it on first access), a divergence from a refresh-frozen ledger that context-close must accommodate.

#### R2 — Lazy as a generated deferred-reference WRAPPER at the injection point (proxy-intent, rust-native): the bean is never gated globally; instead `@Lazy` on a CONSUMER injects a `Lazy<T>` wrapper that builds-on-first-deref, so laziness is a property of the EDGE, not the node
Question Spring's node-flag model. Spring needs a global LazyInitializationBeanFactoryPostProcessor + lazy-resolution PROXIES precisely because the JVM's eager refresh and runtime proxying make per-edge laziness awkward; but the docs ALREADY say laziness only HOLDS when the REFERENCE is deferred, and v4/v7 ESCALATES `@Lazy` to an injection-point remedy. So make the injection EDGE the primary locus: `@Lazy` at an injection point causes the macro to emit a typed deferred wrapper `Lazy<T>` (a rust-native lazy-resolution proxy: a small handle holding a `OnceCell<T-handle>` + a clone of the resolution closure) INSTEAD of resolving `T` eagerly during populate. The dependent gets `Lazy<MyService>` and calls `.get().await` (or derefs) on first use, which drives creation-on-demand and caches (singleton-cached via the OnceCell for singletons; re-resolves each call for shorter scopes by NOT caching — matching Spring's scope-dependent caching). Because the edge is deferred, the target bean genuinely is NOT built at refresh UNLESS some OTHER eager bean references it directly — which is the honest, structural realization of 'direct injection defeats lazy' (the wrapper is the only thing that defers; a direct edge does not). The GLOBAL FLAG is then a metadata-level default that flips unset injection EDGES to wrapped (with the same three structural exclusions applied to the TARGET bean: never wrap an edge to an infrastructure/SmartInitializing/auto-startup bean), achieving 'default everything lazy' as 'default every deferrable edge wrapped' — but a bean with NO eager wrapped-or-unwrapped dependents simply is never built (true laziness by graph reachability). `@Lazy` on a bean DEFINITION (component/config) still exists as R1's flag for the 'no dependents until someone get()s it' case, so R2 SUBSUMES R1's node-flag for the definition case and ADDS the edge-wrapper for the injection-point case — but leads with the edge model as the thing that actually makes laziness hold. The wrapper is origin-agnostic: `Lazy<T>` holds an erased resolution closure that calls the container's get, so a WASM-backed or test-double target resolves identically on first deref.

**Pros:** Makes laziness ACTUALLY HOLD by construction (the brief's core intent — relocate failure): a wrapped edge genuinely defers, and a bean with no non-deferred dependents is never built, so 'I marked it @Lazy and it still built at boot' (Spring's #1 documented trap) becomes structurally impossible at a wrapped edge — laziness is visible IN THE TYPE (`Lazy<T>`), honest and self-documenting (§2.9, and matches the deferral-primitives philosophy of encoding deferral in the type).; No global mutating post-processor pass: laziness is a property of edges and the reachability graph, not a refresh-time rewrite of definitions — cleaner than Spring's LazyInitializationBeanFactoryPostProcessor, and the 'no eager dependents → never built' case is pure graph reachability (the planner in bean-instantiation R2 can even PROVE which beans are reachable-only-through-Lazy edges and exclude them from the eager wave).; Singleton-cached-vs-re-resolved-per-scope is a natural property of the wrapper (OnceCell for singletons, no-cache for prototype/scoped) — directly realizing docs 2589 without special-casing, and the wrapper is the SAME machinery deferral-primitives needs for ObjectProvider, so this UNIFIES `@Lazy`-edge and ObjectProvider into one deferred-handle family (§2.11).; Async-honest: `Lazy<T>::get()` is `.await`, so the execution-context cost of first-access is EXPLICIT at the call site (the developer wrote `.get().await`), not hidden behind a transparent proxy that secretly blocks — a strict DX improvement over Spring's invisible synchronous proxy (§2.3).; Origin-agnostic by construction: the wrapper holds an erased resolution closure over the container, so native/WASM-module/test-double targets all resolve on first deref through the identical path — the dynamic 'classpath' slots in with no special lazy handling.; Eliminates the @EventListener-silently-lost footgun's surprise: because wrapping is an edge property visible in the type, a bean is only 'lazy' from a consumer's view — the bean DEFINITION still registers its listeners at refresh unless it's also definition-@Lazy with no eager reachability, making the lost-listener case rarer and more diagnosable.

**Cons:** Diverges from Spring's transparent-proxy DX: the consumer must hold `Lazy<MyService>` and call `.get().await` rather than just `MyService` — laziness is NO LONGER transparent at the use site, which is more honest but more ceremony, and breaks the 'inject the type, use it normally' feel for the global-flag case where the user didn't ask for a wrapper (the global flag wrapping ALL edges would force `Lazy<_>` everywhere, which is unacceptable DX — so the global flag likely must fall back to R1's node-gate, meaning R2 doesn't cleanly cover the global-flag intent).; The global-default-lazy intent is awkward: you cannot transparently wrap every edge as `Lazy<T>` without rewriting consumer signatures (impossible at runtime, invasive at build time), so 'spring.main.lazy-initialization=true' must EITHER fall back to R1's node-flag gate (reintroducing the direct-injection trap the wrapper avoided) OR generate a transparent deref-proxy (Rust has no transparent runtime proxy; would need a generated newtype implementing the target's trait and deferring each method — heavy codegen, only works for trait-typed injection, see R3).; `Lazy<T>` re-resolving-per-access for non-singleton scopes means a wrapper holding a closure that re-enters the container's get on every deref — a HOT-path resolution cost for scoped beans, and the closure must capture an origin-agnostic container handle (an Arc-ish back-reference, in tension with §2.5 minimize-Arc) that also raises a lifecycle question (the wrapper outlives a single resolution, so it pins a container reference).; Two models coexist (edge-wrapper for injection-point @Lazy + node-flag for definition-@Lazy/global-flag) that must agree on the three exclusions and the singleton-cache semantics — a coherence burden, and the boundary between 'this @Lazy wrapped the edge' and 'this @Lazy gated the node' may confuse users (Spring presents one annotation; leaf would present two behaviors).; Overlaps deferral-primitives so heavily that `@Lazy`-edge might just BE 'ObjectProvider with eager-cache-on-first-get' — risking that this feature has no independent realization and is entirely subsumed by deferral-primitives' handle family (a scoping question for Phase 2/3, flagged), which would make R2's contribution purely 'the global-flag default and the exclusions', i.e. R1.

**Mechanisms considered:** typed `Lazy<T>` wrapper (OnceCell<T-handle> + erased resolution closure) emitted by the macro at a `@Lazy` injection point instead of eager-resolving T; laziness-as-edge-property: a target reachable only through `Lazy<_>` (or no) edges is never built (graph reachability), making 'direct injection defeats lazy' structural; singleton-cached (OnceCell) vs re-resolved-per-access (no cache) keyed on the target's scope — realizing docs 2589; global flag = default-wrap unset deferrable edges (exclusions applied to the TARGET) — with the honest caveat that transparent global wrapping needs a fallback to R1's node-gate; definition-`@Lazy` retained as R1's node-flag for the no-dependents case; R2 leads with the edge-wrapper and subsumes the node-flag; origin-agnostic erased resolution closure so native/WASM/test-double targets resolve identically on first deref — unifies with deferral-primitives' handle family

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The `Lazy<T>` wrapper type lives in leaf-core (a stable public type); the macro emits its construction at the injection point via an absolute path (§99-102), holding no logic. Because laziness is an EDGE property determined by what the CONSUMER's macro emits, it is fully local to each crate's compilation — no cross-crate registry needed for the edge case (a strong T1 property: per-edge laziness needs zero link-time collection, unlike the node-flag global-default which needs whole-graph reachability). The global-flag-default-wrap, however, needs to know the TARGET bean's scope and exclusion status to decide wrap-eligibility — which is cross-crate metadata (the target may live in another crate), so it must read the resolved descriptor table, coupling it to the same link-time registry the rest of the system uses. The exclude-filter SPI is identical to R1 (a registered filter contribution). The 'reachable only through Lazy edges → never built' optimization needs whole-graph edge analysis, which is exactly what a build-time codegen/planner pass (bean-instantiation R2) could compute — best AOT fit, but at runtime needs the conservative over-approximation R2-there flagged. Generics: `Lazy<Component<T>>` still can't register the target; the wrapper is generic-friendly on the CONSUMER side but the target descriptor must be concrete (macro hard-errors on generic targets as everywhere). WASM-module duality: a `Lazy<T>` edge to a WASM-backed target resolves through the target's origin-agnostic factory on first deref — the wrapper doesn't care about origin (CANDIDATE: static-registry targets and dynamic directory-scanned WASM targets both resolved identically by the wrapper's erased closure — not a decision).
