**T2 — execution context:** The wrapper is CONSTRUCTED at the consumer's populate time (cold path, refresh, on the bootstrap task) holding only a closure and an empty OnceCell — no target construction yet. FIRST DEREF (`Lazy<T>::get().await`) runs on WHATEVER task calls it — explicitly async, so the execution-context cost is visible at the call site (§2.3); a blocking target factory spawn_blocks there. For a SINGLETON target, the OnceCell + the container's per-bean creation guard cooperate: the first deref builds-and-publishes-and-caches (subsequent derefs hit the OnceCell lock-free, reactive-not-polling §2.4); concurrent first-derefs serialize on the per-bean guard, not a global lock (§2.5). For PROTOTYPE/scoped targets the wrapper re-resolves each deref (no cache) — each `.get().await` is a fresh creation on the caller's task (hot-path cost, the documented re-resolve-per-access behavior). CANCELLATION: a cancelled first-deref drops the in-flight target build (clearing in_creation, running explicit async cleanup — no async Drop §86); the OnceCell stays empty so the next deref retries; the wrapper itself dropped without ever deref'ing builds nothing (true deferral — nothing to clean). The wrapper holds an origin-agnostic container back-reference for re-resolution, whose lifetime must not outlive the container (a §2.5 concern: minimize the Arc, scope the handle). Errors are rich ResolveError attributed to the deref site (§2.9). TEARDOWN: a singleton built via a Lazy deref registers teardown like any singleton (context-close drains it); the wrapper itself owns no teardown (it's a handle). For the global-flag node-gate fallback, T2 is identical to R1's first-access path.

#### R3 — Unified deferral policy over an origin-agnostic Provider: `@Lazy`, the global flag, and exclude-filters are one composable LazinessPolicy that decorates how a Provider is SCHEDULED for the eager pass — laziness is a scheduling decision, not a bean kind or an edge type
Push unification (§2.11) hardest: there is ONE origin-agnostic `Provider` per bean (shared with bean-instantiation R3 / bean-registry R3), and laziness is purely a question of WHEN the eager pass schedules that Provider's creation — never a separate bean kind, never (necessarily) a separate edge type. Model a `LazinessPolicy` the eager pass consults per Provider: `fn schedule(&self, p: &Provider, ctx: &RefreshCtx) -> Schedule` returning `Schedule::Eager` (build in the eager pass), `Schedule::Deferred` (skip; build on first demand), or `Schedule::DeferredHandle` (skip AND require consumers to hold a deferral handle — the bridge to deferral-primitives). The DEFAULT policy returns Eager. `@Lazy` on a definition installs a per-Provider override → Deferred. The GLOBAL FLAG installs a context-wide policy → Deferred-by-default, COMPOSED with an EXCLUSION policy (a `Vec<Box<dyn LazinessExcludeFilter>>` plus the built-in three: infrastructure-role, SmartInitializing-marker, started-at-refresh-lifecycle) that forces those back to Eager — exclusions are just higher-priority policy entries, so 'global lazy except infrastructure' is policy COMPOSITION, not special-casing. `@Lazy(false)` is a highest-priority per-Provider Eager override. The policies form an ORDERED chain (per-bean-explicit > exclude-filter > global-default > framework-default), evaluated once per Provider at refresh to yield each Provider's effective Schedule — making the precedence rules (which Spring scatters across @Lazy(false), exclusions, and the flag) an explicit, inspectable, testable ordered table (§2.9 diagnosability). The eager pass becomes: for each Provider, `match policy.schedule(p) { Eager => create_now, Deferred|DeferredHandle => register_lazy_slot }`. A `register_lazy_slot` installs a first-access trampoline that, on demand, drives the SAME Provider through the SAME creation strategy bundle (bean-instantiation R3's Creator) — so a lazy bean is created and published IDENTICALLY to an eager one, just later. DeferredHandle additionally signals the consumer-side macro to emit a deferral handle (delegating the wrapper to deferral-primitives), unifying R2's edge model as a POLICY OUTCOME rather than a separate mechanism. This makes `@Lazy`, the global flag, exclude-filters, and the deferral-handle requirement all aspects of ONE policy the eager pass already consults — and keeps the door open for future scheduling modes (e.g. background-lazy: defer to a background executor, bridging background-bootstrap).

**Pros:** Maximal unification (§2.11): per-bean @Lazy, the global flag, the three structural exclusions, the exclude-filter SPI, and @Lazy(false) all become ENTRIES in one ordered LazinessPolicy chain that yields a per-Provider Schedule — Spring's scattered precedence (flag vs explicit vs exclusion) becomes a single inspectable table, and new modes (background-lazy, condition-driven-lazy) slot in as new Schedule variants without touching the eager pass.; Origin-agnostic by the strongest construction: laziness decorates a Provider's SCHEDULE, and the Provider is the only construction seam — so native/FactoryBean-product/WASM-module/test-double beans are all gated, deferred, and first-access-built through the identical strategy bundle (the dynamic 'classpath' needs zero lazy-specific handling; a WASM Provider is scheduled Deferred and built on demand like any other).; The precedence table is the richest possible diagnostic (§2.9): a `lazy report` (analogous to condition-report) can show, per bean, its effective Schedule AND WHICH policy entry decided it ('bean X: Deferred — global flag; bean Y: Eager — excluded by InfrastructureRoleFilter; bean Z: Eager — @Lazy(false) override') — turning Spring's opaque 'why did this build at boot?' into an accountable, debuggable verdict.; Composes the global-flag intent cleanly WITHOUT R2's transparent-wrapper problem: 'global lazy' is Schedule::Deferred for all non-excluded Providers (the eager pass just skips them and installs first-access trampolines) — no consumer signature rewriting, no transparent-proxy impossibility; the DeferredHandle variant opt-in handles the 'make laziness actually hold' case by delegating to deferral-primitives, so R3 gets R1's transparent-global-default AND R2's honest-deferral as two policy outcomes.; Bridges background-bootstrap and the AOT lineage: a `Schedule::Background` (defer to bootstrap executor) is a natural future variant, and the whole policy evaluation is a pure function over Providers+config that a build-time codegen pass could pre-compute into a static schedule (best compile-vs-runtime fit, §3) — the eager pass consumes the schedule whether computed at build or refresh.; Keeps the eager-pass driver oblivious to laziness mechanics: bean-instantiation just asks the policy for a Schedule and either creates-now or installs-a-trampoline — laziness is fully factored out as a consulted policy, so this feature owns its logic without entangling the creation sequence (clean separation, testable in isolation).

**Cons:** Most indirection and the strongest risk of over-engineering Phase 1: a full ordered-policy-chain + Schedule-enum + trampoline SPI risks DECLARING a 'laziness is a pluggable scheduling policy' meta-architecture that Phase 2 owns — must be held as a CANDIDATE shape; its generality only pays off if leaf ships an opinionated invisible default (which then resembles R1), questioning whether the policy SPI earns its weight for what is, in the common case, a single boolean flag.; The `Box<dyn LazinessExcludeFilter>` chain + per-Provider policy evaluation is runtime ceremony for what is usually a static decision — boxed-dyn dispatch and a policy walk per bean at refresh (cold, so tolerable) but it WIDENS the stable ABI (the policy/filter traits and the Schedule enum become public surface the whole workspace may reference), a real cost weighed against R1's plain flag.; Heavily depends on bean-instantiation adopting the strategy-bundle Provider model (its R3) — if Phase 2 picks bean-instantiation's R1 (literal doCreateBean) or R2 (plan-then-execute) instead, R3-here's 'schedule a Provider through the strategy bundle' loses its substrate and must be re-expressed, so this realization is coupled to a sibling's deferred decision (flagged as a phase2Question).; The DeferredHandle variant that 'signals the consumer macro to emit a deferral handle' requires cross-compilation-unit coordination (the policy is evaluated where the bean is DEFINED/at refresh, but the handle must be emitted where the bean is CONSUMED, possibly another crate) — a real T1 tension: a runtime policy verdict cannot retroactively change a consumer crate's already-compiled injection-point code, so DeferredHandle can only be a BUILD-TIME or definition-time signal, not a refresh-time one.; Over-unification could blur user mental models: presenting `@Lazy` as 'one entry in a scheduling policy chain with four precedence levels and three exclusion filters' is more conceptual surface than Spring's 'mark it lazy, it builds later' — DX (#1) may prefer R1's plain flag with the policy kept invisible, making R3's explicit policy machinery an internal detail rather than a user-facing model.; Same first-access execution-context and no-async-Drop cleanup concerns as R1/R2 (a trampoline-driven first build runs on the caller's task; cancellation must clean partial construction) — the policy abstraction does not dissolve these; it just relocates where the trampoline is installed, so the hardest async concerns persist behind more indirection.

**Mechanisms considered:** `LazinessPolicy::schedule(Provider, RefreshCtx) -> Schedule {Eager, Deferred, DeferredHandle, (future) Background}` consulted by the eager pass per Provider; ordered policy chain (per-bean-explicit > exclude-filter > global-default > framework-default-Eager) yielding one effective Schedule per Provider — Spring's scattered precedence as one inspectable table; exclusions as higher-priority policy entries (built-in Infrastructure/SmartInitializing/auto-startup filters + a `Box<dyn LazinessExcludeFilter>` SPI list), not special-cases; `register_lazy_slot` = first-access trampoline that drives the SAME Provider through bean-instantiation R3's Creator strategy bundle — lazy built identically to eager; DeferredHandle as the bridge to deferral-primitives (build-time/definition-time signal to emit a consumer-side handle), subsuming R2's edge model as a policy outcome; a `lazy report` (condition-report analogue) emitting per-bean effective Schedule + deciding policy entry for diagnosability

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The Provider and the strategy bundle live in leaf-core (shared with bean-instantiation/bean-registry R3); LazinessPolicy, Schedule, and the LazinessExcludeFilter trait are leaf-core public surface — a WIDER stable ABI than R1's flag, the cost of the unification. The per-Provider Schedule is computed at refresh from the descriptor table (collected via the static link-time registry / build-time codegen, substrate-neutral) OR pre-computed by a build-time codegen pass into a static schedule (best AOT fit — the policy is a pure function over Providers+config). Exclude-filters are cross-crate contributions (registered like any other, the DSL-registrar seam) — CANDIDATE: a static link-time filter registry as default 'classpath' PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned source for WASM-layer filters (not a decision). The DeferredHandle T1 tension is sharp: the policy verdict is computed at the DEFINITION site / refresh, but the consumer-side handle emission happens in possibly-another crate at COMPILE time — so DeferredHandle cannot be a purely-runtime decision; it must be expressible at build time (a codegen pass that, knowing the policy, emits the consumer handle) OR reduced to 'the consumer opted into a handle via @Lazy at the injection point' (R2's local model). ANTI-DCE (§139-152): like R1, the self-check must count registered Providers/definitions, not instances, so a Deferred bean still proves its crate linked; the `lazy report` doubles as a discovery diagnostic (a crate expected to contribute beans showing zero Providers signals a DCE/Layer-0 problem). Generics: no Provider until concrete → macro hard-errors. WASM duality: a WASM-backed Provider is scheduled by the same policy and first-access-built through the same Creator — most natural slot-in of the three.

**T2 — execution context:** Policy evaluation runs at REFRESH (cold, bootstrap task, synchronous) — a pure per-Provider walk yielding Schedules; no IO/await. The eager pass then creates Eager Providers and installs first-access trampolines for Deferred/DeferredHandle ones. FIRST-ACCESS via a trampoline runs on the CALLER's task (same execution-context shift as R1: a blocking factory spawn_blocks; the cost is relocated to wherever first-access happens — §2.3 explicit). Concurrent first-accessors serialize on bean-instantiation's per-bean creation guard via the trampoline (never global, §2.5); the strategy bundle's PublicationStrategy publishes once into `ready` (singleton-cached) or returns an owned move (prototype, no cache). The CreationGuard strategy keeps init `.await`s out from under a held guard (dissolving the @PostConstruct-under-lock trap on the hot-path lazy build). CANCELLATION: a cancelled trampoline build drops the partial instance, the strategy's cleanup ledger runs explicit async teardown (no async Drop §86), and the lazy slot stays unfilled for a clean retry — the policy/trampoline must specify this since the Provider may have already acquired resources. Errors are rich ResolveError attributed to the first-access site, ideally annotated with the bean's effective Schedule and deciding policy entry for the fullest diagnostic (§2.9). The Background Schedule variant (future) would run the build on a bootstrap executor (cold path, background-bootstrap's context) rather than the caller's — an explicit alternative execution context the policy selects. TEARDOWN: a trampoline-built singleton registers reverse-dependency teardown on publish (context-close drains it via shutdown().await); the ledger grows past end-of-refresh as Deferred beans materialize (same as R1).

### Rust clashes
- Spring's lazy-resolution PROXY has no rust-native equivalent — Rust has no runtime subclassing/CGLIB and no transparent JDK dynamic proxy. Spring's `@Lazy` at an injection point injects a proxy that LOOKS like `MyService` but secretly resolves-and-delegates on first method call; this is impossible to do transparently in Rust without either (a) a typed wrapper the consumer must explicitly `.get().await` (R2/R3 DeferredHandle — honest but non-transparent, breaks 'inject T, use T'), or (b) generating a newtype that impls the target's TRAIT and defers each method (only works for trait-typed injection, heavy codegen, and async methods force boxed-future shims at the dyn boundary §160). leaf must choose deferral-VISIBLE-in-the-type over Spring's invisible proxy — a DX divergence that is arguably more honest (matches deferral-primitives' philosophy) but loses Spring's drop-in transparency. The 'singleton-cached vs re-resolved-per-scope proxy' behavior (docs 2589) must then live in the wrapper's caching logic, not a proxy's.
- First access is `.await`, not a synchronous call — relocating COLD boot cost onto a potentially HOT request path with an EXECUTION-CONTEXT hazard Spring never had. Spring's lazy bean builds synchronously on first proxy call (it may block, but on the JVM that's 'just' a blocked thread). In async leaf, first-access creation runs construct/populate/init `.await` points on WHATEVER task first touches the bean — if that's a latency-sensitive request, a slow/blocking lazy factory now stalls a hot path (or must spawn_blocking) and concurrent first-accessors serialize on the per-bean creation guard, importing bean-instantiation's cold-path concurrency onto the hot path. leaf must make this execution-context shift EXPLICIT (§2.3) — the rust-native sharp edge of Spring's hidden synchronous-proxy cost — rather than silently inheriting 'lazy is free until you touch it'.
- The global-default-lazy flag CANNOT transparently wrap every edge in Rust the way Spring's BeanFactoryPostProcessor rewrites every definition. Spring flips a flag and a post-processor mutates all unset definitions; the proxies make it transparent. In Rust, 'default everything lazy' as an EDGE property (R2) would force `Lazy<_>` into every consumer signature (impossible at runtime, invasive at build time), so the global flag must fall back to a NODE-gate (R1/R3 Schedule::Deferred) that just skips the eager pass and builds on first DIRECT access — which re-imports the 'direct injection defeats lazy' trap. leaf cannot have BOTH transparent-global-default AND laziness-actually-holds without generated trait-newtype proxies; the flag's realization locus (runtime node-gate vs build-time edge-codegen) is entangled with the compile-vs-runtime keystone.
- No async Drop makes a partially-constructed lazy bean's cleanup a non-syntactic discipline ON THE HOT PATH. A first-access build that acquires an async-cleanup resource then fails/cancels (e.g. the triggering request was cancelled mid-build) cannot self-clean via Drop (§86); the trampoline/wrapper must retain enough state to run registered async cleanup explicitly AND leave the lazy slot unfilled so the next accessor retries cleanly — and because lazy beans materialize AFTER refresh, the context-close teardown ledger cannot be frozen at end-of-refresh; it must grow as lazy singletons publish, complicating reverse-order shutdown (a divergence from a refresh-frozen ledger that context-close must accommodate).
- `@Lazy`-but-registered is INDISTINGUISHABLE from silently-DCE'd to a naive anti-DCE check — a cross-crate hazard. The cross-crate doc's mandatory self-check (§152) guards against a contributing crate being dead-code-eliminated (its registrations vanish into an empty iterable). But a legitimately-lazy bean is registered-yet-uninstantiated — exactly what a DCE'd bean looks like to an instance-count check. leaf's self-check must count DEFINITIONS/Providers (which prove the crate linked), not instances, or a lazy bean would either mask a real DCE failure or trigger a false alarm. This couples laziness to the discovery/self-check design.
- Cascading `@Lazy` from a config-class to its beans is a JVM annotation-inheritance idiom that becomes a MACRO-EMIT-TIME default in Rust, not a runtime walk. Spring cascades `@Lazy` on a `@Configuration` to its `@Bean` methods via annotation processing. Rust has no runtime annotation inheritance; the thin macro must, knowing the enclosing config's lazy state, stamp each emitted bean's flag as None-inherits-or-explicit (§2.10 — logic stays hand-writable, cargo-expand shows the resolved flag). This is fine but means the cascade is fixed at compile time and cannot be influenced by runtime config the way the global flag can — a subtle precedence interaction (config-cascade is compile-time, global-flag is runtime) leaf must order explicitly.
- Spring's `@EventListener`-silently-lost-when-lazy footgun should NOT be inherited unmentioned. Because a lazy bean isn't built at refresh, its event-listener registrations (events/@EventListener) never happen — Spring's classic 'my listener never fires' bug. In leaf this is worse to debug across crates. leaf should either (a) make event-listener-bearing beans a structural exclusion (force-eager, like SmartInitializing) or (b) emit a diagnostic when a bean with listener registrations is marked lazy — turning Spring's silent trap into an actionable warning (§2.9). This is an interaction with events that the exclusion policy must decide.
- The 'started-at-refresh auto-startup lifecycle bean ignores @Lazy' rule (docs 419, SPR-7014) is a structural exclusion that must be honored, not a bug. A lazy SmartLifecycle-analogue with auto-startup is STILL started at refresh because runtime-lifecycle's auto-start forces initialization regardless of the lazy flag — so `@Lazy` 'barely affects' it. leaf's exclusion policy must encode this (auto-startup runtime-lifecycle beans are force-eager), or a user marking such a bean lazy gets surprising no-effect behavior; this is an interaction with runtime-lifecycle the exclusion set must capture.

### Interactions
- **bean-instantiation** — PRIMARY UPSTREAM. bean-instantiation's eager pre-instantiation pass reads `!abstract && singleton && !lazy` as its build gate; THIS feature OWNS the `!lazy` term and its resolution policy. lazy-initialization tells the eager driver to SKIP a lazy singleton; first-access then drives bean-instantiation's normal create_singleton sequence (construct→populate→init→publish) under the same per-bean creation guard, so a lazy bean is built and published IDENTICALLY to an eager one, just later. The boundary: bean-instantiation owns the creation SEQUENCE and its guard; lazy-initialization owns WHEN (eager vs first-access) that sequence runs. In bean-instantiation's R2 (plan-then-execute), lazy beans are EXCLUDED from the plan and built via the recursive fallback driver on first access.
- **deferral-primitives** — TIGHTEST COUPLING / SHARED MACHINERY. Spring's `@Lazy` at an injection point IS a deferral primitive (a lazy-resolution proxy), and the docs prefer ObjectProvider over `@Lazy` for nuanced cases. leaf's `@Lazy`-edge wrapper (R2) and DeferredHandle outcome (R3) are the SAME deferred-handle family deferral-primitives provides — `Lazy<T>` is essentially 'ObjectProvider with cache-on-first-get'. The critical shared fact: laziness only HOLDS when the reference is deferred via a handle (or no eager dependents); a direct injection defeats it. This feature provides the node-flag/global-default/exclusions; deferral-primitives provides the handle types that make laziness actually hold. Phase 2/3 must decide whether `@Lazy`-edge is a distinct mechanism or fully subsumed by deferral-primitives' handles (a flagged phase2Question).
- **refresh-lifecycle** — UPSTREAM DRIVER. refresh-lifecycle invokes the eager pre-instantiation pass at a fixed point in bring-up; the lazy-resolution policy pass (resolve each unset definition's effective lazy flag, applying global flag + exclusions) runs at refresh SETUP just before the eager pass — the literal LazyInitializationBeanFactoryPostProcessor intent (a post-processor registered before refresh finalizes the eager set, docs 293). refresh-lifecycle owns the ordered bring-up; lazy-initialization owns the policy step that determines which beans the eager pass skips.
- **bean-definition** — UPSTREAM. The `lazy: Option<bool>` flag (None=unset/global-eligible, Some=explicit override for @Lazy(false)) is a field on the merged definition. bean-definition fixes the metadata record; lazy-initialization reads the flag and resolves its effective value against the global flag + exclusions. The config-class cascade is a metadata default stamped at the bean-definition emit site (macro-time), not a runtime walk.
- **bean-registry** — UPSTREAM. The lazy singleton's slot exists in the registry's cache but stays UNFILLED until first access publishes into `ready` (the registry's existing publication IS the singleton-cache, realizing docs 2589's 'caches on first access for singletons'). bean-registry owns the cache/slot; lazy-initialization governs when the slot gets filled. The candidate-set production is unaffected — a lazy bean is still a resolvable candidate.
- **smart-initializing** — STRUCTURAL EXCLUSION. SmartInitializingSingleton-analogue beans MUST be force-eager because their single after-all-eager-singletons callback would never fire if they were lazy — laziness would silently break the contract. lazy-initialization's exclusion policy hard-excludes them (one of the three structural exclusions, docs 2591). smart-initializing defines the marker; lazy-initialization honors it as a never-lazy predicate.
- **runtime-lifecycle** — STRUCTURAL EXCLUSION + a documented near-no-op. An auto-startup runtime-lifecycle bean is STILL started at refresh regardless of `@Lazy` (auto-start forces init; `@Lazy` 'barely affects SmartLifecycle beans', docs 419, SPR-7014). lazy-initialization's exclusion policy must force-eager auto-startup lifecycle beans (or accept that the lifecycle start will build them anyway), encoding this as an exclusion so the user isn't surprised by a no-effect @Lazy. runtime-lifecycle owns auto-start; lazy-initialization defers to it.
- **post-processor-spi** — STRUCTURAL EXCLUSION. Infrastructure/ROLE_INFRASTRUCTURE beans (the framework's own ordered post-processors and machinery) are force-eager — deferring them would break the dogfooded infrastructure that makes declarative features work (docs 2591). lazy-initialization's exclusion policy excludes by role; post-processor-spi defines the infrastructure role that marks these beans.
- **background-bootstrap** — COMPLEMENTARY / v4-v7 GLUE. `@Lazy` (or a deferral handle) on a DEPENDENT's injection point is the MANDATORY glue that lets a background-init bean actually run in parallel — a non-lazy direct dependent BLOCKS on the background bean, defeating the concurrency (docs 2006, 2593). So lazy-initialization's deferred-reference requirement is what unblocks background-bootstrap. A future Schedule::Background (R3) would bridge the two: defer-to-bootstrap-executor as a laziness mode. background-bootstrap owns the concurrent-init executor; lazy-initialization provides the deferred reference that enables the parallelism.
- **context-close** — DOWNSTREAM TEARDOWN COMPLICATION. A lazily-created singleton (built at first access, AFTER refresh) must still register reverse-dependency teardown data so context-close's shutdown().await drains it in reverse order — meaning the teardown ledger CANNOT be frozen at end-of-refresh; it grows as lazy beans materialize. context-close defines the stop-then-destroy-in-reverse sequence; lazy-initialization forces the ledger to be append-grown past refresh (a divergence from a refresh-frozen ledger, compounded by no async Drop §86).
- **events** — FOOTGUN INTERACTION. A lazy bean carrying @EventListener-analogue registrations silently LOSES them — it isn't built at refresh, so its listeners never register ('my listener never fires', docs 1901). lazy-initialization must either add event-listener-bearing beans to the structural exclusions (force-eager) or emit a diagnostic when such a bean is marked lazy. events owns listener registration; lazy-initialization must decide whether to exclude or warn (a flagged tension), turning Spring's silent trap into an actionable one (§2.9).
- **configuration-classes** — CASCADE SOURCE. `@Lazy` on a config-class CASCADES to the beans it declares (docs 2581). lazy-initialization realizes this as a macro-emit-time metadata default (the bean's lazy flag inherits the enclosing config's unless explicitly set). configuration-classes owns the config-class-declares-beans model; lazy-initialization stamps the cascaded default at emit time.
- **scopes** — SCOPE-DEPENDENT CACHING + no-op-on-prototype. The lazy-resolution wrapper/proxy caches on first access for SINGLETONS but re-resolves every access for shorter scopes (docs 2589); `@Lazy` on a prototype is a no-op (prototypes are never eager). scopes fixes per-scope multiplicity; lazy-initialization's caching behavior (OnceCell-cache for singleton targets, re-resolve for prototype/scoped) is keyed on the target's scope.
- **autowiring-resolution** — DEFEAT-MECHANISM SITE. The 'direct injection defeats lazy' trap occurs DURING autowiring-resolution's populate: if an eager bean resolves a lazy bean directly by type, resolution recursively builds it at refresh. lazy-initialization relies on autowiring-resolution to either resolve a deferral HANDLE (laziness holds) or a direct reference (laziness defeated). autowiring-resolution owns the resolution; lazy-initialization defines what 'deferred reference' means and can hook a diagnostic when a direct edge forces a lazy bean eager.
- **condition-report** — DIAGNOSTIC ANALOGUE. R3's per-bean effective-Schedule report (which policy entry decided eager-vs-lazy, and why) is the lazy-initialization analogue of condition-report's match/no-match accountability (§2.9). condition-report establishes the human-readable-verdict pattern; a `lazy report` reuses it to make 'why did this bean build at boot / defer?' accountable.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Is `@Lazy`-at-an-injection-point a DISTINCT mechanism or fully SUBSUMED by deferral-primitives' handle family? `Lazy<T>` is essentially 'ObjectProvider with cache-on-first-get'; if deferral-primitives already provides a cached single-valued handle, this feature's injection-point realization collapses into it, leaving lazy-initialization owning only the node-flag, the global default, and the exclusions. Shared with deferral-primitives — must be resolved before either commits to a handle type.
- The global-default-lazy flag's REALIZATION LOCUS and TRANSPARENCY: is it a refresh-time node-gate (skip the eager pass, build on first DIRECT access — re-imports the direct-injection-defeats-lazy trap), or a build-time edge-codegen that transparently wraps deferrable edges (laziness actually holds, but needs generated trait-newtype proxies and only works for trait-typed injection)? Rust has no transparent runtime proxy, so 'global lazy AND laziness holds AND drop-in transparency' may be unachievable simultaneously — entangled with the compile-vs-runtime keystone (§3) and the proxy-vs-wrapper decision. Cross-cuts deferral-primitives and bean-instantiation.
- Does laziness need a generated transparent PROXY at all, or is deferral-visible-in-the-type (`Lazy<T>` / a handle the consumer must `.get().await`) the leaf answer? A transparent proxy (generated trait-newtype) preserves Spring's 'inject T, use T' DX but forces boxed-future shims at the async dyn boundary (§160), only works for trait-typed injection, and is heavy codegen; a visible wrapper is honest and unified with deferral-primitives but breaks transparency. This is a DX(#1)-vs-fidelity call shared with deferral-primitives, scoped-proxies, and aop-proxying (which all face the 'how does leaf realize a proxy without CGLIB' question).
- What is the execution-context contract for FIRST-ACCESS creation, given it runs on the caller's (possibly hot-path) task? A blocking lazy factory must spawn_blocking; concurrent first-accessors serialize on the per-bean creation guard; a slow lazy init stalls every concurrent first-accessor on what may be a request path. Should leaf constrain WHERE first-access may happen (e.g. forbid lazy beans with blocking factories, or auto-spawn_blocking, or offer a Background schedule), and how is this surfaced to the user (§2.3)? Shared with bean-instantiation, task-execution, and the async-execution model.
- How does context-close's teardown ledger accommodate lazily-created singletons that materialize AFTER refresh? The ledger cannot be frozen at end-of-refresh (lazy beans join it on first access), and with no async Drop (§86) a lazily-built bean's reverse-order async teardown must be captured at its first-access publish time. Is the ledger append-grown with concurrent first-access publishes, and how is reverse-dependency order maintained when dependencies are themselves discovered lazily? Shared with context-close and bean-instantiation.
- How is the @EventListener-silently-lost-when-lazy footgun (and analogous register-at-refresh-only registrations) handled — force-eager exclusion, build-time diagnostic, or accepted-and-documented? A lazy bean never registers its listeners at refresh; Spring leaves this silent. leaf could exclude listener-bearing beans from laziness, warn at build/refresh, or accept it — a §2.9-diagnostics-vs-DX call shared with events, smart-initializing, and any feature whose registration happens at refresh (scheduling, aware-callbacks).
- Is the laziness decision a runtime POLICY (R3's ordered LazinessPolicy chain consulted per Provider) or a static metadata flag resolved once (R1)? The policy-chain maximizes unification/diagnosability and bridges future modes (Background, condition-driven), but widens the stable ABI and borders on pre-deciding a 'scheduling-policy' meta-architecture; the plain flag is lower-ceremony and DX-invisible. This is a unification(§2.11)-vs-DX(§2.1) call shared with bean-instantiation (whose R3 strategy-bundle R3-here depends on) and conditions (whose predicate model the policy chain resembles).
- Are the exclude-filters a RUNTIME SPI (a registered list evaluated at refresh) or a BUILD-TIME predicate (cfg/codegen)? Spring's LazyInitializationExcludeFilter is a runtime SPI for DSLs/registrars; leaf's compile-vs-runtime-undecided world (§3) could make exclusions a build-time codegen input instead. The locus interacts with the discovery substrate (a runtime filter is a cross-crate contribution like any other; a build-time predicate is codegen) — shared with the cross-crate composition model and conditions.
- How does the anti-DCE self-check (§152) distinguish a legitimately-lazy-but-registered bean from a silently-DCE'd one? Both look like 'registered descriptor, no instance'. The check must count DEFINITIONS/Providers (proving the crate linked) not instances — but this constrains how laziness is represented in the registry and how the self-check is written. Shared with the cross-crate composition/discovery family and bean-instantiation's eager-pass self-check.
- How do the multiple laziness-precedence inputs ORDER when some are compile-time and some runtime? Config-class cascade is compile-time (macro-stamped), @Lazy(false) is compile-time (explicit flag), the global flag is runtime (config-bound), exclude-filters may be either. Spring resolves all at refresh; leaf's split-locus model needs an explicit, documented precedence (per-bean-explicit > exclude > global > cascade-default?) that survives the compile-vs-runtime boundary. Shared with environment/config-data (the global flag's binding) and the compile-vs-runtime keystone.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the precise feature section — `@Lazy and spring.main.lazy-initialization` (2580-2593: the relocate-not-remove framing, the four traps incl. direct-injection-defeats-lazy and @Lazy(false)-override and singleton-cached-vs-re-resolved, the global-flag LazyInitializationBeanFactoryPostProcessor with its three structural exclusions + the LazyInitializationExcludeFilter SPI, the lazy-SmartLifecycle-still-started rule, and the v4/v7 escalation to required-remedy + background-bootstrap glue) — plus the surrounding intent that anchors this feature: the eager-vs-lazy refresh distinction and fail-fast philosophy (122, 548, 816, 2196), the deferral-primitives section that owns the handle types `@Lazy` overlaps (787-796, esp. the docs' own 'rather limited' verdict on the @Lazy proxy vs ObjectProvider), the @EventListener-silently-lost footgun (1901), the @Lazy-barely-affects-SmartLifecycle rule (419), the config-scan lazyInit default and the candidate-resolver chain where @Lazy proxies are installed (526, 548, 2603-2610), and the background-bootstrap non-lazy-dependents-block mechanics (2004-2010). I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc (linkme-default thin-data-macro §166-167, absolute-path ABI §89-102, generics-cannot-register §153, the mandatory anti-DCE self-check §139-152 — which directly clashes with lazy-but-registered beans, the donsz scope-not-implicitly-global warning, Architectures A/B §277-291) and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.1 DX-first, §2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimize-Arc, §2.9 rich-early-diagnostics, §2.10 thin-macros, §2.11 unification) and the stable envelope (RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe forcing boxed-future shims for any generated proxy §160, no async Drop as a first-class constraint §86, OnceCell/Arc<dyn Any> registry primitives §17/§81, trait upcasting 1.86 §80 for the wrapper's erased target).

CRITICAL SCOPING NOTE for Phase 2/3: this feature is a GATE + a POLICY, not a construction path — it deliberately does NOT own the creation sequence (bean-instantiation's), the singleton-handle/slot (bean-registry's), or the scope policy (scopes'). It owns: (a) the lazy FLAG and its resolution (per-bean @Lazy / @Lazy(false)-override / config-cascade / global-default), (b) the three structural EXCLUSIONS (infrastructure-role, SmartInitializing-marker, auto-startup-lifecycle) + the exclude-filter SPI, and (c) the relocate-failure-to-first-access contract. The three realizations diverge on WHERE laziness lives: R1 = a NODE flag on the definition that gates the eager pass, first-access drives the normal creation sequence (Spring-faithful, lowest surface, but inherits the direct-injection-defeats-lazy trap and can't make laziness transparently hold under a global flag without the node-gate); R2 = an EDGE property — `@Lazy` at an injection point emits a typed `Lazy<T>` wrapper so laziness HOLDS by construction and is visible-in-the-type (honest, unifies with deferral-primitives, but non-transparent DX and the global-default-wrap is awkward/impossible transparently, so it must fall back to R1's node-gate for the flag); R3 = a unified SCHEDULING POLICY — `@Lazy`/global-flag/exclusions/@Lazy(false) all collapse into one ordered LazinessPolicy yielding a per-Provider Schedule the eager pass consults (maximal unification + best diagnosability + bridges background-bootstrap and AOT, but most indirection, depends on bean-instantiation's R3 Provider model, and borders on pre-deciding a meta-architecture). R1↔R2 differ on node-flag-vs-edge-property; R3 generalizes both as policy outcomes (its Schedule::Deferred is R1's node-gate, its DeferredHandle is R2's edge-wrapper). All three are Phase-2-composable: R3's policy can host R1's flag and R2's wrapper as outcomes.

Three things to carry forward: (1) The single sharpest rust clash is the LAZY-RESOLUTION PROXY — Spring's transparent 'inject T, get a proxy that builds-on-first-call' has NO rust-native equivalent (no CGLIB/JDK proxy), so leaf must choose deferral-VISIBLE-in-the-type (a `Lazy<T>`/handle the consumer `.get().await`s) over invisible transparency, OR generate trait-newtype proxies (only for trait-typed injection, heavy, boxed-future shims at the async dyn boundary). This forecloses Spring's drop-in DX and is shared with deferral-primitives/scoped-proxies/aop-proxying (the workspace-wide 'how does leaf proxy without CGLIB' question). (2) FIRST-ACCESS IS `.await` ON THE CALLER'S TASK — laziness relocates cold-boot cost onto a possibly-hot request path with an execution-context hazard Spring never had (a blocking lazy factory stalls a request or must spawn_blocking; concurrent first-accessors serialize on bean-instantiation's per-bean guard, importing cold-path concurrency onto the hot path). This must be EXPLICIT (§2.3), not silently inherited. (3) LAZY-BUT-REGISTERED CLASHES WITH THE ANTI-DCE SELF-CHECK — a legitimately-lazy uninstantiated bean is indistinguishable from a silently-DCE'd one to a naive instance-count check; the self-check must count DEFINITIONS/Providers (proving the crate linked) not instances, coupling laziness to the discovery design (§152). Plus the inherited footgun: a lazy bean silently loses @EventListener registration — leaf should exclude-or-warn rather than inherit Spring's silence.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization keeps the lazy decision abstract over whatever creation sequence/handle/registry Phase 2 picks, keeps the proxy-vs-wrapper question open, keeps the global-flag's compile-vs-runtime locus open, keeps the exclude-filters as runtime-SPI-OR-build-time-predicate, and keeps the deferred handle delegated to deferral-primitives. The two-source duality (static link-time filter registry + optional dynamic directory-scanned source for a WASM-module layer's exclude-filters) is captured as a CANDIDATE, not a decision. Naming (`resolve_lazy`, `Lazy<T>`, `LazinessPolicy`, `Schedule::{Eager,Deferred,DeferredHandle,Background}`, `LazinessExcludeFilter`, `register_lazy_slot`, `lazy report`) is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Relevant grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (2580-2593 the @Lazy/global-flag section incl. exclusions+SPI+v4/v7 escalation; 787-796 deferral-primitives + the 'rather limited' proxy verdict; 122/548/816/2196 eager-vs-lazy fail-fast intent; 1901 @EventListener-lost; 419 @Lazy-barely-affects-SmartLifecycle; 293 LazyInitializationBeanFactoryPostProcessor registration timing; 2004-2010 background-bootstrap non-lazy-dependents-block; 2589 singleton-cached-vs-re-resolved; 2603-2610 candidate-resolver chain where @Lazy proxies install); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2 esp. 2.1/2.3/2.4/2.5/2.9/2.10/2.11, deferred keystones §3, topology/DX §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (anti-DCE self-check + Layer-0/A/B §139-152, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI §89-102, generics-cannot-register §153, linkme-default §166-167, Architectures A/B §277-291); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe → boxed-future proxy shims §160-176, no async Drop first-class constraint §86, trait upcasting 1.86 §80, const TypeId 1.91 + Arc<dyn Any> registry §17/§81, OnceCell for the wrapper cache).

## Circular Reference Handling  `[circular-references]`

Spring's load-bearing intent (lines 773-785, 2190-2192, 2518-2539): the container TOLERATES circular dependencies among setter/field-injected singletons but FAILS FAST on constructor cycles — because the distinction is a logical truth, not a policy choice. A constructor cycle is *logically unsatisfiable* (neither object can be the first to exist: each needs the other to be live before `this` is a live reference), so failing at startup is correct and the ONLY honest outcome. A setter/field cycle *is* satisfiable because there exists a moment where a half-built (instantiated-but-not-yet-populated) instance exists and can be handed to a collaborator. The MECHANISM Spring uses to exploit that moment is the early-singleton reference via the three-level cache: after instantiation but before population, IF `earlySingletonExposure` holds (`isSingleton && allowCircularReferences && isSingletonCurrentlyInCreation(name)`), the container registers an `ObjectFactory` — crucially NOT the raw instance — into the middle `singletonFactories` cache level, so that when a mid-cycle collaborator resolves this bean, `getEarlyBeanReference` runs (computing an AOP early-proxy lazily-and-once, recorded to avoid double-wrap), promoting the result into `earlySingletonObjects`. The factory-not-instance choice is the high-design-intent decision: it defers computing the early reference so that (a) if no cycle materializes the factory is NEVER called and no premature proxy is built, and (b) the early reference and the final singleton become the SAME object whenever possible. The raw-vs-wrapped consistency check (`allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping`, lines 781, 2537) is the integrity guarantee: if a bean is AOP-wrapped AFTER a collaborator already injected its raw early reference, that collaborator would silently hold an un-advised object (bypassing @Transactional/security) — Spring detects this mismatch (the final exposedObject differs from the raw bean, the bean has real dependents) and throws rather than papering over it. There are TWO distinct origins of the same `BeanCurrentlyInCreationException` (lines 2535-2537): (1) a genuinely-unresolvable cycle (the fatal constructor case — re-entrant creation finds the name already in-creation with no early reference available), and (2) the resolved-but-inconsistent raw-vs-wrapped case. Spring Boot's modern posture (lines 2192, 2539): `spring.main.allow-circular-references=false` BY DEFAULT since 2.6 — even setter/field cycles fail fast unless explicitly opted in; the early-singleton machinery is a *pragmatic escape hatch, not an endorsement* — a cycle still signals tangled design, and the recommendation is to remove it (or break it with @Lazy/ObjectProvider at an injection point). AOT-optimized contexts (lines 785, 2539) have NO early-exposure machinery (beans come from generated suppliers) so ALL injected cycles fail at startup under AOT — the prescribed fix is @Lazy/ObjectProvider deferral. leaf must replicate this INTENT — the constructor-vs-setter logical distinction, fail-fast on the unsatisfiable case with the richest cycle diagnostic, the satisfiable case broken by exposing a not-yet-fully-initialized reference into the cycle, the early-reference-becomes-the-final-object-when-possible identity, the consistency guarantee that no collaborator ends up holding a stale/un-advised reference, the off-by-default-pragmatic-escape-hatch posture, and the deferral-handle as the *recommended* break — WITHOUT Spring's mechanisms it sheds: the GC'd reference aliasing (a Rust early reference is an owned/Arc'd handle with explicit ownership, not a freely-replaceable pointer); the global-mutex/lenient-lock lineage entangled with cycle detection; the assumption that the early reference is a runtime ObjectFactory closure that can be lazily evaluated under a lock (in async Rust a lazily-computed early reference must not block the executor); and the assumption of native-only construction (the early reference and the cycle policy must be origin-agnostic so a WASM-module-sourced or test-double bean participates identically). It must NOT pick whether the early reference rides a two- or three-level cache (that is the proxy/registry keystone, shared with bean-registry and aop-proxying), the singleton-handle/ownership type the early reference is shaped as (the scope/ownership keystone), whether cycle detection is a runtime re-entrancy sentinel or a build/plan-time graph analysis, or whether the early-proxy sub-tier exists at all (depends on the unsettled proxy story) — those are Phase 2.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Runtime re-entrancy sentinel + early-reference cache slot (Spring-faithful, rust-retyped): cycle handling is a property of the creation re-entry, detected and broken AS it happens
Mirror Spring's runtime mechanism directly, retyped onto leaf's async creation driver (bean-instantiation R1) and the registry's cache slots (bean-registry). This feature OWNS the cycle POLICY layered onto that driver — it does not own the driver or the cache, it owns 'what happens when creation re-enters a name already in-creation' and 'what reference is exposed to break a satisfiable cycle'. (1) DETECTION is the in_creation sentinel set the driver already maintains: when populate→resolve→get re-enters create for a name N already in in_creation, that IS a cycle. The policy decides the verdict: if an EARLY REFERENCE is available for N (N was already instantiated, so a half-built handle exists), break the cycle by handing back that early reference (satisfiable setter/field case); if NO early reference is available (N is still mid-instantiation — the constructor case, because the early-exposure slot is only registered AFTER instantiate returns), it is logically unsatisfiable → fatal CycleError. (2) The EARLY-EXPOSURE HOOK this feature places at the structurally-correct moment in the per-bean sequence (after instantiate, before populate): if `early_exposure = singleton && allow_circular && in_creation(N)`, register an `EarlyRef` entry — a deferred computation, NOT the eagerly-finished handle — into the registry's early-reference cache slot, so a mid-cycle resolver of N gets that early reference (and, if a proxy mechanism exists, the early reference is the proxy, computed lazily-and-once and recorded so the after-init step won't double-wrap). The deferred-not-eager choice is preserved: if no cycle materializes, the EarlyRef is never forced, no premature proxy is built, and the early reference and final singleton are the SAME handle whenever possible. (3) The CONSISTENCY CHECK at publish: when create finishes N and is about to publish the final handle, if N was early-exposed AND the final handle differs from the early reference handed out (it got wrapped) AND N has real dependents that took the raw early reference, fail with a rich 'collaborator X holds a stale/un-advised reference to N' error (the allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping analogue) rather than silently leaving a dependent holding the wrong object. (4) POLICY GATE: `allow_circular` defaults OFF (Spring Boot posture) — by default even a satisfiable setter/field cycle is reported at boot as a CycleError with the full path and the deferral-handle fix hint, opt-in to tolerate. The constructor case is ALWAYS fatal regardless of the flag. The DEFERRAL escape hatch (deferral-primitives) is the recommended break: an injection point typed as a deferral handle does NOT trigger recursive creation during populate, so it removes the edge from the cycle entirely.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: the constructor-vs-setter verdict falls out of exactly WHEN the early-exposure slot is registered (after instantiate) — a constructor cycle re-enters before any half-built handle exists and is structurally fatal; a setter/field cycle re-enters after, so an early reference exists to break it. This is Spring's own logical distinction realized by the same temporal fact, maximally teachable.; Dovetails precisely with bean-instantiation R1's recursive creation driver and bean-registry's cache slots — this feature is a thin policy layer (detection verdict + hook placement + consistency check) over machinery those features already own, with a sharp boundary and no duplicated logic.; The deferred-not-eager EarlyRef preserves Spring's two best properties: no premature proxy when no cycle materializes, and early-reference-equals-final-handle identity whenever possible — and it keeps the early reference ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC (it wraps whatever erased handle the driver produced, native/WASM/test-double identically).; Naturally handles dynamically-discovered cycles: a cycle that only materializes through by-type/qualifier resolution or a conditionally-registered bean is detected at the moment of re-entry, with no requirement to know the edge ahead of time — strictly more general than any static analysis.; The off-by-default policy gate + deferral-handle-as-recommended-break reproduces Spring Boot's modern posture exactly: cycles are a smell reported loudly at boot, tolerance is explicit opt-in, and the honest fix (deferral at an injection point) is surfaced in the diagnostic.; Cancellation/partial-construction is contained: if a cycle is detected fatal, no further work happens; if an early-exposed bean's build is cancelled mid-populate, the EarlyRef slot and in_creation entry are unwound by the driver's cleanup path (this feature just contributes the slot to that ledger).

**Cons:** The early-reference handle is the deepest impedance: Spring's early reference is a GC'd pointer freely aliased and later transparently replaced by the wrapped object; a Rust early reference is an owned/Arc'd handle that, once handed to a collaborator, CANNOT be retroactively swapped for the wrapped version — so the raw-vs-wrapped 'becomes the same object' only holds if the early reference IS already the (proxy or final) handle, forcing either eager proxy decision at exposure time or accepting that a wrapped bean in a cycle is a hard error (no silent reconciliation). This is the central unresolved tension.; Depends on a mutable mid-build cache slot (register EarlyRef during creation) — couples to whether the registry is mutable-at-refresh (bean-registry R1/R3) vs frozen (R2); if frozen, the early-reference slot needs a separate mutable side-table, a divergence to reconcile.; The consistency check only has meaning once a proxy mechanism exists; until aop-proxying is settled, the check is partly scaffolding (a no-op for un-proxied beans), and WHAT the early reference should be (raw vs early-proxy) is undecidable now — so the hook placement is correct but its payload is speculative.; Cycle detection by re-entrancy is INHERENTLY a runtime, per-build event — the richest cycle diagnostic (full path) is only assembled as the stack unwinds at the moment of detection, which is later/less complete than a graph analysis could give before any construction (a §2.9 timing weakness vs R2).; An async recursive creation that re-enters across .await holding per-bean guards is exactly where a naive same-name re-entrant wait could deadlock instead of being detected as a cycle — the detection MUST fire before any guard-wait on the same name, threading correctness through the (bean-instantiation-owned) guard logic this feature depends on.

**Mechanisms considered:** in_creation re-entrancy sentinel (owned by bean-instantiation) as the cycle DETECTOR; this feature supplies the verdict function (early-ref-available → break, else → fatal); early-exposure hook placed after instantiate / before populate: register a deferred EarlyRef (not the eager handle) into the registry's early-reference cache slot when singleton && allow_circular && in_creation; deferred-computation EarlyRef so no premature proxy is built if no cycle materializes; early-reference-equals-final-handle identity when possible; publish-time raw-vs-wrapped consistency check (allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping analogue) → rich error if a dependent holds a stale/un-advised early reference; off-by-default allow_circular policy gate (Spring Boot posture); constructor cycle always fatal regardless; deferral-primitive injection point removes the edge during populate (recommended break) — origin-agnostic early-ref handle wrapping whatever the driver produced

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Substrate-neutral on collection: cycle detection consumes the in_creation sentinel and the registry's early-ref slot regardless of how descriptors were collected (linkme/codegen/explicit). What this feature FIXES cross-crate: the cycle POLICY (verdict + hook + consistency check) lives in leaf-core alongside the creation driver and registry; contributing crates supply only descriptors with declared injection edges (which the macro emits via absolute paths), never cycle logic. Cross-crate cycles are the realistic case (bean in crate X depends on a bean in crate Y that depends back) and R1 handles them transparently because detection is at runtime re-entry — no crate needs to know about the other's beans at compile time. The anti-DCE self-check (cross-crate §152/§345) is orthogonal but synergistic: a silently-DCE'd crate whose beans never registered would surface as an UNSATISFIED dependency (a missing-candidate error from autowiring-resolution), not a cycle — so the cycle diagnostic must be distinguishable from the missing-bean diagnostic to avoid misleading users. Generics cannot register (cross-crate §153/§349) so the descriptor set the detector walks holds only concrete beans; a generic bean's per-instantiation shim participates in cycle detection as a concrete node. The two-source duality slots in cleanly: a WASM-module-sourced bean re-entered during a cycle is detected and (if satisfiable) early-exposed through the IDENTICAL slot — the dynamic 'classpath' bean participates in cycle handling with no new path (CANDIDATE, not a decision). The allow_circular flag is a context-level config (one value), not a cross-crate ABI surface.

**T2 — execution context:** Cycle detection and early-exposure run on the bootstrap task during the eager pass (cold path) for eager singletons, and on the calling task for lazy/on-demand beans — the SAME task the (re-entrant) creation runs on, so the in_creation sentinel and the cycle verdict are observed within one logical creation context, not across threads. The early-reference computation, if a proxy is involved, must NOT .await blocking work (it runs mid-cycle-resolution, re-entrant into the creation machinery) — building the early stand-in must be synchronous/cheap (charter §2.3: no blocking the executor under a re-entrant resolution). Background-init beans (background-bootstrap) building leniently on a separate task complicate detection: a cross-task same-name re-entry is the benign lenient-locking race (one task builds, the other reconciles via the ready cache), NOT a cycle — so the cycle detector must distinguish 'this task is re-entering its own in-flight creation' (a real cycle) from 'another task is building this name' (benign) using per-creation-context state, not a global in_creation set (charter §2.5: no global lock; the sentinel must be reasoned about per-creation-context). CANCELLATION: if an early-exposed bean's build is cancelled or fails mid-populate, the EarlyRef slot and the in_creation entry must be unwound deterministically — and because there is no async Drop, a collaborator that already took the early reference of a now-failed bean holds a handle to a partially-constructed object; the all-or-nothing eager pass must abort the whole graph so no such dangling early reference survives a boot failure (this feature contributes the early-ref slot to the driver's unwind ledger). ERRORS propagate as a rich CycleError carrying the full cycle path (the chain of beans/injection-points forming the loop), the verdict (constructor=fatal vs setter=tolerable-but-off-by-default), and the deferral-handle fix hint (§2.9). The consistency-check error names the stale-holding dependent. Constructor-cycle fatality aborts refresh all-or-nothing.

#### R2 — Build/plan-time cycle classification: the dependency graph is analyzed before construction; constructor cycles are a compile/plan error, setter cycles are a planned break-point
Question whether cycle handling must be a runtime re-entrancy event at all. leaf can know much of the dependency graph (declared injection edges per bean descriptor) BEFORE constructing anything — so cycle CLASSIFICATION becomes a graph-analysis step (build-time codegen, or a refresh-time plan pass over the frozen descriptor set), aligning with the AOT lineage where Spring itself moves cycle-failure to startup/AOT (lines 785, 2539). ANALYZE phase: build the dependency DAG from declared edges, where each edge is tagged by injection STYLE (constructor=mandatory/eager, setter/field=breakable, deferral-handle=non-edge). Run cycle detection (Tarjan/DFS) over the graph. VERDICTS as a classification result, the EARLIEST and RICHEST possible diagnostic (§2.9 — before any instance exists): (a) a cycle composed ENTIRELY of mandatory (constructor) edges is logically unsatisfiable → a hard error with the full cycle path, emitted at build time (if codegen) or at plan time (if runtime-assembled), strictly better than R1's mid-creation detection because it names the whole loop before construction; (b) a cycle containing at least one breakable (setter/field) edge is satisfiable → mark that edge as a PLANNED BREAK-POINT: the bean on the consuming side of the break is constructed with an early-reference dependency, and the planner emits an instantiate-then-populate node pair (the early reference is a PLAN artifact between the two halves, not a runtime-discovered race); (c) by leaf's stricter default (Spring Boot posture), even a breakable cycle is a plan error by default, pointing at the deferral-handle/@Lazy-equivalent fix — opt-in to auto-break. A deferral-handle edge is removed from the graph entirely (the recommended break), so a cycle broken by deferral simply isn't a cycle in the analysis. The early-reference is materialized only at the planned break-point (between the instantiate and populate halves of the consuming bean), so the three-level/middle cache becomes an explicit planned node rather than a runtime sentinel. Origin-agnostic: a WASM-module bean's declared edges participate in the graph identically.

**Pros:** EARLIEST, RICHEST diagnostic (§2.9): the full cycle path is named BEFORE any instance is constructed — and if codegen-time, potentially as a compile/build error with the whole loop spelled out, which is strictly more complete than R1's stack-unwind-at-detection. Constructor cycles become logically-impossible-to-ship rather than fail-at-boot.; Strongest AOT-lineage fit (charter §3 compile-vs-runtime keystone): cycle classification is exactly the kind of deterministic graph analysis Spring's AOT pass does, and it mirrors Spring's own v4/v7 move (AOT contexts fail on injected cycles) — a build-time pass could emit the cycle verdict as a const artifact, the runtime-assembly path computes the identical verdict at refresh.; Cycle handling becomes data-shape, not a runtime race: the early reference is a planned instantiate-then-populate node pair, so there is no runtime re-entrancy sentinel to reason about for the planned set, no same-name re-entrant deadlock surface, and no need for a mutable mid-build cache slot — cleaner against charter §2.5.; Cleanly separates the two BeanCurrentlyInCreation origins Spring conflates (lines 2535-2537): the unsatisfiable-cycle case is a CLASSIFICATION verdict (constructor loop), entirely distinct in code from any runtime build inconsistency — the user gets a categorically clearer error.; The deferral-handle-removes-the-edge semantics is trivially expressed (drop the edge before analysis), making 'break the cycle with a deferral handle' a first-class, statically-verifiable fix — the analysis can even SUGGEST which edge to convert (the one whose removal breaks the most cycles).; Pairs naturally with bean-instantiation R2's plan-then-execute wave model — the cycle classification IS part of computing the topological plan (a cycle is what a topological sort reports as unorderable), so the two features share the same graph pass.

**Cons:** Requires injection EDGES to be statically knowable, but many edges are dynamic in Spring's model (by-type/qualifier resolution depending on what's registered, ObjectProvider lookups, FactoryBean products whose type is only known post-creation, conditionally-registered beans) — a static graph must conservatively OVER-approximate edges, which can report a FALSE cycle (an edge that wouldn't actually form at runtime) — a worse failure than missing a real one, because it blocks a valid app. This is the central tension.; A conservative over-approximation that reports cycles users don't actually have is a DX disaster (§2.1) — and tightening the approximation (resolving which candidate a by-type edge actually picks) essentially requires running candidate-resolution at analysis time, partially reintroducing the runtime resolution it sought to avoid.; Dynamically-materialized cycles (a cycle that only forms through a runtime by-type pick or a conditional bean) are INVISIBLE to a static analysis that under-approximates — so R2 needs a runtime re-entrancy fallback (R1) anyway for the edges it couldn't classify, meaning two cycle paths that must agree on verdicts and diagnostics (a coherence burden, and the fallback is R1 regardless).; Mid-bootstrap definition registration (an init-method/registrar adding a bean that introduces a NEW edge) has no node in an already-computed graph — R2 must forbid it (a divergence from Spring's dynamism, entangled with the bean-registry mutable-vs-frozen and programmatic-registration decisions) or re-run the analysis, undermining the 'analyze once' value.; The planned break-point's early reference STILL faces the Rust ownership impedance: even as a plan artifact, the early-instantiate-half handle handed to the consumer cannot be retroactively swapped for a later-wrapped version — so if the broken bean gets proxied, the same raw-vs-wrapped hard-error tension as R1 reappears at the planned break-point.; Build-time-codegen cycle analysis couples to whichever cross-crate substrate Phase 2 picks: the graph must see ALL participating crates' edges at once, which only the binary/aggregator (Architecture B/C) can do — a cycle spanning crates X↔Y is invisible to either crate's own build.rs (cross-crate §357: build.rs can't scan siblings).

**Mechanisms considered:** graph-analysis cycle classification (Tarjan/DFS) over declared injection edges, tagged by style (constructor=mandatory, setter/field=breakable, deferral=non-edge); verdict as a classification result: all-mandatory loop → hard build/plan error with full path; loop-with-a-breakable-edge → planned break-point (or plan error by strict default); planned instantiate-then-populate node pair as the early-reference materialization (the middle cache level becomes a plan artifact, not a runtime sentinel); deferral-handle edge removed before analysis (recommended break, statically verifiable); analysis can suggest which edge to convert; conservative edge over-approximation for dynamic (by-type/qualifier/ObjectProvider/conditional) edges + a runtime re-entrancy fallback (R1) for unclassifiable edges; shared graph pass with bean-instantiation R2's topological wave plan (a cycle is an unorderable component)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for the AOT/build-time lineage but the HARDEST cross-crate case: a cycle spanning crates (X's bean ↔ Y's bean) can only be detected where BOTH crates' edges are visible — i.e. at the binary/aggregator (Architecture B/C), never in either crate's own build.rs (cross-crate §357: info flows downward only, build.rs can't see siblings). So build-time cycle analysis MUST be anchored at the app root over the linked descriptor set, or done at refresh time over the assembled graph. The cycle verdict is the IDEAL place to fuse with the anti-DCE self-check AND the missing-candidate check (cross-crate §152): a silently-DCE'd crate's missing beans show up as unsatisfiable edges (missing candidate), distinct from a cycle (a satisfiable-but-circular loop) — the analysis can name precisely which it is, strictly better diagnostics than R1. The dynamic-edge over-approximation problem is the real T1 cost: by-type/qualifier/conditional edges couple the cycle analysis to candidate-resolver's output, and a WASM-module-sourced bean contributing a late edge either must register before analysis (tension with build-time codegen) or force a re-analysis (feeding the runtime fallback). Both the static link-time registry and the optional dynamic directory-scanned source are CANDIDATES feeding the analysis's input edge set (not a decision). Generics: only concrete nodes in the graph (cross-crate §153); a generic bean's instantiation shim is a concrete node. The codegen path emits the verdict as a const artifact only on platforms where the whole graph is build-visible (Architecture C); elsewhere it degrades to the runtime plan/fallback.

**T2 — execution context:** ANALYZE phase is fully synchronous cold-path: build-time (codegen) or refresh-time (after descriptor freeze, single-threaded, no async, no IO) — it reads declared edges and runs DFS/Tarjan, producing a verdict + break-point list. NO async, NO executor concern in the analysis itself. The EXECUTE phase (constructing the planned graph) runs on the bootstrap task; the early-reference at a planned break-point is materialized between the instantiate and populate halves of the consuming bean — synchronous handoff, no .await across the break (the early stand-in build must not block the executor, §2.3). Because the satisfiable cycles are pre-classified as planned break-points, there is NO runtime re-entrancy sentinel and NO same-name re-entrant deadlock surface for the planned set (charter §2.5 — strongest posture). The runtime FALLBACK (for dynamically-materialized cycles the analysis couldn't classify) runs on the calling task with R1's re-entrancy detection — its execution context is R1's. CANCELLATION: cancelling refresh during execution drops in-flight construction; an early reference handed across a planned break-point to a now-cancelled consumer must be unwound on the structured-scope unwind path (no async Drop), and a constructor-cycle verdict aborts before ANY construction so there is nothing to clean. ERRORS: analysis-phase cycle errors carry the FULL loop path at the earliest point (before construction), with the constructor-vs-breakable verdict and the deferral fix hint (§2.9) — the richest possible diagnostic; false-positive over-approximated cycles are the failure mode to guard against (a valid app blocked), so the analysis must clearly mark which edges are conservatively-assumed vs declared so a user can see WHY a cycle was reported.

#### R3 — Deferral-first / explicit-break: leaf does NOT auto-tolerate cycles at all — every cycle is broken by an explicit deferral handle at an injection point, and the early reference is a deferral primitive
Push the charter's intent-not-mechanism mandate (§2.2) and Spring Boot's own modern posture hardest: Spring already DEFAULTS allow-circular-references=false and AOT contexts fail on ALL injected cycles, prescribing @Lazy/ObjectProvider as the fix (lines 785, 2192, 2539) — so the early-singleton machinery is increasingly a legacy escape hatch, not the recommended path. leaf can take that to its logical conclusion: do NOT build the implicit early-singleton-exposure machinery at all; instead, REQUIRE that any cycle be broken by an EXPLICIT deferral handle at an injection point (deferral-primitives), and make the cycle DETECTOR exist purely to FAIL FAST with a diagnostic that NAMES which edge to convert. The model: (1) DETECTION (runtime re-entrancy as R1, or graph analysis as R2 — this realization is agnostic to which) exists ONLY to report cycles, never to silently break them. (2) ALL cycles — constructor AND setter/field — are reported as a CycleError by default, because in Rust the setter/field 'satisfiable' distinction is less compelling: there is no GC'd freely-aliasable early reference, exposing a partially-constructed handle is an ownership/soundness hazard, and a deferral handle is a CLEANER, type-honest break than a hidden half-built reference. (3) The BREAK is always explicit and visible in the type: an injection point typed as a deferral handle (ObjectProvider/lazy-resolution-handle equivalent) does NOT participate as a construction-time edge — it resolves its target on first call, AFTER both beans are fully built — so the cycle is structurally removed and there is no early reference to manage at all. The deferral handle IS the rust-native early reference: instead of Spring's implicitly-exposed raw half-built instance, leaf hands the consumer a typed handle that lazily resolves the FULLY-built target. (4) For the cases where Spring's early-exposure genuinely earns its keep (a cycle that truly needs a mid-build reference, e.g. AOP early-proxy into a cycle), provide an EXPLICIT opt-in early-reference primitive — a deferral handle variant that resolves to the early (possibly-proxy) reference — rather than implicit auto-exposure. This makes the early-singleton machinery an explicit, named, opt-in handle, not invisible default behavior. Origin-agnostic: a deferral handle resolves its target through the same get path regardless of the target's origin.

**Pros:** Most rust-native and most honest (§2.2): the deferral handle makes the cycle-break VISIBLE in the type signature (the consumer's field is ObjectProvider<T>, not T), exactly as Spring's own docs recommend (lines 794-796: 'encoding laziness in the type keeps the eager model intact while making deferral visible and honest rather than hidden') — leaf elevates the recommended fix to the ONLY path, eliminating the hidden half-built-reference footgun entirely.; Sidesteps the deepest impedance (the raw-vs-wrapped ownership tension) COMPLETELY: there is no early reference to retroactively swap for a wrapped version, because the deferral handle resolves the FULLY-built (already-wrapped-if-proxied) target on first call — so the allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping consistency check has no reason to exist, and a collaborator can NEVER hold a stale/un-advised reference (it always resolves the final object).; Aligns perfectly with the AOT future (lines 785/2539: AOT contexts fail on injected cycles, prescribe @Lazy/ObjectProvider) — leaf's default behavior IS the AOT behavior, so there is no runtime-vs-AOT behavioral divergence (a real Spring wart: setter cycles 'work' on the JVM but fail under AOT — leaf has ONE behavior everywhere).; Minimal machinery: no three-level cache middle tier needed for cycle-breaking (the deferral handle is the break), no mutable mid-build early-ref slot, no early-exposure hook in the per-bean sequence, no consistency check — dramatically less surface in the hottest, most-coupled part of the engine, and the proxy/early-reference keystone (whether a third cache level exists) is largely MOOT for cycles.; Strongest diagnostic posture (§2.9): since the ONLY resolution is an explicit deferral handle, the CycleError can name the exact edge and say 'convert injection point X from `T` to `ObjectProvider<T>` to break this cycle' — an actionable, copy-pasteable fix, far better than 'a cycle exists, maybe set allow-circular-references=true'.; Unifies cycle-handling with deferral-primitives (§2.11): the cycle-break is not a bespoke mechanism but a reuse of the deferral handle leaf already needs for optional/lazy/multiple access — one primitive serves cycle-breaking, laziness, and optionality.

**Cons:** A genuine DIVERGENCE from Spring's runtime behavior: Spring TOLERATES setter/field cycles by default at runtime (allow-circular-references is a Boot default, the framework core defaults true) — code that wires a setter cycle and 'just works' on Spring would require an explicit deferral handle in leaf. This is a real parity gap (the brief's intent says 'tolerates setter/field singleton cycles'), defensible by Spring Boot's own off-by-default posture but still a behavioral difference users porting code will hit.; Loses the early-AOP-proxy-into-a-cycle capability as a DEFAULT (lines 777, 1766, 2531): the whole point of exposing a FACTORY (not the instance) early is so a proxy can be computed lazily-and-once and injected into the cycle so collaborators get the advised object — if cycles are always broken by a deferral handle resolving the FINAL object, this works for the consumer (it gets the proxy on first call) but does NOT support the case where the bean genuinely needs a mid-construction reference to its cyclic collaborator (rare, but Spring supports it); the explicit opt-in early-reference primitive (4) re-adds this, partially walking back the simplification.; Pushes a burden onto the USER: every cycle, even a benign two-bean setter cycle, requires a code change (convert to a deferral handle) — more ceremony than Spring's silent tolerance (a §2.1 DX cost, mitigated only if the diagnostic makes the fix trivial), and may feel heavy-handed for users who expect Spring's permissiveness.; The deferral handle's first-call resolution is a HOT-path concern: a deferral handle resolved on every call (for non-singleton targets) or cached on first call (for singletons) must be cheap and waker-driven (§2.4) — and resolving across .await re-enters the get path, which must not deadlock if the target is somehow still in-creation (an edge case the deferral model mostly avoids by resolving post-build, but must be reasoned about).; Still NEEDS a cycle DETECTOR (R1's re-entrancy or R2's graph analysis) to produce the fail-fast diagnostic — so R3 does not eliminate detection, it only eliminates auto-tolerance; the detector's design (and its T1/T2) is inherited from R1 or R2, making R3 a POLICY choice layered on one of them rather than a fully independent mechanism.; If a user has a deep/complex cycle, mechanically converting the right edge to a deferral handle may be non-obvious despite the diagnostic — and converting the WRONG edge (e.g. a constructor edge that can't be deferral-injected because constructor args are mandatory/immutable) may not break the cycle, requiring the user to understand the constructor-vs-setter distinction anyway.

**Mechanisms considered:** cycle detector (re-entrancy R1 OR graph analysis R2) used ONLY to fail-fast with a diagnostic, never to silently break; ALL cycles reported by default (no implicit setter/field tolerance) — the rust-native posture that a half-built early reference is an ownership/soundness hazard not worth the implicit machinery; deferral handle (ObjectProvider/lazy-resolution-handle, from deferral-primitives) as THE cycle break: a deferral-typed edge is removed from construction-time wiring, resolved post-build on first call; deferral handle resolves the FULLY-built (already-wrapped) target → no raw-vs-wrapped consistency check needed, no early reference to swap; explicit opt-in early-reference primitive (a deferral-handle variant resolving the early/proxy reference) for the rare genuine mid-build-reference case — named, not implicit; diagnostic names the exact edge to convert ('change X: T to X: ObjectProvider<T>'); unifies cycle-break with deferral/lazy/optional (§2.11)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Cleanest cross-crate story of the three: a cycle spanning crates X↔Y is broken by a deferral handle at ONE injection point in either crate (a local, compile-visible change in the crate that owns the consuming injection point) — no cross-crate coordination, no need for the binary/aggregator to see the whole graph to BREAK the cycle (only the detector needs whole-graph visibility, which it gets at the app root per cross-crate §269/§357). The deferral handle is origin-agnostic — it resolves its target through the standard get path, so a deferral handle pointing at a WASM-module-sourced or test-double target works identically (the dynamic 'classpath' bean is resolved post-build like any other; CANDIDATE, not a decision). Detection's T1 is inherited from R1 (runtime, substrate-neutral) or R2 (graph analysis, anchored at the app root). The anti-DCE self-check (cross-crate §152) composes well: a DCE'd crate's beans show as missing candidates (resolved by the deferral handle's get failing with a missing-bean error), distinct from a cycle — and since cycles are always fail-fast, there is no silent half-built-reference path that could mask a DCE problem. Generics: a deferral handle for a generic target still needs the concrete instantiation registered (cross-crate §153), same hard-error discipline; the handle itself over a concrete target is fine. No new cross-crate ABI surface beyond the deferral handle type (shared with deferral-primitives) and the cycle detector.

**T2 — execution context:** Detection runs cold-path (R1 re-entrancy on the creation task, or R2 analysis synchronously before/at refresh) — inherited from whichever detector. The KEY T2 difference is the BREAK: a deferral-handle edge does NOT trigger recursive construction during populate, so there is no mid-cycle re-entry, no early-reference computation under a re-entrant resolution, and no same-name re-entrant deadlock surface for cycle-broken edges (charter §2.5/§2.3 — the cleanest posture: the cycle is structurally absent from the construction-time call graph). The deferral handle's RESOLUTION is the runtime execution concern: on first call it resolves the target via the standard get path (for a singleton, cached thereafter; for shorter scopes, re-resolved) — this resolution is async and must be waker-driven on hot paths (§2.4), and crucially it resolves the FULLY-built target so there is no partial-construction or in-creation race (the deferral handle is called after refresh completes, when all eager singletons exist).
