CANCELLATION: since the cycle is broken post-build, there is no partially-constructed early reference to clean up on a cancelled build (a strict improvement over R1/R2 — no async-Drop hazard for cycle early references, because there are none); a cancelled deferral-handle resolution is just a cancelled get. The explicit opt-in early-reference primitive (rare case) DOES re-introduce a mid-build reference and inherits R1's cancellation/cleanup concern for that handle specifically. ERRORS: the CycleError (fail-fast, from the detector) carries the full path + the exact edge-to-convert fix hint (§2.9 — most actionable); a deferral-handle resolution that finds no target produces a missing-bean error distinct from a cycle error.

### Rust clashes
- The early reference cannot be retroactively swapped for the wrapped object — Rust ownership vs Spring's GC'd reference aliasing. THE central clash. On the JVM the early reference is a heap pointer freely aliased by collaborators, and Spring relies on the early reference and the final (possibly-wrapped) singleton becoming the SAME object whenever possible (line 2531), with allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping (lines 781, 2537) detecting the cases where they DON'T. In Rust, once a collaborator owns/Arc's an early-reference handle, there is no mechanism to transparently replace it with a later-wrapped handle — the collaborator holds what it was handed. So leaf has only stark choices: (a) the early reference IS already the final/proxy handle at exposure time (forcing the proxy decision eager, or accepting no proxy in cycles), or (b) a wrapped bean in a tolerated cycle is a HARD error (no silent reconciliation — the allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping 'allow' branch has no sound Rust analogue), or (c) R3's escape: break cycles with deferral handles that resolve the FINAL object, so there is never an early reference to swap. The 'becomes the same object' identity Spring leans on is not freely available.
- Exposing a partially-constructed instance into a cycle is an ownership/soundness hazard with no async-Drop safety net. Spring's early reference is a not-yet-populated, not-yet-initialized instance handed to a collaborator; on the JVM a half-built object is just a normal (if logically incomplete) object the GC manages. In Rust, handing out a handle to an instance whose fields are not yet populated (and whose init/@PostConstruct has not run) means a collaborator may call methods on a bean in an invalid state — and if the early-exposed bean's build then FAILS or is cancelled, the collaborator holds a handle to a partially-constructed object that cannot self-clean (no async Drop — nightly-doc §86/§273). The all-or-nothing eager pass must guarantee NO dangling early reference survives a boot failure, and the early reference's TYPE must make 'this is not fully initialized' either impossible to misuse or explicitly marked — a discipline Spring never needs.
- The constructor-vs-setter distinction maps onto Rust's construction model differently — and arguably MORE cleanly, but with a twist. Spring's distinction rests on 'a constructor argument must exist before this references a live object' (line 2533) — true in Rust too: a bean whose dependency is a constructor parameter literally cannot be built before that dependency exists, so a constructor cycle is unsatisfiable for the SAME structural reason. BUT Rust's constructor injection (preferred, mandatory, immutable per injection-styles) is even MORE rigid than the JVM's — there is no way to construct a Rust struct with a temporarily-absent field then set it later unless the field is Option<T> or a deferral handle. So the 'setter/field cycle is satisfiable' case is narrower in idiomatic Rust (a field that can hold a not-yet-ready reference must be Option/handle-typed), pushing leaf toward R3's deferral-handle model as the natural break and making implicit early-exposure feel less idiomatic than it is on the JVM.
- The early-reference 'ObjectFactory, not the instance' deferral was designed for a lazily-computed AOP proxy under a lock — in async Rust a lazily-computed early reference must not block the executor. Spring registers an ObjectFactory (a closure) into singletonFactories specifically so getEarlyBeanReference can run SmartInstantiationAwareBeanPostProcessors to compute the eventual proxy lazily-and-once (line 2531), and this computation rides the singleton lock. In async Rust, this early-reference computation runs mid-cycle-resolution, re-entrant into the creation machinery — it must NOT .await blocking work (charter §2.3: no blocking the executor under a re-entrant resolution), and it must not deadlock on a guard the in-flight creation holds. The lazy-computed-once-and-recorded property (avoiding double-wrap) must be preserved without Spring's lock-based memoization.
- leaf's proxy mechanism doesn't exist yet, so WHAT the early reference IS (raw vs early-proxy) and WHETHER a third cache level is needed are undecidable now. The entire early-exposure-of-a-factory design (lines 777, 1766, 2531) exists to support early AOP proxy injection into cycles — but leaf has no CGLIB, no settled proxy mechanism (aop-proxying is unresolved), and the early-proxy sub-tier's value depends on that decision. This feature must place the early-exposure HOOK at the structurally-correct moment (after instantiate, before populate, only under satisfiable-cycle conditions) and keep it proxy-mechanism-agnostic and origin-agnostic, while acknowledging that the raw-vs-wrapped consistency check has NO meaning until the proxy story exists, and the middle cache level may not earn its keep at all (a phase2Question shared with aop-proxying and bean-registry).
- Constructor-cycle fail-fast is a place Rust can be MORE honest than Spring — potentially at compile time. Spring detects constructor cycles at load/creation time and throws BeanCurrentlyInCreationException (lines 775, 2533). In Rust, because constructor edges are declarable metadata (the descriptor names constructor-arg dependencies) and the whole graph CAN be analyzed before construction (R2), a constructor cycle could be caught at build-time codegen with the full loop named — earlier and richer than any runtime exception (§2.9). The clash is only that this requires the graph to be statically knowable, which dynamic by-type/qualifier/conditional edges undermine (the over-approximation false-positive risk) — so the richest-diagnostic timing (§2.9) trades against the dynamism that makes some edges runtime-only.
- The benign-lenient-locking BeanCurrentlyInCreation (one origin) must NOT be conflated with a real cycle (the other origin) — and in async Rust the distinction is cross-task, not cross-thread. Spring has TWO origins of the same exception (lines 2535-2537, 1984-1986): a genuine cycle (fatal) vs a benign lenient-locking race where another thread already picked up the bean (tolerated and skipped). In leaf's async model, background-init beans (background-bootstrap) building on a separate task can re-enter the same name — this is the benign race (one task builds, the other reconciles via the ready cache), NOT a cycle. The cycle detector must distinguish 'THIS creation context is re-entering its own in-flight build' (a real cycle) from 'ANOTHER task is building this name' (benign) — which means the in_creation sentinel cannot be a flat global set checked identically by all tasks (charter §2.5); it must be reasoned about per-creation-context, or the detector will false-positive a benign cross-task race as a fatal cycle.
- AOT/native behavioral divergence is a Spring WART leaf can avoid by making the default IS the AOT behavior. Spring has a real inconsistency (lines 785, 2539): setter/field cycles 'work' at regular runtime but FAIL under AOT (no early-exposure machinery in generated suppliers) — so an app's cycle behavior changes when you switch to native compilation, prescribing @Lazy/ObjectProvider as the AOT fix. leaf can dissolve this wart entirely (R3): if the DEFAULT is 'cycles fail fast, break them with a deferral handle', there is ONE behavior across runtime-assembled and codegen/AOT-style contexts — no surprising divergence. The clash is the parity cost: this diverges from Spring's runtime tolerance (the brief's stated intent 'tolerates setter/field cycles'), defensible by Spring Boot's own off-by-default posture but a genuine behavioral difference.

### Interactions
- **bean-instantiation** — PRIMARY UPSTREAM — the host of the cycle MECHANISM. bean-instantiation owns the per-bean construct→expose-early→populate→init→publish sequence, the in_creation sentinel, and the creation guard; THIS feature owns the cycle POLICY layered onto it: the verdict function (re-entry into an in-creation name → break via early reference if available, else fatal), the placement of the early-exposure hook at the structurally-correct moment (after instantiate, before populate, only under satisfiable-cycle conditions), and the publish-time raw-vs-wrapped consistency check. bean-instantiation's intentRecap explicitly names this as its TIGHTEST coupling. The boundary: bean-instantiation = the sequence and its sentinel; circular-references = what the sentinel's verdict is and what reference breaks a satisfiable cycle. In R2, cycle classification moves to a graph pass that bean-instantiation R2's plan-then-execute shares.
- **bean-registry** — UPSTREAM — owns the cache the early reference rides on. The early-reference cache slot (Spring's middle singletonFactories→earlySingletonObjects tier) is bean-registry's data structure; this feature places a deferred EarlyRef into it at the right moment and reads it to break a cycle. Whether the registry has a third (middle) cache level AT ALL is bean-registry's keystone question, and whether it earns its keep depends largely on cycle-breaking + early-proxy needs — a shared phase2Question. The 'who owns the raw target once an early reference is handed out, and can it not also be injected raw elsewhere' is a bean-registry ownership concern this feature surfaces (the allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping analogue).
- **deferral-primitives** — TIGHTEST COUPLING for the BREAK (and the whole of R3). A deferral handle (ObjectProvider/lazy-resolution proxy) injection point does NOT participate as a construction-time edge — it resolves its target post-build on first call — so it structurally REMOVES an edge from the cycle (Spring's own recommended fix, lines 794-796, and the AOT-prescribed break, lines 785/2539). deferral-primitives defines the handle types and their re-resolution semantics; this feature uses them as the sanctioned cycle-break and, in R3, as the ONLY cycle-break (the deferral handle IS the rust-native early reference, resolving the FULLY-built target). The cycle diagnostic names the exact edge to convert to a deferral handle.
- **lazy-initialization** — SIBLING BREAK. @Lazy at an injection point (a lazy-resolution proxy) is the other Spring-sanctioned cycle break (lines 785, 2593): it defers a singleton's creation so the cyclic edge isn't constructed eagerly. lazy-initialization defines the @Lazy/global-flag semantics; this feature treats a lazy injection point like a deferral handle — it removes/defers the construction-time edge, breaking the cycle. The diagnostic may suggest @Lazy as an alternative to ObjectProvider where the consumer wants the bean's type rather than a handle.
- **aop-proxying** — TIGHT COUPLING via early-proxy exposure. aop-proxying's intentRecap names early-proxy exposure into cycles (getEarlyBeanReference, lines 777/1766/2531) as its integration point with THIS feature: if a singleton in a tolerated cycle gets proxied, the early reference handed to mid-cycle collaborators must be the PROXY (computed lazily-and-once, recorded to avoid double-wrap), or collaborators bypass advice. The factory-not-instance early-exposure exists SPECIFICALLY so this lazy-once proxy computation can happen. This feature places the early-exposure hook; aop-proxying supplies what the early reference becomes (raw vs early-proxy). Since leaf's proxy story is unsettled, the raw-vs-wrapped consistency check and whether the middle cache level is needed are jointly open (a shared phase2Question).
- **auto-proxy-creator** — COUPLED via the consistency check. auto-proxy-creator decides whether a bean is wrapped (does any advisor match) and produces the wrapped handle at after-init. The raw-vs-wrapped consistency check (this feature's allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping analogue) fires precisely when auto-proxy-creator wrapped a bean AFTER a cyclic collaborator already took its raw early reference — so this feature must coordinate with auto-proxy-creator's wrap decision to know whether the final handle differs from the early reference. The early-proxy-recorded-to-avoid-double-wrap mechanism is shared machinery (auto-proxy-creator records the early proxy; this feature triggers it during cycle resolution).
- **injection-styles** — DEFINES the constructor-vs-setter distinction the verdict rests on. injection-styles defines constructor (preferred, mandatory, immutable), setter (optional), and field injection. THIS feature's core verdict — constructor cycle = fatal, setter/field cycle = satisfiable (Spring lines 775/2533) — is read directly off the injection STYLE of each edge: a constructor edge is a mandatory/eager edge in any cycle analysis (R2 tags it 'mandatory'), a setter/field edge is breakable. The rustClash notes Rust's constructor injection is even more rigid (no temporarily-absent field without Option/handle typing), narrowing the satisfiable case.
- **autowiring-resolution** — UPSTREAM — produces the edges and triggers the re-entry. The populate phase delegates to autowiring-resolution to resolve each injection point, which recursively re-enters get() to create dependencies — and THAT re-entry into an in-creation name is exactly where R1's cycle detection fires. autowiring-resolution defines the edges (and the DYNAMIC by-type/qualifier edges that R2's static analysis must conservatively over-approximate — the central R2 tension). A deferral-handle or @Lazy injection point is resolved by autowiring-resolution WITHOUT triggering recursive creation, which is how the edge is removed from the cycle.
- **refresh-lifecycle** — UPSTREAM DRIVER — sets the allow_circular policy and aborts on fatal cycles. refresh-lifecycle's bring-up (Spring's customizeBeanFactory, line 130/293) is where allowCircularReferences is configured on the factory before the eager pass; a fatal constructor-cycle (or, by default, any cycle) verdict during the eager pass aborts refresh all-or-nothing. In R2, the cycle-classification graph pass is a refresh-time (or build-time) step refresh-lifecycle invokes before/as part of computing the construction plan.
- **concurrency-contract** — COUPLED via the benign-vs-real-cycle distinction. concurrency-contract frames safe publication of fully-built singletons and the no-global-lock doctrine (§2.5). The cycle detector must distinguish a real same-creation-context cycle from the BENIGN cross-task lenient-locking re-entry (Spring's two origins, lines 1984-1986/2535-2537) — which means the in_creation sentinel must be reasoned about per-creation-context, not as a global set, honoring concurrency-contract's no-global-lock posture. A wrongly-conflated benign race would false-positive as a fatal cycle.
- **background-bootstrap** — INTERACTS via the benign cross-task re-entry. background-bootstrap beans build leniently on a separate task; a cross-task re-entry into the same in-creation name is the benign lenient-locking race (one task builds, the other reconciles via the ready cache), NOT a cycle (rustClash: cross-task, not cross-thread). This feature's detector must NOT treat a background-build re-entry as a fatal cycle. The cycle verdict logic and the strict/lenient creation policy (bean-instantiation/background-bootstrap) must agree on what re-entry means.
- **context-close** — COUPLED via partial-construction cleanup. Because there is no async Drop, if an early-exposed bean's build fails/is cancelled mid-cycle, the EarlyRef slot and in_creation entry must be unwound, and a collaborator holding the early reference of a now-failed bean is a dangling-handle hazard — the all-or-nothing eager pass must abort so no such reference survives a boot failure. context-close defines the explicit reverse-order shutdown; this feature ensures cycle early-references are part of the unwind ledger on failure (R3 mostly dissolves this: deferral-handle breaks resolve post-build, so there are no cycle early references to clean up).
- **smart-initializing** — NEIGHBOUR — the all-singletons-exist boundary is when deferral-handle cycle-breaks become safe to resolve. R3's deferral handles resolve the FULLY-built target; the smart-initializing callback fires after every eager singleton exists, which is the earliest point a cycle-broken deferral handle is guaranteed to resolve a complete target. This feature's deferral-break model relies on the post-all-singletons boundary smart-initializing provides for safe first-call resolution.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Does leaf TOLERATE setter/field singleton cycles by default (R1/R2 — Spring-runtime-faithful, requires the early-exposure machinery and the raw-vs-wrapped consistency check), or does it make the default fail-fast-and-break-with-a-deferral-handle (R3 — Spring-Boot-posture-and-AOT-faithful, dissolves the runtime-vs-AOT divergence, sheds the early-reference ownership impedance, but diverges from the brief's literal 'tolerates setter/field cycles' intent)? This is the keystone policy call for THIS feature and it cross-cuts DX (§2.1: Spring permissiveness vs rust-honesty), the scope/ownership keystone (whether an early reference exists at all), and the AOT/compile-vs-runtime lineage (§3). Held unresolved.
- Is cycle handling a RUNTIME re-entrancy event (R1 — detected as creation re-enters an in-creation name, handles dynamic edges, but is the latest/least-complete diagnostic and re-creates a same-name re-entrant deadlock surface) or a BUILD/PLAN-time graph CLASSIFICATION (R2 — earliest+richest diagnostic, AOT-friendly, no runtime sentinel for classified cycles, but requires statically-knowable edges and risks FALSE-POSITIVE cycles from conservatively over-approximated dynamic by-type/qualifier/conditional edges)? Cross-cuts bean-instantiation (recursive-walk vs plan-then-execute), the compile-vs-runtime split (§3), and candidate-resolver (whose dynamic edges undermine static analysis). Needs the whole catalog to judge.
- What IS the early reference, concretely, given Rust ownership and no GC aliasing? Spring's early reference and final singleton 'become the same object whenever possible' via freely-aliasable GC pointers; in Rust an early-reference handle once handed out cannot be retroactively swapped for a wrapped version. Is the early reference (a) eagerly the final/proxy handle at exposure time, (b) a raw handle where any later wrapping is a HARD error (no silent allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping reconciliation), or (c) eliminated entirely by R3's deferral-handle-resolves-the-final-object break? This is the scope/ownership keystone (§3) viewed through cycle-breaking. Shared with bean-registry, aop-proxying, scopes.
- Does the registry need a THIRD (middle) cache level for cycle early-references at all, and if so what does the early-exposure hook place there? Spring's middle singletonFactories tier exists ONLY to compute an AOP early-proxy lazily-and-once — and leaf's proxy mechanism (aop-proxying/auto-proxy-creator) is unsettled with no CGLIB, so whether the third level earns its keep is undecidable until the proxying strategy is chosen, and R3 makes it largely moot for cycles. Shared with bean-registry and aop-proxying.
- How does the cycle detector distinguish a REAL same-creation-context cycle from the BENIGN cross-task lenient-locking re-entry (Spring's two distinct BeanCurrentlyInCreation origins) without a global in_creation set? In leaf's async model a background-init bean re-entering the same name on a separate task is benign (another task is building it), not a cycle — so the sentinel must be reasoned about per-creation-context (charter §2.5: no global lock), and conflating the two would false-positive a benign race as a fatal cycle. Shared with background-bootstrap, concurrency-contract, bean-instantiation.
- What is the partial-construction cleanup contract for a cycle early-reference when an early-exposed bean's build fails or is cancelled, given no async Drop? A collaborator that already took the early reference holds a handle to a partially-constructed object; the all-or-nothing eager pass must abort so no such dangling reference survives a boot failure — via a structured-concurrency scope, an explicit cleanup ledger, or (R3) eliminating early references entirely so the hazard cannot arise. Shared with context-close and the async-execution model.
- Does leaf preserve Spring's runtime-vs-AOT behavioral divergence (setter cycles work at runtime, fail under AOT — lines 785/2539) or dissolve it by making ONE cycle behavior across runtime-assembled and codegen/AOT contexts (R3's default IS the AOT behavior)? A single behavior is more predictable (§2.1, end-user predictability per charter §8) but means runtime-assembly cannot offer the more-permissive Spring-runtime tolerance even where it could. Cross-cuts the compile-vs-runtime split (§3) and the conditional/codegen strategy.
- Where is whole-graph cycle detection anchored cross-crate, and how does it compose with the anti-DCE self-check and the missing-candidate check? A cycle spanning crates X↔Y is only visible at the binary/aggregator (cross-crate §269/§357: build.rs can't scan siblings) — so R2's build-time analysis must anchor at the app root, and the cycle diagnostic must be DISTINGUISHABLE from a missing-candidate diagnostic (a DCE'd crate's missing bean is an unsatisfiable edge, not a cycle). How the optional dynamic directory-scanned (WASM-module) source's late edges reconcile with build-time cycle analysis (must-register-before-analysis vs re-analyze) is unresolved (a CANDIDATE, not a decision). Shared with the whole discovery/SPI family.
- Is the early-AOP-proxy-into-a-cycle capability (the genuine need for a mid-construction reference to a cyclic collaborator that is the eventual PROXY) a first-class supported case, or an explicit opt-in (R3's named early-reference primitive), or dropped? Spring supports it implicitly via the factory-not-instance exposure; in Rust it forces eager proxy decision at exposure time and re-introduces the raw-vs-wrapped tension. Its value depends entirely on the unsettled proxy story. Shared with aop-proxying, auto-proxy-creator.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the precise feature section the brief named — line 773 'Circular dependencies and the early-singleton reference' and its body (773-785: the tolerate-setter/fail-constructor distinction, earlySingletonExposure conditions, addSingletonFactory-before-populateBean, the constructor case as logically-unsatisfiable-and-fatal, allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping at 781, the pragmatic-escape-hatch-not-endorsement design intent at 783, and the AOT-fails-on-cycles change at 785) — plus the deeper mechanics that are this feature's body: the three-level singleton cache and the high-design-intent factory-not-instance choice (2518-2533), the two distinct BeanCurrentlyInCreation origins (2535-2537), the AOT no-early-exposure-machinery change (2539), the strict-vs-lenient locking that produces the BENIGN BeanCurrentlyInCreation (2506-2516, 1984-1986), the Spring-Boot-off-by-default-since-2.6 posture (2190-2192), and the deferral-primitives recommended-break (787-796). I separated THIS feature (the cycle POLICY: detection verdict, early-exposure-hook placement, consistency check, off-by-default posture, deferral-as-recommended-break) from its upstream bean-instantiation (which owns the creation SEQUENCE and the in_creation sentinel and explicitly names circular-references as its tightest coupling) and aop-proxying (which owns what the early reference BECOMES when proxied). I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc (the four architectures A/B/C/D at 277-331, the anti-DCE silent-failure self-check mandate at 152/345, build.rs-can't-scan-siblings at 357, generics-can't-register at 153/349, force-linking-at-the-binary at 269, scope-discovery-not-global at 362) and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.5 no-global-lock, §2.9 rich-early-diagnostics, §2.2 intent-not-mechanism) and the nightly envelope (no async Drop as a first-class constraint at §86/§273-281, boxed futures at the dyn boundary, trait upcasting 1.86, three-level cache shape).

The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that DEFINES this feature — WHEN and HOW cycles are detected and broken: R1 = runtime re-entrancy sentinel + early-reference cache slot (Spring-faithful, the verdict and break happen AS creation re-enters; highest fidelity, handles dynamic edges, dovetails with bean-instantiation R1, but inherits the raw-vs-wrapped ownership impedance and the latest-diagnostic timing); R2 = build/plan-time graph CLASSIFICATION (cycles classified before construction; earliest+richest diagnostic, AOT-friendly, no runtime sentinel for classified cycles, pairs with bean-instantiation R2's wave plan, but requires static edges and risks FALSE-POSITIVE cycles from over-approximated dynamic edges); R3 = deferral-first/explicit-break (leaf does NOT auto-tolerate cycles — every cycle is fail-fast and broken by an explicit deferral handle that resolves the FULLY-built target; most rust-native and honest, sheds the ownership impedance and the runtime-vs-AOT divergence entirely, unifies with deferral-primitives, but diverges from Spring's runtime tolerance and the brief's literal 'tolerates setter/field cycles' intent). R1 vs R2 differ on runtime-event vs build-time-analysis (and R3's detector is inherited from one of them, making R3 a POLICY layered on R1/R2 rather than a wholly independent detection mechanism — flagged honestly in R3's cons).

Things I deliberately did NOT decide (held as phase2Questions): the tolerate-vs-fail-fast default policy (the keystone for this feature); runtime-event vs build-time-classification detection; WHAT the early reference is given Rust ownership and no GC aliasing (the scope/ownership keystone viewed through cycles); whether a third cache level is needed (the proxy/registry keystone); how to distinguish a real cycle from the benign cross-task lenient-locking race without a global set; the no-async-Drop partial-construction cleanup contract; whether to preserve or dissolve Spring's runtime-vs-AOT divergence; where whole-graph detection anchors cross-crate; and whether early-AOP-proxy-into-a-cycle is first-class/opt-in/dropped. Every realization keeps the early reference (where it exists) ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC (wraps whatever erased handle the driver produced, native/WASM/test-double identically; a deferral-handle break resolves any-origin target through the standard get path), keeps the constructor-vs-setter verdict read off injection-styles, reproduces the fail-fast-on-constructor-cycle intent with the richest diagnostic each model allows, and surfaces the deferral-handle as the recommended/honest break (Spring's own recommendation). The two-source duality (static link-time registry + optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source) is captured as both feeding the same edge/descriptor set the detector walks — cleanest in R1/R3 (runtime/deferral), a real timing tension with R2's build-time analysis — and flagged as a CANDIDATE, not a decision. Naming ('EarlyRef', 'CycleError', 'in_creation', 'early-reference cache slot', 'break-point', 'allow_circular') is Phase-1 placeholder.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: no decision on the singleton-handle/ownership type, the two-vs-three-level cache, runtime-vs-build-time detection, or the proxy story. I wrote no files. Relevant grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (773-785 the named feature section: tolerate-setter/fail-constructor, early-singleton exposure, allowRawInjectionDespiteWrapping, escape-hatch-not-endorsement intent, AOT-fails-on-cycles; 2518-2533 three-level cache + factory-not-instance high-design-intent choice + constructor-cycles-deliberately-unresolvable; 2535-2537 two BeanCurrentlyInCreation origins; 2539 AOT no-early-exposure-machinery; 2506-2516 + 1984-1986 strict/lenient locking + benign BeanCurrentlyInCreation; 2190-2192 Spring-Boot-off-by-default-since-2.6; 787-796 deferral-primitives recommended-break + visible-in-the-type design intent; 952-954/1766-1768 getEarlyBeanReference for AOP+cycles); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2 esp. 2.1/2.2/2.3/2.5/2.9, deferred keystones §3, topology §6, end-user-predictability §8); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (DCE silent-failure + self-check mandate 152/345, build.rs-can't-scan-siblings 357, generics-can't-register 153/349, force-linking-at-binary 269, scope-not-global 362, Architectures A/B/C/D 277-331); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (no async Drop as first-class constraint 86/273-281, RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe forcing boxed futures 160-176, trait upcasting 1.86 118-126, three-level Arc<dyn Any> registry 17/81).

## FactoryBean Indirection  `[factory-bean]`

Spring's `FactoryBean<T>` / `SmartFactoryBean<T>` is a NAME-INDIRECTION + SEPARATE-LIFECYCLE contract: a bean registered under name `N` is itself a *factory*, but `getBean(N)` resolves to `factory.getObject()` — the PRODUCT (type `T`), not the factory. The factory is reachable only through the deliberately-awkward `&N` dereference sigil (`FACTORY_BEAN_PREFIX`); `transformedBeanName` strips `&`+aliases so factory and product share ONE canonical definition name, and dereferencing a non-factory is a hard error (`BeanIsNotAFactoryException`). The intent to replicate (NOT the JVM mechanism): (1) NAME→PRODUCT INDIRECTION as the default resolve, with an escape-hatch handle for the factory itself — the mental model is 'a name denotes a usable collaborator regardless of how it was built; the factory is a construction detail consumers must not couple to, so getting at it is made awkward on purpose.' (2) A PROGRAMMATIC, ANNOTATION-FREE construction contract: `getObject()`/`getObjectType()` may run very early (ahead of any post-processor / annotation-driven injection), so this is the sanctioned route for *complex stateful construction* that ordinary `@Bean`-method-style (configuration-classes) instantiation can't express — the 50+ Spring FactoryBeans (`ProxyFactoryBean`, `JndiObjectFactoryBean`, `LocalSessionFactoryBean`) live here. (3) SEPARATE CACHING & LIFECYCLE: the singleton product lives in a SEPARATE cache (`factoryBeanObjectCache`) keyed by the same canonical name — one name, two cached objects; caching is gated on `factory.isSingleton()` with a double-checked read/create/re-read so a re-entrant `getBean` during `getObject()` preserves singleton identity; a `null` product becomes a `NullBean` sentinel. (4) TYPE MATCHING WITHOUT REALIZATION: `getObjectType()` lets the container do autowiring/type-match WITHOUT calling `getObject()` (returning unknown makes the factory gracefully invisible to autowiring, not an error); v7's `SmartFactoryBean.supportsType(Class)`+`getObject(Class)` extend this to ONE factory exposing MULTIPLE product types (Hibernate `Session`/`StatelessSession`), consulted before the single-type query. (5) EAGER-GATING: during the eager pre-instantiation pass the FACTORY is always created (it is itself a singleton bean), but the PRODUCT is created eagerly ONLY if it's a `SmartFactoryBean` with `isEagerInit()==true` — plain factory products stay lazy until first resolve; `isPrototype()` is NOT the inverse of `isSingleton()` (a scoped product can be neither). (6) ORPHANED PRODUCT LIFECYCLE: the container manages ONLY the factory's lifecycle; the product's `close()` is NOT called automatically — the factory must implement `DisposableBean` and delegate. leaf must replicate this INTENT — a name that resolves to a product with a `&`-style escape hatch to the factory, an annotation-free async construction contract usable for complex stateful builds, separate product caching/identity, type-match-without-realize, factory-always/product-lazy eager-gating, and a disciplined teardown story — WITHOUT Spring's `&`-string-prefix sigil parsing, its dual-monitor (`singletonLock`+`synchronized(factory)`) locking, its GC-orphaned product, or its assumption of native-only construction (a FactoryBean must be just one ORIGIN of a product, so a WASM-module/test-double factory plugs into the identical product-resolution path). It must NOT pick the singleton-handle type, the product cache substrate, whether the factory↔product relationship is encoded as two bean-registry slots or one provider wrapping another, the async-across-dyn boxing standard, or how much type-info is build-time vs runtime — those are Phase 2.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — FactoryBean as a typed Provider trait + a dual-slot registry shape: the product is a derived bean-registry entry whose origin-agnostic factory delegates to the user factory; the factory itself is a sibling slot reached by a typed `Factory<T>` handle (Spring-faithful, two-slots, re-typed)
Mirror Spring's one-name-two-objects shape directly, retyped to eliminate the `&`-string-prefix and the dual-monitor lock. A user factory implements `trait FactoryBean { type Product: ...; async fn create(&self, cx: &ResolveCtx) -> Result<Self::Product, ResolveError>; fn product_type(&self) -> ProductType; fn is_singleton(&self) -> bool { true } }` plus an optional `trait SmartFactoryBean: FactoryBean { fn is_eager_init(&self) -> bool { false } fn supports_type(&self, t: TypeKey) -> bool; async fn create_as(&self, t: TypeKey, cx: &ResolveCtx) -> Result<ErasedProduct, ResolveError> }`. The macro that marks a FactoryBean emits TWO bean-definitions (bean-definition owns the record) sharing ONE canonical name: (a) the FACTORY definition — an ordinary singleton bean whose origin-agnostic boxed-future factory (the seam bean-instantiation constructs through) builds the user's factory struct; (b) a derived PRODUCT definition whose origin-agnostic factory is a thin closure `move |cx| factory_handle.create(cx)` — i.e. the product's construction recipe IS 'resolve the factory singleton, await its create'. The registry (bean-registry) now holds both, but candidate-resolution/autowiring sees ONLY the product slot for ordinary `get<T>()`/injection — the product type is what `product_type()`/`supports_type()` advertises, so type-matching-without-realization is the product slot carrying a STATIC product TypeKey (declared by the macro from the trait's `Product` assoc type, or set explicitly via an attribute when the type can't be deduced) consulted during candidate matching WITHOUT awaiting create. The `&` escape hatch becomes a TYPED handle `Factory<T>` (a deferral-primitive-style injectable) or `get_factory::<F>(name)` — dereferencing a non-factory is a compile-time/registry-lookup error, not a runtime sigil parse. Eager-gating: bean-instantiation's eager pass treats the FACTORY definition as a normal eager singleton (always built), and the PRODUCT definition as eager ONLY if the factory is Smart with `is_eager_init()` (else marked lazy on the product slot — relocated to first resolve, reusing lazy-initialization). Separate caching: the product slot has its OWN registry cache entry (bean-registry's existing singleton cache, second key), so 'one name two cached objects' is two registry slots with a factory→product link; product singleton identity + the re-entrant-during-create double-check is bean-instantiation's existing same-bean creation guard applied to the product slot. SmartFactoryBean multi-type: `supports_type`+`create_as` register MULTIPLE product TypeKeys against the one product slot (candidate-resolver consults them), and the realize path dispatches to `create_as(t)`. Teardown: the FACTORY registers its own DisposableBean shutdown with context-close; the product's teardown is a Phase-2 policy knob (orphan = Spring-faithful, or factory-delegated).

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity to the one-name-two-objects model: factory and product are distinct registry slots sharing a canonical name, product is the default resolve, factory is the awkward escape hatch — maps 1:1 to Spring while dropping the `&`-string sigil for a typed handle (more rust-native, compile-checked dereference).; Reuses upstream machinery cleanly: the product slot is just another bean-definition whose origin-agnostic factory delegates, so bean-instantiation's construct→populate→init→publish sequence, its same-bean creation guard (giving the re-entrant-during-create double-check for free), and bean-registry's singleton cache (giving separate product caching as a second slot) ALL apply unchanged — minimal new machinery.; Type-match-without-realization falls out structurally: the product slot carries a STATIC product TypeKey for candidate-resolver, so autowiring matches against the declared product type WITHOUT awaiting create — the graceful-invisibility (unknown type ⇒ not a candidate) is just an absent/None TypeKey.; Origin-agnostic by construction: 'FactoryBean' is simply one way to populate the product slot's origin-agnostic factory closure — a WASM-module or test-double product source populates the SAME product slot identically, so the dynamic 'classpath' slots in without a FactoryBean-specific path.; Eager-gating reduces to a per-slot lazy flag bean-instantiation already honors (factory eager, product lazy-unless-eager-init), and SmartFactoryBean multi-type is just multiple TypeKeys on the product slot — both ride existing candidate/lazy mechanisms.; Cleanest neighbour story: deferral-primitives' `Factory<T>` handle and the `get_factory` escape hatch reuse the same injectable-handle shape, and the annotation-free contract is honored because the factory's `create` runs as a plain async fn needing no post-processor.

**Cons:** Two registry slots per FactoryBean DOUBLES the definition count and forces bean-registry/bean-naming to model a factory→product LINK (which slot is the dereference target of which) — a structural coupling that bean-naming's collision rules must understand (the product slot 'owns' the public name; the factory slot is name-shadowed behind the handle), and listing/introspection must hide or specially-mark the factory slot.; The product slot's origin-agnostic factory closure CAPTURES a handle to the factory singleton and awaits its `create` during the product's own creation — so resolving the product RE-ENTERS resolution for the factory, a mutually-recursive async creation across `.await` (the same recursion/lock-ordering hazard bean-instantiation flags) now baked into every FactoryBean by construction, even acyclic ones.; Spring's same-`getObject()`-from-two-threads defensive `synchronized(factory)` has to be re-derived as 'the product slot's creation guard serializes concurrent first-creation AND the factory's `create` is called at most once per product realization' — but a NON-singleton product (scoped/prototype) calls `create` repeatedly with NO guard, exactly Spring's note that the factory author owns thread-safety there; getting that boundary right (guarded singleton create vs unguarded repeated create) is subtle.; Declaring the product TypeKey statically from the trait's `Product` assoc type works for single-type factories but SmartFactoryBean multi-type (`supports_type` over a dynamic set) can't always be known at macro time → needs a runtime-advertised TypeKey set, partially defeating type-match-without-realization for the multi-type case (must consult a runtime list, not a static const).; The factory↔product teardown split (factory disposable, product orphaned-or-delegated) is two lifecycle ownerships for one logical thing — and with no async Drop, an orphaned pooled-product is a leak; making the product's teardown ride the factory's registered shutdown (more disciplined than Spring) couples the product slot's lifecycle back to the factory slot, re-entangling what the two-slot split separated.; Risks over-fitting bean-registry to a FactoryBean-specific 'derived slot + link' notion when a generic provider-of-provider shape (R2) might serve more features — pre-committing the registry to dual-slots is a shape decision bordering on Phase-2 territory.

**Mechanisms considered:** typed `FactoryBean` / `SmartFactoryBean` traits (async create + product_type + is_singleton + is_eager_init + supports_type/create_as), implemented by the user, marked by a thin attribute macro; macro emits TWO bean-definitions sharing one canonical name: a factory singleton slot + a derived product slot whose origin-agnostic boxed-future factory delegates `move |cx| factory.create(cx)`; product slot carries a STATIC product TypeKey (from the `Product` assoc type or an explicit attribute) for candidate-resolver, enabling type-match without awaiting create; absent TypeKey ⇒ graceful autowiring-invisibility; typed `Factory<T>` injectable handle + `get_factory::<F>(name)` as the `&` escape hatch — dereference of a non-factory is a compile/lookup error, not runtime sigil parse; eager-gating via per-slot lazy flag (factory eager singleton; product lazy unless Smart+eager-init) reusing lazy-initialization; multi-type via multiple TypeKeys + create_as dispatch; product singleton identity + re-entrant-during-create double-check via bean-instantiation's existing same-bean creation guard on the product slot; teardown split (factory disposable; product orphan-or-delegated) as a Phase-2 knob

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Substrate-neutral on collection — a FactoryBean contributes the SAME thin `const` descriptor(s) every other bean does (cross-crate doc §167's `&'static Descriptor` shape), just TWO descriptors (factory + derived product) sharing a canonical name, emitted via absolute paths by the thin macro (§89-102), holding zero logic. The factory↔product LINK must be expressible in the descriptor data (the product descriptor names which factory descriptor produces it) so the link survives across the link-time registry without a runtime classpath. The static product TypeKey on the product descriptor is exactly the kind of metadata that makes type-match-without-realization work from a flat descriptor table — and it is what a build.rs/codegen path (Arch C) could emit when the trait's `Product` assoc type is monomorphic. GENERICS clash hard here: a generic FactoryBean<P> has no `'static` symbol/TypeKey until concretely instantiated, so the macro hard-errors (§349) with a per-instantiation `register_factory_bean!(ConcreteFactory)` escape hatch — and a factory whose PRODUCT is generic is doubly blocked. The anti-DCE self-check (§152) must count BOTH slots: a silently-DCE'd FactoryBean crate must surface as a missing factory AND missing product, named, at boot. The WASM-module 'classpath' is one producer of product descriptors (its product slot's factory dispatches into the module) feeding the SAME registry the eager pass walks — CANDIDATES: a static link-time registry (default) PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned source both contributing product/factory descriptors, NOT a decision. No new cross-crate ABI beyond the descriptor's factory-link field.

**T2 — execution context:** The FACTORY is created on the bootstrap task during the eager pass (cold path) like any singleton — its construction is an `.await` and a blocking factory-construction must use spawn_blocking (§2.3). The PRODUCT's `create` is the critical execution-context site: Spring runs `getObject()` UNDER the singleton lock (the same deadlock trap as @PostConstruct — a heavy/re-entrant `getObject` deadlocks); leaf must NOT hold any creation guard across the user `create().await`. The rust-native discipline: the product slot's creation guard serializes only the COMMIT (publish into the product cache), while `create().await` runs OUTSIDE a held guard (dissolving Spring's getObject-under-lock trap), with the re-entrant-during-create case reconciled by the double-checked cache read. A SINGLETON product's `create` is awaited at most once (cold, on first resolve or eager-init), result published into the product cache with safe publication (§concurrency-contract). A NON-singleton (prototype/scoped) product calls `create` repeatedly on the resolving task (potentially a hot path for prototype products) with NO guard — so the FactoryBean author owns `create`'s thread-safety/reentrancy there (Spring's exact note), and `create` may `.await` (DB/pool) but a blocking impl must spawn_blocking. CANCELLATION: cancelling an in-flight product `create` drops the partial product; because there is no async Drop, a `create` that already acquired an async-cleanup resource before failing/cancelling must surface that resource to the container's explicit-cleanup path (the factory's registered shutdown) — leaving it inside the half-built product with only sync Drop is the leak hazard. ERRORS: a failed `create` is a rich ResolveError naming the factory, the requested product TypeKey, and the assembly chain (§2.9); a dereference-non-factory is a distinct typed error. TEARDOWN: the factory's `DisposableBean::shutdown().await` runs in reverse order via context-close; whether the product's teardown rides it or is orphaned is the Phase-2 lifecycle knob (orphaned product with async cleanup = a leak with no async Drop).

#### R2 — FactoryBean is NOT special: it is one impl of the universal origin-agnostic `Provider` (provider-of-a-provider); resolution composes providers, the factory is a Provider whose product is another Provider, and `&` is 'resolve the providing Provider instead of its product'
Push unification (§2.11) and origin-agnosticism hardest: REMOVE the FactoryBean concept as a distinct registry citizen. The irreducible unit is the origin-agnostic `Provider` shared with bean-registry/bean-instantiation: `trait Provider { fn product_type(&self) -> ProductType; fn provide<'a>(&'a self, cx: &'a ResolveCtx) -> BoxFuture<'a, Result<ErasedBean, ResolveError>>; }`. A native bean is a Provider; a WASM bridge is a Provider; a test double is a Provider; and a FactoryBean is JUST a Provider whose `provide` (a) lazily realizes/caches the user factory object and (b) calls its `create` — the PRODUCT-providing Provider IS the registry citizen, and the factory object is an internal detail it owns. There is no second slot: the name `N` maps to a `ProductProvider` that wraps the factory. The user writes a `FactoryBean`-marked struct (the SAME trait surface as R1: async `create`, `product_type`, eager/singleton flags, supports_type), and the macro emits ONE descriptor whose Provider is a `FactoryBeanProvider` adapter — it owns the factory's own construction recipe internally, realizes the factory once (its own OnceCell-style guard), and delegates `provide` to `factory.create`. The `&` escape hatch is reframed as a RESOLUTION MODE on the same name: `resolve(name, mode = Product)` (default) vs `resolve(name, mode = Provider)` — the latter returns the factory object itself (typed via `get_factory::<F>`), implemented because the `FactoryBeanProvider` exposes its inner factory on request and any non-FactoryBean Provider returns a 'not a factory' error for `mode=Provider`. Separate caching becomes: the ProductProvider holds the product cache (singleton ⇒ a memoized cell inside the Provider; prototype ⇒ no memo), and the registry's singleton cache holds the Provider itself — so 'two cached objects' is 'the registry caches the Provider, the Provider caches its product,' a clean ownership split with no second registry slot. Type-match-without-realization: `product_type()` on the Provider answers candidate-resolver without `provide`; multi-type SmartFactoryBean is a Provider that answers `supports_type` over a set and whose `provide_as(t)` dispatches. Eager-gating: the eager pass realizes every Provider's PREREQUISITE (here, builds the factory object) always, but calls `provide` (realizes the product) eagerly only if the Provider self-reports eager (Smart+eager-init) — generalizing 'factory always, product lazy' to 'Provider always available, product realized per the Provider's eager policy.' Teardown: the Provider registers its own shutdown; a FactoryBeanProvider's shutdown disposes the factory and (policy) optionally the product.

**Pros:** Maximal unification (§2.11) and the brief's origin-agnostic mandate taken to its conclusion: FactoryBean stops being a special registry citizen — native/FactoryBean-product/WASM/test-double are ALL Providers, so the future dynamic 'classpath' and a FactoryBean are literally the same kind of thing (a Provider), eliminating a whole special-case path in bean-registry, candidate-resolver, and bean-instantiation.; No second registry slot and no factory→product link to model: the name maps to one Provider that internally owns the factory and the product cache, so bean-naming/listing/collision rules stay simple (one name, one citizen) and the 'two cached objects' becomes a clean ownership split (registry caches the Provider; Provider caches its product) rather than a dual-slot coupling.; The `&` dereference becomes a RESOLUTION MODE (Product vs Provider) on one name rather than a sigil-parsed shadow name — rust-native, typed (`get_factory::<F>`), and uniformly defined (any Provider can answer 'are you a factory?'), honoring the 'make dereference deliberately awkward but typed' intent without string-prefix parsing.; The product memo lives INSIDE the Provider, so the singleton-product double-check (re-entrant getObject during create) is a single OnceCell-style guard the Provider owns — no cross-slot reconciliation, and a prototype/scoped product simply has no memo (re-create each provide), cleanly expressing Spring's 'isPrototype not the inverse of isSingleton' as a per-Provider caching policy.; Type-match-without-realization is the Provider's `product_type()`/`supports_type()` — a method that must answer WITHOUT calling `provide`, exactly Spring's getObjectType-without-getObject contract, and it generalizes to any Provider (a WASM bridge advertises its product type the same way).; Best fit for a future where 'how a bean is built' is uniformly pluggable: ProxyFactoryBean-style stateful construction, scoped-proxy targets, and even auto-proxy wrapping could all be Providers wrapping Providers — composing rather than special-casing.

**Cons:** Borders on DECLARING the meta-architecture Phase 2 owns: 'everything is a Provider and resolution composes Providers' is a registry-substrate + creation-driver decision (the §3 type-erasure/registry keystone) — holding it strictly as a CANDIDATE shape for THIS feature is hard when its whole value is that it dissolves the feature into the substrate.; Collapsing factory+product into one Provider HIDES the factory as a registry citizen — but Spring deliberately keeps the factory addressable (`&N`) and the factory IS often a full bean with its own injected dependencies, lifecycle callbacks, and post-processing; making it an internal detail of the Provider means the factory object may NOT get the full bean treatment (Aware/init/BPP) unless the FactoryBeanProvider replicates that internally, re-implementing bean-instantiation's sequence inside the Provider (duplication / drift risk).; The Provider owning its own product memo means the product's creation guard, safe-publication, and cancellation/partial-cleanup are RE-IMPLEMENTED per Provider rather than ridden on bean-instantiation's one guarded sequence — every Provider author (or at least the FactoryBeanProvider) must get safe publication and no-async-Drop cleanup right, a contract spread across implementers (the same coherence cost R3 of bean-instantiation flagged).; `provide` returning a BoxFuture at a dyn boundary is mandatory (origin-agnosticism requires dyn; AFIT isn't dyn-safe), so EVERY product realization pays a boxed-future alloc — fine for singletons (cold) but a real per-resolution cost for prototype/scoped products on hot paths (§2.5 minimal-boxing tension), and now it's the universal path, not an opt-in.; The factory's own dependencies must be resolved when the FactoryBeanProvider realizes the factory — so a Provider's `provide` re-enters resolution to build the factory (and the factory's deps), the same mutual-recursion-across-await as R1, now inside an opaque Provider where the lock-ordering reasoning is harder to see.; Resolution MODE (Product vs Provider) threaded through the resolve API widens every lookup signature with a mode the 99% case ignores — minor DX tax, and `get_factory` must downcast the Provider to expose its inner factory, a dyn-to-concrete step that's awkward when the Provider is itself erased.

**Mechanisms considered:** universal origin-agnostic `Provider` trait (product_type + async provide → ErasedBean) shared with bean-registry/bean-instantiation; native/FactoryBean/WASM/test-double are all impls; FactoryBean as a `FactoryBeanProvider` adapter: owns the factory's construction recipe internally, realizes the factory once (internal OnceCell guard), delegates `provide` to `factory.create`; one descriptor per FactoryBean (no second slot); name → ProductProvider; registry caches the Provider, the Provider caches its product (singleton ⇒ memo cell, prototype ⇒ no memo); `&` reframed as a resolution MODE (Product default vs Provider) + typed `get_factory::<F>` that downcasts the Provider to expose its inner factory; non-factory Providers error on Provider mode; type-match-without-realization via Provider `product_type()`/`supports_type()` answering candidate-resolver without calling provide; multi-type via provide_as(t); eager-gating generalized: eager pass makes every Provider available; calls provide eagerly only if the Provider self-reports eager (Smart+eager-init)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for the cross-crate layered model AND the two-source duality (cross-crate doc): a static link-time registry contributes native + FactoryBean Providers; an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM source contributes WASM-bridge Providers — ALL the same Provider stream feeding one resolution path, so the WASM 'classpath' is the most natural slot-in of the three (CANDIDATES, not a decision). ONE descriptor per FactoryBean (vs R1's two) keeps the link-time registry flatter and the anti-DCE self-check simpler (count Providers; a missing FactoryBean is one missing Provider, named, at boot). leaf-core hosts the `Provider` trait + `Descriptor`; the thin macro emits a descriptor whose factory builds the FactoryBeanProvider via absolute paths (§89-102), zero logic. The product TypeKey on the descriptor (for type-match-without-realize) is what a build.rs/codegen path (Arch C) emits when the product type is monomorphic. GENERICS clash: a generic FactoryBean has no `'static` Provider until instantiated → macro hard-errors (§349) with a per-instantiation registration escape hatch. The cost vs R1: making Provider a universal, ultra-stable cross-workspace ABI (every emitted descriptor hard-codes paths to it) WIDENS the stable-ABI commitment — a real T1 weight, since the whole workspace depends on the Provider signature never breaking. No factory→product link field needed (the link is internal to the Provider), which is simpler cross-crate than R1.

**T2 — execution context:** The FactoryBeanProvider is registered/available with no async at link/registry build. Realizing the FACTORY (building the factory object + its deps) happens lazily on first `provide` (or eagerly in the eager pass) on the bootstrap task — an `.await`; a blocking factory build uses spawn_blocking (§2.3). The product `provide().await` is the execution-context-critical site and MUST NOT run under a held global/creation guard (dissolving Spring's getObject-under-singleton-lock deadlock trap): the Provider's internal singleton memo guards only the COMMIT, while `factory.create().await` runs outside the guard, the re-entrant case reconciled by the memo's double-check. SINGLETON product: `create` awaited at most once, memoized with safe publication (§concurrency-contract). PROTOTYPE/SCOPED product: `provide` re-runs `create` on the resolving task (a hot path for prototype products) with NO memo — author owns thread-safety; may `.await`, blocking impls spawn_blocking. Because the product memo is INSIDE the Provider, safe publication is the Provider's responsibility (a coherence cost). CANCELLATION: cancelling an in-flight `provide` drops the partial product; with no async Drop, any async-cleanup resource the half-built product already holds must be surfaced to the Provider's registered shutdown path, not left to sync Drop. ERRORS: a failed `provide` is a rich ResolveError naming the Provider/factory, requested TypeKey, and chain (§2.9); `mode=Provider` on a non-factory Provider is a distinct typed error. TEARDOWN: each Provider registers its shutdown with context-close; a FactoryBeanProvider's shutdown disposes the factory (await) and optionally the product per the orphan-vs-delegate Phase-2 policy — orphaned async-cleanup product = leak with no async Drop.

#### R3 — Indirection-as-metadata: NO factory trait at runtime — a FactoryBean is just a normal bean whose definition carries an `indirection` recipe (a typed async builder closure + product TypeKey + eager/cache policy); resolution is data-driven, the `&` handle is the bean itself
Question whether FactoryBean needs a runtime trait or a second citizen AT ALL. Spring needs `FactoryBean` as a runtime interface because it discovers factory-ness by `instanceof` after constructing the bean and dispatches dynamically. leaf's intent (annotation-free programmatic construction with name→product indirection) can be served by METADATA on the bean-definition instead: a bean is marked as 'producing' another type, and the relationship is DATA the resolver reads — not a trait it dynamic-dispatches. The user writes a plain bean (a struct with whatever injected deps/lifecycle it wants — it gets the FULL bean treatment, Aware/init/BPP, because it IS a normal bean) and annotates a method `#[produces]`/`#[factory] async fn build(&self, cx) -> Product`. The thin macro reads this and emits, on the bean's definition (bean-definition owns it), an `IndirectionRecipe { product_type: TypeKey, build: const fn-ptr that erases &Self + ResolveCtx → BoxFuture<ErasedProduct>, cache_policy: Singleton|Prototype|Scoped, eager: bool, multi_type: &[(TypeKey, build_as_ptr)] }`. There is NO `FactoryBean` trait object at runtime — `build` is a free-standing erased async fn pointer the macro derives from the annotated method (thin-macro §2.10: it emits exactly the closure the user could have written). bean-registry/candidate-resolver, on encountering a definition with an `IndirectionRecipe`, treat the PRODUCT TypeKey as the autowiring-visible type for ordinary `get<T>()`/injection (type-match-without-realize is reading `recipe.product_type` — no realization, no trait call), and on resolve, build the underlying bean (full sequence) then run `recipe.build(&bean, cx)` to get the product, caching the product per `recipe.cache_policy`. The `&` escape hatch is the most rust-native of the three: the underlying bean is STILL directly resolvable by its concrete type `get::<TheFactoryStruct>()` (it's a normal bean), so 'the factory' needs no special handle — `&N` becomes 'ask for the factory's own type' and 'N' (the public product name) becomes 'ask for the product type'; the awkwardness is that you must name the concrete factory type, which is exactly the discouragement intent. Separate caching: the product is cached under the recipe's policy in a product-cache keyed by (definition, product TypeKey) — bean-registry's cache with a compound key, or a per-definition memo. Eager-gating: the eager pass builds the bean always (it's eager), runs `recipe.build` eagerly only if `recipe.eager` (Smart+eager-init analogue) else lazy. Multi-type SmartFactoryBean: the recipe's `multi_type` table lists (TypeKey, build_as_ptr) pairs the macro emits from multiple annotated methods — candidate-resolver consults the table; type-match-without-realize stays pure metadata. Teardown: the bean's normal DisposableBean shutdown disposes the factory; product teardown rides an optional `#[produces(dispose_with = ...)]` recipe field (disciplined, divergent from Spring's orphan) or stays orphaned.

**Pros:** Most rust-native and the THINNEST runtime surface: NO `FactoryBean` trait, NO trait-object dispatch, NO second registry citizen — just metadata (an `IndirectionRecipe`) on an otherwise-normal bean-definition that the resolver reads, so the macro stays maximally thin (§2.10, emits exactly the erased build closure the user could write) and `cargo expand` shows a plain bean + a plain async fn.; The factory IS a normal bean and gets the FULL bean treatment (injected deps, Aware, init/@PostConstruct, BPP/post-processing) — fixing R2's biggest weakness where the factory was hidden inside a Provider and risked NOT getting the full lifecycle; here the factory is a first-class bean and the product is its declared output.; The `&` escape hatch needs ZERO new mechanism: the factory is resolvable by its concrete type `get::<TheFactoryStruct>()` while the public product name resolves to the product — the 'deliberately awkward dereference' is naturally expressed as 'you must name the concrete factory type to reach it,' the most idiomatic-Rust reading of Spring's discourage-dereference intent.; Type-match-without-realization is PURE metadata (`recipe.product_type` / the multi_type table) read by candidate-resolver with no trait call and no realization — the strongest, cheapest realization of Spring's getObjectType-without-getObject, and it's exactly the kind of static metadata a build.rs/codegen path (Arch C) can emit and check.; Best AOT-lineage fit (§3 compile-vs-runtime): the IndirectionRecipe is flat const data (TypeKeys + fn-pointers + policy flags) — a build-time pass could emit and validate it (product type known, eager policy known) with no runtime trait machinery, aligning with the compile-vs-runtime keystone better than a runtime trait (R1/R2).; Eager-gating and multi-type are recipe FIELDS (a bool, a table) the eager pass and candidate-resolver read — no trait methods to dispatch — so SmartFactoryBean's eager/multi-type extensions are data, not a trait hierarchy, keeping the common single-type case zero-overhead.

**Cons:** Loses runtime polymorphism Spring's `FactoryBean` interface gives: a recipe is FIXED at the definition (emitted by the macro from annotations), so a bean that DECIDES at runtime whether/what it produces (a factory whose product type or even factory-ness depends on injected config) is awkward — Spring's `getObjectType()` can return a runtime-computed type and `isSingleton()` can be dynamic; a static recipe can't, so genuinely-dynamic factories (some of Spring's 50+, e.g. JNDI-driven) may need an escape hatch back to a runtime trait (re-introducing R1/R2's trait for the dynamic minority).; The erased `build` fn-pointer takes `&Self` (the factory bean) but the recipe is type-erased on the definition — so calling `recipe.build` requires the resolver to hand the erased factory bean back to a fn-pointer that re-asserts its concrete type (a downcast or a monomorphized shim the macro emits); getting this erase/re-assert seam sound and zero-cost is the subtle part, and it's where the macro carries the most (still hand-writable) glue.; Multi-type via a `multi_type` table emitted from multiple annotated methods is clean for a known set but can't express Spring v7's `supports_type(Class)` over an OPEN/dynamic set (a factory that supports any subtype computed at runtime) — same dynamic-factory limitation, narrower than R1/R2's runtime `supports_type`.; 'A bean produces another type' as definition metadata may confuse bean-naming/candidate-resolver: the definition's OWN type (the factory struct) AND its product TypeKey are both autowiring-relevant (the factory by concrete type for the `&` hatch, the product by the public name) — so one definition advertises TWO types to candidate-resolver with different visibility rules (product = public/by-name, factory = by-concrete-type-only), a dual-visibility the resolver must encode.; Product caching keyed by (definition, product TypeKey) is a compound-key cache distinct from the singleton cache — either bean-registry grows a second keying scheme or the recipe carries a per-definition memo, a caching-shape decision that leans on bean-registry's substrate (Phase-2 territory).; Still inherits the no-async-Drop orphaned-product hazard and the build-re-enters-resolution recursion (building the factory bean's deps), and the dispose_with recipe field (disciplined product teardown) is a divergence from Spring's orphan model that couples product teardown to the recipe — a real but unforced lifecycle decision.

**Mechanisms considered:** NO runtime FactoryBean trait — an `IndirectionRecipe` (product TypeKey + erased async build fn-ptr + cache_policy + eager flag + multi_type table) as metadata on a normal bean-definition; `#[factory]`/`#[produces] async fn build(&self, cx) -> Product` on a plain bean; thin macro derives the erased build closure (exactly hand-writable code) and the recipe const data; factory is a first-class normal bean (full Aware/init/BPP treatment); `&` escape hatch = resolve the factory's concrete type `get::<FactoryStruct>()`; public name resolves to product; type-match-without-realize = candidate-resolver reads `recipe.product_type` / multi_type table — pure metadata, no trait call, no realization; product cached per `recipe.cache_policy` under a compound (definition, product TypeKey) key or a per-definition memo; eager-gating via `recipe.eager` flag read by the eager pass; multi-type via a `multi_type` table of (TypeKey, build_as_ptr) from multiple annotated methods; runtime-dynamic factories fall back to an R1/R2-style trait escape hatch

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Strongest AOT/build-time fit: the IndirectionRecipe is flat const data (TypeKeys + fn-pointers + flags) — exactly what a build.rs/codegen path (Arch C) can EMIT and statically VALIDATE (product type present, eager policy coherent), and what the link-time registry (Arch A/B) carries as one descriptor per factory bean (no second slot, like R2). The thin macro emits the recipe via absolute paths (§89-102) holding zero logic — the erased build fn-pointer is the only emitted glue and it's hand-writable. The anti-DCE self-check (§152) counts factory beans like any bean; a missing one (silent DCE) surfaces as a missing definition with its declared product type, named, at boot — and because the product TypeKey is metadata, the self-check can even assert 'expected a producer of type T, found none' (richer than a bare count). GENERICS clash: a generic factory bean OR a generic product has no `'static` TypeKey/fn-pointer until instantiated → macro hard-errors (§349) with a per-instantiation `register_factory!(ConcreteFactory)` escape hatch emitting a concrete recipe. The build fn-pointer erasing `&Self` must re-assert the concrete type via a macro-emitted monomorphized shim (the erase/re-assert seam) — purely local to the contributing crate, no cross-crate ABI beyond the descriptor's recipe fields. WASM 'classpath': a WASM-sourced factory contributes a recipe whose build fn-pointer dispatches into the module — same descriptor stream the resolver reads (CANDIDATES: static link-time registry default + optional dynamic directory-scanned source, not a decision). Smallest stable-ABI surface of the three (no Provider trait to freeze — just the Descriptor/recipe data shape).

**T2 — execution context:** The factory bean is built on the bootstrap task during the eager pass (cold path) via bean-instantiation's normal sequence — it's a normal bean, so its construct→populate→init→publish (incl. async @PostConstruct-analogue) runs exactly as any bean, NOT under a held creation guard across user init (dissolving the @PostConstruct-under-lock trap §1972). The product `recipe.build(&factory, cx).await` is the execution-context-critical site: it MUST NOT run under a held guard across the user build `.await` (Spring's getObject-under-singleton-lock deadlock dissolved); only the product-cache COMMIT is guarded, the re-entrant-during-build case reconciled by a double-checked product-cache read. SINGLETON product: `build` awaited at most once (cold, first resolve or eager), published with safe publication (§concurrency-contract). PROTOTYPE/SCOPED product: `build` re-runs on the resolving task (hot path for prototype products) per `recipe.cache_policy`, NO guard — the factory author owns `build` thread-safety; `build` may `.await` (DB/pool), blocking impls spawn_blocking (§2.3). CANCELLATION: cancelling an in-flight `build` drops the partial product; with no async Drop, an async-cleanup resource the half-built product already holds must be surfaced to the factory bean's registered shutdown (or the dispose_with recipe path), not left to sync Drop. ERRORS: a failed `build` is a rich ResolveError naming the factory bean, requested product TypeKey, and chain (§2.9); reaching the factory by `get::<FactoryStruct>()` when no such bean exists is an ordinary no-such-bean error (no special dereference error needed). TEARDOWN: the factory bean's normal `DisposableBean::shutdown().await` runs in reverse order via context-close (it's a normal bean); the product's teardown rides the optional `dispose_with` recipe (disciplined) or stays orphaned (Spring-faithful) — orphaned async-cleanup product = leak with no async Drop, the unforced Phase-2 lifecycle decision.

### Rust clashes
- The `&`-string-prefix dereference sigil is a JVM string-name trick with no rust-native equivalent — and shouldn't have one. Spring overloads ONE string namespace: `getBean("N")` ⇒ product, `getBean("&N")` ⇒ factory, with `transformedBeanName` stripping `&`+aliases so both share a canonical name, and a runtime `BeanIsNotAFactoryException` if you dereference a non-factory. In Rust, typed lookup makes this awkward-by-string indirection unnecessary and worse: leaf should express the dereference as a TYPED handle (`Factory<T>`/`get_factory::<F>`, R1), a resolution MODE (Product vs Provider, R2), or simply naming the concrete factory type (`get::<FactoryStruct>()`, R3) — turning Spring's runtime sigil-parse + runtime not-a-factory error into a compile-time/typed-lookup affordance. The intent (a name denotes the product; reaching the factory is deliberately awkward) is preserved; the mechanism (string-prefix overloading) is dropped.
- getObject()-under-the-singleton-lock is a deadlock trap that becomes an EXECUTOR-blocking hazard in async Rust — and FactoryBean is explicitly called out as sharing this trap (§1972: 'a singleton FactoryBean is processed within the general singleton lock as well'). Spring runs `getObject()` under both the registry singleton lock AND a defensive `synchronized(factory)` monitor; a heavy or re-entrant `getObject` deadlocks exactly like a heavy @PostConstruct. In async Rust the analogue is worse: a product `create().await` under a held creation guard ties up an executor worker and can deadlock the pool if the awaited work re-enters resolution for the same guard. leaf must NOT hold any guard across the user `create`/`build` `.await` — guard only the product-cache COMMIT, run user construction outside the guard, and reconcile the re-entrant-during-create case via a double-checked product-cache read (the rust-native dissolution of the trap, mirroring bean-instantiation's no-guard-across-init rule).
- The defensive `synchronized(factory)` (a SECOND monitor specifically for non-thread-safe getObject called concurrently from a background thread and the main thread) is a JVM intrinsic-lock pattern that clashes with §2.5 (no global locks, minimal Mutex). Spring adds it precisely because background bootstrapping can call the same `getObject()` concurrently with the main thread. In leaf's no-global-lock world the realization is: a SINGLETON product's `create` is awaited at most once behind the product slot's same-bean creation guard (so concurrent first-creation serializes on the COMMIT, not the user code); a NON-singleton (prototype/scoped) product calls `create` with NO guard, and — exactly as Spring's v7 SmartFactoryBean does by EXCLUDING smart factories from the defensive monitor — the thread-safety/reentrancy of repeated `create` becomes the FACTORY AUTHOR's contract, documented, not a framework lock. Re-deriving where the guard applies (guarded singleton commit vs unguarded repeated create) is the subtle concurrency call.
- The orphaned product + no async Drop = a leak hazard with no GC backstop. Spring creates the factory eagerly, the product lazily, caches the product separately, and does NOT call the product's `close()` automatically — the FactoryBean must implement DisposableBean and delegate; GC eventually reclaims a forgotten product. In Rust with container-driven explicit teardown (context-close) and NO async Drop (§86/§273), an orphaned product holding an async-cleanup resource (a pooled DataSource, a spawned task) CANNOT self-clean via Drop — it is a silent leak. leaf must decide whether the product's async teardown rides the factory's registered `shutdown().await` (more disciplined than Spring — couples product lifecycle back to the factory) or stays orphaned (Spring-faithful but leak-prone for async-cleanup products) — either way the eager-gating (factory-always, product-lazy) is reproduced while the teardown ownership is reconciled with context-close. This is the FactoryBean-specific facet of bean-instantiation's no-async-Drop partial-cleanup clash.
- getObjectType()-without-getObject() (type-match-without-realization) maps to STATIC product-type metadata in Rust, but the dynamic case clashes. Spring's `getObjectType()` lets the container autowire-match WITHOUT calling `getObject()`, returning null ⇒ graceful invisibility to autowiring (NOT an error). leaf realizes the common case as a STATIC product TypeKey on the descriptor/recipe (R1/R3) or a Provider `product_type()` (R2) consulted by candidate-resolver with zero realization — strictly cleaner than a runtime call. BUT Spring's `getObjectType()` may return a RUNTIME-COMPUTED type (JNDI/config-driven factories), which a static TypeKey can't express; the rust-native answer for that minority is either an explicit attribute declaring the product type (the `OBJECT_TYPE_ATTRIBUTE`/`setTargetType` analogue) or a runtime-trait escape hatch — and 'unknown product type ⇒ gracefully not an autowiring candidate' must be modeled as an absent/None TypeKey, not an error.
- The factory IS often a full bean (injected deps, lifecycle, post-processing) — so collapsing it into an internal detail (R2's Provider) clashes with giving it the full bean treatment. Spring's FactoryBean is itself a managed bean addressable by `&N` with its own dependencies and DisposableBean. In Rust, R1 (a real factory slot) and R3 (a normal bean carrying a recipe) preserve full bean treatment; R2 (factory hidden inside a Provider) risks the factory NOT getting Aware/init/BPP unless the Provider re-implements bean-instantiation's sequence internally. This is a genuine tension between maximal unification (FactoryBean = just a Provider) and the requirement that the factory be a first-class bean — a Phase-2 unification-vs-fidelity call.
- Building the product RE-ENTERS resolution to build the factory (and the factory's deps) — mutual recursion across `.await`. Whether the product is a derived slot delegating to the factory (R1), a Provider realizing its inner factory (R2), or a recipe running on the resolved factory bean (R3), resolving the product first requires resolving the factory, which requires resolving the factory's dependencies — a recursive async creation graph. This re-enters the same lock-ordering / async-recursion-depth hazard bean-instantiation flags, and FactoryBean GUARANTEES at least one level of it for every factory (the factory→product hop), even for acyclic graphs — so the no-guard-across-user-code discipline is not optional polish but a correctness requirement here.
- SmartFactoryBean's v7 multi-type (`supports_type(Class)` + `<S> getObject(Class)`, one factory exposing Session AND StatelessSession) clashes with static type metadata. A static product TypeKey (or even a fixed multi_type table, R3) handles a KNOWN set of product types, but `supports_type` over an OPEN/runtime-computed set (any subtype the factory can produce) needs a runtime predicate — so the multi-type SmartFactoryBean case partially defeats type-match-without-realization (candidate-resolver must consult a runtime `supports_type` rather than a static TypeKey list). leaf must decide whether to support only a static, macro-declared multi-type set (cheap, AOT-friendly, covers Hibernate's two-type case) or a full runtime `supports_type` predicate (Spring-faithful, costs realization-time type queries) — and `isPrototype()` NOT being the inverse of `isSingleton()` means the product's cache policy (singleton/prototype/scoped/none) is a THIRD axis the recipe/Provider must carry independently of the singleton flag.

### Interactions
- **bean-instantiation** — PRIMARY UPSTREAM. bean-instantiation is the creation DRIVER and eager pass this feature plugs into. It reproduces FactoryBean eager-gating in its eager loop (create the FACTORY always — it's a singleton bean; create the PRODUCT eagerly ONLY if Smart+is_eager_init, else lazy on first resolve). The product's construction is one more invocation of bean-instantiation's construct→populate→init→publish sequence (R1: on the derived product slot; R3: as `recipe.build` after the factory bean is built), and the no-guard-across-user-`create`/`build`-`.await` discipline IS bean-instantiation's no-guard-across-init rule applied to `getObject()` (which §1972 says shares the same lock/trap). The product's same-bean creation guard + double-checked re-entrant read come from bean-instantiation's guard.
- **bean-registry** — PRIMARY UPSTREAM. bean-registry owns the stores. The SEPARATE product cache ('one name, two cached objects') is realized over the registry's singleton cache — R1 as a second registry slot (factory slot + derived product slot, with a factory→product link the registry must model); R2 as the registry caching the Provider while the Provider caches its product (clean ownership split, one slot); R3 as a compound-key (definition, product TypeKey) product cache or a per-definition memo. The double-checked read/create/re-read that preserves singleton identity under a re-entrant `getBean` is the registry's cache access discipline. Whether factory↔product is two slots or one-wrapping-another is a shared shape question.
- **bean-definition** — UPSTREAM. The factory-ness and product metadata live on the definition. R1 emits TWO definitions sharing a canonical name (factory + derived product, with the link as definition data); R3 puts an `IndirectionRecipe` (product TypeKey + erased build fn-ptr + cache_policy + eager flag + multi_type table) on ONE definition; R2 puts a FactoryBeanProvider as the definition's origin-agnostic factory.
