The product TypeKey on the definition is the static-metadata realization of `getObjectType()`-without-`getObject()`, and the `OBJECT_TYPE_ATTRIBUTE`/`setTargetType` analogue (explicit product-type declaration when it can't be deduced) is a definition attribute the macro can set.
- **candidate-resolver** — TIGHTLY COUPLED. Type-match-without-realization is candidate-resolver reading the product TypeKey (R1/R3 metadata, R2 `Provider::product_type()`) so a FactoryBean's PRODUCT is the autowiring-visible candidate WITHOUT realizing it — and an unknown/None product type makes the factory gracefully NOT a candidate (Spring's null-getObjectType invisibility, not an error). SmartFactoryBean multi-type means the resolver consults a SET of product TypeKeys (or a runtime `supports_type` predicate) for one factory — the v7 'supports_type before getObjectType' ordering. candidate-resolver must distinguish the product (matched by type/name, public) from the factory (reachable only by the explicit `&`/typed-handle/concrete-type route).
- **autowiring-resolution** — DOWNSTREAM CONSUMER + the recursion source. Ordinary `get<T>()`/injection resolves to the PRODUCT (the indirection's whole point) — autowiring never sees the factory unless the injection point asks for the factory's type/handle explicitly. Resolving the product re-enters autowiring-resolution to build the factory and the factory's own dependencies (the factory→product hop guarantees at least one recursion level per FactoryBean), making creation mutually recursive across `.await` — the documented hazard. The `&` escape-hatch injection (a `Factory<T>` handle or the concrete factory type) is a distinct injection point autowiring must route to the factory, not the product.
- **scopes** — TIGHTLY COUPLED. SmartFactoryBean's `isPrototype()` NOT being the inverse of `isSingleton()` means the PRODUCT's multiplicity is an independent axis: a product can be singleton (cached once), prototype (re-`create` each resolve, hand-off-and-forget), or scoped (per-scope-key) — orthogonal to the FACTORY's own scope (the factory is virtually always a singleton). scopes fixes the product's cache/teardown policy (R3's `recipe.cache_policy`; R2's per-Provider memo policy); this feature supplies the construction recipe and honors whatever multiplicity scopes assigns to the product.
- **lazy-initialization** — DIRECT. Eager-gating IS lazy semantics on the PRODUCT: the factory is eager, the product is lazy UNLESS Smart+is_eager_init — so a plain FactoryBean's product is registered/visible-by-type but NOT built during the eager pass, its construction relocated to first resolve. lazy-initialization defines the per-bean lazy gate the eager pass reads; this feature sets the product's lazy flag from the factory's eager-init policy.
- **deferral-primitives** — NEIGHBOUR — shared handle shape. The `&` dereference escape hatch (a typed `Factory<T>` injectable that resolves the factory rather than its product, R1) reuses deferral-primitives' injectable-handle pattern (ObjectProvider-style: a handle that defers/redirects resolution). Conceptually a FactoryBean is the framework-author analogue of what ObjectFactory/ObjectProvider give consumers — both are 'a thing you resolve that yields another thing' — so the handle machinery and the lazy-realization-on-call semantics are shared.
- **configuration-classes** — CONTRAST / boundary-definition. Spring's design intent explicitly contrasts FactoryBean (programmatic, annotation-free, runs early ahead of post-processors, for COMPLEX STATEFUL construction) with `@Bean` factory methods (run inside a fully-initialized container, for ordinary instantiation). leaf's configuration-classes equivalent is the ordinary path; factory-bean is the early/stateful escape hatch. The boundary — when a user should write a config-class factory method vs a FactoryBean — must be as clear in leaf as in Spring, and R3's `#[factory] async fn build` deliberately blurs toward the config-method feel while keeping the indirection/early-construction semantics.
- **context-close** — DOWNSTREAM. The factory's teardown is container-managed (it's a bean): the factory registers its `DisposableBean::shutdown().await` run in reverse dependency order by context-close. The PRODUCT's teardown is the open lifecycle question — orphaned (Spring-faithful, leak-prone with no async Drop) or riding the factory's registered shutdown (disciplined, R3's optional `dispose_with` recipe). context-close defines the stop-then-destroy-in-reverse sequence; this feature decides whether the product enters that ledger.
- **lifecycle-callbacks** — UPSTREAM (for the factory) + CONTRAST (for the product). The factory bean gets the full ordered init chain (Aware → before-init → @PostConstruct/init → after-init) because it IS a normal bean (strongest in R3 where the factory is unambiguously a first-class bean; the risk in R2 is the factory hidden in a Provider may bypass this). The PRODUCT, by contrast, is constructed by the factory's `create`/`build` and does NOT automatically get the container's init chain or destroy callbacks (the orphaned-lifecycle intent) — the factory author owns the product's initialization inside `create`.
- **bean-naming** — COUPLED (R1 especially). The 'one canonical name, factory+product' model means bean-naming must understand the factory↔product name relationship: in R1 the product slot OWNS the public name and the factory slot is shadowed behind the typed handle (two slots, one public name); in R2/R3 there is one citizen so naming stays simple but the factory is also reachable by its concrete TYPE (a second addressing axis). Spring's `transformedBeanName` (strip `&`+aliases to one canonical name) is the JVM mechanism this replaces; bean-naming's collision/alias rules must not treat the factory and product as a name collision.
- **auto-proxy-creator** — NEIGHBOUR / canonical use case. Spring's `ProxyFactoryBean` is THE flagship FactoryBean — a factory whose product is a proxy. leaf's eventual proxying mechanism (auto-proxy-creator) is one consumer/producer of the FactoryBean pattern (a factory whose `create` returns a proxy as the product). This is noted because it validates the origin-agnostic product seam: a proxy product is constructed and published through the identical product-resolution path as a native product — the proxy mechanism plugs in as a factory's output, not a special case.
- **post-processor-spi** — CONTEXT / timing constraint. Spring's design intent stresses that `getObjectType()`/`getObject()` 'may arrive early in the bootstrap process, even ahead of any post-processor setup' — which is WHY FactoryBean is annotation-free and programmatic (it can't rely on annotation-driven injection that post-processors enable). leaf must preserve this: the factory's `create`/`build` contract must be usable BEFORE the full post-processor machinery is active, so it cannot depend on BPP-stage decoration of its inputs — the annotation-free, plain-async-fn nature of the contract honors this timing constraint.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Factory↔product registry SHAPE: is a FactoryBean TWO registry slots sharing a canonical name with a factory→product link (R1, Spring-faithful, doubles definition count, couples bean-naming/listing), ONE Provider that internally owns the factory and the product cache (R2, maximal unification but hides the factory as a registry citizen and risks it not getting full bean treatment), or ONE normal bean-definition carrying an indirection recipe with the product as derived metadata (R3, thinnest runtime, factory is first-class, but loses runtime polymorphism)? This is a registry-substrate shape decision shared with bean-registry and the §3 type-erasure/registry keystone.
- Is FactoryBean a distinct concept at all, or just one impl of a universal origin-agnostic `Provider` (R2)? Dissolving FactoryBean into the Provider substrate maximizes unification (§2.11) and the origin-agnostic mandate (native/FactoryBean/WASM/test-double all Providers, best WASM slot-in) but borders on DECLARING the registry/creation meta-architecture Phase 2 owns, widens the cross-workspace stable ABI (every descriptor hard-codes the Provider signature), and risks the factory not getting full bean lifecycle. Shared with bean-registry and bean-instantiation (the recurring 'one engine vs pluggable-provider-kernel' question).
- The `&` dereference escape-hatch realization: a typed `Factory<T>`/`get_factory::<F>` injectable handle (R1, reuses deferral-primitives), a resolution MODE (Product vs Provider) on the lookup API (R2, widens every signature), or simply naming the concrete factory type `get::<FactoryStruct>()` (R3, zero new mechanism, most idiomatic)? All three drop Spring's `&`-string sigil; the choice trades off DX, the lookup-API surface, and how bean-naming models the dual addressing (public product name vs factory-by-type). Shared with deferral-primitives and bean-naming.
- Product teardown ownership given NO async Drop: orphaned (Spring-faithful, but an async-cleanup product — pooled DataSource, spawned task — is a SILENT leak with no GC backstop) or riding the factory's registered `DisposableBean::shutdown().await` (more disciplined, couples product lifecycle to the factory, R3's optional `dispose_with`)? Spring orphans the product and relies on GC; leaf has neither GC nor async Drop, so leaving this open risks shipping leak-prone factories on the common path. Shared with context-close, bean-instantiation (the no-async-Drop partial-cleanup clash), and scopes.
- Type-match-without-realization for the DYNAMIC case: the common case is a STATIC product TypeKey on the descriptor/recipe (cheap, AOT-friendly, no realization) — but Spring's `getObjectType()` can return a RUNTIME-COMPUTED type and v7 `supports_type(Class)` is an OPEN runtime predicate (one factory, multiple/any product types). Does leaf support only a static macro-declared product-type set (covers Hibernate's two-type case, AOT-checkable) or a full runtime `supports_type` predicate (Spring-faithful, costs realization-time type queries and partially defeats no-realize matching)? Shared with candidate-resolver and the §3 compile-vs-runtime split.
- Product CACHE substrate and keying: 'one name, two cached objects' as a second registry slot (R1), a memo INSIDE the Provider (R2), or a compound (definition, product TypeKey) key / per-definition memo (R3) — and how the double-checked re-entrant-during-`create` identity guarantee is realized over leaf's chosen singleton-cache substrate without a global lock. The product's cache POLICY is also a third axis (singleton/prototype/scoped/none) independent of the factory's scope, since `isPrototype()` is not the inverse of `isSingleton()`. Shared with bean-registry and scopes.
- Where the product `create`/`build` guard boundary sits in the no-global-lock world: a SINGLETON product's create must be awaited at most once (guard the COMMIT, not the user `.await`) while a NON-singleton product re-runs create with NO guard (factory author owns thread-safety, exactly Spring v7 excluding SmartFactoryBeans from the defensive monitor). Getting 'guarded singleton commit vs unguarded repeated create' right — and dissolving Spring's getObject-under-singleton-lock trap rather than inheriting it — is a concurrency call shared with bean-instantiation (its creation-guard substrate and no-guard-across-init rule).
- Does leaf support genuinely-DYNAMIC factories (factory-ness, product type, or singleton-ness decided at runtime from injected config — some of Spring's 50+, e.g. JNDI-driven)? R3's static recipe can't express them and would need a runtime-trait escape hatch (re-introducing R1/R2's trait for the dynamic minority); R1/R2's runtime trait handles them natively at the cost of static metadata. Whether to optimize the common static case and offer a dynamic escape hatch, or carry a runtime trait throughout, is a Phase-2 fidelity-vs-AOT call. Shared with the §3 compile-vs-runtime split and configuration-classes.
- Annotation-free / early-availability contract vs leaf's post-processor and async-init machinery: Spring's FactoryBean must work AHEAD of post-processor setup (so it can't rely on annotation-driven injection of its inputs). In leaf's async-first world, what does 'early, before post-processors' mean for a factory whose `create` is async and may need injected collaborators — and how does this reconcile with the refresh ordering where post-processors register before the eager pass? Shared with post-processor-spi, refresh-lifecycle, and the async-execution model.
- The two-source duality for FactoryBean products: the static link-time registry (default 'classpath') AND an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source both contribute factories/products to the SAME resolution path the eager pass and candidate-resolver walk — most natural in R2 (a WASM factory is just another Provider), needing a factory→product link field in R1's descriptor, a recipe-with-module-dispatching-build-ptr in R3. How a dynamically-sourced factory's product type is advertised for type-match-without-realize (when the WASM module's product type isn't known statically) is unresolved (a CANDIDATE, not a decision). Shared with the whole discovery/SPI family and bean-instantiation.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the precise feature section — `FactoryBean and SmartFactoryBean indirection` (2541-2557): name→product indirection with the `&` dereference sigil and shared canonical name (2543); the design-intent mental model 'a name denotes a usable collaborator; the factory is a construction detail consumers must not couple to; dereferencing is deliberately awkward' + the annotation-free programmatic contract that runs early ahead of post-processors, for complex stateful construction (2545); the separate `factoryBeanObjectCache` (one name, two cached objects) with the double-checked read/create/re-read preserving singleton identity (2547); the two-locks story (singletonLock + defensive synchronized(factory)) and the ORPHANED product lifecycle (container manages only the factory; product close() not auto-called; NullBean for null) (2549-2551); getObjectType()-without-getObject() type matching, null⇒graceful autowiring invisibility, OBJECT_TYPE_ATTRIBUTE/setTargetType for static product-type declaration (2553); the eager-gating (factory always, product eager only if Smart+isEagerInit; isPrototype not the inverse of isSingleton) (2555); and the v7 changes — supports_type(Class)+getObject(Class) for multi-type injection, isTypeMatch consulting supports_type before getObjectType, SmartFactoryBeans EXCLUDED from both the product cache and the defensive monitor (2557). I also read the adjacent 'FactoryBean.getObject() shares the same lock' note (1972-1974) tying it to the @PostConstruct-under-lock deadlock trap.

I grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc (thin-macro/absolute-path ABI §89-102, the §167 `&'static Descriptor` of fn-pointers shape that erases generics, generics-cannot-register §349 + per-instantiation register-shim, the mandatory anti-DCE self-check §152, linkme-default §166, the four Architectures A/B/C/D §277-331 with C as the AOT/build-time fit and the two-source duality, the donsz.nl scope-not-implicitly-global warning §362) and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimal-Arc/boxing, §2.9 rich-early-diagnostics, §2.10 thin-macros, §2.11 unification) and the stable envelope (RPITIT/AFIT NOT dyn-safe forcing boxed-future factories at the dyn boundary §160-176, trait upcasting 1.86 for the Arc<dyn> supertype-injection §118-126, const TypeId 1.91 + Arc<dyn Any> registry §16-17/§81, NO async Drop as a first-class constraint §86/§273-281 with explicit container-driven shutdown the only honest answer).

The three realizations diverge on WHERE the indirection lives: R1 = the factory and product are TWO registry slots sharing a canonical name (Spring-faithful one-name-two-objects, product is the default resolve, factory reached by a typed `Factory<T>` handle replacing the `&` sigil — highest fidelity, reuses bean-instantiation/bean-registry cleanly, but doubles definitions and bakes a factory→product recursion into every FactoryBean); R2 = FactoryBean is NOT special — it's one impl of a universal origin-agnostic `Provider`, the name maps to a product-Provider that internally owns the factory, `&` is a resolution MODE (maximal unification + best WASM slot-in, but borders on declaring the registry meta-architecture and risks the factory not getting full bean treatment); R3 = indirection-as-metadata — NO runtime FactoryBean trait, just an `IndirectionRecipe` (product TypeKey + erased async build fn-ptr + cache/eager/multi-type policy) on an otherwise-NORMAL bean, the `&` hatch is simply naming the concrete factory type (thinnest runtime, factory is unambiguously first-class, best AOT fit, but loses runtime polymorphism for genuinely-dynamic factories). R1↔R2 differ on two-slots vs one-Provider-wrapping; R3 differs on runtime-trait vs pure-metadata. They're genuinely divergent yet Phase-2-composable (R2's Provider could host R1's two-slot or R3's recipe internally).

Three things to carry forward: (1) The single most load-bearing rust-native move is replacing the `&`-string-prefix SIGIL with a TYPED dereference (handle, mode, or concrete-type lookup) — Spring's string-namespace overloading is a JVM trick Rust's typed lookup makes both unnecessary and worse, and dropping it turns a runtime not-a-factory error into a compile/typed-lookup affordance while preserving the 'deliberately awkward dereference' intent. (2) getObject()-under-the-singleton-lock is EXPLICITLY a shared deadlock trap (§1972) that in async Rust becomes an executor-blocking hazard — leaf must guard only the product-cache COMMIT and run the user `create`/`build` `.await` OUTSIDE any guard, with singleton-product create awaited once (guarded commit) but NON-singleton product create unguarded (factory author owns thread-safety, exactly as v7 excludes SmartFactoryBeans from the defensive monitor). (3) The ORPHANED product + NO async Drop is a genuine leak hazard with no GC backstop — Spring forgets the product and relies on GC; leaf has neither, so the open decision (orphan = Spring-faithful but leak-prone vs ride-the-factory's-shutdown = disciplined but couples lifecycles) is a real Phase-2 call surfaced as a phase2Question shared with context-close.

I refused to pick the meta-architecture: every realization keeps the product's construction recipe an origin-agnostic boxed-future seam (WASM-module/test-double ready), keeps the product TypeKey abstract for type-match-without-realize, keeps the factory↔product registry shape and the singleton-handle type open, and is compatible with cross-crate Arch A/B/C/D. The two-source duality (static link-time registry + optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM source) is captured as multiple producers of the same factory/product descriptor stream — most natural in R2 — and flagged as a CANDIDATE not a decision. A boundary note vs configuration-classes: FactoryBean is the EARLY/complex-stateful/annotation-free escape hatch, config-class factory methods are the ordinary in-a-fully-initialized-container path — that contrast (Spring's explicit framing) must stay as clear in leaf. Naming ('FactoryBean', 'SmartFactoryBean', 'Provider', 'FactoryBeanProvider', 'IndirectionRecipe', 'ProductProvider', 'Factory<T>', 'ProductType'/'TypeKey', 'ErasedProduct', 'ResolveCtx', '#[factory]'/'#[produces]') is Phase-1 placeholder. I wrote no files. Relevant grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (2541-2557 the FactoryBean/SmartFactoryBean section; 1972-1974 getObject-shares-the-lock; 1978-1984 the strict→lenient/thread-aware singleton locking context); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2 esp. 2.3/2.4/2.5/2.9/2.10/2.11, deferred keystones §3, topology §6); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (thin-macro/absolute-path ABI §89-102, descriptor-of-fn-pointers shape §167, generics-cannot-register §349, anti-DCE self-check §152, Architectures A/B/C/D §277-337, donsz.nl scope-not-global §362, hard gotchas §341-357); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (RPITIT/AFIT-not-dyn-safe forcing boxed-future factories §160-176/§258-261, trait upcasting 1.86 §118-126, const TypeId 1.91 + Arc<dyn Any> §16-17/§81, NO async Drop first-class constraint §86/§273-281).

## Bean Scopes & Scope SPI  `[scopes]`

Spring's scope model answers ONE question per bean definition: how many instances exist and how long does each live — and the reference itself frames it as the *primary concurrency design lever* ("stateless → singleton, stateful → prototype/request"), pushing mutable per-interaction state into a narrower scope so the container can mechanically honor it instead of asking developers to reason about locks (charter §2.5 / concurrency-contract). Built-ins: SINGLETON (one instance per container per definition — NOT GoF per-classloader — eagerly pre-instantiated at refresh, safely published, destruction callbacks DO run at context-close in reverse dependency order); PROTOTYPE (a fresh fully-initialized instance per get()/injection-point resolution, after which the container HANDS OFF AND FORGETS — init callbacks run but destruction callbacks NEVER do; the client owns cleanup; tracking prototypes for destruction would be the very memory leak the hand-off contract avoids); and the WEB scopes REQUEST / SESSION / APPLICATION / WEBSOCKET, each bound to an ambient per-execution context (application-scope ≠ container singleton: it is per-ServletContext, shareable across contexts). Custom scopes plug in via the `Scope` SPI — `get(name, objectFactory)`, `remove(name)`, `registerDestructionCallback(name, cb)`, `resolveContextualObject(key)`, `getConversationId()` — registered on the factory (registerScope / CustomScopeConfigurer); the built-in `singleton`/`prototype` names CANNOT be overridden. Three hidden mechanics drive parity: (1) the singleton-holds-shorter-scope GOTCHA — a shorter-lived bean injected into a longer-lived one is resolved exactly once at the holder's construction and frozen forever, silently defeating the narrower scope; sanctioned fixes are a deferral handle (ObjectProvider/Provider, → deferral-primitives) or a scoped proxy (→ scoped-proxies) that re-resolves the live target per call. (2) Init-runs/destroy-doesn't asymmetry for prototypes, and the requirement that scope-contextual objects (the current request/session) be reachable by custom-scope authors. (3) Scope is metadata ON the definition (bean-definition carries `scope`, child-wins on merge) but the actual instance-storage + lifecycle behavior is a SEPARATE pluggable strategy the engine consults at resolution time — the definition references a scope BY IDENTITY, the registered Scope owns the cache. leaf must replicate this INTENT — a small set of built-in multiplicities/lifetimes plus an origin-agnostic, registerable (NOT implicitly global) scope extension point, with the prototype hand-off-and-forget and the singleton-safe-publication contracts intact — WITHOUT importing Spring's ThreadLocal-bound web-scope mechanism (which silently breaks across `.await` in async Rust) or its string-keyed reflective `get(name, factory)` shape, and WITHOUT a global lock (§2.5).

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Scope as an erased async trait object (`dyn Scope`) keyed by identity in the registry; each scope owns its instance cache + destruction ledger (literal Scope-SPI, rust-retyped)
Mirror Spring's `Scope` SPI directly as a Rust async trait, retyped to eliminate the string-keyed reflective shape. `trait Scope: Send + Sync { async fn get(&self, key: &ScopeKey, factory: &dyn ErasedFactory) -> Result<ScopedInstance, ResolveError>; async fn remove(&self, key: &ScopeKey) -> Option<ScopedInstance>; fn register_destruction(&self, key: &ScopeKey, cb: DestructionCallback); fn resolve_contextual(&self, key: ContextKey) -> Option<ContextualObject>; fn conversation_id(&self) -> Option<ConversationId>; }`. `ScopeKey` is a typed (BeanName + TypeId) pair, not a String; `ErasedFactory` is the ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC erased async constructor handed in from bean-definition's `Factory` (so the same shape serves a native bean, a test double, or a future WASM-module product — the scope never assumes how the instance is built, only that it can `await` one on a cache miss). The registry (bean-registry) holds a `ScopeId -> Arc<dyn Scope>` map; built-in `Singleton` and `Prototype` are themselves `dyn Scope` impls living in leaf-core, registered under reserved identities that registration REFUSES to overwrite (Spring's 'cannot override singleton/prototype'). At resolution (bean-instantiation), the engine reads the definition's `scope` identity, looks up the `dyn Scope`, and calls `get`. SINGLETON's impl is the creation-guarded cache + a destruction ledger drained at context-close; PROTOTYPE's impl ALWAYS builds, caches NOTHING, registers NO destruction callback (hand-off-and-forget falls out structurally — there is simply no ledger entry). Web/custom scopes are user/integration `dyn Scope` impls that resolve their live store via `resolve_contextual` against an ambient context the runtime layer binds. Destruction callbacks are explicit (no async Drop): `DestructionCallback` is an erased async closure the scope invokes on `remove`/scope-end; the engine drives it, never Drop.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity: a one-to-one, teachable mapping of Spring's Scope SPI — the five methods survive with rust types, so a Spring user reads it instantly; custom scopes are 'implement this trait and register it', exactly the SPI promise.; Each scope OWNING its own cache + destruction ledger is the cleanest separation: singleton's safe-publication+destroy and prototype's forget-immediately are two independent impls of one trait, not special-cased branches in the engine — the engine just calls `get` and is scope-agnostic.; Origin-agnostic by construction: `get(key, factory)` takes an erased factory, so the scope never assumes native construction — a WASM-module or dynamic source slots in as just another factory behind the same call; honors the brief's origin-agnostic mandate directly.; Prototype's hand-off-and-forget is enforced by ABSENCE (no ledger entry, no cache write) rather than a flag the engine must remember to check — the no-destroy contract can't be accidentally violated.; `resolve_contextual` cleanly carries the 'expose the current request/session to scope authors' hidden concept, and a custom scope can be added by a downstream crate with zero leaf-core changes (charter §6 pluggability).

**Cons:** `dyn Scope` with `async fn` forces boxed futures (AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-compatible) → an alloc per `get` even for the singleton first-resolution and EVERY prototype/scoped resolution — directly in tension with §2.5 (minimal boxing) and a real cost on the prototype/request hot path (flagged for Phase 2's async-model decision).; An erased `dyn Scope` cache stores `ScopedInstance` as erased `Arc<dyn Any + Send + Sync>` → per-scope downcast on every resolution and Arc churn, where a typed fast path could avoid it for the common singleton case.; Putting singleton's creation-guard inside a `dyn Scope` impl risks re-introducing a coarse lock if done naively; must be fine-grained/per-key (§2.5) and interact correctly with the registry's three-level cache (bean-instantiation) — the boundary between 'scope owns the cache' and 'bean-registry owns the singleton cache' is genuinely ambiguous and must be drawn (who owns the singleton map — the Singleton scope object or the registry?).; Five-method trait is wider than leaf strictly needs for built-ins; `resolve_contextual`/`conversation_id` are only meaningful for context-bound scopes, so built-ins return None — a slightly leaky abstraction (Spring has the same wart with SimpleThreadScope).; Web-scope binding still needs an ambient-context mechanism underneath `resolve_contextual`; the trait abstracts it but does not SOLVE the ThreadLocal-vs-`.await` problem — that lives in the runtime/context-propagation layer this trait merely calls into.

**Mechanisms considered:** async `trait Scope` as a `dyn`-stored trait object keyed by a `ScopeId` in the registry (boxed-future methods to stay dyn-compatible); typed `ScopeKey` (BeanName+TypeId) replacing Spring's String key; `ContextKey` for resolveContextualObject; erased `ErasedFactory` passed into `get` (origin-agnostic construction recipe reused from bean-definition's Factory); built-in Singleton/Prototype as reserved `dyn Scope` impls in leaf-core; registration hard-refuses overwriting reserved identities; `DestructionCallback` as an erased async closure invoked by the engine (no async Drop) — prototype registers none; Scope SPI discovery via explicit registerScope-analogue + optional linkme/inventory descriptor for auto-discovered custom scopes (CANDIDATE substrate, not a decision)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Custom scopes are a textbook discovery/SPI surface, so the brief's duality applies directly: a STATIC link-time registry of `ScopeDescriptor`s (the default 'classpath' — each descriptor a thin const: `{ scope_id, display_name, make: fn() -> Arc<dyn Scope> }`, collected via linkme on server targets / build.rs codegen elsewhere) PLUS an optional explicit `register_scope(id, scope)` programmatic path AND a future dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source contributing scopes through the SAME `dyn Scope`/descriptor shape — all CANDIDATES, not a decision. Critically, scope discovery must NOT be implicitly global (the donsz.nl predictability warning): a transitive dep silently injecting a 'request' scope that rebinds everyone's beans is exactly the surprise to avoid — so custom-scope activation should be opt-in/scoped (analogue of Boot's component-scan basePackages), and the mandatory anti-DCE self-check (cross-crate doc §152) must verify an expected scope-contributing crate actually produced its `ScopeDescriptor` (a silently-DCE'd scope crate = beans resolving under a missing scope = a loud DefinitionError, not a silent wrong-multiplicity bug). `leaf-macros` stays thin: it emits only `::leaf_core::ScopeDescriptor { .. }` absolute-path seeds; the `dyn Scope` impl and the registry live in `leaf-core`/integration crates. Built-in Singleton/Prototype need NO discovery (compiled into leaf-core). Generics: a scope is a concrete `'static` impl so it registers fine; the BEANS under it still cannot be generic-registered (inherited constraint, macro hard-errors).

**T2 — execution context:** Scope METHODS are async (boxed futures): `get` may `await` the factory on a cache miss — the only async/blocking-capable point, and it runs in whatever execution context the resolving caller is in (bean-instantiation's). Singleton-scope `get` runs cold (refresh/first-access) under a FINE-GRAINED per-key creation guard (never a global lock §2.5), and its `@PostConstruct`-analogue runs under that guard — the deadlock-trap (Spring's init-under-singleton-lock) must be preserved as a documented hazard with the smart-initializing escape hatch (a post-all-singletons lock-free callback). Prototype `get` runs on the HOT path (every resolution), builds + inits + hands off, registers NO destruction — so cancellation of a prototype `get` future simply drops a half-built instance (no ledger, no leak). Web/custom-scope `get` runs on the request hot path and must resolve its live store from an ambient async-context (task-local-style), NOT a ThreadLocal — because a ThreadLocal silently goes stale across `.await`/thread-hops (§2.3/§2.4; this is THE execution-context clash for web scopes); cancellation mid-request must leave the scope store consistent and eventually trigger its destruction callbacks. Destruction callbacks are async and DRIVEN BY THE CONTAINER (no async Drop §nightly-doc): singleton callbacks fire at context-close in reverse dependency order (context-close); request/session callbacks fire at scope-end (request completion) via the runtime layer; prototype callbacks never fire. Errors at any `get` surface as a rich ResolveError carrying the scope identity + bean + what the factory needed (§2.9).

#### R2 — Unified typed `ScopeContext` ambient + enum-dispatched built-ins with a `CustomScope` backend escape; multiplicity = a policy read from an explicit async-carried context
Invert the model: rather than 'scope owns a cache behind a fat trait', make the AMBIENT EXECUTION CONTEXT first-class and the scope a small POLICY over it. A `ScopeContext` is an explicit, async-context-propagated value (a task-local / structured-concurrency-scoped handle — leaf's rust-native answer to Spring's ThreadLocal request binding, surviving `.await` because it rides the async context, charter §2.3/§2.4) that exposes the active 'store' for each context-bound scope: `request_store`, `session_store`, etc., each an interior keyed map of live instances + a teardown ledger, opened/closed by the runtime at request/session boundaries. The scope MODEL itself is a tight enum for the built-ins — `enum Scope { Singleton, Prototype, Request, Session, Application, WebSocket, Custom(CustomScopeId) }` — so the engine STATICALLY dispatches the common cases (no boxed-future-per-get for singleton/prototype; singleton consults the registry's typed singleton cache, prototype builds-and-forgets inline) and only falls into a `dyn CustomScope` backend (a slimmer trait: `async fn get_or_create(&self, ctx: &ScopeContext, key, factory) -> Result<..>; fn on_teardown(&self, ctx, cb);`) for genuinely custom scopes. Built-in web scopes are NOT trait objects — they are enum arms that index into the `ScopeContext`'s typed stores, so they share one teardown/cancellation machine. Custom scopes get the SAME `ScopeContext` (their store can be a slot in it or self-managed) and the same teardown ledger, so 'expose contextual objects to scope authors' becomes 'the CustomScope is handed the live ScopeContext'. Scope mismatch (singleton-holds-shorter) is detected at WIRING time as a typed condition: a longer-lived bean directly depending on a shorter-lived one is a fail-fast diagnostic unless the dep is a deferral handle (deferral-primitives) or a scoped proxy (scoped-proxies) — the gotcha is surfaced loudly rather than silently frozen.

**Pros:** Best on §2.5 overhead: built-in singleton/prototype/web scopes are enum-dispatched with NO boxed future and NO `dyn` downcast per resolution; boxing/dyn cost is confined to the genuinely-custom-scope edge — the hot prototype/request path stays lean, honoring 'low overhead #2' without sacrificing common-case DX.; Makes the async execution-context discipline EXPLICIT and central (§2.3/§2.4): the `ScopeContext` is a visible, propagated value, not a hidden ThreadLocal — so 'what is the current request store, and does it survive this `.await`/spawn?' is answerable by looking at the context, and it composes with context-propagation's capture/restore SPI at thread/task hops.; Unifies all context-bound scopes (request/session/app/websocket/custom) onto ONE teardown + cancellation machine (charter §2.11 unification) — register/fire/cancel destruction callbacks once, reused across every non-singleton context scope, instead of each scope reimplementing a ledger (R1's risk).; Surfaces the singleton-holds-shorter GOTCHA as a fail-fast wiring diagnostic (richest-diagnostic timing §2.9) rather than Spring's silent freeze — a strict DX win over the parity target while preserving the sanctioned escapes (deferral handle / scoped proxy).; Slimmer `CustomScope` trait (the contextual-object concern is subsumed by passing the whole `ScopeContext`) is easier to implement correctly than R1's five-method SPI, while still origin-agnostic (factory passed in).

**Cons:** Enum of built-in scopes is a CLOSED set in leaf-core — adding a new first-class built-in (e.g. a 'tenant' scope) means editing the enum, so custom scopes are slightly second-class (the `Custom` arm) vs R1's uniform trait; a judgment call on how much web/custom symmetry matters.; The `ScopeContext` ambient is a heavy Phase-2-entangled primitive: it presupposes a chosen async-context-propagation mechanism (task-local vs explicit-arg vs a leaf context object) — a deferred keystone (§3, T2) — so R2 leans hardest on an unresolved decision and is the most coupled to it.; Two dispatch paths (enum fast path + `dyn CustomScope` slow path) is more engine machinery and two code paths to keep semantically identical (teardown ordering, cancellation, error shape must match across both) — a coherence burden.; Wiring-time scope-mismatch detection needs the resolver (autowiring-resolution) to know each bean's scope AND the holder's scope at analysis time — couples scopes tightly to resolution and to whether the graph is analyzed at build vs startup (deferred §3); if resolution is purely runtime, 'fail-fast at wiring' degrades to first-resolution.; Putting per-scope stores INTO one `ScopeContext` risks an over-broad shared structure; the request store and session store have different lifetimes/owners (request nested in session), so the context must model nesting/lifetime relationships, not a flat bag — non-trivial.

**Mechanisms considered:** `enum Scope` for built-ins (static dispatch, no boxed future on the hot path) + a `Custom(CustomScopeId)` arm; explicit async-context-propagated `ScopeContext` (task-local / structured-concurrency-scoped) as leaf's rust-native replacement for Spring's ThreadLocal request binding; slim `dyn CustomScope` backend trait (handed the whole ScopeContext, so contextual-object access is implicit) for the open extension edge; one unified teardown ledger + cancellation machine in ScopeContext, shared by all context-bound scopes; fail-fast wiring-time scope-mismatch diagnostic (longer-holds-shorter) with deferral-handle / scoped-proxy as the sanctioned escapes; integration with context-propagation capture/restore at task/thread hops; built-in singleton/prototype bypass ScopeContext entirely

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Built-in scopes need no cross-crate discovery (the enum is in leaf-core), which SHRINKS the silent-DCE surface vs R1 — the most common scopes can never silently vanish. Only `Custom` scopes use the discovery substrate: a `CustomScopeDescriptor` collected via the same static link-time registry (linkme default / build.rs codegen / explicit register — CANDIDATES) and the optional dynamic WASM-module source, all feeding the `Custom(CustomScopeId)` registry. The anti-DCE self-check (cross-crate doc §152) still applies to custom scopes (an expected custom-scope crate producing zero descriptors → loud warning). Because the WEB scopes are built-in here, the request/session machinery ships in leaf-framework and a web integration crate (out of core scope per charter §1) provides only the ScopeContext BINDING (opening a request store on inbound request) — not the scope definitions themselves; this keeps the core web-scope-aware without depending on a web crate (charter §6, deps point inward). Implicit-global hazard is LOWER than R1 (fewer discoverable scopes), but the ScopeContext binding source must still be explicit/opt-in (don't let a transitive dep silently start opening request stores). Macro stays thin (emits only `CustomScopeDescriptor` seeds). Generics-cannot-register inherited for beans; scopes themselves are concrete.

**T2 — execution context:** This realization is fundamentally ABOUT execution context. The `ScopeContext` is the explicit per-execution ambient (§2.3): opened by the runtime/web layer at a request/session/websocket boundary on the serving task, propagated across `.await` because it rides the async context (NOT a ThreadLocal — the whole point: a ThreadLocal would silently detach on the first thread-hop/spawn, §2.4/context-propagation), and CLOSED deterministically at boundary end, which is when its teardown ledger fires (async destruction callbacks, container-driven, no async Drop). Singleton: enum arm hits the typed singleton cache under a fine-grained per-key guard (no global lock §2.5), destruction at context-close in reverse order. Prototype: enum arm builds-inits-hands off inline on the hot path, registers nothing — cancellation drops a half-built value cleanly. Request/session/custom: `get_or_create` may `await` the factory on miss; runs on the request hot path and MUST be reactive/notification-driven, not polling (§2.4); cancellation of the serving task must still run the scope's teardown (structured-concurrency scope-guard ensures the store is torn down even on cancel/panic — the rust-native analogue of try-with-resources that Spring's ThreadLocal binding lacks). Cross-task hops (work handed to an executor, task-execution/async-execution) require context-propagation to capture+restore the ScopeContext or the moved work loses its request store — an explicit seam, never automatic. Errors carry scope identity + boundary state (§2.9).

#### R3 — Scope reduced to two orthogonal primitives: a `Multiplicity` policy + a pluggable `InstanceStore` provider; 'a scope' is just (policy, store-key-source, teardown-ledger)
Question whether 'Scope' should be a single concept at all. Decompose it into the two things it actually decides: (a) MULTIPLICITY — `enum Multiplicity { Once, PerResolution, PerContextKey }` (singleton = Once; prototype = PerResolution with teardown disabled; every context-bound scope = PerContextKey) — and (b) a STORE provider that, given the current ambient, yields a keyed instance cache + a teardown ledger. A `ScopeDef { multiplicity, store: StoreSource, teardown: TeardownPolicy }` is the small, mostly-data descriptor a definition references. The built-ins are just `ScopeDef` constants: `SINGLETON = { Once, ContainerStore, TeardownAtClose }`, `PROTOTYPE = { PerResolution, NoStore, TeardownNever }` (hand-off-and-forget = NoStore + TeardownNever, purely declarative), `REQUEST = { PerContextKey, AmbientStore(RequestKey), TeardownAtKeyEnd }`. A `StoreSource` is an origin-agnostic provider: `fn current_store(&self, ambient: &Ambient) -> Option<&dyn InstanceStore>` where `InstanceStore` is the only real trait — `async fn get_or_init(&self, key, factory) -> Result<..>; fn ledger(&self) -> &TeardownLedger;`. Custom scopes contribute a `StoreSource` (where do my instances live?) and pick a `Multiplicity` + `TeardownPolicy` — they do NOT reimplement a whole SPI, they fill in 'where's my store' and 'how many'. This makes the model maximally DECLARATIVE and unified: singleton/prototype/web/custom differ only in the THREE data fields, and ONE engine path reads them (look up the StoreSource's current store via the ambient; if None and Multiplicity::Once use the container store; if PerResolution skip the store; else key into it). Teardown is one ledger type reused everywhere; prototype's TeardownNever means no ledger writes — the no-destroy contract is a policy value, impossible to get wrong.

**Pros:** Maximal unification (§2.11): singleton, prototype, all web scopes, and custom scopes are ONE engine path differing only in three data fields — no per-scope code, no enum-vs-trait split; the smallest possible mechanism surface, the charter's explicit goal.; Prototype hand-off-and-forget and singleton destroy-at-close become DECLARATIVE policy values (`TeardownNever` / `TeardownAtClose`), not behavioral code — provably correct by construction and trivially testable (a policy table, not branches).; `InstanceStore` is the single narrow trait (one async method + a ledger accessor) — far smaller than R1's five-method SPI; custom-scope authors implement the least possible, and it's origin-agnostic (factory passed to `get_or_init`).; Cleanly separates the TWO Phase-2-entangled concerns: `Multiplicity` is pure policy decidable now; the AMBIENT/StoreSource (where context-bound stores live) is the only part touching the deferred async-context model — so most of scopes is settle-able without waiting on that keystone, isolating the unknown.; Most analyzable/AOT-friendly (the AOT lineage §3): a `ScopeDef` is a flat const, so a build-time pass can know every bean's multiplicity and validate scope-mismatch (longer-holds-shorter) statically; aligns with 'definitions knowable at build time'.

**Cons:** Furthest from Spring's mental model: there is no `Scope` object with five methods — a Spring user porting a custom `Scope` impl must re-decompose it into (Multiplicity, StoreSource, TeardownPolicy), a translation cost and a parity-of-FEEL risk (the SPI shape Spring users know is gone, even if the intent is fully covered).; The decomposition is a BET that all real scopes factor cleanly into these three axes; an exotic scope with coupled multiplicity+store logic (e.g. a scope whose key-cardinality depends on store state) may not, forcing either an awkward StoreSource hack or a fourth axis — the abstraction could leak.; `resolveContextualObject`/`getConversationId` have no obvious home (they're scope-instance methods in Spring); they'd have to hang off `StoreSource` or `Ambient`, slightly diluting the clean three-axis story.; More indirection per resolution (read ScopeDef → resolve StoreSource → get current store → key in) than R2's direct enum arm; on the hot prototype path the `PerResolution`/`NoStore` branch is cheap, but the context-bound path adds a StoreSource lookup that must be made allocation-free (§2.5) to not regress.; Risks being TOO clever — the indirection and the novel vocabulary (Multiplicity/StoreSource/Ambient) cost DX (§2.1) for readers who just wanted 'singleton vs prototype'; the elegance must not bury the common case.

**Mechanisms considered:** `enum Multiplicity { Once, PerResolution, PerContextKey }` as pure decidable-now policy; `ScopeDef { multiplicity, store: StoreSource, teardown: TeardownPolicy }` flat-const descriptor referenced by bean-definition; origin-agnostic `StoreSource` provider yielding the current `&dyn InstanceStore` from an `Ambient` (the deferred async-context handle); single narrow `InstanceStore` trait: `async fn get_or_init(key, factory)` + `fn ledger()` — the only real trait in the model; one reusable `TeardownLedger` + `TeardownPolicy` (Never/AtClose/AtKeyEnd) — prototype = Never = no writes; build-time scope-mismatch validation over flat ScopeDefs (longer-holds-shorter) as an AOT-style check; custom scopes register a (StoreSource, Multiplicity, TeardownPolicy) triple via descriptor (CANDIDATE substrate)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Built-in `ScopeDef` constants live in leaf-core (no discovery, no DCE risk — the lowest-risk of the three for the common scopes). A custom scope contributes a `StoreSource` impl + a `ScopeDef` const via the same static link-time registry (linkme/codegen/explicit — CANDIDATES) plus the optional dynamic WASM-module source; because the contributed unit is mostly DATA (a const ScopeDef + a small StoreSource), it is the friendliest of the three to a const-descriptor / linkme path and to a future `#[global_registration]` opaque-iterator world. The mandatory anti-DCE self-check applies to custom StoreSources (expected-vs-found). Scope discovery stays explicitly NON-global (donsz.nl predictability): a custom scope's StoreSource only activates for definitions that reference its ScopeId, and the activation must be opt-in (no transitive dep silently rebinding multiplicities). Thin macro emits only the const `ScopeDef` seed + a reference to the StoreSource ctor. The web-scope StoreSources (RequestStore source, etc.) ship in leaf-framework but their AMBIENT binding comes from a web integration crate (charter §1/§6 — core stays web-aware without depending on web). Generics-cannot-register inherited for beans; ScopeDefs/StoreSources are concrete.

**T2 — execution context:** `Multiplicity` is pure synchronous policy (read on the resolution path, no async). The only async/blocking point is `InstanceStore::get_or_init`, which `await`s the factory on a miss — run in the resolving caller's context. `Once` (singleton): the ContainerStore's `get_or_init` runs under a fine-grained per-key guard (no global lock §2.5), init under that guard (the deadlock-trap preserved + smart-initializing escape). `PerResolution` (prototype): NO store touched, factory awaited inline on the hot path, ledger never written → cancellation drops a half-built value with nothing to clean up. `PerContextKey` (web/custom): the StoreSource resolves the current `&dyn InstanceStore` from the `Ambient` — which MUST be an async-context-propagated handle (task-local/structured-scope), NOT a ThreadLocal, for the same §2.3/§2.4 reason (a ThreadLocal store reference silently detaches across `.await`/spawn). The store's `get_or_init` may `await` (reactive, not polling, on the request hot path §2.4); cancellation/panic of the serving task must still drain the store's TeardownLedger at key-end (structured-concurrency guard ensures teardown runs even on cancel — the leak-free analogue Spring's ThreadLocal binding lacks). Teardown is container-driven async (no async Drop §nightly-doc), policy-gated: AtClose drains at context-close in reverse dependency order (context-close); AtKeyEnd drains when the context key (request/session) ends; Never (prototype) never drains. Cross-task hops need context-propagation to move the Ambient or the StoreSource finds no current store (explicit seam). Errors carry the ScopeDef + the contextual key + factory expectation (§2.9).

### Rust clashes
- ThreadLocal-bound web scopes vs `.await`/thread-hops: Spring binds request/session scope to the servicing thread via a `ThreadLocal` (RequestContextHolder), exploiting the servlet invariant that a request stays on one thread. In async Rust a task migrates across worker threads at every `.await` and may be split across executors, so a ThreadLocal-backed scope store SILENTLY goes stale or detaches mid-request — the exact failure Spring itself flags for virtual threads. leaf MUST bind context-scoped instances to an async-propagated context (a task-local / structured-concurrency-scoped handle), integrated with context-propagation's capture/restore at every task hop — never a raw ThreadLocal. This is the single deepest execution-context clash for THIS feature (charter §2.3/§2.4).
- No async Drop → destruction callbacks cannot ride RAII: Spring runs @PreDestroy/DisposableBean for singletons at context-close and for request/session beans at scope-end. Rust has no async Drop (a first-class charter constraint), so scoped-instance teardown CANNOT be a Drop impl that awaits — it must be an explicit, container-driven async teardown ledger drained at the scope boundary (context-close, request-end). Prototype's 'never destroyed' contract is the easy case (no ledger). The hard case is request/session: the scope-end teardown must run even on task cancellation or panic, which needs a structured-concurrency scope-guard (the rust-native, leak-free replacement for Spring's try-with-resources ThreadLocal restore) rather than relying on Drop.
- String-keyed reflective Scope SPI vs typed keys + erased factory: Spring's `Scope.get(String name, ObjectFactory<?> objectFactory)` keys by bean-name String and returns `Object`. Rust has no class-by-name/reflection, so the key becomes a typed (BeanName + TypeId) `ScopeKey` and the factory an erased async constructor (boxed-future fn-pointer, because AFIT/RPITIT are not dyn-compatible) reused from bean-definition's origin-agnostic Factory. The retype is faithful to intent but loses the String-bag flexibility — and the erased factory forces a boxed future (alloc per construction), a cost acceptable for singletons but a Phase-2 hot-path concern for prototype/request scopes.
- Ownership/lifetime of scoped instances (THE deferred keystone §3 viewed through scopes): Spring's GC frees scoped instances whenever they become unreachable; Rust must DECIDE the owner. Who owns a request-scoped instance — the request store (dropped at request-end), and is it handed out as `Arc<dyn Trait>`, an owned move, or an arena index? Prototype is 'caller owns the move' cleanly; singleton is 'container owns, shares as Arc/borrow'; but request/session need a store whose lifetime is the scope boundary and whose instances may be referenced by in-flight work — forcing an Arc/handle model and raising the question of dangling references if work outlives the scope. This is unresolvable here and shapes the whole scoped-instance API; surfaced as a phase2Question.
- Singleton-holds-shorter-scope gotcha has NO silent-freeze option in safe Rust the way Spring has: Spring silently resolves the shorter-lived dependency once and freezes it (a latent bug). In Rust, a singleton holding a direct `Arc<RequestScopedBean>` is not just wrong but often un-typeable (the request bean has no stable identity outside a request) — so the mismatch tends to surface as a wiring error or forces a deferral handle / scoped-proxy. This is arguably a Rust ADVANTAGE (the footgun is harder to fire), but it means leaf must make the deferral-handle (deferral-primitives) and scoped-proxy (scoped-proxies) paths first-class and ergonomic, since the 'just inject it directly' shortcut Spring tolerates is unavailable.
- Custom-scope discovery must not be implicitly global (the donsz.nl predictability constraint): Spring's `registerScope` is an explicit factory call, but a leaf link-time/auto-discovery substrate could let a transitive dependency silently inject a scope that rebinds multiplicities across the app — a surprise/predictability hazard the cross-crate doc explicitly warns against. So scope discovery, even when riding linkme/inventory, must be opt-in/scoped (the analogue of Boot's component-scan basePackages + exclusions), and the anti-DCE self-check must catch a scope-contributing crate that silently produced nothing — otherwise beans resolve under a missing scope with no error.
- Enum-of-built-in-scopes vs open trait is a closed-vs-open tension with no free answer: a tight enum (R2/R3) gives static dispatch + no boxed-future on the hot path (honoring §2.5) but makes new first-class scopes a core edit; a uniform `dyn Scope` trait (R1) is open and Spring-faithful but pays a boxed future + downcast per resolution. The web scopes are 'built-in' in Spring yet plug through the SAME SPI as custom — leaf cannot have it both ways for free, and the resolution interacts with the deferred async-execution-model decision (boxed-future Scope vs monomorphized fast path).

### Interactions
- **bean-definition** — UPSTREAM. `scope` is a metadata field ON the definition (carried per the asymmetric merge rule: child-wins on scope). The definition references a scope BY IDENTITY (an enum value or a ScopeId), NOT the scope's behavior — this feature defines what those identities MEAN (multiplicity/lifetime) and the engine consults the registered scope at resolution. The definition's origin-agnostic Factory is the construction recipe every scope's get/get_or_init awaits.
- **bean-instantiation** — PRIMARY CONSUMER. The instantiation engine reads the definition's scope and routes creation through it: singleton → the creation-guarded three-level cache (this feature defines that singleton means once+cached+published); prototype → build-init-handoff-forget; context scopes → the scope's store. The three-level cache and creation lock LIVE in bean-instantiation; scopes defines the per-scope policy that cache obeys. The ownership boundary (does the Singleton scope own the cache or does bean-instantiation?) is a phase2Question.
- **bean-registry** — Stores the scope registry (ScopeId → scope impl/descriptor) alongside definitions, owns the singleton cache, and enforces 'cannot override singleton/prototype' at scope registration (the reserved-identity rule). The register-scope-analogue and any custom-scope discovery results land here.
- **scoped-proxies** — TIGHTEST SIBLING. A scoped proxy is THE sanctioned fix for the singleton-holds-shorter gotcha this feature defines: it holds a stable handle in the longer-lived bean and re-resolves the live shorter-lived target per call via THIS feature's scope-resolution path. scopes defines the multiplicity/lifetime + the resolution entry point; scoped-proxies defines the per-call indirection that uses it. The proxyMode metadata pairs scope with proxy.
- **deferral-primitives** — The OTHER sanctioned fix for the scope-mismatch gotcha: an ObjectProvider/Provider-analogue is a live handle that re-resolves against current scope state on each call (vs freezing one instance). For prototype/request scopes, a deferral handle is the honest, debuggable alternative to a scoped proxy — both route through this feature's resolution; the type signature makes the per-call re-resolution explicit.
- **concurrency-contract** — DEFINING RELATIONSHIP. This feature IS the realization of 'scope as the primary concurrency design tool' the concurrency-contract frames: the container guarantees safe publication of singletons but NOT runtime method thread-safety, so narrowing scope (singleton→prototype/request) is the mechanical answer to mutable per-interaction state. scopes provides the multiplicities; concurrency-contract provides the doctrine that selects among them.
- **lifecycle-callbacks** — Scope determines WHETHER destruction callbacks run: singleton → run at context-close (reverse dependency order); request/session/custom → run at scope-end; PROTOTYPE → init runs but destruction NEVER runs (hand-off-and-forget). lifecycle-callbacks defines the init/destroy ordering; scopes defines the per-scope teardown gating and supplies the teardown ledger the boundary drains.
- **context-close** — Singleton-scope teardown is driven BY context-close: at close, Lifecycle beans stop, then singletons are destroyed in reverse dependency order by draining this feature's singleton teardown ledger. context-close owns the close sequence; scopes owns the singleton ledger and the reverse-order destruction contract for that scope.
- **context-propagation** — CRITICAL ENABLER for web/context scopes. The ambient store reference for request/session/custom scopes must survive `.await` and thread/task hops — exactly context-propagation's capture/restore SPI job. When scoped work is handed to an executor (task-execution/async-execution), context-propagation must move the ScopeContext/Ambient or the moved work loses its scope store. The rust-native replacement for Spring's ThreadLocal request binding lives at this seam.
- **locale-context** — Sibling ambient: 'the current request/session' that web scopes key on is the same per-execution ambient locale-context exposes (current locale/request attributes). resolveContextualObject (the Scope SPI's contextual-object accessor) overlaps with locale-context's 'current X' lookups; both are inputs context-propagation moves across boundaries.
- **refresh-lifecycle** — Singleton eager pre-instantiation happens during refresh (the eager-instantiate-singletons step): scopes defines that singleton means eager-at-refresh (fail-fast wiring validation), while prototype/lazy/context scopes are NOT eagerly instantiated. refresh-lifecycle owns the bring-up order; scopes defines which scope participates in eager instantiation.
- **smart-initializing** — The sanctioned escape from the singleton-creation-lock deadlock trap: heavy cross-bean work that would deadlock under the per-singleton creation guard (init-under-lock) moves to the post-all-singletons lock-free callback. smart-initializing fires only for eager singletons — explicitly NOT for prototype or any non-singleton scope, a boundary scopes must respect.
- **lazy-initialization** — Orthogonal-but-interacting with singleton scope: lazy relocates a SINGLETON's creation from refresh to first access without changing its multiplicity (still one cached instance). Prototype/context scopes are inherently lazy (created on resolution). scopes defines multiplicity; lazy-initialization defines WHEN the singleton's single instance is born.
- **factory-bean** — A factory bean's PRODUCT has its own scope distinct from the factory's; the produced-type and scope interact (a scoped product can be isSingleton()==false yet not prototype). scopes must apply to the product, not the factory, when the engine resolves the dereferenced product — coordinating with factory-bean's separate caching/lifecycle.
- **task-execution** — When scoped work crosses to a TaskExecutor-analogue thread/task, the scope's ambient store does NOT follow automatically (a fresh task has no request store) — task-execution must cooperate with context-propagation to carry the ScopeContext, or the executed work resolves context-scoped beans incorrectly. The explicit thread-hop seam where scopes meet the async execution model.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- The scope/lifetime/OWNERSHIP model (charter §3 keystone, viewed through scopes): who OWNS a scoped instance and how is it handed out — `Arc<dyn Trait>`, owned move, or arena/store index? Singleton (container-owned, shared) vs prototype (caller-owned move) vs request/session (store-owned, dropped at boundary, but possibly referenced by in-flight work) have DIFFERENT ownership shapes; this cannot be settled per-realization and dictates the whole scoped-instance API, the Arc-vs-borrow question, and whether work can dangle past a scope boundary. THE deepest cross-cutting unknown for this feature.
- The async execution & context-propagation model (charter §3/T2): what is the rust-native ambient that request/session/websocket scopes bind to — a tokio task-local, a structured-concurrency scope value, an explicit leaf context object threaded through resolution, or a context-propagation capture/restore handle? Shared with context-propagation/locale-context/task-execution and MUST be resolved before web-scope storage can be designed; it determines whether ThreadLocal is truly banished and how scope stores survive `.await`/spawn/cancellation.
- Boxed-future `dyn Scope`/`InstanceStore` (open, Spring-faithful, alloc-per-get) vs enum-dispatched built-ins + a narrow custom backend (lean hot path, closed built-in set): this is the async-execution-model decision (§3) intersecting §2.5 overhead and §2.11 unification — it picks R1's openness vs R2/R3's hot-path leanness, and depends on how costly per-resolution boxing is judged for the prototype/request hot path vs how important uniform web-scope-as-SPI parity is.
- Where does scope-mismatch detection (singleton-holds-shorter) happen — build-time static analysis (possible if the graph is AOT-analyzed, the §3 compile-vs-runtime split), a dedicated startup validation pass, or first-resolution runtime? This couples scopes to autowiring-resolution and to how much of the graph is frozen at build time, and decides whether the gotcha is caught with the richest diagnostic (§2.9) before any request runs.
- Cross-crate discovery substrate for CUSTOM scopes (the brief's static-link-time-registry-PLUS-dynamic-WASM-source duality): is custom-scope contribution via linkme const descriptors (server default), build.rs codegen (portable/wasm), explicit register_scope, or a dynamic directory-scanned module source — and how is it kept opt-in/non-global (donsz.nl predictability) with an anti-DCE self-check? Shared with the whole discovery/SPI family (component-scanning, post-processor-spi, condition-family); a unified substrate decision, not a per-feature one.
- Open `dyn Scope` SPI vs the decomposed (Multiplicity, StoreSource, TeardownPolicy) model (R1 vs R3): is 'a scope' one extensible object, or two orthogonal primitives (how-many + where-stored)? A unification call needing the whole catalog — does decomposing buy real reuse across other features, or does it cost the Spring-familiar SPI shape (parity-of-feel) for too little gain? Interacts with how resolveContextualObject/conversationId are homed.
- Teardown-on-cancellation guarantee for context scopes: when a serving task is cancelled or panics mid-request, what mechanism guarantees the request/session scope's destruction callbacks still run (structured-concurrency scope-guard? a drop-bomb that schedules async teardown? the runtime's request-completion hook)? Given no async Drop, this is a non-trivial reliability question shared with context-close and the async-execution model — leaving it open risks scope-store leaks under the common cancellation path.
- Should the web scopes (request/session/application/websocket) be FIRST-CLASS in leaf-framework core (R2/R3 enum/ScopeDef) or themselves plugged via the custom-scope SPI (R1), given the core must not depend on a web crate (charter §1/§6)? The split between 'core defines the web-scope semantics + store shape' and 'a web integration crate provides only the ambient binding' must be drawn so the core stays web-aware without a web dependency — and so application-scope's per-ServletContext-analogue semantics (≠ container singleton) have a home.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent — read the Bean scopes section (lines 812-862) in full incl. all four hidden concepts (prototype-never-destroyed, singleton-holds-prototype gotcha, application-scope≠singleton, web-scope request binding), the Scope SPI signature (838-848), scoped proxies (850-862, 1755-1762), and 'Scope as the primary concurrency design tool' (1948-1952) plus the singleton-creation-lock deadlock trap (1956-1966). The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters for THIS feature — HOW a scope is modeled and WHERE scoped-instance storage + lifecycle live: R1 = one open `dyn Scope` trait object owning its own cache+ledger (literal Spring SPI, rust-retyped; most faithful, most boxing); R2 = explicit async-propagated `ScopeContext` ambient + enum-dispatched built-ins with a slim custom backend (leanest hot path, makes the §2.3 execution-context discipline central, surfaces the mismatch gotcha as fail-fast); R3 = decompose 'scope' into orthogonal (Multiplicity policy + pluggable InstanceStore provider + TeardownPolicy) data axes (maximal unification §2.11, most AOT-analyzable, furthest from Spring's SPI feel). R1↔R3 differ on open-trait-vs-decomposed-data; R2 differs by elevating the ambient context to first-class. I stayed substrate-neutral on cross-crate discovery (linkme/codegen/explicit/WASM-source all CANDIDATES) and refused to pick the ownership or async-context model (deferred keystones, surfaced as phase2Questions).

Three things to carry forward: (1) The deepest clash for THIS feature is ThreadLocal-bound web scopes vs `.await` — Spring's request/session binding rides a ThreadLocal that silently detaches across async thread-hops; leaf's web/context scopes MUST bind to an async-propagated ambient (task-local/structured-scope) integrated with context-propagation, never a raw ThreadLocal (§2.3/§2.4). Unresolvable in Phase 1 because it depends on the deferred async-context model. (2) Prototype's hand-off-and-forget (init runs, destroy NEVER) and singleton's destroy-at-close are the two poles of the teardown contract; with NO async Drop they must be a container-driven async teardown LEDGER drained at scope boundaries — and the request/session case must drain even on cancellation/panic (structured-concurrency guard, the leak-free replacement for try-with-resources). All three realizations encode prototype's no-destroy as ABSENCE (no ledger entry) so the contract can't be violated. (3) The singleton-holds-shorter-scope gotcha is, in Rust, often a near-untypeable error rather than Spring's silent freeze — a latent ADVANTAGE — but it makes the deferral-handle (deferral-primitives) and scoped-proxy (scoped-proxies) paths load-bearing, not optional, since the 'inject directly' shortcut is gone.

The brief's two-source duality (static link-time scope registry as default 'classpath' PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source) is captured as multiple PRODUCERS of the same ScopeDescriptor/StoreSource, with the explicit warning that scope discovery must stay opt-in/non-global (donsz.nl predictability — a transitive dep silently rebinding multiplicities is exactly the surprise to avoid) and must carry the mandatory anti-DCE self-check. Built-in singleton/prototype (and, in R2/R3, web scopes) need NO discovery — they ship in leaf-core — which materially shrinks the silent-DCE surface vs treating every scope as discovered. I refused to pick the meta-architecture; every realization is compatible with cross-crate Arch A/B/C/D and any ownership/registry/async-context substrate, by keeping scope descriptors thin and the construction recipe (the erased origin-agnostic Factory from bean-definition) abstract and WASM-ready.

Naming note: `Scope`/`ScopeContext`/`ScopeKey`/`ScopeDef`/`Multiplicity`/`StoreSource`/`InstanceStore`/`TeardownLedger`/`DestructionCallback`/`Ambient` are Phase-1 placeholders. I wrote no files; this is the complete Phase-1 design for scopes. Grounded source paths (absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (812-862 the scope model + Scope SPI + scoped proxies + the four hidden concepts; 1755-1762 scoped-proxy intent; 1948-1952 scope-as-concurrency-lever; 1956-1966 singleton-creation-lock deadlock trap + smart-initializing escape; 2475 smart-initializing not-for-non-singletons; 2091-2164 the ThreadLocal-vs-thread-hop ambient-context problem + context-propagation capture/restore SPI); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2 esp. 2.3/2.4/2.5/2.9/2.11, deferred keystones §3 ownership + async-context + erasure substrate, topology §6 deps-point-inward); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/DCE silent-failure §135-152, generics-cannot-register §153, donsz.nl scope-discovery-not-global §36, Architectures A-D §277-336, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI §89-102, self-check mandate §152/269); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (async Drop absent/avoid as first-class constraint §63/86, AFIT/RPITIT-not-dyn-compatible forcing boxed-future scope methods, Any+downcast + const TypeId for the registry substrate, trait upcasting 1.86). Upstream bean-definition design consumed: scope is a definition field referenced by identity, the origin-agnostic erased Factory is the construction recipe every scope awaits.

## Bean Naming, Aliases & Collisions  `[bean-naming]`

Spring gives every bean an identity layer that is deliberately separate from its type and its definition: (1) a DETERMINISTIC DEFAULT NAME so the common type-driven-wiring case costs zero ceremony yet a stable by-name handle always exists — the decapitalized simple class name (`MovieFinderImpl`→`movieFinderImpl`, `mypackage.MyJdbcDao`→`myJdbcDao`), with the load-bearing acronym edge case (if the first TWO chars are uppercase, casing is preserved: `URLFooServiceImpl` stays as-is, NOT `uRLFooServiceImpl` — a silent-no-match footgun for by-name/qualifier lookups), and for `@Bean`-method-style factories the METHOD NAME is the default; an explicit name on the stereotype/`@Bean`/`@Named` overrides the default and the stereotype chosen never changes naming (uniform `value()` contract); (2) MULTIPLE IDENTIFIERS PER BEAN where the FIRST is the canonical name and the rest are ALIASES, so one shared instance (`dataSource`) can be addressed under many per-subsystem names (`subsystemA-dataSource`, `subsystemB-dataSource`) — identity is the stable anchor, names are negotiable surface, and aliases can even be registered for a bean defined elsewhere (forward/cross-definition aliasing); and (3) COLLISIONS AS LOUD FAILURES, not silent shadowing — two distinct scenarios: definition OVERRIDING (a second definition registered under an existing name; raw Spring allows-and-silently-wins but Spring BOOT flips `allowBeanDefinitionOverriding=false` so a same-name registration is a loud startup failure with a remediation hint, because Boot auto-registers hundreds of invisible beans and a stray shadow would be an invisible-cause bug), and scanning CONFLICT (`ConflictingBeanDefinitionException` when a generated name clashes with a genuinely-different definition), softened by `isCompatible` which tolerates re-detecting the SAME class (so overlapping scans don't error). The cross-package clash (two `MyService` in different packages → same default name) is resolved by an OPT-IN fully-qualified-name generator, NOT by auto-prefixing — short names stay the pleasant default, FQN is opt-in only when you actually clash (auto-package-resolution was requested and deliberately rejected). The throughline is fail-fast determinism over silent guessing: a name's identity must be predictable, and any name ambiguity is a design decision the developer must make explicit. leaf must replicate this INTENT — a deterministic derived default, a canonical+aliases multi-identity model, and loud-by-default collision handling — WITHOUT Spring's reflective decapitalize-the-runtime-class-name mechanism (Rust has no class-by-name and no runtime simple-name introspection), so name derivation moves to the macro/declaration site and the name model becomes a typed, interned identity layer the registry (bean-registry) enforces.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Macro-derived canonical name + interned alias graph in the registry; collision policy as a registry guard with a stable error taxonomy
Treat the NAME as data the macro derives at the declaration site and the ALIAS GRAPH + COLLISION POLICY as registry-owned state. (a) DERIVATION: the `#[component]`/`#[bean]` macro sees the type path / method name in its own token stream and emits a `const` derived name string into the bean's seed (the `RawBeanDefinition` from bean-definition). It applies the decapitalization rule — including the acronym edge case (first two chars uppercase → preserve) — at expansion time as a pure const/`fn` over the simple identifier (the last path segment), so `MovieFinderImpl`→`movieFinderImpl`, `URLFooServiceImpl`→unchanged. An explicit `#[component(name = "x")]` / `#[bean(name = ["a","b"])]` overrides; the first listed is canonical, the rest are seed-level aliases. A `fully_qualified` flag (or a context-level default generator selection) makes the canonical name the full path (`my_mod::MyService`) — the opt-in FQN escape hatch. (b) IDENTITY MODEL: a `BeanName` is an interned handle (a `u32`/`Symbol` into a per-registry string interner, NOT a heap `String` per reference — honors §2.5) so name comparisons are integer-equality and the hot by-name lookup is a `HashMap<BeanName, DefIndex>`. The registry holds the canonical-name→def map PLUS an alias→canonical map (`HashMap<BeanName, BeanName>`); `resolve(name)` follows alias→canonical in one hop. Aliases form a directed graph resolved to canonical at registration (cycle-checked: `a→b→a` is a loud error). (c) COLLISIONS: registration is a guarded write. Registering a canonical name already present, or an alias that shadows an existing canonical/alias, triggers the collision policy. Default = LOUD (`NameCollisionError` with both contributors' provenance — declaration sites via `proc_macro::Span`-embedded location in the seed); an `allow_override` toggle (per-registry, the `allowBeanDefinitionOverriding` analogue) flips to last-wins. An `is_compatible` predicate (same TypeId + same factory identity = same component re-detected) tolerates idempotent re-registration without erroring, mirroring Spring's re-scan tolerance.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity with the least machinery: canonical-name, alias-map, and a guarded-write collision policy map almost 1:1 onto Spring's `SimpleAliasRegistry` + `DefaultListableBeanFactory` override check — teachable to Spring users, and the override-vs-conflict distinction is preserved exactly.; Name derivation at the macro site is the only place the 'decapitalized simple name' rule CAN live in Rust (no runtime simple-name reflection), and emitting it as a `const` string keeps the macro thin (§2.10) — it emits exactly what a hand author would write (`name: "movieFinderImpl"`). `cargo expand` shows the literal derived name.; Interned `BeanName` makes by-name lookup and alias resolution integer-cheap and heap-light (one interner per registry, not a `String` per edge) — directly serves §2.5 and the hot-path discipline for by-name/qualifier resolution (candidate-resolver reads these).; Collision policy is a single guarded chokepoint, so the loud-by-default contract is enforced in ONE place with ONE rich error type — the §2.9 diagnostic (names both definitions, their TypeIds, both declaration sites, and the remediation: rename or enable override).; `is_compatible` re-scan tolerance falls out naturally and prevents the overlapping-scan false-positive that Spring users hit — a strictly-better DX than erroring on every duplicate.

**Cons:** The acronym edge case must be reimplemented byte-for-byte to match Spring's observable rule, and it operates on the Rust SIMPLE name (last path segment) which is a different lexicon than Java class names (snake_case modules, `_`-bearing idents) — the rule may read oddly on idiomatic Rust types and needs its own well-specified, tested spec rather than a literal port.; An interner is per-registry mutable state; in a container HIERARCHY (container-hierarchy) names cross registry boundaries, so either interners are shared (a cross-registry concern / possible contention) or names are compared by string at the boundary — an unresolved interaction the simple model glosses.; Alias graph + cycle check + compatibility predicate are runtime registration-time logic; a misconfigured forward alias (alias to a not-yet-registered canonical) needs a deferred-resolution pass or a two-phase 'register all, then resolve aliases' step, adding a small ordering subtlety.; `allow_override` as a single per-registry boolean is coarse — Spring Boot's real story has nuance (auto-config beans back off via conditions, not override); a single toggle may push too much policy onto one flag (the genuinely-loud cases vs the intended-override cases blur).; Derived-name determinism depends on the macro seeing a stable simple name; type aliases, re-exports, and generic instantiations (which can't be registered anyway) make 'the simple name' less unambiguous than Java's class name — edge cases the derivation spec must pin down.

**Mechanisms considered:** proc-macro emits a `const` derived canonical-name string (decapitalize + acronym rule applied at expansion via a pure fn) into the bean-definition seed; explicit name overrides; interned `BeanName` (`Symbol`/`u32`) per-registry interner; `HashMap<BeanName, DefIndex>` canonical map + `HashMap<BeanName, BeanName>` alias→canonical map; alias graph resolved-to-canonical at registration with cycle detection (`a→b→a` = loud error); guarded-write collision policy at the registration chokepoint: loud `NameCollisionError` by default, `allow_override` toggle for last-wins; `is_compatible(existing, incoming)` predicate (same TypeId + same factory fn-pointer identity) tolerating idempotent re-registration (re-scan analogue); opt-in `fully_qualified` flag / context-level FQN generator selection for the cross-package-clash escape hatch; `proc_macro::Span` location (stable 1.88) embedded in the seed for collision-error provenance
