**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Names are derived per-declaration-site by the macro and travel INSIDE the bean-definition seed (`RawBeanDefinition`), so they are substrate-neutral: the same derived-name string + alias list flows whether seeds arrive via linkme distributed-slice, inventory, build.rs codegen, or explicit `register`. The derivation fn lives in leaf-core (a pure `fn derive_default_name(simple: &str) -> Cow<str>`), the macro calls it at expansion OR emits a const literal — either way no logic is trapped in the macro (§2.10) and the rule is unit-testable in a normal crate. The COLLISION CHECK is pure runtime data work over the collected seeds inside the registry — immune to DCE itself, but it can only check what was actually linked: a registration-only crate the binary never references contributes no seed (Layer 0), so its name simply never appears and CANNOT collide — meaning a silently-dropped contribution is invisible to the collision detector. This makes bean-naming a PRIME consumer of the mandated anti-DCE self-check: the self-check (expected-count / build-time manifest cross-check) is what turns 'expected bean `redisTemplate` from leaf_redis, found none' into a legible error, since the name model alone can't distinguish 'not registered' from 'never linked'. Aliases registered FOR A BEAN DEFINED ELSEWHERE (Spring's cross-definition `<alias>`) cross crate boundaries cleanly as just another seed naming a target canonical name — but introduce a forward-reference / ordering question (the target may be in another crate's slice, registration order unspecified per the cross-crate doc), so alias resolution MUST be order-independent (resolve in a pass after all seeds are collected, never rely on registration order). Generics cannot be registered (no `'static` symbol), so a generic component has no derived name; the macro hard-errors and the `register_bean!(ConcreteType)` escape hatch supplies a concrete name. The opt-in FQN generator is the cross-CRATE collision remedy (two crates' same-simple-name components) — directly analogous to `FullyQualifiedAnnotationBeanNameGenerator`.

**T2 — execution context:** Entirely synchronous, cold-path, definition-phase work: name derivation is compile-time (macro expansion) or const; interning, alias-graph resolution, cycle detection, and the collision guard all run during registration BEFORE any instance exists — no async, no blocking, no I/O. The collision/alias-cycle errors surface at the earliest-richest point (registration / a startup validation pass), the §2.9 timing — a loud `NameCollisionError`/`AliasCycleError` with full provenance (both declaration sites, TypeIds, remediation) rather than a silent shadow that detonates at resolution time. Once registration is sealed, the canonical+alias maps are READ-ONLY and shared without locks (safe publication of immutable data — by-name resolution on the hot path is lock-free integer-keyed map reads, no global lock per §2.5). By-name resolution at runtime is a hot path but purely a read (alias→canonical one hop + map get) — reactive/notification-free, no polling. No cancellation concern (cold-path registration; hot-path reads are infallible map lookups returning Option). No teardown concern (names are interned data + maps, hold no resources). The interner write phase is single-threaded cold-path; if parallel definition loading is ever wanted, interning needs a concurrent interner or per-shard interners merged at seal — flagged, not resolved here.

#### R2 — Identity as a first-class TypeId-anchored value, names as a pure OVERLAY index; default name is lazy/derived-on-demand, never the primary key
Invert Spring's name-centricity to fit Rust's type-first reality. Spring is name-keyed because the JVM resolves beans by string name pervasively; leaf's PRIMARY identity is the TypeId (most wiring is by-type per the Spring doc's own 'name is a fallback handle, not its essence'). So the registry's primary key is `(TypeId, disambiguator)` and NAMES are a separate, OPTIONAL overlay index layered on top — a `NameIndex` mapping `BeanName → BeanRef` that is consulted ONLY for explicit by-name/qualifier lookups. Crucially, the default derived name need NOT be materialized eagerly: a bean carries its simple-name fragment (emitted by the macro) and the canonical default name is COMPUTED on demand (and memoized) the first time a by-name path needs it — because in the common all-by-type case the name is never observed, so deriving/interning it is wasted work (low-overhead #2). Explicit names and aliases ARE eagerly registered into the `NameIndex` (they exist precisely to be looked up). The `NameIndex` is itself a small composable component: it OWNS the alias graph, the collision policy, and the FQN-vs-simple generator strategy as a pluggable `NameGenerator` (the `BeanNameGenerator` analogue — a trait the context selects, default = simple-decapitalized, opt-in = fully-qualified). Collisions are detected when a name is ADDED to the index (explicit names/aliases at registration) and, for lazily-derived defaults, at first materialization — a default-name clash between two by-type beans only becomes an error if/when someone tries to address them by that ambiguous default name, mirroring how Spring's generated-name conflict is really about NAMED access (and letting purely-by-type beans with colliding simple names coexist harmlessly until named).

**Pros:** Best alignment with the charter's low-overhead #2 and the Spring doc's own framing ('most beans are matched by type and never referenced by name'): the all-by-type common case pays ZERO naming cost — no interning, no string, no collision check until a name is actually used. Lazy derivation is the rust-native optimization of Spring's always-eager naming.; A pluggable `NameGenerator` trait is the clean rust-native analogue of `BeanNameGenerator`/`FullyQualifiedAnnotationBeanNameGenerator` — the simple-vs-FQN choice (and any custom scheme) is a strategy object the context holds, exactly Spring's `nameGenerator` attribute, with an obvious escape hatch.; Decoupling identity (TypeId-anchored) from names (overlay) means two by-type beans with the same simple class name DON'T conflict unless someone names them — eliminating the cross-package-clash false-positive at its root for the common case, and turning the FQN generator from a workaround into a deliberate naming-policy choice.; The `NameIndex` as a separable component is a natural unification seam: container-hierarchy can stack NameIndexes (child overlays parent, local-name-shadows-parent), and the alias graph + collision policy live in one cohesive, testable unit rather than smeared across the registry.; Lazy collision detection ('error only when an ambiguous name is actually requested') is arguably a RICHER §2.9 diagnostic: the error fires at the exact by-name access that's ambiguous, naming the requester and all colliding candidates, rather than a blanket registration-time failure — closer to NoUniqueBean's 'name the injection point' instinct.

**Cons:** DEVIATES from Spring's loud-FAIL-AT-STARTUP determinism: deferring default-name collision to first-access means a latent ambiguity can lurk past startup and detonate at runtime — directly in tension with the §65 'a context that started is a context that works' eager-fail value and Boot's deliberate loud-startup-failure flip. A startup validation pass would be needed to recover eagerness, partly negating the laziness win.; Lazy/memoized derivation introduces interior mutability on the naming path (the memo cache) — a `RwLock`/`OnceCell` per name or a concurrent map — which fights §2.5 (minimize locks); getting lock-free memoization right on the by-name hot path is fiddly and may not be worth it for a cold-ish lookup.; Two-tier identity (TypeId primary, name overlay) is a bigger conceptual departure from Spring; users porting `getBean("name")` mental models meet a system where names are second-class, which can surprise (and the Spring doc DOES treat name as a real identifier, not purely a fallback — aliases/namespaces are a first-class feature, not an afterthought).; A pluggable `NameGenerator` that runs at registration is fine, but if it can run at LAZY materialization too, its execution context is split (cold registration vs first-access hot path) — the generator must be pure/cheap/non-blocking in both, a constraint that's easy to violate.; The 'don't conflict until named' rule complicates collection-injection's name-keyed Map (`Map<BeanName, Bean>`): building that map forces materialization of ALL default names at once, re-triggering the eager collision question precisely where the laziness was supposed to help.

**Mechanisms considered:** TypeId-anchored primary identity `(TypeId, disambiguator)`; `NameIndex` as a separate overlay (`BeanName → BeanRef`) consulted only for by-name/qualifier access; lazy + memoized default-name derivation (macro emits the simple-name fragment; canonical name computed on first by-name need) — eager only for explicit names/aliases; pluggable `NameGenerator` trait (default simple-decapitalized, opt-in fully-qualified, custom) = `BeanNameGenerator`/`FullyQualifiedAnnotationBeanNameGenerator` analogue selected by the context; alias graph + collision policy encapsulated inside the `NameIndex` component (separable, hierarchy-stackable); lazy collision detection (default-name clash errors only at ambiguous by-name access; explicit-name clash errors at registration); optional startup-validation pass to force-materialize + check all names eagerly, recovering Boot's loud-startup determinism on demand; OnceCell/concurrent-map memoization for derived names (interior mutability, flagged as a §2.5 tension)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Same seed-travels-with-definition substrate-neutrality as R1 for the simple-name fragment + explicit names/aliases (linkme/inventory/codegen/explicit all carry them). The `NameGenerator` strategy lives in leaf-core and is selected by the context, not baked into seeds — so a downstream binary can switch its whole app to FQN naming WITHOUT touching contributing crates (one context-level decision), a cleaner cross-crate knob than R1's per-seed flag for the global case. Lazy derivation interacts subtly with cross-crate collection: because default names aren't materialized until used, a silently-DCE-dropped crate's beans are doubly invisible (no seed AND no name ever computed) — the anti-DCE self-check is even MORE essential here and must run at the build-time/startup layer, not rely on collision detection (which may never fire if the name is never requested). Aliases-for-beans-defined-elsewhere and the order-independence requirement are identical to R1 (resolve in a post-collection pass). The TypeId-primary key leans on `const TypeId::of` (stable 1.91) so seeds can embed their own type key const-promoted (per the cross-crate doc's descriptor recommendation), making the `(TypeId, disambiguator)` primary key build-time-constructible. Generics still cannot be registered (hard-error in macro); a concrete `register_bean!` supplies both the TypeId and any name.

**T2 — execution context:** Mixed-context, which is its defining risk: explicit-name/alias registration + collision check are synchronous cold-path (definition phase), identical to R1. But LAZY default-name derivation + collision detection can occur on the by-name resolution HOT PATH (first time a default name is requested at runtime), which (a) introduces memoization interior-mutability on a hot path (must be lock-free or fine-grained per §2.5) and (b) relocates a class of collision errors from startup to runtime — softening the §2.9 'as early as the richest diagnostic allows' / 'context that started works' guarantee unless a startup validation pass force-materializes names. The `NameGenerator` must be pure, cheap, non-blocking, non-async in BOTH contexts (it's pure string computation — no I/O, no await), which the trait should constrain/document. Once names are materialized and sealed (or after the optional validation pass), the NameIndex is read-only and lock-free on the hot path, same as R1. No cancellation/teardown concern for the name data itself. The key T2 tension to flag: this realization trades the charter's eager-fail determinism for low-overhead laziness, and recovering determinism requires re-adding an eager startup pass — so the laziness benefit is real only if by-name access is genuinely rare AND eager validation is opt-out.

#### R3 — Names verified/frozen at BUILD time: derivation + alias resolution + collision detection as a codegen pass, emitting a perfect-hash name table and a compile/build error on clash
Push the entire naming layer toward build time, riding the AOT lineage (the Spring doc and charter both note 'definitions knowable at build time'). The macro still derives the default name at the declaration site, but the CROSS-CRATE name picture — full alias graph, all canonical names, all collisions — is assembled by a build-time codegen step at the ROOT/binary crate (the sqlx-style `cargo metadata`-driven aggregator the cross-crate doc calls the portable fallback). That step: (1) collects every contributed name/alias seed across the resolved dependency graph; (2) runs derivation (decapitalize + acronym rule) and alias resolution (cycle check) as plain Rust at build time; (3) DETECTS COLLISIONS THEN — a same-canonical-name clash or alias-shadow becomes a BUILD error (a generated `compile_error!` or a build-script failure) with full provenance, when override is off; (4) emits a FROZEN name table — ideally a perfect-hash map (`phf`) from `BeanName` → `DefId` plus a pre-resolved alias→canonical table — `include!`'d into the binary. Runtime by-name lookup becomes a zero-collision perfect-hash get with NO registration-time interning, alias-walking, or collision checking at all (it was all done at build time). The override toggle and FQN-vs-simple choice are build-time configuration consumed by the codegen pass. This is the maximal-determinism, minimal-runtime-cost end of the spectrum: collisions caught before the program ever runs, and the hot-path name table is a static perfect hash.

**Pros:** Maximal fail-fast determinism — the charter's & Spring's deepest naming value: a name collision is caught at BUILD time, before the binary exists, the earliest possible point (§2.9 timing), strictly louder than even Boot's startup failure. No latent ambiguity can ship.; Lowest possible runtime cost (low-overhead #2): a frozen `phf` perfect-hash name table means by-name lookup is a static, collision-free, lock-free get with zero startup interning/alias-resolution work — the hot path is as cheap as it can be, no per-registry interner, no runtime collision guard.; Sidesteps the cross-crate silent-DCE hazard at its root for naming: the build-time aggregator walks the RESOLVED dependency graph (`cargo metadata` / manifest), so it sees every opted-in contribution regardless of whether the linker would have GC'd it — and can emit the force-link references the cross-crate doc mandates, turning 'expected bean from leaf_redis, found none' into a build error rather than a silent empty registry. The anti-DCE self-check becomes intrinsic, not bolted-on.; Alias resolution + cycle detection + FQN policy as build-time plain Rust are trivially testable and order-independent by construction (the aggregator controls iteration order), eliminating R1/R2's registration-order subtleties.; Frozen table is immutable, trivially shareable, no locks (§2.5), and aligns with the AOT/build-time-determinism direction the Spring doc itself leans toward — a forward-compatible bet.

**Cons:** Heaviest tooling burden and the least 'just works': requires a build-time aggregator at the binary crate (a `leaf-codegen`/build.rs step + a manifest/`cargo metadata` walk) — the cross-crate doc's portable fallback, but it's real build infrastructure, extra build time, and a non-trivial DX onboarding cost (a misconfigured aggregator = confusing build errors), in tension with DX #1 for the setup experience even as it improves the steady-state.; FREEZES the name graph at build time, which fights any DYNAMIC definition source: programmatic-registration (imperative/looping/conditional bean registration at runtime) and the optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module 'classpath' contribute names the build-time table can't know — so R3 needs a hybrid runtime-overlay fallback for dynamic names, partly re-introducing R1's runtime machinery and undercutting the 'all at build time' purity.; Build-time collision detection can only see what the dependency-graph walk includes; runtime-conditional beans (conditions/profiles) that may or may not register make 'is this a real collision?' undecidable at build time — the pass must either over-report (false collisions for beans that wouldn't both activate) or defer conditional cases to runtime, complicating the clean story.; `compile_error!`/build-script failures risk being LESS legible than a rich runtime diagnostic (the charter warns against forcing checks to compile-time when it yields a cryptic error) — a perfect-hash-table build failure naming generated symbols may be harder to action than a startup `NameCollisionError`, unless the codegen invests heavily in error formatting.; `phf`/build-time codegen is a real dependency and a meta-architecture lean (codegen-over-runtime) that Phase 2 must own — adopting it here would pre-bias the deferred metadata/codegen-boundary decision (§3), so it can only be presented as a candidate, not chosen.

**Mechanisms considered:** build-time aggregator at the root/binary crate (sqlx-style `cargo metadata` / manifest walk) collecting all name/alias seeds across the resolved graph; build-time derivation + alias-resolution + cycle-detection as plain Rust in `leaf-codegen`; build-time collision detection → generated `compile_error!` / build-script failure with provenance (loud-by-default; override is build config); frozen perfect-hash name table (`phf`) `BeanName → DefId` + pre-resolved alias→canonical table, `include!`'d into the binary; build-time emission of force-link references (anti-DCE) + intrinsic expected-vs-found self-check; FQN-vs-simple + override toggle as build-time configuration consumed by the codegen pass; hybrid runtime-overlay name index for dynamic sources (programmatic-registration, WASM-module classpath) layered atop the frozen build-time table

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** This realization IS a cross-crate strategy: it embraces the cross-crate doc's recommended reliable path (build-time codegen over the resolved dependency graph at the binary crate) precisely to defeat the silent-DCE/Layer-0 hazards that plague the link-time substrates for the OTHER realizations. The build-time aggregator sees the whole opted-in graph via `cargo metadata`/manifest (not the linker's reachability view), so it can both detect cross-crate name collisions deterministically AND emit force-link references so contributing crates aren't GC'd — making the anti-DCE self-check intrinsic. It is the natural home for the dual-source CANDIDATE: the static build-time table is the default 'classpath', and an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source is a SECOND name producer layered as a runtime overlay atop the frozen table (two producers, one resolved view) — flagged as a candidate, not a decision. Cost: it leans hardest on tooling and is the least platform-portable-by-default in setup (though arguably MORE portable at runtime — works on wasm/embedded where linkme doesn't, per the doc). Generics still can't be registered; the aggregator can at least DETECT a generic-component declaration and emit a clear build error pointing to `register_bean!`. The frozen `phf` table embeds `const TypeId`s (stable 1.91) for the type-anchored entries.

**T2 — execution context:** The naming WORK (derivation, alias resolution, collision detection) moves entirely OUT of any runtime execution context into the build process — no async, no blocking, no runtime cost for the static portion; it runs in the build toolchain (cargo/build.rs/codegen), single-threaded, deterministic, before the program exists. Runtime by-name lookup against the frozen `phf` table is a pure, lock-free, infallible-or-Option static read — the cheapest hot path of the three, no interior mutability, no startup interning. Collision errors surface at BUILD time (compile_error!/build failure), the earliest possible §2.9 timing, with no runtime cancellation/teardown concern for the static table. The ONLY runtime execution context appears in the hybrid overlay for DYNAMIC names (programmatic-registration, WASM-module source): that overlay re-introduces R1-style synchronous cold-path registration + collision-against-the-frozen-table checking (a dynamic name colliding with a frozen one is a runtime error), and must layer without a global lock (a small runtime name overlay map checked after the frozen table). So T2 is bimodal: static names = zero runtime context (pure build-time), dynamic names = R1's cold-path runtime registration. Cancellation/teardown N/A for names (pure data).

### Rust clashes
- No runtime simple-name reflection: Spring derives the default name by reflecting the runtime `Class.getSimpleName()` and decapitalizing it. Rust has NO class-by-name and NO way to get a type's 'simple name' as data at runtime (`std::any::type_name` is a best-effort, NON-guaranteed, fully-qualified, possibly-mangled debug string — explicitly unsuitable as an identity key per the nightly doc's 'human-facing diagnostics only'). So the decapitalized-simple-name derivation MUST happen at the macro/declaration site over the syntactic type path the macro can see, not at runtime — name derivation is a compile-time text transform, not a reflective query. This is the central mechanism inversion for this feature.
- The acronym edge case re-specified for a different lexicon: Spring's 'preserve casing if first two chars are uppercase' rule was tuned for Java ClassNames. Applied to Rust simple names (last path segment of a type), the same rule must be reimplemented as a pure tested fn, but Rust idioms differ — type names are CamelCase like Java, yet modules are snake_case and re-exports/type-aliases can change what 'the simple name' even is. The observable rule can be matched, but the input lexicon and the 'what is the simple name' question (alias? re-export? generic instantiation?) need an explicit leaf spec rather than a literal port.
- Name as `String` vs interned/typed identity: Spring uses `String` bean names pervasively (heap strings, hash-by-string everywhere). Idiomatic Rust + §2.5 (stack-over-heap, minimal churn) push toward an interned `BeanName` (a `Symbol`/`u32`) or even a type-level key — turning Spring's stringly-typed identity into a typed, integer-comparable handle. This is a net win but means a per-registry interner (mutable cold-path state) and a cross-registry-boundary question in hierarchies that Spring's global string space doesn't have.
- No global classpath / silent-DCE makes 'collision' detectable only over what was actually LINKED: Spring detects collisions over a runtime-enumerable classpath. In Rust the registry sees only seeds from crates the binary actually references; a silently-DCE'd or never-path-referenced contributing crate (Layer 0 of the cross-crate doc) contributes NO name, so it can never be detected as a collision AND its absence is itself silent. The loud-collision contract therefore has a blind spot the anti-DCE self-check (expected-vs-found) must cover — a same-name collision is loud, but a MISSING expected bean is a different, equally-important silent failure the name model alone can't catch.
- Generics have no registrable name: a generic `Component<T>` has no `'static` symbol, no TypeId, and thus no derivable canonical name until instantiated (per both the cross-crate and nightly docs). Spring registers generic classes by name freely; leaf cannot. The macro must hard-error on a generic component and force a per-instantiation `register_bean!(ConcreteType)` that supplies a concrete name — so 'every bean has a deterministic default name' holds only for concrete types, and generic-as-implicit-qualifier naming (candidate-resolver) keys off concrete TypeIds.
- Override-as-mutation vs immutable/owned registration: Spring's `allowBeanDefinitionOverriding` silently REPLACES an entry in a mutable map. In Rust the registry is more naturally append-with-guard; 'override' must be an explicit, policy-gated replacement (last-wins) rather than incidental map mutation, and the loud-by-default path must produce a rich owned error rather than relying on the silent map-put semantics Spring inherited. The Boot flip (default false) aligns perfectly with Rust's preference for explicit-over-silent, so the IMPROVED default (loud) is also the more rust-native one.
- Forward/cross-definition aliases vs registration-order non-determinism: Spring's `<alias>` can name a target defined elsewhere, relying on a mutable registry resolved at lookup time. In Rust, cross-crate registration ORDER is explicitly unspecified (linkme section order / inventory constructor order, per the cross-crate doc — 'never rely on registration order'), so an alias-for-a-bean-defined-elsewhere CANNOT be resolved eagerly at its own registration point. Alias resolution must be a deliberate order-independent pass AFTER all seeds are collected — a structural consequence of no-global-classpath that Spring's mutable-registry model never had to confront.
- FQN-collision-resolution policy is a strategy object, not a runtime class swap: Spring swaps in `FullyQualifiedAnnotationBeanNameGenerator` (a class with a no-arg ctor, loaded by name). leaf has no load-by-name; the simple-vs-FQN choice becomes a typed `NameGenerator` strategy the context holds (or a build-time config) — cleaner, but it means the generator must be chosen at compile/context-config time, not reflectively, and a per-seed override flag vs a context-wide strategy is a real design fork (R1 vs R2/R3).

### Interactions
- **bean-definition** — Upstream dependency: the canonical name + alias list are FIELDS on the bean-definition seed/record (`RawBeanDefinition`), derived at the macro site. bean-definition owns the record shape; this feature defines what the name/alias fields MEAN (derivation rule, canonical-first ordering) and how they're computed. The acronym/decapitalize derivation produces the string that lands in the definition's name field.
- **bean-registry** — Load-bearing consumer/owner: the registry STORES the canonical-name→def map, the alias→canonical map, and OWNS the collision-policy guard (the `allow_override` toggle, the `is_compatible` re-scan predicate, the loud `NameCollisionError`). Spring's `SimpleAliasRegistry` + `DefaultListableBeanFactory` override-check live here. This feature defines the policy; bean-registry is where the maps live and the guard fires.
- **component-scanning** — Scanning is the producer that triggers default-name derivation (no explicit name → derive) and is the source of the `ConflictingBeanDefinitionException` scenario + the `isCompatible` re-scan tolerance. The opt-in FQN generator is configured at the scan boundary (Spring's `@ComponentScan(nameGenerator=...)`). Overlapping-scan duplicate-of-same-class must be tolerated, genuinely-different same-name must be loud.
- **component-stereotypes** — Stereotype annotations (`@Component`/`@Service`/etc analogues) are the declaration sites the macro reads to derive the name; per Spring, the stereotype chosen NEVER changes naming (uniform name contract). An explicit name on any stereotype overrides the default identically — leaf must keep the name-attribute contract uniform across all stereotype macros.
- **configuration-classes** — `@Bean`-method-equivalent factories default their name to the METHOD NAME (not a type-decapitalization), and accept an explicit name list with canonical-first aliases (`@Bean({"dataSource","a-ds"})`). This feature must handle the two derivation sources — type-simple-name for components vs method-name for factory-style beans — as distinct default rules feeding the same name model.
- **candidate-resolver** — By-name matching and `@Qualifier("name")` resolution read the canonical+alias maps this feature owns; the acronym edge case directly causes the silent-no-match qualifier footgun Spring documents (`@Qualifier("uRLFooServiceImpl")` misses). leaf must surface a rich 'no bean named X; did you mean Y?' diagnostic when a qualifier name misses, rather than a silent no-match.
- **autowiring-resolution** — Name is the LAST layer of Spring's fail-fast resolution policy (type → generics → qualifier → primary/fallback → NAME). This feature supplies the by-name fallback resolution (alias→canonical→def) that autowiring-resolution invokes only after type/qualifier layers fail to uniquely resolve — the deterministic-handle-of-last-resort.
- **collection-injection** — The name-keyed Map injection (`Map<BeanName, Bean>`) is built from this feature's canonical-name map — every matching bean's CANONICAL name becomes a map key. This forces materialization of all relevant default names at once (a tension specifically for R2's lazy-derivation, where building the map re-triggers eager naming).
- **container-hierarchy** — A child registry's name SHADOWS a parent's same-name bean (local-name-wins, the §185 rule) — distinct from definition-parent template merge. The alias/canonical maps must support hierarchy-aware by-name lookup (check local, then delegate to parent) while listing stays local-only (`containsLocalBean` analogue). Cross-registry name comparison raises the shared-vs-per-registry interner question (R1's con).
- **programmatic-registration** — The imperative registration escape hatch (BeanRegistrar analogue) MINTS names + aliases at runtime through the same name model — must offer a builder that supplies explicit names/aliases and goes through the SAME collision guard. This is the dynamic-name source that R3's build-time-frozen table cannot pre-know, forcing R3's runtime-overlay hybrid.
- **factory-bean** — A factory bean and its product are distinct identities; the name model must address the PRODUCT by the bean's name while the factory itself is reachable via a dereference escape hatch (Spring's `&name`). The name layer needs a convention to name/address the factory-vs-product distinction without collision.
- **value-injection** — `@Value`/SpEL bean references (`#{otherBean}`) resolve a bean BY NAME through this feature's maps — the expression layer (expression-language) ultimately calls the same alias→canonical→def by-name path, so the name model is the resolution target for name-bearing expressions.
- **post-processor-spi** — A BFPP-analogue could rewrite a definition's name/aliases during the cold rewrite window (e.g. a renaming/aliasing post-processor) — the name fields must be editable in the same rewrite window bean-definition exposes, and any such edit must re-run through the collision guard before the window freezes.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Per-seed name-flag (R1) vs context-wide pluggable NameGenerator strategy (R2/R3) vs build-time-frozen table (R3): where does the simple-vs-FQN-vs-custom naming policy live, and is it chosen per-declaration, per-context, or at build time? This couples to the deferred metadata/codegen-boundary and conditional-strategy decisions and to how container-hierarchy stacks naming.
- Eager-at-startup vs lazy-at-first-use vs build-time collision detection — a direct collision (pun intended) between the charter's fail-fast/'context that started works' determinism (§65/§2.9) and low-overhead #2. R2's laziness saves work but relocates a class of errors to runtime; R3's build-time check is loudest but heaviest. The timing of collision detection is a §2.9 unified-diagnostics-model question that needs the whole catalog (esp. how often by-name access actually happens) to settle.
- Is `BeanName` an interned `Symbol`/`u32`, a heap `String`, an arena-indexed handle, or a type-level key? This is the naming corner of the deferred type-erasure/registry-substrate and ownership/lifetime keystones — it drives the interner's existence, the cross-registry-boundary behavior in hierarchies, and whether by-name lookup is integer- or string-keyed on the hot path. Cannot be settled per-feature.
- Cross-registry name identity in container hierarchies: if names are interned per-registry, how do parent/child lookups compare names across interner boundaries without contention or a shared global interner (a §2.5 concern)? Or are names compared by string at the boundary? This cross-cuts container-hierarchy and the interner decision and is unresolved across all three realizations.
- Order-independent alias resolution as a mandated post-collection pass: given registration order is unspecified across the cross-crate substrates, where does the 'resolve all aliases after all seeds collected' pass live, and how does it compose with conditional/profile registration (an alias to a conditionally-absent bean)? Cross-cuts conditions, profiles, and the registration substrate.
- How does the loud-collision contract interoperate with the anti-DCE self-check for the INVERSE failure (a missing expected bean)? A same-name collision is loud, but a silently-DCE'd contributing crate produces no name and no collision — the two failure modes (present-and-clashing vs absent-but-expected) need a UNIFIED diagnostic story, tying bean-naming to the cross-crate composition model and the expected-vs-found self-check mandate.
- The `allow_override` granularity question: Spring Boot's real behavior is conditions-back-off (auto-config yields via `@ConditionalOnMissingBean`), NOT blanket override. Is leaf's override a single coarse per-registry toggle, a per-bean/per-source policy, or subsumed entirely by the conditional strategy + primary/fallback so that 'override' rarely needs the loud-failure escape valve? Cross-cuts conditions, auto-configuration, primary-fallback.
- Dynamic-name sources (programmatic-registration at runtime, the optional WASM-module directory-scanned 'classpath') vs a build-time-frozen name table (R3): if any names are minted at runtime, the frozen-table purity breaks and a runtime overlay is needed — how do a static build-time name view and a dynamic runtime name view compose into one resolution path without re-introducing the full runtime machinery R3 tried to avoid? Cross-cuts the static-vs-dynamic-source duality and the metadata/codegen boundary.
- Specification of 'the simple name' for Rust types: how does derivation handle type aliases, re-exports, nested/associated types, and the difference between the declared ident and any re-export path? Spring's `getSimpleName` is unambiguous; Rust's macro sees a syntactic path that may not be the canonical one. A precise leaf naming spec (and whether `proc_macro::Span`/path normalization is involved) is needed and cross-cuts the macro/codegen boundary.
- Provenance depth for collision diagnostics: how much declaration-site provenance (both contributors' file/line via `proc_macro::Span`, TypeIds, the alias chain that led to the clash) is retained to make `NameCollisionError` actionable, and is that retained always or only in a diagnostic mode (memory cost)? Cross-cuts the unified §2.9 diagnostics model and startup-instrumentation.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent — I read the dedicated feature section (lines 627-678: the decapitalized-default + acronym edge case at 641-645, the 6.0 `uncapitalizeAsProperty` provenance note, the canonical+aliases/per-subsystem-namespace model at 647-657, and the full override-vs-conflict + `isCompatible` + FQN-generator + Boot `allow-bean-definition-overriding=false` story at 659-678), plus the cross-references (uniform stereotype name contract at 444-450/516, `@Bean` method-name default + alias array at 1013, the hierarchy shadowing rule at 185-188, the FQN `nameGenerator` attribute at 540/595-596, and the fail-fast-over-silent-guessing philosophy at 65/625). The three realizations are genuinely DIVERGENT on the axis that matters for THIS feature — WHERE the name layer's work (derivation, alias resolution, collision detection) happens and how name relates to identity: R1 = names are first-class registry data, derived at macro site, alias graph + loud collision guard at runtime registration (literal Spring, rust-owned, interned) — the safe default; R2 = identity is TypeId-primary and names are a LAZY overlay index with a pluggable NameGenerator, paying zero naming cost in the all-by-type common case (the low-overhead play, but it trades eager-fail determinism for laziness); R3 = the whole name layer is FROZEN AT BUILD TIME via a root-crate codegen aggregator emitting a perfect-hash table and turning collisions into build errors (the max-determinism/min-runtime-cost AOT play, heaviest tooling). R1↔R2 differ on eager-vs-lazy + name-centric-vs-type-centric; R3 differs on runtime-vs-build-time and is the cross-crate-reliability play.

Three carry-forwards. (1) The single deepest clash for THIS feature is no-runtime-simple-name-reflection: Spring's whole default-naming mechanism (reflect Class.getSimpleName + decapitalize) has no Rust runtime analogue, so derivation MUST move to the macro/declaration site as a compile-time text transform — and the acronym edge case must be re-specified and tested for the Rust lexicon, not literally ported. (2) The loud-collision contract has a Rust-specific BLIND SPOT: collision detection only sees what was actually linked, so a silently-DCE'd/never-referenced contributing crate produces no name and thus never collides — the present-and-clashing failure (loud) and the absent-but-expected failure (silent) are TWO failure modes that need a unified diagnostic story tied to the mandated anti-DCE self-check; bean-naming is a prime consumer of that self-check. (3) Cross-crate registration ORDER is unspecified, so forward/cross-definition aliases (Spring's `<alias>` for a bean defined elsewhere) CANNOT resolve eagerly — alias resolution must be an order-independent post-collection pass in all realizations.

The dual-source duality the brief asks me to flag (static link-time/build-time registry as the default 'classpath' PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module 'classpath') surfaces here as: dynamic name sources mint names/aliases at runtime through the same collision guard (most natural in R1; forces a runtime-overlay hybrid atop R3's frozen table) — left as CANDIDATES, not decided. I stayed substrate-neutral on the collection mechanism (linkme/inventory/codegen/explicit) and on the BeanName-representation/interner and the eager-vs-lazy-vs-build-time-timing questions — all flagged as cross-cutting phase2Questions, not re-litigated per realization. I deliberately did NOT pick the meta-architecture: every realization is compatible with cross-crate Arch A-D and with whatever registry/ownership substrate Phase 2 picks, by keeping the name seed thin (a derived const string + alias list on the bean-definition) and the alias/collision logic as pure data work in leaf-core.

Naming note: 'BeanName', 'NameIndex', 'NameGenerator', 'NameCollisionError', 'AliasCycleError', 'is_compatible' are Phase-1 placeholders. I wrote no files; this is the complete Phase-1 design for bean-naming. Grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (627-678 the naming/alias/collision section incl. acronym edge case, isCompatible, FQN generator, Boot override flip; 185-188 hierarchy shadow; 444-450/516 uniform stereotype name; 540/595-596 nameGenerator attribute; 1013 @Bean method-name default; 65/625 fail-fast philosophy); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/DCE silent-failure, generics-cannot-register, registration-order-unspecified, sqlx-style build-time aggregator + self-check mandate, thin-macro/absolute-path ABI); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (stable envelope: const TypeId 1.91, Any+downcast, proc_macro::Span 1.88 for provenance, type_name is diagnostics-only / NOT an identity key, linkme>=0.3.24/inventory both stable).

## Component Stereotypes & Composed Annotations  `[component-stereotypes]`

Spring's stereotype family rests on ONE deliberately minimal idea: a managed bean is anything meta-annotated with `@Component`. `@Component` is the NOUN ("this is a container-managed bean"); `@Service`/`@Repository`/`@Controller` are ADJECTIVES describing architectural role, and at the CONTAINER level they are functionally IDENTICAL to `@Component` — they register no extra post-processor, trigger no extra proxying, change nothing about instantiation, and (because `value()`/`name()` is `@AliasFor(Component.value())`) produce identical bean-name behavior. The whole point is the separation of SEMANTICS (documentation-as-code: "...acting as a service-layer facade") from BEHAVIOR (none extra). Three structural payoffs follow: (1) USER-EXTENSIBLE VOCABULARY — anyone mints `@DomainService`/`@OrderProcessor` meta-annotated with `@Component` and it "just works": discovery, naming, and indexing all follow transitively because the scanner's default include filter is "meta-present `@Component`" (resolved through MergedAnnotations) and `@Component` is itself meta-annotated `@Indexed`, so indexability follows the SAME transitive edge — no per-stereotype special-casing anywhere. (2) FUTURE-PROOFING — the docs reserve the right that stereotypes "may carry additional semantics in future releases"; because the marker is already in place, behavior can be attached later without a breaking migration. (3) ROLE is ORTHOGONAL — `@Role` (APPLICATION/SUPPORT/INFRASTRUCTURE) is purely informational tooling/diagnostics classification, deliberately decoupled from "what kind of component" so the two axes never entangle. The ONE exception that proves the rule is `@Repository`: the only core stereotype with real mechanics — a BeanPostProcessor (PersistenceExceptionTranslationPostProcessor) detects beans whose CLASS carries `@Repository` and weaves a DataAccessException-translation advisor; even this is just "the marker is a natural AOP pointcut," it is opt-in (Boot auto-registers it), the marker annotation type is PLUGGABLE (repositoryAnnotationType — you can point it at your own `@Dao`), and that behavior lives in the exception-translation feature, NOT in the stereotype itself. leaf must replicate the INTENT: a base "managed bean" marker plus a user-extensible family of role markers that are container-behaviorally equivalent to it, where minting a new stereotype is a first-class one-step DX, naming forwards uniformly, discovery/indexing follow the meta-presence edge for free, role is a separate orthogonal axis, and any future "this marker is a pointcut for behavior X" is expressible without special-casing each stereotype. This feature is almost pure CONSUMER of the annotation-model (meta-presence + `@AliasFor(Component.value)` name-forwarding + distance-ordered merge) and a producer of the per-bean role/name/scope defaults that bean-definition, bean-naming, candidate-resolver, and component-scanning read.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Codegen-flattened stereotype: the stereotype macro emits the SAME ComponentDescriptor seed as @component (vocabulary is a macro layer)
A stereotype is a MACRO that expands, at the bean's declaration site, to exactly the descriptor a plain `#[component]` would emit — plus a role/marker tag — so 'meta-presence' is resolved by codegen FLATTENING, never at runtime. leaf ships base `#[component]` and the four core stereotypes `#[service]`/`#[repository]`/`#[controller]`/`#[configuration]` as thin attribute macros, each a one-liner over `leaf-codegen`: `#[service]` ≡ `leaf_codegen::component_descriptor!(self, role = Application, name_attr = name, meta_present = [Component, Service], indexed = true)`. A USER stereotype is authored by re-exporting that helper: `#[macro_export] macro stereotype!{ domain_service => component(role=Application, meta=[Component, Service, DomainService]) }`, or a `#[derive(Stereotype)]`-style helper that stamps out a new attribute macro forwarding into `leaf_codegen`. The artifact every path emits is one `&'static ComponentDescriptor { type_id: TypeId, produced_type: TypeId, name: BeanName (default = decapitalized type ident, or the `name`/`value` attr — the `@AliasFor(Component.value)` forwarding resolved AT EXPANSION TIME), scope, lazy: Option<bool>, role: Role, qualifiers, meta_markers: &'static [MarkerId], factory: <origin-agnostic erased async ctor> }` registered into the discovery substrate (the static link-time 'classpath' by default; an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source can push descriptors of the identical shape — origin-agnostic). Container behavior reads ONLY this flat descriptor, so `#[service]` and `#[component]` are byte-for-byte identical to the engine except for the role tag and the `meta_markers` list — Spring's 'functionally identical' becomes a STRUCTURAL identity, not a convention. `@Repository`-style behavior is NOT in the stereotype: the descriptor merely records `meta_markers` containing `MarkerId::REPOSITORY`, and the exception-translation feature later queries 'which descriptors carry REPOSITORY' to attach its advisor — the pointcut is data, the behavior lives in its own crate.

**Pros:** Spring's 'functionally identical to @Component' becomes a compile-time STRUCTURAL fact: every stereotype lowers to the same descriptor shape, so it is impossible for a stereotype to accidentally diverge in container behavior — the strongest possible parity with the noun/adjective intent.; Zero runtime overhead and 'read without constructing the bean' for free: role/name/meta-markers are const data in the descriptor, read on the cold scanning/registration path with no instance — directly serves component-scanning's 'meta-present @Component' include filter and conditions' pre-construction reads (invariant #2 low-overhead).; Bean-name forwarding (`@AliasFor(Component.value)`) is resolved at expansion: the macro computes the default decapitalized name or honors `name=`/`value=` uniformly, so naming is identical across all stereotypes BY CONSTRUCTION — matches the 'stereotype never changes naming' hidden concept.; Thin macros (invariant #10): each stereotype macro is a one-liner over `leaf-codegen`; the flattening/name/role logic is a normal tested library; `cargo expand` shows a legible flat `ComponentDescriptor` a user could have hand-written.; Indexing-follows-meta-presence falls out: because the descriptor already carries `meta_markers`, the 'indexed via @Component' transitive edge is just 'this descriptor is in the table' — no separate indexer special-casing per stereotype.; Substrate-neutral and origin-agnostic: the same descriptor shape rides linkme/inventory/codegen, and a WASM-module-contributed bean produces the same descriptor with a different factory — the stereotype layer doesn't care where the bean is constructed.

**Cons:** User-extensibility costs a MACRO definition, not just a type/trait: minting `@DomainService` means declaring a new attribute macro (via leaf's `stereotype!` helper), which is heavier than Spring's 'annotate an annotation with @Component' — the DX is good but not literally one annotation on an annotation.; The vocabulary is closed at the MACRO layer: a downstream crate cannot compose a stereotype purely by re-using a marker TYPE; it must invoke leaf's codegen helper, so the 'extensible vocabulary' lives in macro-space, with the single-invocation proc-macro scope (a macro can't inspect the base stereotype's real definition) forcing `@AliasFor`-reciprocity checks against an imported const schema or a deferred pass.; Flattening freezes the merge at expansion: a stereotype default (e.g. role) cannot be RE-MERGED with a runtime overlay (a profile-supplied role override); any such late override must be a separate runtime layer — fine for stereotypes (Spring's are static) but a constraint to note.; Two core stereotypes that a user composes together (a stereotype meta-present in another) require the user's helper to forward the FULL flattened meta_markers list; getting transitive composition (`@RestController` = `@Controller` = `@Component`) right is codegen work leaf must own and test once.; Generic beans cannot be registered (no monomorphized symbol/TypeId) — the stereotype macro must hard-error on generic items and offer a per-instantiation `register_component!(Concrete)` escape hatch, same limit as every descriptor-based feature.

**Mechanisms considered:** proc-macro attribute per stereotype, each a thin one-liner over a `leaf-codegen` `component_descriptor!` helper that flattens meta-presence + resolves the name alias at expansion; a `stereotype!{ name => component(role, meta=[...]) }` user-facing macro-generator (or `#[derive(Stereotype)]`) so minting a new stereotype is one declaration; `&'static ComponentDescriptor` with a `meta_markers: &[MarkerId]` field carrying the flattened transitive marker set (the @Indexed-follows-@Component edge as data); const `TypeId`/`MarkerId` keys; `proc_macro::Span` provenance embedded for diagnostics; origin-agnostic erased async factory fn-pointer in the descriptor (WASM-module-ready); linkme distributed-slice as default static 'classpath' PLUS optional inventory/dynamic source pushing identical descriptors (CANDIDATES)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The descriptor is emitted into the declaring crate and rides whatever links it; meta-presence is FLATTENED into the descriptor's `meta_markers` at expansion, so the runtime never traverses an annotation tree across crates. A downstream stereotype macro forwards into base markers via absolute paths (`::leaf_core::markers::COMPONENT`), satisfying proc-macro hygiene. The cross-crate hazards are the shared discovery ones, NOT new to this feature: (Layer 0) a registration-only stereotype crate the binary never references contributes nothing → the app-root must force-link contributing crates (`enable!`/`scan!` prelude emitting `use crate as _;`); (DCE Layers A/B) require Rust ≥ 1.89 ELF `#[used(linker)]` default or `-z nostart-stop-gc`, pin `linkme >= 0.3.24`; (silent-empty-iterable) a self-check must assert expected-vs-found descriptor counts per contributing crate. Two stereotypes from two crates that both set a name for the same type can't be cross-checked at either site (single-invocation scope) → duplicate/conflict detection is a binary/startup self-check over the collected table. The static link-time table is the default 'classpath'; an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source contributes descriptors of the identical shape into the same table — a SECOND PRODUCER, not a second code path (CANDIDATE, not a decision). Generics: macro hard-errors, `register_component!(Concrete)` escape hatch.

**T2 — execution context:** Stereotype resolution is entirely COMPILE-TIME + COLD-PATH. The flattening/name-aliasing/role-tagging runs in the proc-macro/codegen process during compilation (nothing async, nothing blocks at runtime). At runtime the descriptor is `&'static` const data read SYNCHRONOUSLY on the startup/cold path (scanning's include-filter check, registration, role/name resolution) — never on a hot async path, so no executor, no `.await`, no cancellation surface for the stereotype layer itself. The async `factory` the descriptor CARRIES is invoked later by bean-instantiation, not by this feature. Errors are compile-time `compile_error!` (a malformed stereotype macro invocation, a generic annotated item, a same-type alias mismatch checked against the imported schema) or a startup self-check diagnostic (cross-crate name/duplicate conflict, expected-but-absent contribution) — both surface BEFORE any bean future spawns, so they never interact with async cancellation. If a linkme distributed slice is used (no life-before-main), there is no pre-main code at all; if inventory is the substrate for the dynamic source, those constructors run pre-main, must not panic/await, and only push descriptors (never live objects).

#### R2 — Marker traits + trait upcasting: a stereotype IS a trait, meta-presence IS a supertrait edge (type-native vocabulary)
Drop the string/MarkerId-keyed model for the COMPOSITION axis and make each stereotype a TYPE. Base: `trait Component { fn descriptor(&self) -> &'static ComponentMeta; }` (or a marker `trait Component {}` plus an associated `const META`). A stereotype is a trait with a SUPERTRAIT bound — `trait Service: Component {}`, `trait Repository: Component {}` — so META-PRESENCE is literally Rust supertrait reachability and the noun/adjective relationship is encoded in the type system: a `@Service` bean is-a `@Component` by `Service: Component`. A USER stereotype is the most idiomatic thing possible: `trait DomainService: Service {}` plus a blanket `impl<T: DomainServiceMarker> DomainService for T {}` wired by a derive — no codegen-helper indirection, declaring a new vocabulary word is declaring a trait. The leaf derive `#[derive(Service)]` (or `#[service]` shorthand) implements the marker trait(s) for the annotated type and fills an associated `const META: ComponentMeta` carrying role/name/scope (the name alias is a const projection: a stereotype's name attr is defined as forwarding `Component`'s name slot, enforced by a `const { assert!(...) }`). The registry stores `Arc<dyn Component>` and uses trait upcasting (stable 1.86) so a query for 'all `@Service` beans' is `Arc<dyn Component>` upcast/downcast to `dyn Service`, and 'injectable as supertype' (candidate-resolver) is the same coercion. `@Repository`-style behavior keys off the marker trait: the exception-translation feature bounds on `T: Repository` (compile-time) or scans descriptors for the Repository marker. DISCOVERY still needs the type registered into the substrate (a descriptor or `inventory::submit!`), so this realization layers the TYPE-based composition on top of a descriptor seed for scanning — it is type-native for the VOCABULARY but not self-sufficient for cross-crate discovery.

**Pros:** Maximally rust-native, most idiomatic 'extensible vocabulary': minting a stereotype is declaring a trait with a supertrait bound — closer to Spring's 'annotate an annotation' than any macro-generator, and the noun/adjective is/a relationship is in the type system.; Meta-presence and distance are COMPILE-TIME trait facts (supertrait reachability / depth) — the lowest possible overhead and strongest type safety; a query for `dyn Service` can only resolve beans that genuinely declared the stereotype.; Trait upcasting (stable 1.86) gives Spring's 'injectable as any supertype' for free at the registry layer, tightly aligning with candidate-resolver/autowiring-resolution — register once as `dyn Component`, satisfy queries for any declared supertrait.; `@Repository`-as-pointcut becomes a clean trait bound: a behavior feature (exception-translation) can `impl` only for `T: Repository`, so 'attach behavior to a stereotype later' is a non-breaking additive trait impl — directly serves the future-semantics intent.; Strongest diagnostics for the 'wrong stereotype' case: a bean that claims a stereotype it can't satisfy fails to compile with a focused `#[diagnostic::on_unimplemented]` message (invariant #9).

**Cons:** NOT self-sufficient for cross-crate discovery: a marker trait is compiler-resolved, but to be SCANNED the type must still be registered into the link-time/inventory/codegen substrate (it has no classpath presence as a type) — so this realization MUST be paired with a descriptor seed (R1-style) for discovery, making it a composition axis rather than a complete answer.; Dynamic/origin-agnostic sources don't fit: a WASM-module-contributed bean has no Rust type to bound on, so the type-as-stereotype model cannot represent dynamically-discovered stereotypes — it must delegate those to a record/descriptor shim, clashing with the origin-agnostic guardrail unless paired.; 'Nearest-wins' across UNRELATED stereotypes on one type is awkward: traits compose vertically (supertraits) but a type implementing two independent stereotype traits whose META consts disagree (e.g. two different default roles) has no trait-level 'aggregate index' — needs an out-of-band ordering rule, partially reintroducing a merge step, and edges toward specialization (an AVOID per the envelope) if used to express override.; `fn descriptor(&self)` needs an INSTANCE; anything conditions must read before construction has to live on associated `const META`, which is more rigid and can't carry runtime-overlaid values — a hard execution-context constraint on what attributes can exist where.; Enumerating 'all stereotypes/attributes of a type X' for tooling/diagnostics is hard (no flat attribute map), hurting the rich-diagnostics + config-metadata-style enumeration intent; blanket-impl + supertrait wiring can also produce coherence-error noise needing `#[diagnostic::do_not_recommend]`.

**Mechanisms considered:** marker trait per stereotype with supertrait bounds (`Service: Component`, user `DomainService: Service`) for meta-presence/distance as supertrait reachability; associated `const META: ComponentMeta` (role/name/scope) on the marker trait, read without an instance; const-projection name alias + `const { assert!(...) }` for identical-default enforcement; trait upcasting (stable 1.86) `Arc<dyn Component>` → `Arc<dyn Service>` for supertype queries / candidate-resolver; `#[diagnostic::on_unimplemented]` / `#[diagnostic::do_not_recommend]` for stereotype-bound error quality; a paired descriptor/`inventory::submit!` seed for the actual cross-crate DISCOVERY (this axis is not self-sufficient); explicitly NOT specialization for nearest-wins (envelope: avoid)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Trait impls are cross-crate-natural and the orphan rule is satisfied because the annotated type is local — so the VOCABULARY layer is immune to the DCE/Layer-0 hazards (meta-presence is compiler-resolved, not link-collected) — a genuine reliability win for composition. BUT discovery is not: to be scanned, an annotated type must still be registered into the discovery substrate exactly like every other component, so the trait-based stereotype rides ON TOP of whatever cross-crate mechanism Phase 2 picks (linkme/inventory/codegen) via a paired descriptor seed — and that seed inherits all the Layer-0/force-link/silent-empty self-check obligations. Because the META is on the type, two crates can each define their own stereotype traits and impl them for their own types with no central registry edit — best extensibility for the STATIC type-known case. Dynamic origins (WASM-module) cannot be expressed as types, so this realization is NON-TOTAL alone and must hand dynamic stereotypes to a descriptor/record shim. Generics: a generic bean still has no monomorphized symbol for discovery; the marker trait can be `impl`'d generically but the per-instantiation registration shim is still required for scanning.

**T2 — execution context:** Almost entirely compile-time: meta-presence/distance via the trait solver during compilation; `const META` evaluated at const-eval time. The only runtime touchpoints are the upcast coercions and `dyn Any` downcasts in the registry — O(1) synchronous vtable/TypeId operations on the cold resolution/scanning path, no `.await`, no blocking, no cancellation surface. `fn descriptor(&self)` accessors, where used, run synchronously AFTER construction and so are unusable for pre-construction conditions — a hard execution-context constraint pushing condition-relevant stereotype attributes (role, name, scope-default) onto associated `const META`. Errors are compile-time trait/coherence/const-assert failures (the earliest, richest possible per invariant #9) for the composition axis; the paired discovery seed contributes the same startup self-check timing as R1 for cross-crate count/conflict checks. No async Drop concern (markers hold no resources).

#### R3 — Runtime stereotype REGISTRY: thin per-stereotype declarations + a startup descriptor that maps marker → defaults/behavior-binding (late-bindable, pluggable repositoryAnnotationType analogue)
Keep the descriptor flat and DON'T flatten meta-presence at expansion — instead model the stereotype VOCABULARY itself as registered data so it is late-bindable and pluggable, mirroring Spring's runtime MergedAnnotations + the pluggable `repositoryAnnotationType` knob. The bean macro `#[component]`/`#[service]` emits a minimal descriptor carrying its DIRECTLY-applied marker(s) (`applied_markers: &[MarkerId]`, NOT the flattened transitive set) plus name/scope. SEPARATELY, each stereotype is itself a registered `StereotypeDef { marker: MarkerId, meta_present: &[MarkerId] (its own meta edges, e.g. Service → Component), default_role: Role, name_attr_alias: AliasEdge (the @AliasFor(Component.value) forwarding as DATA), indexed: bool }` contributed into a stereotype registry (the same discovery substrate). At STARTUP, a small engine in leaf-core computes the transitive closure: 'is type T meta-present @Component?' = walk T's applied_markers through the StereotypeDef.meta_present edges. This is the runtime MergedAnnotations analogue scoped to stereotypes. The payoff is OPENNESS: (a) a stereotype's behavior-binding is data — exception-translation registers 'beans carrying marker X get advisor Y', and the marker X is configurable (the `repositoryAnnotationType` pluggability: point translation at a user `@Dao` marker by registering a binding, not by recompiling); (b) `@Role` overrides and even dynamically-discovered (WASM-module) stereotypes fold into the same closure since the engine merges by data, not by where the data came from. A USER stereotype is: emit a `StereotypeDef` (one thin const) declaring `meta_present = [Component]` + defaults; the bean macro then just lists the user's marker. Stereotypes stay container-behaviorally identical to @Component because the closure only computes 'is-meta-present-Component + role/name', exactly Spring's 'register no extra behavior'.

**Pros:** Most faithful to Spring's runtime model and the pluggability intent: the `repositoryAnnotationType` knob ('point exception translation at your own @Dao') becomes a runtime binding registration, not a recompile — and 'stereotypes may carry additional semantics later' is a non-breaking data registration of a new marker→behavior binding.; Late-bindable + origin-agnostic: dynamically-discovered stereotypes (a WASM-module contributing both StereotypeDefs and beans) and any profile/environment role overlay fold into the SAME transitive-closure engine, because it merges by data not by site — keeps the vocabulary open where R1's flattening freezes it.; Cross-crate meta-presence CONFLICTS and duplicate-name detection happen naturally where the full descriptor + StereotypeDef set is visible (binary/startup) — turns the single-invocation blind spot into a runtime check with full context and rich diagnostics.; Macros stay maximally thin (#10): the bean macro emits a flat descriptor with DIRECT markers only; the stereotype macro emits one `StereotypeDef` const; ALL meta-presence/closure/alias logic lives in a normal `leaf-core` engine, fully unit-testable without compilation.; The stereotype registry doubles as the enumerable metadata source tooling wants: 'list all stereotypes, their meta edges, their default roles, and which beans carry them' is a flat queryable table (better than R2's trait-only model) for condition-report/config-metadata-style introspection.

**Cons:** Runtime transitive-closure cost on the cold path: computing meta-presence at startup for every bean × stereotype is more work than R1's pre-flattened descriptor; must be done once and cached (a startup-built map), and the cache touches the no-global-locks discipline (must be write-once-then-read, not a live lock).; Meta-presence misconfiguration (a StereotypeDef pointing at a non-existent base marker, an alias mismatch) regresses to RUNTIME/startup detection — exactly Spring's late-failure trap; invariant #9 prefers the earliest rich diagnostic, so this needs a dedicated startup validation pass (or a build-time lint) to recover early failure, where R1 catches malformed cases at compile time.; More moving parts at runtime: a StereotypeDef table, a closure engine, a cache — heavier than a const read; the descriptor must carry direct markers AND the engine must be correct about transitive distance (nearest-wins) as DATA, never link/section order (which is unspecified).; Two registries to keep coherent (bean descriptors + stereotype defs) and a happens-before discipline: all StereotypeDefs and any late overlays must be installed before the closure that consumes them — a documented ordering constraint.; The openness is also a footgun: 'anyone can register a stereotype→behavior binding' can surprise; needs the binding to be auditable (provenance: which crate registered which marker→advisor) so a silently-attached behavior is explainable.

**Mechanisms considered:** bean macro emits a flat descriptor carrying DIRECT `applied_markers` only (non-flattened); stereotype macro emits a separate const `StereotypeDef { marker, meta_present, default_role, name_attr_alias, indexed }`; a startup transitive-closure engine in leaf-core computing meta-presence + nearest-wins role/name from the StereotypeDef edge table (runtime MergedAnnotations analogue scoped to stereotypes); write-once startup-built cache of (TypeId → resolved {is_component, role, name, markers}) to amortize the closure — no live lock; marker→behavior BINDING registry (exception-translation's advisor keyed to a configurable marker = the pluggable repositoryAnnotationType analogue); late runtime overlay records for profile/dynamic-source-contributed stereotypes and role overrides (origin-agnostic); dedicated startup validation pass / optional build-time lint for alias + meta-presence misconfiguration (recovering some early-failure)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Descriptors and StereotypeDefs are both const and travel with their declaring crates; the FULL set (which beans carry which markers, which StereotypeDefs define which edges) is only knowable at the binary, so the closure engine is fed by the same discovery substrate as component-scanning (linkme/inventory/codegen) and is ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC: a record can come from a statically-linked crate OR a dynamically-loaded WASM-module layer that contributes StereotypeDefs + descriptors at runtime, since the engine merges by data. This is the most natural fit of the three for the static-classpath PLUS dynamic-WASM-source DUALITY — both are just producers feeding one closure (CANDIDATE, not a decision). Cross-crate meta-presence conflicts and duplicate names are detected at closure-assembly time with full context. Inherits the shared Layer-0/force-link/silent-empty obligations (the self-check can be richer here: 'expected a StereotypeDef from leaf_redis, found none'). Generics: descriptors still can't self-register a generic bean; per-instantiation `register_component!(Concrete)` required. The marker→behavior binding lets a behavior crate (leaf-persistence) attach to a stereotype WITHOUT editing leaf-core — dependencies still point inward.

**T2 — execution context:** The closure/merge runs at RUNTIME but strictly on the COLD path — startup scanning/registration planning — never on the steady-state hot path (honoring invariant #4: heavier work only on cold paths). It is synchronous CPU work bounded by the descriptor + StereotypeDef count, no `.await`, so it occupies the cold-path context that calls it and must not block an executor thread for long (fine — bounded). Late overlays (profile role override, dynamic-source stereotypes) MUST be installed before the closure that consumes them (a happens-before discipline the engine documents). Errors (meta-present base missing, alias mismatch, cross-crate name/duplicate conflict) are produced synchronously as rich diagnostics during the startup validation pass, BEFORE any bean future spawns — no async cancellation interaction. The write-once cache must use write-once-then-read (or fine-grained) semantics to avoid a global lock on the cold path. If inventory backs the dynamic source, its pre-main constructors only push descriptors/defs (never live objects, never async/IO).

### Rust clashes
- No annotation inheritance / no meta-annotations in Rust: Java lets you annotate `@Service` WITH `@Component` and the JVM sees `@Component` transitively. Rust attribute macros are inert and have NO transitive presence — `#[service]` carries nothing about `#[component]` unless leaf MANUFACTURES the edge: by codegen flattening (R1), by supertrait reachability (R2), or by an emitted StereotypeDef meta-edge table read at startup (R3). There is no language-level 'meta-present' concept to lean on; the entire noun/adjective is-a relationship must be encoded by one of these mechanisms.
- No runtime reflection over annotations: Spring resolves 'is this type meta-present @Component?' via MergedAnnotations over `java.lang.reflect` at runtime. Rust has zero runtime attribute reflection, so every fact the container needs (role, default name, meta-marker set) must be deliberately materialized into const/trait data at compile time — nothing can be 'read off the type' later. This is WHY a stereotype must lower to a descriptor/const/trait, not merely be an attribute.
- 'A bean is anything meta-annotated @Component' (the scanner's default include filter) has no classpath to scan: Spring's ClassPathScanningCandidateComponentProvider enumerates the classpath and asks each type 'meta-present @Component?'. Rust has no global classpath — the set of stereotype-bearing types across crates is only knowable where the crate graph is visible (the binary), assembled via link-time registry / inventory / build-time codegen, with the Layer-0 (crate must be referenced) and DCE silent-empty hazards. So the include-filter check shifts from 'scan and ask' to 'read the descriptor's already-materialized meta-marker set' — discovery is registration, not scanning.
- Single-invocation proc-macro scope defeats `@AliasFor(Component.value)` cross-checking at the source: a stereotype macro sees only its OWN invocation tokens and cannot inspect the base `@Component`'s real declaration to validate name-alias reciprocity/identical-default (which Spring checks at merge time). leaf must check the alias against a hard-coded/imported const schema (absolute paths to leaf-core) at expansion (R1/R2 compile-time) or defer it to a startup/build pass (R3) — it cannot be a free local check.
- No classpath for the cross-crate 'aggregate' set means stereotype CONFLICTS (two crates naming the same type, or contradictory stereotype defaults) are structurally a binary/startup concern, not a per-site one — unlike Spring where the conflict surfaces when the single classpath is scanned.
- `@Repository`'s 'detect beans whose CLASS carries @Repository and weave an advisor' has no reflective class-scan in Rust: the pointcut must be DATA (a meta-marker on the descriptor that exception-translation queries, R1/R3) or a TRAIT BOUND (`T: Repository`, R2). The pluggable `repositoryAnnotationType` ('point translation at your own @Dao') is trivial as a runtime binding (R3) but awkward as a frozen compile-time bound (R2) — a genuine open-vs-closed tension this feature inherits from exception-translation.
- `@Role` orthogonality survives cleanly (role is just a const field on the descriptor / META), BUT 'nearest-wins' distance-ordering between a directly-applied attribute and a stereotype-default has no language analogue — it must be data resolved at codegen (R1) or runtime (R3), NEVER link/section order (unspecified). In R2 the supertrait lattice gives a partial order but unrelated stereotypes on one type have no aggregate index, edging toward specialization (an AVOID) if used for override.
- Generic beans cannot be link-registered: a generic `Component<T>` has no monomorphized symbol/TypeId until instantiated, so a stereotype on a generic type cannot produce a single registered descriptor — the macro must hard-error and demand per-instantiation `register_component!(Concrete)`. Spring's type-erased annotations have no such limit.
- Spring tolerates merge-time RUNTIME failure for stereotype/alias misconfiguration; leaf's invariant #9 wants the earliest rich diagnostic, so compile-time `compile_error!` (R1/R2) is a divergence-as-improvement — but only the codegen/trait realizations achieve it; the runtime-registry realization (R3) inherits Spring's late-failure trap unless given a startup validation pass or build-time lint.

### Interactions
- **annotation-model** — DIRECT upstream dependency and the engine BENEATH this feature. Stereotypes ARE the canonical consumer of annotation-model's three capabilities: meta-presence ('is this type meta-present @Component?'), the `@AliasFor(Component.value)` name-forwarding (uniform bean-naming across stereotypes), and distance-ordered merge (directly-applied @Scope/role beats a stereotype default). R1 consumes annotation-model's compile-time-flattened descriptor; R3 consumes its runtime MergedAnnotations engine; R2 substitutes supertrait reachability for meta-presence. The annotation-model author's openNote explicitly flags THIS feature as its first consumer to co-review — the stereotype-minting DX here MUST match the vocabulary-extensibility cost of annotation-model's chosen realization.
- **component-scanning** — PRIMARY consumer. Scanning's default include filter is literally 'meta-present @Component', which is exactly the meta-presence fact this feature materializes; scanning reads the stereotype's role/name/scope defaults from discovered candidates WITHOUT loading/constructing them. The cross-crate assembly of stereotype-bearing descriptors shares scanning's discovery substrate (link-time slice / inventory / build-time codegen) — same Layer-0/force-link/silent-empty self-check obligations. @Indexed-follows-@Component means indexability is the same transitive edge, not per-stereotype special-casing.
- **bean-definition** — Stereotype annotations are the DECLARATION SITES the macro reads to emit the bean-definition seed: the stereotype sets the definition's role (APPLICATION default), default name, and any scope/lazy carried on the marker. 'Stereotype is metadata-about-the-source that becomes definition fields' — the descriptor this feature emits IS (a thin form of) bean-definition's RawBeanDefinition seed; the two features must agree on that seed shape (role/name/scope/meta-markers fields).
- **bean-naming** — The uniform-naming-across-stereotypes contract lives here: because each stereotype's name/value attr is `@AliasFor(Component.value)`, the default decapitalized-type-name and explicit-name override behave identically regardless of which stereotype is chosen. This feature resolves the name (at expansion in R1, on the const META in R2, via the alias-edge closure in R3); bean-naming owns the collision/override-vs-shadow policy and the fully-qualified-name fallback for same-simple-name clashes.
- **candidate-resolver / autowiring-resolution** — R2's trait-upcasting directly serves the 'injectable as any supertype' resolution model (register `Arc<dyn Component>`, satisfy a `dyn Service` query). A stereotype marker can also act as an implicit qualifier the resolver matches on; the resolver reads the merged stereotype/qualifier metadata to decide which beans QUALIFY. The role tag is NOT a selection input (orthogonal), but stereotype-as-qualifier is.
- **scopes** — A stereotype may carry a default @Scope (Spring's stereotypes don't, but user stereotypes can compose one), read via the merged view with distance-ordering: a directly-applied @Scope beats a stereotype-default scope (the 'nearest-wins' / @Scope-not-inherited-from-supertype rule). This feature carries the scope default; scopes owns the multiplicity/lifetime semantics.
- **exception-translation** — The ONE behavior-bearing stereotype's mechanics live HERE-adjacent: @Repository is just a natural pointcut, and exception-translation is the feature that weaves the DataAccessException-translation advisor onto beans carrying the Repository marker. This feature provides the marker (as a meta-marker on the descriptor in R1/R3, or a `T: Repository` bound in R2); exception-translation provides the advisor. The pluggable repositoryAnnotationType ('point translation at your own @Dao') is a binding in R3, a configurable marker query in R1, or a trait bound in R2 — the open-vs-closed tension is shared between the two features.
- **aop-proxying / auto-proxy-creator** — Stereotypes are 'clean pointcut targets for AOP and tooling' (Spring's stated intent): a stereotype marker is a natural pointcut for any advisor (the @Repository→translation case is the canonical instance, but @Service/@Controller can be advice targets too). The auto-proxy-creator queries 'which beans carry stereotype/marker X' from the descriptors this feature produces.
- **configuration-classes / import-composition** — @Configuration is itself a @Component-meta-present stereotype (so config classes are discovered by the same scanning path), and Boot's @SpringBootApplication composes @Configuration + @ComponentScan + @EnableAutoConfiguration via @AliasFor attribute-override forwarding — the same composed-annotation machinery this feature exercises. leaf's leaf-boot entry annotation will be built on whatever stereotype-composition mechanism is chosen here.
- **conditions / condition-family** — A condition must read a stereotype's metadata (role, markers, name) WITHOUT constructing the bean to decide registration — this feature supplies the 'read without constructing' const data (R1 descriptor / R2 const META / R3 closure result). Whether ANY stereotype attribute a condition needs is runtime-overlaid (profile-supplied) decides if R1's fully-precomputed model suffices or R3's late-bindable layer is needed — a phase2Question.
- **startup-instrumentation / condition-report / config-metadata** — Tooling wants to ENUMERATE 'all stereotypes, their meta edges, default roles, and which beans carry them' with declaration-site provenance for self-documenting, accountable diagnostics. R1/R3's flat descriptor/registry expose an enumerable table + `proc_macro::Span` provenance; R2's trait-only model makes enumeration harder (no flat map). The @Role classification feeds tooling's 'filter infrastructure beans out of overviews'.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Where is meta-presence resolved — compile-time flattening (R1, frozen, zero-overhead, earliest diagnostics) vs runtime closure over a StereotypeDef edge table (R3, late-bindable, origin-agnostic, Spring-faithful) vs the type system's supertrait lattice (R2)? This is a direct instance of the deferred metadata/codegen-boundary and compile-vs-runtime decisions, and it must be co-resolved with annotation-model's identical question (the two features share the same merge engine — they must NOT diverge).
- Is leaf's stereotype vocabulary expressed as DATA (MarkerId-keyed descriptors, R1/R3) or as TYPES (marker traits with supertrait bounds, R2)? This couples to the type-erasure & registry substrate decision and determines whether dynamically-discovered (WASM-module) stereotypes can exist at all — a type-based vocabulary cannot represent a marker that has no Rust type, while a record-based one can.
