- What is the stereotype-minting DX, and does its extensibility cost match annotation-model's chosen realization? Spring's bar is 'annotate your annotation with @Component' (one line); R1 costs a macro-generator invocation, R2 costs a trait + derive, R3 costs a StereotypeDef const. The annotation-model author explicitly asked for THIS to be co-reviewed — the answer is cross-cutting, not resolvable within either feature alone.
- How is the @Repository-style 'marker is a pointcut for behavior' modeled, and how pluggable is repositoryAnnotationType? A runtime marker→advisor binding (R3) makes 'point translation at your own @Dao' trivial but defers misconfiguration to startup; a compile-time `T: Repository` bound (R2) is type-safe but frozen. This is shared with exception-translation and aop-proxying and is an instance of the open-vs-closed and conditional-strategy tensions — do not resolve here.
- Should stereotype/@AliasFor misconfiguration fail at compile time (compile_error!, R1/R2), at a build-time validation pass, or at startup (R3)? An instance of the deferred unified error/diagnostics model and the invariant-#9 'earliest richest diagnostic' principle; the answer differs per realization and rides on the cross-crate-conflict-detection point (which is structurally a binary/startup concern regardless).
- How is the cross-crate aggregate set of stereotype-bearing descriptors assembled and force-linked, and where are cross-crate name/meta-presence conflicts detected? This rides entirely on the deferred cross-crate composition & tooling model (link-time slice vs inventory vs build-time codegen) plus the Layer-0/DCE/silent-empty self-check mandate — shared with component-scanning and every discovery feature; the static-classpath-plus-optional-dynamic-WASM-source duality is a candidate to keep open here.
- Is there a single shared descriptor/seed that this feature, bean-definition, and component-scanning all consume (one ComponentDescriptor/RawBeanDefinition shape), per unification invariant #11 — or does each carry its own? The stereotype descriptor IS effectively bean-definition's seed plus scanning's include-filter data; whether they unify wants resolution alongside the registry substrate so the whole framework reads one shape.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent — read the full stereotype section (lines 429-483: noun/adjective design intent, uniform naming via @AliasFor(Component.value), @Controller as the web-flavored core stereotype, @Repository as the lone behavior-bearing exception with its pluggable repositoryAnnotationType; lines 488-516: meta-presence transitivity, the three @AliasFor modes; line 560: @Component-is-meta-annotated-@Indexed so indexing follows the SAME transitive edge; lines 614-616: @Role as deliberately-orthogonal informational classification). The single load-bearing intent is the SEPARATION OF ARCHITECTURAL SEMANTICS FROM CONTAINER BEHAVIOR: a stereotype must be container-behaviorally IDENTICAL to the base @Component marker (no extra post-processor, no extra proxying, no instantiation change), differing only in documented role + being a clean pointcut target. Every realization preserves this — R1 makes it a compile-time structural identity (strongest), R2 makes it an is-a type relationship, R3 makes it 'the closure only computes is-meta-present-Component + role/name'.

The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters for THIS feature — WHERE the noun/adjective meta-presence edge is manufactured and how the vocabulary is extended: R1 = codegen flattening (vocabulary is a macro layer; meta-presence frozen into the descriptor at expansion; best overhead + earliest diagnostics); R2 = marker traits + supertrait reachability + trait upcasting (vocabulary is the type system; most rust-native minting DX; but NOT self-sufficient for cross-crate discovery and cannot represent dynamic/WASM stereotypes — must pair with a descriptor seed); R3 = runtime stereotype registry computing the meta-presence closure from emitted StereotypeDef edges (vocabulary is registered data; late-bindable, origin-agnostic, Spring-faithful, best fit for the static-plus-dynamic-source duality and the pluggable repositoryAnnotationType; but defers misconfiguration to startup). They are composable along a 'how frozen is the meta-presence edge' axis, not mutually exclusive.

I deliberately stayed substrate-neutral on the cross-crate collection mechanism (linkme vs inventory vs build-time codegen) and on the registry/ownership question — both are Phase 2 decisions surfaced in phase2Questions, and this feature is a near-pure CONSUMER of annotation-model and the discovery substrate rather than a definer of either.

Strong recommendation to Phase 2/3, echoing the annotation-model author's openNote: this feature and annotation-model MUST be co-resolved — stereotypes are annotation-model's first and defining consumer, and the stereotype-minting DX (the noun/adjective extensibility cost) is exactly the test of whether annotation-model's chosen merge realization delivers Spring-grade 'mint a stereotype and it just works'. A likely hybrid mirrors annotation-model's own recommendation: R1 (compile-time-flattened descriptor) as the DEFAULT for the static common case, R3's runtime closure kept as the late-merge/origin-agnostic layer for dynamic (WASM-module) and profile-overlaid stereotypes, and R2's trait-upcasting reserved specifically for the registry's 'injectable as supertype' coercion (candidate-resolver) rather than as the whole vocabulary. The @Repository behavior must NOT live in this feature — it is a marker (data/trait) here, with the advisor owned by exception-translation; keep the pointcut origin-agnostic so a behavior crate attaches without editing leaf-core.

Names ('ComponentDescriptor', 'StereotypeDef', 'MarkerId', 'ComponentMeta', 'Role', marker traits 'Component/Service/Repository') are Phase-1 placeholders. Wrote no files. Grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (lines 429-483, 488-516, 560, 614-616); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (Layer-0/DCE silent-empty, linkme-default + register-thin-descriptors-not-components guidance lines 165-171, generics-cannot-register, force-link mandate); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (stable envelope: const TypeId 1.91, trait upcasting 1.86, Any+downcast, #[diagnostic::on_unimplemented/do_not_recommend], specialization=AVOID).

## Component Scanning / Discovery  `[component-scanning]`

Spring's component scanning is a five-stage COLD-PATH discovery pipeline whose intent leaf must replicate (not its mechanism): (1) BASE-PACKAGE SCOPE RESOLUTION — `@ComponentScan` resolves a set of base packages (explicit `basePackages`/`basePackageClasses`, `${...}` placeholders, or — crucially — DEFAULTING to the package of the annotated class, which is why `@SpringBootApplication` sits at the package root and discovery "radiates downward"). Scope is deliberately bounded, not global. (2) RESOURCE ENUMERATION — the package maps to `classpath*:.../**/*.class`, a tunable `resourcePattern`. (3) METADATA READING WITHOUT LOADING — a `MetadataReader` reads `ClassMetadata`+`AnnotationMetadata` from BYTECODE so a candidate can be inspected (and rejected) WITHOUT running static initializers, force-loading transitive types, or bloating the ClassLoader; "discovery is a metadata query, not an execution step." (4) FILTERING — `isCandidateComponent` = matches NO exclude filter AND ≥1 include filter, with EXCLUDES EVALUATED FIRST and short-circuiting ("includes define the universe of interest, excludes carve out exceptions, exceptions win"); the sole default include filter is "meta-present @Component" (so every stereotype is caught transitively without enumeration); FilterType ∈ {ANNOTATION, ASSIGNABLE_TYPE, ASPECTJ, REGEX, CUSTOM}, CUSTOM being the metadata-driven escape hatch; a second gate requires the type be concrete+independent (not interface/abstract/non-static-inner) unless it declares `@Lookup`. (5) REGISTRATION — generate the name (pluggable `nameGenerator`, FQN generator for cross-package simple-name clashes), resolve `@Scope` ON THE CONCRETE CLASS ONLY (no scope inheritance), apply common-definition annotations (@Lazy/@Primary/@DependsOn/@Role/@Description), optionally wrap in a scoped proxy, and register a definition. Default `lazyInit=false` (eager singletons = fail-fast at refresh). Re-detection of the SAME class is compatible/tolerated (`isCompatible`); only a same-name-but-different-source clash is a real conflict. The Candidate Index (`@Indexed`→`spring.components`) was the build-time precompute optimization whose all-or-nothing/silent-empty failure mode drove its deprecation in favor of holistic AOT — a direct cautionary tale for leaf's discovery substrate. This feature is the PRIMARY consumer of annotation-model (meta-presence include filter) and component-stereotypes (the marker set), produces the seed that bean-definition/bean-registry register, and is the canonical instance of the cross-crate discovery problem (no classpath; link-time/codegen substrate; Layer-0/DCE/silent-empty hazards).

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Macro-flattened candidate descriptors + runtime filter pipeline (link-time-collected 'classpath', filters applied as data at startup)
There is NO scan and NO bytecode reader. The `#[component]`/stereotype macro emits, per annotated type, a single `&'static CandidateDescriptor` (the same seed shape bean-definition consumes) into the discovery substrate (the default static link-time 'classpath' — a distributed slice; an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source can push descriptors of the identical shape into the same table, origin-agnostic). The descriptor carries the already-FLATTENED metadata scanning needs to FILTER without loading: `{ type_id, produced_type, name: BeanName, scope, lazy: Option<bool>, role: Role, primary/fallback, meta_markers: &'static [MarkerId], assignable_types: &'static [TypeId], origin_module_path: &'static str, span_provenance, factory: <origin-agnostic erased async ctor> }`. At STARTUP (cold path) a `ScanPlan` runs Spring's pipeline as pure data: (a) SCOPE — instead of base-packages-on-a-classpath, the scan is scoped to a set of declared module-path prefixes / opted-in crates (the rust-native analogue of `basePackages`; the default 'radiate downward from the annotated root' becomes 'the app's own crate + explicitly enabled integration crates' per the cross-crate doc's scope-don't-make-it-global mandate); (b) FILTER — an ordered `Vec<Filter>` where each `Filter` is an enum {MetaPresent(MarkerId), AssignableTo(TypeId), ModulePathRegex(...), Custom(fn(&CandidateDescriptor)->bool)} — EXCLUDES evaluated first and short-circuit, then ≥1 include must match; default include filter = `MetaPresent(COMPONENT)`, exactly Spring's; the CUSTOM filter gets the whole descriptor (the metadata-driven escape hatch — and because the descriptor is const data, it is literally 'read without loading/constructing'); (c) the concrete+independent gate is a const flag the macro sets (it hard-errors on generic/abstract targets, offering `register_component!(Concrete)`); (d) REGISTER — survivors flow to bean-naming/bean-definition. Filtering is a synchronous fold over `&'static [CandidateDescriptor]`; nothing is constructed.

**Pros:** 'Read metadata without loading' falls out structurally and is STRICTLY STRONGER than Spring's bytecode trick: the descriptor is const data wholly decoupled from the type's constructor, so a CUSTOM filter or a condition can reject a candidate with zero risk of running an initializer or force-loading a transitive type (invariant #2; the no-load intent realized natively).; Lowest overhead (invariant #2): no I/O, no bytecode parsing, no resource enumeration — the entire pipeline is a synchronous fold over a contiguous const slice on the cold startup path; the substrate (linkme) is zero runtime collection cost.; Exclude-then-include + the filter-type enum map 1:1 onto Spring's `isCandidateComponent`, so the mental model ('includes define the universe, excludes carve exceptions, exceptions win') and the CUSTOM escape hatch transfer verbatim — high intent parity.; Thin macros (invariant #10): the macro emits one hand-writable const descriptor; ALL scope/filter/registration logic lives in a normal, unit-testable `leaf-core` `ScanPlan` engine; `cargo expand` shows a flat descriptor.; Origin-agnostic and substrate-neutral: the same descriptor shape rides linkme/inventory/codegen and a WASM-contributed candidate filters identically — discovery is filtering-the-registry, not scanning-a-classpath.; Scoping is honest and predictable (cross-crate doc's #1 concern): 'base packages' become module-path prefixes / opted-in crates, so a transitive dependency cannot silently inject beans — it must be in scope.

**Cons:** The 'classpath' is whatever was link-collected, so it inherits ALL the discovery hazards as the feature's own: Layer-0 (a registration-only crate the binary never references contributes nothing → needs the `enable!`/`scan!` force-link prelude emitting `use crate as _;`), DCE Layers A/B (needs Rust ≥1.89 ELF `#[used(linker)]` default or `-z nostart-stop-gc`, pinned `linkme ≥0.3.24`), and the SILENT-EMPTY failure mode (a self-check must assert expected-vs-found candidate counts per scoped crate — Spring's deprecated-index footgun reincarnated if not guarded).; Filters frozen on metadata available at the SCAN: a filter that wants to match on a fact only known at runtime (a resolved property value) needs that fact materialized into the descriptor or pushed into a runtime overlay — the REGEX/ASPECTJ filter analogues are over module-path strings, not over a live type hierarchy.; 'Base package = annotated class's package' has no clean Rust analogue: module paths are not first-class scan targets the way Java packages are; the default-scope rule must be re-specified (likely 'the binary crate + enabled crates'), a deliberate divergence to document.; No 'resource enumeration' stage to tune (`resourcePattern`) — that lever simply does not exist; narrowing scope is by crate/module-prefix instead, a smaller surface.; Cross-crate duplicate/conflict detection (Spring's `isCompatible` re-scan tolerance vs ConflictingBeanDefinitionException) is a binary/startup self-check over the collected table, not a per-site check (single-invocation proc-macro scope).

**Mechanisms considered:** proc-macro emitting one flat `&'static CandidateDescriptor` per component (the shared bean-definition seed) into a distributed slice; linkme distributed-slice as the default static 'classpath' + optional inventory/dynamic WASM source pushing identical descriptors (CANDIDATES, not a decision); a normal `leaf-core` `ScanPlan` engine running exclude-then-include filtering as a synchronous fold over const descriptors; `Filter` enum {MetaPresent(MarkerId), AssignableTo(TypeId), ModulePathRegex, Custom(fn(&Descriptor)->bool)} mirroring Spring's FilterType incl. the CUSTOM metadata escape hatch; const `meta_markers`/`assignable_types`/`origin_module_path` on the descriptor so the include-filter check is a const-data read (no load); scope expressed as module-path prefixes / opted-in crate set (rust-native `basePackages`); force-link `enable!`/`scan!` prelude for Layer-0; startup self-check asserting expected-vs-found candidate counts (defeats silent-empty); `register_component!(Concrete)` escape hatch for generics; macro hard-errors on generic/abstract targets

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** This realization IS the cross-crate discovery problem in its purest form. Descriptors are emitted into their declaring crate and ride whatever links it; the FULL candidate set is only knowable at the binary, so the `ScanPlan` engine is fed by the same substrate Phase 2 picks (linkme default / inventory / build-time codegen). The Spring 'classpath' becomes 'the union of descriptors in linked, in-scope crates'. Hazards owned here: Layer-0 (force-link the in-scope crates via a generated `scan!{ leaf_redis, app_crate }`/`#[leaf::main]` prelude emitting `use … as _;`); DCE A/B (Rust ≥1.89 ELF default or `-Clink-arg=-Wl,-z,nostart-stop-gc`; pin `linkme ≥0.3.24`; document GNU-ld-vs-LLD); SILENT-EMPTY (a `leaf doctor`/startup self-check asserting each in-scope crate contributed its expected count — directly answering the deprecated-index all-or-nothing footgun). Base-package SCOPING maps to module-path prefixes / opted-in crates, honoring 'scope discovery, don't make it implicitly global'. The static link-time table is the default 'classpath'; a dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source is a SECOND PRODUCER of identical descriptors, not a second code path (CANDIDATE). Generics: hard-error + `register_component!`. Must test the FINAL BINARY under release+LTO+--gc-sections across the target/linker matrix, not lib tests (which hide dropping).

**T2 — execution context:** Entirely COLD-PATH. Macro flattening runs at COMPILE time. The `ScanPlan` (scope resolution + exclude-then-include filtering + registration planning) runs SYNCHRONOUSLY at startup/refresh, bounded by descriptor count — CPU-only, no `.await`, no blocking I/O (there is no classpath to read), so it cannot stall an executor for long and has no cancellation surface of its own. The descriptor's async `factory` is invoked LATER by bean-instantiation (eager non-lazy singletons at refresh per `lazyInit=false`), not by this feature. Errors are compile-time (`compile_error!` on a generic/abstract component, malformed filter) or a startup VALIDATION-PASS diagnostic (cross-crate name/duplicate conflict, an in-scope crate that contributed zero candidates) — all BEFORE any bean future spawns, so they never interact with async cancellation. If linkme backs the substrate there is no pre-main code; if inventory backs the optional dynamic source, its constructors run pre-main and must only push descriptors (never live objects, never await/IO).

#### R2 — Build-time codegen scan (sqlx/AOT model): a build step sweeps the resolved graph, applies filters, emits an explicit candidate manifest
Lift Spring's index/AOT lesson directly: do the expensive discovery ONCE at build time and emit explicit, inspectable registration — the deterministic middle path the cross-crate doc recommends as the portable fallback. A `leaf-codegen` `build.rs` (or a `cargo leaf prepare` CLI) in the BINARY crate reads the resolved dependency graph (`cargo metadata`) and a manifest convention to learn which in-scope crates participate (the rust-native `basePackages`/starter list), then — per in-scope crate — reads that crate's emitted per-crate candidate manifest (each contributing crate's macro writes a small `OUT_DIR`/checked-in list of its `CandidateDescriptor` metadata: type, markers, assignable types, scope, name). The build step APPLIES THE FILTER PIPELINE AT BUILD TIME (exclude-then-include over the manifest metadata, including REGEX/CUSTOM filters whose inputs are static) and emits an explicit `fn register_scanned(ctx) { ctx.register(leaf_redis::__candidate_FooBar()); … }` into the binary's `OUT_DIR`, `include!`'d into the binary. The result is a flat, checked-in, `--check`-able candidate set the runtime consumes with zero collection magic. Filters that genuinely need runtime data still run at startup over the emitted set (a thin runtime tail), but the bulk of scanning is frozen.

**Pros:** BEST platform reliability (cross-crate doc): fully platform-independent incl. wasm/embedded/staticlib; sidesteps Layer-0, both DCE layers, and non-deterministic DCE ENTIRELY — no silent-empty failure mode, which is precisely the footgun that killed Spring's index.; Most faithful to Spring's ACTUAL trajectory: this IS the AOT/holistic-build-time-precompute lesson the docs say superseded the index — 'build-time processing is the right home for startup-cost optimization', generalized.; Determinism + CI accountability: a checked-in, `--check`-able manifest (sqlx model) means a silently-lost contribution FAILS CI, and the generated registration file is human-inspectable — strong reproducibility DX and the richest possible 'what was discovered and why' diagnostic (invariant #9).; Filters with static inputs (REGEX over type/module names, ASSIGNABLE_TYPE, even most CUSTOM) run at BUILD time with full visibility, so the include/exclude decision is made once and is debuggable — and 'read without loading' is trivially satisfied (no class is ever loaded; only manifest metadata is read).; Lowest RUNTIME overhead: the runtime sees explicit `register(...)` calls, no slice walk, no pre-main code, no conditions to evaluate for pure filtering (only the runtime-data-dependent tail remains).

**Cons:** A build step is required (invariant #1 DX cost): slower iterative builds, `OUT_DIR` hygiene/idempotency obligations, and a `cargo leaf prepare` ceremony for the checked-in path — 'less magic' than R1's annotate-and-forget.; Still NOT a true classpath scan: it enumerates DECLARED in-scope crates via metadata, not 'everything linked' — so it shares R1's scoping reality (which is arguably correct per the predictability mandate, but means a crate not in the manifest is invisible, and that must be a loud diagnostic, not silent).; Two-stage metadata: each contributing crate must emit its per-crate manifest AND the binary build step must consume them — more moving parts and a happens-before ordering (a crate's manifest must exist before the binary's sweep), plus a stable manifest schema to version.; build.rs directionality wall: the build step lives in the BINARY (the only place the whole graph is visible); a kernel/feature crate's build.rs cannot sweep its dependents — so the scan responsibility is structurally pinned to the app root (consistent with the topology but a constraint to honor).; Generic components still cannot be auto-included (no monomorphized identity in the manifest) — per-instantiation `register_component!(Concrete)` remains required; the sweep can at most LIST that an explicit registration is expected.

**Mechanisms considered:** `build.rs`/`cargo leaf prepare` in the binary crate driving a `cargo metadata` sweep of in-scope crates (rust-native basePackages); per-contributing-crate candidate manifests (macro emits a small `OUT_DIR`/checked-in metadata list) consumed by the binary's sweep; build-time application of the exclude-then-include filter pipeline over manifest metadata (REGEX/ASSIGNABLE/CUSTOM with static inputs resolved at build time); emit explicit `fn register_scanned(ctx){ … }` into `OUT_DIR`, `include!`'d into the binary (no link magic); checked-in, `--check`-able manifest (sqlx model) so CI catches lost contributions — the anti-silent-empty mechanism; a thin runtime filter tail for filters whose inputs are only known at runtime (resolved properties); `register_component!(Concrete)` for generics; manifest records expected explicit registrations

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Strongest T1 story: by anchoring whole-graph awareness at the BINARY's build step (the only point that sees the full crate graph) and emitting EXPLICIT registrations, it avoids Layer-0 (the explicit calls reference the crates, forcing link), both DCE layers (no link sections relied upon), and the silent-empty failure mode (the `--check`-able manifest turns a missing contribution into a CI failure). It cannot scan crates the build doesn't know about — scope is the declared in-scope set (cargo metadata + manifest convention), which the cross-crate doc frames as the honest analogue of `basePackages`/starters and the answer to the predictability concern. The build.rs directionality wall means this MUST live in the app root, never the kernel. The same emitted-candidate shape is origin-agnostic; a dynamic WASM-module source would be a separate runtime producer feeding the SAME runtime register API (the static/dynamic duality preserved). Generics: explicit registration shim, recorded in the manifest. The genuinely portable path for wasm/embedded where link sections do not exist.

**T2 — execution context:** Two contexts. BUILD-TIME: the sweep + filter pipeline runs in a host-target build executable during compilation — synchronous, may do file I/O over `OUT_DIR`/manifests, must be deterministic/idempotent (OUT_DIR not cleaned between builds); no async, no runtime relevance. RUNTIME: the emitted `register_scanned` is a flat sequence of synchronous explicit calls on the COLD startup/refresh path — no slice walk, no `.await`; the only runtime work is the thin tail of runtime-data-dependent filters (still cold-path, synchronous, bounded). The async `factory` is invoked later by bean-instantiation. Errors: build-time emit a build `error`/fail-the-build diagnostic with full context (the richest, earliest per invariant #9 for static filters); a stale manifest fails `--check` in CI; runtime-tail filter errors surface in the startup validation pass before bean futures spawn. No pre-main code, no cancellation surface.

#### R3 — Trait-marker discovery via the registry substrate + a runtime ScanFilter SPI (type-native candidacy, late-bindable filters)
Make CANDIDACY type-native and the FILTER PIPELINE a first-class runtime SPI, closer to Spring's pluggable filter objects than to a frozen list. A component is a TYPE implementing a marker trait (`trait Component {}`, stereotypes as supertraits `trait Service: Component {}` per the stereotypes design); the leaf derive both impls the marker AND registers a thin `CandidateDescriptor` into the substrate (the type-as-marker axis is NOT self-sufficient for cross-crate discovery — it still needs a registration seed — so this pairs a type-native candidacy check with a descriptor for collection). The registry stores `Arc<dyn Component>` factories keyed by `TypeId`, using trait upcasting (stable 1.86) so 'injectable as supertype' and 'all beans carrying stereotype S' are vtable coercions. The novel part is the FILTER layer: scanning is a runtime `ScanFilter` chain — `trait ScanFilter { fn verdict(&self, c: &CandidateDescriptor) -> Verdict; }` — registered as ORDERED beans/objects, with built-in implementations for the FilterType analogues (MetaPresent, AssignableTo via TypeId-subtype edges, ModulePathRegex, and a user Custom impl) and the exclude-first-short-circuit semantics enforced by the chain runner. Because filters are objects evaluated at startup over the collected descriptors, they are LATE-BINDABLE: a profile/environment-supplied exclude, or a dynamically-discovered (WASM-module) candidate, folds into the same chain — the most open analogue of `addExcludeFilter`/`addIncludeFilter`. The default chain is exactly one include filter: `MetaPresent(Component)`.

**Pros:** Type-native candidacy gives the strongest type safety and 'injectable as supertype' for free (trait upcasting 1.86) — register once as `dyn Component`, satisfy a `dyn Service` candidate query; tightly aligns with candidate-resolver/autowiring-resolution.; The `ScanFilter` SPI is the closest analogue to Spring's pluggable filter OBJECTS (`addIncludeFilter`/`addExcludeFilter`, FilterType.CUSTOM with full metadata access) and to the sentinel-default 'inherit context strategy then override' pattern — best for users who want metadata-driven, late-bound, composable filter logic.; Late-bindable + origin-agnostic filtering: profile-supplied excludes, environment-driven includes, and dynamically-discovered candidates all fold into ONE ordered chain because it filters by descriptor data, not by where the data came from — keeps discovery open where R1/R2 freeze it.; Filters are first-class beans → enumerable for tooling/diagnostics (condition-report/startup-instrumentation can show the exact include/exclude verdict per candidate with reasons), and the chain is unit-testable as plain objects (invariant #10 — macros stay thin; logic is library code).; 'Read without loading' holds: `ScanFilter::verdict` sees only the const `CandidateDescriptor`, never an instance, so a custom filter cannot trigger construction.

**Cons:** NOT self-sufficient for cross-crate discovery: a marker trait is compiler-resolved, but to be DISCOVERED a type must STILL register a descriptor into the substrate — so this realization carries the SAME Layer-0/DCE/silent-empty obligations as R1 for the registration seed; the type-native part buys candidacy clarity, not collection.; Runtime filter-chain cost on the cold path: evaluating an ordered SPI chain per candidate is more work than R1's const fold and R2's build-time freeze; must be bounded and done once (a startup ScanPlan), and the filter-bean registry brushes against the no-global-locks discipline (must be write-once-then-read).; Dynamic/origin-agnostic candidates clash with the type axis: a WASM-module candidate has no Rust type to mark, so it must enter purely as a descriptor (no marker trait) — meaning the type-native candidacy is non-total and the descriptor path is the real common denominator (an argument the type axis is better reserved for the registry's supertype coercion than as the whole discovery model).; Filter misconfiguration / an empty result regresses to RUNTIME detection (Spring's late-failure trap) unless a startup validation pass reports 'every candidate excluded' / 'expected crate contributed nothing' with reasons — invariant #9 wants the earliest rich diagnostic, which here means a dedicated pass, not a compile error.; Two registries to keep coherent (candidate descriptors + filter beans) plus a happens-before discipline (all filters installed before the ScanPlan that consumes them); blanket-impl marker wiring can produce coherence noise needing `#[diagnostic::do_not_recommend]`.

**Mechanisms considered:** marker trait `Component` + stereotype supertraits, derive impls the marker AND emits a `CandidateDescriptor` seed (candidacy is type-native, collection is descriptor-based); trait upcasting (stable 1.86) `Arc<dyn Component>`→`Arc<dyn Service>` for supertype candidate queries / candidate-resolver alignment; a runtime `ScanFilter` SPI chain (ordered beans) with built-in MetaPresent/AssignableTo/ModulePathRegex/Custom impls and exclude-first short-circuit; TypeId-subtype edge table for the ASSIGNABLE_TYPE filter analogue (no live class hierarchy in Rust); late runtime overlay filters (profile/environment-supplied include/exclude; dynamic WASM-source candidates) folded into one chain; write-once startup ScanPlan caching verdicts; no live global lock; startup validation pass reporting per-candidate include/exclude reasons + zero-contribution crates (recovers early failure); `#[diagnostic::do_not_recommend]` for blanket-impl noise

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Trait impls are cross-crate-natural and orphan-rule-safe (the type is local), so the CANDIDACY axis is immune to DCE/Layer-0 — a reliability win for 'is this a component'. BUT DISCOVERY is not: the descriptor seed rides the SAME substrate as R1 (linkme/inventory/codegen) and inherits Layer-0 force-link, DCE A/B, and silent-empty self-check obligations. The `ScanFilter` chain is assembled where the full descriptor + filter-bean set is visible (binary/startup), so cross-crate include/exclude conflicts and zero-contribution detection happen there with full context. Scope is the in-scope crate set feeding the substrate (same predictability discipline). Dynamic origins (WASM-module) cannot be types → enter as descriptors only, so the descriptor+filter path is the real cross-crate common denominator and the type axis is best reserved for the registry's supertype coercion. Generics: marker can be impl'd generically but discovery still needs a per-instantiation `register_component!(Concrete)`. The marker→behavior edge (e.g. @Repository) stays a descriptor marker / trait bound owned by exception-translation, not by scanning.

**T2 — execution context:** Mostly cold-path. Candidacy (marker trait / supertrait reachability, descriptor const) is COMPILE/const-time. The `ScanFilter` chain runs at STARTUP on the cold path — synchronous CPU work over collected descriptors, bounded by candidate×filter count, no `.await`, no blocking, no cancellation surface; it must not hold a global lock (write-once ScanPlan). Late overlays (profile/dynamic-source filters and candidates) MUST be installed before the ScanPlan consumes them (a documented happens-before). The registry's upcast/downcast coercions are O(1) synchronous vtable/TypeId ops on the cold resolution path. The async `factory` is invoked later by bean-instantiation. Errors: compile-time for a malformed marker/generic target; a startup VALIDATION-PASS diagnostic for filter misconfiguration, all-candidates-excluded, cross-crate conflict, or zero-contribution crate — produced synchronously before any bean future spawns, so no async-cancellation interaction. If inventory backs the dynamic source, its pre-main constructors only push descriptors.

### Rust clashes
- No classpath to scan: Spring's `ClassPathScanningCandidateComponentProvider` enumerates `classpath*:.../**/*.class` and asks each type 'meta-present @Component?'. Rust has NO global, enumerable, program-wide list of linked types; the set of components is only knowable where the crate graph is visible (the binary), assembled via link-time registry / inventory / build-time codegen. So Spring's stage-2 RESOURCE ENUMERATION and stage-3 'scan and ask' collapse into 'filter the already-collected descriptor table' — discovery is registration+filtering, not scanning.
- 'Read bytecode without loading the class' has no direct analogue but a STRONGER native form: there is no bytecode and a linked type is already 'loaded' in the JVM sense. The real cost Spring avoids — running the constructor / forcing transitive type loading — is avoided in Rust by reading a const `CandidateDescriptor` decoupled from the type's constructor. The intent ('discovery is a metadata query, not an execution step') is preserved by making all filter-relevant metadata const data; nothing can be 'read off the type' reflectively later, so it must be deliberately materialized at compile time.
- Base-package SCOPING does not map cleanly: Java packages are first-class scan roots and 'default = annotated class's package, radiate downward' is a clean rule. Rust module paths are not scan targets and there is no downward-radiating enumeration; the scope analogue is a set of opted-in crates / module-path prefixes. This is also where the cross-crate predictability mandate bites — the #[global_registration] author's conclusion is to NOT make discovery truly global, so leaf SHOULD re-specify base-packages as an explicit in-scope crate/prefix set (Spring Boot's `basePackages`/starter-list analogue), a deliberate, principled divergence.
- The DCE silent-empty failure mode is Spring's deprecated-index footgun reincarnated, and WORSE because it is the default path: a dropped registration (Layer-0 unreferenced crate, or `--gc-sections`/reachability) yields an EMPTY candidate set with NO error, can pass in debug and fail in release, and vary by linker. Spring deprecated its index precisely for the all-or-nothing/silent-invisible-beans failure; leaf must add an expected-vs-found self-check or it ships the same footgun as its DEFAULT discovery behavior.
- Single-invocation proc-macro scope defeats per-site conflict detection: Spring detects a `ConflictingBeanDefinitionException` when the single classpath is scanned and tolerates `isCompatible` re-detection of the same class. In Rust a scanning macro sees only its own invocation and cannot see sibling/downstream candidates, so duplicate-name and same-name-different-source conflicts are structurally a BINARY/STARTUP self-check over the collected table, not a per-site check.
- Generic components cannot be link-registered: a generic `Foo<T>` has no monomorphized symbol/TypeId until instantiated, so a `@Component`-on-a-generic has no single discoverable candidate. The macro must hard-error and demand per-instantiation `register_component!(Concrete)`. Spring's type-erased annotations have no such limit; this also breaks the clean 'scan a package, get all components' promise for generic beans.
- The CUSTOM filter's power changes character: Spring's CUSTOM filter has full `MetadataReader` access to make metadata-driven decisions without loading. In leaf the equivalent input is the const `CandidateDescriptor`, which is MORE constrained (only what the macro materialized) but SAFER (cannot accidentally load/construct). REGEX/ASPECTJ filters over a type/package NAME become regex over module-path/type-name strings; there is no live type hierarchy to AspectJ-match, and ASSIGNABLE_TYPE needs an explicit TypeId-subtype edge table (no runtime RTTI).
- The concrete+independent gate and the `@Lookup` exception don't translate directly: 'not an interface/abstract/non-static-inner unless it declares @Lookup methods' is a JVM-reflection gate. In Rust the macro knows statically whether the target is a concrete type, and 'abstract @Component' / non-static-inner classes have no analogue; the @Lookup-method-injection escape hatch (subclass to implement lookup) maps to a different deferral-primitive mechanism, so this gate is mostly subsumed by the macro hard-erroring on non-constructible targets.
- `@Scope` is read on the concrete class ONLY (no inheritance) — this actually MAPS WELL to Rust (a descriptor carries its own scope; there is no definition inheritance unless leaf builds one), so it is a clash that resolves in leaf's favor, but the 'parent bean-definition template + merged definition' that bean-definition models must be reconciled with 'scope is not inherited from a supertype'.
- Spring tolerates merge-time / scan-time RUNTIME failure for conflicts; invariant #9 wants the earliest rich diagnostic. Compile-time `compile_error!` (R1/R3 for malformed/generic targets) and build-time fail (R2) are divergence-as-improvement, but cross-crate conflicts and silent-empty are STRUCTURALLY binary/startup concerns regardless of realization — so leaf cannot push all scanning failures to compile time and must invest in a dedicated startup validation pass.

### Interactions
- **annotation-model** — DIRECT upstream and the engine beneath the default include filter. Scanning's sole default include filter is 'meta-present @Component', which IS annotation-model's meta-presence capability; whichever realization annotation-model picks (compile-time-flattened descriptor / runtime MergedAnnotations closure / supertrait reachability) determines how the include-filter check is evaluated here. The two MUST share the same meta-presence engine so the filter and the rest of the framework never diverge — co-resolve.
- **component-stereotypes** — DIRECT upstream and the reason scanning catches every stereotype 'for free'. Stereotypes materialize the `meta_markers` set the include filter reads; @Component-is-meta-present-@Indexed means indexability/discoverability follow the SAME transitive edge with no per-stereotype special-casing. Scanning consumes the stereotype's role/name/scope defaults from discovered candidates WITHOUT constructing them, and shares the exact discovery substrate + Layer-0/force-link/silent-empty self-check obligations. The CandidateDescriptor this feature filters IS the stereotype's emitted descriptor.
- **bean-definition** — PRIMARY downstream producer relationship. The CandidateDescriptor scanning emits/filters IS (a thin form of) bean-definition's RawBeanDefinition seed — the two features must agree on ONE seed shape (type, produced_type, name, scope, lazy, role, primary/fallback, meta_markers, assignable_types, factory). Registration (stage 5) hands survivors to bean-definition; the 'scope read on concrete class only, no inheritance' rule must reconcile with bean-definition's parent-template/merged-definition model.
- **bean-registry / container-core** — Scanning's stage-5 registration registers definitions INTO bean-registry; container-core's refresh-lifecycle drives WHEN scanning runs (a cold-path step before eager instantiation). The collected descriptor table is effectively the registry's input; container-core auto-detects post-processors among scanned candidates.
- **bean-naming** — Stage-5 name generation is delegated to bean-naming: default decapitalized type name, the pluggable nameGenerator analogue, and the FullyQualifiedAnnotationBeanNameGenerator analogue for cross-package (cross-module) simple-name clashes. Scanning supplies the candidate's default name; bean-naming owns collision-vs-override policy and the FQN fallback. The `isCompatible` re-detection tolerance vs ConflictingBeanDefinitionException distinction is shared between scanning's startup self-check and bean-naming.
- **conditions / condition-family** — Conditions are the runtime gate AFTER filtering: scanning's include/exclude filters decide 'what COULD be a candidate' (over const metadata, without constructing); conditions decide 'what DOES register' as a pure predicate over container state. The cross-crate doc is explicit that link-time collection answers 'what could contribute' and runtime conditions answer 'what wires up' — so @Conditional gating runs over the filtered candidate set. Whether any FILTER input is runtime-overlaid (profile-supplied exclude) decides if R1's frozen filters suffice or R3's late-bindable ScanFilter SPI is needed — a phase2Question.
- **profiles** — @Profile is just a @Conditional, but a profile-supplied INCLUDE/EXCLUDE (e.g. exclude a candidate in a given environment) is a filter-layer concern that wants late binding — directly motivating R3's runtime ScanFilter overlay vs R1's compile-time-frozen filter list. Profiles thus stress the 'how frozen are the filters' axis.
- **configuration-classes / import-composition / auto-configuration** — @Configuration is a @Component-meta-present stereotype, so config classes are DISCOVERED by the same scanning path; leaf-boot's entry annotation composes scan + config + auto-config like @SpringBootApplication. Critically, Spring's AutoConfigurationExcludeFilter ensures auto-configs (which live in libraries and must NOT be discovered transitively/duplicated) enter ONLY via the import selector, not via scanning — leaf must replicate this exclude-from-scan boundary, and auto-config's separate `.imports`-style candidate source is a DIFFERENT discovery channel from component scanning (deliberately, per Spring's rejection of scanning for auto-configs).
- **scopes / scoped-proxies** — Stage-5 resolves @Scope on the concrete class (scopeResolver analogue) and optionally wraps in a scoped proxy (scopedProxy DEFAULT/NO/INTERFACES/TARGET_CLASS analogue) so a singleton can hold a stable handle to a shorter-lived bean. Scanning carries the scope default and the proxy-mode hint on the descriptor; scopes/scoped-proxies own the multiplicity/lifetime and proxy semantics.
- **lazy-initialization / bean-instantiation / refresh-lifecycle** — `lazyInit=false` (eager singletons, fail-fast at refresh) is scanning's default lazy policy; the descriptor carries a per-candidate lazy override, and the global-lazy-with-exclude-filters lever is a sibling concern. Scanning produces the candidate set; refresh-lifecycle eagerly instantiates the non-lazy singletons via bean-instantiation's async factory invoked from the descriptor — the execution-context boundary where this cold-path feature hands off to the async construction path.
- **programmatic-registration** — The explicit escape hatch at the bottom of every realization (invariant #10): `register_component!(Concrete)` for generics, and the bevy-style explicit `App::with(plugin())` path (Architecture D) are programmatic-registration's domain — the always-available manual fallback when auto-discovery is unavailable (wasm/embedded) or unwanted. Scanning and programmatic-registration must feed the SAME registry/descriptor shape.
- **startup-instrumentation / condition-report** — Scanning is a named, timed startup step (a node in the startup-step tree) and a prime source of accountable diagnostics: the self-check ('crate X contributed zero candidates', 'candidate Y excluded by filter Z', cross-crate name conflict) is exactly the human-readable match/no-match reasoning condition-report records. R1/R2's flat descriptor + span provenance and R3's enumerable filter beans expose this; this is the headline DEFENSE against the silent-empty DCE footgun.
- **resource-loading** — In Spring, scanning is BUILT ON the ResourceLoader / pattern-resolver (classpath* resolution, resourcePattern). In leaf this dependency largely DISSOLVES — there is no classpath resource to enumerate — so the resource-loading coupling becomes vestigial for the static substrate, surviving only for the optional dynamic directory-scanned (WASM-module) source that genuinely reads a directory/resources at runtime. A notable Spring coupling that leaf's clash analysis shows can be proved unnecessary for the common case (invariant #2).

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Where is the FILTER pipeline applied — frozen at compile-time over flattened descriptors (R1, zero-overhead, earliest diagnostics), at build-time over a metadata manifest (R2, deterministic/portable/CI-checkable, Spring-AOT-faithful), or at runtime via a late-bindable ScanFilter SPI (R3, profile/dynamic-source-overlay-capable, closest to Spring's pluggable filter objects)? This is a direct instance of the deferred compile-vs-runtime and metadata/codegen-boundary decisions, and it pivots on whether any include/exclude filter needs runtime-overlaid inputs.
- Which cross-crate substrate is the DEFAULT 'classpath' for the static common case — link-time distributed slices (linkme, zero-overhead, server-target-fragile) vs build-time codegen sweep (sqlx/AOT model, portable, fully avoids the silent-empty footgun) — and is the static-link-time registry PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module source the right duality? This rides entirely on the deferred cross-crate composition & tooling model and must be co-decided with EVERY discovery feature (stereotypes, conditions, auto-config), since they share one substrate.
- How is base-package SCOPING re-expressed natively, and is the default 'radiate downward from the app root' = 'the binary crate + explicitly enabled integration crates'? This is the concrete instance of the cross-crate doc's 'scope discovery, don't make it implicitly global' mandate and the #[global_registration]-author's predictability conclusion — it determines how a `scan!`/`enable!`/basePackages-analogue surface looks and feels (invariant #1 DX).
- Where and how loud is the SILENT-EMPTY guard — a startup expected-vs-found self-check (R1/R3), a `--check`-able checked-in manifest that fails CI (R2), or both — and what is its diagnostic shape? Spring deprecated its index precisely over this footgun; leaf cannot ship the same all-or-nothing-invisible-beans behavior. This is an instance of the deferred unified error/diagnostics model and invariant #9's earliest-richest-diagnostic principle, co-owned with startup-instrumentation/condition-report.
- Is there ONE shared descriptor/seed that this feature, component-stereotypes, bean-definition, and bean-registry all consume (one CandidateDescriptor/RawBeanDefinition shape), per unification invariant #11 — or does each carry its own? The scanned candidate IS the stereotype's descriptor IS bean-definition's seed IS scanning's filter input; whether they unify wants resolution alongside the type-erasure & registry substrate so the whole framework reads one shape.
- How does the @Conditional gate compose with the filter pipeline, and is the boundary 'filters (over const metadata, no construction) decide what COULD be a candidate; conditions (over container state) decide what DOES register' the right split — or do they unify into one predicate layer? This couples to the deferred conditional strategy (cfg vs codegen vs runtime gates) and the deterministic-given-the-final-resolved-feature-set requirement.
- How is the AutoConfigurationExcludeFilter boundary realized so auto-config candidates (a separate `.imports`-style discovery channel) are NOT also picked up by component scanning, and what is the general model for MULTIPLE discovery channels (user components vs auto-configs vs config-properties) feeding the registry without double-registration? Shared with auto-configuration/import-composition; an instance of keeping discovery channels distinct yet unified at the registry.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent — read the full Classpath Scanning Mechanics section (lines 518-556: the five-stage pipeline, exclude-then-include with @Component as sole default filter, the FilterType enum incl. CUSTOM metadata escape hatch, the concrete+independent/@Lookup second gate, read-bytecode-don't-load design intent, naming/scope/proxy hooks with sentinel defaults, scope-not-inherited, lazyInit=false fail-fast), the Candidate Index section (558-576: @Indexed-follows-@Component, the all-or-nothing/silent-empty failure mode that drove deprecation toward holistic AOT — the single most important cautionary tale for leaf's substrate), the @SpringBootApplication composition (578-605, incl. AutoConfigurationExcludeFilter / why auto-configs are NOT scanned), and name-collision handling (659-678: isCompatible re-scan tolerance vs ConflictingBeanDefinitionException, FQN generator). The load-bearing intent that survives the JVM→Rust translation is: SCOPED (not global) discovery + exclude-then-include filtering + read-metadata-without-constructing + name/scope/proxy resolution at registration. The mechanism that does NOT survive is 'scan a classpath' — there is none — so all three realizations agree that discovery = filter-the-collected-registry, and diverge on WHERE filtering/scoping happens.

The three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters for THIS feature — WHERE the filter pipeline runs and how scope is expressed: R1 = macro-flattened descriptors + RUNTIME filter fold (annotate-and-forget, zero collection overhead, but owns the full DCE/Layer-0/silent-empty hazard as its default behavior); R2 = BUILD-TIME codegen sweep (sqlx/AOT model — the most faithful to Spring's own index→AOT trajectory, fully platform-portable, no silent-empty, at the cost of a build step); R3 = type-native candidacy + a runtime ScanFilter SPI (closest to Spring's pluggable filter objects, late-bindable for profiles/dynamic sources, but the type axis is non-total for cross-crate discovery and must pair with a descriptor seed). They are composable along a 'how frozen are the filters and the candidate set' axis, not mutually exclusive — a likely hybrid is R1/R2 as the static default with R3's ScanFilter SPI as the late-binding/dynamic-source overlay.

I deliberately stayed substrate-neutral (linkme vs inventory vs build-time codegen) and did not pick the scope-surface syntax — both are Phase 2 decisions surfaced in phase2Questions. This feature is a near-pure CONSUMER of annotation-model (the meta-presence include filter) and component-stereotypes (the marker set), and the canonical PRODUCER of the cross-crate discovery problem; its substrate decision MUST be co-resolved with every other discovery feature (stereotypes, conditions, auto-config) since they share one 'classpath'. The strongest cross-cutting recommendation, echoing the cross-crate doc: whatever substrate is chosen, the SILENT-EMPTY self-check is non-optional — Spring deprecated its index over exactly this footgun, and leaf would otherwise ship it as the DEFAULT discovery behavior. One notable clash-that-resolves-in-leaf's-favor: Spring's heavy ResourceLoader coupling for scanning largely DISSOLVES (no classpath resources to enumerate), surviving only for the optional dynamic WASM-module source — an instance of invariant #2's 'prove a JVM-routing-around feature unnecessary'.

Names ('CandidateDescriptor', 'ScanPlan', 'ScanFilter', 'Filter'/Verdict, 'MarkerId', 'Role', 'BeanName') are Phase-1 placeholders. Wrote no files. Grounded source paths (all absolute): /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (lines 427-444 stereotype-as-include-filter, 518-556 scanning pipeline, 558-576 candidate index + silent-empty footgun, 578-605 @SpringBootApplication + AutoConfigurationExcludeFilter, 659-678 name collisions); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md (invariants §2, deferred keystones §3, threaded T1/T2 §4); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (global-classpath gap, Layer-0/DCE-A/B/silent-empty, linkme-default + register-thin-descriptors lines 165-171, build-time-codegen sqlx model Architectures B/C/D lines 277-337, scope-don't-make-global lines 36/362, force-link mandate, generics-cannot-register); /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (const TypeId 1.91, trait upcasting 1.86, Any+downcast, inventory/linkme stable, #[diagnostic::*], specialization=AVOID).

## Programmatic Registration (BeanRegistrar)  `[programmatic-registration]`

Spring 7's `BeanRegistrar` is the blessed, AOT-analyzable escape hatch for IMPERATIVE bean registration — the bridge between the convenience of declarative `@Bean`/`#[bean]` and the raw power of hand-minting definitions. Its single method `void register(BeanRegistry, Environment)` reads like ordinary host-language code: `if`/`for`/`switch` and `env.matchesProfiles(...)` decide WHAT to register, so dynamic/looping/multiple/conditional registration that "gets in the way" of one-bean-per-`@Bean`-method declarations has a clean home. Three load-bearing intents I must replicate (intent, not mechanism): (1) THE INSTANCE-SUPPLIER MODEL IS THE WHOLE POINT — `registerBean(name, Type, spec -> spec.prototype().lazyInit().description(...).supplier(ctx -> new Bar(ctx.bean(Foo.class))))` describes a bean's INTENT via a curated `Spec` (a discoverable subset of the sprawling definition surface: scope/prototype, lazy, primary, fallback, order, infrastructure-role, not-autowirable, background-init, description, supplier) rather than hand-assembling a raw definition; (2) SUPPLIERS PULL, THEY DO NOT AUTOWIRE — inside a supplier you EXPLICITLY retrieve collaborators via `ctx.bean(Type)`/`ctx.beanProvider(Type)` (no reflective constructor autowiring into the lambda), and `getPreferredConstructors()` returns null when a supplier is set, making the lambda the SOLE instantiation path; this explicitness is exactly what keeps the wiring statically analyzable, because arbitrary reflective autowiring inside a lambda would not be; (3) IT IS A DEFINITION-PHASE ACTIVITY, RUN ONCE — wired via `@Import` (not `@Bean`) so it slots into config parsing and its definitions exist BEFORE instantiation and are build-time-analyzable (a `@Bean`-produced registrar would run too late). Crowning subtlety: under AOT the registrar's own registration path is IGNORED (`aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration=true`) to avoid double-registration, and the AOT engine instead generates code that simply RE-INVOKES `register(...)` at startup through a fresh adapter — because an opaque instance-supplier lambda "cannot be statically analyzed" for default codegen, so the imperative recipe is preserved by re-running it, with `ImportAware` metadata reattached. The contract is deliberately NARROW: it receives only `Environment` (not the full context), keeping decisions frozen-once at definition time. leaf must replicate this INTENT — a functional, low-ceremony, AOT/build-analyzable imperative-registration contract with an explicit-pull instance-supplier model and a curated intent-describing spec, sitting OVER bean-registry's `register_definition` SPI and reachable via import-composition — WITHOUT the JVM mechanisms it rests on: reflective autowiring, a runtime `RootBeanDefinition` property bag, life-before-main, and the build-time-vs-runtime ambiguity the JVM tolerates. The deep question is whether this stays a thin imperative façade over the same descriptor stream the macros emit (so a hand-written registrar and a `#[bean]` produce indistinguishable definitions), or whether the supplier's opaqueness forces a runtime-re-invoke island that the build-time/codegen substrate cannot freeze.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Imperative closure over the live DefinitionRegistry: a `Registrar` fn(&mut DefinitionRegistry, &Environment) with a fluent typed `BeanSpec<T>` and an explicit-pull async supplier
Mirror Spring's shape directly, rust-native. A registrar is the functional unit `trait Registrar { fn register(&self, reg: &mut DefinitionRegistry, env: &Environment) -> Result<(), RegisterError>; }` (also satisfiable by a plain closure `Fn(&mut DefinitionRegistry, &Environment)` via a blanket impl, the lambda feel). It runs in the COLD, synchronous definition/planning phase (the same window configuration-classes' descriptors land in, before any instance exists), reachable via import-composition's altitude-4 hook (`#[import(MyRegistrar)]`) or an explicit `App::with_registrar(...)` builder call (the §2.10 escape hatch, the GenericApplicationContext.register analogue).

The fluent spec is a TYPED builder: `reg.register_bean::<Bar>("bar", |spec| spec.prototype().lazy().primary().order(10).description("...").supplier(|ctx| async move { Ok(Bar::new(ctx.bean::<Foo>().await?)) }))`. `BeanSpec<T>` is a curated, discoverable subset of the full definition surface (scope/prototype, lazy, primary, fallback, order, infrastructure-role, not-autowirable, background-init, description, supplier) — `describe the bean's intent`, never hand-assemble a RawBeanDefinition. Each `register_bean` call lowers to ONE `RawBeanDefinition` pushed through bean-registry's `register_definition` SPI — so a registrar-minted definition is INDISTINGUISHABLE from a `#[bean]`-minted one downstream (unification §2.11). The produced TypeId is the spec's `T` (the most-concrete-type rule); a `register_bean_as::<dyn Trait, Bar>` form registers the `dyn Trait` injectable view explicitly (no runtime isAssignableFrom).

The supplier is the load-bearing piece: an `async` closure `Fn(&ResolveCtx) -> impl Future<Output = Result<T, ResolveError>>`, type-erased into the descriptor's origin-agnostic boxed-future Factory (the same Factory shape bean-registry already stores) — so a registrar-supplied bean publishes into the singleton cache exactly like a native one, and a WASM-module/test-double supplier slots in identically. SUPPLIERS PULL: `ctx.bean::<Foo>().await` / `ctx.bean_provider::<Foo>()` delegate to the engine's get-path; there is NO reflective autowiring into the closure (Rust has none anyway, so this is the natural shape, not a restriction). When a supplier is set, it is the SOLE instantiation path (the getPreferredConstructors==null analogue is intrinsic — a closure has no constructor to introspect).

Conditional/looping registration is just host control flow inside `register`: `if env.matches_profiles("baz") { for n in runtime_list() { reg.register_bean::<Widget>(&n, ..); } }`. The narrow `&Environment`-only contract (not the full container) keeps decisions made-once and side-effect-light.

**Pros:** Highest intent fidelity and best DX (§2.1): reads like Spring's `BeanRegistrar` — plain `if`/`for` imperative code, a fluent intent-describing `BeanSpec`, explicit-pull suppliers — so a Spring user ports a registrar almost line-for-line, and the closure-as-Registrar blanket impl gives the lambda feel.; Maximal UNIFICATION (§2.11): every `register_bean` lowers to ONE `RawBeanDefinition` through bean-registry's existing `register_definition` SPI, and the supplier erases to the SAME origin-agnostic boxed-future Factory the engine already stores — a registrar-minted bean is indistinguishable from a `#[bean]` one to the rest of the engine; no bespoke 'registrar bean' code path.; 'Suppliers pull, not autowire' is FREE in Rust: there is no reflective autowiring to lose, so `ctx.bean::<T>().await` is simply the natural way a closure gets collaborators — the AOT-analyzability that Spring engineers HAD to design for falls out of the language, and the explicit dependency edges are legible for diagnostics (§2.9).; Curated typed `BeanSpec<T>` is a discoverable, autocompleted, compile-checked subset of the definition surface — strictly better than Spring's stringly fluent spec, and it cannot express an invalid combination the type system forbids (e.g. supplier-plus-autowire-constructor is unrepresentable).; Thin macros (§2.10): the `#[import(MyRegistrar)]`/derive emits only an edge descriptor + a trait-object seed; the heavy logic (running registrars, spec→definition lowering) lives in a normal engine crate; `cargo expand` shows a hand-writable trait impl and one descriptor. The `App::with_registrar` builder is a pure escape hatch needing no macro at all.

**Cons:** The async-closure supplier MUST be type-erased to a boxed future at the descriptor boundary (AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe), so every registrar-built bean pays a Box-per-construction — cold/fine for singletons but a real cost for prototype/scoped beans minted in a loop, exactly the hot-path tension §2.5 warns about.; An OPAQUE supplier closure is the direct analogue of Spring's 'cannot be statically analyzed' problem: a build-time/codegen substrate (Phase-2 keystone) cannot freeze the lambda's body into generated wiring — so a registrar is the one island that may have to be RE-INVOKED at startup rather than compiled away, partially defeating an AOT-lineage 'definitions knowable at build time' goal (this is precisely Spring's `aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration` concession).; `&mut DefinitionRegistry` is a mutable handle into the engine during the planning phase — it presumes a MUTABLE registry at registration time, which clashes head-on with bean-registry's R2 immutable-snapshot/freeze-early shape (a registrar must run strictly before freeze, and cannot run during preInstantiate the way Spring's can).; Conditional registration via `env.matches_profiles(...)` inside the closure DUPLICATES the conditions/condition-family machinery in imperative form — a registrar can make a registration decision that the declarative condition report (condition-report) cannot see/explain, creating a diagnostics blind spot unless the registrar's decisions are themselves recorded.; The narrow `&Environment`-only contract is faithful but limiting: a real registrar sometimes wants more planning context (the active definition set, a resource loader) — Spring threads these via Aware/constructor injection; leaf must decide what planning handles a registrar legitimately gets without widening it into a god-object.

**Mechanisms considered:** `trait Registrar { fn register(&self, reg: &mut DefinitionRegistry, env: &Environment) -> Result<(),RegisterError>; }` + blanket impl for `Fn(&mut DefinitionRegistry,&Environment)` (closure-as-registrar lambda feel); fluent typed `BeanSpec<T>` builder (prototype/lazy/primary/fallback/order/infrastructure/not-autowirable/background-init/description/supplier) — curated subset, never raw definition assembly; async-closure supplier `Fn(&ResolveCtx) -> impl Future<Output=Result<T,ResolveError>>` erased to the engine's origin-agnostic boxed-future Factory; explicit-pull `ctx.bean::<T>()`/`ctx.bean_provider::<T>()`; each `register_bean` lowers to ONE RawBeanDefinition via bean-registry's `register_definition` SPI (unification); `register_bean_as::<dyn Trait,Concrete>` for explicit injectable trait views; reachability: import-composition altitude-4 `#[import(MyRegistrar)]` (link-time/codegen-collected) OR explicit `App::with_registrar(..)` builder call (Architecture D escape hatch); narrow `&Environment`-only planning context; host control flow (`if`/`for`) inside `register` for conditional/looping registration; profile checks via `env.matches_profiles(..)`

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Substrate-neutral over bean-registry's `register_definition` SPI and import-composition's contributor stream. A registrar is a `'static` value (a unit struct impl or a closure captured in a `const`/`static` descriptor) collected like any contributor — via linkme distributed-slice (Arch A/B), build.rs codegen (Arch C), or the explicit `App::with_registrar` call (Arch D). KEY T1 NUANCE: unlike a `#[bean]` descriptor (pure const data), a registrar carries an OPAQUE closure body the build-time/codegen substrate cannot introspect — so on the codegen path it can at best emit a force-linked CALL to `registrar.register(...)` re-invoked at startup (the rust-native `aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration` + `BeanRegistrarAotContribution.re-invoke` analogue), not freeze the minted definitions into generated source. The registrar TYPE must be path-referenced to escape Layer-0 DCE — an `#[import(MyRegistrar)]` in crate A naming crate B's registrar path-references it (safe); a registration-only registrar in an unreferenced crate vanishes silently, so the anti-DCE self-check must count expected-vs-found registrars per source crate and fail loud. The supplier closure capturing collaborator TypeIds is fine cross-crate (TypeId is `'static`), but a GENERIC registrar (or `register_bean::<Generic<T>>`) has no `'static` symbol until instantiated → macro hard-errors with a `register_concrete!(Bar)` shim. The supplier's `ctx.bean::<Foo>()` is a runtime engine call resolving against the WHOLE binary's definition set, so a registrar in crate A can pull a collaborator contributed by crate C — the cross-crate wiring works at runtime even though neither crate's build pass could see the other. Origin-agnostic: a registrar contributed by a dynamic WASM-module 'classpath' registers through the SAME `register_definition` API with a supplier that bridges into the module — CANDIDATE (static link-time registrars + optional dynamic directory-scanned registrars = two producers of one registration API), not a decision.

**T2 — execution context:** `register(&mut reg, &env)` runs in the COLD, synchronous definition/planning phase — single-threaded, around (or before) main, BEFORE any instance exists. It is PURE/synchronous registration logic: it observes `Environment` and mints definitions; it MUST NOT block, do IO, or be async (mirroring the read-mostly planning discipline of selectors/conditions — a registrar that 'needs to await' is a design smell; that work belongs in a bean's supplier, not in planning). Errors from `register` (duplicate name under no-override, invalid spec combo, generic target) propagate as a rich `RegisterError` at the planning phase — the earliest, richest point (§2.9), naming the registrar, the bean, and the offending call, strictly before any async construction. The SUPPLIER, by contrast, is the async seam: erased to the engine's boxed-future Factory, it is awaited by the get/eager-instantiate driver in phase 3, under bean-registry's per-bean creation guard (NEVER a global lock §2.5). A supplier may `.await` (it pulls collaborators via `ctx.bean().await`, which may itself construct DB pools) but MUST NOT block the executor — a blocking supplier is documented to use spawn_blocking (execution-context discipline; context-propagation is Phase-2's). Cancellation of an in-flight eager build that runs a supplier drops the partially-constructed bean and unwinds the in-creation set exactly as any Factory. Because there is no async Drop, a registrar-minted bean needing async teardown declares it on the bean (DisposableBean analogue), driven by the container's reverse-order `shutdown().await` (context-close) — the registrar itself owns no runtime lifecycle. Safe publication of the finished supplier-built singleton into `ready` is bean-registry's §concurrency-contract realization.

#### R2 — Declarative-by-default, opaque-supplier-by-exception: the registrar emits the SAME descriptor stream as `#[bean]`; only `.supplier(closure)` opts into the runtime-re-invoke island
Take the AOT-determinism lesson as the organizing principle and SPLIT the registrar's output by analyzability, so the common case stays build-time-frozen and only genuine dynamism becomes a runtime island. A registrar still reads imperatively, but the `BeanSpec` distinguishes two registration KINDS:

(a) DECLARATIVE registration — `reg.register_bean::<Bar>("bar", |spec| spec.prototype().lazy().wired())` where `.wired()` means 'construct `Bar` via its declared constructor/factory, resolving collaborators the SAME way `#[bean]` does' (the engine's normal wiring path). This produces a descriptor IDENTICAL to what `#[bean] fn bar(foo: &Foo) -> Bar` emits — a const, analyzable, build-time-freezable RawBeanDefinition with a typed Factory the codegen substrate CAN lower to generated source. The registrar is just imperative SUGAR for emitting many such descriptors in a loop/branch — the dynamism is in WHICH definitions exist, not in HOW they construct.

(b) OPAQUE-supplier registration — `spec.supplier(|ctx| async move { ... })` — the explicit escape into a non-analyzable construction recipe. This descriptor is flagged `opaque_supplier=true` (the `aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration` analogue): the build-time/codegen substrate does NOT try to freeze the closure body; instead it records that the registrar must be RE-INVOKED at startup to re-mint these specific definitions, OR (link-time/runtime substrate) the closure is simply stored as a boxed Factory and run at construction. The opaque path is the minority, loudly-marked case.

The insight: Spring's `BeanRegistrar` conflates 'imperative control over WHICH beans' with 'an opaque supplier for HOW each is built', but in Rust those separate cleanly — most imperative/looping/conditional registration needs only (a) (e.g. 'register one `Handler` per discovered route name, each wired normally'), and only genuinely bespoke construction needs (b). leaf defaults registrars to the analyzable (a) form and makes (b) a deliberate, visible opt-out — so the registrar is mostly build-time-determinism-preserving, and the runtime-re-invoke island shrinks to exactly the closures that need it.

Reachability/spec/control-flow are as R1 (import-composition altitude-4 or `App::with_registrar`, typed `BeanSpec`, `&Environment` contract). The added machinery is the kind-split in the spec and the `opaque_supplier` flag the codegen substrate keys on.

**Pros:** Honors the AOT lineage (§3 compile-vs-runtime) far better than R1: the COMMON imperative case (loop/branch over which beans, each normally wired) stays build-time-analyzable and freezable into generated source — the runtime-re-invoke island shrinks to only the explicitly-opaque `.supplier(closure)` minority, so 'definitions knowable at build time' largely survives even through a registrar.; Sharpest diagnostics-timing (§2.9): a `.wired()` registration's wiring errors (missing collaborator, ambiguity) can be caught at the build/freeze validation pass like any `#[bean]`, while only opaque-supplier beans defer to runtime — strictly earlier failure for the common case than R1's uniformly-runtime supplier.; Cleanly separates two concerns Spring conflated — WHICH beans (imperative control flow, the registrar's real value) vs HOW each constructs (wiring recipe) — which is more honest about Rust, where there is no reflective autowiring to make 'opaque supplier' the only option; most registrars never need an opaque closure at all.; The `opaque_supplier=true` flag IS the rust-native `aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration` mechanism made explicit and per-definition, so the build-time substrate has a precise, loud signal for exactly which registrations it cannot freeze — turning Spring's whole-registrar concession into a fine-grained, mostly-avoidable one.; Lower Box/heap churn (§2.5) in the common case: `.wired()` beans go through the engine's normal monomorphizable construction path (no captured closure), so only true opaque suppliers pay the boxed-future cost — better for prototype/scoped beans minted in loops.

**Cons:** More concepts than R1: two registration KINDS (`.wired()` vs `.supplier()`) plus an `opaque_supplier` flag is a richer surface to teach, and the distinction ('is my construction analyzable?') leaks an implementation/AOT concern into the user-facing spec — a DX cost (§2.1) versus R1's one uniform supplier.; `.wired()` registration must reconstruct the engine's normal collaborator-resolution from a programmatic call site (the registrar names a TYPE and the engine wires it), which re-derives `#[bean]`-style parameter injection imperatively — duplicating wiring logic and re-introducing the by-type/by-name resolution the supplier model deliberately made explicit; the two paths can subtly diverge.; Pre-judges the compile-vs-runtime split (a Phase-2 keystone §3): baking an `opaque_supplier`/build-time-freezable distinction INTO the spec assumes a build-time substrate exists and matters — on a pure-runtime substrate the whole kind-split is dead weight, so this realization is partly betting on an undecided meta-architecture.; The runtime-re-invoke path for opaque suppliers has the SAME subtlety Spring documents (re-running `register()` must re-apply init/destroy customizers a naive rerun would lose — Spring's `customizers` MultiValueMap channel) — leaf inherits this 'don't lose wiring on re-invoke' hazard for the opaque island.; 'Most registration is `.wired()`' is an assumption that may not hold: if real leaf use cases overwhelmingly need bespoke construction (the reason to reach for a registrar at all), the analyzable-default optimization rarely fires and the extra machinery earns little — an empirical bet Phase 1 can't settle.

**Mechanisms considered:** two registration KINDS in `BeanSpec`: `.wired()` (engine constructs via declared ctor/factory — descriptor identical to `#[bean]`, build-time-freezable) vs `.supplier(closure)` (opaque async recipe); `opaque_supplier=true` flag per definition = the rust-native `aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration`; codegen substrate freezes `.wired()` definitions, re-invokes the registrar only for opaque-supplier ones; separate 'WHICH beans' (imperative control flow) from 'HOW each builds' (wiring recipe) — most loops/branches need only `.wired()`, opaque supplier is the marked minority; build/freeze-time wiring validation for `.wired()` beans (errors caught early like any `#[bean]`); runtime deferral only for opaque suppliers; runtime-re-invoke adapter for opaque-supplier registrars (Spring BeanRegistrarAotContribution analogue) with a customizer-replay channel to not lose init/destroy on rerun; same reachability/spec/`&Environment` surface as R1; thin macro emits the kind-flag as descriptor data

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** This realization is the best fit for the build-time-codegen substrate (Arch C) and the AOT lineage: `.wired()` registrations emit the SAME const-analyzable descriptors as `#[bean]`, so a codegen pass can freeze them into generated `register_all` source exactly like any contributor — the registrar's imperative control flow runs at BUILD time (in build.rs / a `cargo leaf prepare` step) to decide WHICH `.wired()` definitions to emit, then bakes them flat. Only `opaque_supplier` definitions resist this: they degrade to a runtime-re-invoke of `register()` (or a stored boxed Factory on the link-time/runtime substrate), the one island the build pass cannot freeze — flagged loudly per-definition. CRITICAL T1 LIMIT: a build.rs/proc-macro sees only its OWN crate, so a registrar in the binary crate can run its control flow at build time, but a registrar contributed by a foreign crate can only be freeze-evaluated if that crate emits a build-time manifest/const the binary's pass aggregates (cargo-metadata-driven) — otherwise it too degrades to runtime re-invoke; so the build-time-freezable property is strongest intra-crate, weaker cross-crate (the same intra-vs-cross-crate asymmetry that haunts configuration-classes/import-composition codegen). Layer-0 DCE, generic-hard-error, and the anti-DCE self-check are as R1. The runtime-re-invoke island for opaque suppliers must itself be force-linked and counted by the self-check (a registrar that should re-invoke but whose crate was DCE'd = silent missing beans). Origin-agnostic and two-source-duality as R1: a WASM-module registrar's opaque supplier is just another runtime-island Factory.

**T2 — execution context:** Two timing regimes by registration kind. `.wired()` registrations: the registrar's imperative control flow runs in the COLD planning phase (sync, single-threaded, no IO/block) — or, on the codegen substrate, at BUILD time — and emits analyzable descriptors whose CONSTRUCTION later runs through the engine's normal async wiring path (per-bean creation guard, no global lock §2.5, boxed-future at the dyn edge only where the Factory is dyn). Their wiring errors surface at the build/freeze validation pass (earliest §2.9). OPAQUE-supplier registrations: the supplier closure is the async seam, run at construction in phase 3 like R1's supplier — awaited under the per-bean guard, may `.await` to pull collaborators, must not block (spawn_blocking otherwise), cancellation drops the partial bean. The runtime-re-invoke path (re-running `register()` at startup for opaque-supplier registrars on the codegen substrate) is a COLD startup step: synchronous, single-threaded, before instantiation, mirroring Spring's `BeanRegistrarAotContribution` re-invoke — and it must replay any init/destroy customizers so the rerun doesn't lose wiring (the documented hazard). Errors split by timing: `.wired()` wiring failures at build/freeze; opaque-supplier construction failures at eager-instantiate; re-invoke failures at the cold startup re-invoke step — each richly attributed to the registrar and the specific call. No async Drop → registrar-minted beans declare async teardown on the bean, driven by the container's reverse-order shutdown (context-close).

#### R3 — Registry-DSL as a thin generative trait over the engine SPI: `register!`/builder macro lowers an imperative block to const descriptors + a residual runtime island, unifying with the macro emit path
Push UNIFICATION (§2.11) and THIN-MACRO (§2.10) hardest: erase the distinction between 'a registrar' and 'the macro emit path' by treating BOTH as producers of the same descriptor stream, where a registrar is whatever code calls a small, hand-writable `BeanRegistry` SPI — and a `register! { }` macro/DSL is pure sugar that lowers an imperative block to exactly the const descriptors + SPI calls a user could have hand-written.

The irreducible primitive is the `BeanRegistry` SPI itself (the hand-writable API the charter §2.10 demands always exists): `trait BeanRegistry { fn register<T: 'static>(&mut self, name: Option<&str>, spec: BeanSpec<T>); fn register_alias(&mut self, alias: &str, target: &str); fn env(&self) -> &Environment; }`. A registrar is ANY `fn(&mut dyn BeanRegistry)` — no special trait needed beyond the closure type; the `Environment` is reachable via `reg.env()` (even narrower than passing it, and uniform). This is the bedrock escape hatch: anyone can hand-write `fn my_reg(reg: &mut dyn BeanRegistry) { reg.register::<Foo>(None, BeanSpec::new()); ... }` and add it via `App::with_registrar(my_reg)`.
