Over that bedrock sits a `register! { }` DSL macro that makes the imperative form ergonomic and, crucially, SPLITS its body at expansion: static/literal registrations (`register Bar { lazy; primary; supplier = |ctx| async { .. } }`) lower to const descriptors emitted into the SAME distributed-slice/codegen stream as `#[bean]` (so they are collected and analyzable identically), while genuinely dynamic parts (`for n in runtime_list() { register Widget named n; }`) lower to a residual `fn(&mut dyn BeanRegistry)` runtime island. The macro is THIN: `cargo expand` shows literal descriptor `static`s for the static part and a plain SPI-calling closure for the dynamic part — nothing the user couldn't have written. The DSL is the only 'new' surface; it carries no logic, just routes each statement to the hand-writable primitive.

The `BeanSpec<T>` and explicit-pull async supplier are as R1. The difference is the FRAMING: there is no first-class `Registrar` concept distinct from the macro emit path — `#[bean]`, `#[component]`, a `register!` block, and a hand-written `fn(&mut dyn BeanRegistry)` are FOUR syntaxes for ONE act (contributing descriptors to the engine via the BeanRegistry SPI), differing only in ergonomics and how much is static-vs-runtime.

**Pros:** Deepest unification (§2.11): a registrar is not a special subsystem but simply 'code that calls the BeanRegistry SPI' — the SAME SPI the `#[bean]`/`#[component]` macros target — so there is ONE registration primitive and zero bespoke 'registrar bean' machinery; the whole feature reduces to 'the hand-writable API behind the macros, exposed directly'.; Purest thin-macro story (§2.10): the bedrock is a hand-writable `fn(&mut dyn BeanRegistry)` that needs NO macro at all (the ultimate escape hatch), and the `register!` DSL is provably thin — `cargo expand` shows literal descriptor statics + a plain SPI-calling closure, nothing hidden; this is the charter's 'macros only emit what you could hand-write' taken literally.; The expansion-time static-vs-dynamic split (literal registrations → const descriptors in the shared stream; loops/branches → runtime island) gives R2's AOT-analyzability benefit WITHOUT a user-facing `.wired()`/`opaque` distinction — the macro infers it from whether a statement is literal or control-flow-dependent, so DX stays simple and most static registrations are freezable for free.; `fn(&mut dyn BeanRegistry)` as the unit is trivially composable and testable: a registrar is a normal function unit-testable against a mock `BeanRegistry` with no container (the testability §ethos), and `App::with_registrar` composition is bevy-plugin-simple (Arch D), maximally reliable across platforms.; Smallest stable ABI surface for the macro to target: `leaf-core` exposes just the `BeanRegistry` trait + `BeanSpec` + `Descriptor`, which the thin macro hard-codes absolute paths to — a minimal, semver-stable seam (the §6 small-kernel topology).

**Cons:** Most macro machinery to build and the riskiest to get right: a `register! { }` DSL that PARSES an imperative block and decides per-statement whether it lowers to a const descriptor or a runtime island is a non-trivial proc-macro (statement classification, hygiene, capturing the right spans) — and if it mis-classifies, it silently moves a registration between build-time and runtime, a subtle correctness/diagnostics hazard the charter's 'nothing hidden in a macro' (§2.10) is wary of.; Erasing the first-class `Registrar` concept costs PARITY LEGIBILITY: a Spring user looking for 'where is BeanRegistrar' finds 'it's just the SPI behind the macros, call it however' — conceptually cleaner but a porting/teachability friction, and the release-notes framing ('the blessed functional contract') has no single named analogue.; The DSL's static-vs-dynamic auto-split is a CONVENIENCE that hides the very compile-vs-runtime boundary (Phase-2 keystone §3) the user may need to reason about — the opposite tension from R2, which made it explicit; auto-inference is better DX but worse for predictability of WHEN a registration is decided (and where its errors surface).; `&mut dyn BeanRegistry` dyn-dispatches every registration call — trivial cost (cold planning phase, one vtable hop per register call) but it does make the bedrock primitive dyn rather than monomorphized; a generic `register::<T>` on a `dyn` trait needs the method to be generic-over-T which forces either a boxed/erased spec or a non-dyn-object trait (a small but real object-safety wrinkle to resolve).; Betting that `#[bean]`, `#[component]`, `register!`, and hand-written registrars truly are 'one act' may UNDER-serve cases where the registrar genuinely needs richer planning context than the SPI exposes (active definition set, resource loader) — the minimal `BeanRegistry` SPI is deliberately narrow, and widening it to serve a real registrar need erodes the 'tiny unified primitive' that justifies the design.

**Mechanisms considered:** bedrock primitive = a hand-writable `trait BeanRegistry { fn register<T:'static>(..); fn register_alias(..); fn env(&self)->&Environment; }`; a registrar is ANY `fn(&mut dyn BeanRegistry)` (no special trait), added via `App::with_registrar`; `register! { }` DSL macro lowering an imperative block: literal registrations → const descriptors in the SAME distributed-slice/codegen stream as `#[bean]`; control-flow-dependent ones → a residual `fn(&mut dyn BeanRegistry)` runtime island; one registration ACT, four syntaxes (`#[bean]`/`#[component]`/`register!`/hand-written fn) all targeting the one BeanRegistry SPI — no first-class distinct Registrar subsystem (maximal unification); expansion-time static-vs-dynamic statement classification to auto-derive build-time-freezable vs runtime-island (R2's split without a user-facing flag); `BeanSpec<T>` + explicit-pull async supplier as R1; `reg.env()` for the narrow planning context (even narrower than passing &Environment); minimal stable `leaf-core` ABI (BeanRegistry + BeanSpec + Descriptor) the thin macro hard-codes absolute paths to; object-safety handled via an erased-spec or sealed helper for the generic `register::<T>`

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Most substrate-flexible because the bedrock is just SPI calls: a hand-written `fn(&mut dyn BeanRegistry)` works identically under linkme (the fn is named in a descriptor), codegen (the fn is called in generated `register_all`), or explicit `App::with_registrar` (Arch D) — and is the ONLY form guaranteed on wasm/embedded (no link magic needed), so it's the universal escape hatch the cross-crate doc wants. The `register! { }` DSL's static part emits const descriptors into the SAME stream as `#[bean]` (collected via whatever substrate Phase 2 picks), while its dynamic-island part is a force-linked fn call — so a registrar splits across the same build-time/runtime seam as R2 but with the split inferred by the macro rather than user-flagged. Layer-0: the registrar fn/type must be path-referenced (an `App::with_registrar(crate_b::reg)` or `#[import]` references it); a registration-only crate's registrar vanishes silently → anti-DCE self-check counts expected-vs-found registrar markers per crate. Generics hard-error with a concrete shim (a generic `register::<Generic<T>>` has no `'static` symbol; the macro errors). The minimal `BeanRegistry` SPI being the macro's target means the stable ABI the macro hard-codes is tiny (good for semver, §6). Origin-agnostic + two-source duality: a WASM-module's registrations come through the same `&mut dyn BeanRegistry` SPI (the module's bridge calls `reg.register(..)` with a supplier dispatching into the module) — CANDIDATE; the dyn `BeanRegistry` trait is exactly the origin-blind seam that lets a native, codegen, hand-written, or WASM-sourced registrar all register indistinguishably.

**T2 — execution context:** All registration (whether via macro-emitted descriptor, `register!` dynamic island, or hand-written fn) runs in the COLD planning phase — synchronous, single-threaded, around main, before any instance — calling the `BeanRegistry` SPI to mint definitions. It is pure registration logic: no IO, no block, no async (the `reg.env()` read is sync); a registration error (duplicate, invalid spec, generic target) is a rich planning-phase `RegisterError` (§2.9). The `register!` DSL's STATIC part may be lowered to const descriptors evaluated even earlier (build/link time) on a codegen substrate; its DYNAMIC island runs as a fn at the cold planning phase. The SUPPLIER (in any syntax) is the only async seam: the explicit-pull async closure erases to the engine's boxed-future Factory, awaited at construction (phase 3) under the per-bean creation guard (no global lock §2.5), may `.await` to pull collaborators, must not block (spawn_blocking otherwise), cancellation drops the partial bean and unwinds in-creation. The `&mut dyn BeanRegistry` is held only transiently during the synchronous planning call and never crosses an await or thread boundary (so the dyn handle needs no Send/Sync gymnastics). No async Drop → registrar-minted beans declare async teardown on the bean (DisposableBean analogue), driven by the container's reverse-order `shutdown().await` (context-close); the registrar fn itself owns no runtime lifecycle and nothing to cancel post-planning.

### Rust clashes
- SUPPLIERS-PULL-NOT-AUTOWIRE IS FREE IN RUST — the central inversion. Spring engineers deliberately designed `.supplier(ctx -> new Bar(ctx.bean(Foo.class)))` with EXPLICIT pulls (no constructor autowiring into the lambda) PRECISELY to keep the wiring statically analyzable for AOT, because arbitrary reflective autowiring inside a lambda would defeat native-image codegen. Rust has no reflective autowiring at all, so the explicit-pull supplier is simply the NATURAL and only way a closure obtains collaborators — `ctx.bean::<Foo>().await`. What Spring had to engineer for, leaf gets for free: the supplier model is the default, not a concession, and the dependency edges are statically legible. The clash is purely conceptual (a Spring user must unlearn 'why is autowiring disabled here?') — the answer is 'there was never any autowiring to disable'.
- THE OPAQUE SUPPLIER CLOSURE IS THE AOT-ANALYZABILITY WALL — Spring's `aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration` re-invoke pattern transfers literally. Spring's AOT engine throws 'Default code generation is not supported for bean definitions declaring an instance supplier callback' on an opaque `setInstanceSupplier(lambda)`, and routes around it by RE-INVOKING `register()` at startup. leaf hits the identical wall: a supplier closure's body cannot be introspected by a build.rs/proc-macro/codegen pass, so on a build-time/codegen substrate a registrar's opaque-supplier definitions cannot be frozen into generated source — they must be re-minted at runtime by re-running the registrar (R1/R2/R3's runtime-re-invoke island). This is the sharpest tension with the AOT-lineage 'definitions knowable at build time' goal: a registrar is the one construct whose construction recipe may be irreducibly runtime. R2's split (declarative `.wired()` freezable, opaque-supplier deferred) is the rust-native fine-graining of Spring's whole-registrar concession.
- ASYNC SUPPLIER MUST ERASE TO A BOXED FUTURE AT THE DESCRIPTOR SEAM (AFIT/RPITIT not dyn-safe). The supplier is `async`, but the descriptor's Factory is stored type-erased and origin-agnostic (so native, codegen, test-double, and WASM-module suppliers slot in uniformly), and async-fn-in-trait / RPITIT are NOT dyn-compatible → the supplier's future must be `Pin<Box<dyn Future + Send + '_>>`, one heap alloc per construction. Cold/acceptable for singletons (build once), but a real §2.5 cost for prototype/scoped beans minted in a registrar loop. leaf must keep a monomorphized zero-cost typed construction path (the `.wired()`/declared-factory form) distinct from the boxed opaque-supplier path; it cannot have both on one surface on stable (Phase-2's async-execution-model decision).
- `&mut DefinitionRegistry`/`&mut BeanRegistry` PRESUMES A MUTABLE REGISTRY AT REGISTRATION TIME — clashes with the freeze-early/immutable-snapshot registry shape. Spring's `BeanRegistry.registerBean` mutates a live `BeanDefinitionRegistry`, and Spring even tolerates registration during preInstantiate. bean-registry's R2 realization (immutable RegistrySnapshot frozen at a hard phase boundary) cannot accept mutation after freeze — so a leaf registrar MUST run strictly in the pre-freeze definition window, and cannot mint definitions mid-instantiation the way Spring's can. This is a known tension flagged in bean-registry's phase2Questions (mutable-concurrent vs immutable-snapshot registry); programmatic-registration is the feature that most directly exercises it (the §2.10 escape hatch writing into `register_definition` before freeze).
- NO LIFE-BEFORE-MAIN FOR THE REGISTRAR — it is a descriptor/fn collected statically, RUN in the cold planning phase, never pre-main. Spring's registrars run during synchronous config parsing. The cross-crate doc warns life-before-main (inventory constructors) is hostile to async/safety: no panic, no async, unspecified order, std maybe not ready. leaf must therefore collect the registrar as a `'static` descriptor/fn-pointer (linkme const, or codegen call, or explicit `with`) and EXECUTE `register()` inside `main`'s cold planning phase under the runtime — NOT in a pre-main constructor. The supplier inside it defers all real init/IO/async to construction time. This is the rust-native 'register descriptors/factories, not live objects' discipline applied to the imperative hook.
- GENERICS CANNOT BE A REGISTRAR OR A `register::<Generic<T>>` TARGET. A generic registrar type, or `reg.register_bean::<Cache<T>>(...)`, has no `'static` symbol/TypeId until monomorphized, so neither link-time collection nor a concrete descriptor can name it. The macro must HARD-ERROR on a generic registrar/target with a `register_concrete!(Cache<i32>)` per-instantiation shim — the same constraint that hits every contributor kind (bean-registry, configuration-classes, import-composition), applied here. The supplier closure CAPTURING generic-typed collaborators is fine only once the bean type is concrete; the produced bean's TypeId must be a concrete monomorphization.
- CONDITIONAL REGISTRATION VIA HOST CONTROL FLOW IS A DIAGNOSTICS BLIND SPOT vs declarative conditions. Spring's `env.matchesProfiles(...)` inside `register()` makes a registration decision in imperative code the declarative condition machinery cannot see. leaf's condition-report (which records every match/no-match with a reason) cannot explain a bean a registrar's `if` silently chose not to register — the imperative branch is opaque to the report. This clashes with §2.9 (rich, complete diagnostics): a registrar's conditional decisions must either be recorded explicitly (the registrar reporting its branch outcomes) or accepted as a legible-only-by-reading-the-code gap, unlike declarative `#[conditional]` beans. It also entangles with the Phase-2 conditional strategy (cfg vs codegen vs runtime gate): an `env`-reading registrar is inherently a runtime gate, not a cfg.
- NO ASYNC DROP → a registrar-minted bean's async teardown rides the container, not Drop. Spring's registrar can set destroy-method/DisposableBean wiring on the spec, torn down under the same lifecycle infrastructure. Rust has no async Drop (experimental-incomplete, crashes on tokio shutdown), so a registrar-minted bean holding async-cleanup resources (a supplier that built a DB pool) MUST declare explicit async teardown (a DisposableBean-analogue on the bean), driven by the container's reverse-dependency-order `shutdown().await` (context-close). The registrar itself owns no runtime lifecycle — it only mints the definition carrying the teardown intent; the BeanSpec must be able to carry a destroy/shutdown hook the container drives, not a Drop the language drives.

### Interactions
- **bean-registry** — THE substrate this feature writes into. A registrar's every `register_bean` call lowers to ONE RawBeanDefinition pushed through bean-registry's `register_definition` SPI (named in bean-registry's own interactions as 'programmatic-registration writes definitions imperatively into the registry's register_definition SPI, the stable public escape hatch §2.10'). The supplier erases to bean-registry's origin-agnostic boxed-future Factory and publishes into the singleton cache identically to a native bean. The hard tension: bean-registry's R2 immutable-snapshot/freeze-early shape requires the registrar to run strictly pre-freeze, whereas its R1/R3 mutable-registry shapes accommodate registration naturally — programmatic-registration is the feature that most exercises bean-registry's mutable-vs-immutable phase2Question.
- **import-composition** — The PRIMARY reachability path and the feature where this is already unified. import-composition explicitly names altitude-4 (`ImportBeanDefinitionRegistrar`) as unifying with programmatic-registration, citing Spring 7's `BeanRegistrar` as the modern successor that DEPRECATES `ImportBeanDefinitionRegistrar`. A registrar is contributed via `#[import(MyRegistrar)]` (import-composition's altitude-4 hook) or the explicit `App::with_registrar` escape hatch. import-composition's R2 keeps exactly this single imperative hook; its R3 leaves the registrar as the one RUNTIME residual (re-invoked at startup) — directly the same runtime-re-invoke island this feature's R1/R2/R3 describe. The 'registrar may not register a definition-phase post-processor' rule (Spring's BDRPP constraint) is enforced here as a loud diagnostic.
- **configuration-classes** — The declarative SIBLING the registrar complements: `#[bean]` methods declare one-concrete-bean-each, the registrar handles imperative/looping/multiple/conditional registration that 'gets in the way' of `@Bean` methods (Spring's named design intent). Both lower to the SAME RawBeanDefinition + typed Factory descriptor stream, so a registrar-minted and a `#[bean]`-minted definition are indistinguishable downstream (the unification §2.11). R2/R3's `.wired()`/static-split makes the common registrar case emit descriptors IDENTICAL to a `#[bean]`, reusing configuration-classes' analyzable lowering.
- **bean-definition** — The registrar produces RawBeanDefinitions (bean-definition's metamodel record). The fluent `BeanSpec<T>` is a curated, discoverable subset of bean-definition's full surface (scope/prototype, lazy, primary, fallback, order, infrastructure-role, not-autowirable, background-init, description, supplier) — 'describe the bean's intent' rather than hand-assemble. The 'most concrete produced type' rule (bean-definition's matching prerequisite) applies: `register_bean::<T>` fixes the produced TypeId to `T`; `register_bean_as::<dyn Trait, Concrete>` registers an explicit injectable trait view (no runtime isAssignableFrom).
- **deferral-primitives** — The supplier's explicit-pull model IS deferral made imperative: `ctx.bean::<Foo>().await` / `ctx.bean_provider::<Foo>()` are exactly ObjectProvider/ObjectFactory-style deferred, optional (0..1), or multiple (0..N) access inside the construction closure. A registrar supplier that pulls a `bean_provider` defers/optionalizes a collaborator the same way a constructor parameter typed `ObjectProvider<T>` would — so deferral-primitives' deferred-access vocabulary is what the supplier's `ctx` exposes.
- **scopes** — `BeanSpec::prototype()`/`scope(...)` set the bean's multiplicity/lifetime — the registrar is a primary way to register prototype/custom-scoped beans imperatively (Spring 7 added `scope()` to the spec in 7.0.4, and Bean Overrides gained prototype/custom-scope support in the same release). A prototype bean minted by a registrar with an opaque supplier re-runs that supplier per resolution — the supplier-per-instance cost (boxed future per construction) is most visible here (the §2.5 hot-path tension).
- **lazy-initialization** — `BeanSpec::lazy()` marks a registrar-minted singleton lazy (Spring's `.lazyInit()`), so bean-registry's eager-instantiate pass skips it at refresh and defers construction (running the supplier) to first `get` — relocating the supplier's potential failure to first access exactly as lazy-initialization defines for any bean.
- **conditions** — Two routes to conditional registration that must coexist: (a) the registrar is GATED as a whole by a declarative condition on its `#[import]` (Spring: 'gateable with @Conditional'), evaluated by the conditions machinery; (b) the registrar makes FINE-GRAINED decisions imperatively inside `register()` via `env.matches_profiles(...)`/host `if`. Route (b) is a runtime gate invisible to condition-report (the diagnostics blind-spot rustClash), and entangles with the Phase-2 conditional strategy (an env-reading registrar is inherently runtime, not cfg).
- **profiles** — The canonical conditional-registration example: `if env.matches_profiles("baz") { reg.register_bean::<Bar>(..) }`. profiles (just a @Conditional) reaches the registrar through the narrow `&Environment` contract — `env.matches_profiles(...)` is the registrar's primary branching input, the rust-native form of Spring's `env.matchesProfiles(...)` and Kotlin's `profile("baz") { }` DSL block.
- **environment** — The registrar's ONLY ambient input by deliberate design — Spring passes `Environment` (not the full context) to keep the contract narrow and decisions side-effect-light. leaf threads `&Environment` (or `reg.env()` in R3) as a read-only planning input for profile/property-driven registration; the narrowness is a feature (a registrar observes config and votes/mints, it does not reach into the live container during planning).
- **context-close** — Because there is no async Drop, a registrar-minted bean's async teardown must be declared on the bean (a DisposableBean-analogue carried by the BeanSpec, e.g. a shutdown hook) and driven by the container's reverse-dependency-order `shutdown().await` — context-close owns the teardown sequence; the registrar only mints the definition carrying the teardown intent. The BeanSpec must be able to express a container-driven shutdown hook, not rely on Drop.
- **post-processor-spi** — The registrar runs in the definition-EDITING window (after the config-parsing BDRPP-analogue, like Spring's ImportBeanDefinitionRegistrar running after the BDRPP phase), and CANNOT itself register a definition-phase post-processor — Spring forbids a registrar from registering a BDRPP because it already runs too late. leaf turns this silent 'too late' into a LOUD compile/plan diagnostic (a registrar's attempt to register a definition-phase post-processor is rejected), a §2.9 improvement over Spring's silent unsupported behavior.
- **refresh-lifecycle** — The registrar's `register()` slots into refresh-lifecycle's fixed-order template at the definition-contribution/editing window — after eager config contribution, before the freeze/markBeanAsCreated boundary and before instantiation — so its definitions exist before any bean is built (Spring: wired via @Import so definitions exist before instantiation; a @Bean-produced registrar would run too late). R2/R3's build-time-freezable split moves the registrar's control flow earlier (to build time) where possible, leaving only the opaque-supplier residual in the runtime template.
- **bean-naming** — `register_bean("name", ...)` and `register_alias` exercise bean-naming's name map + alias + collision policy: a registrar registering a duplicate name under no-override is a LOUD RegisterError (bean-naming's collision-as-loud-error, not silent shadowing). Spring's known v7 bug (#36648 — registrar beans initially not honoring default allow-bean-definition-overriding) is the cautionary tale: leaf must ensure registrar-minted definitions go through the SAME override/collision policy as macro-minted ones (unification).
- **autowiring-resolution** — The supplier's `ctx.bean::<Foo>().await` delegates to the engine's get/resolution path, so the registrar pulls collaborators through the SAME fail-fast autowiring-resolution policy a constructor parameter would (type → generics → qualifier → primary/fallback → name), surfacing the same NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException-analogue on ambiguity. `BeanSpec::not_autowirable()`/`primary()`/`fallback()`/`order()` set the flags autowiring-resolution and primary-fallback read during candidate selection of the registrar-minted bean.
- **background-bootstrap** — `BeanSpec::background_init()` marks a registrar-minted slow singleton for concurrent init on the bootstrap executor (Spring's `backgroundInit()` on the spec), participating in bean-registry's strict-vs-lenient per-bean creation guard exactly like a macro-minted background-init bean — the registrar is just another way to set the background-init flag on a definition.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Does the supplier's OPAQUE-CLOSURE analyzability wall force a permanent runtime-re-invoke island, and how big is it? On a build-time/codegen substrate (Phase-2 compile-vs-runtime keystone), a registrar's opaque `.supplier(closure)` definitions cannot be frozen into generated source (Spring's exact `aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration` problem) — so the registrar must be re-invoked at startup for those. R2 shrinks this island to only opaque suppliers (declarative `.wired()` freezes); R1 makes the whole registrar a runtime island; R3 auto-infers the split. Whether leaf accepts a runtime-re-invoke island at all — and whether the re-invoke must replay init/destroy customizers to not lose wiring (Spring's `customizers` MultiValueMap hazard) — depends on the undecided compile-vs-runtime split and the metadata/codegen boundary.
- WHEN can a registrar run relative to the registry freeze, and can it run mid-instantiation? bean-registry's R2 immutable-snapshot shape forbids post-freeze mutation, so a registrar must run strictly pre-freeze and CANNOT mint definitions during preInstantiate the way Spring's can; bean-registry's R1/R3 mutable shapes accommodate it. This is the same mutable-concurrent-vs-immutable-snapshot registry question bean-registry flags, viewed from the imperative-registration side — does leaf preserve Spring's mid-bootstrap registration freedom or take the freeze-early AOT shape? Cross-cuts bean-registry, refresh-lifecycle, conditions.
- Is the async opaque-supplier's boxed-future-per-construction acceptable, or does the hot path (prototype/scoped beans minted in a registrar loop) need a monomorphized zero-cost path? AFIT/RPITIT-not-dyn-safe forces a Box per construction at the origin-agnostic Factory seam (§2.5 tension). For singletons it's cold/fine; for prototypes/scoped it's a hot cost. Whether leaf keeps a monomorphized typed construction path (R2's `.wired()`) distinct from the boxed opaque-supplier path is the async-execution-model decision (§3) viewed through the registrar's supplier — and it interacts with the scope/ownership keystone (how a prototype's per-instance handle is stored).
- How is a registrar's IMPERATIVE conditional decision made accountable to the condition report? A registrar's `if env.matches_profiles(...)` branch silently chooses what to register, invisible to condition-report (which explains every declarative match/no-match). Does leaf require registrars to RECORD their branch outcomes (a reporting channel), accept the legible-only-by-reading-the-code gap, or constrain imperative conditions? This cross-cuts the unified diagnostics model (§2.9) and the conditional strategy (an env-reading registrar is inherently a runtime gate, not cfg).
- What PLANNING CONTEXT does a registrar legitimately get beyond `&Environment`? Spring keeps the contract narrow (Environment only) but threads more (BeanFactory/ResourceLoader) via Aware/constructor injection for richer registrars. leaf's narrow `&Environment`/`reg.env()` is faithful but may under-serve a registrar that needs the active definition set or a resource loader (e.g. to probe a dynamic module directory). Widening the contract erodes the narrowness; this cross-cuts aware-callbacks, resource-loading, and the async/execution-context model (does a registrar ever legitimately need to await, e.g. to scan a directory — and where would that async work live without violating §2.3?).
- Is there a first-class `Registrar` CONCEPT, or is it 'just the BeanRegistry SPI behind the macros' (R3)? R1/R2 keep a named `Registrar` trait (Spring-parity legibility); R3 erases it into 'one registration act, four syntaxes' (maximal unification §2.11, but loses the named-contract parity Spring's release notes emphasize). This is the unification-vs-parity-legibility call, and it cross-cuts whether the BeanRegistry SPI is THE shared registration primitive all of bean-registry/configuration-classes/import-composition target, or one of several seams.
- The two-source duality for registrars: a static link-time/codegen registrar (default 'classpath') PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned WASM-module registrar, both feeding the ONE registration SPI with origin-agnostic suppliers. R1/R3's mutable-SPI shape accommodates a dynamic-source registrar naturally; R2/R3's build-time-freeze shape can only treat a WASM-sourced registrar as a runtime island (its closure has no build-time presence). How a dynamic-source registrar reconciles with any build-time freezing — and how the anti-DCE self-check counts a registrar whose crate the binary never path-references (silent missing beans) — is unresolved (CANDIDATE, not a decision), cross-cutting the cross-crate composition model.

### Notes
Confidence: high on intent. I read the dedicated feature section (1058-1086: the functional `register(BeanRegistry, Environment)` contract, the fluent `.prototype()/.lazyInit()/.description()/.supplier(ctx->..)` spec, `ctx.bean(Type)` explicit-pull, @Import wiring + @Conditional gating, ImportAware-capability, the named design intent that @Bean-method-per-one-concrete-bean 'gets in the way' of dynamic/looping/multiple registration so BeanRegistrar replaces the AOT-unfriendly BDRPP/ImportBeanDefinitionRegistrar, and the load-bearing hidden concept that the instance supplier is the AOT-friendly part) plus the two deeper treatments (2311-2343: the eight registerBean overloads, the curated Spec as a discoverable subset of AbstractBeanDefinition, suppliers-pull-not-autowire as an intentional AOT choice, env.matchesProfiles + arbitrary control flow; 2644-2678: the full Spec→RootBeanDefinition-setter mapping incl. backgroundInit/fallback/infrastructure/notAutowirable/order/scope-added-7.0.4, getPreferredConstructors==null when a supplier is set making the lambda the sole instantiation path, infrastructure()-vs-notAutowirable() distinction, and the build-time-determinism-requirement that profile/property decisions freeze at AOT build time), and the AOT seams (2671-2688, 2421, 71, 207, 964: aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration=true, BeanRegistrarAotContribution re-invoking register() at startup through a fresh BeanRegistryAdapter, ImportAware metadata reattachment, the customizers MultiValueMap re-apply channel, and 'Default code generation is not supported for bean definitions declaring an instance supplier callback' as the determinism-over-dynamism wall). I also noted the v7 bug #36648 (registrar beans initially not honoring default allow-bean-definition-overriding) at 2464/3003.\n\nI grounded T1 against the cross-crate doc (Layer-0 must-be-path-referenced 149; the three DCE layers + silent-failure self-check mandate 135-152/345-360; linkme-default + thin-const-descriptor + ordering-as-DATA 165-171; Architectures A/B/C/D 277-337 with D as the universal escape hatch and C as the wasm/build-time path; generics-cannot-register + macro-hard-error + register_component!(Concrete) shim 153/349; build.rs/proc-macro single-crate walls 357; scope-discovery-not-global 362; life-before-main is hostile to async, register descriptors/factories not live objects 155) and T2 against the charter invariants (§2.3 async-first/explicit-execution-context, §2.4 reactive-hot/poll-cold, §2.5 no-global-lock/minimal-Arc, §2.9 rich-early diagnostics, §2.10 thin-macros/escape-hatch-at-every-level, §2.11 unification) and the nightly envelope (RPITIT/AFIT stabilized 1.75 but NOT dyn-compatible → boxed future at the dyn/origin-agnostic Factory seam 160-182; async closures stable 1.85 model async factories but store boxed 264; no async Drop, experimental-incomplete with tokio-shutdown crashes → explicit container-driven reverse-order shutdown().await is strictly better 273-281; const-eval baseline for const descriptors 216-224).\n\nThe three realizations are genuinely divergent on the axis that matters for THIS feature — how the OPAQUE-SUPPLIER analyzability wall (Spring's own central design constraint) is handled relative to the build-time/runtime split: R1 = a faithful imperative closure over the live DefinitionRegistry with one uniform async supplier (highest Spring fidelity + best DX, but the whole registrar is a runtime island whose suppliers can't be frozen); R2 = declarative-by-default with the supplier opaque-by-exception, splitting `.wired()` (build-time-freezable, identical to #[bean]) from `.supplier(closure)` (the loudly-flagged runtime-re-invoke island = rust-native per-definition aotProcessingIgnoreRegistration) — best AOT-lineage fit and earliest diagnostics, but more concepts and pre-judges the compile-vs-runtime split; R3 = no first-class Registrar at all, just a thin `BeanRegistry` SPI + a `register!` DSL that auto-splits static-vs-dynamic statements, unifying the registrar with the #[bean]/#[component] macro emit path (maximal unification + purest thin-macro/escape-hatch story, but the most macro machinery and erases the named-contract parity). R1 vs R2 differ on uniform-supplier-vs-analyzability-split; R3 differs on first-class-concept-vs-just-the-SPI-behind-the-macros.\n\nThings I deliberately did NOT decide (held as Phase-2 questions): the size/necessity of the runtime-re-invoke island and the compile-vs-runtime split; whether a registrar can run mid-instantiation vs strictly pre-freeze (bean-registry's mutable-vs-immutable registry); whether the boxed-future-per-construction supplier cost needs a monomorphized escape; how an imperative registrar's conditional decisions become accountable to condition-report; what planning context beyond &Environment a registrar gets; whether there's a first-class Registrar concept or just the SPI; the two-source (link-time + WASM-module) duality and the anti-DCE self-check for registrars. Every realization keeps the supplier/Factory seam ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC (a native, codegen, hand-written, test-double, or WASM-module-sourced registrar registers indistinguishably through the same SPI) and routes through linkme/codegen/explicit (Arch A/B/C/D, with D — App::with_registrar / a hand-written fn(&mut dyn BeanRegistry) — as the always-present escape hatch §2.10). I built concretely on the two provided upstream designs: bean-registry (the register_definition SPI as the stable §2.10 escape hatch this feature writes into, the origin-agnostic boxed-future Factory the supplier erases to, the no-global-lock per-bean creation guard, the freeze boundary that constrains when a registrar may run) and import-composition (which already UNIFIES altitude-4/ImportBeanDefinitionRegistrar with programmatic-registration as the Spring-7 BeanRegistrar successor, supplies the #[import(MyRegistrar)] reachability path and the runtime-re-invoke-island framing, and the registrar-cannot-register-a-BDRPP loud-diagnostic rule).\n\nNaming ('Registrar', 'BeanRegistry', 'DefinitionRegistry', 'BeanSpec<T>', 'ResolveCtx', 'RawBeanDefinition', 'Factory', 'ErasedBean', 'Environment', 'App::with_registrar', 'register!', '.wired()', 'opaque_supplier') is Phase-1 placeholder. ## Background / Parallel Bean Initialization  `[background-bootstrap]`

Spring's `@Bean(bootstrap = Bean.Bootstrap.BACKGROUND)` (6.2, carried into 7.0) is a SURGICAL, OPT-IN tool for the ONE real startup bottleneck — a slow, independent singleton (the canonical case: a JPA `EntityManagerFactory`/connection pool whose provider init is seconds long). The precise intent to replicate (Spring doc lines 1978-2012, 2466-2516): (1) MARK, don't globalize — Spring deliberately refuses to parallelize all bean creation (that reintroduces the deadlock/ordering hazards it spent years taming and makes startup nondeterministic); instead the user explicitly marks the slow independent beans, explicitly provides the executor (so they own the thread budget), and explicitly models the dependency edges. \"A surgical tool for the real bottleneck, not global concurrency semantics imposed on every bean.\" (2) THE EXECUTOR IS REQUIRED — background init runs `instantiateSingletonInBackgroundThread` on a `bootstrapExecutor` bean (`DefaultListableBeanFactory.setBootstrapExecutor`); WITHOUT one, background markers are SILENTLY IGNORED at runtime (Boot 3.5+/4 auto-configures a `bootstrapExecutor` delegating to `applicationTaskExecutor`). (3) DEPENDENCY MODELING IS THE USER'S JOB — you don't get parallelism for free: a NON-`@Lazy` injection point INTO a background bean BLOCKS the dependent's creation until the background bean is ready, so real parallelism requires `@Lazy`/`ObjectProvider` on the dependent (the deferral handle is the cycle/serialization breaker); and `@DependsOn` targets are forced to init on the MAIN bootstrap thread BEFORE the background bean's init is triggered (a `@DependsOn` dependency is never parallelized by the dependent's marker). (4) JOIN-BEFORE-REFRESH-COMPLETES — Phase 1 of `preInstantiateSingletons` collects `CompletableFuture`s for the background beans and `CompletableFuture.allOf(...).join()`s them before refresh completes; all non-`@Lazy` background inits MUST finish by end of context startup (\"a context that started is a context that works\" extends to background beans — they are joined, not fire-and-forget). The JPA precedent: a proxy is injectable immediately, first real access blocks until bootstrap completes, but completion is ENFORCED before context-refresh ends so DB-infrastructure availability is predictable. (5) THE LENIENT/THREAD-AWARE LOCKING THAT MAKES IT SAFE — Spring moved OFF a global singleton mutex to a per-registry `ReentrantLock` + `lenientCreationLock`/`Condition` with a per-thread strict-vs-lenient policy (`isCurrentThreadAllowedToHoldSingletonLock`: null=forced full lock, true=may-lock-but-accept-lenient-fallback, false=forced-lenient). The MAIN bootstrap thread is strict; BACKGROUND init threads are ALWAYS lenient; unmanaged threads are inferred from a thread-name-prefix comparison against the captured `mainThreadPrefix`. A `true`-flagged thread that loses `tryLock()` builds the bean leniently OUTSIDE the lock (tracked in `singletonsInLenientCreation` with a hand-rolled deadlock detector) rather than blocking on a lock held by user init code; the trade is that the main thread now TOLERATES and SKIPS a benign `BeanCurrentlyInCreationException` (issue #34349), trusting another thread to finish. \"Pay a little determinism to buy parallelism and far fewer deadlocks.\" leaf must replicate ALL of this INTENT — the opt-in marker, the explicit required executor (with loud-not-silent behavior when absent — a charter-§2.9 divergence from Spring's silent-ignore), the dependency-edge modeling (non-lazy dependent blocks; deferral handle is the parallelism enabler; `@DependsOn`-analogue forces main-thread-first), the join-before-refresh-completes all-or-nothing discipline, and the lenient/thread-aware no-global-lock creation policy — WITHOUT Spring's global-mutex lineage, its thread-name-prefix heuristic (no Rust analogue), its blocking `.join()` on a thread (in async Rust this is an `.await` on a join handle / structured-concurrency scope), its under-lock @PostConstruct (executor-blocking in async Rust), or its assumption of native-only construction (a background-marked bean's construction recipe must be origin-agnostic so a WASM-module/test-double provider initializes concurrently and joins identically). CRITICAL SCOPING: this feature is NOT the creation driver and NOT the executor — bean-instantiation OWNS the construct→populate→init→publish sequence, the eager two-pass loop, and the per-bean strict/lenient creation guard; task-execution OWNS the injectable lifecycle-bound executor. This feature is the MARKER + ITS SEMANTICS: which beans init concurrently, how their dependency edges serialize vs parallelize, how their in-flight futures are collected and joined before refresh completes, and how the strict/lenient flag is threaded into the creation guard for background-thread builds. It must NOT pick the marker's surface syntax vs config form, the executor handle type, the creation-guard substrate, the join/structured-concurrency primitive, or whether the dependency graph is discovered recursively vs planned — those are Phase 2 or owned upstream.

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Literal marker-on-definition + a background-future ledger spliced into the eager pass (Spring-faithful, async-retyped)
Mirror Spring most directly. A bean is marked background-init at the DEFINITION level — a `background_init: bool` (plus a resolved `bootstrap_executor` selector) field on the merged definition (bean-definition owns the record; this feature only reads the flag and supplies its meaning), emitted by a thin attribute the same way `lazy`/`primary` are (e.g. `#[bean(bootstrap = background)]` — surface syntax is Phase 2). The eager pre-instantiation pass (bean-instantiation's two-loop `preInstantiateSingletons` analogue) is SPLICED, not rewritten: during Phase 1, for each `!abstract && singleton && !lazy` definition, the driver branches on the marker — an UNMARKED bean is constructed inline on the main bootstrap task as today; a MARKED bean's construction future is NOT awaited inline but `spawn`ed onto the injected `bootstrapExecutor` (resolved from task-execution by role/qualifier) and its join handle pushed into a per-refresh `BackgroundLedger { Vec<(DefIdx, JoinHandle<Result<(), ResolveError>>)> }`. The background build runs the SAME construct→populate→init→publish sequence (bean-instantiation's driver) but with the per-creation strict/lenient flag set to LENIENT (the rust-native `isCurrentThreadAllowedToHoldSingletonLock`==false): it builds outside the per-bean creation guard and reconciles a benign in-creation race, exactly as bean-instantiation's lenient path specifies. DEPENDENCY-EDGE MODELING is realized at the populate step and is the heart of this realization: (a) when ANY bean's populate resolves a NON-deferred injection point whose target is a background-marked bean still in-flight, the resolving task AWAITS that bean's ledger join handle (`.await` on the future, NOT a thread block) before proceeding — this is Spring's "non-lazy dependent blocks until the background bean is ready," realized as an async await on the in-flight future rather than a parked thread; (b) when the injection point is a DEFERRAL HANDLE (ObjectProvider/lazy-resolution proxy from deferral-primitives), populate injects the handle WITHOUT awaiting — the target resolves on the handle's first call, which is the parallelism enabler (the dependent proceeds concurrently); (c) `@DependsOn`-analogue edges are resolved EAGERLY ON THE MAIN bootstrap task BEFORE the marked bean's construction future is spawned — the driver forces those targets to completion on the main task first, so a depends-on target is never itself parallelized by the dependent's marker. JOIN-BEFORE-REFRESH: after Phase 1's main-task loop finishes spawning and inline-building, the eager pass AWAITS the whole ledger (`join_all`/try_join over the handles) — all non-lazy background inits must complete before Phase 2 (the SmartInitializing callback) fires and before refresh returns; any background build's error aborts refresh all-or-nothing (the join surfaces the first failure with its full assembly chain). EXECUTOR-REQUIRED is LOUD not silent (charter §2.9 divergence from Spring): if any bean is background-marked but no `bootstrapExecutor` resolves, the eager pass fails at refresh with a rich diagnostic naming the marked beans and the missing executor role — NOT a silent downgrade to inline init (though a documented config escape hatch may opt into silent-inline-fallback for portability).

**Pros:** Tightest intent fidelity: the marker-on-definition, the spawn-onto-bootstrapExecutor, the non-lazy-dependent-blocks / deferral-handle-enables-parallelism / DependsOn-forces-main-first edge rules, and the join-before-refresh-completes all map 1:1 to Spring (lines 2004-2012, 2477) — maximally teachable and traceable, and it reuses bean-instantiation's existing lenient creation path verbatim rather than inventing a new concurrency model.; Minimal new surface: this feature adds a definition flag, a per-refresh ledger, three populate-time edge rules, and a join step — it does NOT duplicate the creation driver, the eager loop, the creation guard, or the executor (all owned upstream), satisfying the charter's unification pressure (§2.11) and keeping the feature genuinely surgical.; Async-native realization of Spring's two blocking hazards: the dependent's "block until ready" becomes an `.await` on the in-flight future (frees the executor worker instead of parking a thread), and the end-of-refresh `.join()` becomes a `join_all().await` — both dissolve Spring's thread-blocking into cooperative await points (charter §2.3).; Loud-on-missing-executor (vs Spring's silent ignore) is a direct §2.9 diagnostic win: a background marker that silently does nothing is exactly the kind of quietly-wrong behavior the charter forbids — the rich "these beans were marked background but no bootstrapExecutor exists" error catches the most common misconfiguration at boot.; Origin-agnostic by construction: the spawned future calls bean-instantiation's erased boxed-future factory, so a WASM-module/test-double background bean initializes concurrently and joins through the identical ledger — the feature can't tell native from dynamic-source construction.; Reuses the strict/lenient flag bean-instantiation already threads through the creation context — background==lenient is a single per-creation flag value, not a new mechanism, so the hardest-won concurrency code lives in ONE place (bean-instantiation) and this feature only sets the flag.

**Cons:** Inherits bean-instantiation's single highest-risk dependency: the lenient "build outside the guard and reconcile a benign in-creation race" is Spring's hardest-won code; if that upstream path is wrong, every background bean is wrong — this feature's correctness is fully hostage to a deferred upstream keystone.; The populate-time "await the in-flight background future" edge rule makes ordinary bean resolution mutually entangled with the background ledger across `.await` points — a dependent awaiting a background bean that itself (transitively, via a non-deferred edge) awaits the dependent is a deadlock the await-graph must detect (Spring's hand-rolled `checkDependentWaitingThreads` deadlock detector has no free Rust analogue; structured concurrency helps but doesn't auto-detect a logical await cycle).; Determining "is this injection-point target a background-marked bean still in-flight?" at populate time couples this feature to autowiring-resolution's resolution path and to a live view of the ledger — a cross-feature read on every non-deferred injection point during background-active refresh, a hot-ish coupling on the cold path.; The marker is on the SINGLETON definition only; FactoryBean-product background-init, scoped beans, and lazy beans are explicitly out of scope (Spring fires background only for non-lazy singletons) — the driver must correctly REFUSE the marker on ineligible beans (loud error), an extra validation surface.; Join-all-before-refresh means the SLOWEST background bean gates refresh completion just as in Spring — correct per intent, but it means a mis-marked fast-but-contended bean can serialize worse than if left inline (the lenient reconciliation overhead can exceed the parallelism gain for non-slow beans), a footgun the diagnostics should surface but can't prevent.

**Mechanisms considered:** definition-level `background_init` flag + resolved bootstrap-executor selector (emitted by a thin attribute like lazy/primary), read by the eager pass; per-refresh `BackgroundLedger` of (DefIdx, JoinHandle) — spawn marked beans onto the injected bootstrapExecutor, collect handles; per-creation strict/lenient flag (background==lenient) threaded into bean-instantiation's existing creation guard — reuse, not reinvent; populate-time edge rules: non-deferred injection of an in-flight background bean → await its ledger handle; deferral handle → inject without awaiting; DependsOn → force-build on main task before spawning; join-before-refresh: join_all/try_join the ledger after Phase-1 main loop, before SmartInitializing; first error aborts refresh all-or-nothing; loud-on-missing-executor self-check (charter §2.9) replacing Spring's silent-ignore, with an optional documented silent-inline-fallback config escape hatch

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Substrate-neutral on collection — the marker is a flag on whatever merged def the registry holds, regardless of how descriptors were collected (linkme distributed-slice default / build.rs codegen / explicit). What this feature FIXES cross-crate: the background marker is a thin const field the macro emits via absolute paths (no logic in the macro, charter §2.10); the ledger, the spawn-onto-executor, the edge rules, and the join all live in leaf-core alongside the eager pass. The mandatory `bootstrapExecutor` is the SAME Layer-0 DCE hazard task-execution flagged, sharpened here: the executor lives in a runtime integration crate (leaf-tokio); if that crate is declared-but-never-path-referenced its registration vanishes and every background marker silently does nothing — so the loud-on-missing-executor self-check is THIS feature's contribution to the cross-crate anti-DCE mandate (cross-crate doc §152): at refresh, if background-marked beans exist, assert a bootstrapExecutor was found, naming the marked beans and the expected executor role if not. The marker descriptor must survive DCE the same as any bean descriptor (it IS a bean descriptor field). A WASM-module 'classpath' contributes background-marked descriptors identically — the spawn calls the module-dispatching factory; the ledger and join are origin-blind (CANDIDATES: a static link-time registry default PLUS an optional dynamic directory-scanned source, both feeding the SAME eager pass the marker is read in — not a decision). The bootstrap-executor SELECTOR resolution is by role/qualifier through autowiring-resolution (task-execution's named applicationTaskExecutor-backed bootstrapExecutor), so a downstream crate can override the bootstrap executor by registering a primary/qualified one — no cross-crate ABI beyond the executor trait task-execution already exposes.

**T2 — execution context:** The eager pass runs at REFRESH on the main bootstrap task (cold path). UNMARKED beans build inline on that task; MARKED beans' construction futures are `spawn`ed onto the injected bootstrapExecutor (task-execution's lifecycle-bound facility) and run CONCURRENTLY with the main loop. Each background build runs bean-instantiation's construct→populate→init→publish sequence with the per-creation flag set LENIENT — it builds OUTSIDE the per-bean creation guard and reconciles a benign in-creation race; it may `.await` (DB pools — the canonical slow case) and a blocking provider must use spawn_blocking (charter §2.3). The Send/'static question surfaces sharply: a background build runs on a (likely multi-thread) bootstrap executor, so its construction future must be Send+'static — a marker on a bean whose factory captures a non-Send is a compile-or-startup error (a real constraint with no Spring analogue, shared with task-execution). DEPENDENCY BLOCKING: a non-deferred dependent AWAITS the target's ledger join handle (cooperative await, frees the worker) — Spring's thread-block becomes an `.await`; a deferral-handle dependent does NOT await (proceeds concurrently — the parallelism enabler); a DependsOn target is force-built on the MAIN task before the dependent's future is spawned. JOIN: after the Phase-1 main loop, the eager pass `join_all/try_join`s the ledger — all non-lazy background inits complete before the SmartInitializing callback fires (which itself runs OUTSIDE any guard, the sanctioned place for cross-bean work). CANCELLATION: cancelling refresh must cancel the bootstrap executor's in-flight background tasks (structured-concurrency scope or explicit abort of the ledger handles), and because there is no async Drop, a partially-built background bean holding async-cleanup resources must run its registered cleanup explicitly on the unwind path — the ledger retains enough state to do so; the all-or-nothing eager pass must leave NO half-built background bean with dangling resources on abort. A background build's error propagates as a rich ResolveError through its join handle carrying its assembly chain (charter §2.9); the join surfaces the first failure and aborts refresh. DEADLOCK: a logical await cycle (background A's non-deferred edge awaits B, B's awaits A) must be detected — the await-graph or the per-bean lenient fallback must break it, the rust-native analogue of Spring's tolerated-benign-in-creation skip. Teardown of published background singletons is the normal reverse-order shutdown().await (context-close); they are published into `ready` identically to inline beans.

#### R2 — Concurrency as a derived dependency-graph plan: background-eligibility is COMPUTED (independence-proven), the marker only HINTS/forces (plan-then-execute)
Question whether the user should hand-pick which beans run concurrently at all. Spring forces manual marking because the JVM discovers the dependency graph lazily during creation and has no phase boundary — it CAN'T know which beans are independent, so it makes the user assert it. leaf's intent (surgical, explicit-dependency-modeled, fail-fast) can be served by COMPUTING independence from the dependency graph (dovetails with bean-instantiation's R2 plan-then-execute). PLAN phase (cold, after registry freeze): walk merged defs and their declared dependency edges (constructor/setter/field + DependsOn + deferral-handle edges) to compute the topological creation plan partitioned into dependency WAVES. A bean is BACKGROUND-ELIGIBLE iff it is a non-lazy singleton with no not-yet-built non-deferred dependencies at its wave (i.e., genuinely independent at that point) — independence is DERIVED, not asserted. The user-facing MARKER becomes a HINT + an override, not the source of truth: `#[bean(bootstrap = background)]` means "this is the slow one I care about — schedule it eagerly/aggressively on the bootstrap executor and DON'T let the planner skip parallelizing it," and a `bootstrap = main`/`#[depends_on]` is a FORCE-SERIAL override pinning a bean (and its DependsOn targets) to the main wave/task. EXECUTE phase: walk waves in order; within a wave, every node is independent BY CONSTRUCTION, so the executor is free to fan them out onto the bootstrapExecutor concurrently — background init is no longer a per-bean lenient-locking mode but "the wave executor runs planner-proven-independent nodes wherever, and prioritizes user-hinted slow nodes." The non-lazy-dependent-blocks rule falls out of wave ordering (a dependent is in a LATER wave than its non-deferred dependency, so it simply isn't started until the dependency's wave completes — no await-on-in-flight-future, no deadlock detector); the deferral-handle-enables-parallelism rule falls out of the planner treating a deferral edge as NON-blocking (it doesn't create a wave-ordering constraint, so dependent and target can share a wave); DependsOn falls out as a forced edge pinning the target to an earlier wave on the main task. JOIN-BEFORE-REFRESH is the wave executor completing the last wave. The marker's residual job is purely TUNING — which independent beans to actually push onto the bootstrap executor (vs build inline) and with what priority — because not every independent bean is worth a spawn (the spawn/await overhead only pays for the genuinely slow ones, which is exactly what the user knows and the planner doesn't).

**Pros:** No per-bean lenient creation guard on the background path AT ALL (the strongest charter §2.5 answer, inheriting bean-instantiation R2's posture): the plan proves a wave's nodes are independent, so concurrent background nodes never contend on a not-yet-built bean — Spring's hardest-won strict/lenient reconciliation and its hand-rolled deadlock detector simply aren't needed for planned background beans.; The three Spring edge rules become STRUCTURAL consequences of wave ordering rather than runtime await-rules: non-lazy-dependent-blocks = it's in a later wave; deferral-enables-parallelism = the edge doesn't constrain wave order; DependsOn-forces-main-first = a forced earlier-wave edge — far easier to reason about, and a logical await cycle is impossible (a cycle is a PLAN error caught before any construction, the richest+earliest diagnostic, charter §2.9).; Catches the most common Spring footgun automatically: Spring users who mark a bean background but forget `@Lazy` on the dependent get NO parallelism and don't know why — here the planner SHOWS the dependency structure, so the diagnostics can tell the user exactly why their marked bean isn't parallelizing ("bean X is marked background but dependent Y injects it non-deferred, forcing Y to wave N+1"), turning a silent non-win into an actionable report.; Best AOT-lineage fit (charter §3): the wave plan + background-eligibility verdict is exactly what a build-time pass could EMIT as a const schedule, so the expensive independence analysis can move to build time later without changing the executor — and the marker-as-hint survives as build-time tuning metadata.; Decouples "is it safe to parallelize?" (planner, automatic) from "is it worth parallelizing?" (user marker, tuning) — a cleaner separation than Spring conflating both into one manual marker, and it means a user can mark the slow bean without also having to manually verify its independence (the planner verifies).; The bootstrapExecutor still owns the thread budget (user provides it), preserving Spring's "you own the thread budget" intent, while the planner owns correctness — the division of labor matches the charter's abuse-resistant-threading goal (§2.3).

**Cons:** Diverges from Spring's mental model most: "the marker decides what runs in background" becomes "the planner decides what CAN, the marker tunes what SHOULD" — a real teaching/parity cost, and a user expecting their marker to be the sole authority may be surprised the planner won't parallelize a bean it can't prove independent.; Inherits bean-instantiation R2's core weakness wholesale: dependency edges must be knowable BEFORE construction, but by-type/qualifier/ObjectProvider/FactoryBean-product edges may only materialize at resolution time — so the background-eligibility verdict rests on a conservative over-approximation, which can mark a genuinely-independent slow bean as NOT background-eligible (false dependency), defeating the parallelism the user explicitly asked for (a worse outcome than R1's runtime await, which sees the real edge).; Forbids/complicates Spring's mid-bootstrap dynamism: an init-method/registrar registering a new background-marked def mid-creation has no node in an already-computed plan — entangled with the bean-registry mutable-vs-frozen decision; if frozen, this is a divergence from Spring that this feature can't resolve alone.; Two execution paths must stay coherent (the planned wave executor for eligible beans + a recursive fallback for lazy/dynamically-discovered/on-demand beans, which is essentially R1's await-on-in-flight model) — and the fallback IS R1, so the planner's value is only as large as the fraction of background beans it can prove eligible statically.; More cold-path work and memory: computing the full DAG + waves + per-bean eligibility for the whole singleton set just to parallelize a handful of slow beans is overhead Spring's targeted manual marking avoids — acceptable at boot but real, and the analysis tightness directly bounds the realized parallelism.; The marker's demotion to a hint risks under-delivering on the user's explicit intent: if the planner conservatively refuses to background a bean the user KNOWS is independent, the user has no way to override safety (only to tune) without a force-parallelize escape hatch that re-introduces R1's manual-assertion risk for that bean.

**Mechanisms considered:** plan phase: topological dependency-wave partition (reuse bean-instantiation R2's plan) extended with a per-node background-eligibility verdict = independent-non-lazy-singleton; marker demoted to HINT (schedule eagerly/aggressively + don't-skip-parallelizing) + force-serial override (bootstrap=main / DependsOn pins to earlier wave on main task); execute phase: wave-ordered, intra-wave concurrent fan-out onto the bootstrapExecutor, prioritizing user-hinted slow nodes — no per-bean creation guard on the planned path; edge rules as structural wave-ordering consequences: non-deferred edge = later wave; deferral edge = non-constraining; DependsOn = forced earlier-wave edge; background-eligibility false-negative diagnostic: report WHY a marked bean isn't parallelizing (which dependent forces it later) — turns Spring's silent non-win into an actionable report; conservative edge over-approximation + recursive R1-style fallback (await-on-in-flight) for lazy/dynamically-discovered/on-demand beans

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for the AOT/build-time lineage (shared with bean-instantiation R2): the wave plan + background-eligibility verdicts are exactly what a build.rs/codegen pass could EMIT as a const artifact, and the runtime-assembly path computes the identical plan at refresh. The plan computation is the IDEAL place to run the anti-DCE self-check AND background-eligibility validation together (cross-crate doc §152): a silently-DCE'd contributing crate shows up as a missing node / unsatisfiable edge → a loud plan-time error, AND a background-marked bean whose dependency structure can't be resolved is caught at plan time naming the unsatisfiable edge. The dynamic-edge problem is the real T1 tension (inherited from bean-instantiation R2): by-type/qualifier/ObjectProvider/FactoryBean-product edges may only be knowable at runtime, so a build-time plan must conservatively include all candidate edges (coupling to candidate-resolver), and conservative edges can wrongly mark a slow bean non-eligible. A WASM-module 'classpath' contributing late background-marked definitions either must register before planning (a pre-freeze source — tension with build-time planning) or force a re-plan / fall to the recursive fallback (CANDIDATES: static link-time registry default PLUS optional dynamic directory-scanned source feeding the planner's input set — not a decision). The mandatory bootstrapExecutor's Layer-0 DCE hazard is identical to R1 — the plan-time validation also asserts the executor exists if any background-eligible/hinted nodes will be fanned out. Generics-cannot-register inherited (plan holds concrete nodes; macro hard-errors on a generic background bean).

**T2 — execution context:** PLAN phase is synchronous cold-path (after freeze, single-threaded, no IO) — reads merged defs + edges, produces a wave schedule with per-node background verdicts. EXECUTE phase runs on the main bootstrap task; each wave is a bounded concurrent fan-out of construction futures onto the bootstrapExecutor (task-execution's facility), wrapped in a structured-concurrency scope so a failure/cancel in one node cancels the wave and unwinds cleanly (charter §2.3). Because the plan proves intra-wave independence, there is NO per-bean creation guard and NO await-on-in-flight-future and NO deadlock-detector on the planned background path — the strongest no-global-lock posture (§2.5). Background-eligible/hinted nodes' construction futures must be Send+'static to run on the (multi-thread) bootstrap executor (same constraint as R1, surfaced at compile/plan time — a marked bean with a non-Send factory is a plan-time/compile error). Construction may `.await` (DB pools); blocking factories use spawn_blocking. SAFE PUBLICATION: each node moves its finished handle into `ready` after init; a later wave reads `ready` lock-free (deps already published). The non-lazy-dependent-blocks intent is realized as wave ORDERING (the dependent's wave doesn't start until the dependency's wave completes) — no thread block, no await-on-future. CANCELLATION: cancelling refresh cancels the structured scope → in-flight background construction futures are dropped; partially-built background beans with async cleanup must run it explicitly (no async Drop) on the unwind path; un-published nodes hold nothing in `ready` to leak. The recursive FALLBACK driver (for lazy/dynamic/on-demand background beans) runs R1's await-on-in-flight model with the per-bean lenient guard for the rare contended case. SmartInitializing fires after the final wave, outside any guard, on the main task. Errors are rich ResolveError: plan-phase errors carry the full graph context (cycle, unsatisfiable edge, why-not-parallelizable) at the earliest point; execute-phase errors carry the node's assembly chain. Teardown is normal reverse-order shutdown().await (context-close).

#### R3 — Background-bootstrap as a declarative startup POLICY bound from config + a thin orchestrator over an origin-agnostic init-task stream (policy-as-data)
Push 'explicit, owned, tunable, chosen by configuration' (Spring's design intent: you own the thread budget, you mark explicitly) to its logical end and lean hardest into unification (§2.11) and origin-agnosticism. Instead of (only) a per-bean code marker, express background-bootstrap as a named STARTUP POLICY — data bound from the Environment via config-properties/binder — that names which beans (by name/qualifier/role/predicate) init concurrently, on which named bootstrap executor, with which dependency-edge treatment and which join-timeout. The unit the orchestrator consumes is an origin-agnostic `InitTask { def_idx, kind: Background|Main, executor: ExecutorHandle, edges: resolved-from-graph }` — a small descriptor, NOT a live object, so a native bean, a FactoryBean-product, a WASM-module bean, or a test-double are ALL background-schedulable identically (the orchestrator can't tell them apart). The thin `BackgroundOrchestrator` does exactly three things: (1) PARTITION the eager singleton set into main-task vs background-executor init tasks per the policy + the per-bean marker (marker and config policy MERGE — the marker is the per-bean default, the config policy is the central override, mirroring Spring's `spring.task.execution.mode=force`-style central lever and the JPA precedent of declaring the bootstrap executor in config); (2) SPAWN background init tasks onto the named executor, collecting an origin-blind join-handle stream; (3) JOIN the stream before refresh completes, with a policy-driven join-timeout and a policy-driven on-missing-executor behavior (loud-fail by default, silent-inline-fallback if the policy opts in — both are POLICY values, not hardcoded). The dependency-edge rules (non-deferred-dependent-blocks, deferral-enables, DependsOn-forces-main) are EDGE TREATMENTS the orchestrator applies uniformly, and the policy can TUNE them (e.g. a strict policy that treats ALL edges as blocking, a lenient policy that maximizes overlap) without touching bean code. This makes the whole feature a thin data-driven scheduler: the per-bean marker is the ergonomic default; the config policy is the central, inspectable, relaxed-bound, origin-tracked tuning surface (dogfooding config-properties/binder/config-metadata exactly like task-execution's R3 ExecutionProfiles); and the orchestrator is a small origin-agnostic splice into the eager pass. A custom orchestrator (or a WASM-aware one) can be registered behind the same seam — origin-agnosticism at the orchestrator level.

**Pros:** Most literal realization of Spring's 'explicit, you-own-the-budget, surgical, central-config-driven' intent AND the JPA-precedent of declaring the bootstrap executor in config: which beans background-init, on which executor, with what join-timeout, becomes inspectable bound data — central tuning/monitoring is uniform with all other leaf config (config-properties/binder/config-metadata reuse, strong unification §2.11).; Maximal origin-agnosticism (the brief's core mandate): the orchestrator consumes origin-blind InitTask descriptors, so a WASM-module/test-double/FactoryBean-product background bean is scheduled, spawned, and joined identically — the future dynamic 'classpath' is not a special path, just more InitTasks; the brief's 'provider of dyn Trait must not assume native construction' is honored at the scheduling seam.; Tunable edge treatment without code changes: a user can flip a too-aggressive parallelization to strict-blocking, or set a join-timeout, or override which executor backs background init, all from config — Spring's central force-mode lever generalized, and a far better operational story than Spring's recompile-to-retune marker.; Catches misconfiguration as a data/validation problem: a policy naming a bean that isn't background-eligible, or a missing named executor, or a marker contradicting a force-serial policy, is caught by a startup validation pass over the policy table with a rich diagnostic (charter §2.9) — and the policy table is a trivially inspectable diagnostic surface ("which beans are background, on which executor, with what timeout, which call sites forced them").; Marker + config MERGE keeps DX high (the common case is just the per-bean marker, zero config) while exposing the central lever only when needed (low-ceremony default, full control on demand — charter §2.1) — the marker isn't replaced, it's the default the policy can override.; The orchestrator is a thin, swappable seam (origin-agnostic at the orchestrator level), so Phase 2 can host R1's await-on-in-flight ledger OR R2's wave-planner BEHIND the orchestrator as the partition/spawn/join implementation — R3 is the most Phase-2-composable shape, deferring the mechanism while fixing the data-driven, origin-agnostic, config-tunable surface.

**Cons:** Heaviest machinery and most risk of Phase-1 over-reach: a config-bound policy + an orchestrator seam borders on DECLARING a 'background-bootstrap is a tunable subsystem' meta-architecture that Phase 2 owns — must be held strictly as a CANDIDATE; its generality only earns its weight if leaf ships an invisible opinionated default (which then resembles R1, questioning the policy tax for a surgical feature).; 'Which beans background-init chosen by a config policy resolved at runtime' trades compile-time legibility for flexibility — a typo'd bean name / missing executor name in the policy is a runtime-or-startup error, not a compile error (charter §2.9 prefers the earliest legible diagnostic; mitigated only by a startup-validation pass over the policy table), and it's a real divergence from Spring's purely-code marker.; Couples a deliberately surgical feature to the entire config/environment/binder stack (config-properties, binder, relaxed-binding, origin-tracking, config-metadata) — a heavy dependency web for what Spring expresses as a single `@Bean` attribute; if those neighbours' designs shift, this feature's surface shifts with them.; Marker-vs-policy MERGE semantics are subtle: what wins when a per-bean marker says background but the central policy force-serializes it (or vice versa)? Spring's force-mode is unambiguous (central wins); leaf must define the precedence, and a wrong merge silently mis-schedules — a new correctness surface Spring doesn't have.; Still doesn't dissolve the hard parts: the orchestrator must STILL apply the dependency-edge rules and STILL spawn+join, so it sits on top of R1's await-on-in-flight or R2's wave plan — the policy layer adds tuning/inspection but the underlying concurrency correctness (lenient reconciliation or wave independence) is unchanged and equally hard, just one layer down.; An origin-agnostic orchestrator seam widens the stable-ABI surface (the InitTask descriptor + orchestrator trait become cross-crate contracts a custom orchestrator implements) — powerful but a real cost the charter's minimal-stable-ABI preference (cross-crate doc §102) weighs against, for a feature that may not need that extensibility.

**Mechanisms considered:** named startup POLICY bound from the Environment via config-properties/binder (which beans by name/qualifier/role/predicate, which named executor, edge treatment, join-timeout, on-missing-executor behavior); origin-agnostic `InitTask { def_idx, kind, executor handle, resolved edges }` descriptor stream — native/FactoryBean-product/WASM/test-double scheduled identically; thin `BackgroundOrchestrator`: partition (marker + policy MERGE) → spawn onto named executor → join-before-refresh with policy-driven timeout + on-missing behavior; marker-as-default + config-policy-as-central-override (Spring force-mode analogue + JPA-bootstrap-executor-in-config precedent), with defined precedence; policy-driven edge treatment (strict-all-blocking vs lenient-max-overlap) tunable without touching bean code; startup-validation pass over the policy table (charter §2.9) + inspectable policy diagnostic surface; orchestrator is a swappable origin-agnostic seam hosting R1/R2 as the impl

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Best fit for the two-source duality AND the config-as-runtime-classpath story (shared with task-execution R3): the policy VALUES come from the Environment (config files/env vars) resolved at runtime, so retuning which beans background-init / which executor / what timeout needs NO recompile and NO cross-crate registration — sidestepping the link-time DCE hazard for the TUNING surface entirely. What still crosses crates: the per-bean marker is a thin definition-flag descriptor (linkme/codegen/explicit, substrate-neutral) the macro emits via absolute paths; the mandatory bootstrapExecutor lives in a runtime integration crate (leaf-tokio) and is the SAME worse-than-ordinary Layer-0 DCE hazard (cross-crate doc §149) — but here the policy's on-missing-executor behavior is itself DATA (loud-fail default vs silent-inline opt-in), and the startup-validation pass over the policy table is the natural anti-DCE self-check (§152): a policy naming background beans with no resolvable executor is a loud validation failure naming the beans and the missing executor. A WASM-module 'classpath' contributes background-marked InitTasks; the orchestrator schedules them origin-blind (CANDIDATES: static link-time registry default PLUS optional dynamic directory-scanned source, both producing InitTasks the orchestrator partitions — most natural of the three for the WASM slot-in, not a decision). leaf-core hosts the InitTask descriptor, the orchestrator trait, and the policy schema — a minimal stable ABI the thin macro targets (emits the marker flag) and a custom orchestrator could implement (a widened-ABI cost flagged in cons). Generics-cannot-register inherited (marker hard-errors on generic background beans).

**T2 — execution context:** Policy binding is synchronous cold-path during the Environment-ready phase (before the eager pass). The orchestrator runs at REFRESH on the main bootstrap task: PARTITION is synchronous (merge marker + policy, resolve edges, build the InitTask stream); SPAWN fans background InitTasks onto the named bootstrapExecutor (task-execution's lifecycle-bound facility); JOIN awaits the origin-blind handle stream before refresh completes, bounded by the policy join-timeout (a timed `.await` — on timeout, a policy-driven loud failure with the still-pending beans named, charter §2.9). Background construction runs bean-instantiation's sequence; on a multi-thread bootstrap executor the future must be Send+'static (same constraint as R1/R2). Edge treatment is applied by the orchestrator: non-deferred-dependent awaits the target's handle (or, per a strict policy, is force-serialized to main); deferral-handle proceeds concurrently; DependsOn forces the target onto the main task first. The orchestrator is the single chokepoint to emit per-policy metrics/observation (the central-monitoring intent) and named startup-instrumentation steps. CANCELLATION: cancelling refresh cancels the orchestrator's spawned tasks (structured scope or explicit abort of the handle stream); partially-built background beans with async cleanup run it explicitly (no async Drop) on the unwind path; the orchestrator retains the ledger to do so. Errors are rich ResolveError attributed to the failing InitTask (the seam gives precise 'background init failed for bean X on executor Y' diagnostics); a join-timeout is a distinct rich error naming the slow beans. SmartInitializing fires after the join completes, outside any guard, on the main task. SAFE PUBLICATION and TEARDOWN are unchanged from R1/R2 (publish into `ready` after init; reverse-order shutdown().await via context-close; the orchestrator owns no teardown — published background singletons are ordinary singletons).

### Rust clashes
- Spring's `.join()` on a thread becomes an `.await` on a join handle — a reframing, not a port. Spring collects `CompletableFuture`s for background beans and `CompletableFuture.allOf(...).join()`s them on the main bootstrap thread before refresh completes (line 2477). In async-first Rust, `.join()`-as-thread-block is exactly the executor-blocking hazard the charter forbids (§2.3): blocking the main bootstrap task on a parked thread ties up an executor worker. The rust-native realization is `join_all/try_join.await` over join handles (or a structured-concurrency scope's completion) — cooperative await, not a parked thread. The 'non-lazy dependent blocks until the background bean is ready' rule has the same fate: Spring parks the dependent's thread; leaf must `.await` the in-flight future, freeing the worker. This is intent-preserving but mechanically different.
- The thread-name-prefix heuristic for strict-vs-lenient has NO Rust analogue. Spring's `isCurrentThreadAllowedToHoldSingletonLock()` infers an unmanaged thread's strict/lenient status by comparing its thread name against the captured `mainThreadPrefix` (line 2510) — a JVM-thread-identity hack. Rust async tasks have no stable thread identity (work-stealing migrates a task across threads mid-execution) and the 'main bootstrap thread' is a task, not a thread. So the strict/lenient policy CANNOT be thread-inferred; it must be carried as an EXPLICIT per-creation flag in the creation context (background==lenient, main==strict), threaded through the recursive creation call graph (bean-instantiation's concern) — and must survive a task being moved across threads or a spawn onto the bootstrap executor. Inferring it from the ambient thread is actively wrong.
- Send + 'static fractures the marker's freedom. Spring background beans carry no such bound — any bean can be `@Bean(bootstrap=BACKGROUND)`. In Rust, a background build runs on a (likely multi-thread) bootstrap executor, so its WHOLE construction future (factory + populate + init) must be Send + 'static.
