BUT it inherits primary-fallback's cross-crate coupling: '@Fallback wins only if it is the sole non-fallback' is decided over the FULLY-collected candidate set from ALL linked crates, so the precedence of an infrastructure handle now depends on whether some downstream crate registered a competing bean of that type — a whole-graph, assembly-time count the macro cannot see locally (T1), making this strictly more cross-crate-entangled than the pure-table approach for what is conceptually a container-local handle.

**T2 — execution context:** Registration is synchronous on the bring-up thread (cold path); the synthetic definition's instance-supplier (`|_| handle.clone()`) returns an already-live handle, so it never blocks or awaits despite living on the async construction path. Resolution reads go through the same candidate-resolution machinery as any bean, inheriting whatever lock/snapshot discipline bean-instantiation settles on for the definition map; no new concurrency surface beyond what the registry already has. Cancellation/error propagation is identical to any other definition lookup.

#### Compile-time-typed 'context services' bundle threaded through the resolution Ctx; runtime table only as the dyn/dynamic-source escape hatch
Lean into Rust's static typing: the common infrastructure handles are NOT erased into a TypeId table on the happy path. Instead they live in a typed `ContextServices` bundle (a struct of `EventPublisher`, `ResourceLoader`, `EnvironmentHandle`, the container handle) that the engine threads into every factory invocation as part of the resolution `Ctx` (the `ctx` a supplier already pulls collaborators from, à la programmatic-registration's `ctx.bean(T)`). A bean that wants the publisher writes `#[autowired] publisher: EventPublisher` and the thin macro emits, at the injection point, `ctx.context_service::<EventPublisher>()` — a typed, monomorphized lookup against the bundle, NO TypeId table, NO erasure. A SECONDARY runtime `extra_providers` table (as in realization 1) exists ONLY for the origin-agnostic / dynamic cases: a WASM-module-provided handle or a runtime-registered service the macro could not see at the injection site — keeping the dynamic door open without paying erasure cost on the native common path. Lowest precedence: a real bean of the type is always preferred; the context_service call is the fallback the injection-point resolution falls to only when no bean definition matches. The `ignore`-Aware half: callback delivery (aware-callbacks) and this typed-pull are BOTH explicit at the declaration site (`impl ContextAware` vs `#[autowired]`), so the split is purely syntactic and the `ignore` mechanism is unneeded — but a NEW residual appears: if the precedence decision ('no bean of this type, so use the context service') is pushed toward compile time, the macro cannot see cross-crate bean registrations, so it likely must stay a runtime fall-through anyway (surfaced as a phase2Question).

**Pros:** Lowest overhead (charter §2 priority #2): the common infrastructure handles avoid erasure, Arc-churn, and TypeId hashing on the native happy path — typed, monomorphized, stack-friendly.; Async-context-clean: the bundle rides the same resolution `Ctx` already threaded through async construction, so 'what runs where' for handle access is identical to normal collaborator pull — no separate lookup path.; Keeps a runtime table strictly as the dynamic/dyn escape hatch, so origin-agnosticism is preserved for WASM/test-double without imposing its cost on every bean.; DX: `#[autowired] publisher: EventPublisher` reads identically to injecting any collaborator — no special API, the split is invisible to users.; Thin macros: the emitted `ctx.context_service::<T>()` is exactly what a user could hand-write against the public Ctx — satisfies §2.10.

**Cons:** Splitting native (typed bundle) vs dynamic (runtime table) creates TWO resolution paths for the SAME conceptual feature — a unification cost (§2.11) and a place for the two to drift; the lowest-precedence rule must hold identically in both.; The 'does a bean of this type exist? if not, use the context service' precedence presumes whole-bean-set visibility at the injection site — but cross-crate the macro sees only its own invocation (T1), so it CANNOT know whether another crate registered a real EventPublisher bean; the precedence decision likely defers to runtime/assembly anyway, partially defeating the static win.; The typed bundle must enumerate the known handles at the kernel level; adding a new infrastructure handle type later touches the bundle struct (less open than a TypeId table) — though the runtime table escape mitigates this.; If a user's real bean should shadow a context service, that shadowing needs runtime cross-crate knowledge the macro lacks — so the 'no erasure' benefit may apply cleanly only to handles with NO possible user bean of that type (the container handle itself), not to publisher/resource-loader which users could redefine.; Container self-reference (the bundle holding the container handle) still raises the Arc-cycle/ownership Phase-2 question.

**Mechanisms considered:** generics / monomorphized typed lookup (ContextServices struct threaded through Ctx); thin proc-macro emitting a hand-writable ctx.context_service::<T>() call; runtime registry as a secondary escape hatch for dynamic/dyn origins; dyn dispatch only on the dynamic path, concrete types on the native path

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The typed-bundle native path is fully monomorphized within each consuming crate and needs no cross-crate registry — but that is exactly its T1 weakness: a downstream crate's #[autowired] macro cannot know whether another crate registered a competing real bean of the handle's type, so the lowest-precedence 'real bean shadows handle' decision cannot be made at the macro site and must be resolved at runtime/assembly when the full bean set is known. The secondary runtime table handles the genuinely dynamic origins (WASM module, runtime-registered services). No link-time collection, no DCE hazard; the bundle is constructed by the binary's façade, the only place that sees the whole graph.

**T2 — execution context:** Context-service access happens INSIDE the async construction path: ctx.context_service::<T>() is a synchronous, non-blocking field read on a Ctx already in scope during a bean's async factory — it never awaits and never blocks the executor, so it adds no execution-context risk beyond the construction call already in flight. Errors (handle absent) surface as a normal resolution error propagated up the construction future. The bundle is built once at bring-up (cold path, sync); the secondary table follows realization 1's frozen-after-bring-up, lock-free-read discipline.

### Rust clashes
- NO REFLECTIVE SETTERS TO IGNORE: `ignoreDependencyInterface(Class)` exists only because Spring's setter-injection path reflectively SCANS a bean for `setX` methods and would otherwise treat an Aware setter as an autowire point. Rust has no such scan — injection points are explicitly captured at the declaration site by the thin macro, and Aware-style callbacks (if leaf even has them) are a distinct opt-in trait impl. So the `ignore` HALF of the split has no reflective target and very likely COLLAPSES; the open question is whether ANY suppression survives (e.g. preventing a handle-type from being a normal autowire candidate) or whether the whole concept reduces to just the registration side.
- NOT-A-BEAN-DEFINITION vs Rust's uniform construction: Spring can store a live, already-constructed object in `resolvableDependencies` that has no `BeanDefinition` and no factory. In Rust the engine's resolution machinery is built around typed factories/suppliers per the bean-definition redesign; a handle that is 'just an already-built value with no recipe' is a special shape (a pre-resolved instance) that the registry must accommodate without a construction recipe — closer to the singleton CACHE than the definition MAP.
- CONTAINER SELF-REFERENCE / OWNERSHIP: Spring stores the GC'd `ApplicationContext` handle in a map the context itself owns — harmless under GC. In Rust this is a literal reference cycle (container owns table owns Arc<container>), forcing a Weak handle, an interior-mutability split, or a borrow discipline. The handle TYPE is the same erased-handle keystone as a cached singleton (Phase 2), so this cannot be settled here — but the cycle is a Rust-specific hazard the JVM never surfaces.
- LOWEST-PRECEDENCE AS ORDER vs SYNTHETIC PRIORITY: Spring encodes 'lowest precedence' as the last branch of `determineAutowireCandidate`. In Rust this can be either a query-order fall-through (consult the extra table only when the definition path is empty) or a `default_candidate=false`/@Fallback flag — i.e. it maps onto leaf's ALREADY-designed candidate-resolver/primary-fallback levers rather than needing a new tie-break rung. Picking which is a unification call, not a JVM transliteration.
- TYPE-MATCH WITHOUT RUNTIME REFLECTION: Spring matches the resolvable by `isAssignable(type, ...)` reflectively, including supertype/interface matches. Rust must do this via concrete TypeId equality plus stable (1.86) trait-upcasting for the declared-supertrait case; an injection point asking for a trait the handle implements must have that supertrait relationship DECLARED, and unrelated-trait queries need explicit registration per requested type — generics cannot be registered at all.

### Interactions
- **bean-registry** — bean-registry's intent already states it 'OWNS the resolvableDependencies map'. This feature is the REGISTRATION SPI and lookup-contribution side of exactly that map. Realization 1 adds the second resolution source (extra_providers) directly onto the engine; realization 2 instead routes through the definition store. Either way the stored handle MUST be the same origin-agnostic erased handle shape bean-registry holds in its singleton cache — the keystone the registry already commits to.
- **autowiring-resolution** — autowiring-resolution names resolvableDependencies as the LOWEST-precedence step of determineAutowireCandidate. This feature must plug into that ladder as the final fall-through (realization 1: only consulted when the definition path is empty; realization 2/3: via default_candidate/fallback or a runtime fall-through). It contributes the candidate of last resort but does NOT own the tie-break order.
- **candidate-resolver / primary-fallback** — Realization 2 reuses the default_candidate=false / @Fallback lever as the precise meaning of 'lowest precedence' — making this feature a CONSUMER of candidate-resolver's filtering flags and primary-fallback's demote-unless-sole-non-fallback semantics. Even in realization 1, the rule 'a real bean of type T always shadows the infrastructure handle' is conceptually the same demotion.
- **aware-callbacks (out-of-area)** — This is the OTHER half of Spring's split: aware-callbacks delivers the SAME infrastructure objects via callback, while this feature delivers them by-type. In Spring `ignoreDependencyInterface` prevents the by-type path from also grabbing the Aware setter. In Rust the two channels are syntactically distinct (trait impl vs #[autowired] point), so the de-collision is structural, not a runtime call — but the boundary between the two features must be drawn so neither double-delivers nor silently drops a handle.
- **container-core** — container-core's façade is what CALLS register_resolvable for each context service (container handle, event publisher, resource loader, Environment) during prepareBeanFactory-equivalent bring-up, before eager instantiation. container-core 'gestures at context services'; this feature provides the concrete SPI by which those services become injectable, so it claims the registration SPI container-core deferred.
- **events, resource-loading, environment, messages-i18n** — These are the CONCRETE handles published through this SPI: ApplicationEventPublisher (events/multicaster), ResourceLoader (resource-loading), Environment (environment), MessageSource (messages-i18n). Each must expose an erased, origin-agnostic handle this feature can register; their realizations must agree on the handle's trait shape so trait-upcasting-based type-match works.
- **programmatic-registration** — Realizations 2 and 3 lean on the instance-supplier / ctx.bean(T) model programmatic-registration designs: realization 2 registers the handle as a synthetic definition with a closure supplier; realization 3 threads handles through the same resolution Ctx a supplier pulls collaborators from. The 'register a pre-built handle' SPI is a natural sibling of programmatic-registration's registerBean(spec.supplier(...)).
- **scopes / concurrency-contract** — Infrastructure handles are de-facto singletons published once at bring-up and read concurrently by every bean under construction; they inherit the singleton safe-publication contract (concurrency-contract) and the scope/ownership keystone (scopes). The container-self-reference Arc-cycle hazard sits squarely on the unsettled ownership model.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Does the `ignoreDependencyInterface` HALF survive at all in Rust? With no reflective setter scan, callback-injection (Aware) and by-type-injection are syntactically distinct at the declaration site — so is there ANYTHING to 'ignore', or does the registration side become the entire feature? If a residual survives, what is it (e.g. a rule that a handle-type is excluded from being a normal autowire candidate)?
- Is a resolvable handle a SECOND resolution source on the engine (a non-definition extra-providers table) or a SYNTHETIC bean definition with default_candidate=false/INFRASTRUCTURE role? This is the unification question: reuse the candidate-resolver/primary-fallback levers vs add a distinct last-resort path. It cannot be settled without the registry-substrate decision.
- How is the container's OWN handle stored without an Arc reference cycle (container owns the table that owns an Arc to the container)? Weak handle, interior-mutability split, or a borrow-scoped handle? This rides entirely on the scope/lifetime/ownership keystone (§3).
- What is the canonical erased handle SHAPE for an infrastructure service, and does trait-upcasting (1.86) suffice to match an injection point that asks for a declared supertrait of the handle? Concrete-struct handles (the container itself) cannot be upcast — do they need exact-TypeId-only matching while trait handles get upcast matching?
- Should 'lowest precedence' be realized as runtime query-order fall-through, as a default_candidate/@Fallback flag, or (realization 3) as a build/assembly-time emission decision — and if build-time, can the diagnostic for 'real bean shadowed the infrastructure handle' stay legible (§2.9), given the macro cannot see cross-crate bean registrations (T1)?
- Predictability/surprise (echoing the #[global_registration] author): if a user accidentally registers a real bean of an infrastructure handle's type, it silently shadows the framework's handle. Should this be loud (a conflict/diagnostic), documented-and-allowed, or forbidden for known handle types?
- Do resolvable handles ever participate in collection injection? Spring: never (single-valued only). If realization 2 makes them synthetic beans, default_candidate=false EXCLUDES them from plain List<T> but a QUALIFIED List<T> would include them — diverging from Spring. Must be reconciled with collection-injection.
- Origin-agnostic dynamic source: should a WASM-module / runtime layer be able to register a resolvable handle AFTER bring-up (mutating the table at runtime), or is the table frozen at the definition-freeze boundary like everything else? Affects whether the table needs interior mutability and a concurrency story on the read-hot autowiring path.

### Notes
Grounding read: Spring intent at docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md lines 355 (prepareBeanFactory enumerates the resolvable deps + Aware processor), 691 (determineAutowireCandidate tie-break ends at resolvableDependency), 703-705 (the resolvableDependencies hidden-concept block), and 2492-2496 (the registerResolvableDependency / ignoreDependencyInterface split, dogfooded via ApplicationContextAwareProcessor as a BPP). Charter invariants honored: stable-only (TypeId/Any/trait-upcasting per nightly doc lines 11-17, 80-81); thin macros (realization 3 emits only hand-writable ctx.context_service calls); origin-agnostic handles preserved across all three; no global lock introduced. Cross-crate (T1): this feature is NOT a link-time discovery feature — infrastructure handles are RUNTIME-published by the container façade during bring-up, NOT collected via linkme/inventory; so the static-link-time-registry-vs-dynamic-directory-scanned-source duality applies only to the ORIGIN of a handle (a WASM-module layer registering a publisher), not to the registration SPI itself, which is a plain runtime method call on the engine. The macro cannot, however, see cross-crate bean registrations, which constrains realization 3's compile-time precedence decision (noted as a phase2Question). T2 (execution context): registration is a synchronous, single-threaded, cold-path operation during bring-up before eager instantiation — no async, no blocking, no cancellation. The read path (consulting the table during autowiring) is on the resolution hot path and must be lock-free/notification-friendly (charter §2.4); a frozen-after-bring-up table is naturally lock-free for reads. Did NOT resolve the meta-architecture (handle shape, ownership/Arc-cycle, registry substrate, table-vs-synthetic-definition) — those are Phase 2 and surfaced above. src/lib.rs is empty and there is no docs/design/phase1 yet; no prior leaf code constrains this design.

## NullBean sentinel (representing a deliberately-absent / null-valued bean)  `[extra-4]`

Spring's `NullBean` is an internal sentinel object that the container substitutes whenever a `@Bean` factory method or a `FactoryBean.getObject()` legitimately returns `null` (FactoryBeanRegistrySupport / doc lines 2551, 693, 2563-2565). Its single job is to keep THREE states distinct that would otherwise collapse into two: (A) "this bean exists and was deliberately produced as null/absent", (B) "this bean exists and is a real value", (C) "no bean of this name/type was ever defined". Without the sentinel, a null-producing factory is indistinguishable from a missing definition, and the container could neither register/type-match it nor satisfy an optional injection point with an EXPLICIT absence. The sentinel is consumed across the whole resolution path, not just inside FactoryBean: (1) registration/type-matching — a NullBean still occupies a name slot and still answers `getBeanNamesForType` so by-type queries and collection scans see it as a (null-valued) member; (2) optional injection — an `@Autowired(required=false)` / `Optional<T>` / `@Nullable` point is satisfied by the NullBean as a definite-absence rather than failing with `NoSuchBeanDefinitionException` (doc 693), while a strict point still treats it as effectively unsatisfiable; (3) deferral — `ObjectProvider.getObject()` is strict (never returns the null), `getIfAvailable()` maps the NullBean to its absence-tolerant result, `getIfUnique()` likewise — the three-tier strictness ladder (doc 2563-2565) must agree on whether NullBean counts as "present"; (4) the in-creation guard — a FactoryBean that returns null WHILE IN CREATION does NOT yield a NullBean but raises `BeanCurrentlyInCreationException` (the null is treated as "not done yet", not "deliberately absent"). The CROSS-CUTTING tension the completeness critic surfaced: in Rust, `Option<T>` already models present-but-none natively, so the real design question is the BOUNDARY — does leaf need a distinct sentinel at all, or does "a bean that resolves to None" collapse into the registry's natural `Option`/erased-handle representation? No existing leaf design (factory-bean treats it as a one-line FactoryBean detail; autowiring-resolution mentions zero-candidates→optional but not present-but-null; deferral-primitives names getIfAvailable but not what a null PRODUCT maps to; collection-injection never says whether a null member is included or skipped) draws that boundary. leaf must replicate the INTENT — a uniform, resolution-path-wide representation that keeps present-but-null distinct from never-defined, satisfies optional points as explicit absence, agrees with the deferral strictness ladder, and turns null-while-in-creation into a cycle error — WITHOUT importing Spring's reflective `equals`-returns-true-for-null `NullBean` object placed into the singleton cache as a real heap object, its `Object`-typed cache that can hold a marker class, or its assumption that the producer is always a native factory (a WASM-module/test-double factory that yields nothing must funnel into the identical absent-representation). It must NOT decide whether the sentinel is a registry-substrate marker, a typestate on the erased handle, or simply Rust `Option<Arc<dyn Any>>` — that is the registry-substrate / scope-ownership keystone owned by Phase 2.

### Candidate realizations

#### Option-native: present-but-none collapses into the registry's optional handle (no distinct sentinel class)
There is NO NullBean *object*. The registry's per-slot value handle is already an `Option`-shaped erased handle — model it as `enum Resolved { Value(Arc<dyn Any + Send + Sync>), AbsentByProduction, /* the slot exists, the producer ran, it yielded nothing */ }` stored against the bean name, vs. the slot simply NOT EXISTING (= never defined, the `None` of the name→slot map lookup). So the three Spring states map to: (A) present-but-null = `Some(slot) where slot == Resolved::AbsentByProduction`; (B) real value = `Some(slot) where slot == Resolved::Value(..)`; (C) no such bean = `None` from the name/type lookup. A `#[bean]` factory whose signature is `async fn make() -> Option<T>` (or `-> Result<Option<T>, _>`) declares at the call site that it may legitimately produce nothing; the thin macro emits a factory closure returning `Resolved::AbsentByProduction` when the user's fn returns `None`. Type-matching: the slot is still indexed under `TypeId::of::<T>()` (and supertraits via upcasting) so `bean_names_for_type::<T>()` returns it — the slot carries its declared `TypeId` independently of whether it currently holds a value, exactly like Spring's `getObjectType()` answering without realizing. Optional injection: `resolve::<T>()` (strict) on an `AbsentByProduction` slot is an explicit, richly-diagnosed unsatisfiable ("bean `foo` exists but was produced as absent; injection point requires a value"); `resolve_optional::<Option<T>>()` / a `#[nullable]`-marked point maps `AbsentByProduction` → `Ok(None)` and a missing slot ALSO → `Ok(None)` — the two converge for the consumer (both are 'you get None') but the DIAGNOSTIC and the count differ. Deferral ladder lives in the provider methods, not the data: `get()` strict (AbsentByProduction → error), `get_if_available()` (AbsentByProduction → Ok(None), still errors on >1), `get_if_unique()` (Ok(None) on absent or ambiguous). In-creation guard: the per-bean creation re-entrancy sentinel (owned by bean-instantiation) wraps factory invocation; if the factory yields `None` while its own name is marked in-creation, the engine raises the cycle error instead of recording `AbsentByProduction` — the null is interpreted as 'not finished' not 'deliberately empty'.

**Pros:** Most rust-native: leans on `Option`/enum exactly where Rust already wants it; no phantom heap sentinel object, honoring §2.5 memory discipline (no extra Arc'd marker per null bean).; DX win: a factory's `-> Option<T>` signature makes 'may be absent' visible IN THE TYPE at the declaration site — the same honesty the charter prizes (cf. deferral-primitives' 'deferral visible in the type').; Cleanly distinguishes the three states with a tiny enum the registry already needs for empty slots; the never-defined vs produced-absent line falls out of map-miss vs `AbsentByProduction`.; Diagnostics are rich (§2.9): the engine knows WHICH of the three states it is in, so it can say 'exists-but-empty' vs 'never defined' rather than one generic NoSuchBean.

**Cons:** Pushes a shape onto the registry-substrate handle (the keystone Phase 2 owns) — if Phase 2 picks `Arc<dyn Any>` without a per-slot enum, this needs a wrapper; risk of pre-deciding the substrate.; The collection-injection question (is an `AbsentByProduction` member included in a `Vec<T>` or silently skipped?) must be answered consistently and isn't free — an enum member in the candidate stream needs a documented filter rule.; A factory that returns a *runtime-decided* nothing (e.g. config-driven) but is typed `-> T` not `-> Option<T>` cannot express absence — forces every conditionally-absent producer to opt into the `Option` return shape (a real ergonomic ask, though arguably correct).; `Option<T>` at an injection point now has two sources (missing slot vs produced-absent) that the consumer cannot tell apart without asking the provider — fine for most code, but loses Spring's ability to treat 'exists-but-null' as a type-match member while 'missing' is not.

**Mechanisms considered:** proc-macro captures `-> Option<T>`/`-> Result<Option<T>>` factory return shape and emits a thin closure mapping user-None to a `Resolved::AbsentByProduction` slot value; runtime registry: name→slot map where slot-presence answers never-defined, and an in-slot enum variant answers present-but-null; `Any` + downcast + trait upcasting (all stable) to keep the slot type-indexed under TypeId even when valueless; injection-point descriptor (macro-emitted) carries the optionality/arity flag that decides the AbsentByProduction→None fold

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** No link-time registration or DCE hazard from the sentinel itself — the `AbsentByProduction` value is recorded at production time inside the running container, never link-collected. The only cross-crate ABI surface is that a downstream/sibling crate's `#[bean] -> Option<T>` factory must funnel its `None` through the same `leaf-core` `Resolved` enum the macro hard-codes a path to (per the cross-crate doc, emitted code references the registry type by absolute `$crate`/fully-qualified path). Keep the `Resolved` enum in `leaf-core` (not the proc-macro crate, which can export only macros) and ultra-stable. A WASM-module/test-double factory yielding nothing uses the identical `Resolved::AbsentByProduction` path — origin-agnostic, no native-construction assumption baked into the variant.

**T2 — execution context:** Recording `AbsentByProduction` happens on the COLD creation path: once per singleton, after the async producer future completes; the lock-free hot read of a present value never branches on the absent arm in steady state. The null-while-in-creation→cycle-error derivation must be async-safe: the creation re-entrancy/epoch guard (owned by bean-instantiation) spans `.await` points with no executor-blocking lock; if the producer future is CANCELLED mid-flight, NO `AbsentByProduction` is recorded — a cancelled producer is 'not done' (identical to in-creation), never 'deliberately absent'. Errors propagate as a typed unsatisfiable carrying the three-state discriminant for §2.9 diagnostics.

#### Typed absence-marker stored in the erased handle (a rust-native NullBean preserving Spring's exists-and-typed semantics)
Keep a genuine sentinel, but make it a zero-size typed marker rather than a reflective object. The singleton/product cache value is an erased handle `BeanHandle` that can hold either `Present(Arc<dyn Any + Send + Sync>)` or `NullProduct { declared: TypeId, supertraits: &'static [TypeId] }`. The `NullProduct` variant is the rust analogue of Spring's `NullBean`: it OCCUPIES the cache slot, carries the full type identity it WOULD have had, and so participates in type-matching as a first-class (but valueless) member — `is_type_match::<T>()` is true, `bean_names_for_type::<T>()` includes it, even though dereferencing it for a value yields absence. This preserves Spring's subtle property that a null bean is STILL a typed member of the container (doc 2551 'still register and type-match a bean whose value is legitimately null'). Resolution: a strict `resolve::<T>()` against a `NullProduct` returns a typed-absence error carrying the declared TypeId and the producer's origin; an optional point unwraps it to `None`. The macro path is unchanged for value beans; a `-> Option<T>`/`-> Result<Option<T>>` factory records `NullProduct` when the user yields nothing. FactoryBean parity is exact: `factory.get_object()` returning `None` → engine stores `NullProduct` in the product cache (separate from the factory's own `Present` slot), while `get_object_type()` (the static declared type) still answers type queries WITHOUT realizing. In-creation: the creation guard intercepts a `None` returned while in-creation and raises the cycle error before the `NullProduct` is ever recorded. Collection-injection rule is explicit and matches Spring: a `NullProduct` member is COUNTED for type-match/uniqueness arbitration (so it can make a single-valued point ambiguous or, as the lone candidate, satisfy an optional point) but is FILTERED OUT of the materialized `Vec<T>`/`Map<name,T>` values (you cannot put 'nothing' into a `Vec<T>`), with the name retained for diagnostics.

**Pros:** Highest INTENT fidelity to Spring: a null bean stays a typed, name-occupying, type-matchable member — preserves the exists-and-typed-but-null semantics that the Option-collapse loses.; The declared-TypeId-without-value mirrors Spring's `getObjectType()` answering without `getObject()` (factory-bean's 'type-match-without-realize') — one mechanism serves both NullBean and lazy/factory type-matching.; Clear, single home for the 'is a null member counted in a collection?' rule (counted-for-arbitration, filtered-from-values) — resolves a real cross-feature ambiguity in one place.; Origin-agnostic by construction: a WASM-module/test-double factory yielding nothing produces the identical `NullProduct` handle; the variant says nothing about how it was (not) built.

**Cons:** Reintroduces a sentinel concept the charter's rust-native lens questions — must justify why `Option`-collapse (Realization A) is insufficient (answer: only for the type-match-as-member property, which may or may not be worth the complexity).; Bakes a two-variant handle into the registry substrate (Phase 2 keystone) more heavily than A — stronger pre-decision risk.; More surface area: every consumer that reads a `BeanHandle` must handle the `NullProduct` arm or risk a panic/incorrect treat-as-present; thin macros must reliably never emit a path that dereferences it as a value.; The 'counted but filtered' collection rule, while faithful, is a subtle footgun: a null member can silently make a single-valued injection ambiguous (NoUniqueBean) — Spring-accurate but surprising; needs a first-class diagnostic.

**Mechanisms considered:** a two-variant erased handle enum (`Present(Arc<dyn Any+Send+Sync>)` vs `NullProduct{declared:TypeId, supertraits:&'static [TypeId]}`) as the registry's stored bean value; `const TypeId::of` (stable 1.91) + `&'static [TypeId]` supertrait list to let the valueless slot answer type/upcast queries; trait upcasting (stable 1.86) so the declared-type membership still satisfies supertrait by-type queries even with no value; macro-captured declared product type recorded onto the merged definition (mirrors factory-bean's static `getObjectType()`/`setTargetType`)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Sentinel is runtime-only; no link-time/DCE exposure. The cross-crate ABI is the `BeanHandle` enum and the recorded `declared: TypeId` + `supertraits` slice — these must live in `leaf-core` (the macro crate can export only macros) with an ultra-stable shape, since emitted factory code and downstream type-match queries reference them by absolute path. A downstream crate's null-producing factory emits a `NullProduct` carrying its statically-known declared TypeId/supertraits (captured by the macro at the declaration site — Rust has no runtime type-name reflection, so the type identity MUST be macro-captured). WASM/dynamic providers slot in identically by supplying the same declared-TypeId metadata.

**T2 — execution context:** Storing a `NullProduct` is a cold-path, once-per-singleton write after the async producer completes; the type-match query path reads the declared TypeId without touching any value future. Hot reads of `Present` values never construct or branch on `NullProduct` beyond the enum tag. In-creation interception and cancellation behave as in Realization A: a `None` returned while the bean's creation epoch is open, or a cancelled producer future, NEVER records a `NullProduct` (it is 'not done', surfaced as the cycle error / left unrecorded). No executor-blocking lock; the typed-absence error carries declared TypeId + origin for diagnostics.

#### No stored sentinel — absence is computed at resolution time from a per-slot producer outcome (lazy/cold-path, runtime-evaluated)
Store nothing special in the hot value cache; instead, each bean slot records its PRODUCER OUTCOME as side metadata the resolver evaluates lazily, never materializing a marker into the lock-free fast-read path. A value bean caches `Arc<dyn Any>` as usual; a producer that ran and yielded nothing records a tiny `Outcome::ProducedAbsent { type_id, origin, produced_epoch }` in a side table OUTSIDE the singleton fast-read cache (so the hot read only ever sees real `Arc` values — §2.4). Resolution consults the outcome table only on the COLD path (a strict read of a present value never touches it). The three states are computed: hot-cache hit = real value; outcome-table hit (`ProducedAbsent`) = present-but-null; neither = never defined. The deferral ladder and optionality are pure functions over these three computed states — `get_if_available` folds `ProducedAbsent` and never-defined both to `None` but tags the diagnostic with which one it was. The in-creation case is not a stored state at all: it is derived — if a resolution requests a name whose creation epoch is currently OPEN and the producer signalled nothing, the resolver synthesizes the `BeanCurrentlyInCreation` error rather than reading `ProducedAbsent` (which is only recorded AFTER creation closes). Collection-injection: the resolver building a `Vec<T>` walks the candidate name set; for each it asks the outcome (value vs ProducedAbsent vs in-creation) and includes only realized values, optionally surfacing a count of absent members in a condition-report-style trace. This treats null-vs-absent as a DERIVED diagnostic property rather than a data type, keeping the sentinel entirely off the value-handle and out of the substrate.

**Pros:** Keeps the hot resolution path pristine: the lock-free singleton fast-read never encounters a sentinel variant, satisfying §2.4 (reactive hot path, no marker branch in steady state) and §2.5 (no per-null heap object).; Least pre-deciding of the registry substrate — the value-handle stays whatever Phase 2 picks; absence is side-table metadata, swappable independently.; Natural fit for runtime-evaluated conditional production (a factory whose absence depends on Environment/profile), aligning with the cross-crate doc's guidance to push conditional logic into runtime evaluation over collected descriptors.; Richest diagnostics: an explicit outcome record carries origin + epoch, so 'exists-but-produced-absent' errors name the exact producer and when — strong §2.9 alignment.

**Cons:** A null bean is NOT a type-match member by default (the side-table is consulted, not the type index) — so it LOSES Spring's 'null bean still answers getBeanNamesForType' unless the side-table is also threaded into type queries, which re-adds complexity.; Two stores (value cache + outcome table) to keep coherent — a coherence/ordering concern that risks exactly the kind of subtle race the singleton cache design works to avoid; needs careful epoch ordering.; Deferral providers must always consult the cold-path table even for a present miss, blurring the hot/cold split for ObjectProvider call sites that genuinely re-resolve every call.; 'Absence is derived, not stored' makes the in-creation-vs-deliberate-absent distinction depend on epoch bookkeeping being exactly right — a correctness-critical timing invariant that's easy to get subtly wrong across async suspension points.

**Mechanisms considered:** a side metadata table `name→Outcome` (`ProducedAbsent{type_id, origin, produced_epoch}`) separate from the lock-free singleton value cache; creation-epoch/re-entrancy bookkeeping (shared with bean-instantiation) to derive in-creation-vs-deliberate-absent rather than store it; runtime evaluation over collected descriptors (per cross-crate doc) so config/profile-driven absence is computed, not link-time-frozen; pure-function resolver folds (three computed states → strict error / Ok(None) / cycle error) instead of a stored discriminant

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** No sentinel data crosses the link boundary; the outcome table is built at runtime inside the container. Cross-crate touch is identical to the others: a downstream null-producing factory funnels its `None` through a `leaf-core` API that records a `ProducedAbsent` outcome (macro-emitted path, absolute-resolvable). Because absence is RUNTIME-COMPUTED, this realization is the most comfortable with the cross-crate doc's directive to keep all conditional/feature/profile logic in runtime evaluation over descriptors rather than in pre-main/link-time code. Origin-agnostic: a WASM/dynamic provider that yields nothing records the same `ProducedAbsent` outcome.

**T2 — execution context:** Outcome recording is strictly cold-path (after the async producer completes); the lock-free hot read of present singletons never consults the outcome table, preserving the reactive hot path (§2.4). The correctness-critical invariant is async timing: `ProducedAbsent` is written ONLY after the creation epoch CLOSES, so a `None` observed while the epoch is open (or a producer future cancelled mid-`.await`) is derived as in-creation/not-done, never as deliberate absence — this epoch ordering must hold across suspension points without an executor-blocking lock (the coherence between the value cache and the outcome table is the sharpest async hazard of this realization). Errors carry origin+epoch for diagnostics; cancellation leaves no outcome recorded.

### Rust clashes
- Spring's `NullBean` is a real heap `Object` whose `equals(null)==true` placed INTO the singleton cache; Rust has no such trick — a null can't be a typed Object you store and later compare-equal-to-null. The carrier must be an explicit enum/`Option` variant, which forces a design decision (sentinel variant vs `Option`-collapse) that the JVM never had to make.
- `Option<T>` ALREADY models present-but-none natively, so Spring's whole reason for `NullBean` (the JVM null can't be both 'in the cache' and 'type-matchable') partly evaporates — but only partly: the property 'a null bean is still a TYPED member that answers by-type queries and can make a single-valued point ambiguous' does NOT fall out of `Option` for free, since a `None` value carries no type identity. This is the exact boundary no existing leaf design draws.
- Spring distinguishes 'exists-but-null' from 'never-defined' via two different runtime structures (a NullBean in the cache vs a map miss). In Rust both naturally want to be `None` (from the consumer's view, `resolve_optional` yields `None` either way), collapsing a distinction Spring keeps — leaf must decide whether the DIAGNOSTIC distinction (and the type-match-membership distinction) is worth preserving when the CONSUMER outcome is identical.
- Spring reads required-ness / `@Nullable` / `Optional` reflectively at the injection point at runtime; Rust has no runtime annotation reflection, so 'this point tolerates a null bean' must be captured at the declaration site by the thin macro into the injection-point descriptor (arity/optionality flag) — the present-but-null→None mapping is a compile-time-captured property, not a runtime-discovered one.
- Spring's null-while-in-creation→`BeanCurrentlyInCreationException` relies on a synchronous re-entrant `getBean` under a lock detecting the in-creation flag. In async leaf, 'in creation' spans `.await` points and the producer is an async closure, so the distinction between 'yielded None because deliberately absent' and 'yielded None because the cycle isn't resolved yet' must be derived from an async-safe creation-epoch/re-entrancy guard, not a thread-held lock — and must not block the executor (§2.3).
- Spring's collection injection silently includes/excludes a null member based on JVM null semantics inside its aggregation loop; Rust cannot put 'nothing' into a `Vec<T>`, so leaf must make an EXPLICIT rule (count-for-arbitration-but-filter-from-values, or skip-entirely) where Spring got an implicit one — a behavioural choice that must be consciously reconciled, not inherited.

### Interactions
- **factory-bean** — Primary producer of the sentinel: a FactoryBean/`#[bean]` async factory returning `Option<T>::None` (or a null product) is THE source of a present-but-null bean. The product cache (separate from the factory slot) must hold the chosen absent-representation; `get_object_type()`/declared-TypeId must still answer type queries WITHOUT realizing the (absent) product. factory-bean's intentRecap currently treats this as a one-line detail — this feature OWNS that line and makes it cross-cutting.
- **autowiring-resolution** — Consumer: the single-valued resolver must map a present-but-null candidate distinctly from zero-candidates. A strict required point sees present-but-null as an explicit-absence error (richer than NoSuchBean); an optional point (required=false/Option/`#[nullable]`) folds it to None. The 'zero candidates → NoSuchBean unless optional' rule (doc 693) gains a third arm: 'one present-but-null candidate → typed-absence, optional-tolerant'. The optionality flag is captured at the declaration site by the macro into the injection-point descriptor.
- **deferral-primitives** — Consumer: the three-tier strictness ladder (getObject strict / getIfAvailable absence-tolerant / getIfUnique both-tolerant, doc 2563-2565) must agree on whether a present-but-null bean counts as 'available'. Spring: getObject throws on it (it's null), getIfAvailable returns null (absent), getIfUnique returns null — i.e. NullBean behaves as absence for the provider. leaf's provider methods are the home of the present-but-null→outcome mapping; the underlying carrier (Realization A/B/C) feeds them.
- **collection-injection** — Consumer with an UNRESOLVED rule this feature must fix: is a present-but-null member counted in a `Vec<T>`/`Set<T>`/`Map<name,T>`? Rust cannot store 'nothing' in `Vec<T>`. Proposed reconciliation (Spring-faithful): counted for type-match/arbitration (can make single-valued ambiguous, can be the lone optional-satisfier) but filtered from the materialized collection values, name retained for diagnostics. Must be decided consistently with @Primary/@Fallback never collapsing collections (doc 722).
- **bean-instantiation** — Owns the creation guard that turns null-while-in-creation into a cycle error rather than recording the sentinel — the null is interpreted as 'not finished' not 'deliberately absent'. The async creation-epoch/re-entrancy sentinel (which bean-instantiation owns) must be consulted by this feature's record-absence step; recording ProducedAbsent/NullProduct happens only AFTER creation closes.
- **bean-registry** — Owns the singleton/product cache and the type index. Whichever carrier is chosen (Option-collapse enum slot, NullProduct handle variant, or side-table outcome) lives ON or BESIDE the registry's stores; the never-defined-vs-present-but-null distinction is fundamentally a registry-slot-presence question. This feature constrains but must NOT pick the registry substrate (Phase 2 keystone).
- **circular-references** — Shares the in-creation distinction: a null returned mid-cycle is one of the BeanCurrentlyInCreation origins. The present-but-null representation must not be confusable with the early-singleton/half-built reference that circular-references exposes into a cycle — both are 'not a final value yet' states that must stay distinct.
- **conditions** — Adjacent-but-distinct boundary: a bean CONDITIONALLY NOT REGISTERED (never-defined, state C) is the conditions feature's job; a bean that IS registered but PRODUCES nothing at runtime (present-but-null, state A) is this feature's job. condition-report records 'not registered'; this feature's diagnostics record 'registered but produced absent'. A factory whose absence is config/profile-driven blurs the line and must be consciously routed to one or the other.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Does leaf need a distinct sentinel at all, or does present-but-null collapse into the registry's natural `Option`/erased-handle representation? This is the boundary the completeness critic flagged — it hinges on whether the 'a null bean is still a TYPED, by-type-matchable member' property (which `Option<None>` loses) is worth preserving, and it cannot be answered without the registry-substrate keystone decision.
- Whichever carrier is chosen, it touches the type-erasure & registry substrate keystone (how `dyn` beans are stored/keyed): is the absent-representation a variant of the stored handle, a side-table, or a map-presence fact? Phase 2 must settle this together with the substrate, not piecemeal.
- Should the never-defined (state C) and present-but-null (state A) distinction be preserved only for DIAGNOSTICS, or also for behaviour (type-match membership, collection counting, ambiguity arbitration)? Spring preserves both; the consumer outcome is often identical (both yield None) — leaf must decide how much of the distinction earns its keep.
- What is the async-safe mechanism for distinguishing 'producer yielded None deliberately' from 'producer yielded None because its cycle is unresolved (in-creation)' across `.await` suspension points, without a thread-held lock and without blocking the executor? Shared with bean-instantiation's creation-guard and circular-references' in-creation detection. Must also define cancellation: a cancelled producer leaves NO absence recorded.
- The collection-injection rule for null members (count-for-arbitration-but-filter-from-values vs skip-entirely) must be unified with the single-valued optionality mapping and with the @Primary/@Fallback-never-collapse-collections invariant — one canonical aggregation/arbitration mechanism, not two.
- How is 'this injection point tolerates a null/absent bean' (the `Option<T>` / `required=false` / `#[nullable]` arity flag) captured by the thin macro into the injection-point descriptor, and is that the SAME descriptor field deferral-primitives and collection-injection read for arity/optionality? (Unification per §2.11.)
- Where does a runtime-conditionally-absent producer belong — is 'a factory that decides at runtime to produce nothing' a present-but-null bean (this feature) or should it be re-expressed as a runtime condition (conditions/condition-family)? The cross-crate doc pushes conditional logic to runtime evaluation over descriptors, which may make the two converge.
- Must the absent-representation be origin-agnostic enough that a future WASM-module/test-double factory yielding nothing funnels into the identical path? (Charter requires origin-agnostic providers; confirm the carrier carries no native-construction assumption.)

### Notes
Scoping note: this feature is NARROW (the representation + resolution-path agreement for present-but-null) but CROSS-CUTTING (it constrains factory-bean, autowiring-resolution, deferral-primitives, collection-injection, bean-instantiation, bean-registry, and borders conditions). It is almost entirely a CONSEQUENCE of two Phase 2 keystones — the registry/type-erasure substrate and the scope/ownership model — so the three realizations are deliberately framed as 'how absence rides the eventual handle' rather than as independent mechanisms; Phase 2 should treat them as facets of the substrate decision, not a separate pick. The single sharpest unresolved behavioural choice handed forward is the collection-injection rule for null members (Rust cannot store 'nothing' in a Vec<T>, so Spring's implicit JVM-null behaviour MUST become an explicit documented rule), and second the async creation-epoch invariant that keeps deliberate-absence distinct from in-creation/cancelled. Grounding files read: charter /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-14-leaf-charter-design.md; Spring intent /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/spring-boot-4-di-design.md (lines 2551, 693, 2563-2565, 707-722, 734); rust capability /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-nightly-features-tradeoffs.md (Any+downcast, trait upcasting 1.86, const TypeId 1.91 — all stable, no nightly needed); cross-crate /home/jan/projects/testing/leaf/docs/research/rust-cross-crate-composition.md (sentinel is runtime-only, no link-time/DCE exposure; only the leaf-core absent-representation ABI is shared and must live outside the proc-macro crate). All three realizations are buildable on 100% stable Rust.

## Mid-bootstrap dynamic definition registration (the defensive-copy / register-during-preInstantiate freedom)  `[extra-5]`

Spring's `preInstantiateSingletons` iterates a DEFENSIVE COPY of `beanDefinitionNames` (doc lines 2466-2475) precisely so that work running mid-eager-pass — an init-method / `@PostConstruct`, a `SmartInitializingSingleton.afterSingletonsInstantiated()` (doc 2471, 369, fired in pass 2 outside the creation lock), or a `BeanRegistrar`/import-driven contribution that lands late — can register NEW bean definitions WITHOUT a `ConcurrentModificationException`. New definitions can legitimately appear AFTER the eager pass has begun, and the iteration completes deterministically over the snapshot taken at pass start; definitions added after that snapshot are simply NOT eagerly driven by this pass (they remain for first-access / a later pass to handle). This is the load-bearing dynamism in direct tension with two neighbour decisions: (a) bean-registry R2's "freeze-early immutable snapshot" registry shape, and (b) bean-instantiation R2's "plan-then-execute" model — both assume the definition set is sealed before the eager pass runs. Spring also has TWO related-but-distinct boundaries that this feature must keep separate: the CONFIGURATION FREEZE (`freezeConfiguration()` in `finishBeanFactoryInitialization`, doc 362 — a tooling/optimization hint that caches `frozenBeanDefinitionNames` and answers by-type queries faster, NOT a hard write-lock) versus the per-bean `markBeanAsCreated` freeze (doc 2452 — the "no more BFPP edits to THIS merged def" line, set lazily on first touch). The intent leaf must replicate: the eager driver operates over a STABLE VIEW of the names it committed to instantiate, while still PERMITTING definitions to be added concurrently/re-entrantly during that pass; a defined, charter-§2.9-loud policy for what happens to those late definitions (driven this pass? deferred? rejected after a hard freeze?); and a clean separation between "the eager pass's working set is fixed" and "the registry is permanently immutable" — the latter is a STRONGER claim Spring never actually makes during refresh. The whole reason this is a named cross-cutting feature, not three deferred questions, is that configuration-classes (registering @Bean defs), programmatic-registration (registrars), conditions (late conditional registration), and background-bootstrap (late futures) ALL need ONE coherent timing contract for "when may a new definition appear, and what is guaranteed about it."

### Candidate realizations

#### R1 — Generational append-only registry with an explicit phase machine (snapshot-the-working-set, never seal the store mid-refresh)
The registry is an APPEND-MOSTLY structure with a monotonic generation counter and an explicit lifecycle PHASE enum (`Loading` → `DefinitionsOpen` → `EagerPass{snapshot_gen}` → `PostEager` → `Frozen`). The eager driver does NOT iterate the live name list; at pass start it takes a cheap O(1) `Snapshot` — conceptually a captured `(generation, len)` watermark plus a stable index view (`Arc<[NameId]>` cloned by pointer, or a generation-tagged slice). It iterates that snapshot. Definition registration (`register_definition`) remains OPEN and lock-light during `DefinitionsOpen`, `EagerPass`, and `PostEager`: appends bump the generation and push onto the tail; they NEVER mutate the slice the in-flight pass holds, so no CME-analogue and no `&mut` aliasing. After pass 1 completes over its snapshot, the driver re-checks the watermark: if `current_gen > snapshot_gen`, late definitions exist — the framework runs a bounded FIXPOINT (drive the newly-appended non-lazy singletons in a follow-up sub-pass, snapshot again, repeat until the generation stops advancing or a cap is hit). Pass 2 (the SmartInitializingSingleton-analogue) runs only after the fixpoint settles, so the 'after EVERY eager singleton exists' guarantee holds. The hard `Frozen` transition happens AFTER refresh completes (or never, for a long-lived dynamic context), converting the structure to a read-optimized immutable view (the bean-registry R2 snapshot) for steady-state lock-free reads. Per-definition `markBeanAsCreated`-analogue freezing (no more blueprint edits to THIS def) is orthogonal and independent of the store-level phase. API feel: `registry.register(def)?` returns `Result` (loud on post-`Frozen` registration per §2.9: `RegistrationAfterFreeze { name, phase, hint }`); the driver internally calls `registry.snapshot_eager_targets()` and `registry.late_targets_since(gen)`.

**Pros:** Directly preserves Spring's exact intent: the eager pass sees a stable working set AND late registration is permitted, with the SmartInitializingSingleton 'everything exists' guarantee intact via the fixpoint.; Append-only is Rust-friendly: a tail-push that never reallocates the snapshot's view avoids `&mut` aliasing entirely; readers hold an `Arc<[T]>` clone, writers append to a separate growable tail — no global write-lock on the read path (charter §2.5).; Cleanly separates the two Spring boundaries: store-phase freeze (late) vs per-def merged-freeze (eager, lazy) — removes the conflation that made three neighbours defer this.; The fixpoint cap gives a §2.9-rich diagnostic for runaway late registration (a registrar that registers a registrar that…), instead of silent infinite churn.; Leaves the door open for a context that stays in `PostEager`/never-`Frozen` (a dynamically reconfigurable container) — the immutable snapshot is an OPT-IN steady-state optimization, not a forced invariant.

**Cons:** A fixpoint over generations is more machinery than Spring's single defensive copy + 'whatever's added is just there next iteration'; risk of over-engineering if late registration is rare.; Append-only + generation watermark complicates by-type indexing: the type index must also be append-aware (or rebuilt at each generation boundary), nudging toward the very 'frozen index' that's in tension here.; If a late definition depends on a bean already eagerly built and published, ordering subtleties arise (the late def's deps may already be sealed) — needs a clear rule.; The 'drive late non-lazy singletons in a follow-up sub-pass' decision quietly re-opens whether late defs are eager-or-lazy by default — a policy choice that must be pinned, not assumed.

**Mechanisms considered:** runtime registry (append-only Vec/segmented store + generation counter; the primary substrate); interior mutability via append-only structures (e.g. boxcar/elsa-style stable-address growable, or `Arc<[T]>` swap) to avoid a write-lock on reads; OnceLock/arc-swap for the eventual `Frozen` immutable-view publication; proc-macro-emitted const descriptors feed the INITIAL load; the late-append path is purely runtime (macros can't know about runtime-registered defs)

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** The INITIAL definition set still arrives via the cross-crate link-time/inventory substrate (the static 'classpath' — const descriptors collected by linkme/inventory; doc cross-crate §substitute-design-space). This realization's late-append path is RUNTIME-ONLY and per-context, so it sidesteps the cross-crate sealing problem entirely: a downstream crate can't 'append at link time after the eager pass' — that's nonsensical — it contributes a const descriptor that's present from `Loading`. The dynamic source (the optional WASM-module 'classpath') maps cleanly onto the SAME runtime-append API: a module loaded mid-bootstrap calls `registry.register(def)?` exactly as native code does, ORIGIN-AGNOSTIC (the def carries an erased provider handle, never assuming native construction). DCE hazard is confined to the static substrate (force-link/`#[used]` at the binary), unaffected by runtime appends. T1 caveat: the fixpoint must be deterministic across crate-link-order so two builds with the same crates produce the same eager-drive sequence — keying late-target ordering on registration generation (a runtime event order) rather than link order is the safe choice, but must be documented as 'registration order, not link order'.

**T2 — execution context:** Runs on the MAIN bootstrap execution context (the refresh task). Registration appends are non-blocking and must NOT take a global lock (§2.5) — a tail-push under a fine-grained per-registry guard or a lock-free append. The eager driver is async: each `preInstantiateSingleton`-analogue is an `.await` (first-access is an await per charter); late registration triggered FROM inside an awaited init-method/factory runs on that same task, so the append is re-entrant w.r.t. the driver — the snapshot model makes this safe (the driver isn't holding a `&mut` to the store across the await). The SmartInitializingSingleton-analogue (pass 2) runs OUTSIDE any creation lock (doc 369) on the bootstrap context after the fixpoint settles. Cancellation: if refresh is cancelled mid-fixpoint, the all-or-nothing teardown (refresh-lifecycle) destroys whatever was published; late-but-not-yet-driven defs are simply discarded with the aborted context. Errors in a late def's eager drive propagate as a refresh failure with the §2.9 causal chain naming the late def AND the registration site (captured `proc_macro::Span`/runtime caller) that introduced it.

#### R2 — Two-tier registry: a SEALED static plan + a small dynamic OVERLAY only for mid-bootstrap additions
Lean INTO bean-registry R2's freeze-early/immutable snapshot and bean-instantiation R2's plan-then-execute — but make the seal apply only to the STATICALLY-KNOWN tier. The registry is two layers: (1) the BASE PLAN — an immutable, build/link-time-frozen `Arc<DefinitionPlan>` (all const descriptors from the static classpath + parsed config), frozen BEFORE the eager pass; the eager driver executes this plan, which is genuinely a sealed snapshot (so plan-then-execute and freeze-early both hold for the common case, with full lock-free read performance). (2) The OVERLAY — a separate, small, append-only `DynamicOverlay` that captures ONLY definitions registered AFTER the seal (i.e. mid-bootstrap additions from init-methods/registrars/SmartInitializing). Lookups consult overlay-then-base (overlay shadows/extends; mirrors container-hierarchy's 'lowest wins' shape). The eager driver, after executing the sealed base plan, drains the overlay: any non-lazy singleton added there is driven in a 'late additions' sub-pass, then (if that sub-pass added more) drains again — bounded. The KEY framing: the expensive, optimized, AOT-friendly machinery only ever sees an immutable plan; the dynamic freedom is quarantined into a clearly-named, deliberately-small overlay that pays a slightly higher (still lock-light) access cost and is understood to be the 'escape hatch', not the fast path. At refresh end, the overlay can be FOLDED into a new immutable base for steady-state (or kept live for a dynamic context).

**Pros:** Maximally compatible with the neighbours' freeze-early/plan-then-execute decisions: the base plan IS the sealed snapshot they want; nothing about the common path changes.; Makes the cost of dynamism VISIBLE and bounded — overlay lookups are explicitly the slower tier, which honestly signals 'mid-bootstrap registration is an escape hatch, not the default' (matches Spring's own posture that this is for registrars/init-methods, not routine).; The base/overlay split mirrors the AOT 'definitions knowable at build time; late ones re-run at startup' lineage (doc line 71) and programmatic-registration's runtime-re-invoke island — a natural home for the BeanRegistrar opaque-supplier case.; Strong T1 story: the base plan is exactly what the static cross-crate substrate produces; the overlay is purely a runtime, per-context concept — the two map onto the two 'classpaths' (static link-time + dynamic WASM-module) cleanly.; Easier to reason about determinism: the base plan's order is fixed at build/seal time; only the (rare) overlay carries runtime-order nondeterminism, and it's isolated.

**Cons:** TWO lookup paths is a real complexity and a correctness hazard: every by-name/by-type query, candidate-resolution, and collection-injection aggregation must consult BOTH tiers and merge consistently (shadowing, de-dup, ordering) — easy to get subtly wrong, and a §2.9 footgun if a bean 'mysteriously' resolves differently depending on which tier it's in.; Risks ENTRENCHING a second-class citizenship for late defs: if overlay beans don't participate identically in candidate-resolution / @Primary / collection ordering, leaf silently diverges from Spring where a late-registered bean is a full peer.; The 'fold overlay into base' step is a non-trivial rebuild (re-index by type, recompute merged defs) — if done per sub-pass it's costly; if deferred it leaves the two-tier cost during all of refresh.; Defining when a bean MUST go in the base vs MAY go in the overlay invites confusion (is a config-class @Bean def base or overlay? it's parsed during BFPP, before the seal — so base — but a registrar's def is overlay): the seal point becomes a subtle, load-bearing line users must understand.

**Mechanisms considered:** OnceLock/arc-swap to publish the immutable base plan; build.rs/proc-macro const descriptors as the base-plan source; runtime registry (small append-only overlay) layered over the immutable base; container-hierarchy-style overlay-then-base lookup composition (reuse the same 'lowest-factory-wins' delegation primitive); linkme/inventory for the base tier only; overlay never crosses crates; AOT-style runtime-re-invoke for opaque BeanRegistrar suppliers landing in the overlay

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Cleanest two-classpath mapping of the three realizations. The BASE PLAN is assembled from the static link-time registry (inventory/linkme const descriptors collected at the binary crate — the only point seeing the whole crate graph; doc cross-crate §global-classpath-gap). Cross-crate contributions are ALWAYS base-tier (they're known at link time), so freeze-early genuinely applies to them — no cross-crate def ever needs the overlay. The OVERLAY is per-context, runtime-only, never linked: a sibling/downstream crate cannot inject into the overlay (good — that's the predictability concern from the donsz.nl blog, doc §global-classpath-gap, addressed by construction). A dynamic WASM-module source is the canonical overlay producer: loaded at runtime, it registers via the same origin-agnostic `register(def)` into the overlay, and its beans are full peers AS LONG AS the two-tier merge is faithful (the central con). DCE protection is needed only for the base tier (force-link the inventory section at the binary). The seal point must be defined relative to the cross-crate assembly: base = everything the binary's link-time collection + config-parse produced before `EagerPass`.

**T2 — execution context:** Base-plan execution is the main bootstrap context, lock-free reads off the immutable `Arc<DefinitionPlan>` (ideal for the async driver's concurrent/background-init reads — background-bootstrap threads read the base with zero contention). Overlay appends happen on whatever context the registering code runs (main bootstrap task for init-methods; possibly a background-init task for a late registrar — so the overlay's append guard must be `Send`/thread-safe but fine-grained, never a global lock, §2.5). The 'drain overlay' sub-passes run on the main bootstrap context after the base pass. Because the overlay is separate from the base, a background-init task appending to the overlay while the main task reads the base needs NO synchronization between them (different structures) — a genuine concurrency win and a clean execution-context boundary. Cancellation/error: a fault while draining the overlay fails refresh with a §2.9 chain that explicitly tags the bean as overlay/late-registered and names its origin; the all-or-nothing teardown covers both tiers. async-Drop absence (nightly doc) means overlay teardown is explicit container-driven shutdown, same as base.

#### R3 — No mid-bootstrap registration: a closed two-phase model that moves ALL contribution before a single hard freeze (prove the dynamism unnecessary)
Question whether leaf NEEDS Spring's register-during-eager-pass freedom at all (charter §2.2: a JVM feature may be realized differently — or proven unnecessary). Spring needs the defensive copy because its definition-contribution phase (BFPP/BDRPP, @Configuration parsing, ImportRegistrars) and its instantiation phase OVERLAP in time and share one mutable registry, AND because some contributors (init-methods, SmartInitializingSingleton) legitimately discover new beans only after instantiation begins. R3's claim: in leaf, the contribution phase can be made STRICTLY CLOSED before instantiation starts. All definition sources are pulled forward into a dedicated, possibly-iterated DEFINITION-ASSEMBLY phase that runs to a FIXPOINT before any instance is built: static descriptors load, config-classes parse, registrars run, conditions evaluate, deferred selectors merge — and if a registrar registers another registrar, the assembly phase re-runs until no new definitions appear (the fixpoint Spring achieves implicitly). Only THEN does a single, hard, irreversible `freeze()` seal an immutable plan, and the eager driver executes a genuinely-sealed snapshot (plan-then-execute and freeze-early hold absolutely, with zero overlay/zero generational complexity). The cases Spring handles via mid-eager-pass registration are redirected: an init-method that wants to 'register a bean' must instead be expressed as a registrar contributing to the assembly fixpoint (run earlier); a SmartInitializingSingleton that aggregates does NOT register (it already can't, in Spring's lock-free pass, do anything that affects the current pass meaningfully) — it only reads. Late registration AFTER freeze is a LOUD §2.9 error (`RegistrationAfterFreeze`) with a remediation hint ('move this to a BeanRegistrar so it runs in the assembly phase').

**Pros:** Simplest, most analyzable, most AOT-aligned: a single immutable plan, no overlay, no generations, no fixpoint-during-instantiation — exactly what bean-registry R2 and bean-instantiation R2 want, with the strongest lock-free steady-state.; Best matches the v4/v7 direction (doc line 71): 'definitions knowable at build time; instances are not' — pushing ALL definition discovery before the freeze is the AOT philosophy taken to its logical end.; Eliminates a whole class of order-dependent, hard-to-diagnose bugs (a bean's resolution depending on whether it was registered before/after the eager pass began) — the §2.9 'loud over silent' posture by construction.; The assembly-phase fixpoint cleanly absorbs registrars-registering-registrars, configuration-classes, conditions, and deferred import selectors into ONE well-defined convergence step — a single timing contract for all those neighbours.; Cross-crate clean: the whole contribution set (static + dynamic-module) must declare itself by the close of assembly; nothing can sneak in later, which is the predictability stance the cross-crate doc endorses.

**Cons:** This DROPS a real Spring behaviour. If any genuine use case requires discovering a NEW bean only after some singletons are built (e.g. a bean whose init inspects runtime state then contributes definitions), R3 forbids it — a parity gap that must be justified, not assumed away.; Forcing 'register only in the assembly phase' may push complexity onto users: an init-method that conceptually wants to register must be refactored into a registrar with possibly-awkward access to the not-yet-built graph (the assembly phase has NO instances — registrars get Environment-only, per programmatic-registration's narrow contract).; SmartInitializingSingleton in Spring runs AFTER instantiation specifically so it can see all beans; if such a callback legitimately needs to register a derived bean (rare but real), R3 has no home for it — it would have to relocate to a second refresh or be disallowed.; A hard irreversible freeze precludes a genuinely dynamic/reconfigurable runtime container (hot-reload, plugin-loaded-at-runtime beans) unless modeled as a fresh child context — a design constraint that may bite the WASM-module-loaded-at-runtime scenario.; 'Prove it unnecessary' is a strong claim that needs evidence across configuration-classes/conditions/background-bootstrap before it can be trusted; if wrong, it's the most expensive realization to walk back.

**Mechanisms considered:** proc-macro/build.rs const descriptors + a runtime assembly-phase fixpoint over registrars/conditions (no late mutation at all); OnceLock-published single immutable plan (`freeze()` is a one-way publish); linkme/inventory for static contribution; dynamic-module contributions must register before assembly closes (a defined load-window); runtime evaluation of conditions/profiles over collected descriptors BEFORE freeze (matches cross-crate doc §38: move conditional logic into runtime evaluation over descriptors), then seal

**T1 — cross-crate/tooling:** Strongest seal story. The binary crate collects ALL static descriptors (inventory/linkme) — the whole-crate-graph view (doc §global-classpath-gap) — then the assembly fixpoint runs registrars/conditions over them and seals. No definition can appear after link+assembly, so freeze-early is total. The dynamic WASM-module 'classpath' must be loaded WITHIN a defined assembly window (before `freeze()`): a module loaded after freeze can only contribute by spawning a CHILD context (container-hierarchy), not by mutating the sealed parent — which is arguably the cleaner, more predictable model for runtime plugins anyway, and sidesteps the donsz.nl predictability concern entirely. DCE protection for the static section at the binary as usual. The cost: the dynamic-source integration must commit to 'declare all your beans before the parent context freezes', which is a sharper constraint than R1/R2 place on a WASM layer.

**T2 — execution context:** The assembly fixpoint runs entirely on the bootstrap context BEFORE the eager async driver starts — a clean phase boundary: contribution (may evaluate conditions, read Environment; no instances exist, nothing to block on except config I/O which is cold-path per §2.4) THEN sealed instantiation (the async driver over an immutable plan). No re-entrant registration during the eager pass means the driver NEVER observes a mutating store — the simplest possible execution-context contract: reads are lock-free and immutable for the entire instantiation phase, ideal for concurrent background-bootstrap init (zero contention, zero generation-recheck). Cancellation during assembly discards the unsealed plan; cancellation during instantiation triggers all-or-nothing teardown of published singletons. A post-freeze registration attempt is a synchronous loud error (no await), caught immediately with a §2.9 hint. The absence of mid-pass mutation also means the SmartInitializingSingleton-analogue is purely a read/aggregate callback with a trivially-safe execution context (outside any lock, after the sealed pass).

### Rust clashes
- No CME to fear, but a STRONGER hazard takes its place: Rust forbids holding a `&mut` (or even mutating through a shared ref without interior mutability) to the registry while the eager driver iterates it. Spring's 'defensive copy' is a GC-cheap `ArrayList` clone of name strings; in Rust the equivalent must be an explicit ownership-clean snapshot (`Arc<[NameId]>` pointer-clone, or a generation watermark over an append-only store) so the driver never aliases a structure that registration mutates. The dynamism is admissible ONLY if registration is append-only / re-entrancy-safe by construction, not by copying.
- The eager pass is ASYNC (each instantiation is an `.await`, charter §2.3). Spring's mid-pass registration is synchronous and re-entrant on one thread; in leaf a late registration can be triggered from inside an awaited init-method, OR from a background-bootstrap task on another execution context. So 'register during the pass' is not just re-entrancy — it can be genuine cross-task concurrency, which the registry's append path must tolerate WITHOUT a global lock (§2.5). Spring's own move off the global mutex to lenient locking (doc 2506-2514) is the precedent, but leaf must express it in async terms.
- Link-time/inventory registries (the cross-crate substrate) are INHERENTLY SEALED at link time — a downstream crate's contributions are fixed before `main`. This is the opposite of 'register at runtime mid-bootstrap'. The two are not in conflict (cross-crate defs are always present from `Loading`; runtime-late defs are a separate per-context concern) but the design MUST keep them distinct or risk implying you can link-register after the eager pass (nonsensical).
- The AOT lineage (doc line 71: 'definitions knowable at build time; instances are not') and `BeanRegistrar`'s opaque-supplier-can't-be-statically-analyzed problem (programmatic-registration) pull HARD toward sealing definitions early. Mid-bootstrap registration is the residual runtime dynamism that resists full build-time freezing — leaf must decide how much to preserve vs how much to redirect into the build-time/assembly phase.
- Spring conflates (in casual reading) two different boundaries that Rust forces us to separate: the soft `freezeConfiguration()` (a by-type-query CACHE hint, doc 362) vs the per-bean `markBeanAsCreated` merged-def freeze (doc 2452) vs an actual immutable-store seal (which Spring NEVER does during refresh). bean-registry R2's 'freeze-early immutable snapshot' risks importing a seal Spring doesn't have. Pinning which boundary is which is prerequisite to deciding whether late registration is even possible.
- Determinism across builds: Spring's late-def ordering is incidental (whatever order init-methods happen to register). Rust link order is not a stable contract, so any realization that drives late defs must key their ordering on a STABLE runtime event (registration generation/order) not on link order — else two builds of the same workspace produce different eager-drive sequences, violating the 'a context that started is a context that works' predictability.

### Interactions
- **bean-registry** — DEEPEST tension and the reason this feature exists: bean-registry R2's 'freeze-early immutable snapshot' must reconcile with 'definitions may appear after the eager pass begins.' This feature supplies the missing contract — whether the store is append-mostly-then-sealed (R1), base+overlay (R2), or assembly-fixpoint-then-hard-seal (R3). bean-registry owns the store substrate and the merged-def freeze; this feature owns WHEN the store stops accepting writes relative to the eager pass.
- **bean-instantiation** — bean-instantiation R2's 'plan-then-execute' and its two-pass eager loop over a defensive copy are precisely the consumer of this contract. This feature defines what 'the defensive copy' becomes in Rust (snapshot/watermark/sealed plan) and whether pass-1's iteration may discover late-added singletons. The two MUST agree on: is the eager working set fixed at pass start, and are late non-lazy singletons driven this refresh or deferred?
- **programmatic-registration** — BeanRegistrar is THE blessed mid-bootstrap contributor. Its opaque-instance-supplier (can't-be-statically-analyzed) is the case that most resists build-time sealing and most wants a runtime registration window. This feature decides whether a registrar runs in a pre-eager assembly phase (R3), appends to a live store during the pass (R1), or lands in an overlay (R2). programmatic-registration's phase2Question 'when-can-a-registrar-run' is answered HERE.
- **configuration-classes** — @Bean factory methods register definitions during config parsing (a BFPP-analogue, before the eager pass) — these are the COMMON case that's already-closed before instantiation in all three realizations. The interaction is confirming config-class-registered defs are base/assembly-tier (sealed before eager), distinct from the rarer truly-late additions. Resolves part of configuration-classes' implicit timing assumptions.
- **conditions** — Late conditional registration: a condition evaluated mid-assembly may add/skip a definition. This feature must place condition evaluation relative to the freeze — the cross-crate doc (§38) recommends evaluating conditions at RUNTIME over collected descriptors BEFORE sealing, which fits R3's assembly fixpoint and R1's DefinitionsOpen phase. Conditions are a definition-contribution input, not a mid-eager-pass mutation, in the cleanest framings.
- **background-bootstrap** — Background-init tasks run on separate execution contexts and may register late defs concurrently (not just re-entrantly). background-bootstrap's 'late-def question' is answered by this feature's no-global-lock append contract (T2). Also: the join-before-refresh-completes discipline must encompass any late defs a background task registers — the fixpoint/drain (R1/R2) or the closed-assembly (R3) must account for background-contributed definitions before pass 2.
- **refresh-lifecycle** — The fixed-order refresh template owns the PHASE transitions this feature relies on (load definitions → BFPPs → BPPs → eager pre-instantiate → SmartInitializing). This feature inserts/clarifies the freeze boundary's position in that template and the all-or-nothing teardown semantics when a late-def's eager drive fails.
- **smart-initializing** — The SmartInitializingSingleton-analogue is the pass-2 callback that must run after EVERY eager singleton exists (doc 369, outside the creation lock). This feature guarantees that invariant survives late registration — pass 2 fires only after the late-def fixpoint/drain settles (R1/R2) or trivially (R3, since no late defs exist). Spring fires it outside the lock specifically as the safe place for cross-bean aggregation, which late registration must not violate.
- **container-hierarchy** — R2's base+overlay lookup reuses the same overlay-then-base / 'lowest-wins' delegation shape as parent/child lookups — a candidate for shared machinery (charter §2.11 unification). Also: R3's 'register-after-freeze → spawn a child context' redirect leans directly on hierarchy for the runtime-plugin case.
