- **post-processor-spi** — BDRPP (postProcessBeanDefinitionRegistry) is Spring's sanctioned add/remove-definitions hook, running strictly before the eager pass. This feature's freeze boundary IS the 'no more BFPP/BDRPP edits' line (doc 2452 markBeanAsCreated). The interaction: BDRPP-tier contribution is always pre-eager (closed in all realizations); this feature distinguishes that from post-eager-pass-begin additions.

### Open questions for Phase 2
- Is the registry append-mostly-then-sealed (R1), base+immutable + runtime overlay (R2), or strictly closed before a single hard freeze (R3)? This is THE cross-cutting decision that determines whether bean-registry R2's freeze-early snapshot and bean-instantiation R2's plan-then-execute are admissible as-is or must be relaxed.
- Does leaf preserve Spring's register-AFTER-eager-pass-begins freedom at all, or redirect all contribution into a pre-instantiation assembly fixpoint (proving the mid-pass dynamism unnecessary, charter §2.2)? If preserved, are late non-lazy singletons driven THIS refresh (fixpoint/drain) or deferred to first access?
- What exactly is 'the freeze'? Pin the distinct boundaries Spring conflates: (a) the soft by-type-query cache hint (freezeConfiguration), (b) the per-bean merged-def freeze (markBeanAsCreated), (c) an actual immutable-store seal. Which of these leaf has, and where each sits in the refresh template.
- How is mid-bootstrap registration made re-entrancy- AND cross-task-concurrency-safe without a global lock (§2.5), given the eager pass is async and background-bootstrap tasks may register on other execution contexts? (Spring's strict/lenient locking, doc 2506, is the precedent to re-express in async terms.)
- Where do conditions and profiles evaluate relative to the freeze — at runtime over collected descriptors before sealing (cross-crate doc §38), or as a separate gate — and is that consistent across conditions/profiles/configuration-classes/auto-configuration?
- How does the dynamic source (the optional WASM-module 'classpath') participate: register into a live store/overlay mid-bootstrap (R1/R2), or only within a defined pre-freeze load window / via a child context (R3)? This determines the runtime-plugin model and must stay origin-agnostic.
- If late registration is bounded by a fixpoint (R1/R2), what is the cap and the §2.9 diagnostic for a runaway registrar-registers-registrar cycle, and how is the late-drive ORDER kept deterministic across builds (registration-order, NOT link-order)?
- Does a SmartInitializingSingleton-analogue (or any post-instantiation callback) have any sanctioned way to contribute a definition, or is post-eager registration always a loud error? (Spring's pass-2 callback runs after all singletons exist; whether it may register is unclear and affects parity.)

### Notes
Grounding read: charter (full), Spring DI design doc lines 350-378 (refresh template, freeze position, SmartInitializingSingleton outside-lock), 2452 (markBeanAsCreated freeze + stale-not-remove invalidation), 2462-2464 (registerBeanDefinition + override + the v7 #36648 registrar-override bug), 2466-2516 (the preInstantiateSingletons two-pass + defensive-copy rationale + strict/lenient locking), 71/130/134 (AOT 'definitions knowable at build time', allowBeanDefinitionOverriding/allowCircularReferences as per-factory toggles, GenericApplicationContext frozen-for-AOT); cross-crate doc §global-classpath-gap + §substitute-design-space + §38 (move conditional logic to runtime over descriptors) + the donsz.nl predictability warning (scope discovery, don't make it implicitly global); nightly doc TL;DR (fully stable-buildable; inventory primary + linkme optional; AFIT/RPITIT/async-closures stable; async-Drop NOT stable → explicit shutdown). Kept implementation-agnostic per the guardrails: presented R1/R2/R3 as divergent candidates spanning preserve-the-dynamism (R1), quarantine-it (R2), and prove-it-unnecessary (R3); did NOT pick the registry substrate, the seal locus, or the async-concurrency primitive — all surfaced as phase2Questions. The single most important framing surfaced for Phase 2: Spring does NOT actually seal its definition store during refresh — 'freeze' is a query-cache hint plus a per-bean edit boundary — so any leaf design importing a hard immutable-store seal mid-refresh is making a STRONGER claim than Spring and must own the consequence (it forbids the register-mid-bootstrap freedom). This feature is the named owner of that decision; the three neighbours (bean-registry, programmatic-registration, background-bootstrap) should drop their deferred phase2 versions of it and point here.

## Area coherence notes

- DUPLICATED MECHANISM — the @Order/@Priority/Ordered comparator is independently claimed by THREE designs. collection-injection says 'where does the ONE canonical comparator live' is its phase2 keystone; autowiring-resolution claims the SAME comparator as its tie-break layer 5d and asks 'where does the one canonical comparator live'; deferral-primitives says orderedStream() 'applies the SAME @Order/@Priority/Ordered comparator collection-injection uses'. All three correctly assert it must be unified, but none is designated the OWNER. This is a latent triplication: unless one feature is made the home of the comparator (with the others consuming it), three realizations could each materialize their own, and the Ordered-interface-beats-annotation-value precedence (which has no rust runtime analogue and must be a captured provenance flag) could be implemented inconsistently. Recommend: collection-injection owns the comparator; autowiring-resolution and deferral-primitives consume it.
- CONTRADICTION-IN-WAITING — setter/field circular-reference tolerance. circular-references R1/R2 assume the early-singleton-exposure machinery EXISTS (a half-built instance handed into the cycle), and bean-instantiation's per-bean sequence places the early-exposure hook 'after instantiate, before populate'. But injection-styles' R1 (constructor-first-collapse) and R3 (typed builder) STRUCTURALLY CANNOT produce a half-built instance — there is no instantiated-but-unpopulated object — and injection-styles itself flags that only its R2 (two-phase construct-then-populate) can offer setter-cycle tolerance. So circular-references' tolerate-setter-cycles realization is only viable if injection-styles chose R2; if injection-styles picks R1/R3 (which it leans toward, citing Rust aliasing + AOT + Spring-Boot's own off-by-default), then circular-references MUST be R3 (deferral-only, no early reference). These two features' realization choices are JOINTLY constrained but their design docs present the choices as independent. They must be co-decided.
- COHERENT-BUT-UNDER-SPECIFIED SEAM — the three-level vs two-level singleton cache is questioned identically by bean-registry, bean-instantiation, AND circular-references (each lists 'do we need the middle singletonFactories level' as a phase2Question, each noting it exists only for AOP early-proxy-lazily-once and that leaf's proxy story is unsettled). This is consistent (all defer to the proxy decision) but means the cache SHAPE — a foundational data structure that bean-registry 'owns' — cannot be settled until auto-proxy-creator/aop-proxying (out-of-area) lands. The area should flag that bean-registry's cache shape is BLOCKED on an out-of-area proxy decision, so it is not silently assumed resolved.
- OVERLAP — @Lazy-at-injection-point vs deferral-primitives' handle family is flagged from BOTH sides (lazy-initialization asks 'is @Lazy-edge subsumed by deferral-primitives' handles', deferral-primitives asks the mirror) and both say it 'must be resolved jointly'. This is a healthy mutual cross-reference, but it means TWO designs currently both describe a cached-single-value deferred handle (lazy-initialization's Lazy<T>, deferral-primitives' ObjectProvider-with-cache) without one being authoritative. Risk: two handle types with near-identical semantics ship. The area should pick deferral-primitives as the handle-family owner and reduce lazy-initialization to the node-flag + global-default + exclusions (which is what both designs already gesture toward).
- MISMATCH — 'two parents' naming hazard is correctly identified by bean-definition (definition-parent / parentName template merge) and container-hierarchy (context-parent / lookup delegation) as orthogonal-but-confusable, and both ask Phase 2 to fix a clear naming separation. Consistent and well-flagged. The remaining latent issue: bean-naming's hierarchy interaction ('a child bean shadows a parent's same-name bean — SILENT and intended') sits in deliberate CONTRAST to bean-naming's within-container rule ('same name twice = LOUD BeanDefinitionOverrideException'). bean-naming flags this contrast, but the cross-feature contract (registry enforces loud-local-collision, hierarchy enforces silent-cross-level-shadow) spans bean-naming + bean-registry + container-hierarchy and no single design owns the combined 'when is a same-name a loud error vs a silent shadow' decision table. Worth a unified statement.
- CONSISTENT BOUNDARY, WORTH CONFIRMING — value-injection (@Value/${...}/#{}) is uniformly treated as a NEIGHBORING feature OUTSIDE this area: autowiring-resolution short-circuits it before bean resolution (step 2), candidate-resolver excludes it, injection-styles tags points bean-vs-value and hands value points off, deferral-primitives declares it a non-interaction. This is coherent across all five designs (no contradiction). The only residual: injection-styles notes its #[inject(default=..)] for optional setters OVERLAPS value-injection's defaulting and 'the boundary must be drawn so they don't both own a param's default' — a genuine but correctly-flagged seam. No action beyond ensuring value-injection (out-of-area) reciprocates the contract.
- REALIZATION-COUPLING — primary-fallback R3 (shared LayerVerdict pipeline) is explicitly admissible ONLY IF autowiring-resolution also chose its layered-pipeline R2; primary-fallback's own phase2 says its realization is 'JOINTLY decided with autowiring-resolution, not independently'. Correctly flagged from primary-fallback's side. autowiring-resolution's design does not reciprocally note that its R2 choice constrains primary-fallback's R3 availability. Minor asymmetry, but the joint-decision should be acknowledged in both to avoid a Phase-3 mismatch.
- FACTORY-BEAN vs PROVIDER UNIFICATION TENSION — factory-bean's R2 ('FactoryBean is just one impl of the universal origin-agnostic Provider') and bean-registry's R3 ('provider-store kernel') and bean-instantiation's R3 (pluggable creation strategy over a Provider) all gesture at the SAME 'everything is a Provider' substrate, and each independently warns it 'borders on pre-deciding the meta-architecture Phase 2 owns'. This is a coherent shared instinct, but it means the 'one universal Provider abstraction' idea is partially designed in three places with three slightly different shapes (Provider-of-Provider, provider-store-kernel, pluggable-creation-strategy). If Phase 2 adopts a universal Provider it must reconcile all three; if it does not, all three R-variants fall together. The area should note these three R-variants are effectively ONE bet on the registry/creation meta-architecture, not three independent options.
